as
The PRIME MINISTER moved:
"That this House do adopt the Standing
Orders relating to Public Business in use
in the House of Commons of the United
Kingdom, with such modifications
appear in manuscript in the volume now
in the custody of the Clerk of the House;
and do further adopt the rules of Parlia
mentary practice and procedure set forth
in the twelfth edition of Sir Erskine
May's treatise on the law, privileges, proceedings, and usages of Parliament."
Question put and agreed to.
The PRIME MINISTER: I beg to
move,-"That on the first occasion that
this House resolves itself into Committee,
Mr. Thomas Moles do take the Chair
as Chairman of Ways and Means."
Question put and agreed to.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have to announce
the following dates and times in connection with the election by this House of 24
Senators. The time for nominations will
expire at 12 o'clock noon, on Saturday,
the 11th inst. If there is a contest, the
poll will open at 8 a.m. on Monday, the
13th inst., and close at 3 p.m on Thursday,
the 16th. The voting, if any, will be by
post, and the necessary instructions are
being sent this evening to every hon.
Member of this House at the address
the form on which he was
given on
nominated a candidate for this House.
Adjournment.-Resolved, "That this
House do adjourn until Wednesday, the
22nd of June, at 11.30 a.m."—[The Prime
Minister].
Adjourned accordingly at ten
minutes to one of the clock, till
Wednesday, the 22nd June, at
11.30 a m.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
Members' Friends
Adjournment
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Wednesday, 22nd June, 1921.
The House met at half an hour after
Eleven of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Prayers were said by the Reverend
Samuel Thompson, M.A.
Mr. SPEAKER: As there is no immediate business before the House, I propose to suspend the sitting until 12-20.
Perhaps this will be a convenient time
for me to let the hon. Members know with
regard to the arrangements for the attendance of Members' friends at the debate
which will take place to-morrow on the
King's Speech. The arrangements are
that Members can obtain from the
Serjeant-at-Arms a book containing
tickets, which when signed by Members
will admit to the Chamber to-morrow up
to the limit of the seating accommodation
of the room.
The sitting now stands suspended until
12-20.
Suspended accordingly at
Twenty Minutes before
Twelve o'clock.
The House resumed at twenty minutes
after Twelve of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
The Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod
(Sir Frederick William Moneypenny,
M.V.O., C.B.E.) was announced.
Addressing Mr. SPEAKER, the Gentleman
Usher said: The King commands this
Honourable House to attend His Majesty
immediately in the Senate.
Mr. SPEAKER, accompanied by the Prime
Minister and other Members, went to the
Senate.
After the delivery of His Majesty's
Message Mr. SPEAKER and hon. Members
returned to the House.
ADJOURNMENT.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until To-morrow at 11.15 a.m."—
[Captain Dixon].
Adjourned accordingly at
Fifteen Minutes before
One of the Clock, until Tomorrow. at 11.15 a.m.
Council of Ireland
Sessional Orders
23 JUNE 1921.
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Thursday, 23rd June, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after
Eleven of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Prayers were said by the Reverend
Hugh McKeag.
Α message was delivered by the
Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod, as
follows:
Mr. SPEAKER, I am directed by His
Excellency the Lord Lieutenant of Ireland as Viceroy to ask the immediate
attendance of this Honourable House in
the Senate to hear the King's Speech
read.
Accordingly, Mr. SPEAKER and the
House went up to attend His Excellency
the Lord Lieutenant, who, standing
before the Throne, read His Majesty's most
gracious Speech.
On their return,
COUNCIL OF IRELAND.
The PRIME MINISTER: I beg to
move-
That the Members of this House do
proceed at once to the election of thirteen
of their number to be Members of the
Council of Ireland; that such election be
by ballot; and that Mr. SPEAKER do make
the
necessary arrangements for such
election.
Question put and agreed to.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have to announce
that in accordance with the resolution
which the House has just passed, I have
made the following arrangements for the
election of thirteen Members of the
Council of Ireland:-
The ballot will take place between
twelve and one o'clock to-dav; Members
will be able to obtain ballot papers from
the officials of the House in the next
room, and they will see from the papers
the way in which they are to vote.
The
poll will close at one o'clock, and as soon
as the counting has taken place I will
cause an announcement of the figures to
be placed outside this House, somewhere
at the door.
B
MEMBERS ELECTED.
The following Members were (by ballot)
elected Members of the Council of Ireland:
-Sir R. N. Anderson, Rt. Hon. John M.
Andrews, Mr. J. Milne Barbour, Rt. Hon.
Sir R. Dawson Bates, Mr. William Coote,
Rt. Hon. Sir James Craig, Bart.; Captain.
Herbert Dixon, Mr. William Grant, Dr.
Robert J. Johnstone, Sir Crawford
McCullagh, Mr. Samuel McGuffin, Mr.
Robert J. McKeown, and Major David G.
Shillington.
SESSIONAL ORDERS.
ELECTIONS.
Ordered, That all Members who are returned for two or more places in any part
of Northern Ireland do make their Election
for which of the places they will serve,
within one week after it shall appear that
there is no question upon the Return for
that place; and if any thing shall come in
question touching the Return or Election
of any Member, he is to withdraw during
the time the matter is in debate; and that
all Members returned upon double Returns do withdraw till the Returns are
determined.
Resolved, That if it shall appear that
any person hath been elected or returned a
Member of this House, or endeavoured so
to be, by Bribery, or any other corrupt.
practices, this House will proceed with the
utmost severity against all such persons as
shall have been wilfully concerned in such
Bribery or other corrupt practices.
WITNESSES.
Resolved, That if it shall appear that
any person hath been tampering with any
Witness, in respect of his evidence to be
given to this House, or any Committee
thereof, or directly or indirectly hath
endeavoured to deter or hinder any person
from appearing or giving evidence, the
same is declared to be a high crime or misdemeanor; and this House will proceed
with the utmost severity against such
offender.
Resolved, That if it shall appear that
any person hath given false evidence in any
case before this House, or any Committee
thereof, this House will proceed with the
utmost severity against such offender.
ROYAL IRISH CONSTABULARY
Ordered. That the Commissioner of
the Royal Irish Constabulary
do
His Majesty's Message HOUSE OF COMMONS

His Majesty's Message
of
I could not have allowed myself to give
Ireland by deputy alone My carnest
prayers and good wishes in the new era
which opens with this ceremony, and I
have therefore come in person, as the
Head of the Empire, to inaugurate this
Parliament on Irish soil.
take care that, during the Session
of Parliament, the passage through
the streets leading to this House be kept
free and open, and that no obstruction be
permitted to hinder the passage
Members to and from this House, and that
no disorder be allowed in the precincts
or in the passages leading to this House,
during the Sitting of Parliament, and that
there be no annoyance therein or thereabouts; and that the Serjeant at Arms
attending this House do communicate this
Order to the Commissioner of the Royal
Irish Constabulary aforesaid.
VOTES, PROCEEDINGS AND RECORDS.
Ordered, That the Votes, Proceedings
and Records of this House be printed,
being first perused by Mr. SPEAKER; and
that he do appoint the printing thereof;
and that no person but such as he shall
appoint do presume to print the same.
PRIVILEGES.
Ordered, That a Committee of Privileges
be appointed.
OUTLAWRIES BILL.
Bill "for the more effectual preventing
Clandestine Outlawries" read the first
time, and ordered to be read a second time.
KING'S MESSAGE.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have to acquaint the
House that this House yesterday attended
in the Senate, where His Majesty the King
was pleased to deliver a Gracious Message
to both Houses of Parliament for Northern
Ireland.
His Majesty's Message was as follows:-
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons-
For all who love Ireland, as I do with
all my heart, this is a profoundly
moving occasion in Irish history. My
memories of the Irish people date back to
the time when I spent many happy days
in Ireland as a midshipman. My
affection for the Irish people has been
deepened by the successive visits since
that time, and I have watched with
constant sympathy the course of their
affairs.
I inaugurate it with deep-felt hope,
and I feel assured that you will do your
utmost to make it an instrument of
happiness and good government for all
parts of the community which you
represent.
This is a great and critical occasion
in the history of the Six Counties, but
not for the Six Counties alone, for everything which interests them touches Ireland,
and everything which touches Ireland
finds an echo in the remotest parts of the
Empire.
Few things are more earnestly desired
throughout the English speaking world
than a satisfactory solution of the agelong Irish problems, which for generations embarrassed our forefathers, as they
now weigh heavily upon us.
Most certainly there is no wish nearer
My own heart than that every man of
Irish birth, whatever be his creed and
wherever be his home, should work in
loyal co-operation with the free communities on which the British Empire is based.
I am confident that the important
matters entrusted to the control and
guidance of the Northern Parliament will
be managed with wisdom and with
moderation, with fairness and due regard
to every faith and interest, and with no
abatement of that patriotic devotion to
the Empire which you proved so gallantly
in the Great War.
Full partnership in the United Kingdom and religious freedom Ireland has
His Majesty's Message.
His Majesty's Speech.
23 JUNE 1921.
long enjoyed. She now has conferred
upon her the duty of dealing with all the
essential tasks of domestic legislation
and government; and I feel no misgiving as to the spirit in which you who
stand here to-day will carry out the allimportant functions entrusted to your
care.
My hope is broader still. The eyes of
the whole Empire are on Ireland to-day—
that Empire in which so many nations
and races have come together in spite of
ancient feuds, and in which new nations
have come to birth within the lifetime of
the youngest in this Hall.
I am emboldened by that thought to
look beyond the sorrow and the anxiety
which have clouded of late My vision of
Irish affairs. I speak from a full heart
when I pray that My coming to Ireland
to-day may prove to be the first step
towards an end of strife amongst her
people, whatever their race or creed. In
that hope, I appeal to all Irishmen to
pause, to stretch out the hand of forbearance and conciliation, to forgive and to
forget, and to join in making for the land
which they love a new era of peace,
contentment, and goodwill.
It is My earnest desire that in Southern
Ireland, too, there may ere long take
place a parallel to what is now passing
in this Hall; that there a similar occasion may present itself and a similar
ceremony be performed.
For this the Parliament of the United
Kingdom has in the fullest measure provided the powers; for this the Parliament of Ulster is pointing the way. The
future lies in the hands of My Irish
people themselves.
May this historic gathering be the
prelude of a day in which the Irish
people, North and South, under one
Parliament or two, as those Parliaments
may themselves decide, shall work together
in common love for Ireland upon the
sure foundations of mutual justice and
respect.
ADDRESS TO HIS MAJESTY.
The PRIME MINISTER: I beg to
move that a humble Address be presented
to His Majesty as follows:-
Most Gracious Sovereign, we your
Majesty's most dutiful and loyal subjects,
the Commons of Northern Ireland, in
Parliament assembled, beg leave to offer
our humble thanks to Your Majesty for the
most Gracious Message
Message which Your
Majesty has delivered to both Houses of
Parliament of Northern Ireland.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): I beg to second.
Question put, and agreed to.
Ordered, That the said Address be presented to His Majesty by such Members of
this House as are of His Majesty's Most
Honourable Privy Council in Ireland.
KING'S SPEECH.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have to acquaint the
House that this House has this day
attended His Excellency the Lord
Lieutenant of Ireland in the Senate, where
His Excellency was pleased to make a
Speech from the Throne to both Houses of
Parliament, of which Speech I have for
greater accuracy obtained a copy. It is
as follows:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I earnestly pray that the Government
of Ireland Act, under which your Parliament has now been constitutionally
established, and which confers wide
powers of self-government upon the Irish
people, and provides for the creation of a
Council of Ireland, in which representatives of the North and South shall meet in
conference, may, under the Providence of
Almighty God, lead to the establishment of
His Majesty's Speech. HOUSE OF COMMONS
Debate on the Address

earnest and sympathetic interest throughout the Empire.
peace throughout Ireland in certain
areas of which the situation causes grave
anxiety. Violence or disturbance inevitably retards the realization of that
security and social order upon which all
hopes of a happy and prosperous country
must depend.
Your first duty must be to ensure
efficient and economical administration,
and your energies will for some time be
closely applied to the important task of
perfecting the administration of those
Services which have been transferred to
your control.
For this purpose Departments of the
Prime Minister and of the Ministers of
Finance, Home Affairs, Labour, Education, Agriculture, and Commerce, have
been determined.
The People, whom you represent,
understand the impossibility of introducing legislation until these Departments are in full working order. They
understand equally well that, when this
has been accomplished,
an
earnest
endeavour will be made to develop still
further the resources of Northern Ireland,
expand trade, improve agriculture and
other industries, re-model education,
amend the licensing laws, and ensure a
brighter future for the great masses of
workers in your midst.
It will be your duty, while pursuing
this policy and effectively guarding the
safety of all persons and property, to
furnish such an example of wise and
just government as shall inspire confidence
throughout the country, and thus contribute to the healing of differences that
have long disturbed Ireland.
Your labours will be watched with
Members of the House of Commons-
The Estimates for the Services for the
remainder of the present Financial Year
will be laid before you in due course, and
will necessarily engage your earnest
consideration.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons-
I feel confident that the Parliament here
established will prove worthy of your
pride in the Empire and your traditional
loyalty to the Throne and Constitution.
And I pray that Almighty God may
bestow His abundant blessing upon your
deliberations.
Major SHILLINGTON (in Military
Uniform): I beg to move:
"That an humble Address be presented
to His Excellency as follows:
Your Excellency,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Commons of
Northern Ireland, in Parliament
assembled, beg leave to offer our
humble thanks to your Excellency for
the Speech which your Excellency has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament of Northern Ireland."
me.
As one whose first insight this is into
Parliamentary procedure I would respectfully ask the indulgence of the House for
any shortcomings in carrying out the
honourable task that has been allotted to
I fully realize the honour that has
been conferred on me in asking me to
move this address, which honour I do not
in any way look on as a personal one,
but rather as a compliment to the many
thousands of loyal men of Ulster who
during the great war so nobly responded
to the call of King and country, and I
can assure hon. Members of this House
that this compliment will be fully
appreciated not only by all ex-service
men in Ulster, but by those who are still
serving His Majesty, and also by the
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
23 JUNE 1921.
relatives and friends of those who laid
down their lives in the greatest conflict
the world has ever known. We are
opening a new page of history. Our
Northern Parliament has now been
established, and we are taking on our
shoulders the extra duties and responsibilities of an outpost of our great Empire.
Yesterday's proceedings will be a great
inspiration and encouragement to us in
the days to come, and we are profoundly
grateful that His Majesty the King did
us the very high honour of coming to
open our first Parliament, and that Her
Majesty the Queen was able to accompany
him. The unparalleled scenes of enthusiasm that were witnessed in this city
yesterday proved once more, if such proof
were necessary, that the people of Ulster
are the most loyal of all His Majesty's
subjects.
His Majesty in his gracious Speech
refers to the fact that the Government of
Ireland Act provides for the creation of
a Council of Ireland. I am sure it is the
earnest, desire of all the hon. Members
of this House that this Council should be
formed as soon as possible, so that representatives of all the different interests
in Ireland may have an opportunity of
conferring together and finding some
means under Divine guidance of improving the deplorable state of affairs.
that unfortunately exists in certain parts
of our beloved land. In this new Parliament of ours we are anxious at all times
to keep before us the higher ideals and
traditions of the British House of Commons, but in setting up our own different
Departments we must bear in mind the
powers that have been conferred on us,
and the amount of territory that we are
called on to govern, and see that from
the start all these Departments are
administered with economy, combined
with efficiency. Our people fully realize
that it will take some considerable time
to get these Departments into full
working order, but it is wonderful what
can be done when the wish and the will
to do are present, and when much useful
legislation is awaiting consideration.
As representing along with my hon.
and learned Friend a county which has been
called-and I think those hon. Members
who know the county will admit rightly
called-the garden of Ireland, I
glad to see that legislation for the benefit
of agriculturists is to be introduced. I am
am
also glad to know that the great question
of education is to be considered. As regards this we have a great lot of leeway to
make up.
The amending of the licensing
laws is a matter that will commend itself
to all right-thinking people. The other
matters mentioned are certain to receive
the hearty support and sympathetic cooperation of all the hon. Members of the
House. We will be careful in all our acts,
to inspire confidence amongst all classes
of the community, and by the help of
Almighty God to always keep before us the
vision of what we would like, not only
our own province, but our whole country
to eventually attain. We are anxious
that the establishment of our own Parliament will increase more than ever our
loyalty to the Throne and Constitution and
our pride in the British Empire.
Captain MULHOLLAND (in military
uniform): I rise to second the motion
which has been SO ably and
hon.
eloquently moved by my
and
gallant Friend, the Member for the
County of Armagh. I, like him, would
crave
the indulgence of this House.
It is usual on an occasion like this to ask
right hon. and hon. Members to look with
favourable view on the few words that a
new Member, who is unaccustomed to this
kind of Parliamentary procedure, may
desire to address to them. I consider that
this compliment has not been paid so much
to me as to that enormous body of loyal
electors of the County of Down whose
hearts beat with enthusiasm and pride and
with loyalty to His Majesty the King. I
also feel that it is a distinct and true
compliment to the ex-service men, because
not only has an ex-serviceman been chosen
to move this motion, but an ex-serviceman
has been chosen to second it. No doubt
that compliment is really well deserved.
because our lads here in the six counties
went out to France and fought like men,
who were born and bred in Ulster, and
like men who have at the bottom of their
hearts the most deep and loyal conviction
that the Empire stands first. Therefore
they were prepared to lay down their lives
in defence of those principles which they
all hold so dear.
Now if I may I will just say one or two
words about the King's visit to Belfast.
I think the whole Empire has realized
that we gave His Gracious Majesty
right royal reception in the city of
Belfast. I know myself from watching
a
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Captain Mulholland]
His Majesty's face and the face of the
Queen that they were most deeply and
profoundly moved by the enthusiastic
reception they received not only in this
House, but in the Ulster Hall and
throughout the whole of the streets of the
City of Belfast. The King represents to
us far more than a mere figurehead at
the head of the nation-it is His Majesty's
primary duty to act as figurehead over
the Empire-but I look upon him as that
dear link which binds all the various
units of the Empire into an indissoluble
chain. I may say that we are proud
to form one link in that chain, and we
intend always to remain one link in that
chain which holds the whole Empire together in one solid compact body. Now
His Majesty, in his gracious Speech,
states
It is our duty effectively to guard the
safety of all persons and property."
I feel sure that our new Government will
make full use of any powers that may be
entrusted to them with regard to the
preservation of law and order in the six
counties; I do feel certain that we
can rely on them to administer fair, openminded, impartial justice to all people
of all classes and creeds in this community; and provided they behave as
loyal and dutiful citizens of His Majesty
the King, they will have nothing to fear
from this new Government of Northern
Ireland.
His Majesty further makes mention of
the necessity of re-modelling education.
Now we all know, and right hon.
and hon. Members will
agree with
me, that the future of this country
rests in the satchel of the schoolboy. They realize, as my hon. and
gallant Friend stated, that education in
the past has not been what it should be.
They realize that the primary and ultimate
object of education is the production of
loyal and patriotic citizens, and that our
education in the past has not been
altogether directed towards that end.
Those right hon. and hon. Members who
take an interest in education have
observed that the curriculum used in our
National Schools tends towards disloyalty,
rather than loyalty and that curriculum
rests entirely on the personality of the
men who are at the head of the schools.
Provided you have a man who is loyal
himself, he will teach his pupils to be loyal,
no matter what books may pass into his
hands. But I do feel that the whole
system of education in Northern Ireland
requires, as His Gracious Majesty says, to
be completely re-modelled from top to
bottom. After all, the children, who
should be taught loyalty and a proper
feeling for the Imperial connection in our
National Schools, are the future men and
women of this country, and it is our duty
and our privilege to see that they are
educated in those principles of loyalty
which are so important to the future welfare and prosperity of the six counties.
I may mention that, as most of you right
hon, and hon. Members know, the teaching
of history in the National Schools makes
no mention of that beneficent legislation
which has been passed to ameliorate the
condition of farmers and labourers
throughout the whole of Ireland. What
we want to do is to come forward, and not
to look back, to try to forget the past, to
move forward with one view only, and that
is to make this country what it should be,
a real Garden of Eden.
His Majesty has expressed in his
gracious Speech an earnest desire for
peace throughout the whole of Ireland.
We, Sir, have taken the first step in
accepting the responsibility for these six
counties. The next step must and should
be taken by the South and West. It is
now up to them to work their Parliament,
as we intend to work our Parliament
here, and I pray that Almighty God may
touch their hearts and minds and direct
them towards that direction which we so
much desire, that is, that they may, perhaps, derive from us some portion of that
intense loyalty which we hold for our beloved King. I hope that the visit of
His Majesty yesterday may, perhaps,
touch some spark which lies dormant in
their hearts, and if that be So,
and
can
if they
show us that
they intend make their Parliament
to
a success and to return to
their allegiance to His Majesty the King,
I feel sure that all right hon. and hon.
Members of this House will stretch forth
the right hand of fellowship towards
them, and assist them by every means in
their power to make this dear island of
ours a country of prosperity, and a
country which is moving foward in the
path of progress and will always remain
an integral unit of our British Empire.
Mr. COOTE: I wish to rise at this point
to express my thanks, and I am sure I
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
23 JUNE 1921.
voice the opinions of the House when I say
the appreciation and thanks of this House,
for the excellent manner in which this
motion has been moved and seconded by
the hon. and gallant Members. I think,
if I may say so, that they would do credit
to a larger and older assembly; and their
words will be read with very great interest as coming from two hon. and
gallant Members making their first
speeches in this House. With regard to
the King's Speech, I would like to
make some observations. Running
through the Message, the gracious Message, and realizing the sentiments which
seemed to be in the minds of the mover
of the Address and also of the seconder,
there is a great hope that law and order
will be not only maintained at its present
standard, but that the people in these
six counties, in the area of this Parliament, will be protected in their lives and
in their property. Well, Sir, we all reecho those sentiments, but I would like
very simply to ask how can you expect,
life and property to be safe either in
these six counties or in any other part
of Ireland while the leaders of all this
murder gang, leaders who are known to
His Majesty's Government, remain at
large. And they ask Members of this
House and the leaders of the House to
hold out their hands to be shaken by
hands red with the blood of innocent
people of this country. These are things
the Ulster people feel very deeply about
and cannot understand, and, indeed, all
ordinary people cannot understand. The
camp followers of these people and many
youths who are the press gang of this
organization are being very properly
hunted to earth, and very properly
brought to justice, but their leaders,
whose dwellings are known to the Government, and who are probably under their
protection, are at large and allowed to
remain at large.
Another point I would like to make
at the starting of the business of this
House is that in the present state of
Ireland we as representatives of the
people of Ulster I believe are voicing the
sentiments of the majority of the people
of Ulster when I say that we shall view
with very great apprehension any trifling
with the machinery set up by the British
Parliament through this Parliament and
the Parliament in the South in any
negotiations which may take place for a
settlement in Ireland. We are most
anxious to collaborate with the South of
Ireland, with every honest man and
woman in the South of Ireland who have
love of their country at heart. We do
not mind what is their creed, or what
their politics are. We wish for the
peace, security, and prosperity of everybody in this island. We are willing to
reach out the hand of fellowship in any
place in which we honourably can; but
we submit, Sir, that now that the
machinery has been set in motion there
must be no further intriguing. If we
are to retain the confidence
People of Ulster we must loyally
abide by the machinery provided by
Parliament for the coming together of
the people of the South and North, and
in doing so we shall arrive much more
quickly at an honourable peace.
of the
Then there was also expressed in the
Gracious Speech delivered by His
Excellency to-day the hope that the
question of finance will be very carefully
looked into and very carefully considered
in this House. We are very glad the
Government put these words into the
mouth of His Excellency in his Gracious
Speech. We are at one with that sentiment,
and we as ordinary Members, the private
Members of this House, want to say
here and now that we will give every
support to the right hon. the Minister of
Finance in every effort he will make, and
he will require to make continuous effort
I am sure, but we will try to do what we
can to support him in economizing the
resources of this Parliament from
wasteful legislation, or wasteful expenditure, or unnecessary omnces or foolish
salaries. We shall try and support him
in maintaining as big a margin as he can
for the full development of the resources
of Ulster.
any
Might I ask in connection with this
matter when we may expect estimates? I
can quite realize that there must be some
intervening time before Parliament really
settles down to the business of legislation,
but we would be glad to know when we
may expect the estimates to be laid on the
table of this House. I noticed in a
speech by the Chief Whip of one of the
parties who ought now to be represented in
this House, that he deplores the fact that
there could be no real opposition in this
Parliament. I would like to assure my
hon. Friend that he is under a very great
misapprehension if he thinks that there is
any body of Ulstermen who would walk
into an Assembly and swallow any pill
Debate on the Address 32
Debate on the Address
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote]
which the Government of the day might
throw at them. That is not our nature.
It is the nature of Ulstermen to go into
questions of finance, and to try to get the
best value they can for their money.
We
are here to say to the Government that we
will support them in every lawful and
laudable enterprise in which they are
engaged. We will give them wholehearted support, but we will discuss in the
most searching manner every Bill,
especially money Bills, that is presented to
this House.
I want to say this to my hon. Friends
who are Private Members like myself, it is
the Private Members who really control
and shape legislation in any Assembly. I
do expect in this House that hon.
Members will take an interest in all
legislation, and that they will bring
the most searching criticism to bear on all
measures. It will be friendly criticism I
hope, but if we find that it comes to a
matter of principle, speaking for myself, I
am prepared to divide the House if I think
the Government are bringing in proposals
that are not really in the interests of my
constituents and of Ulster as a whole.
There will be a healthy atmosphere of
criticism in this House. Do not let the
Government run away with the idea that
they are simply to come in with their Bills
and that we will swallow them. They will
find themselves in a minority some day if
they do that. The only thing I want to say,
in conclusion, is that although we may be
looked upon here at the moment as being
in the nature of a party, we shall always
retain our right to see that the people, no
matter to what party they belong, get
healthy legislation in keeping with the
resources of Ulster. In other words we
must try to make the most of the finances
committed to the care of this House.
There are services which we should try to
get re-modelled. I hope the Governmentwill re model all services on an economic
basis, do away with a lot of unnecessary
things and unnecessary offices, and remove
all limpets. I hope we shall have no
limpets in our Ulster Parliament. I hope
every man and woman employed here will
do real work and give loyal service, and
get reasonable pay for doing it. So far as
we are concerned we shall watch the course
of Parliament, and lock to it to keep its
life clean, true and noble, and in accordance with the ideals of the people of Ulster
I congratulate most heartily
the
hon. Members who have moved and
seconded the motion. I again express
congratulations to them for the manner in
which they conducted these proceedings
for the first time, and for the excellent
language in which they introduced the
resolution to the House.
I
Mr. DONALD : I do not know on
which side of the House I find myself
after the speech just made by my hon.
Friend. At the outset, however, I just
wish like him to congratulate the hon.
and gallant Member for Armagh,
who moved, and the hon. and
gallant Member for Down who
seconded the resolution. May I say that
I regretted the absence of any reference
in the Speech of His Majesty to a subject
of urgent and pressing importance.
refer to unemployment. I hope, however,
that this Parliament in the very near
future will do something in regard to
the matter. The matter keenly affects
the workers of Belfast, and more especially
those ex-servicemen who find themselves
out of employment. I hope, Sir, when
the Prime Minister rises to make his reply
he will give us some idea of what the
policy of this Parliament will be in the
near future in regard to the question I
have called attention to. Of course, I
do not forget that the industrial problems.
which concern us in this island of ours
are no greater than in other parts of the
Kingdom. These problems are both
numerous and complex. There are dark
clouds hanging over the South and West
of Ireland, but all these difficulties are
as nothing to the greater trouble which
Great Britain passed through and successfully coped with during the past five
years. 1 am convinced that if we face
these problems fairly and trust to the
sound sense and steadfast courage of our
race we shall emerge successfully from our
present trials. War, whether industrial
or political, can be brought about in a
day, or even in a minute, put you cannot
make peace in the same short space of
time.
Reference has been made to the present
state of the country. It is desirable and
imperative that there should be law and
order-real law and order-and not of
the sham quality. I hope our Government will take this matter up at an early
date. After all the Imperial House of
Parliament is very largely responsible for
much of the lawlessness that has been
allowed to go on in Ireland. When we
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
23 JUNE 1921.
here tackle the question let us see to it
that our people will be able to go to their
daily avocations with a sense of safety
and security. I would also like to see the
Government tackling another problem of
serious importance-the boycotting of the
North. To my mind, Sir, this is a great
question. People to my own knowledge
have been discharged from their positions
in factories, in wholesale industries, and
even in the shipbuilding yards. The boycott has been directed against the export
of Belfast goods to other parts of Ireland.
I do hope, Sir, that this House will take
steps, and immediate steps, to relieve this
boycott. Whether our enemies like it or
not, I think that steps should be taken
in order that our people may get restored
to their employment. This thing is becoming worse daily, and I hope that the
Government will do something in the
direction of relieving the boycott in our
city especially.
Before sitting down, I wish to congratulate my hon. and gallant Friends,
and I also wish to say that I am delighted
to see my hon. Friend from Tyrone and
Fermanagh, and we may expect from him
some lively times in future debates.
on
The PRIME MINISTER: It has
been the custom in
the Imperia
Parliament for the Prime Minister
rising to reply to the various questions that
have arisen in consequence of this motion
first of all, to pay a tribute to the
hon. Members-in this case the hon. and
gallant Members-who move and second
the address; and I cannot help feeling that
during the many years I have spent in the
Imperial Parliament I have seldom heard
speeches more concise, to the point,
eloquent, and, if I may say so, breathing
with the spirit which permeates us all on
this occasion, so soon after His Gracious
Majesty's visit, than the speeches of my
two hon. and gallant Friends. It is not
an easy task to come for the first time to a
new House and to break the ice on the first
day and to do it so well as it has just been
performed by these two hon. and gallant
Members. It has been the custom, of
course, to exercise a great discretion in the
choice of those who are to have the
privilege and the duty upon this day or
occasion. I think our choice has been fully
justified. The name borne by the hon.
and gallant Member for Armagh, long
associated with that county, is a name
which we believe will contribute very much
C
to the debates of this Assembly in the
future. My hon, and gallant Friend and
colleague in the membership for the
County of Down is, I believe, one of the
rising young men of Ulster, along with
many others who have come forward within
recent years and recent months, and I
believe he will stand out in future years,
and be a worthy follower of an old Member
of the County Down-his grandfather.
to
All our hearts are still full of the
moving spectacle we had the honour
witness yesterday, and it would be
hardly possible for me to stand at this
table without expressing my heartfelt
thanks to the citizens of Belfast on the one
hand, and all those who crowded into the
city from the surrounding districts on the
other, for not only having given their
Gracious Majesties a welcome the like of
which I believe they never received in any
quarter of their Empire before, but also
for the fact that throughout the whole of
these proceedings the utmost good humour
and good behaviour prevailed. There was
not one single thing from morning to
evening, to mar the spectacle, which will
stand out as a credit to the people of
Ulster.
We were very much touched by Her
Majesty accompanying His Majesty on this
occasion, and I know from personal conversation with them both that they were
graciously pleased to
to say that their
reception will last as a red-letter day in
their memories.
His Excellency the Lord Lieutenant
has now visited us for the second time.
We gave him a right royal welcome on the
first occasion, and to-day once more, as His
Majesty's mouth-piece in reading the
Gracious Speech from the Throne, we
welcome him, and say to him that he will
ever receive the most cordial welcome in
Belfast and the six counties, and that we
hope that for years to come he may occupy
the high and the proud position which he
now adorns.
To pass on to the other matters referred
to in His Gracious Majesty's Speech, it is
extremely difficult, as all must know, to
outline definitely a policy for the
immediate future. There has been, since
we were asked to take up and work the
Government of Ireland Act, an enormous
amount of work to do. The preparations
in connection, even with the visit yesterday
occupied all the time of the Government
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
HOUSE OF COMMONS
The Prime Minister]
and myself, and it was impossible to turn
to those problems which must necessarily
occupy a great deal of our time in the near
future. It is quite impossible to do much
until we have our departments here in
the capital city fully established, but I can
assure hon. Members in all quarters of the
House that the Government, represented
by my right hon. Friends beside me here,
and others, are as anxious to get on with
the work as any individual in the House.
My hon. Friend,
Friend, the Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh, has made a speech,
part of which, of Course as he knows
perfectly well, should have been delivered
in the Imperial House of Commons, because
we here have not had handed to us the
reserved services, and consequently all debates affecting them should take place on
the floor of the House of Commons in
Westminster. He touched upon economy.
Might I associate myself with him
immediately on that point, because as a
Member in various capacities in the
Imperial Parliament, although we did not
always throw the blame upon the
individual members of the House, we, who
were Members of the Government, found
tremendous pressure brought to bear upon
us by Members of the House of Commons,
which entailed enormous expenditure. We
will be a small community. Every action
will be open to the closest analysis and to
the closest criticism. But I venture to say
that we have an admirable opportunity, in
establishing our departments, of beginning
on a sound basis, and it will be up to our
successors not to depart from it.
But a great responsibility rests upon
hon. Members, and they are apt very often
-instead of turning down claims themselves, and taking the responsibility of
putting off clamours from their
constituencies for this, that, and the other,
--they are apt to come to the Government, and press these claims upon the
Government, and then blame the Government if they do not carry them out. I
think it will be a very good thing for
hon. Members to take the subject of my
hon, Friend's speech and extract these
sentiments from it, keep them in their
bedrooms and other places, and say to
themselves-"We must bear that in
mind." Does anyone imagine that my
right hon. Friend beside me will be a
spendthrift, or that my hon. Friend the
Financial Secretary is likely to cast on
all sides the limited resources of our exchequer? Certainly not; and my Rt. hon.
Friend. who has undertaken one of the
most important duties in connection with
commerce, will back them up, on the
other hand, in trying to bring to this
community that trade which necessarily
brings with it prosperity to the whole
province. I think my hon. Friend
may rest assured that at every turn our
financial side of Government is fully safeguarded by the appointments which I
have had the honour to make.
Well now, if I may turn to my
hon. Friend, one of the Members for East
Belfast, I do not think he need for a
moment feel that the Government have
neglected or will neglect most earnest
consideration of the problems associated
with unemployment. They are not matters
which can be brought into this House
immediately because it will be my duty
presently to move that we adjourn to a
late date in September in order to give
time to get our Departments established.
but I can assure him that I have already
taken all the steps that I can privately to
ensure that as far as possible we should
get our fullest possible share of those
necessities for carrying on industries and
at the same time secure orders, the filling
of which brings work to the loom and
yards and to our manufacturing districts
throughout the country. At all events,
Sir, I myself and my colleagues are at the
disposal of the people of Northern Ireland. We have nothing in our view
except the welfare of the people. Our
duty and our privilege are from now onwards to have our Parliament well established, to look to the people as a whole, to
set ourselves to probe to the bottom those
problems that have retarded progress in
the past, to do everything that lies in
our power to help forward developments
in the town and country, so that the
Parliament established yesterday by His
Gracious Majesty may at all events be a
Parliament which has set out upon its
task fully realizing the responsibility that
rests upon it, and fully determined to maintain the highest traditions of any Parliament in the British Empire. Sir, over and
above that my hon. and gallant Friend,
the Member for Co. Down referred to itover and above that, every person inside
our particular boundary may rest assured
that there will be nothing meted out to
them but the strictest justice. None need
be afraid, the laws must be obeyed.
will be cautious in our legislation. We
We
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
23 JUNE 1921.
will be absolutely honest and fair in
administering the law. When we take
over the police, as I hope we will be able
to do at no great distance of time, and be
responsible for the maintenance of law
and order within the boundaries of
Northern Ireland-when we do that men's
lives shall be very dear to us, and just
as we treasure those heroes who fought
in the great war, so we shall safeguard
those who take the heavy responsibility
of working either as constables, special
constables, or any other force it may be
necessary to raise. We shall treasure
them in our hearts, and everything that
is necessary to provide for them and for
those who may be left behind them in
case of murder, such as has taken place
throughout other parts of Ireland, shall
be done. All that will take time, and I
must ask hon. Members to give us time to
perfect our machinery, time to get down
to work, time to show the Ulster people
that in us they have not only a Parliament, but an executive which is desirous
to help them at every turn.
SO
With these words I desire again to
express my own heartfelt thanks that
everything yesterday passed off
splendidly to the renown of our people
here. I thank hon. Members, many of
whom worked so hard to make that visit
a success. I say in conclusion, that when
we meet again in the autumn we will be
able to go straight ahead and to bring
to the people of Northern Ireland some
of the benefits which have been indicated
in the Gracious Speech from the Throne.
Question put, and agreed to.
Ordered, That the said Address be presented to His Excellency by such Members
of this House as are of His Majesty's
Most Honourable Privy Council.
Captain DIXON: I beg to move that
this House will, at its next sitting, resolve
itself into a Committee to consider the
supply to be granted to His Majesty.
Question put, and agreed to.
Captain DIXON: I beg to move that
this House will, at its next Sitting,
resolve itself into a Committee to
consider the ways and means of raising
the supply to be granted to His Majesty.
Question put, and agreed to.
The PRIME MINISTER: I beg to
move, "That the cordial thanks of this
House be extended to the Lord Mayor
of Belfast and the Corporation for their
kindness in granting the use of the City
Hall for the initial meeting of the House
of Commons and for the State opening of
the Parliament of Northern Ireland, and
further to the Lord Mayor, Town Clerk,
City Chamberlain, and other officials for
their unfailing courtesy and generous
assistance.
That this resolution be entered upon the
Records of this House, and that Mr.
Speaker do convey it in a suitable manner
to the Lord Mayor.
Question put, and agreed to.
ADJOURNMENT.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until Tuesday, the 20th day of
September next."-[Captain Dixon].
Adjourned accordingly at
Ten Minutes before One of
the Clock, till Tuesday,
20th September.
Oral Answers. 40
Oral Answers.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
HOUSE OF COMMONS,

Tuesday, 20th September, 1921.
The House met at a quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
ORAL ANSWERS TO QUESTIONS
Mr. DONALD: Mr. SPEAKER, may I
ask the Prime Minister a question of
urgent public importance?
Mr. SPEAKER: As I think the hon.
Gentleman knows quite well from his experience in the Imperial Parliament,
private notice questions must be questions
of which notice is given to me and also
to the Minister of whom they are to
be asked, otherwise I am afraid he cannot
ask the question now.
Mr. DONALD: I bow to your ruling,
Sir. I am fully aware of the procedure
in the other House, but certainly this is
of such importance that I thought I might
put it to-day. I put the question down
as very important at the request of the
public in my constituency.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid I cannot
allow the question to-day as no notice has
been given either to me or to the Minister,
but if the hon. Member will let me know
to-morrow, I have no doubt if the
question is urgent he can put it as a
private notice question.
Mr. COOTE: On a point of order,
Sir, I should like your ruling on a question
which I think is of public importance.
If I may read the question:
that
Mr.
If Mr. SPEAKER is aware
Eamonn De Valera, one of the Members
elected-
Mr. SPEAKER: Would the hon.
Gentleman raise that question later?
There is a private notice question, and he
can raise it when the private notice
question is ended.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
Mr. LYNN: I beg to ask the Prime
Minister whether he can allocate a day for
the discussion of the general situation
in Ireland as it affects Northern Ireland?
The PRIME MINISTER: I think it
would be for the convenience of the House
if I were to make a statement on the
motion for the adjournment this afternoon and arrange for a debate to-morrow
-a free debate to-morrow-also on the
motion for adjournment, which will allow
Members in all parts of the House to raise
any question which they desire.
Mr. LYNN: I am quite agreeable so
long as we get a day to discuss the
question.
PRESERVATION OF THE PEACE.
Mr. McGUFFIN (by Private Notice)
asked the Prime Minister if he can inform
the House as to which authority the duty
of the maintenance of the peace in the
City of Belfast and the Northern area
generally is at present entrusted; whether
in a recent outbreak in Belfast in which
eighteen lives were lost and over one
hundred injured unnecessary delay in the
intervention of the police and military was
responsible for those disastrous results;
whether he can state the cause of this
delay, and whether at present a clear and
definite understanding exists in respect
to the preservation of the peace in the
areas referred to; whether in view of the
serious outbreak of disorder in Belfast on
Sunday evening last, in which two young
women lost their lives, he can inform the
House what are the powers of the
Northern Parliament for the enforcement
of law and order?
For any
The PRIME MINISTER: The duty of
maintaining the peace within the area of
Northern Ireland at present rests entirely
with the Imperial authorities. As regards Belfast, it has been the practice to
act in consultation with the Lord Mayor
as Chief Magistrate of the city.
information regarding the specific case
mentioned by my hon. Friend, who is a
Member of the Imperial House of
Commons, he should address a question to
the Chief Secretary and the Secretary of
State for War in the Imperial Parliament. As regards the last part of the
question, under Section 9 (1)
of the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, the Royal Irish
Constabulary is a reserved service
until such date, not being later than
three years after the appointed day, as
His Majesty in Council may determine.
I may add that the members of my
Cabinet and myself have met the Lord
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
20 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Mayor and the military and police
authorities in conference in an advisory
capacity on sever occasions, and our
joint efforts will continue to be devoted to
the preservation of the peace and the
protection of life and property.
Mr. McGUFFIN: May I ask the
Prime Minister a supplementary question
as to whether there is any prospect of the
Northern Parliament at a future date
having control of law and order in the
area that they are called upon to
administer?
The
PRIME MINISTER: Yes,
certainly, and I propose to deal partly
with that point in the statement I am
about to make to the House; but my hon.
Friend knows perfectly well that when
the question was under discussion in the
Imperial House it was at first proposed
that the police were not to be transferred
to Northern or Southern Ireland until the
expiration of three years. During the
course of the debate that was altered to
within three years, and we hope to act
in a reasonable time in getting over
complete control of the police.
Mr. DONALD: Seeing that the question
put covers largely the question I was
about to put to-day, may I ask the Prime
Minister was he consulted, and also the
Lord Mayor, recently about putting a
patch of my constituency in East Belfast
under a special Curfew, and, therefore, is
it the intention of the Government in regard to York Street and North Queen
Street to put a bridge across from
Gallaher's factory to York Street Mill, so
that General Macready can review the
situation for himself? It is ridiculous.
Mr. COOTE: Arising out of the answer
of the Prime Minister, is he aware that
the request of the Chief Magistrate of
this city was refused at the beginning,
and, therefore, that, in the ordinary
course of things, if attention had been
paid to the request of the Chief
Magistrate, in all probability much loss
of life might have been averted?
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps I may point.
out to the hon. Member what I am sure
he will appreciate. These are extremely
difficult questions to raise in this House,
though I should be the last person to
desire in any way to fetter the natural
desire of hon. Members to discuss this
important domestic question. At the
same time hon. Members must remember
that it is strictly out of order in this
House to discuss such questions, as the
actual conduct of the military and police
on particular occasions, which, as the
Prime Minister has just said, are entirely
matters within the jurisdiction at present
of the Imperial Parliament. Of course,
any question addressed to this Government must be in the form of whether or
not this Government will approach the
Imperial Government in these matters.
Mr. COOTE: Surely, Mr. SPEAKER-I
put it to you with the greatest possible
respect-in a matter of this kind, in
which the peace of the city and the lives
of the citizens are at stake, it is eminently
a question that ought to be put to the
Prime Minister of this House, to see if he
has exhausted all the avenues of influence
with Dublin Castle and the Government.
Mr. SPEAKER: Certainly.
Mr. COOTE: And if, having failed, as
they have failed, what steps does he propose to take in order to see to it that no
future occurrences of this kind will happen through the carelessness of Dublin
Castle rendering nugatory the action of
the police and military in this city?
The PRIME MINISTER: In reply to
my hon. Friend, the Member for the
East Division of Belfast (Mr. Donald), I
would say that the question of the imposition or the modification of the Curfew
Order is a matter for the Lord Mayor
and his advisors, in consideration not only
of the state of the city but also of the
immediate outlook, and of course that is a
matter with which the Government at present could not concern itself. With
regard to the suggested action by the
military, my hon. Friend must know that
the military in matters of this kind are
guided entirely by the Commander-in-
Chief, who is under the control only of
the Secretary of State for War, and any
question affecting their tactics in the
matter my hon. Friend knows quite well
he must reserve until he takes his seat in
the Imperial Parliament. With regard
to what has fallen from my hon. Friend,
the Member for Fermanagh and Tyrone
(Mr. Coote), we have as a Cabinet offered
our best advice on all occasions when there
has been difficulty in Belfast. We allow
no feelings of our own to influence us,
and we stand on no ceremony, but we
assist in every possible way we can the
Lord Mayor and the military and the
police. A great deal has been done, and
further plans have been made by all concerned, which I trust sincerely will be
sufficient for any emergency that may
Select Committee.
Stormont Castle.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister]
arise; but we will keep in close touch with
those authorities, and needless to say our
advice will always be at their service for
the preservation of the peace and law and
order in the Northern counties.
"That the Committee have power to
send for persons, papers and records;
"That the Committee have leave to sit
notwithstanding the adjournment of the
House;
"That three be the quorum of the
Committee."[Captain Dixon].
Select Committees Appointed
REMUNERATION OF MINISTERS.
Resolved: *
"That notwithstanding anything contained in the Standing Orders, the Select
Committee on Remuneration of Ministers,
etc., may consist of more than five
Members, and the motion for the appointment of the Committee may be taken at
this day's Sitting."-[Captain Dixon].
Resolved:
"That a Select Committee be appointed.
to consider what the remuneration of
Ministers, Parliamentary Secretaries,
Assistant Parliamentary Secretaries and
H.M.'s Attorney-General for Northern
Ireland should be;
"That Dr. John Campbell, Mr. Lloyd
Campbell, Mrs. Chichester, Mr. Grant,
Mr. McMullan, Major Shillington and
Mr. Twaddell, be members of the Select
Committee;
"That Dr. John Campbell do act as
Chairman thereof;
"That the Committee have power to
send for persons, papers and records;
"That the Committee have leave to sit
notwithstanding the adjournment of the
House;
"That five be the quorum of the
Committee."[Captain Dixon].
HOUSE OF COMMONS (KITCHEN
AND REFRESHMENT ROOMS
(JOINT COMMITTEE)).
Resolved:
"That a Select Committee be appointed,
with such Members as may be added
thereto by the Senate, to control the
arrangements for the Kitchen and
Refreshment Rooms for both Houses;
"That the Committee do consist of five
Members of this House with an equal
number of Members of the Senate;
"That Mr. Burn, Captain Mulholland,
Sir Crawford McCullagh, Dr. Morrison
and Mrs. McMordie, be Members of the
Select Committee;
STORMONT CASTLE DEMESNE.
Resolved:
"That this House approves of Stormont Castle Demesne as the place where
the new Parliament Houses and
Ministerial Buildings shall be erected, and
as the place to be determined as the seat
of the Government of Northern Ireland
as and when suitable provision has been
made therefor."-[The Prime Minister].
