KING'S MESSAGE.
(ANSWER TO ADDRESS.)
The PRIME MINISTER reported His
Majesty's answer to the Address
followeth: :-
US
I have received with great satisfaction
the loyal and dutiful expression of your
thanks for the message which I delivered
at the inauguration of the Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
LORD LIEUTENANT'S SPEECH.
(ANSWER TO ADDRESS.)
The PRIME MINISTER reported the
Lord Lieutenant's answer to the Address
as followeth :-
20 SEPTEMBER 1921. Debate on Adjournment. 46
Orders of the Day.
I have received with great satisfaction
the expression of your thanks for the
speech which I delivered at the opening
of the first Parliament of Northern
Ireland.
THE OFFICE OF SPEAKER.
*
Resolved, that a humble Address be
presented to His Excellency the Lord
Lieutenant, praying him, on behalf of His
Majesty, to make provision for the support and dignity of the office of Speaker
of the Senate of Northern Ireland.-[The
Prime Minister.]
Resolved, That a humble Address be
presented to His Excellency the Lord
Lieutenant, praying him, on behalf of
His Majesty, to make provision for the
support and dignity of the Speaker of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland.
-[The Prime Minister.]
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
SUPPLY (COMMITTEE).
WAYS AND MEANS (COMMITTEE).
Postponed until to-morrow. [Capt.
Dixon.]
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
CAPT. DIXON: I beg to move: That
this House do now adjourn."
Mr. COOTE: On a point of order, I
wish your ruling, Sir, on this question:
"If Mr. Speaker is aware that Mr. Eamonn
de Valera, one of the Members elected
for the County of Down, was born in the
United States of America, of alien parentage, and is not a naturalised British
subject, and consequently not capable to
represent or serve in this Parliament;
and whether his election will now be
declared void, and a writ issued for a
new election?
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid I will only
be able to allow the hon. Member to
raise the question as a matter of privilege
because after the Orders of the Day have
been read, of course, an ordinary question
could not be dealt with. But as a matter of
privilege, which I think it is, it ought to
be dealt with immediately if raised.
ruling on that point, I think, cannot be
other than this-that we in this House,
under an Act of Parliament, have the
same election laws as those which govern
My
the elections to the Imperial Parliament,
and I feel that as the Gentleman in
question has been now a Member of the
Imperial Parliament for about three
years, and no steps have been taken in
regard to this matter, it hardly devolves
upon me, as representing the rights of
this House, to take any steps so far as
the Northern Parliament of Ireland is
concerned.
THE PRIME MINISTER'S
STATEMENT.
The PRIME MINISTER: I think that
I would be consulting the interests of all
Members in the House, and also satisfying
opinion outside the House, if I took this
early opportunity of giving some comprehensive statement regarding the interval
that has elapsed since the 24th June last,
when this House stood adjourned up to
the present time. At that time my chief
reason for asking for so prolonged an
interval was that I hoped we would have
been in a position to secure the transfer
of various services under the Government
of Ireland Act, that my Ministers would
have their departments in thorough-going
order, and that we could report, at all
events, to the House, not necessarily the
possibility of immediate legislation but at
all events that the full machinery of
Government was now in your hands, and
that you would be able to proceed, as we
have all been looking forward to, with the
carrying out of the Act passed by the
Imperial Parliament. When I asked for
this long adjournment, neither I nor any
member of my Cabinet had any knowledge, direct or indirect, that an invitation
would be issued by Mr. Lloyd George for
a conference between Sinn Fein and themselves on the one hand, and between the
British Government and ourselves on the
other. The invitation, following on our
long adjournment, came as much as a
surprise to my colleagues and myself as
it did to any private Member of the House
or anyone of the public outside. The
general expectation that I enjoyed with
all others in the Northern area was that
failing functioning by the South some
form of Crown Colony Government would
be put into operation under Section 72
of the Act.
A reasonable time I took to be not
months but weeks, but so quickly after
the adjournment took place did the invitation issue that I was not expecting, of
course, any action in that particular
direction between those two dates. When
Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 49
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister]
the invitation to the conference reached
my Ministers and myself from Downing
Street, we had many anxious meetings,
but eventually, as all are aware, we came
to the conclusion on the whole it was
better for us to accept that invitation.
In the first place we feared, and I think
we rightly feared, that to make a strictly
negative answer on that occasion would
have instantly prejudiced the whole of
Ulster in the eyes of our sympathisers not
only in Great Britain but throughout the
colonies and other parts of the world.
Secondly, it would have risked a
settlement behind our backs, and
although it may be possible to stand
by, whilst others are carving out
our destinies, I would not take the responsibility of allowing others to say what
the future might be, regarding this
Province of ours in regard to those
reserved services which, up to the present,
have not been transferred under the Act.
On the whole we thought it much better
that if there was to be a conference, and
the conference was called without our
knowledge or without our having been in
any way consulted, we thought we could
best look after the interests of our
people by being present at that
conference to see that nothing was given
away behind our backs.
Thirdly, when asked for an answer we
gave our answer, and I fear had we given
a negative reply that the representatives
of Sinn Fein in the South of Ireland
might have taken that negative reply as
their model, and said if the North will not
go in we will not go in, and the whole pressure of public opinion would have been
brought to bear upon us, with the result
that in the end we might have been forced
to go into the conference, and tha
would have been in my opinion an initial
error, by having to give way after we had
deliberately made up our minds. It was
a grave risk to take to say no" on that
occasion for that reason alone. Another
point, Mr. SPEAKER, is that in all the.
dealings regarding Ulster, whatever

secrecy there may be regarding other parts
of Ireland, the leader of the Ulster people
in the past-and I intend in that direction
to follow his footsteps-has always been
open and above board, and every action
that is taken shall be subject to the approval of my colleagues in this House. It
is perfectly true that we have nothing to
give away in that conference. It is
equally true that not one of my colleagues
has the slightest desire to give anything
away; and my last argument, and perhaps it supersedes all others, is that we
have a good cause, we have nothing to be
ashamed of, and we are prepared to argue
it in any council or across any table.
Now, Mr. SPEAKER, that invitation was
followed by a truce. We had nothing
whatever to do with that truce, nor had
we anything to do with the terms of the
truce. The idea of entering into the
truce and the fixing of the terms were a
matter entirely for the Imperial Government. Directly or indirectly I did
nothing and knew nothing whatever
about the matter.
Subsequent to the truce, as is now well
known by the public, a letter was
addressed by Mr. Lloyd George to Mr. De
Valera, the terms of which are fresh in
your memory, but I do not think it will
be asking too much of the patience of the
House if I were to read the reply that I
sent on behalf of your Cabinet on that
occasion, because I intend to refer to it
in slight detail. I wrote as follows on
the 29th July:-
My Dear Prime Minister,
Your proposals for an Irish settlement
have now been exhaustively examined
by my Cabinet and myself.
We realise that the preamble is
specially addressed to Mr. de Valera and
his followers, and observe that it implies that difficulties have long existed
throughout the Empire and America
attributable to persons of Irish extraction.
In fairness to the Ulster people, I
must point out that they have always
aimed at the retention of their citizenship in the United Kingdom and the
Empire, of which they are proud to
form a part, and that there are not to
be found in any quarter of the world
more loyal citizens than those of Ulster
descent. They hold fast to cherished
traditions, and deeply resent any infringement of their rights and privileges, which belong equally to them and
to the other citizens within the Empire.
In order that you may correctly
understand the attitude we propose to
adopt, it is necessary that I should call
to your mind the sacrifices we have so
recently made in agreeing to self-
Government and consenting to the
Debate on Adjournment. 20 SEPTEMBER 1921. Debate on Adjournment. 50
establishment of a Parliament for
Northern Ireland. Much against our
wish, but in the interests of peace, we
accepted this as a final settlement of the
long outstanding difficulty with which
Great Britain had been confronted.
We are now busily engaged in ratifying our part of this solemn bargain,
while Irishmen outside the Northern
area, who in the past have struggled
for Home Rule, have chosen to repudiate
the Government of Ireland Act and to
press Great Britain for wider powers.
To join in such pressure is repugnant
to the people of Northern Ireland.
In the further interest of peace we
therefore respectfully decline to determine, or interfere with, the terms
of any
settlement between Great
Britain and Southern Ireland. It cannot then be said that "Ulster blocks
the way." Similarly if there exists an
equal desire for peace on the part of
Sinn Fein, they will respect the status
quo in Ulster, and will refrain from
any interference with our Parliament
and rights, which under no circumstances can we admit.
In adopting this course we rely on the
British people, who charged us with the
responsibility of undertaking our own
Parliamentary institutions, to safeguard
the ties that bind us to Great
Britain and the Empire, to ensure
that we are not prejudiced by any
terms entered into between them
and Mr. de Valera, and to maintain the
just equality exhibited throughout the
Government of Ireland Act.
in
Our acceptance of your original invitation to meet in conference
London still holds good, and if at any
time our assistance is again desired we
are available, but I feel bound to
acquaint you that no meeting is possible
between Mr. de Valera and myself until
he recognises that Northern Ireland
will not submit to any authority other
than His Majesty the King and the
Parliament of the United Kingdom,
and admits the sanctity of the existing
powers and privileges of the Parliament
and Government of Northern Ireland.
Let me asure you that peace is as
earnestly desired by my Government
and myself as by you, and that we are
prepared, when you and the British
Government arrive at a satisfactory
settlement, to co-operate with Southern
Ireland on equal terms (as do American
States) for the future welfare of our
common country.
That concludes the letter with the
exception about sending it to the Press.
Now, that is our letter, and on that
we still stand. There is no way in which
we can modify the letter, and there is
certainly no desire on our part to in any
way enlarge the terms we have laid down.
The long pause that has taken place without outward action by either myself or
any members of my Government is
because once having clearly fixed in
our minds this determination to stand
aside while the other two were endeavouring to come to some settlement-once
having come to that decision, we have most
loyally adhered to the principles that it
lays down. Consequently it has required
very great patience on our part, and forbearance by all Members of this House and
the public outside-forbearance towards us
for which I must now here at the earliest
moment convey my very best thanks. It
is sometimes much easier to be in the
fighting line than to be lurking far behind,
but I feel we would not have been
interpreting properly the soul of the
Ulster people if, having declined to mix
ourselves in any way with the negotiations
going on, we had come out in the open
and laid down terms or ventured to
dictate in any way to the Imperial
Government or its Prime Minister how
they should conduct their affairs.
As I stand here to-day I believe that our
action has been fully justified, and that
the feeling across the Channel in favour
of the Ulster people is better than at any
time in the political history of the country.
Our duties are not solely concerned with
watching over local affairs, but they entail
upon us the graver responsibility to watch
most cautiously the
the trend of public
opinion in Great Britain and especially
amongst the electorate. All the signs
point to the fact that Sinn Fein is now
better understood by people even than it
was before, and the Imperial attitude of
Ulster is winning more and more favour.
Getting back to the first conference at
Buckingham Palace our revered leader,
Lord Carson, and myself have had to bear
in negotiations in various conferences not
only Ulster's burden so far as our rights
and privileges were concerned, but also
the old burden of presenting the Imperial case. Although at the conference
there was an interchange of views, it
51 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 52
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister]
broke down on what one might term the
highest Imperial interests, yet the invariable statement throughout the world
was that Ulster blocked the way. Now
by Ulster standing aside the British
Cabinet and the British Prime Minister
offering to come face to face with these
Republicans and rebels, can
bear
upon their own shoulders the Imperial
responsibility, and they can look to us for
guidance as to affairs affecting Ulster.
And in that position we now have it today. I repeat, and it cannot be too
often repeated, that Ulster by her sacrifice
in accepting this Act has gone the very
farthest she can in meeting the difficulties
of Great Britain. And from the attitude
indicated in that letter neither one of my
Ministers nor myself will budge a single
inch. Well, Sir, from then until the
present day is common property, and you
have watched, and I have watched, the
interchange of first letters and now telegrams between the Prime Minister and
the Sinn Fein leader. How it will work
out no one can say. With such conflicting
interests, it is impossible to forecast for
one day the progress of negotiations. But,
meanwhile, we have been pressing for the
completion of the Act.
We have been pressing that no delay
should now exist for bringing the last
Orders in Council into force, in order that
we here may grip the reins of Government as intended by the Act, and get on
with the business we are prepared to do.
Do not run away with the idea that
we have not been energetic in pressing
the British Government to put the full
powers in our hands. Far from it;
I do not think anything more
could be done than we have done in that
direction. It may be that it was thought
by the Imperial authorities that to take
action in that direction would have prejudiced the chance of an agreement; but
I again say that that is a matter for them
to decide upon, and a matter about which
it would have been impossible for us to
come out into the open without having
been charged again as the chief culprits
for breaking down the neogtiations, and
wanting war instead of peace. But I
may say that there is no reason now left,
in my opinion, why Section 72 and
Section 69 of the Act should not be put
into immediate operation. I see no reason
at all, and I do not think that
we
can
be any more blamed for
The
having prejudiced the negotiations or for
having been lacking in our desire to see
them come to a fruitful termination.
Therefore, I say that from the date
of the meeting of this House, from the
reassembling of this Parliament, we shall
bring all the pressure at our command to
bear to have the full power placed in our
hands. Four vital functions prescribed
under the Act are held up until either
the South functions or Crown Colony
Government is put into operation.
first of these I would treat entirely by
itself. It is the most important, and it
was referred to at question time to-day. I
refer to the police. The most important
power that is withheld from us at the
moment is the control of the police force.
Well, Sir, I do not desire to lay too much
There are
emphasis upon that point.
considerable difficulties in the way, which
only the Cabinet know of; but I will go
so far as to say this-that we can only be
held responsible for the upkeep and the
charge of a constabulary force on a reasonable basis, and because of murder and
assassination in the South and the consequent establishment of a large force of
police and specials and so forth, the cost
should not be put upon the shoulders of
the people of Northern Ireland.
Any small-I see the Chancellor of the
Exchequer smiling-any large balance that
is left over at the end of the financial year
must be devoted to those ameliorative
measures that we have outlined from time
to time in our public utterances, and it
would be an intolerable situation if in quelling a rebellion in the whole of Ireland
Northern Ireland was swamped by the cost
of the large force necessary to co-operate
with the military in putting down
that rebellion. Therefore I do not
wish to say too much to-day in regard to
taking over the police. It is quite unnecessary to remind the House that taking over
the police means taking over the cost of the
police and the cost of the Special Constabulary, A, B, and C, out of which we here hope
to make a model constabulary force.
the problem with which your Cabinet ist
confronted at the moment is that of securing
on the one hand the fullest possible control
of the police force a normal size police
force-and on the other hand to see most
carefully that our revenue is not frittered
away entirely by such an enormous unkeep.
With regard to three particular bodies
which should come into operation, I take
the Council of Ireland first because it is
But
Debate on Adjournment. 20 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment.
the least important. The Council of
Ireland can only function when
representatives that are returned from
the South function as we are doing here, or
else by the imposition of Crown Colony
Government. The Council of Ireland deals
only with minor matters, and really the
North here is very little affected whether
the Council ever comes into operation
or not. That is the truth of the matter.
With regard to No. 2-the Civil Service
Committee-it can only be set up when the
South functions or Crown Colony Government of some form is instituted, and until
that takes place the appointed day cannot be
fixed for the transfer of the various departments that are at present running in Dublin,
a share of whose civil servants must be taken
over by the Northern Parliament. Therefore we are hampered-I admit it-at every
turn. The third-a very important function-which is still withheld, is the Joint
Exchequer Board, regarding which the
Minister of Finance will have a word to say
on Thursday when we reach the Vote on
Account. I am glad to be able
tɔ announce to the House that
Lord Colwyn at the request of
the Government, has consented to act
as Chairman of the Joint Exchequer Board.
I think possibly some of my right hon. and
hon. Friends will have knowledge of Lord
Colwyn's magnificent work during the war
and the time of reconstruction. I believe,
also, he was chairman of one of the
Boards closely associated with our linen
industry, and the manufacturing of cloth
for the wings of our flying services. I know
him personally, and I believe for a distinguished position of this kind Ireland--I
won't say Ulster-could not have secured
the services of a more able or better man.
Having shown you how we have been
hampered and the difficulties with which
we had to contend, and the responsibilities
which rested on our shoulders may I show
a brighter side of the picture, and one
which, after all, is full of encouragement?
In this connection each step, as I enumerated, is an earnest of the intention and
desire of the Imperial Government to
proceed steadily ahead with the Act. It
is a good voucher of their ultimate intention
and good faith, and having passed this Act,
and having the presence of His Majesty
to
open our Parliament, and his two
Speeches from the Throne, one delivered by
himself, and the other on his behalf by His
Excellency the Lord Lieutenant, all show
me that the steps that have been taken are
an earnest of the ultimate intention that
we shall be in reality given the full powers
conveyed to us under the Act. I have no
doubt whatever on that subject-none
whatever. Patience may have to be
exercised for some little time longer.
There may be what we consider undue procrastination on the other side, but the
solemn pledges given on the floor of the
Imperial House of Commons, and the solemn
pledge given by the greatest party of the
State the Unionist party of Great Britain,
and the feelings which I know exist in
the minds of our best friends in England—
all those are guarantees that we shall not
be disappointed. In May last Parliament
was summoned in His Majesty's name, and
we all know the steps that were taken, and
the extraordinary example of loyalty that
pervaded the country at the election time.
It is fresh in our memories, and by the
exercise of the, people's votes we are here
to-day. We are never likely to forget it.
In June Members of both Houses took the
oath and elected their Speakers. Ministers
were appointed by the Lord Lieutenant to
form a Government, and the Parliament
was formally opened for business by His
Majesty. His Majesty's Speech enjoined
upon Parliament the important task of
perfecting the administration of those
services which have been transferred to
our control, and also regarding the setting
up of Departments of Government for the
future consideration of estimates. Parliament duly elected its representatives to the
Council of Ireland with a view to harmonious
action and uniformity in certain matters
between North and South. On the 24th
June, Parliament adjourned until 20th
September in order to provide an interval
of time, during which plans for adminis
tration and finance might be fully developed.
Since the adjournment our Lord Chief
Justice has been appointed, temporary
accommodation in this building has been
secured for both Houses of Parliament, and
Ministerial offices have been obtained in the
city, convenient for the transfer, when it
takes place, of the civil service
and now we have secured a permanent
site for our Parliament buildings
and ministerial offices. Outstanding, of
course, are the Courts of Justice, and I can
assure the House and those outside the
House who have written to me and who are
waiting upon me by deputation, that there
is going to be a proper site selected for the
Courts of Justice, convenient not only for
the judges and lawyers who have to make
use of them, but also for the public who
55 Debate on Adjournment.

HOUSE OF COMMONS
Debate on Adjournment.
[Prime Minister.]
attend. Several sites are in view, any one
of which would give the greatest satisfaction to the public as a whole, and, I understand, to the legal profession. Each one
may rest assured that the Lord Chief
Justice, assisted by those who are chiefly
interested in the matter, is keeping in close
touch with me and with the Office of Works
in London, in order that the very best site
may be obtained for that purpose, and none
other will be selected, and I believe that
great satisfaction will be given in the end.
Now, Sir, during this interregnum I would
confess I personally have passed through
a time of very great anxiety. My colleagues
and I have been very busily occupied, if
not here, across the water, protecting the
interests of our people, and we will have
to pass through considerably more anxiety,
I feel satisfied of that, but we are certainly
not lacking in the desire or in the courage to
face anything that presents itself. Not the
least of our duties has been the picking up
of the reins of Government-not an easy
matter. It is not like making an appointment in the Civil Service through an
examination, where years of study have
brought a man to the position. I understand that in Dublin there are some twentythree purely Irish Departments staffed with
heads at each, and there are ten other
Departments perhaps more directly attributable to the Imperial Service; but at
all events Dublin is the centre of thirtythree Departments, and the business that
lay to the hands of my colleagues and myself
to narrow down from twenty-three Irish
Departments alone to the five ministries
which are in control here now was not a
matter of great simplicity.
Co-ordination orders and adaptation
orders have to be prepared, and sorting
and shuffling in order to bring these 23
departments down to the five ministries
is something which requires time and care
and deliberation. My colleagues have
spent their time most usefully in picking
up the knowledge that is necessary to
them in the future, and I would not,
therefore, call the past three months by
any means wasted when we consider the
complexity of the problems with which
they are confronted. I am sure you
will all agree with me when I say that
the transfer suddenly of a man, even one
of great business acumen the transfer
from the ordinary paths of life in which
he has been living to those of a minister
with all the responsibility and charge of
a great Government department suddenly
thrust upon him is a matter which even
he must have felt was a considerable
difference from his ordinary life. They
will be all the more ready to take up the
strings. We are ready at this moment.
Our accommodation for the civil service
is almost ready. They at all events are
prepared to take up the reins of office.
When one pictures the finances of our
Northern Government in the future in
all its ramifications and all its difficulties,
when one pictures the co-ordination of
every detail that affects the Home Office,
the Labour Ministry, the Ministry of
Education, and the Ministry of Agriculture
-when one pictures those things one may
say have we compressed enough, have we
done enough in saying that five ministries
are sufficient to control these? Well, Mr.
SPEAKER, on that subject I am perfectly
confident that it will be unsafe to place
on the shoulders of any minister more of
the burden of Government than is
intended under our system.
I know that
there is criticism about the Parliamentary
secretaries. I believe some people have
the idea that these positions are created
for the purpose of creating them.
Mr. LYNN: Hear, hear.
The PRIME MINISTER: My hon.
Friend agrees with that. Well he and
I have sat in the House of Commons for
many years, side by side, and it is not
often, if ever before, I have had to differ
with him even in a matter of comparative
unimportance.
But I must join issue with those who say
that there is any intention on our part of
launching this new Parliament in a sea of
extravagance and of squander mania. I
emphatically protest. My Ministers and
I have exercised every possible care with
regard to every appointment that has
been made, and I would like to point out
that in this matter I have very carefully
searched the precedents in other parts
of our Empire. I got hold of a Canadian
almanac which has been issued for 1920,
and I turned up the Province of Ontario,
and I find there that the Department of
Public Works has a Minister and a
Deputy Minister; that the Department of
Labour has a Minister and Deputy
Minister; that the Department of Public
Highways has a Minister and Deputy
Minister; that the Department of Lands,
Forests, and Mines has a Minister and
Deputy Minister.
Mr. COOTE: What are the salaries?
The PRIME MINISTER: My hon.
Friend opposite, who also sat in the Im-
57 Debate on Adjournment.

20 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment.
to
That
perial House of Commons, will excuse me
if I say I am not dealing with salaries.
think my hon. Friend is unable to restrain
himself. He has a point to make. If I
may say so he will have the whole of tomorrow to make his point. The point I
am coming to is this. This House has
already appointed Committee
a
go into the question of salaries.
does not arise now. Certainly we as
Ministers are not going to fix salaries.
They will be fixed by the House and an
Independent Committee chosen by the
House. Allow me to finish the quotation
from Ontario. The Department of
Education has a Minister and a Deputy
Minister; the Department of Agriculture
has a Minister and a Deputy Minister.
I turn next to Quebec. The Department
of Public Works has a Minister and a
Deputy Minister; the Department of the
Attorney General has the Attorney
General and Deputy Attorney General,
Treasurer and Assistant Treasurer.
Department of Agriculture
The
has a
Minister and Deputy Minister; the Department of Lands and Forests, a Minister
and Deputy Minister; the Department of
Colonisation, Mines, and Fisheries, a
Minister and Deputy Minister. I would
quote from the Province of New Brunswick. The Attorney General has a
Deputy, the Department of Lands
and Mines a Minister and Deputy
Minister, the Department of Agriculture a Minister and Deputy
Minister, and so on. Mr. SPEAKER, we
are building up a new State here. Very
few persons in this wide community have
any experience of Government whatever.
It would be a very grave risk for me or,
indeed, for any Prime Minister to say that
the whole Government of Northern
Ireland shall be in the hands of 3 or 4 or
5 of a committee. It would be a grave
danger, because when the Departments
are all transferred and with the busy
activities of Departmental life it is patent
to everybody to understand that a breakdown in health or even the ordinary
holidays would leave a Department in the
hands of permanent civil servants.
think all sections of the public in the
North here acquiesce at all events in that
one point, that the curse of Ireland has
been the want of
of responsibility of
Ministers to persons in Parliament in
connection with the various Departments.
In other words, it has been found impossible, and in this case I cast no aspersion
on the civil servants of this country.
I
They have a very hard time. But it is in
the condemnation of the system in general
I join the general system whereby a
Chief Secretary in London, unable to
control in any direct sense the twentythree various departments to which I have
referred has of necessity to leave all that
to a permanent civil servant, and when
even Members of Parliament and the
public as a whole approach any of these
departments they know that they are
being dealt with not by the Minister responsible to the House and to the
country but by a Departmental Head. I
want my right hon. and hon. Friends
really to enter into the spirit of this and
become the responsible spokesmen for
their departments in this House and in
the country. And we must have understudies in the Departments in order that
if there is any cause to take the Member
away either across the water for his
health or on duty, there shall always be
a Parliamentary Secretary or an Under-
Secretary prepared to take his place to
answer to this House and to the public
for anything that has taken place in the
Department. That is one great argument
for having your under-secretaries trained
up in the Government of the country so
that at no time can we ever find ourselves
at a loss for some one who understands
the Department thoroughly and personally
without having to go back once more to
Government by the civil servants
alone. But then there is another
point, and a point I would like to
touch upon, and it is important. What
would be the ambitions of the young men
of the future? Inside our Northern Province I hope that the attractions in
Parliamentary life will tempt some who
otherwise might neglect their duty
to their country. I hope that
the attraction of Parliamentary life
will be sufficient to secure for us the
younger generation to take their share in
the responsibility of Government.
Government will eventually be in their
hands. They will have to discharge the
duties of Parliamentary secretaries and
under-secretaries. Take education for
instance. What a splendid vista opens
up. The Minister for Education in the
Northern Parliament would have to bring
the education up to what it is in Scotland.
No man may attain to this position unless
he is well qualified. I hope thereby
a channel or door will be open to all the
young men in Ulster to come along and
The
59 Debate on Adjournment.'
Debate on Adjournment. 60
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Prime Minister.]
to be Ministers of the Crown, to stand here
where I am in this House.
some
In my opinion that is another very
weighty reason why we should have some
one to understudy each of our Ministers.
Now another point is that this is a new
State, it is an experiment we are starting,
and we have to be very sure in beginning
this work that we are properly manned in
order that there shall not be a breakdown
in one of the Departments or in another.
And I am not of opinion at all that the
compensation to a man should simply be
the number of hours that he works. The
compensation to a man also ought to take
into consideration the responsibility that
rests upon his shoulders; and I have had
Underexperience of being an
Secretary in London in the Imperial
Parliament, and I can only say that unless
the under-secretaries took a great part
in the work of their chiefs, their chiefs
could not carry the enormous burdens
they have to bear. Do not for a moment
think that I am comparing the Imperial
Parliament with the work which will rest
upon my colleagues and myself, but the
same argument does apply to a certain
extent, that Ministers must have leisure to
think, to visualise the future, to make
provision for what is coming ahead; and
it is up to the Parliamentary Secretaries
to do as much as possible of the routine
work in order to save them that part of
the burden. However, I only throw those
suggestions out because I feel very
strongly upon them. We have to make
ourselves sure in every point. I want
the advice of a number of people, I do not
want the advice of one or two; I want the
advice of a number of people on the
problems with which I will have to deal
later on. And I say this-that I
would not change one of my Ministers for
any of the Ministers of the Imperial House
of Commons. It sometimes happens, I
think, that in England Ministers are
selected for their great political weight in
the country, possibly for great family
reasons, connection possibly, for this, for
that or for the other. Sir, each of my
Ministers is an expert in his own
particular domain, and I believe carries
the fullest confidence and respect throughout the length and breadth of the Six
Counties; and exactly the same remarks
apply to those who are working with them.
And, when later on, we look back upon
this very engaging and interesting chapter
Ulster history, the establishment of
the first Parliament, and the first Government, I, at all events, will be able to stand
over every appointment I have made, and
thank God every day I had such talent to
choose from. Well now, Sir, that brings
us down again to the present day through
two channels, one the disadvantages and
the other the advantages of the delay that
has taken place. I will go a little further
with regard to the immediate future by
saying that I have the utmost confidence
in the Cabinet Committee appointed by
Mr. Lloyd George to deal with anything
that may eventuate from the present
He has nominated Mr.
negotiations.
Austen Chamberlain, Lord Curzon, Sir
Laming Worthington
Mr.
Evans,
Churchill, Mr. Shortt, Mr. Munro,
Lord Birkenhead, Sir Hamar Greenwood,
and Sir Robert Horne. Those men have
been personal colleagues of mine in the
Imperial Government for some time past.
I pin my faith in them. I pin my
faith in their sincerity to do the right
thing by the Ulster people, and, therefore,
one more bright light at the moment is
that such stalwarts have been chosen to
deal with whatever happens in the immediate future.
I cannot for a moment join anyone in
condemning a single one of my old
colleagues. I believe in my heart that
each and all of them are only desirous
of seeing the Act in full working order and
put into proper operation. May I put
another point. If there is any feeling of
distrust in the country let me recall to the
memories of those who fear disaster taking
place that since the year 1906, more
particularly since the year 1911, we have
time and again been up against periods
of grave anxiety and nerve-shattering
tension. The same pessimism, distrust,
nervousness, have been evinced, I think,
from my knowledge of Ulster, in
decreasing strength from time to time.
We have always surmounted our difficulties; in fact I believe the bigger the
difficulties the better we have been able
to surmount them We may go astray
in matters of detail, but let the Ulster
people come up against a real difficulty,
a real trouble, something that calls for
the best in them, then I have
hesitation in saying they will win through,
win the same laurels of victory as on
former occasions. If peace comes out of
the negotiations what a relief there will
be throughout the length and breadth of
Ireland; a relief for all right thinking men
if peace could be secured in the South and
no
61 Debate on Adjournment.

20 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment.
West, and if people could go untrammeled
about their daily business. We here in
the terms of the letter which I have read
to the House are prepared to work in
friendly rivalry with our fellow countrymen of the South and West, to vie with
them in the government of the people, to
vie with them in the markets of the
world and the industries which have made
our city so great; we are prepared to work
for the betterment of the people of
Ireland, not to quarrel, not to continue
political strife, and it may be that a
time will come when some Irishman on
Irish soil may be able to repeat the
speech-a very notable speech-made by
General Smuts in Capetown a few days
ago. Referring to the union between
Rhodesia and the Union of South Africa,
General Smuts and General Smuts has
taken a certain part in attempting to
bring the negotiations between the South
and West and the British Government to
a successful termination, and is also entitled to speak as one who served in a
Republic himself, and who at one time was.
at war with the British Empire-said
"The Union Government would welcome
the early admission of Rhodesia into the
Union. At the same time they recognise
that in a matter of that kind the initiative
necessarily lay with the people of Rhodesia. It was for them to say whether
and when they were prepared to become a
part of that larger South Africa which
was their destiny."
a.
That was followed later by
statement in which he said "the people
(the people of Rhodesia) must make up
their minds freely and willingly in the
matter." That strikes me as being the
situation as we find it here to-day. The
British Government on the one hand
attempting to come to terms with Sinn
Fein on the other. We are trusting that
peace may come. We are standing aside
that we may thereby not make the
issue more intricate or troublesome.
We are prepared to do our partevery line and every letter-not alone
in the letter but in the spirit
of the Act with which we have been entrusted. We stand to-day as we have ever
stood, without having changed in ideals
or changed our policy; and so the Cabinet
and myself have day by day done our duty
to the very best of our ability in putting
the Act into operation, and safeguarding
your interests, and, above all, looking
after the welfare of the people of Northern
Ireland.
Mr. R. J. LYNN: I only want to intervene in the debate for a few moments,
not to deal with the broad questions which
the Prime Minister has raised and dealt
with so ably. I wish to say that the
question of the size of the Ministry is the
only one on which he and I agree to differ.
It will not make us any worse friends.
We will both hold our views, and are both
determined to put them as strongly as we
can before the House. I think there is
one fallacy running through that part of
the Prime Minister's otherwise excellent
speech. He seems to deal with Northern
Ireland as if it were a very large Kingdom. I agree at once with him regarding
what he said about his Ministry. I have
very high regard for every man of them,
and I know they are men of ability and
loyalty, and men who will do their work
splendidly. I go further and say we
have a Ministry which I think could run
the United
United Kingdom rather than a
province only a little bit bigger in
population than the City of Glasgow. The
Prime Minister pointed out that one
reason why he must have a large Ministry
was that in the past the affairs of Ireland
were badly managed because the Chief
Secretary was not able to look after everything. I think the cause of the failure
to manage Ireland in the past did not lie
with the Chief Secretary not being able to
look after his Department, but because he
did not want to look after his Department.
We have had chief secretaries who have
been able to govern the whole of Ireland,
and govern it well. I will only mention
two of them whose names will appeal to
the Prime Minister. I refer to Mr.
Arthur Balfour and Mr. Walter Long.
Both these men were able to manage
Ireland, and managed it well. They
maintained law and order, and looked
after all the affairs of the country with
one, or at the outside two, undersecretaries. Scotland, I thing it will be
admitted, is very well managed, and
managed by one Secretary of State and one
or two under-secretaries. I submit to
this House, and I go further-
The PRIME MINISTER: I am sure
my hon. Friend would not expect me to
Scotland.
accept that with regard to
Scotland is part of the United Kingdom,
and is run by the Departments in London
in exactly the same way as Yorkshire,
Norfolk, or any other part, with the
exception of those charges, put under the
Secretary of State for Scotland.
63 Debate on Adjournment. HOUSE OF COMMONS
Debate on Adjournment. 64
Mr. LYNN: I am quite well aware
of that, but under the present Act
a great many of the important
services for Northern Ireland will be
run by the Imperial Government. I
was going to say when the Prime
Minister interrupted me, that knowing
his energy and ability, and knowing his
thoroughness in everything he does. I, as
a Member of the House, am prepared to
leave the whole government of Northern
Ireland in his hands, and I believe he
would do it and do it well. After all we
are a small community. Don't let us
make the mistake of thinking we are
bigger numerically-or especially from the
taxpayers' point of view-don't let us
imagine we are bigger than we really are.
I have only one desire in the matter, and
that is to see Ulster run efficiently and
at the same time run economically.
and
state-
Mr. COOTE: I wish to congratulate
the Prime Minister on the
ment he has given to
to the House,
which to some
extent will
have a reassuring effect. On the general
question-the criticism of the policy of
the Cabinet up to the present-I wish
to reserve my remarks until to-morrow,
but in the Prime Minister's speech there
are some things on which I would like
more light and there are others to which
I would take exception. In the first place
the Prime Minister tells us, when he
sketched out the history of the negotiations up to the truce, that "We have
nothing whatever to do with the truce
or the fixing of the terms of the truce."
We do not blame the Prime Minister for
not having anything to do with the fixing
of the truce, but we blame the Prime
Minister and Cabinet for letting the truce
affect Ulster. Ulster had nothing to do
with the British House of Commons in
this matter, between Mr. De Valera, who
is a rebel, and themselves. A truce is
between belligerents at war, but we are not
at war with His Majesty's Government.
We are out to assist His Majesty's
Government in upholding law and order,
and we have no right to suffer from the
effects of this lamentable truce. I hold
it has been-I was going to say criminal,
but I won't use so strong a word-but it
has been remiss on the part of the Cabinet
of Northern Ireland, that they allowed
such instructions to be given to the police
and military to cease taking active steps
to protect the lives and property of the
loyal people of the province irrespective
of class or creed. We had a system of
police arrangements in this Province.
which worked well. I could give you
testimony from many Roman Catholic
people in the province who were satisfied
and thankful that we had such a force as
rendered life and property safe. I can
give the Prime Minister reports. I can
give him authority from many counties,
if not from the whole six, regarding this.
We are a law-abiding people, a people of
good will, who breathed a sigh of relief
when the police arrangements were instituted, and were sorry when they were
taken away. Surely the Prime Minister
is aware of what has been done. Does
he mean to say if he had objected to the
truce in the form in which it was placed
upon us in Ulster that it would have been
enforced? Does he mean to say if he had
gone to the Prime Minister of Great
Britain and said-" You may have a truce
with de Valera, and we are in sympathy
with it as far as is consistent with the
liberty of the people, but if you call off
the soldiers and the police, and neutralise
the A force and disband the B force, and
our people suffer, then we must object,
and strongly object, and you must take
the responsibility." I ask the Prime
Minister has he done this?
Has any
message gone from the Cabinet that there
must be no
law
relinquishing of
and order in Ulster! Ulster is
demanding an answer to this. And yet
take this city of Belfast, which was the
subject of a question in the earlier part.
of the day. About 14 people-or did
my hon. Friend (the Member for North
Belfast) say 30 people were killed?
Mr. McGUFFIN: 18, and over 100
injure 1.
Mr. COOTE: Does
any reasonable
Minister in this House say that if the
same control had been kept on in connection with this Province that any of
those people, humanly speaking, would
have been killed or these 100 people
injured? We had peace until this truce
began, and until the gunmen came down
in pairs and dozens-they are in the city
to-day. We know they are there. There
camp at Ballykinlar about 400
strong, with the Sinn Fein flag flying
there. Is the Prime Minister aware of
this? Has he made any protest to the
Dublin authorities? But I will go

adverse advice given to the police, but
their efforts were neutralised. An officer
came down from Dublin Castle when
is a
65 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 66
20 SEPTEMBER 1921
things got so warm, and the Lord Mayor
had thrown the onus upon the head of
the Government. He had gone to the
police authorities in the middle of this
riot when things had become very grave,
and they said they were powerless to act,
and that they cannot use their revolvers.
Dublin Castle had said they must not use
them. Yet we had a Cabinet here in
Belfast advising for the control of the
North-a Cabinet that has ultimately to
pay for these police. Surely they must
have had some influence with Dublin
Castle, if they had only exercised that
influence. But when the Lord Mayor
made his very strong protest, and was
referred to the military, the General in
command said he had no instructions to
put his military on the streets until the
police authorities had exhausted their
power. This gentleman came down from
Dublin Castle, a Mr. Cope. He went to
the police authorities and said, "You
must arrest any man you find breaking
the law." He went round the city consulting other people, not the Government
of Northern Ireland, and he came back
to the police authorities and said to them,
"If you arrest a Sinn Fein criminal you
must arrest a Unionist alongside him."
He was asked to give it in writing,
and he gave it in writing. And yet we
have a Cabinet looking on and saying
they have confidence in the British
Parliament and the Imperial Cabinet in
Great Britain, and that we are perfectly
right and safe. At the present moment
there is no man or woman safe in Northern
Ireland. We have evidence of
travelling motor loads of ammunition and
guns through Fermanagh and Tyrone
since the truce began. Drilling is going
on in the country It is evident at
Cookstown, where our people in the police
force, when they heard of what was going
on, had to stand to their rifles and go out
and arrest men in the act of drilling or
coming from drilling. This thing is going
on in our midst, and I tell you there is a
spirit of fear and unrest in this Northern
Province that has not been in it since
Sinn Fein organisation commenced.
charge the Government with weakness.
I charge the Goverment with over-confidence in the British Government.
believe we are being made the catspaw of
the British Government in order that the
British Government may exhaust all
their influence with Sinn Fein, no matter
what the consequences to the Ulster
people. While that is going on Sinn Fein
I'
I
on?
comis arming to walk through Ulster, and
create such an upheaval as to render law
and order for a long time impossible. I
want to ask the Prime Minister in l
seriousness-I believe the people will ask
it, and the people are eager to hear your
statement-how long is this thing to go
We wish to trust you, and we have
trusted you. The people of this province
have put such trust in you as never was
put in any man, even including our great
leader, Sir Edward Carson-by a
munity of people in which I think there
were not ten loyalist votes in Ulster not
What have
recorded in your support.
you got to say in reply to this? How long
is this thing to go on? When will it be
possible for our people to lie in their beds
none daring to make them afraid, when
there will be no fear of their houses being
burned before morning, and that they
will not be attacked? The people demand
an answer, and I tell you it is no good
your adjourning Parliament. It will be
the last straw if you adjourn Parliament
without some definite assurance to the
Ulster people that you will put all the
machinery of the Government into motion,
even if needs be that you will come here
to this House and say to the British
Government, after you have exhausted all
diplomacy, that you will take the powers
given to you by His Majesty the King
and the British House of Commons, that
you will take the powers to drive back
the avalanche of Sinn Fein, and allow the
people of the Northern Province to
attend to the ordinary business which
they are anxious to attend to. As to the
direction of the police, I join issue with
the Prime Minister. I do not accept his
dictum that we have nothing to do with
the police for the next three years.
have got to pay for the police force.
We
He
The PRIME MINISTER: Oh, o, I
did not say for the next three years.
will recollect the first reading of the
clause in the Act was that we were not to
get the police for over three years; that
is to say we could not get them over until

foresaw this very difficulty in which we

we have to take over the police within

There is nothing to prevent us taking over the police in a fortnight or three weeks if we could make
the arrangements.
Mr. COOTE: That only strengthens
my argument. Has nothing been done
67 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 68
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote].
by the Cabinet to get control of the
police ? He tells us he has promised
that every action will be taken, subject
to the approval of my colleagues in this
House. I think that is due to us.
Has he consulted his colleagues about
taking over the control of the police?
Has he
made any move because the
prosperity and peace of Ulster are largely
controlled by the police. We do not want
bloodshed. We do not want to bring
about bloodshed. We do not want even
to go on a defensive that will lead to
bloodshed. We are quite ready for the
defensive, but we are not willing to strike
a blow. God knows we do not want to
hurt any man, any brother, be he Roman
Catholic, Protestant, Sinn Feiner, whoever he might be. We have enough to
do to fight the battles of life without trying to fight each other, but we do expect
some adequate system of protection will
be given the people of the country. In
all conscience for the last 30 years we
have been harrassed and made a shuttlecock of by all the English parties. We
want to stand on our own feet independent of the English parties. We want
simply to develop our own resources along
the line given us by the British House of
Commons. We want to assist His
Majesty's Government if His Majesty's
Government is loyal to His Majesty, if
they are loyal to the Constitution of the
Realm and to the unity of Great Britain.
In every pulse we cannot be otherwise
than loyal to the Empire as it stands, but
when the Crown deals with rebels,
humiliates our country, lowers our
standards, and lowers the moral dignity
of this nation, it is time for us to say that
we do not belong to that school, that we
are trained on a different basis with a
different morality, that we will have no
part in anything of the kind, that
ought to have powers to develop our own
destiny within the Empire, loyal to the
flag, and that no one should be permitted
to come in and disturb our peace or mar
it. There is just another matter I want
to comment on. It is the question of
the officials of this House. I was under
the impression that we had reached the
limit in a little country like this-you
could almost walk from one end to the
other in 24 hours. It is in the infant stage
of its activities, we do not know how we
stand financially, and we have great
reforms in face of us. The greatest of all,
we believe, should be some great system
we
of national or of provincial education, and
I think it is in every man's heart here that
there must be means supplied by which
the educational standard of this province
will be
raised second to none in the
Empire. Then we have the tremendous
problem of unemployment. There are
many things we can develop-I want to
deal with this to-morrow, and I will
refrain now from going into it-profitably in these days when employment is
at a discount. I thought the limit had
been reached when I heard and saw there
were to be Cabinet Ministers and a Parliaeach Cabinet
mentary Secretary to
Minister. Now I find-we are told by
the Prime Minister-for the first time
that there is another feature coming to
our Parliamentary life, Assistant Parliamentary Secretaries. Well, Mr. SPEAKER,
if the Prime Minister can create another
office-Deputy Assistant Parliamentary
Secretary he will have almost every
member in the House with him, so that
he is bound to win every time. I think
I can see I hope the Prime Minister will
excuse me if I say it a very clever move
behind all this. If you can get your
secretaries, your Parliamentry secretaries,
and your other secretaries when you come
to go into the Division Lobby against the
Government you will find you are with
the wrong side every time. They can
control the machine. They can batten on
the taxpayers of this country. You cannot interfere with them for their five
years of office. I tell you, Mr. PRIME
MINISTER, you will not get these things
without a struggle. We will not stand
such a thing, we will not stand the tieing
of our hands before the eyes of the men
who have sent us here.
-
We believe in this, we believe in a man
looking after his job, and executing that job
and being reasonably paid for that job, but
we do not believe in piling on
those merely nominal offices, endowing
them with salaries-I
anticipate the salaries.
will not
We have
no right to create these offices and
impose this burden upon a small people,
exceedingly frugal, exceedingly industrious,
who will look carefully into all matters of
finance. I want to say more. There is
nothing in my judgment will be so detrimental to the success of this Parliament
as to send in a wedge between the people
and this Parliament; nothing will do it
more than if you ever exceed your limits
of finance. I know Mr. Chancellor will be
very careful on that score. The day we
69 Debate on Adjournment.

20 SEPTEMBER 1921.
in this House vote extra taxation will be a
sorry day for our popularity with the people
of Ulster, so that we must be very careful.
I merely throw out this general statement
on the matter of officials and their salaries.
Some hon. Members have been asking to-day
"Will this Committee deal with the whole
thing?" No. No. It will come up in the
committee stage, and you can move amendments. I am sure Mr. SPEAKER will give
you all the assistance and all the latitude
that is possible in order that you may bring
your views before this House. In common
with others here I enjoyed very much the
Speech of the Prime Minister. I could wish
he had gone further; I could wish that he
had given us a definite assurance in the
I do
matter before this Parliament rises.
plead with him for his own sake; I plead
with him for the sake of the Cabinet; I

of the peace of Ulster, the mental peace

(6
Question, That this House do now

Adjourned accordingly at a quarter
