bear with me if I try to explain the
present position of the Ministry. It was
hoped that we would be able to go on
under existing conditions, but it was
found by the law adviser to the
Chief Secretary that it would
necessary to set up for Northern Ireland
Commissioners for National Education,
Commissioners for Secondary Education,
and Commissioners for Endowed Schools.
These Commissioners will act along with
the Ministry, and will be entirely under
the Ministry. It is not to be assumed
that this would be a permanent feature of
the Ministry, because that would be dealt
with by whatever legislative action would
be taken on the part of this House.
Owing to the fact that the Civil Service
Committee has not been set up, we have
found ourselves in a serious difficulty
when it comes to the staff and personnel
of our departments. It is to be hoped
that something will be done as speedily as
possible in order that the division of the
105 Debate on Adjournment. 21 SEPTEMBER 1921. Debate on Adjournment. 106
personnel and the staff may take place,
and that we can get our due proportion
in the North. But pending that we have
got to make the best of the position as it
exists to-day, and I think I am right in
saying that in all probability by the early
part of next year the Ministry of Education will be able to function entirely
under the Northern Parliament. We
find that in order to bring about necessary
reforms, reforms which are vital to the
Northern Province, it is necessary to set
up a Commission of Inquiry. That
Commission of Inquiry is being set up by
the Ministry, and I would like with the
forbearance of the House to refer to the
personnel of the Commission. We hoped
to make it a small Commission, but we
found that there were so many parties,
so many unions of teachers, and so on,
that we were obliged to enlarge the scope
so as to make this Committee thoroughly
representative, and I believe when the
names of the Commission are made
public, they will command not only the
confidence of this House but also the
confidence of the Northern Province.
There are many matters which, of course,
it will be our desire to deal with. There
are questions pertaining to the housing.
and the want of housing or suitable housing for schools, and there is also the
question of amalgamation, which I am
sure will be considered by this Commission, but pending some report from the
Commission it is very difficult to take
steps.
This Committee, it is hoped, will go
into all these matters very quickly, and
deal with them as promptly as it is
possible, but I am sure the House will
agree with me that on such an important
matter as education it would be wrong on
the part of that Committee, or on the
part of the Ministry, to take hasty steps
which might turn out to be altogether
against the object which we wish to
attain. Regarding the salaries of
teachers, it is well known that the salaries
of the teachers of the primary schools are
giving satisfaction. and the teachers themselves will admit as much as that. There
is, however, one point in connection with
the primary teachers which will undoubtedly at some stage have to be considered; that is the question of pensions.
That is a matter which at some time or
other will undoubtedly receive the
attention in the first instance, probably, of
this Committee, and then of the Ministry
of Education. I come now, perhaps, to
the one point which seems to be uppermost
in the minds of hon Members in this
House, and which I consider a very difficult and very pressing one. It is that
of the position of secondary education in
Ireland. No one realises more than I do,
and no one realises more than the Minister
of Education, that secondary education
was handed over to the Northern Parliament in a condition which is absolutely
and undoubtedly deplorable. But the Imperial Parliament when they came to consider this question of secondary education.
in Ireland were up against various difficulties, with which we now feel ourselves
confronted. There is the question of
registered and unregistered teachers, and
there is the more difficult question of
private schools. A very big proportion
of the schools for secondary education in
Northern Ireland are privately owned, and
-I do not say it is a fact, but it is
possible it could be a fact that some of
those schools would be doing well, and as
a business concern making money, while
the teachers themselves would be poorly
paid. Now, that is a position which
requires investigation on the part of the
Ministry, and due steps are being taken.
by the Ministry in order to obtain-and
I am sure we will receive the assistance of
the teachers and principals of those schools
--whatever evidence we require in order
to take up this matter as speedily as we
possibly can. When the Burnham scale
was introduced in England it made the
position of secondary teachers here very
much worse in this sense-that they felt
so inadequately paid as compared with
their brothers on the other side. There is
no doubt that in order to maintain
secondary education at the level at which
it should be maintained in the Northern
Province, it will be necessary-and I say
emphatically it is the opinion of the
Ministry that it will be necessary-to
bring the salary of the teacher duly qualified, qualified to the same extent as
teachers of similiar positions in England
and Scotland, up to the salary of the
teacher in England and in Scotland.
We come, therefore, to a very serious
difficulty. There is the question of
finance, and I must say that since I joined
this Ministry and have taken an active
part in trying to solve the problem, J
find finance seems to be the root of all
the trouble. It has been extremely difficult, and it was felt that we here had a
claim against the Imperial Government
107 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 108
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. M'Keown.]
for a return of certain moneys which
might have gone to supplement the
teachers of secondary schools. However,
difficulties arose which I have pointed out,
and it seems that we must expect nothing
from that particular source. I think we
must realise as a House that it will be
to ourselves in this Northern Parliament
that we must look for any assistance which
we can give to the secondary teachers. I
felt, when my hon. Friends were speaking, that I could not, with the exception
of the case I have pointed out, refute
the justice of their cause, because I am
absolutely certain that the position of the
secondary teachers requires immediate
attention on the part of this House. It
has been pointed out that if we do not
do something very quickly indeed that we
will have no secondary teachers when we
have the Bill passed. I am satisfied that
many teachers have gone to England and
Scotland, where they are getting better
terms. I am satisfied more will go if we
do not improve their position here, and
I can only hope that it will be possible
through the assistance of the Minister of
Finance, and through our Northern
Government, to do something which will
speedily relieve what I believe is a very
pressing question indeed. I would just
like to say, in conclusion, that I have
found this question of education in
Northern Ireland a very complex one.
It will be extremely difficult, I am sure.
to devise any scheme which will appeal
to all teachers, but I believe that the
Ministry, with the assistance of the
committee which has been set up, will be
able to devise some scheme having only
the one object-the betterment of education that they will be able to devise a
scheme which will do credit to this
Northern Province. I can only assure the
House that it is the wish and the desire of
the Ministry of Education to see that
every child throughout the Northern
Province receives the same encouragement
and has the same facilities for education
as children have in other parts of the
United Kingdom. And it will
be our desire to forge and
fashion a measure of reform which I believe will bring that about, and, perhaps,
too, taking into account what has been
done in other schemes, we may be able to
produce a measure of reform which will
put this province somewhat ahead of any
other kingdom or province in the world.
Mr. DONALD: I will not occupy the
time of the House more than five minutes.
con-
I want to make one or two observations
on matters not touched upon by any of the
previous speakers. Personally, I
gratulate my hon. Friend, the Parliamentary Secretary for Education and
Commerce, on his very lucid and clear
statement on this all-important question
of education. But there was one matter
that he did not touch upon, and that is the
question of accommodation. I have heard
this question of education debated on for
the past three years. I heard a very
strong appeal from our great leader, Lord
Carson, on behalf of the twenty-four
thousand children who he said were without school accommodation in the city of
Belfast alone. What is the position today? No better, but very much worse.
Yet I have not heard one word to-day
about accommodation. Further, the
position of our Government to-day is a
deplorable one in view of the fact that
we are about to adjourn until 1922, and
the question of education must lapse in the
meantime. We have heard a great deal
about the limits of concession. The limits
of the patience of the people, not only in
the city of Belfast, but in the Six
Counties, have been worn out almost to
the last thread. What do we find in the
streets? Not a word has been said by the
Prime Minister about unemployment.
Ex-Service men say day after day that
they do not want the 7s. 6d. dole for
their wives and families; they want employment. This Government, as we stand,
is held up until 1922. It may be called
together, but it is held up. The Joint
Exchequer Board cannot be set up. I say
it can.
The Prime Minister told us
yesterday that the chairman had been
already appointed by His Majesty's
Government. The Board is composed of
five members-two appointed by the Imperial Treasury, one by the Southern
Parliament, when and if it should function, and one by the Northern Parliament,
which, I hope, will function in reality very
The Act says that three shall form
a quorum. Why not then form a
quorum. Should not the Prime Minister
take his courage in both hands and go to
the British Government and say to them
"Let us get to business." That is a proposition I put forward to the Prime
Minister, and I am sorry he is not here.
The Prime Minister made a statement in
reference to the control of the Royal Irish
Constabulary. Now, the Royal Irish
Constabulary have no truer friend than
myself. I have stood up for law and
order, and always will; but, Sir, the
soon.
109 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 110
21 SEPTEMBER 1921.
trouble of our Government only begins
when they take over the Royal Irish Constabulary.
I have had reports I have made
investigation, and what do I find?-that
in spite of all statements of cur Chief
Secretary and the Prime Minister in the
Imperial Parliament-I find indirectly, that
in the Royal Irish Constabulary to-day in
this province there is a disloyal section that
is indirectly assisting Sinn Fein. There
is a disloyal section of the Royal Irish
Constabulary in our city that is causing
trouble to-day. That is the report given
to me. I have made investigation, and I
find it is true. They have actually refused to arrest Sinn Feiners although they
saw them shoot. They were pointed out.
They have refused to go. That is why I
say the patience of our people has gone
up just a shade too high, and if your
Government propose to take over this
force certainly there will have to be some
investigation as to whether they will take
over the disloyal members of the force or
not who are not really doing their duty.
The SPEAKER: May I call the
attention of the hon. Member to the fact
that he is trespassing upon rather
dangerous ground when he refers to a
force and particular members of that force
when there is nobody in this House to
stand up and defend them from the
charges he makes. He understands that
the proper place to refer to any matter of
that kind is in the Imperial House.
Mr. DONALD: I quite understand
that and bow to your ruling, but I was
referring to the necessity of our Government
taking over in reality the reins of office
in order that the confidence of our people
may be established. Supposing I
raised this very question in the
Imperial House. I am referred to
our own Government. "You have a
Parliament of your own.
Do not raise it
here. We can do nothing, we have no
power." My hon. Friend the Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh is quite correct.
The Home Secretary has no power here
whatever. I want to refer to the matter
I raised in this House yesterday regarding
the question of the Curfew order in this
city of ours. For some reason or other
the authorities-I may be out of order but
I have to make these remarks in view of
the fact that for some reason or other the
authorities have extended Curfew to
three or four streets in the Dock Ward
area of East Belfast, practically a loyal
area. Curfew begins in that area at 8-30
p.m. to the disadvantage of business
people and the public in general. These
people must be indoors at 8-30 at night,
and must remain till next morning in
those little hovels of houses called homes,
there between Gallaher's factory and
York Street Factory, and yet the people
who are causing the disturbance in that
area do not live there at all. North
Queen Street does not come into the new
Curfew area. Lepper street is not in it,
yet these gunmen come from there and are
causing disturbance in that part, practically a loyal area, in which this new
Curfew operates. Whoever is responsible
for the suggestion that the military
authorities should put that area under
Curfew at 8-30? I think it is ridiculous,
and the sooner it is taken off the better.
If there is going to be a Curfew for 8-30
let it be for the whole of the city, seeing
what I say is correct-that it is the men
who come from the border and cause
disturbance amongst loyal citizens.
Nothing was said regarding this real
question of unemployment. I know the
workers have no warmer sympathiser than
my right hon. Friend, the Minister for
Labour, regarding this question, and I
would appeal to him before we adjourn to
tell us something of the proposals he has
in his mind. I know he has some very
good proposals at the back of his mind.
There are ex-servicemen walking the
streets to-day, and I wish he could hold
out some hope to them. A tradesman
stopped me to-day, and said he was quite
willing to take a labouring job at £2 a
week.
He was only getting 7s 6d to keep
a wife and family. I feel very keenly in
this particular matter, and I hope my
right hon. Friend will give us something
before the House adjourns on this
question, and more especially as we find
rents are steadily going up. The rents
these
poor people
steadily
up, and
now 70 per
over what they were in 1914.
Yet wages are decreasing. We have no
of
going
cent.
are
are
I say
Government to bring in any reform.
these are necessary questions which should
be brought before this House in the very
near future. You meet a man in the
street, and he says: "You have no Government. Will it ever be set up?
You are
going to adjourn till February, 1922." The
thing is ridiculous.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr
Andrews): No.
111 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 112
HOUSE OF COMMONS
Mr. DONALD: My right hon. Friend
says "No." I am delighted to hear that.
However, I promised I would not occupy
the time of the House more than five
minutes, and I have gone further than
that; therefore I will close my remarks by
hoping that the Prime Minister and his
colleagues will give us some definite assurance that something more will be done for
our people between this and next year.

Sir CRAWFORD M'CULLAGH: I have
listened with very great interest to the
debate. There have been many excellent
speeches, but I have asked myself the question, What business have we done, and
how much have we accomplished since we
entered the House here yesterday?". Now,
Mr. SPEAKER, as I listened to the very
lucid and the very interesting statement
of the Prime Minister, what appealed to
me most was the letter of the Prime
Minister and Cabinet of Northern Ireland
to Mr. Lloyd George, the British Prime
Minister, dealing with the present
position so far as the conference with Mr.
De Valera is concerned.
I have every
confidence that the Prime Minister and the
Cabinet will treat the matter in the right
and proper fashion. But, Mr. SPEAKER,
we must realise that that conference, whatever the outcome of it may be, will have,
and is bound to have, some effect on this
community, if only in the matter of finance
alone. I was going to refer to what the
hon. Member for North Belfast (Mr.
M'Keown) said in reply. I hope he was
not replying on behalf of the Government,
but only on behalf of education, because
there were many important matters dealt
with here to-day and yesterday that have
not at all been referred to. I may concongratulate him on the statement he made,
because he had to admit that he had done
very little, could do
very
a
there will be a great deal of distress in
our city. What effort has been made to
grapple with it? But the answer, I suppose will be "It is a matter of finance.
We have not got money. We cannot get it."
And take the other very important question,
that is bound to be dealt with by this
Parliament, and is in the transferred services to-day-that is the housing question.
It has not even been mentioned. Everyone knows that that great problem is one
of the very first things that will have to
be taken into consideration. We know that
an arrangement was made in the British
House of Commons, which was undoubtedly
a fine arrangement at the time it was made.
To-day it does not hold good. Altered
circumstances have got to be met, and I
would like to see some office-I do not
know what department it belongs to, I presume it is the Home Office-I would like to
see some effort being made to grapple with
that even to-day.
""
Not a word about it. What I complain
about is this. I do think, after making
all allowance, that the Cabinet must
hustle a bit quicker than they are doing.
We must press the Government to get
down to real facts and down to business,
and instead of talking, as we have been
doing here during the last two days, let
us get along with constructive work. I
admit there are difficulties in the way,
but at the same time I would like to impress upon the Government that they
should, at the earliest moment,
and at every moment, press the
British Government to transfer those
services. I am met with the statement,
'Oh, we cannot do anything until the
Southern Parliament is set up." That is
all right, but there are many services and
many branches that ought to be transferred at once.
little,
Now it is only a matter
of delay, and again it is only a matter of
finance. Really to meet here and go on
discussing is useless; we should get down
to facts and press on with business. I do
hope the Minister responsible-the
Minister of Labour, I hope, will make a
statement, and not put us off with the
one we have heard from the Secretary to
the Minister of Education-will give us
some real information to give out to the
public. At the present moment the
public are disappointed. If we have got
the power we ought to exercise it as soon
as possible.
and that it was entirely matter
of finance, of which we, or they, have not
yet got control. Then we turn again to
the other offices. I do not know whether
the Minister of Finance will, but I suppose
he will, deal with finance to-morrow. Then
there is the Home Secretary. I am told
that we should have got more information
from Ministers than we have already got.
Very important matters that we are called
upon to deal with have not been referred
to. My Friend the hon. Member for East
Belfast (Mr. Donald) referred to Labour.
What attempt is being made to grapple
with that question? Everyone knows perfectly well that for several months of this
season, and possibly of the early spring,
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews) You will appreciate that I am
placed in a somewhat embarrassing posi-
113 Debate on Adjournment. 21 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment. 114
In
tion in being called upon in a maiden
speech to reply for the Government.
dealing with the speech of the hon.
Member for Fermanagh and Tyrone (Mr.
Coote), I am also at a disadvantage, because while he is a trained Parliamentarian, accustomed to hear his voice
ringing out in the halls of Westminster,
I am only, to use his own phrase, one of
the untried and unfledged. Well, many
charges have been made against the
Government. It has occurred to me as
I have listened to the debate during the
last hour or so, that almost all of these
charges were dealt with by the Prime
Minister in his very able and interesting
and instructive speech. But as regards
my own Department, for which I am responsible, I wish to deal with the charges
which the hon. Member for Tyrone and
Fermanagh has levelled against it. He
blames me, I take it, for smoking cigars
with my staff-my faithful staff. I do not
happen to be a smoker at all, so he drew
blank for once in that regard. But he
made a more serious charge. He accused
me of delay in dealing with the unemployment question. I do not think he
quite substantiated that charge with facts.
Mr. COOTE: To a point of order. I
wish to put myself right. I did not make
a charge--
Mr. SPEAKER: That is not a point of
order, but I am sure the right hon.
Gentleman will give way to him.
Mr. COOTE: I simply pointed out the
fact that he made promises with reference
to construction and building at Stormont,
and I hope he will see them carried out,
although I have my doubts.
I
Mr. ANDREWS: I am delighted to
hear that is the hon. Member's view.
did not gather from his speech that it was.
In fact I gathered he was rather indicating that although we really only got
sanction from this House to build on that
property yesterday, there was some delay
taking place as to starting the work.
The hon. Member is entitled to his
opinion. I am equally entitled to mine.
I say there has been no delay. Already
anticipating what the decision of hon.
Members would be, we have been in
negotiation, not only with the representatives of the City Council, but also with the
authorities in Co. Down, with a view to
taking over the roads, and with a view to
arranging for the tram tracks being relaid.
There has been no delay whatever. As
regards the question of unemployment
generally, I am just as sympathetic as any
Member of the House in the difficulties
and the anxieties and the hardships which
the unemployed have got to endure. I
am still not in the position to act as Labour
Minister, except in an advisory capacity.
The Prime Minister explained yesterday,
that although we had got the Act we had
not got the powers transferred to us, and,
therefore, all I can do as Minister of
Labour for Northern Ireland is to act in an
advisory capacity as Minister of Labour
in the United Kingdom. I have done
that. I have pointed out clearly what
the position is here, and I have also explored every avenue in order to see what
can be done at the soonest possible moment
to deal with unemployment. We have
suggested to the British Minister many
methods by which this problem could be
eased. It is not for me to-day to commit
the Government to any particular policy,
but I can assure hon. Mempers that we are
going to deal with the question; that we
will deal with it sympathetically, and that
we will deal with it in a way that will
satisfy the Member for Fermanagh and
Tyrone. There are one or two other
matters to which the hon. Member made
reference. He talked about us not
getting a share of the Road Board Grants,
and grants for the relief of unemployment.
I know something about Co. Down, and
I am sure the hon. Member will be gratified to hear that Co. Down received in a
few months £48,000 from the Road Board
Grant, which they are now spending on the
roads of the county. In addition, we
received a grant from the St. David's
Fund of £3,000 for the relief of unemployment, and that money is being expended
at the present time making a road between
Ballyhalbert pier and the village. I must
pay tribute here to the Corporation of
Belfast for having availed themselves of
these grants, given by the British
Treasury, to a very substantial extent.
They are at present carrying out works
of utility which, I understand, employ
many hundreds of unemployed, and I hope
that that good example will be followed
by our County Councils. So far as I
have any influence I will induce them to
follow this example, and to proceed with
works which will be of advantage to those
Councils when carried out, and which will
in the meantime give employment to those
who at the present time are in want. I.
think there is just one other matter to
which I need refer, and that is this. An
hon. Member said: "Why was not the
H
115 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 116
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Andrews.]
Joint Exchequer Board brought into
operation." It cannot be brought into
operation under the Act at the present
time, because two Members of that Board
have to be appointed by the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, and two by the Parliament of Southern Ireland, or else by the
Crown Colony Government.
Mr. McGUFFIN: That is the point.
Mr. ANDREWS: As regards the Crown
Colony Government, the responsibility for
that not being an accomplished fact today does not rest with the Prime Minister
of Northern Ireland, or even with his
Cabinet. It rests with the Prime Minister
of Great Britain and his Cabinet, and it
is rather hard upon us as a young Government to be called to account for crimes
of omission and commission which are
not committed by us. I know perfectly
well that the Prime Minister of England
would say, and I sympathise with that
argument, that he could not bring in
Crown Colony Government while these
negotiations are being proceeded with,
as it would mean a breach of the truce.
We all want peace in Ireland; hon.
Members opposite all want peace in Ireland, and we in Ulster do not want to have
it said we were the ones who prevented
peace in Ireland, or that we were the ones
who blocked the way to a peaceful solution
of that problem. But while that is so,
I may tell you perfectly candidly that the
Government of Northern Ireland have done
everything possible to point out the
seriousness of the position to the Prime
Minister of England and his Cabinet, and
to urge upon him to allow us to function
at the earliest possible date. I would like
to know what more we can do? The only
other thing that we can do would be to say
"We will not stand that delay," and even
if we said that, have we the force or have
we the will, or have we the right to compel
the British Cabinet to act differently even
if it is right and proper that they should?
We must have a little patience still, and I
think he is not a friend of Ulster who
would wish to divide Ulster at the present
time. And I feel that the man who loves
Ulster will remain patient and will
support the Northern Government in
every way that he possibly can in the very
difficult and perilous times through which
we are passing. Now, I can remember
a good many years; I can remember
for instance, the Ulster Convention which
was held in the Plains in 1893, and within
those walls my own dear father spoke
with some effect. I remember seeing
above the main entrance doors the words
in large letters "United we stand; divided
we fall." True as those words were then,
they are equally true to-day.
And so I say it is the duty of all of us
to rally round our Prime Minister; to give
him all the support and the encouragement which he has the right to expect.
We ought to remember what he has done
for Ulster in the past. We ought to remember also what he has sacrificed to
come here to give us of his best; and if I
may presume to say it, I think hon.
Members ought to rally round the
Cabinet also, and ought to support us in
this very difficult and trying time through
which we are passing. I think they owe
that to the Cabinet. I think hon.
Members owe it to Ulster. I think it is
owed to the memory of those of our
fathers who have
gone, and to our
children who are coming forward to bear
a measure of responsibility probably not
much lighter than that which we are at
the present moment called upon to bear.
Question--" That this House do now
adjourn," put, and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
five minutes after Five of
the clock.
Parliamentary Papers. 22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Thursday, 22nd September, 1921.
The House met at a quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PARLIAMENTARY PAPERS.
Mr. SPEAKER: I just want to remind
hon. Members-I think most of them
know it-that there is a Vote Office in
this building in the Library, and that all
Parliamentary papers can be had there
after they have been issued. Members who wish the papers sent to their
private address should give notice to the
officer in charge of the Vote Office, and
if
within the city, they will be distributed, I hope every morning in time to
permit of hon. Members reading them
before the business of the day.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: While dealing with
that question, Sir, may I
ask you
whether the Standing Orders of the House
have been printed, and if so is it not the
custom to have a copy of them sent to
each Member of the House in advance?
Mr. SPEAKER: The Standing Orders
have been printed. The hon. Member
could have had them in the Vote Office
any day since the House has met and it
is not customary to send them out or anv
other
papers unless the Members give
notice at the Vote Office, but they are
available at this moment. The hon.
Member could have had a copy of them
at any time by asking for them at the
Vote Office.
Mr. McGUFFIN: I ask the question.
because application was made at your own
office prior to the meeting of Parliament,
and the individual was refused. I was
not able to be present myself, but now
that you have told us to-day it is quite
sufficient. Thank you very much.
MESSAGE FROM THE SENATE,
The Senate concur in the Resolution
communicated by the Commons, viz.:
"That it is expedient that a Joint Committee of Senators and Commons be
appointed to control the arrangements for
the Kitchen and Refreshment Rooms of
both Houses."
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
SUPPLY.
1ST ALLOTTED DAY.
Considered in Committee.
Mr. MOLES in the Chair.
VOTE ON ACCOUNT.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
1. "That a sum not exceeding £130,000
be granted to His Majesty on account for
or towards defraying the charges of the
several salaries for which a Vote on
Account is required for the period ending
the 31st March, 1922."
Mr. COOTE: On a point of order,
before the Minister of Finance moves for
this Vote, I want your ruling. Is this
merely a Vote on Account coming before
this House to-day, which will not be the
subject of any amendment further than
the discussion in Committee to-day, or is
it a Vote on Account, as I am led to think
it must be, carrying with it certain
Estimates which may be only temporary or
may not.
They are submitted to us in
the form of Estimates, and they give us a
schedule of the offices to be created
and the salaries attached to those offices,
for which this vote is required to-day.
Now, I submit-I admit that the Government are in an awkward position, because
this House is not a House which has been
in existence prior to but from the middle
of this Session, so to speak-but I submit
that if we are to discuss the Estimates, we
ought to discuss them in the ordinary
way, and I think we should insist on
discussing them in the ordinary way, that
is put
down amendments to certain
Estimates and discuss them. I ask for
your ruling; is this coming up in proper
form; and what is the course we should
adopt in order to deal with the matter,
because it really depends upon your ruling
as to what course we shall adopt?
The CHAIRMAN: The question the
hon. Member puts is one that will, perhaps, require a little elaboration in
answer. The House is committed to
nothing except the terms of the motion
which I have read to it if it adopts that
motion. When a Vote on Account is submitted, the Committee can do one of three
things with it, and they can do nothing
else. They can adopt it, they can reject
it, or they can reduce it. Those are the
only three courses open to the Committee
on this motion. The hon. Member has
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Chairman.]
stated what really is the difficulty.
Certain outgoings must be met. They
can only be met, in the circumstances
under which this House has been called
together, by a Vote on Account. Estimates
in the way the hon. Member desires will,
in due course be submitted to the
Committee, and will be open to discussion
that is always proper when a Vote in
Supply is being taken.
Mr. COOTE: That is next Session?
The CHAIRMAN: About that I have
no knowledge. That is for the Government. The Government will declare
their intentions in due course. This is
simply an expedient devised to meet the
temporary and peculiar circumstances in
which the Committee is called together.
Mr. COOTE: If you will grant me
indulgence, Sir, to speak again, I would
like to ask you: Assuming that these are
simply tentative Estimates, we hope it
will be declared so by the Ministers when
they rise to address the House, and they
will guarantee that this is without prejudice to anything that may happen when
these Estimates come up for confirmation
again. If they fill these offices in the
meantime they must do so at their peril,
as we will take no responsibility whatever
for them?
The CHAIRMAN: The hon. Member
knows that it is not, of course, for me to
direct the course the Government will
pursue in this matter. The only question
for me is whether the motion before the
House is a proper motion. I rule now that
it is so, and that it is not in my province
to ask them to give the assurance the hon.
Member asks for.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): Rising for the first time as I do
in my position as Minister of Finance of
the Northern Government-(Hon. Members: "Hear, hear ")-I hope I may rely
upon the forbearance, I might say the
sympathy, of the Committee in the somewhat novel and altogether unusual circumstances in which I find myself, and also
because of the somewhat confused and unsettled condition of things generally.
Now, finance is a prosaic subject. It does
not lend itself at all to rhetorical effort
such as my friend the hon. Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) is
such a master of. I am doubtful to-day
whether I should regard myself in a very
happy position or as a very miserable
mortal. I am naturally pleased we have
accomplished so much. My right hon.
Friend, the Prime Minister, sketched in
outline in his admirable address two days
ago what we have done, that we have
gained a Constitution, that we have
established a Parliament, that we had laid
the foundations of all the great
administrative departments; in fact, that
we have prepared everything for the whole
machinery of Government pending the
transfer of services a great deal more, I
think, than the average man recks of, and
which I think should be regarded, so far
as it goes, as exceedingly satisfactory.
But on the other hand, I must and am
bound to confess that I am greatly disappointed. I did hope we should have
seen before to-day the full fruition of all
our labours, and the coping stone placed
upon the completed edifice. If the conditions in Ireland were normal and if the
current of events had flowed on in an even
course in accordance with the provisions
of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, I
should have been standing here to-day
submitting a complete estimate or series
of estimates of the full revenue and expenditure of the Province for the year
beginning 1st April last, and ending 31st
March of next year.
When three months ago Their Majesties
the King and Queen opened our Parliament amid scenes of enthusiasm unprecedented, I think, in recent years, and
especially when His Majesty enjoined
upon us the prosecution of our schemes of
Government in the establishment of our
Constitution, I had no doubt in my mind
at that time that we should have been
able when we met Parliament to-day to
report on the establishment of all the
administrative departments, and that by
1st October, the half period, the middle
period of the financial year, we should
have been in a position to have collected
the transferred revenue, that is the
revenue over which we here in this
Province have control, such revenue, for
example, as death duties, land annuities,
entertainment tax, stamp duties, etc.
Well, on the other hand, I had no doubt
at that day we should also have been
enabled through the machinery of our
various departments to have begun the consideration of all expenditure-expenditure
on education, grants to local authorities
and other matters of that kind. But none
of the conditions necessary to produce the
results I have mentioned materialised. My
duty, to-day, therefore, is to submit provisional Estimates for the necessary Parlia
mentary and departmental expenditure
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
from the 7th June the day on which
Parliament first met-until the end of the
present financial year, and I have to ask
the Committee for that purpose for a Vote
on Account to enable me to provide for
such expenditure.
I think at this point it might be convenient for me if I supplemented what my
right hon. Friend, the Prime Minister,
stated in relation to the difficulties of
establishing the three joint bodies which
are necessary for carrying out the full and
entire provisions of the Act, and more
especially as the constitution of one of these
bodies particularly affects our finances. The
Government of Ireland Act from first to
last contemplated concurrent action of two
Governments of some kind and especially
-I am dealing now with my own department-the establishment of a joint body
called the Civil Service Committee. Provision was made in the Act, and we are,
of course, all aware of this, that in case
the Parliament of Southern Ireland-I am
dealing with Southern Ireland at the
moment-it was quite clearly contemplated
that there would be some difficulty in regard to the establishment of the Parliament
of Southern Ireland-refused to function,
and the members did not take the oath of
allegiance within a period of fourteen
days from the time Parliament was summoned, an alternative was to be provided.
(Hon. Members: "Hear! hear!")
Southern Parliament did not function on
the day on which Parliament was summoned
and fourteen days after-that was on the
12th of July-it was the duty of the
Government, under the Act, to establish
an alternative body, which would take the
place of the Parliament of Southern Ireland, and nominate the members of the
joint bodies concerned. The Government
failed to carry out their obligations under
the Act, and we are left in the position of
which the Committee are fully aware. But
there is another alternative, and I mention
this in view of the criticisms that have been
levied against the Government of supineness
and indifference in regard to the inception
and the provision of this Parliamentthere is another alternative which we laymen, at any rate interpret as suitable to fill
in the gaps and enable the nominees of the
joint bodies to be appointed. Section 69
of the Government of Ireland Act states
that:
The
His Majesty may, by Orders in Council
(in this Act referred to as the Irish Transfer Orders), make such regulations as may
seem necessary or proper for setting in
motion the Parliaments and Governments
of Southern and Northern Ireland.
This is the gravamen of the sentence.
And also in any other matter for which
it seems to His Majesty necessary or pro
per to make provision for the purpose of
bringing this Act into full operation, or
for giving full effect to any provisions of
this Act, or to any future transfers under
or by virtue of this Act of a reserved
service.
as an
Now, we put that forward
alternative, and claimed the right to have
the nominees upon the joint bodies concerned. Distinguished local counsel was
of opinion that that clause of the Act was
operative in the circumstances, but the
Law Officers of the Crown decided that
taking in view the whole current arrangements of the Act it was impossible
to issue the transfers required for the
establishment of the joint bodies. We
had, therefore, of course, to bow to the
decision of His Majesty's Government in
the matter. But it must not be supposed
that we allowed all this subject which has
been discussed ad nauseum to go by
default, because we were clearly alive to
the difficulties, not only in regard to
finance, but in regard to the machinery
of all the other Departments of the State,
which are rendered helpless in the
circumstances. We are, therefore, neither
able to collect revenue on the 1st October,
as we contemplated, nor are we able to
make the necessary expenditure. The
Imperial Government continues to
function in all these matters as it has done
for countless years. It receives all the
revenue of the province, and it distributes
all the money grants to all the
beneficiaries, whoever they may be.
Now, the natural question probably
arises if we have got no revenue how have
we got any money to pay the necessary
current expenses to which we are subject?
Well, frankly, I borrowed the
the money.
There is an institution in existence in the
Imperial Parliament designed to provide
for exigencies as they arise during the
period when Parliament is not in Session,
a permanent fund, and it is intended to
meet any contingency that may arise, the
Civil Contingencies Fund. Its name
describes exactly what it contemplates.
We applied to the Imperial Treasury for
a grant from that fund at the point when
it was beginning to be exceedingly
doubtful whether we should have our
transfer orders in time to carry on the
Government. We applied for a sum of
£20,000, which was granted. As time
progressed and it was apparent that the
transfer orders were not likely to be
brought into existence, we made another
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Minister of Finance.]
application for £10,000, and this was
granted. Still again, when beyond all
doubt it was seen that the British Government did not mean to act during the
negotiations that were proceeding between
Sinn Fein and themselves by bringing
into existence that which is colloquially
known as Crown Colony Government, we
began to look at what our probable
liabilities should be from that period until
the end of the present financial year.
There was a period of three months after
the assembling of Parliament which
matured on 7th September, and if we had
allowed that date of the 7th September to
pass we were stranded in regard to any
power of borrowing; that is made perfectly
clear under the Act. Before the 7th
September then, as I say, we had to consider necessary provision of the possible
financial requirements of the Government
up to the end of the financial year, and
especially up till March, at which period
these transfers must come into existence.
We applied for a further £130,000, and
the Treasury granted us that amount.
We have, therefore, borrowed the sum of
£160,000 for the objects which I have
indicated.
On the question of the Vote itself let
me say as regards the details of this expenditure, of which hon. Members may
desire detailed information, my hon.
Friend behind me, the Financial Secretary
of the Treasury (Mr. Barbour) will reply
to any questions that may be asked, and
give any information which may be
required. But I think it proper that I
should bring out into relief two salient
features of the estimates in order that the
Committee may clearly understand that
while the sum of £130,000 seems a large
amount when our expenditure has not
reached such dimensions as would warrant
any very considerable expenditure, there
is one item of £50,000 on account of the
estimate or
of
cost of the liability
£60,780. That is made up of payments
for the Stormont site; the reconditioning
of this building to make it suitable for
our meetings; the furnishing and reconstruction of the Howard Street buildings,
and re-equipment, and also such other
matters in relation to administrative departments as may be necessary. It is right
I should emphasise that although we
must include in our estimates the sum of
£60,000 odd that money will be repaid.
by the Imperial Treasury through the
machinery of the Joint Exchequer Board.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: If it ever operates.
Mr. POLLOCK: But the Act provides
that we, in the first instance, must face
the liability before we can recover from
the Imperial Government. Therefore, it
had to be included in the Estimate, and
it has largely swelled the amount of the
Estimate.
The second item to which I desire to
call attention-and there may be some
discussion on it is our Civil Contingencies Fund, the total Estimate being
£50,000, and the Vote on Account
£45,000. Now, I think I should say
something about the Civil Contingencies
Fund. It is designed for the purpose of
meeting contingencies which may arise.
during the period in which Parliament
does not function. It may be asked by
Members-Why is any liability that
may arise not met out of ordinary cash
balance? The answer to that is thisand I am sure the Committee will
appreciate it that public funds are very
closely regulated by Act of Parliament, and
cannot be disposed of without direct
authority of Parliament. It is to meet
such small variations from this statutory
rule that Parliament must control every
actual expenditure, that this fund is
created, and that we now desire to create,
because I think it is unnecessary to
labour this point very much—our future
at the present time is so indefinite, so uncertain, and the liabilities which we may
be called upon to meet equally indefinite
and equally uncertain, that the Government has come to the conclusion that such
a fund, a permanent fund, should be
created as the Civil Contingencies Fund.
Now I do not think it is necessary for
me to outline such possible liabilities as
may arise, but my hon. Friend, the Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr.
Coote) in a rather weighty attack on the
Government last evening suggested we
were indifferent and lazy, and were allowing events to pass along without attention
or scrutiny, and particularly with regard
to the relief of unemployment, that we
had not proceeded with the development
of the Stormont Estate, although
authority to proceed was only given by
Parliament on the previous day.
Mr. COOTE: I think if you read the
Hansard you will find that I said nothing
of the kind. I said now that Stormont
was an accomplished fact I appealed to
the Minister of Labour to see to it that
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
Supply.

there was no delay, but I did not accuse the amount is approximately £35,000. As
him of deliberate delay.
you know, a Select Committee has been
appointed of private independent Members
to settle the remuneration of all the
Ministerial officers; but bear this in
mind and I hope the Committee will
bear it in mind-that if we can effect
economies in our Ministerial Departments,
such as we contemplate may be possible,
the cost of our whole scheme of Parliament will not exceed the estimates of all
the cost of the Administrative Departments as they were originally administered, because it must be borne in
mind that there were 29 Departments
functioning in Dublin. It would weary the
Committee to go over them, and I do not
think it is necessary. We have concen-
Mr. POLLOCK: I do not suggest that
the hon. Member spoke of deliberate
delay, but deliberate delay and supineness
are somewhat different. However the
point I want to make is this, if the
development of the Stormont site is to be
expedited, and if expenditure is to be
made, obviously the money must be provided for that, and such liability may
amount to a very considerable sum. There
is no means of paying it except by the
establishment of a fund of this kind
which Parliament deliberately earmarks
for the purpose of meeting such contingencies as may arise, and as I have
indicated. A Bill will be prepared later
enabling expenditure to be made by Act
of Parliament if the House and the Committee think it right that such provision
should be made. I have no doubt whatever in view of the somewhat peculiar and
indefinite conditions in which we find ourselves Parliament will agree that some
such provision should be made.
While I am on that subject, I
think it should be mentioned to the
Committee that the total disbursements
we have made up to date amount to
£1,975. I hope that that comparatively
negligible sum will satisfy my hon.
Friend (Mr. Coote); that up to the present
we have not been lavish or prodigal in the
expenditure of public money. But there
are further claims to be met of £1,254,
and when these are paid a total
expenditure of £3,229 will have been
made in connection with items of expenditure that are
necessary, such
salaries of officials and such like.
But no
account has yet been received for books,
stationery, etc., which we estimate at
£1,000. So you see that the total actual
expenditure and the total claims
as
we
have already received amount to only
£4,000, which seems surely an exceedingly
miniature sum relative to the Vote of
£130,000 we ask the Committee to pass.
These payments consist chiefly of salaries
and subsistence expenses of the officers.
The item of £1,254 is for His Majesty's
visit, and the matter is still under
discussion with the Municipal authorities
in regard to the relative liabilities of
Parliament and the Municipality for the
various items.
I should say in estimating for
Parliament itself to the end of the year,
trated and consolidated all these offices
under six heads, and it is hoped that instead of the 29 indicated in the number of
the Dublin Departments, we will be able
at any rate to effect such economies as will
cover the cost of our Parliamentary
machine, and that, therefore, Parliament
itself will not be any additional burden
upon the taxpayers of the Northern
Province.
Now, I think it is right that I should
also inform the Committee that the
scales of the higher posts of the Administrative Departments have been fixed
by the Cabinet, and it has been found
necessary in certain instances to designate
the individuals for duties. The great
majority of these appointments are filled
by officials on loan, and we are under no
liability for them. They are sent here by
the Imperial authority to assist us in the
exceedingly technical and difficult work of
starting all the machinery of our Administrative Departments, and I acknowledge to the full the sympathetic and
generous assistance of the British authorities in giving us the free services of very
highly trained and capable men. In
this matter at any rate, there has been
no niggardly spirit. I wish also to
recognise the assistance we have received
in regard to the temporary arrangements
made for the housing of Parliament, as
well as our administrative offices; and I
do not think it would be invidious if I
recognised the services of Sir Ernest
Clark (Hon. Members: "Hear! hear!")
-work which I fear many Members of the
Committee have but a small appreciation
of. Sir Ernest Clark's work has been
something I cannot praise too highly.
(Hon. Members: "Hear! hear!")
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Minister of Finance.]
as
It must be remembered that Sir Ernest
Clark is not an official of the Northern
Government, and, therefore, all the
services which he has rendered have been
rendered ex gratia; and because of the
great interest he has taken in the consolidation of our work in the Northern
Parliament. (Hon. Members: "Hear!
hear!") Of course the great majority of
our officials will ultimately come from
Dublin, from the existing departments.
But in the meantime it has been necessary
to appoint a considerable number of the
clerical staff and typists, and these have
all been appointed by a Selection Board
which I have had specially appointed in
order that not only the question of
remuneration, but also the question of
stability and status and fitness should be
fully considered. This Selection Board
has, as I say, already made a number of
appointments in connection with the
clerical staff which may be useful in indicating the permanent scheme of finance
-the permanent scheme of finance
contemplated by the Act. Perhaps it
may be appropriate here if I remove some
misconceptions which have been current,
I know, in the matter. In the first
instance there are the resources of the
Northern Parliament, which consist of the
whole of the revenue collected, subject to,
first of all, our contribution to the
Imperial Parliament and Government.
That revenue must meet the expenditure,
and it is immaterial whether that revenue
consists of the reserve services collected
by the Imperial authority or transferred
services, or transferred revenue collected
by us. The reserve revenue, of course,
might be administered by the Imperial
funds, but we do not suffer in any respect
because of that. The Imperial authority
can presumably collect this taxation quite
as economically as we can do, and the
money that is collected is handed over to
us from the Imperial Treasury to meet our
liabilities. But what we want to urge is
this that Ulster is interested in every
penny of the revenue that is raised,
whether it is raised and collected through
the machinery of our own departments or
whether it is collected
collected through the
machinery of the Imperial departments.
(Hon. Members: Hear! hear!") And
being interested in every penny, the
greater the revenue the greater the
balance we shall have left for the development of our own schemes of local reform.
On the other side of the account, if Ulster
is to successfully extend the activities of
her Government in schemes of permanent
advantage to the country, we are interested, I say, in expenditure economically
in every direction.
It is a fallacious idea, a widely mistaken
notion, to think that the more we spend
the better our position when we come
before the Joint Exchequer Board and
the amount of our contribution is determined. That is entirely a fallacy. The
determination of our contribution from
Great Britain will be reached by other
methods than the expenditure of the
Province. (Hon. Members: "Hear!
hear!") The taxable capacity of the
Province will be taken undoubtedly into
account. That is what we must bear in
mind, and it is because of that I consider
it my duty to emphasise that point. The
greater our economy in carrying on the
machinery of our Government the larger
will be the sum for the purpose of our
development. I would also impress this
upon the Committee that we have only
ourselves to depend upon. Indeed, I
think that idea should almost be posted
up in every administrative department of
the Province. It is only out of our own
resources that expenditure can be met.
And while general approval may be given
to economy in the abstract, it seems
sometimes to be rather a different matter
when personal influences and personal desires enter into it. With the idea
which is in my mind at the present moment
I recall that last evening was it not?-my
hon. Friend, the Member for Tyrone and
Fermanagh (Mr. Coote), with a look of
unusual fierceness for one of such
amiable disposition, said, "You must hold
the purse strings tight," and then, "Sir, I
appeal to you on behalf of the secondary
teachers, whose condition is deplorable."
You have on the one side, almost in the
same sentence, that the purse strings have
got to be held tight, and on the other hand
that you are to make a grant to a body of
men who, I agree, are in very deplorable
circumstances. But I would remind my
hon. Friend and others who have touched
upon that subject of secondary education,
that I myself have been brought into very
close touch with, and have served my
apprenticeship, so to speak, in educational
matters during the past few years. In my
former official capacity I was responsible,
or mainly responsible, for the preparation
of a Bill to relieve educational conditions
in Belfast-a Bill which my hon. Friend,
the Member for North Belfast (Mr.
M'Guffin), presented in the Imperial
Parliament, and argued with a cogency
an
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
which gave us general satisfaction. We
all know how that Bill was talked out. I
only mention that to let the Committee
know that I have not any lack of sympathy
whatever for the condition of secondary
education. But, Sir, I wish to say that we
have no money for special grants in view
of the liabilities that are facing us. It is
better that I should be outspoken in this
matter. There is no use pretending we
have an inexhaustible well out of which we
can draw money for any matter which, in
the opinion either of a body or of an individual may be required during the period
with which we are now faced. We cannot
have it both ways. With the funds at our
disposal we must exercise that strict
economy which is necessary, and which
my hon. Friend is determined we shall
exact, I am very glad, I may say, and I
hope my hon. Friend will keep a watchful
eye upon the doings of the Treasury. It
will all contribute to that rigidity of rule
which is so necessary in departments dealing
with finance. But the summary of the
matter is, we must conserve our resources
until we can see a way out of the present
difficulties; and when we can see the way,
and when we reach the point when we have
a surplus, then I think such questions and
such claims as those of secondary teachers
are among the very first that should call
for our sympathy and support. I should
also like to remind my hon. Friend and
colleague in South Belfast in passing that
he seemed to be under the impression
yesterday, when speaking on the housing
question, that a department relating to
housing had been transferred, and that
we as a Govermnent were in some position
to deal with that very serious and important matter.
I should remind my hon. Friend that
there has been no transfer of any department connected with housing or any other
subject which will enable us to finance
either it or take any part in dealing with
the conditions as they exist to-day and
which I know are present in the mind of
my hon. Friend who has taken such a
deep interest in the question of the housing of the people. I think it right I
should mention that, because the report
of the speech may convey the impression
that we are in a position to deal with that
very difficult subject. I have thus far
dealt in a general way with the general
scope of our finance. I hope I have made
the position clear.
I think it will be expected I should
consider for a little what the Ulster position would be, assuming the transfer of
the services had taken place. (Hon. Member: "Hear! hear!'') In order to meet
this question I think it will be necessary
to go back in retrospect, and to touch
upon the earlier arrangements connected
with the financial proposals of the Home
Rule Bill as it was, the Home Rule Act
as it is. It is idle to suppose that we
were satisfied with the financial proposals of that Act. We entered, if I
might mention, one very striking itemwhen I say we, I mean the Finance Committee that was appointed by the
necessary authority to investigate the
whole incidence of the proposals--we
entered a very vigorous protest against
the claim for half of the revenue of our
Province as a contribution to the
Imperial services, when at the same time
the Government was satisfied with a
contribution from Southern Ireland of less
than 30 per cent.-50 per cent. in our
case, and 30 per cent. in the case of the
South of Ireland. We urged upon the
Minister that not only was the revenue of
the Province over-estimated, but that the
expenditure of the Province was underestimated. Let me say now that every
argument we put forward-
An HON. MEMBER: 56
and 44 per cent.
per cent.,
The MINISTER of FINANCE: I was
speaking of contributions in regard to
revenue, half of our revenue, almost
£8,000,000 was to be the contribution to
the Imperial expenditure in the one case,
while the South of Ireland only pays
30 per cent., or £10,000,000. I say every
argument that we used and every point we
made has been fully verified-absolutely
and literally verified. Indications of the
serious slump in trade were just beginning
to manifest themselves. We warned the
Minister that it was not proper to rely
on the continuance of the artificial condition of things that obtained during the
boom-then in the early part of 1920.
It was quite obvious at the time of our
discussion that we had reached the peak,
and we were then going down the decline,
while on the other hand we pointed out
that the schemes of social reform which
were continually being pressed on by the
Imperial authority would inevitably increase the cost of expenditure in our
Northern Province. Looking back upon
the estimates made by our Finance
Committee, we have the satisfaction of
knowing that all these estimates were
I
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Minister of Finance.]
absolutely right and those of the Government were absolutely wrong. However,
we had this safeguard, a safeguard which
was provided by our Ulster Members that
the contribution should be reviewed, and
I have no doubt whatever that in the review of the contribution by the Joint
Exchequer Board, regard will be had to
all the circumstances which I have
mentioned, the changed conditions,
the increase in expenditure caused by
legislation over which we had no control,
and also by the decrease of revenue
caused by circumstances over which
equally we had no control.
The White Paper Estimate on which the
financial Proposals of the Bill were based
showed a surplus revenue, after all expenditure was provided for, of £1,920,000,
whilst some provision was made for some
later expenditure which raised the surplus.
to £2,250,000. I think when I touch on
this matter I should read the declaration
of the Minister who had charge of the
Bill, because it gives an indication of the
intentions of the Government, and will
show that it was the intention of the
Government that Ulster should be
generously treated in this matter. Sir
L. Worthington Evans used these very
significant words which I hope will sink
into the minds of all present :—
The Government recognise that it is most
desirable that the Irish Governments should
not be hampered, but should be allowed a
substantial margin with which to meet any
additional expense which may be incurred
by the establishment of the new Parliaments,
or may be called for by various services
which have hitherto not been developed
perhaps in accordance with the requirements
of the Irish people, and the Imperial contribution has, therefore, been fixed for the two
years at £18,000,000, which, upon the Estimates will leave a surplus of £4.250.000
revenue a year. Nor is this all. As the
Prime Minister announced earlier in the year
the Government have decided to make a free
gift of the existing land annuities, amounting
at present to £3,250,000, which will be available as additional revenue for Ireland. The
surplus and the land purchase annuities
together amount to £7,500,000. There is
included in the expenditure a margin for
future services, which have not yet been set
up, and will not mature fully for some years
to come, of £1,750,000. If this is added there
is a total surplus of £9,250,000 available
for Ireland over and above all existing
expenses after providing for the Imperial
contribution. This gives Southern Ireland a
total surplus of about £7,000,000, and
Northern Ireland a total surplus of £2,250,000.
I do not think, therefore, that it can be said
that an Imperial contribution has been fixed
too high upon the information at present
available. On the contrary, it has been fixed
at an amount which leaves for expenditure
in Ireland a sum very much in excess of the
highest rate of expenditure that there has
ever been in Ireland in times past.
Now, while that statement of the
Minister without Portfolio, the Minister
who had charge of the financial proposals
of the Bill, is not in the nature of a
statutory obligation, we claim that there
is something even more than a moral
obligation on Great Britain to see that at
least in the first year of the functioning
of our Ulster Parliament there shall be,
after all our expenses are paid, a surplus
of £2,250,000. Otherwise the intentions
of the Government will not have been
carried out, and the generous treatment
on which they prided themselves so much
will not be in evidence. I have not the
slightest fear, however, but that the Joint
Exchequer Board, presided over by a
gentleman of the well-known fairness and
strict impartiality of Lord Colwyn will deal
fairly with us, when we come to present
our accounts in this matter.
The Committee will remember that the
Prime Minister announced that Lord
Colwyn had agreed to accept the position
of chairman of that very important body.
From the information which we have compiled it would seem that the expenditure
of the services of Northern Ireland this
year has been greatly increased as compared with last year.
Of that there can
be no doubt, thus again verifying the argu
ment that we used in our conference with
the Minister without Portfolio. Education
alone is expected to cost this year in which
we now stand £337,000 more than last
year, and that is a cumulative increase,
because the full effect of the arbitrator's
award in relation to the salaries of the
National School teachers has not yet had its
full effect. Health and Labour alone, the
Department over which any right hon.
Friend on my right (Mr. Andrews) presides, is estimated to cost £186,000 more
than last year.
The Post Office services
are increased by £210,000, arising, of
course, from the additional bonuses and
larger salaries paid to the officials as com
pared with previous years, and, indeed, it
can be said that in every Department of
the Province there has been an estimated
increased expenditure for the present year.
In addition to what I have already men.
tioned, there has been a very large increase
in Police expenditure--
Mr. M'GUFFIN: The Government
should pay that.
Mr. POLLOCK: For which, indeed, 1
hesitate to mention the amount, because it
is something which would introduce a very
grave subject if we thought that we were
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
to be involved in any liability in regard to
it. But in the opinion of our Government
this is an alternative to military expenditure, and therefore is such that we should
not be called upon to pay. That is to say
it should be an Imperial charge. We
have every reason to believe, and we are
confident that the Imperial Government
will assume, that we will not be under any
liability for the increased cost of the police.
in consequence of the extraordinary conditions which have obtained in Ireland
generally. But under circumstances that
are altogether so surrounded by doubt and
that are so indefinite, no Finance Minister
could be expected to arrive at a definite
conclusion in regard to the financial results
of revenue and expenditure for the coming
year. I am confident that this Province
will be fairly treated in regard to the
financial arrangements that must neces
sarily ensue between our Province and the
Imperial authorities, but with the nebulous
character of the outlook it would not he
prudent for the Government to enter into
fresh obligations involving expenditure
until its resources and the necessary current charges upon it are more definitely
and accurately determined. Now, I think
I have travelled over most of the ground in
relation to the financial situation of our
Province. I should like, as I close, to
touch cursorily on a question of some importance, referred to by my hon. Friend
the Member for Londonderry (Sir Robert
Anderson) in his very interesting speech
yesterday. My hon. Friend spoke on the
subject of fiscal autonomy with an authority
derived from experience wider than that of
the average man, and his opinion on subjects of that kind is entitled to respect.
He said rightly that we cannot interfere
in any arrangements made between the
British Government and Southern Ireland.
That is obvious. My right hon. Friend,
the Prime Minister, has made that abundantly clear in all his communications. I
agree with him that we have a very direct
interest in the results of that arrangement,
and I can give this assurance that the
Government will watch it with the closest
interest.
It is a matter on which we must speak
with the utmost reserve, and no good object
can be served by instituting a discussion
on matters that are purely speculative and
without any knowledge whatever of the
circumstances which we may be called upon
to face. Our Parliament, our Government,
and our province may be called upon to face
serious issues, but we have met serious
issues in the past, and I am perfectly confident, if we only get rid of our doubts,
our fears, and our suspicions, and stand
together shoulder to shoulder as brothers
should stand, that as we have come through
many trials, many sufferings, and many
sacrifices during our forty years of travelling in the wilderness, though perhaps we
are not yet in sight of promised land, yet
I am convinced that with courage and determination-and I speak it reverently
-that if we exhibit courage and determination, the Providence that guided our
fathers so long will carry us safely through.
Had
Mr. LYNN: I beg to move that the sum
of £130,000 be reduced by a sum amounting
to £20,000. In the Imperial Parliament it
is usual to put down a small sum of £5
or £10 simply in order to ventilate ary
different matters that may arise in the Vote,
but in this instance I think it is more
important that we should get to grips and
realities at once. If the Chancellor of the
Exchequer will allow me to say so, it would
be impossible for him to make a prosaic
speech on any subject. I have listened to
him often and I can say with all sincerity
that anything he touches he adorns. I
have only one regret with regard to his
speech, and that is that it was not made
before the Estimates were drafted.
it been I probably would be spared the
necessity of saying anything, because the
pruning knife would have been used a great
deal more vigorously than it has been. I
sympathise with the Chancellor of the
Exchequer just as I sympathise with the
Prime Minister and other members of the
Government in the anomalous position in
which they find themselves. It is not their
fault do not let the public imagine, let
no one imagine we are blaming them for
the anomalous position of affairs. We
know they are as anxious as we are, and
even more so, if it were possible to get
out of that anomalous position. Hitherto
we have been dealing with questions in
academic fashion, because we are dealing
with questions we had really no control
over. To-day we are brought face to face
with reality. We are asked to deal with
questions which the Government of this
House have control over-I mean the finance
of Northern Ireland.
I would like to impress, especially on the
Members who have come into Parliament
this session, how very important it is that
the House of Commons should keep a very
firm control over the national purse.
good deal of the trouble in the Imperial
Parliament for centuries past has been in
A
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Lynn.]
regard to the control of the purse by the
House of Commons. I hope this House
will show its independence and make it
clear that they are going to control the
finances and the national purse, and see
that there is no extravagance in this part
of the United Kingdom. I am sorry to
say in the Imperial Parliament things
have got so big that it is very difficult to
control finance. Anyone who has seen
them passing one hundred million pounds
in an hour or so, or in a few minutes, will
realise how difficult it is to keep in touch
with finance in the Imperial Parliament
But we have no excuse of that kind here.
The Chancellor, in his excellent speech,
pointed out that our revenue will not be
so big that it will not be possible for us to
look over every detail-even the smallest
details. This is a matter over which we can
exercise control, and over which I ask
every Member of the House to exercise
control. I agree with the excellent and very eloquent speech that
the Minister of Labour made yesterday.
In any criticism we offer
there is not the semblance of disunion.
We stand solidly behind the Ministry, but
I want to say for myself-and I am sure I
speak for other Members-that we are not
going to close our eyes and open our
mouths and take anything the Ministry
like to give us. We are here as independent Members, and are going to vote for
the Ministry on every vital question affecting Ulster, but we are not going to accept
everything, especially in the way of public
expenditure, without seeing into it.
want, on my own behalf, to make that
point clear. I shall be an independent
critic of the Government, as I have been
of the Government on the other side on
matters of finance. I shall vote against
them if I think they are wrong and sup
port them if I think they are right, but
I shall do what I think best. Do not let
it be said that because we criticise the
Ministry that we think the Ministry is
incapable of doing its work or that we hold
different views.
here

the Government, to thank the hon. Member and accept every word he says.
know perfectly well, with regard to the

want frank criticism of our estimates or

Mr. LYNN: It is extremely characteris
tic of the straightforwardness which has
always marked the Prime Minister that
he should get up at this stage and make a
statement like that. My criticism is not
put forward for the purpose of weakening
confidence in Ministers. We do not want
to do that, and any criticism that I do
offer-and I promise to offer criticism on
the finances of the Government-is with
the object of strengthening the position
of the Government rather than weakening
it. The Ulsterman always wants to get 20s.
worth of value for every £1 he spends,
and if he had his way he would like to get
21s. worth. It only shows his good sense,
and, therefore, we have to look over every
one of the items raised by Mr. Chancellor
to see that there is not a single £1 spent
that should not be spent. Now, Mr.
Chancellor expressed a pious hope-I hope
it will be realised-that it would be possible
to carry on the Government of Northern
Ireland as cheaply as before this Act was
passed. Quite true, we had a great many
unnecessary Departments in Dublin, but
that is no reason why we should have one
single unnecessary Department or official in
Northern Ireland.
Members:
(Hon.
"Hear! hear!") I am glad to hear that
(6
Hear!" and I hope when the division is
taken the Members of the Committee will
go into the Lobby and vote against some
of the things shown in the estimate which
I hope to prove to the Committee there is
not the slightest necessity for. It is out of
its own revenue that Northern Ireland rust
pay for the expense incurred. Now, when
we were linked up with Great Britain, the
people did not realise how the revenue
came. The Imperial purse was a huge one,
but let me point out a fallacy which I am
afraid exists, namely, that the more money
that is spent the more you get out of Great
Britain when you come to revise the financial estimate. I would like to make two
observations with regard to that. The first
is that even if it were possible, I do not
think it would be honest. The second is
that I think it would be exceedingly ad
economy to start on an extravagant system
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
because you were going to get a few extra
millions out of Great Britain. Let me tell
you this, that you have less chance to-day
of getting extra millions out of Great
Britain than you had when this Act was
passed. Only in the last Session two very
important schemes have been scrapped by
the British Ministry owing to the exigencies of the finance market, namely-Housing
which the Government could not finance,
and which we cannot finance either, and the
scheme with regard to agriculture. I know
the Imperial Government incurred a good
deal of displeasure because of scrapping
them, but do you think they are going to
give us extra millions to be spent here?
Do not let us for a moment vote these large
sums, because they are large sums for us;
do not let us vote these large sums, imagining that we are going to get anything more
out of Great Britain than the statutory
Liability. We have got, as Mr. Chancellor
has said, to depend upon ourselves, and we
have got to exercise the most ruthless
economy in every Department of the State.
He pointed out that while a great many
people paid lip service to economy they
came along afterwards and asked for a huge
expenditure. Mr. Chancellor, I can promise
you, so far as I am concerned, I am going
to practice what I preach. I am not going
to come here and try to get popular applause, which is a very cheap thing to get,
and then come along and press you to make
large payments of money. That is one of
the things we have got to guard against.
We have got to see we only ask for money
that is absolutely necessary in the interests
of the public.
I say there must be the most absolute
economy, and I want to point out some
lines where, in my judgment, there is not
economy. The Prime Minister, on Tuesday
-when I interjected a few remarks on this
question of economy, to the effect that an
initial mistake had been made in creating
too costly a Government, I pointed out
not only in this House, but through another
medium of reaching the public, that
the Northern Parliament was the only
Provincial Parliament in the Empire
that had got Parliamentary Under-Secretaries-the Prime Minister, I am afraid,
seemed to doubt that the other day, and
quite unintentionally, I am sure, he left the
impression in the minds of the House that
in Colonial Parliaments there were deputy Ministers. May I point out to the
Prime Minister that as a matter of fact
deputy Ministers in a Colonial Parliament
are only the permanent heads of Departments, and are not members of Parlia-
He
ment at all. So that this Northern Pariiament is the first Parliament in the Empire,
is the only Parliament in the Empire, except the Imperial Parliament, that has got
Parliamentary Under-Secretaries. I mention that in passing because we have been
starting our career as a Parliament and a
We
Government on far too large a scale.
are like a young couple who want to start
where their parents left off. That is not the
right road to go. We ought to begin in a
small way; we ought to economise our resources, we ought, if necessary, to add to
the number of Ministers. I know one
Member of the House rather objected to
what I said about Under-Secretaries.
suggested, as a matter of fact, that you
ought to have a Deputy-Secretary and Secretaries to the Deputy-Secretary. It is very
easy for the Member of a millionaire constituency to make a suggestion of that kind,
but it is not the sort of thing which appeals
to a constituency like the one I represent,
or the bulk of the constituencies in Ulster.
They want to see things done decently and
in order, but not extravagantly. We are beginning our career by apeing the Imperial
Government, which has huge wealth behind
it. And I want to draw the attention of
the Committee very seriously to
number of items that they will find if
they turn to Page 5 of the Estimates, for
example; I say nothing with regard to
the Chairman of Ways and Means, or
the Chairman of Committees in the
Senate. One point I would like to ask
the Under Secretary for Finance is how
Is the
this money is to be allocated?
£1,500 going to the Chairman of Ways
and Means to be divided between the
