Time to-morrow.
To be read a Third
SUPERANNUATION BILL.
Ccnsidered in Committee.
CLAUSE 1 (Application of Superannuation Acts to Civil Service of Northern
Ireland), ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Specially Appointed Officers)
(1) In the application of the Superannuation Acts to specially appointed officers
as hereinafter defined, the following additional provisions shall have effect:-
(a) A specially appointed officer may
be deemed to be a civil servant,
although he has not received on admission to the Civil Service of
Northern Ireland a certificate from
the Civil Service Commissioners.
(b) For the purpose of computing the
amount of
any superannuation
allowance, additional allowance, compensation or gratuity which may be
granted to a specially appointed
officer, his service in the Civil Service
of the United Kingdom shall be
reckoned as if it had been service in
an established capacity in the permanent Civil Service of Northern
Ireland.
(c) The provisions of section seven of
the Superannuation Act, 1859, with
respect to the granting of allowances
by way of compensation, shall apply
to any specially appointed officer if
he is removed from office in the Civil
Service of Northern Ireland before
he attains the age of sixty years, for
any cause other than misconduct or
incapacity.
(d) Where, in pursuance of section four
of the Superannuation Act, 1914, the
Treasury grant to a specially appointed
officer, upon his retirement from employment in the Civil Service of
Northern Ireland, a superannuation
allowance, additional allowance, or
gratuity in respect of his service in
the Civil Service of the United
Kingdom, any superannuation allowance, additional allowance,
gratuity which the
the Ministry of
Finance have power to grant in pursuance of this Act shall be abated to
the extent of the amount granted by
the Treasury as aforesaid.
or
(2) For the purposes of this section a
specially appointed officer means an officer
(other than a transferred Irish officer)
who, having served in an established
capacity in the permanent Civil Service
of the United Kingdom, is transferred
237 Superannuation Bill. 28 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Superannuation Bill. 238
therefrom to the Civil Service of Northern
Ireland, and becomes a civil servant.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That the Clause stand part of the Bill.'
Mr. COOTE: I would like to move as
an amendment to this Clause to leave
out sub-section "A." I move this
amendment for the purpose of drawing
the attention of the Committee to some
facts which are in the mind of the House
at the present time, that is that there are
certain officers who have been introduced
into the service of this House who have
never been through the Civil Service, who
are receiving very handsome salaries, and
who will, under this Clause, be admissible
to superannuation.
I shall never cease
to agitate until those offices are
eliminated entirely. Let it be understood that I do not object to the men, but
to the offices in a House of this
magnitude with only about 12 millions
of revenue.
Mr. POLLOCK: Fifteen millions.
Mr. COOTE: Well, fifteen millions.
We are simply embarking on a proposition that will not be eventually borne
out by this House, and we must retract
and retract very soon. We shall have to
retract on the suggestions brought before
the House already, for I do maintain that
we cannot keep pace with the start we
have made. The country will never permit us to do so, especially as these offices
are not essential, and believing this, I
move the amendment for the purpose of
further protesting and drawing the
attention of the House to the anomalies
that are included in Sub-section "A" of
Clause 2, and with a view to showing
what a very unfortunate thing it would
be if men are appointed to offices that
we do not believe in, men who further
may hope to be made servants of this
House, admissible to superannuation if
they have the good fortune to run their
tenure. I beg to move.
The PARLIAMENTARY AND FINAN-
CIAL SECRETARY OF THE MINISTRY
OF FINANCE (Mr. Barbour): I think my
hon. Friend has rather misunderstood the
precise application of this Clause here, and
it is a thing we should bear in mind when
considering the provision of other Clauses.
That is when a civil servant is changed
from the Civil Service of the United Kingdom to the Civil Service of Northern Ire
land he practically severs his connection
with the Civil Service of the United Kingdom and makes a fresh entry into the Civil
Service of the Northern Parliament. The
provisions of this Clause show that such an
Officer would not be required to obtain a
fresh certificate from the Civil Service
Commissioners cr such other authority as
may be set up in order to grant these certificates for admission; because admission
is only by certificate from the Civil Service
Commissioners, and it is to obviate the
necessity of him getting a further certificate
to enable him to enter the Civil Service of
the Northern Parliament.
Mr. COOTE: The point I wish to derive
some light upon is this. Assuming he is
not & civil servant transferred from England or Scotland, or wherever you like,
assuming that he has never been a civil
servant, but is brought in now into an
official place in this House and runs the
length of time due by an official, will he
come under the benefits of this Act?
Mr. BARBOUR: My reply to that is that
the definition of the terms of the Civil
Service are clearly laid down in the different
Acts to which reference is made in this Bill.
Mr. COOTE: That is not an answer to my
question.
Mr. BARBOUR: I am coming to that.
I was going to say it may be possible we
should require or deem it advisable to
appoint officers who have not been civil
servants up to the present time.
Mr. COOTE: That is my point.
Mr. BARBOUR: In the event of such an
appointment being made he would have to
comply with the rules laid down by the Civil
Service Commissioners, which would be to
undergo a probationary course of two years
to satisfy their requirements in matters of
education, health, and other conditions
which they lay down.
an
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Will it involve
examination, as is ordinarily observed in the
Civil Service?
Mr. BARBOUR: It would.
Mr. LYNN: I think it is a very important
matter. No one wants to indulge in captious
criticism; we simply want to be clear upon
the point. Let us assume you take in some
person who has not been in the Civil Service,
and for all you know that person may be
fifty-seven or sixty years of age, and he is
possibly not in the very best of health, and
at the end of two or three years he is unable
to serve in his office, what, then, would be
the result? Will we have to pay him full
pension? That is the only point I want
information on.
Adjournment. 240
Superannuation Bill.

Mr. BARBOUR: Of course hon.
Members will understand that this Act is
by reference to existing Acts. It is not like
the Exchequer and Audit Eill, which is a
pretty complete Bill. as put into the hands
of the hon. Members. One of the
conditions is that a man only qualifies for
superannuation under these Acts after
completing a service of ten years.
Mr. LYNN: That is all right, thank you.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
Mr. LYNN: May I ask the Parliamentary
Secretary of the Ministry of Finance what
is the Order of Business for to-morrow?
The PARLIAMENTARY SECRETARY
OF THE MINISTRY OF FINANCE
(Captain Dixon): To-morrow we will have
the Third Readings of the Bills which
went through the House to-day. We
then propose to adjourn from to-morrow
afternoon, that is Thursday, until 2-15 on
Tuesday next.
ADJOURNMENT.
Resolved: That this House do now
adjourn.--[Captain Dixon.]
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
Minutes before Three of the Clock.
240-1-2-3

Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
29 SEPTEMBER 1921.
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Thursday, 29th September, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
ORAL ANSWERS TO QUESTIONS
ROAD MAINTENANCE PROPOSALS.
1. Mr. CRAWFORD asked the Minister
of Home Affairs whether he is aware that,
in addition to the proposals from County
Down for road maintenance, proposals
were sent by the Antrim County Council
in December last to the Ministry of Transport; that in February last a deputation
from the Council interviewed the Ministry
pressing for an early decision; is he further
aware that the Ulster Association of
County Councils communicated with the
Ministry of Transport pointing out the
delay in dealing with the scheme, and
asking that decision should be given before
January; and, in view of the urgency of
the matter and its bearing on the rates
of the whole community, will he press the
Ministry of Transport for an early decision?
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): My reply to the question on this subject by the hon. Member
for South Belfast on the 26th inst. was
based on the following telegram received
from the Ministry of Transport, to which
Department a verbatim copy of the hon.
Member's question had been sent:
No moneys derived from Motor Licence
duties collected this year within area of Six
Counties have been disbursed. So far only
received proposals from County Down;
awaiting others.
A copy of the question now put has
been telegraphed to the Ministry of Transport, and I have received a telegraphic
reply, in which I am now informed that
the previous statement of the Ministry of
Transport, on which my former reply was
based, referred to recent applications for
grants in connection with proceeds of new
motor taxation, and that the communication from the Ulster Association of
County Councils has been received and is
under consideration by that Department.
In order to remove any misunderstanding
between the Ministry of Transport and the
Road Authorities in Northern Ireland, I
propose to send shortly a representative to
London to take the matter up with the
Ministry of Transport Authorities on the
spot, and in the meantime I should be
glad if these Road Authorities would communicate with me at once, giving any particulars of applications they have already
made or propose to make to the Ministry
of Transport.
Mr. CRAWFORD: Arising out of the
answer, may I ask the right hon. Gentleman is it a fact that, although the
Ministry of Transport say they have only
received proposals from County Down, the
Antrim County Council sent theirs in in
December last, and that their application
was in connection with the proceeds of the
new motor taxation?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I understand
that is so.
Mr. CRAWFORD: May I further ask
the right hon. Gentleman to urge strongly
the Ministry of Transport to put the
scheme into operation before January
next, so that at the striking of the rates
for next year the ratepayers may not
suffer from the delay?
Sir DAWSON BATES: As I have said,
I am sending a representative to the
Ministry of Transport, and he personally
can bring this question before them.
UNEMPLOYMENT.
THE CABINET COMMITTEE.
2. Mr. THOMPSON DONALD asked
the Minister of Labour whether Ireland is
represented on the Cabinet Committee to
deal with unemployment, and whether in
the event of legislation being introduced
by the Imperial Government, Ireland's
claims will be considered, whether any
representation has yet been made to the
Imperial Government regarding this
matter?
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews) I am in communication with
the Minister of Labour of the United
Kingdom on this subject, and have represented to him the claims of Northern Ireland to participate in any measures of
relief which the Imperial Cabinet may decide upon.
I have reminaed him that
frequently Ireland has been left out, and
have emphasised that this unemployment
problem is as acute here as in Great
Britain. I have also requested that Northern Ireland should be represented on
any special Committee which may deal
with the matter.
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.

NATIONAL INSURANCE.
3. Mr. THOMPSON DONALD asked
the Minister of Labour if he will state the
cost per person per week of the Health
and Unemployment Insurance to the
worker, to the employer, and to the State
respectively.
Mr. ANDREWS: I would suggest to
my hon. Friend that if he examines the
Health Insurance and Unemployment
Insurance Acts, which can be seen at any
time in the Library, he will find they give
a full account of the contributions and
benefits payable by the employer,
worker, and the State. To give a detailed
statement would, I feel, occupy too much
time of the House.
Mr. DONALD: May I ask will my right
hon. Friend publish it in the report?
Mr. ANDREWS: Certainly.
Mr. DONALD: Thank you.

29 SEPTEMBER 1921.

LABOUR EXCHANGE OFFICIALS.
5. Mr. THOMPSON DONALD asked
the Minister of Labour whether he will
state the number of Labour Exchanges,
together with the number of officials
temporary and permanent in Northern
Ireland; what is the future policy of the
Government with regard to these
Exchanges?
Mr. ANDREWS: There are 13
Employment Exchanges and 23 Branch.
Employment Offices in Northern Ireland.
The number of permanent officials in the
36 offices is 58, and of temporary officials
440. The policy of the Government
with regard to the Exchanges is under
consideration in connection with the
whole question of Unemployment
Insurance, and it is not possible at the
moment to give a considered decision in
the matter.
SPEECH OF MR. R. J. D. NUGENT.
6. Mr. WILLIAM GRANT asked the
Minister of Labour whether he would
care to comment on the speech made by
R J. D. Nugent, Secretary of the
Ancient Order of Hibernians, Insurance
Section, as reported in the "Irish News"
of September 21st?
No
Mr. ANDREWS: I have read with
interest Mr. Nugent's remarks.
suggestion has yet been made, so far as I
am aware, that the responsibility for
administering Health Insurance in
Northern Ireland should be placed on
the Government as from October 1st.
Obviously this is not a convenient date.
I should prefer not to comment on the
rest of his statements at this stage except
to say that I cannot but think that he
has overstated the difficulties involved.
and that I have reason to hope that we
can rely on the co-operation of the
Approved Societies to enable us to adjust
the Irish Health Insurance Scheme to
the requirements of the Government of
Ireland Act with as little dislocation as
is possible. I can assure my hon.
Friend that we shall endeavour to consult, as far as possible, the convenience
of Approved Societies and other interests
concerned, subject to the necessities of the
situation.
7. Mr. LYNN asked the Prime
Minister whether he is in a position to
state when the Northern Law Courts will
commence business?
The PRIME MINISTER: Saturday
next, 1st October. (Hon. Members:
"Hear, hear.")
PROTECTION FOR FUNERAL
PARTIES.
8. Mr. TWADDELL (by Private
Notice) asked the Minister for Home
Affairs whether on Wednesday, 28th
inst., a funeral party returning from the
Cemetery were ambushed at the corner
of Donegall Road, one man being killed
and three others wounded; whether any
person has been arrested in connection
with this outrage, and what steps will be
taken in future to protect the lives of
people who have to pass Sinn Fein
localities on their way to the City
Cemetery to bury their dead?
SIR DAWSON BATES: The reply to
the first part of the question I regret to
say is in the affirmative. I understand
that so far no person has been arrested.
I have been in communication with
the military and police authorities with
a view to ensuring that protection will be
afforded to funeral parties going to and
returning from the City Cemetery.

rising to-day do adjourn till Tuesday
next." [Captain Dixon.]

4 OCTOBER 1921.
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Tuesday, 4th October, 1921.
The House met at a quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Oral Answers to Questions.
THE DROMARA RAILWAY
1. Mr. McBRIDE asked the Minister
for Home Affairs whether he will state the
policy of the Government with regard to
the construction of the Dromara Railway,
seeing that the residents, the Rural Council and County Council have given their
unanimous approval to this scheme, and
whether it is a fact that the Minister of
Transport assured the deputation who met
them in London of the support of the
Imperial Government to this scheme, and
what steps the Government propose to
take in the matter?
The
PARLIAMENTARY
SECRE-
TARY ΤΟ THE MINISTRY OF
EDUCATION (Mr. M'Keown): I have
been asked to reply for my right hon.
Friend. The Government has no powers
at present for dealing with matters of this
kind, but pending the transfer of powers
the Ministry of Commerce is examining the
scheme referred to. When the time comes
the Ministry of Commerce will be prepared
to give sympathetic consideration to all
proposals for the development of the resources of the Northern Counties, having
due regard to the merits of, and the
general consideration affecting, each case.
I have no knowledge regarding the last
part of the question.
ORDERS TO THE POLICE.
2. Mr. LYNN asked the Minister for
Home Affairs whether his attention has
been drawn to a statement in the Press
to the effect that Mr. Cope, an Assistant
Under-Secretary, had issued an order to
the police that when they searched a Sinn
Fein house in Belfast they must search
a Loyalist house; when they arrested a
rebel they must also arrest a Loyalist;
whether he had reason to believe that this
order was issued, and whether he can
state if any protest has been made to the
Imperial Government by the Northern
Government.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): The reply to the first
part of the question is in tne affirmative,
but I have no official knowledge of any
such order having been issued.
As regards the remainder, the Government of
Northern Ireland is gradually establishing
its authority, and I hope tne hon. Member
will not press for particulars as to the
steps found necessary to be taken from
time to time.
Mr. LYNN: Arising out of that answer
is there any means of finding out whether
it is true or not that this order has been
issued?
Sir DAWSON BATES: My hon.
Friend should put down that question in
the Imperial House to the responsible
Minister there.
Mr. GRANT: The Minister of Home
Affairs says he has no knowledge of the
second part of the question, but I can give
him positive proof of four Loyalist people
who have been arrested.
Mr. SPEAKER: Order! order! The
hon. Member may ask a question, but cannot make a speech.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I shall be glad
if my hon. Friend will send the information in to me.
CONSPIRACY TO BOYCOTT.
3. Mr. COOPER asked the Secretary
for Home Affairs whether he is aware that
a criminal conspiracy exists in Enniskillen
to boycott Belfast goods and all persons
dealing with Belfast; that circulars, dated
10th September, 1921, have been sent to
Roman Catholics threatening them with
severe penalties if they continue to deal
with certain houses, and that these circulars contain the names of fifteen houses on
a
white list (where these people are
directed to deal), including Lipton, Ltd.;
Enniskillen Co-operative Society, Ltd.;
Tylers, Ltd.; Dicks, Ltd.; Kerr and Co.;
and J. B. Dillon, and whether, seeing that
there is some doubt if the firms in question
are parties to this intimidation or that
they have entered into this illegal conspiracy, the Secretary for Home Affairs
will communicate with these firms and get
from them definite statements, especially
as Liptons, Tylers, and Dicks do considerable business in Belfast, England,
and Scotland, that the Enniskillen Co--
operative Society has a branch at Ballinamallard and is closely allied with the Scottish Co-operative Society, Ltd., and that
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Cooper]
Mr. J. B. Dillon and Mr. J. M'Govern
(proprietor of Kerr and Co.) hold his
Majesty's Commission of the Peace, and
that Mr. Dillon also holds an important
position in the Scottish Co-operative, Ltd.,
or what action he will take in the matter?
Sir DAWSON BATES, I have seen
copies of circulars, the terms of which
accord with the statements made by the
hon. Member. I am advised that it would
be contrary to the policy of the law to
communicate with the individual firms
named, as suggested by the hon. Member,
by addressing queries which might tend to
incriminate these firms. If the hon.
Member will cause me to be furnished
with further information to prove in a
Court of Law the existence of a boycotting
conspiracy, I will have the matter brought
to the notice of the Attorney-General.
Conspiracy to boycott is a criminal offence,
and it is as well that the public should
be made aware that steps will be taken to
vindicate the law in our own Courts.
LABOUR EXCHANGE COMMITTEE.
4. Mr. DONALD asked the Minister of
Labour whether he will state for what
period the Local Employment Advisory
Committees of the Labour Exchange holds
office; how are these committees appointed; who decides the candidates;
whether, in view of the additional powers
sought by employment committees, the
general public will have a voice in the
selection of these committees ?
In
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): I understand that Local Employment Committees have not been established in Ireland up to the present.
Ireland their functions are performed by
Courts of Referees constituted by the
Minister of Labour, as prescribed in
Section 13 of the Unemployment Insurance Act, 1920. The Chairman and
Deputy-Chairman are appointed annually,
but members of the panel remain thereon
from year to year. The persons consulted
by the Ministry of Labour in the selection
of members of the panel are, as provided
in the Unemployment Insurance Acts and
Regulations, employers and insured contributors
or associations representing
such, and the selection rests with the
Minister. In regard to the last part of
the question I cannot, of course, answer
for the Minister of Labour of the Imperial
Government, but in so far as Northern
Ireland will be concerned the matter shall
have our careful consideration.
Mr. DONALD: Arising out of that
answer, I beg to give notice that on the
motion for the adjournment I will raise
this question of Labour Exchanges and
the administration thereof.
UNEMPLOYMENT.
5. Mr. GRANT asked the Minister of
Labour whether he will make a statement
on the unemployment position in Northern
Ireland before the House adjourns?
Mr. ANDREWS: If the House so desires, I shall make a statement on this
subject on the motion for adjournment.
Mr. DONALD: Arising out of that
answer, would I be in order then in making my remarks after the Minister of
Labour has made his statement to the
House?
Mr. SPEAKER: Certainly.
Mr. DONALD: Thank you.
ALLEGED INTIMIDATION AT
BELFAST DOCKS.
6. Mr. GRANT (By Private Notice)
asked the Minister of Home Affairs
whether his attention has been drawn to
the official communique issued from
the
Headquarters of
15th Infantry
Brigade, Belfast, and published in the
Belfast papers of September 27th, in
which, amongst other things, it is stated
that at about 8 a.m., Roman Catholic
dock labourers were forced to leave their
work on Queen's Quay owing to alleged
intimidation; and whether he is aware
that the Dock Workers' Union through
their Branch Secretary-Mr. W. McCosh
-published the following in the Belfast
Press of the 30th September :-
"That we, the members of No. 27 Branch of
this union, were greatly surprised to see in
your issue of Tuesday, 27th September, a report under the heading "The Official Report,"
that Roman Catholic dock labourers were
forced to leave their work on Queen's Quar
on Monday, 26th September, owing to alleged
intimidation.
"There was no such thing happened as far
as our members were concerned, as we always
try to work agreeably together."
Whether he will make representations in
the proper quarters to prevent the publication of untrue statements of this kind
which are calculated to increase the
unrest, and not allay the trouble which
at present exists in this city.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Strong representations have already been made by the
Prime Minister on this subject, it is
hoped with good effect.
255 Private Bill Procedure.
Interpretation Bill. 256
4 ОСТОВЕН 1921.
Private Bill Procedure.
The PRIME MINISTER: The House
will remember that last week I stated in
answer to a question that, as soon as the
arrangements for Private Bill Procedure
were completed, the dates would be
announced on which promoters
of
Private Bills could deposit their petitions.
In order to bring the matter a stage
nearer completion, I am now formally
laying on the Table of the House draft
Standing Orders which will govern the
procedure regarding Private Bills. My
object in laying the document on the
Table is that the draft Orders may be
printed so as to be available for consideration when we next meet. I may
say that it is proposed by the Authorities
of both Houses to follow the United
Kingdom procedure as has been done in
the case of Public Standing Orders. In
particular I would mention, so as to give
the earliest possible intimation, that the
Westminster dates will be followed, at
any rate for the coming Session. These
dates are so arranged as to require
notices to be given, advertisements to be
published, and plans and Bills to be
deposited during the period from October
to the close of the year, in order that
Parliament, when it meets in the New
Year, may find that the preliminaries
have been duly carried out, and that Bills
are ready for consideration. The most
important difference between the Orders
which are proposed to be framed, and
those which are in force at Westminster,
has been already announced. I refer to
the proposal to adopt a joint procedure
on the part of both Houses when a Bill
reaches the Committee stage. When the
text of the draft Orders is circulated,
Members will be able to study it during
the adjournment, so that when we next
meet the details can be discussed and
amendments made in the draft where
necessary.
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
Resolved: "That notwithstanding any
Standing Order or custom of the House,
the Interpretation Bill (S.) be passed
through all its remaining stages at this
day's Sitting."-[Captain Dixon.]
(6
Interpretation Bill [S].
Order for Second Reading read.
Motion made and Question proposed:
That the Bill be read the Second Time."
The
or
ATTORNEY-GENERAL (Mr.
Best): This Bill comes from the Senate
where it has passed through all its stages,
and it now becomes my duty to move the
Second Reading. The object of the Bill
is to apply to Northern Ireland the
Interpretation Act of 1889. That is an
Act for consolidating enactments relating
to the construction of Acts of Parliament, and for further shortening the
language in Acts of Parliament. (Hon.
Members: "Hear, hear.") The Act of
1889 is an existing law within the meaning of Section 61 of the Government of
Ireland Act, and it therefore applies in
Northern Ireland to the interpretation of
the Acts of the United Kingdom Parliament, whether in the Courts of law
otherwise. It could not, however, be held
to apply automatically to Acts passed by
the Parliament of Northern Ireland, and,
therefore, we require this Act so that
when we have a code of our laws, and
when those laws come to be applied, we
can apply them and interpret them by
means of the Interpretation Act of 1889.
The Act is also useful, as I have stated,
for shortening Acts of Parliament, and I
am sure right hon. and hon. Gentlemen
who are in favour of economy will be very
glad to shorten these Acts of Parliament
and cut down printing as far as possible.
(Hon. Members: "Hear, hear.")
the Members of this House will have
observed from the Order Papers that we
are asked to take all the stages of the Bill
to-day. That has been done with a view
to the convenience of hon. Members who
have given their assent to that course.
I do not think that there are any further
observations necessary for me to make.
If this Bill were an Interpretation Bill
brought in for the first time it might be
necessary to argue the matter, but the
Interpretation Act of 1889 has been
found so very convenient-having been in
use now for over thirty years-that it is
too late now to argue as to its necessity.
I might just say that a very celebrated
Parliamentary Counsel made use of this
expression-he advised every draughtsman to learn the Interpretation Act by
heart; it was so important, because,
by applying its provisions Acts could be
Now

Interpretation Bill.

shortened and could be clarified to such
an extent that it was of supreme importance that it should be applied in
every possible instance. I beg to move
the Second Reading.
Question: ""That the Bill be read the
Second Time," put, and agreed to.

[Mr. MOLES in the Chair.]

Adjournment of the House.
THE PRIME MINISTER'S STATE-
MENT.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That this House, at its rising this
day, do adjourn till Tuesday, December
13th, provided always that if it appears to
the satisfaction of Mr. Speaker, after consultation with the Government, that the
public interest requires that the House
should meet at any earlier time during
the adjournment, Mr. Speaker may give
notice that he is so satisfied, and thereupon the House shall meet at the time
stated in such notice, and shall transact
its business as if it had been duly
adjourned to that time."
The PRIME MINISTER: In moving
the motion that appears on the paper,
that this House at its rising do adjourn
until Tuesday, December 13th, I wish to
say that we have selected that day for
two reasons. At one time we thought
that it might meet the convenience of the
House if we would meet rather earlier, and
then, on consideration of the outlook as it
presents itself to us to-day, we thought it
would be more agreeable to the House as
a whole to adjourn till the later date,
with the power reserved, as is mentioned
in the motion, of calling the
calling the House
together in case developments arose such
as you, Sir, after consultation with the
Government, thought would justify us in
having a meeting of Parliament specially
to discuss whatever difficulties had arisen.
I think that the House will realise that it
is better not only for individual Members but also for the Government, to
reserve this power of calling the House
together. It is somewhat unusual, but
the prospect before us seems also equally
unusual (Hon. Members:
"Hear,
hear.") and no man can tell what the
next few months may bring forth.
would
certainly
feel very much
strengthened by the knowledge that if an
untoward event
at any time I
would have the power at any rate to call
this House together, so that through the
House the feeling of the whole Ulster
community might be voiced on any proposals that are laid before us.
arose
I
The position at the moment is as we!!
known to Members here and to the public
as it is to myself. I have received no
further invitation to a Conference in
London, or elsewhere, and it may be that
I will receive no such invitation in the
near future. But this resolution will
guard against everything that is unexpected, and it gives me, as I say, all
additional strength if I know that I can
immediately consult not only my
colleagues of the Cabinet but also my
colleagues in this House. Therefore I
commend this motion to the House.
I hope that they will feel that we have
done what we could in the matter of
I did n
meeting their convenience.
year.
think it was safe to run much nearer to
Christmas, because we may have-we
will have some business to perform, and
we would like to prorogue before Christmas, and to keep this year's Parliamentary life entirely comprised in the
year, and not carry it over into the new
When we meet on the 13th
December we will be in a better position
to judge as to how long the prorogation
should be for. It may be for a long
time, or it may be for a short time, but
at all events we will keep an open mind
in the matter, and we will at all times
study the feelings and convenience of the
Members of this House. Therefore, Sir,
in moving this, I hope that it will meet
with the approval of all the Members
259 Debate on Adjournment. 4 OCTOBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment.

the Prime Ministers of Ulster are to have
for all time an official residence, which
will get known in much the same way as
the Americans talk about White
House Therefore it is part of Ulster
for all time. I would like to take this
opportunity of expressing our warmest
thanks to the British Government-for
after all it is a matter for the British
Government and for the British Cabinet
and the Chancellor of the Exchequer to
decide upon-for having paid the compliment to Ulster of handing over this
building, or that portion of it, for all
time for successive Prime Ministers.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.") The
Office of Works, I may say, from the very
start of the obligation which rested upon
it to carry out what is laid down in the
Act, have met us, as I think all the
Members will recognise, with the utmost
courtesy, and with both hands in assisting us in every way that they could to
get established in our new Parliamentary
institutions.
May I
take the opportunity at the
same time of announcing to the House
that the First Commissioner of Works
has intimated his approval of Stormont
as a site for the Parliamentary
and
Ministerial buildings, and further, that
the Chancellor of the Exchequer has
offered a portion of the existing building
to be handed over as an official residence
for successive Prime Ministers of
Northern Ireland, the remainder of the
present building being used for departmental purposes.
I would like to add
with regard to those two announcements,
that the Office of Works has therefore
approved of the site, which was unanimously adopted by both the Members
of the Senate and of this House. The
benefit of that is that at an early date
it will be possible to supply to the
authorities here the plans for the development of the estate, and to co-operate
with the City Corporation on the one
hand, and the County Down authorities
on the other, with a view to bringing
the tram lines and roads into proper coordination with whatever development of
the site Stormont may require inside its
own boundaries. There is at the moment
under consideration the undertaking of
that particular site development by une
Office of Works itself, which I personally
favour, because it appears to me to be
the most ready method of getting almost
instantly to work. What we are aiming
at in this is to provide occupation for the
unemployed-(hon. Members: "Hear!
hear!") to get actually into the work
as rapidly as possible, and to absorb as
many of the out-of-work as possible, and
especially those ex-Service men who are
now in a very sad state.
I can only assure the House that, from
the first day the First Commissioner
came over to visit those various sites, and
to talk over prospects with us, we have
been indefatigable in pressing on to get
down to the actual work which will have
to be performed by those who have to
develop and those who have to do the
work upon the building and upon the
ground. We shall continue to keep a
careful eye upon that matter.
The second statement with regard to
the utilization of portion of Stormont
Castle itself for the successive Prime
Ministers of Ulster will, I think, give
general satisfaction to the Members of
the House and to the country, because
they have recognised at all events-I am
not speaking in any personal sense-that
I
Mr. ANDREWS: There appears to be
a general wish amongst hon. Members that
I should make a statement to-day in reference to the question of unemployment,
and that I should, at the same time, take
advantage of the opportunity to refer to
the question of Labour Exchanges.
recognise that in a single speech of reasonable length it would be impossible for me
adequately to deal with this, the greatest
of all social and commercial problems with
which we in Northern Ireland are faced.
We all know that unemployment in our
midst is very general, and we all realise
that, unfortunately, suffering due to it is
very general. I feel that no man who has
a heart can do otherwise at the present
time than stop and think. I think I had
best begin my remarks by placing before
hon. Members some figures indicating the
extent to which unemployment exists in
our midst. Approximately the figure for
Northern Ireland of those who are wholly
unemployed is 78,000, and those who are
on short time 25,000, while in Belfast
alone 45,000 are wholly unemployed, and
12,000 are on short time. These figures
are appalling; but there is another aspect
of the case to which I would like to direct
the attention of this House. In the
United Kingdom, in June last, there was
a percentage of 17.81 unemployed people.
while in Northern Ireland the percentage
was 25.40; a month later-in July-the
figure for the United Kingdom was 14.80.
whilst for Northern Ireland it was 23.20.
R
261 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 262
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Andrews.]
The
Again, in August the last figures
available were the 26th of August-the
figure for the United Kingdom was 13.15,
whilst for Northern Ireland it was 21.62.
Now it will be realised from these figures
that the problem with which this House is
faced is more serious in dimensions than
even the problem for the whole kingdom.
Those figures are based upon an insured
population for the United Kingdom of
twelve millions and for Northern Ireland
of about a quarter of a million.
reason that the figures show so much
against Northern Ireland is due to the
fact that two of our chief industries,
namely, the great shipbuilding industry
and our staple trade-the linen industryhave been hit to an unparalleled extent
at the same time. I am sorry to say that
those with whom I have spoken, and who
are well informed as to the state of the
trade in the shipbuilding industry, take
rather a gloomy view of the position, and
I am afraid that that great industry in
this city and elsewhere has many dark
days to contend with still. As regards
the linen industry, for many reasons I am
very glad to be able to tell this House that
the position is much better. (Hon. Members: "
'Hear, hear.") I am quite confident that within a very few weeks almost
all those factories and mills, which have
been entirely stopped since the beginning
of the year, will have resumed work to
some extent. (Hon. Members: "Hear,
hear.") In addition to that I am convinced that those concerns, which have
been working upon short time, will be in
a position to increase their production to
a larger extent than has been the case for
many months. Now there are two rocks
ahead of the linen industry. One is that
there is undoubtedly throughout the world
a great shortage of raw material; and the
other is that our friends in America seem
to be desirous of placing an additional
tariff upon linen goods, which, if persisted
in, would, no doubt, have a very disastrous effect upon the staple trade of this
province of ours. But the Ministry of
Commerce-it is only right that I should
say so have taken this matter up, and
are making strong representations to those
in authority in the United States in order
to endeavour to prevent this additional--
what I may call super-tax-being placed
upon linen goods entering the United
States of America.
Now there is another aspect of the case
to which I would like to direct the attenupon
ten.
tion of hon. Members. We have heard
a great deal about unemployment benefit,
and we have seen in the papers reports
of various kinds setting out what the position of the Fund is at the present time.
I think I should tell the House that in
1920 the scale of contributions and
benefits was agreed upon to produce a
solvent fund with a percentage of only 5.3
of unemployment. The last Act-or, in
other words, the revised scale-was based
percentage
a
of
Now
have pointed out to-day that
the percentage in Northern Ireland
is 21.62 at the present time, SO
that it shows that the percentage of unemployment in our area is more than
double that which has been provided for
by the Act. In 1920 there was standing
to the credit of the Unemployment Fund
of the United Kingdom the sum of 22
millions, while the position to-day is that
that 22 millions has been exhausted entirely, and five millions have been borrowed
from the Treasury in order that benefits
might be continued to the extent that
they are being continued at the present
time. Now there are those who hold that
all that money has been wasted. I am
quite sure that no hon. Member takes that
view. (Hon. Members: "Hear, hear.")
We all recognise that this money has been
given on a contributory basis in order to
prevent starvation entering many homes:
and we all know also that the distribution
of this money to those who were willing
and anxious to work, but who, through
no fault of their own, were unable to
obtain it, has prevented active revolution
probably in the country. Therefore I
think that those who, at the present time,
talk thoughtlessly about the waste there
has been, and extravagance in connection
with this matter, should take stock of the
position afresh. It is strange that there
is so much talk about the wasting of this
money, and yet so few people seem to
direct their attention to the fact that
thousands of millions have been lost to
this country by reason of the fact that so
many consumers have remained necessarily
consumers while ceasing to produce.
I think here in Northern Ireland it is
up to us to do everything possible in order
to get work for our people. (Hon. Members: "Hear, hear.") And I may say
that the Ministry, over which I have the
honour for the time being to preside, are
doing everything they possibly can in that
direction. We are in the closest and most
friendly touch with the Ministry of
263 Debate on Adjournment.
4 OCTOBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment. 264
un-
Labour of the United Kingdom. We are
also in close association with our public
bodies. The City of Belfast, as I have
already stated in this House, has shown
a splendid example in this direction by
finding work for a great many
employed people, and I am very hopeful
that our county authorities are going to
follow suit and do all they possibly can.
There has been a little confusion as to the
question, to which I referred, in reference
to Road Board grants. When I said that
County Down had received about £48,000
from Road Board grants, and other
grants of a somewhat similar character, I
meant that they had received that from
the old fund. The new fund is still untouched, and it is available for all County
Councils who are willing to undertake
work of reconstruction. There is some
little delay in connection with its distribution, due to the fact that Dublin
Castle in some way seems to lay claim to
hold it for a time. Well, they cannot
hold it for very long-(Hon. Members:
"Hear, hear)-and then it will be available. I am very anxious that it should
be made available for this reason-the old
grants in connection with road construction are almost exhausted, in other words,
the work which they were intended to do
is almost completed. The necessity for
getting the new grants is that the work
may be continued and that men may be
kept in employment who otherwise would
be deprived of the opportunity of earning
an honest livelihood.
Now, I think I ought to tell hon.
Members something about the machinery
that the Ministry of Labour is bringing
into existence here in our own midst for
the purpose of dealing with this unemployment question and other problems
affecting Labour. The
Labour. The work of the
Ministry, as I take it, is primarily that
of negotiation, and conciliation, and arbitration in order to preserve, in the first
place, where possible, commercial peace,
and, in the second place, when troubles
have occurred, to bind up the wounds as
quickly as possible, and get the people
back to work. I think I may claim that in
this connection we have been particularly
fortunate up to the present. We have
prevented a considerable number of
strikes occurring, which, I believe, would
have occurred but for the intervention of
the Labour Ministry. (Hon. Members:
"Hear, hear.") We have been also
fortunate in ending two strikes, one of
which, at any rate, was doing serious.
injury to our province. The building
strike, as you all know, paralysed the
building trade, and to a large extent
injured allied industries for a period of
seven months, and it is a source of gratification to me and those associated with me
to know that work is now going on in the
building trade, and that all those who
were unemployed in consequence of the
strike are now fully and usefully engaged.
Next there is the question of Trade
Boards. As you probably all know, the
Trade Boards were brought into being
to bring employers and employed together
in order that they might settle, with the
help of three appointed members, wages
questions and questions of conditions of
employment. In the first instance they
were intended only to apply to sweated
industries, and in that connection undoubtedly they did very useful work, and
it is claimed that the wages were largely.
increased in these particular industries,
and yet the trade of the firms affected was
not injured to any extent. But Trade
Boards have been worked into other industries where it is claimed they are not
required. That may or may not be so.
It is apparent to me that their machinery
is too cumbersome and too slow. (Hon.
Members: "Hear, hear.") Hon. Members may have observed that the Minister
of Labour for the United Kingdom has
appointed a Committee under the chairmanship of Lord Cave to inquire into the
question of Trade Boards for the Kingdom. But we have special difficulties to
contend with here, and special conditions
to reckon with, and, therefore, with the
approval of the Prime Minister, I propose to appoint a Committee to inquire
into the question of Trade Boards here in
our
own midst, and I have been
particularly fortunate in getting the
Speaker of the Senate, Lord Dufferin,
whose impartiality is beyond question, to
act as Chairman of that Committee. The
Committee will be composed of leading
employers and leading trade unionists, and
I hope they will be able to begin their
work at no distant date.
Then, I pass on to the question of
Labour Exchanges. Labour Exchanges
have been referred to by the hon. Member for East Belfast (Mr. Donald) today. I may tell him that in connection
with these Labour Exchanges I have still
an open mind. It is perfectly true that
265 Debate on Adjournment. HOUSE OF COMMONS
Debate on Adjournment. 266
[Mr. Andrews.]
they have not, to the extent that it was
hoped, discharged the duty for which
they were primarily brought into
existence (Hon. Members: "Hear,
hear') but I would like to correct, if I
may, a statement which the hon. Member for West Belfast (Mr. Lynn) made in
this House a few days ago. I think I
remember him saying that to get a job
through the Labour Exchange cost the
country about £20.
Mr. LYNN: Quite right.
Mr. ANDREWS: I am inclined to
think that the hon. Member has been
reading the "Daily Mail," and it is not
always reliable. (Hon. Members:
"Hear, hear.") I think if he had stated
32/6 instead of £20 he would have been
a great deal nearer the mark.
I may
tell him that the gentleman who took up
this propaganda work in the "Daily
Mail" against Labour Exchanges was
asked to appear before a Committee of
Inquiry into the subject, but could not
be got up to the scratch. Mr. Harold
Cox was also identified with this attack
upon Labour Exchanges, and he did.
come forward and gave evidence before
the Committee, but I understand that it
is on record that he did not produce one
single fact in order to give proof of the
statements that had been made.
But you
see the Labour Exchanges are at the
present time discharging a great many
duties in addition to the work of finding
employment for the unemployed.
think we should remember this-that
though to some extent, they have disappointed their designers, they do find
annually, on an average, about a million
positions for those who are out of employment, and a million is no small number,
and I think that when these Exchanges are
being condemned, these figures should not
be forgotten. I may say that it will
very probably be necessary for us here in
Northern Ireland also to bring together a
Committee not only to inquire into the
working of these Exchanges, but also into
the question of unemployment benefits
and such like matters.
I
I do not think it will be necessary for
me to detain the House any longer, but I
simply want to say this-I hope hon.
Members will be patient with me-(Hon.
Members: "Hear, hear')--and give me
a sporting chance to discharge the duties
of a pretty difficult position. I am told
very frequently that I am bound to fall
between two stools-Labour on the one
hand and Capital on the other
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Not a bit of you.
Mr. ANDREWS: To be perfectly
frank, I am neither disheartened, dismayed, nor discouraged, because I believe
in the good sense of my fellow-countrymen in Northern Ireland. I believe they
all desire the welfare of their country.
Of one thing I can assure hon. Members,
and it is this-that if I feel I am not
succeeding, I will be the first to say, I
go," because I have no ambition to
gratify in this connection beyond that I
desire to serve this new Ulster of ours if
I have the power, to bring a little light
and a little hope into those homes where
little exists at the present time. I met
a friend of mine the other day, whose
friendship I value greatly, and he said:
"Johnnie Andrews, I am inclined to
think you are somewhat of a dangerous
man.' Well, if giving expression to the
views to which I have given expression
to-day constitutes a dangerous man then,
Mr. SPEAKER, I willingly admit that the
charge is true.
Mr. DONALD: I am sure that this
House appreciates very much the speech
of my right hon. Friend. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.") May I say
at the very outset that he will find no
more loyal colleague than myself to back
him up in the very heavy duties he has
taken upon himself as Minister of Labour.
I am not going to criticise either the
Ministry of Labour or the Labour Exchanges. I rise rather to strengthen the
hands of the Minister of Labour and
that department with regard to the administration of its Exchanges. My right
hon. Friend said that we had no Local
Employment Committees in Belfast.
may say for the information of this House
that Belfast was the first to introduce
what is called the L.E.C., the Local
I
Employment Committee. In putting
forward my views to-day, I want to help
the Ministry of Labour out of my lifelong experience of the Trade Union
movement, and my experience goes back
to the time when Mr. Bowman first introduced the Local Employment Committee in Belfast, with the result that
those Local Committees have now been
adopted throughout the country. My
right hon. Friend said that another body
called the Court of Referees dealt with
the question of administration of benefits
Debate on Adjournment. 268
Debate on Adjournment.
4 OCTOBER 1921.
and so forth in Belfast and throughout
the Six Counties. May I say that it is
entirely outside of the jurisdiction of this
Court of Referees to do anything of the
kind. This Court of Referees is purely
a statutory body, and my reason for
raising this question to-day is that I want
not only the taxpayers' money but also
the workpeople's money safeguarded.
I say here and now that I have information that the chairman of this Referees'
Court, who is usually a local magistrate,
signs these documents in hundreds without scrutinising them at all. I want a
Local Employment Committee set up in
Belfast such as previously existed. 1
acted on the first Labour Exchange
Advisory Committee for Ireland. I want
these Local Committees set up at once.
Remember, this is a voluntary Committee--the Labour Ministry supplies
the secretary, and an independent chairman is appointed-on the lines indicateu
by my right hon. Friend, consisting of
employers and representatives of the
organisations, including the Trades
Union. The functions of this body will
be, have been, and ought to be, the same
as on the other side of the water, to
scrutinise every claim for benefit.
Now, what is the position to-day? The
taxpayers' money is being paid out in this
country regardless of what the result may
be. May I just confirm mr statement
with regard to these Local Committees ?
Dealing with these Local Employment
Committees on the Second Reading ot
the Unemployment Insurance Bill this
year, Dr. Macnamara, the Minister of
Labour in the Imperial House, said:
I turned to the Local Employment Committees
-bodies to whose patience and tireless public
service I desire to pay tribute-and asked
them to assist me, in these exceptional
circumstances, by examining the claims of
those who would seek benefit under Clause 3.
Further:
Every care should be taken to see that no
reproach lies against those of us charged to
administer it that, by our laxity, by our
failure to apply the reasonable tests it prescribes, the contributions of hard-working
people, of the employers, and of the State,
are unnecessarily and improperly dissipated.
Further he said:
The experience of the Local Employment
Committees in working the Act during the
last two months places them in a good
position for judging whether there has, in
fact, been such abuse, and for suggesting
appropriate remedies for any abuse beyond
those contained in the instructions issued
by the Department.
Leaving out Ireland-in Ireland, of
course, money is allowed to be disbursed
improperly-Dr. Macnamara, in dealing
with these Committees said:
I have received, and have now before me,
a very valuable series of communications,
which of themselves testify to the public
service which these Local Employment Committees have devoted to this problem.
We know what these Committees have
done across the water. That is why 1
want them set up here. Out of over
600,000 claims across the water they
have rejected 50,000. In the city of
Belfast to-day you never hear of a solitary claim being rejected.
Now, I want to refer to another aspect
of this question. In reply to a question
the other day my right hon. Friend said
that we had some 58 permanent `officials
and 440 temporary officials in these Exchanges. For the life of me I never
could understand why two-thirds of the
permanent officials, and three-fourths of
the temporary officials, in the Six
Counties are Sinn Feiners. I want this
Committee set up because I know that
in a great many cases the claims of loyal
people are refused, but there is no refusal
of the claims of Sinn Feiners. What do
we find to-day? In this country of ours
to-day we find that the British Government, who are continually condemning
Sinn Fein and the I.R.A., are actually
paying these people for what they are
doing in Ireland. I mean that over
50,000 people in Ireland to-day are
drawing money from the Labour Bureaux
and are going to the hills and drilling
as members of the Irish Republican
Army. That is the chief reason why I
want this Committee set up at once, and
it can be set up at once. I speak in the
interests not only of the taxpayers but of
the workers, and I want to tell my right
hon. Friend that we are not going to
take over a body of Sinn Feiners into this
Ulster Parliament. I never could understand why when the Labour Exchanges
wanted twenty officials, and a majority
of loyal applicants put in applications,
fifteen out of the twenty appointed were
Sinn Feiners.
Mr. LYNN: More.
Mr. DONALD: I put it moderately.
Those are the facts. My right hon.
Friend dealt with the unemployment
question. Let me give this House some
idea of what the Minister of Labour in
the Imperial Parliament asked for in the
new Act that is now on the Statute Book.
He said:
