from her own constituencey with regard to
this question. I have a huge pile of letters
from different parts of Ulster, and I can
say that from no county did I receive
more letters than from the county of
Londonderry, protesting against the expenditure in which we indulged last September. Now to get to the facts. I have
listened to every speech that has been
made to-day. I have been listening for
convincing arguments as to why I should
vote in favour of this Report. I have
been treated to eloquence; I have received no arguments. Therefore I feel
I must vote for the amendment, though
the amendment is not as drastic as I should
like it to be. I cannot blame the Com-
Inittee for the fact.
With regard to the Parliamentary
Secretaries, the Members of this House
know that I have protested on every
occasion against them, and I have objected
because it is an innovation. There is not
a single Parliament in the Empire, except
the Imperial Parliament, that has got
Parliamentary Secretaries. I know the
Prime Minister hauled me up, but he
dropped into the unfortunate mistake that
Deputy Ministers in the Colonies were
Parlimentary Secretaries. Deputy
Ministers in the Colonies are simply the
official heads of the Departments, and are
described as Deputy Ministers because
they are not Parliamentary Secretaries
and are not Members of the Parliament.
I say to-day, as I have said on several
occasions in this House, that there is no
necessity whatever for Parliamentary
Secretaries in the Northern Parliament.
I have gone further. I have said that a
small community like ours, only a fraction
larger than the city of Glasgow, does not
need a Ministry of six Cabinet Ministers,
and eight or nine-I think it is nine-
Parliamentary Secretaries. Why the
thing is too absurd. The Ministry as it
stands to-day is big enough to run
Empire instead of a small province. Do
not let us forget what our revenue is
going to be.
an
The Prime Minister, the day before
yesterday, pointed out to us that it would
be necessary to increase the Police Force
very largely. I should fancy that when
Members of this House see the figures relating to the cost of the Police Force they
will get a very serious shock. They will
find those figures a great deal larger than
they imagined, and we see no prospect at
the moment that those figures are going to
be reduced, but we see every prospect that
they are going to be largely increased. I
agree with the Prime Minister that even
if we are going bankrupt it is necessary to
provide a Police Force that will maintain
law and order in this province, because if
we cannot maintain law and order then
we might as well close up this Parliament
and hoist the white flag. The cost of the
police will increase. The cost of education
is also going to increase very largely, and
increase soon. Other services, I am
afraid, will increase too. Will hon.
Members who are anxious to vote these
large sums of money away tell me where
the increased revenue is going to come
from? I think my hon. Friend (Mr.
Barbour) or the Chancellor of the Exchequer said that the revenue for the Six
Counties was something like £12,000,000
last year.
I am not pinning myself down
to the figures, but assuming that the
figures are £12,000,00, we have to contribute £8,000,000 towards the Imperial Exchequer, and that leaves us a very small
margin.
The hon. Member for Londonderry
(Mrs. Chichester) says that this thing will
not entail additional taxation. Where
is the money to come from? Is there some
Ꮓ
Remuneration. 376
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
I
[Mr. Lynn.]
wonderful bottomless purse into which
the Chancellor of the Exchequer can dip
his hand and get out whatever money is
necessary ? If there is I wish he would
give me the hint where it is, and allow me
to operate on it even for a few minutes.
I may be old-fashioned, I may be prosaic,
but I am afraid extra taxation will be
necessary, and that extra taxation will
have to be paid by the citizens. After all,
it is on the individuals you levy the taxation, and they have got to pay whatever
the extra cost is. We should start by
pruning down everywhere it is necessary.
agree with the hon. Member for Londonderry that we should not exercise false
economy. You might exercise false
economy, for instance, in education.
There are other things in which you might
exercise false economy. Everything has
to be decided on its merits, but I say you
will not reduce the efficiency of this
Ministry by adopting the amendment that
my hon. Friend, the Member for Tyrone
and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) has proposed.
I agree with him that so far as the Prime
Minister is concerned we ought not to interfere with the figures that have been
laid down by the Committee. The Prime
Minister, as the official head of this State,
will have enormous expenditure, and he
ought not to be put to any personal cost.
It is right and fitting that he should
uphold the dignity of this office. My
hon. colleague, the Member for West
Belfast (Mr. Twaddell) pointed to the
Colonies, and he gave quite correctly the
total figures for some of the Dominions.
Take, for instance, Australia. Why
Australia is a Continent. True its population is not large, but its area is as
large, if not larger than the whole of
Ireland. What has happened with regard
to Australia? It is laid down in the Act
constituting the Commonwealth
Australia, that the amount to be paid for
the salaries of Ministers is not to be more
than £15,300. £15,300 to be paid for the
salaries of the Ministers of a Continent.
£21,000 to be paid for the salaries of a
Ministry that is to administer a province
with a population the size of Glasgow.
Do hon. Members not see how absurd and
ridiculous it is to try to super-impose an
Imperial organisation on a small province? That is exactly what we are doing.
With regard to Canada, to which my hon.
colleague and Friend referred, I do not
think he mentioned Ontario, for example.
If he takes the Province of Ontario he will
find the population is something like double
of
-I am speaking from memory--that of
Ulster, or about that. I notice that in
Ontario the Cabinet Ministers have to be
efficient, they have to maintain the dignity
of their offices, and they must have those
brains--those special brains that are
necessary for Cabinet Ministers, all for
£1,200 a year. My hon. Friend, the
Member for Londonderry (Mrs.
Chichester), referred to the cost of living.
I have yet to learn that the cost of living,
even in Belfast, is higher than it is in
Toronto, or than it is in Melbourne, or
in any of the Colonies. Why, we know
as a matter of fact that the cost of living
in the Colonies is much higher than it is
at home. If anyone doubts that, I advise
him before discussing it, to refer to the
Board of Trade figures on the subject. In
New Zealand, which is much larger in area
than the whole of Ireland, the Cabinet
Ministers only get £1,300. Surely if we
here agree to £1,500, when New Zealand
only pays £1,300-and the cost of living
is, I think, as high in New Zealand as it is
here I think you might be well satisfied.
One thing I do dread is the turning of
too many Members of this House into officials of the Government. I do not think
it is a healthy thing that too many Members of the House should be under control
of the Government by occupying Government positions, because a one with any
acquaintance with constitutional affis
knows that as a member of a Ministry there
are only two courses open to him. He has
either got to support his Ministry, whether
it is right or wrong, or resign. It is sometimes very difficult to support a Ministry.
I would find it very difficult, for instance,
to support my right hon. Friends opposite
in regard to financial matters at the pre
sent time. Yet it is very difficult to resign,
and very difficult to create what may be
looked upon outside as a division in your
own party. A healthy Parliament should
always be able to keep a control over the
administration, but what we are gravitating towards at the present time is a strong
Ministry that will hold this Parliament in
the hollow of its hand. I for one refuse to
accept a position like that. I say our business is to do the very best we can for the
ratepayers. There is no Member of this
House who is more anxious, or who has
done more in a quiet way to help Ministers.
or who is willing to do more in the future.
than I have or am, but I am not going to
follow them blindly and vote for anything
they say.
When a Minister puts pen to
paper and says "So-and-so is right," I am
not going to accept it as if it were the Ten
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
Commandments engraved on stone. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) I am going to
criticise it. I say in this case the figures
put forward are too high, that we should
come at least to a Colonial level, and that
we should try and do the best we can in
the terrible conditions through which we
are passing. My hon. Friend the Member
for Derry (Mrs. Chichester) referred to the
conditions which exist elsewhere. Think of
the burden of rates that rests upon the
people of Belfast at the present time. I
think the rates in Belfast to-day are over
17/- in the pound, and what they will be
next year I do not know. What the rates
are for the Province generally hon. Members for the different counties know, but I
know that protests are appearing in the
Press every day with regard to these rates.
Is this a time for indulging in unnecessary
expenditure? If it really was necessary to
spend this £21,000 I for one would say,
"do it at once," but I say it is not necessary, and I say you can cut your
salaries down by over one-half of what the
Committee has recommended. I intend to
go into the Division Lobby and vote against
accepting this Report, and I also want to
say that so far as I am concerned I am
going to fight this thing from now until it
reaches the Statute Book, if it ever gets
there.
Mr. ROBB: I rise to say just one or two
words. I might have been prepared to support the amendment that has been proposed if the proposer had included in it a
direction to the Committee, in taking back
the Report which it has presented to the
House, an instruction to them that the
salary of the Attorney-General should have
been increased. I do not quarrel with the
general scheme of salaries the Committee
has proposed except in the one particular
that I think the salary that has been proposed for the Attorney-General of Northern Ireland is entirely insufficient. He represents a great profession, and by his position he is acknowledged as the head of a
great profession, and it does not seem to
me that the Committee has either followed
precedent or dealt with the matter on its
merits. If they had followed precedent
they would have found that in no case is
the salary of the Attorney-General less
than the salary of the corresponding
Cabinet Minister in any part of the United
Kingdom. The Attorney-General for England receives a salary of £7,000 a year, and
gets fees which last year amounted to over
£8,000, and he has as an assistant the
Solicitor-General. If you take Scotland,
the Secretary for Scotland, who is the political head of the administration in that
country, has a salary of £2,000 a year, and
the Lord Advocate, who is in the same
position as our Attorney-General, receives
£5,000 a year. If you take Ireland, the
Attorney-General for Ireland has a salary
of £5,000 a year, with the right of private
practice, and the Chief Secretary for Ireland, who is the Cabinet Minister for that
Department, receives £4,334 a year. The
salary proposed by this Committee for the
Attorney-General for Northern Ireland is a
salary of £1,500 a year, less by £500 than
the salary of a Cabinet Minister. I cannot
see any justification whatever for that. The
Committee have also excluded the
Attorney-General for Northern
land from the receipt of
fees for contentious business.
Ireany
They
say that the Attorney-General for
Northern Ireland is to be allowed to continue his private practice. It is not proposed, I understand, that the Minister of
Finance shall cease to be a director of companies, or that the Financial Secretary to
the Ministry of Finance shall cease to deal
with any of the large businesses with which
he is concerned, or that the Minister of
Labour shall cease to be a flax spinner, or
that the Minister of Home Affairs shall
cease to be a solicitor. It is
answer to the demand that
no
the salary of the Attorney-General
shall be equal to that of a Cabinet
Minister to say that he has a private practice when each of these Ministers is allowed
to continue his business. None of them is
excluded from continuing the work on
which he has been hitherto engaged, and
consequently it is no answer to say that the
Attorney-General is allowed to continue his
private practice. If there is any recommitment of this Report to the Committee
I would wish that the Committee should be
empowered to reconsider the salary of the
Attorney-General with the intention of increasing it to what it ought to be.
Mr. LLOYD CAMPBELL: I am sorry
that the hon. Member for West Belfast
(Mr. Lynn) has left the House, for I wanted
to refer to what he said, and to say that
all the criticism passed upon the Report of the Committee, of which I am a
member, was looked upon, I am sure, by
every one of that Committee as honest,
friendly criticism, and that there is nothing
personal in the matter at all. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I would like to go back,
if I may, on part of the subject. This
House appointed a Committee to draw up
this Report. When the Committee was
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Lloyd Campbell.]
called together we found that every one of
us was without any Parliamentary experi
enos whatever, and I am extremely glad
indeed to hear from the hon. Member for
West Belfast (Mr. Lynn) that it was because he was not able, and not because he
was unwilling, that he did not take up work
on the Committee. I think, considering
that we were all absolute novices
at procedure-I do not know whether I
am in order or not, but if not, I hope it
will be overlooked-that we were very
lucky indeed in having as Secretary of that
Committee Mr. Hotham. We are indebted
to him for keeping us right in everything
connected with procedure. I would not
have taken this opportunity of thanking
him had it not been for a remark which one
hon. Member made. It is a remark which
I personally take as a great slur on anybody
connected with the Committee.
The remark was that apparently the members of
the Committee had been influenced by some
old Parliamentary hand. I hope that we
were above such things, and I want to make
it perfectly clear-in case anybody night
take out of it that our Secretary was in any
way to blame-I want to make it perfectly
clear to the House that he had nothing
whatever to say to this Rort, except to
follow our instructions.
Mr. COOTE: On a point of order, nobody on this side of the House said that
he had. I never had the Secretary of the
Committee in my mind at all
I simply
threw out the suggestion. To imply conduct like that to your Secretary was never
in my mind at all.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I did not even know
who he was.
Mr. LLOYD CAMPBELL: I am not
accusing anybody.
Mr. COOTE: You are accusing me.
Mr. LLOYD CAMPBELL: I am saying
the accusation was thrown out that the
Committee was influenced by an old Parliamentary hand. The only person of experience there was our secretary, and therefore I want to make it perfectly clear to
the public. As regards the debate which
has taken place, I am not going to touch
upon the questions raised, but there has
been a great deal of criticism arising out
of this Clause 13. It seems rather strange
this number 13 cropping up so often.
think it was the hon. Member for Derry
(Sir Robert Anderson) who said that somebody must have been inspired. I had not
intended to make this statement, but I am
I
forced to do it by the criticism which has
taken place. If there is anything wrong
in principle in Clause 13 I am sorry, because I happen, I think, to be the one who
was inspired. I would also like to add
further that we put this suggestion before
some of the people whom we saw in England, in fact all of the people whom we
saw, and they every one approved of it for
the reason that, at the present moment, the
Prime Minister of England has to be guided
too much by the financial position of the
people from whom he can choose his Ministers, and they think that he ought to be
given by this means a freer hand, so that
one man can be put into the position of
head of a department, and another man,
if necessary, can be put into the position of
a Cabinet Minister without being head of
a department. It seems to be the mind of
some that there is something hidden at the
back of this regarding increasing the
Cabinet. On the contrary, our idea has
been to limit the Cabinet, and for that rea
son we have assessed the salary that is to
be paid to the head of a department and to
a Cabinet Minister separately. We have
distinctly stated that no Cabinet Minister
as such is to receive more than £500 a
year, and, as the £2,500 would imply, it
means that if any other person is taken
into the Cabinet, if he receives anything
at all, it will be a share of this £2,500 a
year.
There was another question raised as to
the evidence of the people whom the Sub-
Committee interviewed. It rather struck
me that some of the Members of this
House who spoke on the subject did not
seem to think we had chosen very suitable
persons to get information from. Perhaps
they did not trouble to read in the Report
whom we did get advice from. First of all,
there was Lord Carson-(hon. Members:
Hear, hear)-and I am not going to
attempt to blow Lord Carson's trumpet
in Ulster. We put the whole facts before
him so far as we knew them, and perhaps
he knew them better than we did ourselves. I next come to Lord Colwyn, who
is one of the leading business men in the
United Kingdom. We then went to one
whom I suppose I must call a politician,
a Member of Parliament-Lieutenant-
Commander Hilton Young-and afterwards we went to the permanent head of
the Treasury, who ought to know something about finance. Lastly, we went to
the Hon. F. M. B. Fisher, an ex-Minister
of New Zealand. To those who are acquainted with them I think those names
show that we did our utmost to obtain
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
the very best advice that could be given
on one of the most intricate problems that
I have ever encountered. Some of the
criticism that has been levelled against
the Report has taken the line that we are
starting to spend before we know what
we are going to get. As a business man
I would like to put forward this view.
Here we are in Northern Ireland starting
a new business. If you are going to
cheesepare the salaries of the people whom
we are going to put in to run that business, it is not my idea of the way to make
it a success. On the contrary, if I had
anything to do with starting a new business the first thing I would do would be
to get competent men and pay them
sufficient salaries, and by so doing you
would save money, because an incompetent
man is the most extravagant man you can
put into a job. True economy lies not in
getting a poor man and paying him a poor
salary, or in getting a good man and
paying him a poor salary, but in getting
a good man and paying him a proper
salary. I have not the slightest doubt in
my own mind that the figures we have
arrived at after very long and careful
consideration, are not one penny more
than the respective offices are entitled to.
We took no notice of the individuals who
were holding the various posts. We took
the offices as such, and made what inquiries we could as to the duties. I feel
satisfied that if this House accepts this
Report it will be doing the right thing.
Remarks have been passed in this House
to-day that we in Ulster should not think
that we are too big. I hope we will not
suffer from swelled head, and I do not
think we shall, but at the present time I
do feel that Ulster is in a position probably unique in the history of the British
Empire, and that one of our duties is to
show that we can go along with level head
and level hand, because the time may not
be far distant when we will be able to be
looked upon, as I hope and trust we may,
not only as the saviour of the Empire at
this critical time, but as the best and most
economical unit in the whole of the British
Empire.
Dr. MORRISON: I have been rather
surprised by one of the arguments put
forward by the hon. Member for North
Belfast. I have always had a very high
opinion of the business capacity of the
Belfast merchant, and if the hon. Member
for North Belfast is speaking for the Belfast merchant now he is a different type
of
individual than I had previously
thought him to be. There is an old
maxim which holds in the country if it
does not hold in Belfast, although I
believe it does, and it is this: "You must
creep before you walk." The hon. Member for North Belfast is reversing that
system, and he is proposing, not even to
walk, but to run before he has acquired
the practice of creeping. 1 approach this
question animated by a good many considerations, but there is one consideration
that influences me profoundly, and that
is the effect which our conduct on this
matter and on finance generally will have
on our position in the country.
I have had a long experience of
party politics. I have had a long
experience of helping to hold. a very
critical seat in Ulster. On several
occasions we have had slides that
very seriously endangered our position,
and I can easily conceive that our attitude
on this question may produce a slide that
will destroy us. There is no question that
to-day agricultural Unionists, and indeed
all sections of Unionists, are a united
body. In my opinion-and I give it as
a result of my experience-there is a very
serious danger that if we do not walk
guardedly and carefully we may split the
agricultural vote and our enemies may
come in and form a combination that will
be inimical to Ulster. The Committee
have wandered far and have consulted a
good many
sources of information in
London, as well as the Lord Chief Justice,
different departments of Colonial administration, many directors of big concerns,
high municipal officers, etc., and in the
Report which covers this wide field it
appears to me there are very serious
omissions. I gather from some members
of the Committee that they did consider
other sources of information not mentioned in this report. So far as I can
see the ratepayers have been overlooked,
the relative wealth of different communities has been overlooked or at least not
referred to; the relative area has been
overlooked, and the existing burden of
taxation has been overlooked. Those are
very serious defects. I think the members
of the Committee have gone into their
work with very great care, and have
brought forward very reasonable and
moderate proposals. If it were not for
the very critical position in which we in
Ulster are placed at the present time it
would have given me great pleasure to
vote in favour of the Committee's recommendation. The task of opposing it is to
me a very unpleasant one, but I want to
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Dr. Morrison.]
demonstrate that there is a section in this
House which, however unpleasant the task
may be, will demonstrate to the community that, as far as we are able to control it, the financial business of this
Parliament will be run on economical
and consistent lines. There is
that
no
denying
the fact
in
rural and urban Ulster we are suffering
from unparalleled taxation. It has been
pointed out that it equals, and in many
cases surpasses, rent. In the course of a
long political life I have seen many phases
of Irish politics come and go. We are
now entering on what I think is the third
phase. I remember distinctly the public
outcry against the landlords, and I remember a farmer pointing out to me that
it was the landlords who used to oppress
them. He added, "but we are free from
the landlords, and now it is the clergy
and doctors." But they tell me now that
even these necessary members of the com
munity have been relegated to comparative obscurity and their places have been
taken by politicians.
Sir James Craig, our Prime Minister,
who, I think, notwithstanding the sanction he has given to some of these salaries,
will prove an economical administrator,
has taken up a certain position which he
has fully explained in this House, namely,
that the Government has compressed
twenty-nine departments into five.
That
is a very good thing, but he presumes
that he has liberty to spend on five de
partments the money that was previously
spent on twenty-nine. We regret very
much the absence through illness of the
Prime Minister and the Minister of
Finance, and I am sure I am expressing
the views of every Member of this House
when I say that, next to the Prime
Minister, no man in Ulster gives more
public confidence than the Minister of
Finance. He has made his position clear.
He says economy is to be written over
every door of every public department in
the Northern Parliament, and he further
says he will not be the Minister of
Finance when any additional burden is
placed on the community. I think
that both the Finance Minister
and the Prime Minister are going on a
wholly wrong basis. The outstanding
factor in public administration to-day is
that you have waste and extravagance
everywhere throughout Ulster. We all
hope that in this Northern Parliament we
have established a new era, as I believe
we have, but to set up as a standard that
our future expenditure is not to exceed
what it has been in the past is a wholly
wrong idea.
I think the ratepayers
expect that by efficient administration we
should be able to make a very perceptible
diminution in the taxation prevailing
before we took office, and the Finance
Minister's statement that he will not increase taxation, and that if taxation is
increased he will not be the Finance
Minister, or, in other words, that he will
not go beyond the extravagance that has
ruled in the past, is, in my opinion, simply
an empty phrase. His word
with this as a standard, is also a mere
common-place, futile gesture.
economy,
We want a revolution in this matter,
and the public expect to see it accomplished. We want to get rid of frills and
feathers and sham dignities; we want to
stop the tiny stream that is flowing into
a river to bleed the ratepayers of Ulste
white, and we must start at the top. I
have come into contact with some of the
officials who have been brought here, and
I think they are real good men, who will
be able to do good work for us in Ulster.
Many of the speakers who have addressed
this House to-day do not know anything
of the feeling that exists in rural Ulster
in regard to this question of taxation.
have lived all my life amongst these
people, and I know what they think. I
know they are very anxious about this
matter, and it is out of deference to them,
and very largely as a matter of party
tactics, that I take this stand to-day. I
regret exceedingly having to protest
against this report, but the particular
object I have in view, and one which
should be dear to every one of us, is to
keep Ulster safe not only from without
but from within.
I
Major SHILLINGTON: At this late
hour of the evening I do not intend to
delay the House very long, but, as I am
one of the Members of the Select Committee, if I did not speak some of the
Members who have been criticising might
think we were not unanimous in our recommendations. I would like to assure
the hon. Member for West Belfast
particularly that the Committee were
absolutely unanimous in the Report
which they made. Some comparison has
been made with Australia and the
Colonies, but it has not been emphasised
that Australia is governed by its different
Ministries in various States in addition to
the Commonwealth, and that in addition
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
to the official salaries laid down in these
places there are certain perquisites
attached to these offices. In connection
with a number of them there is free
travelling on State railways and tramways, as well as free postage. As regards
New Zealand, income tax there varies
from 1/- to 3/- in the £, and until you
reach an income of £6,000 your incometax does not rise above 3/- in the £. I
do not think the question of income-tax
has been sufficiently emphasised. We
must remember that there will be a reduction in these salaries for income-tax
of from one-third to one-half. I am quite
as keen on economy as any of the hon.
Members who have criticised this Report.
I myself represent a constituency which
is largely rural, and in it we also have
some large industrial towns. I am quite
prepared to go before my constituents any
day with this Report, and I think it would
be very false economy to cut down these
salaries. We must look at the positions
and not at the people who fill them at the
present time, and should make it
possible for any person to occupy these
posts no matter what his private means
may be.
•
I do not think it is necessary to
weary the House by repeating all the
arguments used to support the Report,
with all of which I am thoroughly in
agreement, but if it is the feeling of the
House that we have not given the
Attorney-General a salary sufficiently high
I am sure the Committee will be glad to
reconsider their decision in this respect.
Mr. MILLER: I would like to join
in the appeal already made to the Government to reconsider their decision in this
matter, and if it would induce the
Government to do so I would suggest that
my hon. Friend, the Member for Tyrone
and Fermanagh, should leave out that
part of his amendment which deals with
figures, and simply agree to refer the
whole matter back to the Committee
without binding them in any way, but in
the hope that they will fall in more readily
than they have done with the sentiments
which have fallen from the lips of hon.
Members here to-day. Since we came
into this House, as well as in public and
private, we have found that the one thing
talked about is economy, and I do not
think the Committee put this in the forefront when considering their recommendations. In passing, I would like to say that
I think it is hardly fair to the Members
of this House to give them a Report of
this kind twenty-four hours before they
are asked to come to a conclusion on it.
Two things struck me on reading this Report. The first was the enormous amount
of trouble the Committee had taken, and
the second was that having taken all this
trouble, and having got all their facts
marshalled, they were not able to come
to a conclusion. They thought they
would stick down flat rates and leave it
to the House itself to decide what the
salaries should be. Those were the
thoughts that occurred to me in connection
with these, in some respects, extraordinary
salaries. The Prime Minister told the
House on one occasion that he was an alltime man.
There was another all-time
man who must be at the beck and call of
the Government from Sunday morning to
Sunday morning, and that is the
Attorney-General. I think no one would
dare to reduce the salaries of those two,
and in my opinion the salaries proposed to
be paid to each of them should have been
increased. That is another reason why I
ask the Government to agree to the Report
going back to the Committee.
I do not quite agree with those hon.
Members who have spoken in favour of
the amendment. For instance, that
famous clause which has excited so much
attention I read in quite a different way
to those who have criticised it, although
of course, I may be wrong. It might
foreshadow, as it is advisable to do, the
case of a man in a very good position to
whom the salary would not be an inducement, but who for a nominal salary would
put his brains and what time he had at
the disposal of the State. I do not wish
to take up the time of the House further,
but I would like again to urge the Govern
ment not to go against the wishes expressed by some hon. Members who are
endeavouring to get this
considered. I appeal to the Governmeni
not to put the question to a division, but
to allow the Committee to reconsider it
with a view to seeing if they cannot fall
in with some of the ideas put forward in
this House to-day.
matter
re-
I
Mr. MARK: At this late hour I do not
intend to take up the time of the House,
but I should like to take this opportunity
of congratulating a former platform
colleague of mine for her very able de
fence of the actions of the Committee.
am bound to say that to what she said I.
for one, did take very great exception.
On the other hand I doubted on this
occasion if I would content myself with
going into the Lobby in opposition for the
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Mark.]
In the
reasons I would like to mention.
first place the amendment asks this report
to be re-committed to the Select Committce
to re-consider their decision. They indicate, as I understand, the lines on which
they should reconsider the Report, but, as
I understand it, the Committee are not
hound to have any regard to them. They
may increase the figures; they are not
committed to anything. I would ask the
Government to allow this Report to be recommitted to the Select Committee to examine and consider it further. Few of
the Members of this House, I am afraid,
Are mixing daily with the poverty-stricken
agriculturists, as they have been described
by my hon. Friend (Sir Robert Anderson)
the Member for Derry City, but if they
were, I think they would see there is a real
danger of losing their confidence in this
matter of finance.
(Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.") In the rural districts of this
Northern Ireland I have never heard a
single man or woman state that he or she
had the slightest fear in regard to the
stand the Prime Minister, or any
Ministers of the Government, would take,
in regard to the vital questions of the day
-the questions that have been absorbing
their minds since we have been elected to
represent this Northern area.
But they
have all expressed themselves freely that
they have very great and very grave fears
on this matter of finance. They would be
satisfied, I have no doubt, if they considered that due importance and due
weight had been accorded to the questions.
before us, and how can the Members of
this House satisfy their constituents better
than on this occasion by referring back
this Report to the Committee which
undertsands it, and which has given it
every consideration, I have no doubt, to
see if any amendment could be made
which would meet with the wishes of the
opposition here. I know it would please
the bulk of the constituents of this
Northern Area to see that we in this
House were taking a real, deep interest in
financial matters. If we simply allowed
Reports to come before this House and
swallowed them like a pill, as the hon
Member for the University (Dr. Campbell)
would say, we would be thought, and
naturally and rightly so, to be giving very
little consideration to the vital questions
that interest our constituents so much.
The question that interests them so
much at present is from what source will
the revenue come. My hon. Friend, the
Member for Antrim (Mr. Crawford) has
indicated the three chief sources from
which the revenue may be drawn, and if
my hon. Friend, the Member for Derry
(Sir Robert Anderson) had his way, I can
assure you that the first of these would be
a rapidly disappearing quantity. In regard
to income tax, I was talking the other day
to a very well qualified man who takes a
very leading part in the auditorial
work of this city, and he told me that the
books of firms revealed an appalling state
of affairs. He told me, further, that he
was afraid in some instances to submit the
balance sheet to the members of the firm.
That being so, I fail to see how we can
depend on any income worth talking
about from income tax. I do not, in saying these things, take any exception in
any way to a single salary fixed by the
Committee, and I give them all credit for
the pains they have taken in coming to the
conclusions at which they have arrived.
I venture to say that, had I been one of
them, I possibly to-day would be in complete agreement with them, but I know
how the country feels on this matter. I
know that we must walk in such a way
that we can defend ourselves when we
appear before our constituents again, and
for that reason I submit that it would be
only wise of this House to re-commit this
Report to the Committee for further consideration. Lines have undoubtedly been
indicated along which we should travel.
The very first of these was practically an
admission by the hon. Member for Tyrone
and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) that the
Prime Minister was underpaid.
I can
assure you, for my part, if it goes back to
the Committee, and if the Committee
state in their new Report that the Prime
Minister is entitled to another £500 a
year, I will heartily support it. I know
he deserves it. A great deal has been
said about my good Friend, the hon, Member for Armagh (Mr. Best), with regard
to his position as Attorney-General for
Northern Ireland. Undoubtedly he has
very momentous duties before him, but I
think, with his skill and ability, before
very long he will make them very much
less, and that we will live to see the time
when the Attorney-General for Northern
Ireland will, from a criminal point of
view, have very little to do. I think that
possibly, for the moment, he is underpaid,
but we are not going to gauge his work by
present-day occurrences, and I think he
will find himself, when things come into a
normal state, fairly well paid. I would
strongly urge upon this House to allow
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
this matter to go back to the Committee,
and to let them come before us again, and
let the country see that we have given due
weight and consideration to every proposal that is contained in the Report.
The PARLIAMENTARY SECRE-
TARY to the MINISTRY of FINANCE
(Captain Dixon): I do not want to detain
the House at any length to-night, and I
regret very much that the Prime
Minister was not able to be present to-day
to hear the admirable debate which we
have all listened to. 1 should have liked
him to have heard the speeches, and I
should very much have liked him to be
present to wind-up the debate. We have
heard a discussion to-day which I think in
the future we will look upon as one of the
models on which discussions should be
carried on in this House. We have had
temperate, able, and eloquent speeches,
and it is very difficult for me to know who
most to congratulate. I do not think that
I, or anyone else in this House, ever heard
a more eloquent maiden speech than that
riade by the Member for Derry (Mrs.
Chichester.) (Hon. Members: Hear, hear)
But I would not be doing my duty if I did
not equally congratulate the member for
Tyrone (Mr. Coote) for the reasoned,
quiet, and admirable way in which he put
his statement when moving the amendment.
Let me tell the House exactly what is
the Government attitude in this matter.
We had nothing to do with the findings
of this Committee. We had no means,
even if we had wished, of influencing the
findings of this Committee, and I say he
would have been a very bold man who
would have gone to the Chairman of this
Committee to have used any back-door
influence. I am not going to answer any
of the points raised with reference to the
salaries which have been laid down for
Ministers. We had nothing to do with
those salaries, and I believe that this
House will accept my statement when I
say that to many of us who happen to be
on the Government Bench at this moment
these salaries are not a matter of enormous
importance. I hope to live to see this
House when the poorest man in Ulster
will be sitting on this very Front Bench,
and will be getting an adequate salary
from Ulster.
That is our position, but before I leave
this subject let me congratulate in the
most hearty manner the Committee who
have gone into this matter, and who have
issued the Report. It is always a difficult
task for a man to sit on a Committee, in
which he knows that whatever his findings
are they cannot be popular. We must remember that the Members who sat on that
Committee, and on whom most of the
riticism fell this afternoon, represent
constituencies in just the same way as
those who criticise them represent constituencies. We must give them creditand I know that the Members who opposed
them give them credit-for taking their
courage in their hands, and they are willing to face their constituents and fight
their seats on what they have done. That is
practically all I have to say on this matter,
but I think it is my duty to say to the
House, which contains many New Members, that I want the Members who are
going to vote against the decision arrived
at to realise that they will vote against
not only a decision of the Cabinet, but a
decision of a Committee of their own
House. I would ask you to think for one
moment before you do this, because remember if you win on this, and if you put
the Government out, you will have shown
to the whole of Ulster that not only do
you not trust your Cabinet, but that you
do not trust even the Select Committee
which you have set up. (Hon. Members:
No, No.) Yes, yes.
Mr. COOTE: You said the Government
had nothing to do with this, that it was a
matter for the Committee.
Captain DIXON: I say that in putting
this matter before the House the Government has to do with it, because as my
hon. Friend (Mr. Barbour) said the
Government has adopted this report.
The findings of this Committee went before
the Cabinet this morning, and the
Cabinet, having gone into the Report most
thoroughly, decided to adopt it. Therefore any vote given against the findings
to-night is a vote against not only the
Select Committee, but against the Government that is now governing Ulster. Do
not let us for one moment forget that.
If the Members of this Parliament will
not trust us even in a matter of £3,000,
£4,000, or £5,000, how can they trust us
in the great things of life and death that
we have to face?
I appeal now to the
men who are going to vote against us to
think for one moment that it will mean
the lowering of the prestige of the Prime
Minister and the prestige of this
Government. I believe if at this
moment they go deep down into
