secretaries, and very important secretaries, who are appointed, and who are
not civil servants. Are they receiving
I could mention them, but I
bonuses?
do not wish to do so.
Mr. BARBOUR: As this is a general
matter perhaps it would be proper for
me to reply. I would like to assure the
hon. Member that the bonuses are only
payable to established civil servants.
They do not apply to temporary clerks
recently appointed.
Mr. COOTE: Or to any of the
secretaries?
Mr. BARBOUR: Where a salary is set
opposite a secretary and it is stated "inclusive" the bonus does not apply.
If the word "inclusive " is not opposite
then the bonus applies.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put, and agreed to.
LAW CHARGES AND CRIMINAL
PROSECUTIONS.
Resolved: "That a sum not exceeding
Six thousand, eight hundred and twelve
pounds be granted to His Majesty for the
Service of the period ending 31st March,
1922, to defray the Expenses of Criminal
Prosecutions and other Law Charges."
COUNTY COURT OFFICERS.
Resolved: "That a sum not exceeding
Five thousand and forty pounds be granted
to His Majesty for the Service of the period
ending 31st March, 1922, to defray the
Salaries, Allowances and Expenses of various County Court Officers.'
Mr. COOTE: I had an amendment down
at B. Page 27. Should I have raised it on
your putting the motion?
The CHAIRMAN: The time to raise it
was when I put the Question.

PRISONS.
Motion made and Question proposed:
That a sum not exceeding Twenty-two
thousand, five hundred pounds be granted
to His Majesty for the Service of the period
ending 31st March, 1922, to defray the
Expenses of Prisons."
Mr. COOTE: I beg to move that “Sub-
Head A" (Pay and Allowances of Officers)
be reduced by £1,000."
I raise the question simply for the purpose of asking a question and to get some
idea as to what steps the Minister of Home
Affairs has taken in order to prevent a repetition of the deplorable events which
happened in Londonderry Prison the
other morning. It is one of the scandals
of Irish Prison Service all over that
prisoners of a certain type can escape, and
escape in such numbers, and so frequently,
and the only explanation apparently is that
there must be great sympathy inside and
that there is very lax administration inside. Whoever is Governor of these prisons
ought to be held responsible for such incidents. I hope that the Minister will
change the whole face of things and give
not only the impression but apply the
reality to the whole situation and see that
all the servants of the Crown will be loyal
to the work they have on hand, and carry
it out with the greatest desire to preserve
the lives and bodies of those inside, and
not under any circumstances aid and abet
those who will try to destroy law and order
in this Province.
Mr. DONALD: I rise to support the
amendment. I think it is very necessary
at this juncture we should have a discussion on this very important matter. I take
it the sum of £12,530 covers a period of
four months for pay and allowances of officers. I think there will have to be some
economy effected with regard to our
prisons. We have prisons in Belfast, Londonderry, and Armagh. I am quite sure
my right hon. Friend (Sir Dawson Bates)
will go round these places himself and investigate what the procedure has been in
them. In reading the report of Mr. Justice
Dodd in connection with the Commission
that was set up, one could not help thinking the condition of affairs was a disgrace
to any country. An inquiry was held in
Belfast-
The CHAIRMAN: I must ask the hon.
Member to remember that having regard
to the fact that we only took over control
from an appointed day obviously the present Minister of Home Affairs could not
be responsible for proceedings long anterior
to his entering upon office. If the hon.
Member wishes, without specifically referring to matters which are clearly excluded
by the date, he can discuss the general incident.
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
Mr. DONALD: I bow to your ruling. I
think these figures seem rather high.
£12,530 for four months means that it will
take a sum of £37,590 for the year. Surely
it is very necessary that discussion should
take place with a view to some economy
being reached with regard to these prisons.
Furthermore, the conduct of officials has
certainly not been creditable. I am quite
satisfied from the reports that I have received that they have not been looking
after their duties when people have been
allowed to escape time and again. Yet
the country is asked to pay high salaries
to these officials. I merely rise to ask my
right hon. Friend will he be good enough
to look into this whole question of our
prisons? I think the expenditure which it
takes to run these prisons should be very
much lower than that indicated in the
Vote.
Before sitting down may I say that I
am entirely dissatisfied with the number of
Members in this House just now, and I
want to enter my protest.. We are discussing here an expenditure of over
£2,000,000, and there are only something
like a dozen Members present. If that is
the interest Members take in the affairs of
the House then we should close down. I
want to enter my protest against such a
disgraceful state of affairs.
Sir DAWSON BATES: With regard to
the point raised by the hon. Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) and
the hon. Member for East Belfast (Mr.
Donald) the prison service, you will
realise, was only taken over on the 1st
December, and we had to take it as it
stood. It was formerly administered by a
Board situated in Dublin. Personally I
am not satisfied with the way prisons are
being conducted in Northern Ireland, and
we are going very closely into the matter
to see, first of all, whether or not we can
get greater efficiency. I realise only too
well there is no Service that requires
loyalty more than the Prisons Service. The
points that the hon. Members have raised
will receive very careful consideration.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put and agreed to.
REFORMATORY AND INDUSTRIAL
SCHOOLS.
Resolved, " That a sum not exceeding
Four thousand eight hundred pounds be
granted to His Majesty for the Service of
the period ending 31st March, 1922, to
defray the Expenses of Reformatory and
Industrial Schools."
CENTRAL CRIMINAL LUNATIC
ASYLUM.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That a sum not exceeding One
thousand six hundred and fifty pounds
be granted to His Majesty for the Service
of the period ending 31st March, 1922,
to defray the Expenses of the Maintenance of Criminal Lunatics."
Mr. CRAWFORD: I beg to ask a
question. Some time ago a promise was
made in regard to the capitation grant
to be given to the various authorities in
charge of asylums. That promise has
not been kept. The grant was smaller
than expected, and the balance has fallen
on local rates. I think some light should
be thrown on that subject before any
money is granted for this purpose.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think
there is some misunderstanding on the
part of the hon. Member. This resolution only deals with the Central Criminal
Lunatic Asylum.
Mr. DONALD: I am sorry for taking
up so much of the time of the House, but
this is a matter of very great importance,
and I rise to move a reduction of £100 in
the sum named in the resolution. I
want to raise an important point with
regard to these asylums. The grants in
1917 and 1918-
The CHAIRMAN: Perhaps there is
a little confusion in this matter. As the
Minister of Home Affairs has just explained, this relates merely to the
Central Criminal Lunatic Asylum, and
the subject raised by the hon. Member is
quite outside the point.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put, and agreed to.
GRANTS TO LOCAL AUTHORITIES.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That a sum not exceeding Eightytwo thousand seven hundred and thirtyfive pounds be granted to His Majesty for
the Service of the period ending 31st
March, 1922, to provide for Grants to
Local Authorities in respect of Health,
Housing, etc., and in Relief of Local
Rates."
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
Mr. COOTE: I wish to move an
Amendment to reduce this amount by
£100.
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon.
Member desire to attach this to some
particular sub-head? If he attaches it
only to the general sum it precludes any
further Amendment.
Mr. COOTE: I attach it to the general
sum at the foot of the page. I wish to
call the attention of the Minister to the
question of sanatoria generally. I submit that we do not get value for our
money in this work, because we do not
follow up the matter of segregation of
the patients. When patients are
brought into a sanatorium they are kept
there while they are in a condition of
comparative strength, but the moment
the medical authority realises that they
are hopelessly incurable they are sent
back to the homes from whence they
came. I feel it is criminal, when these
people are in the most critical and contagious stage of the disease, to send them
back to their homes, after all the expense
that has been incurred on the part of the
county, and I think it renders void the
effectiveness of the relief to these poor
people. I submit that if there were a
doctor for inspecting the dispensaries of
the county, and if the dispensaries were
subsidised in some way, so that excellent
medicines and strengthening food might
be given out to the patients, we could
save a good deal of money that is thrown
away in our sanatoria rate.
I think
this is one of the pieces of legislation
that has been handed on to us by people
who wish to be famous, but who did not
understand what they were doing, and
they have given us something to think
about when they are gone. This work
has been an absolute failure. It has put
additional weight upon the Local
Authorities, and is doing much to eat
up the resources of the counties. I hope
the new Local Government Board will
try to realise the tremendous burdens
that the ratepayers have to bear.
speak of the ratepayers as distinct from
the taxpayers. Their burdens are almost
unbearable. Might I also say to the right
hon. Gentleman that there are too many
compartments here in connection with the
Local Government Board. There could
be a better grouping of the services. I
am not going to indicate how I cannot
at the moment-but if he will look into
the details I think he can do admirable
work for this Parliamentary area if he
I
will try to economise by a better grouping of these services. There is a great
lot of officials, and there is a great lot of
these red-tape orders sent to Boards of
Guardians, Rural Councils, and County
Councils, all causing expense and giving
trouble. They are simply nonsensical,
and the result is that while they do no
good they certainly cost a lot of money.
I hope the right hon. Gentleman will
take these matters into consideration.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I can assure
my hon. Friend that the matters he has
referred to will receive very careful consideration. What we want to do is to
ascertain to what extent we can bring
about some of these reforms by administration rather than by legislation.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear) It may
be we will come to a point at which we
cannot bring about reform by administration, and in that event we will have to
come to the House. As I said before, I
propose to set up a Public Health
Council, on which I hope we will have the
services of medical men, including, I
hope, my hon. Friend sitting opposite
(Dr. Morrison) to advise me in these
matters.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Sir CRAWFORD M'CULLAGH: I
would like to ask the Minister of Home
Affairs a question in regard to the grants
to Local Authorities under the Housing
(Ireland) Act, 1919. What provision
has the Minister of Home Affairs made
for the subsidy that will have to be paid
under the 1919 Housing Act? I would
also like to ask if there is any provision
made for a loan of the money to build
these houses. A Bill was passed through
the Imperial Parliament, and an undertaking was given that if Belfast could not
raise the money the Government would
advance at Treasury rates the amount
required. The amount required is
£500,000. The scheme was well under
way, but if the money is not provided it
will have to be stopped, and consequently
great numbers will be thrown out of employment. I wish to ask is there any
provision for the loan to the Corporation
of Belfast of this £500,000, and also if
there is provision made to meet the subsidy that is now becoming due every day
in increasing amount.
Mr. TWADDELL: i would like to support the statement of the hon. Member for
South Belfast (Sir Crawford M'Cullagh).
We in Belfast are building houses to cost
Supply.
Supply
8 DECEMBER 1921.
£552,000. We were more or less forced
to build these houses by the Local Government Board, who promised that if the
Local Authorities were unable to raise the
money they would provide it at the
Treasury rate. Personally I do not see
any amount in this Estimate for the purpose of loaning money to
to the Local
Authorities at the Government rate.
would like to ask the Minister of Home
Affairs if it is his intention to make the
necessary provision whereby loans may be
given to the Local Authorities.
I
Sir DAWSON BATES: I will clear up
this misunderstanding. In the first place
this Government has not been approached
by the Belfast Corporation for the loan of
any money. Of course, if it is, it will
be a matter for my hon. Friend (Mr.
Barbour) to ascertain where he is going
to get the money.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
think the Cabinet have been approached
and asked if they could advance this money
to the Corporation of Belfast. Of course,
I admit it has not gone before the Government officially, if that is what the Minister
of Home Affairs means.
Mr. BARBOUR: I think the only suggestion made to us was that we might use
our good offices with the Imperial Government to endeavour to obtain assistance
from them. If I may say so, this bargain
was somewhat loosely entered into between
the Belfast Corporation and the representatives of the Imperial Government at
Westminster. There may have been an
obligation given at the time, when the
Imperial Government were very anxious to
get the building of houses undertaken, but
I think the Corporation have not sufficiently protected themselves to secure that
the necessary money supplies would be
forthcoming before they embarked on this
scheme. In the meantime I do not think
they can claim that the Parliament of
Northern Ireland has become a party in
any way to the guarantees that have been
given, or to the scheme upon which they
have embarked.
I
Sir CRAWFORD M'CULLAGH:
quite appreciate what my hon. Friend (Mr.
Barbour) has said, but the position is
simply this: The Imperial Government
undertook to advance money to the Belfast
Corporation, who are not in a position to
raise it at a reasonable rate of interest.
When we asked the Imperial Government
to fulfil their bargain they said the service
is now transferred to the Northern Parliament; let them get the money. That is
the position, and I ask the question, have
you made any provision to advance that
£500,000?
Question put and agreed to.
SPECIAL CONSTABULARY.
Motion made and Question proposed:
"(
That a sum not exceeding Four
hundred and eighty-nine thousand six
hundred and ninety-seven pounds be
granted to His Majesty for the Service of
the period ending 31st March, 1922, to
defray the salaries and expenses of the
Special Constabulary."
Mr. DONALD: To my mind this is a
matter entirely for the Imperial Government. It is entirely out of the question
to ask us to pay this large sum of money
for maintaining law and order. May I
ask if it is not a fact that the Chief Secretary himself sanctioned the establishment
of the Specials here in the North of Ireland? Had it not the sanction in the first
instance of the Imperial Government? If
so, what arrangement was come to with
our home Government in contemplation of
what was likely to happen later on with
regard to the financing and upkeep of this
Special Force? I do not think that we
should be liable for a solitary penny of
the upkeep of this Force even now.
It is
a matter that should be arranged in conjunction with the regular Royal Irish
Constabulary and this new Force which
has been set üp.
Mr. GRANT: I would like to support
the protest of my hon. Friend, the Member
for East Belfast (Mr. Donald). I remember, in 1918, going to the Cabinet in
London to ask them to set up the Special
Constabulary, and they agreed that it was
necessary in Ulster. Therefore I would
also protest against this being charged
against the Northern Parliament. I believe myself it is an Imperial charge, and
while I cannot object to the granting of
the money that has been spent, because I
believe it has been well spent, I do protest
against this being charged to the Northern
Parliament.
Mr. CCOTE: May I also make a contribution to this protest? This Force was
brought into existence, I think, in September, 1920, and it was inaugurated under
the control of the Imperial Parliament and
by direction of the Chief Secretary--both
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
the "A" and "B" classes. They have been
paid out of the funds provided by the
Imperial Parliament. Let us never forget
that there is an equivalent force recruited
and operating in the three counties of
Ulster outside the six that constitute the
Northern Area. Let us also remember as
a justification of our protest that there is
an equivalent force in the South of Ireland
-the Auxiliaries-and they are paid for
by the State, and very properly so. Let
us further observe that these forces were
brought in being in order to ease the
pressure on the military, and to enable
the troops to be kept more and more in
the South of Ireland. Therefore, they
are an auxiliary force to the military.
They are formed into platoons, they have
their commandants, and they are kept as
a quasi-fighting force. They are not an
ordinary police force; they are to be
ordered wherever the Government wills,
I submit that until this country, North
and South, is settled by the British Government, and law and order are maintained,
we have no right to pay one penny for this
Force.
Mr. McBRIDE: I would also like to
enter my protest at this juncture in regard to this matter. During the time of
the Truce the Government withdrew the
"B" Force from their service. They also
prohibited recruiting for the "A" Force.
Why then should the Parliament of
Northern Ireland have to pay for this particular Force if the Imperial Government
controlled it during all this time? I do not
say the money is wasted, because it is
a necessary force, but the Imperial Parliament should bear the expense of it.
Sir DAWSON BATES: May I say that
this Government entirely agree with the
views expressed by hon. Members. With
regard to the remarks of the hon. Member
for County Down (Mr. McBride), he will
recollect that we took over the Special
Constabulary on the 22nd November, and
we have to find the money for that Force,
otherwise it would cease to exist. As re
gards who should ultimately pay for this.
Force, this Government is in negotiation
with the Imperial Government, holding
the view that as this Force is really for the
purpose of taking the place of the troops.
and doing military work, the cost should
fall upon the Imperial Exchequer. We
have received verbal assurances which I
hope may ultimately prove to be worth
something.
Mr. COOTE: May I ask a question? If
we pass this vote now does it not put us
in an awkward position? Are we not by
this vote giving some sort of tacit acknowledgment of our liability to pay, and would
it not be better-if it could possibly be
done to hang up this matter until you
know from the Imperial Government what
is going to happen? We do not wish to
divide the House, but there is a very
strong feeling in all parts of the House
that the British Government brought this
Force into being, they control the old
Constabulary still, and that they have a
hand in the business yet. Until they have
washed their hands of the whole administration, North and South, they have a
right to pay for this Force that they
brought into being. We do not wish to
obstruct the work of the Ministry, but we
wish to refrain from doing anything that
might encourage the Imperial Government to think they had no liability.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not think
the actual payment of the amount will
prejudice us in any way, because we have
put our protest on paper. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) The payment is really
a matter of Hobson's choice. I can
imagine if we said we will disband our
Force that the Imperial Government might
he quite pleased. The question is, if the
Force is to continue this money must
be paid.
Question put and agreed to.
CLASS III.
Ministry of Labour.
Motion made and Question proposed:
"That a sum not exceeding Thirty-six
thousand eight hundred and twenty-one
pounds, including a supplementary sum
of thirty-six thousand six hundred and
sixteen pounds, be granted to His Majesty
for the Service of the Period ending 31st
March, 1922, to defray the Salaries and
certain Expenses of the Ministry of
Labour." (Voted on Account, £1,750).
Mr. COOTE: On a point of order, before the Minister speaks I wish to say that
I was under the impression that we were.
not to go beyond the Estimates for the
Ministry of Home Affairs to-night.
The CHAIRMAN: On that point of
Order, may I call the hon. Member's
attention to the fact that this is set down
in the Orders of the Day.
Mr. COOTE: I would appeal to the
Ministry. A few of us are looking into
these questions, and if they could be left
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
as
over until to-morrow we will give all the
assurances we can to the Government to
shorten the proceedings as much
possible, and to give as little trouble to the
Ministry and staff and save as much time.
as we can.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): As the hon. Member is aware,
it is very desirable that these Estimates
should be got through as quickly
as possible. There was an
arrangement made to-day, but I think the
feeling of the Government was that that
arrangement should only apply to those
Estimates which were not included in the
Orders of the Day. I would point out,
however, to my hon. Friend that there
will be an opportunity again of discussing
these Estimates on Report.
Mr. COOTE: When?
Mr. ANDREWS: When they are sent
down. And therefore it may meet with
the wishes of the hon. Member if we proceed with them now.
Mr. COOTE: The Imperial Parliament
meets next Wednesday, and, personally, I
shall have to leave. I cannot take part
in the discussion of the Estimates after
Monday night. I do not know when the
Report may be sent down.
The CHAIRMAN: No point arises for
me to rule. Notice having been given
that these Estimates were to be set down
for discussion, if the Minister in charge
decides to go on I have no option, but it
is, as my hon. Friend (Mr. Coote) knows,
convenient to discuss these things on the
Report Stage. There is the other
difficulty, but I am afraid that is outside
his control or mine.
Mr. ANDREWS: I think it is better
that we should proceed, because one of the
reasons why we are anxious to get these
Estimates put through quickly is just because the Imperial House does begin on
Wednesday, and probably there can be a
freer discussion now by the Members of
the Imperial House. Therefore, I think
it is better I should follow the example
set by my right hon. Friend, the Minister
of Home Affairs, and that I should make
a general statement now in regard to the
work that the Ministry of Labour is called
upon to undertake. I shall make that
statement as brief as possible.
I may
point out to hon. Members that these
Estimates affecting the Ministry of
Labour are divided into three heads.
First of all, there are the Administrative
Charges, second, there are the Statutory
National Health Insurance Charges, and
third, there are the Statutory Unemployment Insurance Charges. This arrangement was come to as being the most
convenient.
As regards salaries, I would point out
that we are in a large measure traversing
an unknown sea, and that therefore the
sum of £14,578 here included is more or
less of an estimate. I may say, however,
that we have been working up to the
present with a very small staff, because we
did not wish to incur the expense of a
larger one until we felt that the time was
close at hand when we would be obliged to
take over the duties. It will be necessary
now to increase that staff to a considerable
extent, and we have provided in the Estimates for an increase which we consider
to be necessary. I may point out, however, that we expect to make considerable
savings in reference to the staff. Up to
the present the Minstry of Labour has
been dealt with as two Departments, and
combining them, as it is proposed to do,
will allow the supervising staff to deal
with both, and in that way we ought to
have economy right at the beginning.
Then as regards National Health
Insurance, we hope to be able to do witha Commission, which up to the
present, has been a pretty expensive
luxury.
out
As
Again, there is a saving of an important
character which we contemplate.
many hon. Members know, we have had
the absurd custom in the past of
inspectors performing trivial duties, visiting firms on the same day. One would
come to deal with the question of Health
Insurance Accounts. Another would
come to deal with the matter of Unemployment Benefit Cards, while another
would come to see if the humidity records
were correct. Then another might come
to see if the Factory Laws in other
particulars were complied with. We
mean to combine the Inspectorate Department, and to see that one competent man
would come to the factory and perform
all those duties while he is there. I think
there will be a considerable economy in
that respect, and in addition to that I
think it will tend to efficiency.
The chief item included in the Estimates under the head of Administrative
Charges is that of £29,738 put down
GG
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Andrews.]
opposite the sub-head of Employment Exchanges. My hon. Friend, the Member
for Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr Coote),
has already referred to Labour Exchanges.
I do not think it is fitting that I should
say much in reference to them to-day, and
for this reason, I have appointed a Committee to inquire into the question of the
application of Labour Exchanges to
Northern Ireland, and until that Committee has reported, I am keeping an
open mind. I think, however, it is right
that I should go so far as to say that I feel
that Labour Exchanges as placing
organisations have disappointed those who
designed them. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). But at the same time, I think it
is only right I should say that as a medium
for distributing Unemployment Benefit
they have performed an absolutely
necessary work very faithfully and well.
I am aware that certain charges have been
brought against the Labour Exchanges as
regards delay in the payment of Unemployment Benefit under certain circumstances. The Labour Exchanges may
have been responsible in particular cases,
but I think a good deal of blame in this
connection has from time to time been
placed upon the Labour Exchanges which
might fittingly have been allotted to other
persons.
Now, as regards the only other important item in this estimate, I would refer
to what is termed the Agency Services,
namely, an item of £6,200. I am dealing
at the present time with the estimate
which is included under the head of Administrative Charges. It is upon page 42.
That £6,200 is to be paid for agency work
-work performed in keeping records until
such times as we are in a position to
take over the Department, and also part
of it is due for stamps and for matters
dealing with the cards for both
health and unemployment insurance.
The Imperial Government, hon.
Members will understand, are in control of the Post Office, it being a reserved
service, and it is natural they should expect
us to pay for the work we expect them to
perform. The sum they have asked is substantial, and we are doing the best we can
to have it reduced. Dealing with the
matter of Health Insurance, which is on
Page 47, I think the items are very clear
and explain themselves. I am glad to be
able to tell the House I feel that the Fund
is in a sound financial condition, and we
propose to carry on as at present with the
United Kingdom.
There is a Commission sitting in London
and we have been ably represented on that
Commission by Major Conacher, of our
Department. That Commission has been
considering questions in reference to these
matters. I am aware that approved societies are apprehensive as to the future. They
are anxious that health insurance should
be made a reserved Service. But hon.
Members are aware of the fact that we
very reluctantly accepted the Government
of Ireland Act of 1920 as a compromise.
We took the final arrangements in order to
secure Irish peace, and it would seem to me
to be very absurd if we, having undertaken
to work that Act, should go to the Imperial Government and say,
Will you
kindly take back National Health Insurance as a reserved Service?" I regret I
was unable to meet the wishes of the approved societies in this connection, because
after all, it is a matter of high political
importance, and I think what we have got
we have got to hold. I think that will be
the feeling of hon. Members generally. I
may say I am most anxious to meet the
wishes of approved societies as far as possible, and I am quite pleased to give an
undertaking that it is the desire of my
Ministry that we shall work in the most
friendly way with the approved societies,
and, in fact, all those associated with matters of insurance.
pass
I think, with that explanation, I may
to the third and last estimate, which,
after all, is probably the most important.
It is that dealing with the question of unemployment insurance. Hon. Members
know that is a matter which is attracting
a great deal of attention in practically every civilised country, and I think
it has come to be understood that when a
State is not able to find work for its people,
then it is necessary that, at any rate, those
who are willing to work, and cannot get
work, should get something to carry on
with. The first item in that estimate is a
sum of £300,000. Of course that is a
very substantial sum, and it comes about in
this way.
The fund was designed not by
us but by the Imperial Government,
to remain solvent at a certain percentage of unemployment. Unfortunately
owing to trade depression of an abnormal
kind, that percentage has been exceeded to
a very large extent. The result is contributions received from the employers, the
work people and the State have been insufficient to keep the fund solvent,
and this sum is necessary for the
next three months in order to keep the
fund in a solvent condition, so that unem-
"
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
ployment benefit may be continued to our
people.
The next item is a sum of £30,000, which
is the difference between the amount of
contributions to the Unemployed Workers'
Dependants' Fund and the amount of
benefits to be conferred by that
Act. These items are entirely dependent
upon the amount of unemployment that
exists, and I do hope that in our Northern
Area our employers will do all that they
can to solve this problem by finding as
much employment for our people as possible. After all, I believe what our people
want is not unemployment benefit or unemployment doles of this kind; what they
want is work. But when they cannot get
work, as I have already said, it is necessary the State should give them something
to live upon. I feel, as I am sure all my
hon. Friends feel, that in Northern Ireland we cannot afford to have idlers in any
class, but, at the same time, we all deeply
regret the number unemployed, through
no fault of their own, and it is the wish of
hon. Members of this House that this,
comparatively speaking, small weekly sum
should be given in order to keep these
people from dire starvation. The next item
under that head to which I think I ought to
refer is the item indicated "B," namely,
£40,000. That is a State grant to unemployed-that is, the State, under the Act
of 1921, is bound to contribute its share to
the contributions upon which the solvency
of the fund depends.
Again, there is the last item of importance, namely, £32,000, which is the State's
contribution through the Workers' Dependants' Fund. It is only fair I should take
this opportunity of thanking Dr.
Macnamara, the Minister of Labour in
England, and his staff for the kindness and
consideration which they have extended to
me at all times, and to those associated
with me. I have never appealed to Dr.
Macnamara in vain, and when I asked that
two of their trained departmental experts
should be given to me to assist me as permanent officials, to form a very necessary
link, in my opinion, between the two
Ministries, as well as to discharge very important duties, I am glad to say they gave
me of their best. I believe, with these
valuable friends, and other officials equally
faithful and capable of discharging the
duties to which they have been appointed,
and whose services I feel fortunate in securing, I am satisfied the Ministry of Labour
for Northern Ireland, if given time, will
make good.
Mr. DONALD: Dealing with this subject, it is one which naturally appeals to
me from a Labour point of view, and I
must congratulate my right hon. Friend on
the clear and explicit way in which he has
dealt with the various subjects before the
House. I, for one, am perfectly satisfied
that the Ministry of Labour are very fortunate in having at their head my right
hon. Friend, because I know from experience he will deal out justice to everyone,
whether it is in his Department or the
unfortunate unemployed, or otherwise. I
rise to ask a question with regard to Unemployment Exchanges, Page 45 "K." We
have here one manager, first class; five
managers, third class; ten employment officers, not to mention the permanent clerical
staff. My right hon. Friend, in a previous
statement, stated there were 58 permanent
officials. The next item is temporary
clerical staff, and then temporary clerical
and messenger staff, but there is no mention of figures. He also said in a previous
statement the temporary staff included 440
clerks. This is a subject I am deeply interested in, and so far as my judgment
goes I think we here, in the Six Counties,
can put this money to a much better use.
I do not advocate the abolition of these
Exchanges, but I would suggest
to my right hon. Friend-I take it
the services have been transferred-
Mr. ANDREWS: No, they will not
be transferred until 1st January.
Mr. DONALD: Thanks, very much.
I am satisfied he is as anxious as myself for
the proper administration of this Department. This expenditure is far too high
to administer unemployment and such
like, and I would suggest a Committee of
Inquiry should be set up to go into this
matter.
Mr. ANDREWS: I have already
anticipated the hon. Member's wishes,
and have appointed a Committee for the
The names will be published
purpose.
in the course of a few days, and I am

is delighted to hear the right hon.
Gentleman is getting into harness, and
that he is looking to some extent ahead.
I am of opinion that the administration
of unemployment and so forth should be
run by at least half the staffs. The pre-

UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE.
Motion made, and question proposed:
That a sum not exceeding Four
hundred and four thousand six hundred
pounds be granted to His Majesty for
the Service of the period ending 31st
March, 1922, to defray the Contributions
to the Unemployment Insurance Fund,
and the Expenses of Kindred Services,
and for Advances to the Unemployment
and Unemployed Workers' Dependants'
Funds."
Mr. COOTE: In this matter I wish to
call attention to the loose manner in which
this Fund is administered right over the
country.
The CHAIRMAN: Perhaps the hon.
Member will give me a formal motion.
Mr. COOTE: I beg to move "That
sub-head A (Advance to Unemployment
Fund) be reduced by £100," in order to
call attention to the fact that the agencies
at work in various parts of the country are
very loosely conducted. The oversight is bad,
and very many people are receiving unemployment benefit who are in partial employment. Many receive the benefit who
are sons of the proprietors of small farms,
and who have no right to benefit under the
Act. Under the Imperial Government the
manner in which these proceedings were
conducted was more or less very haphazard and slipshod. There was no sufficient guarantee of entire unemployment,
and the real bona-fide unemployed therefore lost to that extent. Money was
spent loosely in other directions. I want
to call attention to this matter, and to say
that greater care should be taken, if I may
suggest, in the selection of the agents
employed, who will do things properly,
Speakers' Salaries. 494

and that there shall be a better oversight,
and that there shall be prosecutions for
infringements of the Order. I merely
wish to raise this matter, and I am sorry
I am kept from calling attention to other
matters.
Mr. GRANT: I should like to support
my hon. Friend, the Member for Tyrone
and Fermanagh, as to the administration
of the unemployment benefit. I have hal
a great deal of experience. I expect my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Labour,
has anticipated what I am going to ask
about having a Committee set up. I would
impress upon him the necessity of taking
people into his confidence, who can give
him all real assistance, and that is the
trade union official, the full-time paid
official. I am sure, if he calls on them,
they will be only too pleased to give him
assistance. I am not criticising this particular Department. I want to see these
frauds wiped out.
.Mr. ANDREWS: I agree with everything my two hon. Friends have said.
After all, this is a legacy which we have
received from the Imperial Government,
and as we do not function until the first
of the new year we have not even had a
sporting chance of rectifying these admitted wrongs. I hope, before many
months, we will be able to have matters
to some extent improved.
As regards
I
what my hon. Friend has said in respect
to consulting Trade Union leaders, I
have already done so. I had representatives of the most important trade unions
as a deputation with me last week, representing one of the most important bodies
in the whole trade union movement.
got a great many very valuable suggestions
from them, and I got promises that they
will co-operate in every way possible with
the Committee I have appointed, in giving
evidence and in any other direction. These
matters have already had the earnest attention of myself and my staff, and we will
not spare ourselves in the future in trying
to do what is best for the people these
Acts are intended to benefit.

Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That this House do agree with the
Senate in the said Amendment."
Mr. COOTE: Would the right hon.
Gentleman explain the Amendment to us?
We have not seen this Amendment of the
other House, and this matter is rather
rushed, if I might say so. Would the
Minister in charge explain to us the effect
of this? We have nothing before us at
all. We want to know what the effect of
this might be. It might be to put some
person in prison or to take someone out
of it.
Mr. TWADDELL: I should like to
support the remarks of my hon. Friend,
the Member for Fermanagh and Tyrone
in order that we may know the effect of
the Amendment.
(Mr.
The ATTORNEY-GENERAL
Best): This is purely a drafting Amendment. In fact, I might say it is a grammatical amendment. The section as sent
from this House read as follows:-
--
Clause 2. This Act may be cited as the
Local Government (Emergency Powers) Act,
1921, and shall be construed as one with the
Local Government (Ireland) Acts, 1898 to 1919,
to the extent that those Acts are in force in
Northern Ireland.
As it stands there it is not grammatical,
and accordingly the Senate suggest an
Amendment that instead of the word
"that" the words "to which" should be
inserted, so that it will read "to the
extent to which those Acts are in force in
Northern Ireland." I suppose if we want
to have Acts of Parliament we want to
have them in grammatical form, and there
are defects enough in the interpretation
of them without having to set the grammar
right.
Question: "That this House do agree
with the Senate in the said Amendment,"
put, and agreed to.

Question: "That this House do now
adjourn," put, and agreed to.

9 DECEMBER 1921.

HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Friday, 9th December, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Business of the House.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That, notwithstanding anything contained in Standing Order No. 15, Paragraph 5, business other than the business
of Supply may be taken before half past
six o'clock this day."
Mr. BURN: Under the Standing
Order referred to it is provided that on
the allotted days for Supply, of which
this is one, no other business can be taken
before half past six. This necessarily
means that if the discussion on the
Estimates is ended to-day before that
hour we would be unable to take another
Order on the Paper, namely the Second
Reading of the Salaries of Ministerial
Offices Bill, and it is to enable us to do
so if necessary that I make this Motion
now. I have to make the Motion owing
to the fact that it does not appear on the
Order Paper, and I can only move it with
the general concurrence of the House.
Question put, and agreed to.
Orders of the Day.
SUPPLY-(2ND ALLOTTED DAY).
In Committee.
Mr. MOLES in the Chair.
CLASS I.
HOUSES OF PARLIAMENT.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That a sum not exceeding £2,695 be
granted to His Majesty to complete the
sum necessary for the service of the period
ending 31st March, 1922, to defray the
salaries and expenses of the Senate and
of the House of Commons (Voted on
Account £14,000).
Mr. COOTE: I desire to raise certain
matters upon this motion. I do not wish
-I do not think it would be proper considering that the Prime Minister, who
has something to do with some of these
negotiations, cannot be in the Houseto divide the House. Therefore, I do
not wish to move a concrete Amendment,
but I wish to call attention to certain
anomalies, and to make a formal protest.
In the salaries and the offices of une
House I see, in the first place, there is a
Counsel to the Speakers of the House of
Commons and of the Senate, who is Examiner of Petitions for Private Bills and
Taxing Officer for both Houses, at a fixed
salary of £1,000 rising to £1,200. We
also see in another place that there is
another Counsel being appointed, I think
to the Treasury. I would like to ask the
Minister in charge to-day if the Government contemplate keeping these salaried
officials, and what they will have to do
as whole-time officers? It needs some explanation. When the Minister replies I
will be very glad if he will take
matters.
a note of these two
I wish also to protest against
the cost of administering the Clerkships
of both Houses. I do not know on what
condition-and that is the reason I do
not wish to put down any amendmentthe two principal Clerks, that is the Clerk
of the House of Commons and the Clerk
of the Senate, who also has a joint office
as Clerk Assistant to the House of Commons at a salary of £1,500 a year, I am
at a loss to know how these appointments
were made, and if the Government had
regard to all the circumstances and the
whole situation, because on the face of
them they look very excessive salaries.
I do not put the matter further because
I quite expect an explanation. We also
see that following those two very responsible and very proper and very estimable
officials there is a second Clerk Assistant
to the House of Commons. He is
available for both Houses. Well, I can
conceive under certain circumstances that
these gentlemen's services will be required.
alternately as the case may be in either
House, but, apart from these officials,
where is the necessity for two assistant
clerks? Here we have five clerks between
the two Houses. Where is the necessity
for these two assistant clerks who follow
subsequently on the rota here at a salary
of £200 rising to £250 a year each?
Those are points I should like cleared up
by the Parliamentary and Financial
Secretary to the Ministry of Finance
when he replies. On the same page, Sub-
Head 'D," "Salaries, Wages, and
Allowances, we do with very great respect feel that the Serjeant-at-Arms and
Black Rod and all these offices are cost-
""
""
ing too much money. We feel they
should be cut down. What is the necessity for an Assistant Sergeant-at-Arms?
Is not it possible to have some gentleman
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
in some of the departments, some of the
secretaries, if you like some clerk, to fill
the position? We can quite understand
that, under circumstances when the
Serjeant-at-Arms might be obliged to
leave the House, we might have some
person to sit in his chair and perform
his duties, but not to have to pay for an
Assistant Serjeant-at-Arms in addition
to what we consider is already an excessive amount. This is an office that
could be filled with great propriety by
most military men. I hope nothing that
I am saying will be taken personally--I
am sure it will not-because we do esteem
the gentleman who at the moment
fills the office; but surely £600 plus £200
for the rent of a house is too much money
for the position of Serjeant-at-Arms for
the time this House is sitting, which we
hope, when we get into our stride, will
only be about three months in the year.
These are matters I wish to put before
the Treasury, and I do it in no censorious
spirit, but in the interests of all concerned
they ought to be considered.
There is another point I wish to
mention, and that is in regard to the
Permanent Secretary to the Cabinet.
have tried to get, and I have got, certain
information on this question. The present holder of this office, who was not a
civil servant, will now have conferred
upon him the dignity of a civil servant,
and, therefore, in addition to his salary
he will come in for bonus, and, if he survives in his office ten years, for pension.
Most of us take exception to the appointment of a Secretary and Assistant Secretrary to the Cabinet. We feel strongly
on the matter when we remember that
up till the war the Prime Minister of
Great Britain and Ireland had not an
official secretary. It seems out of place
that this small House, with its very
meagre income, should have attached to
the Cabinet and Prime Minister these
two very important officials.
I have dealt with the question of
Parliamentary Counsel. There are three
offices of this kind--Parliamentary
Counsel, who enjoys a salary of £1,000
rising to £1,200, Counsel to Mr. Speaker,
and Counsel to the Treasury. Surely we
do not want three counsel at salaries of
such importance. Surely those offices.
could in some way be amalgamated. I
do not know if that could be done, but
I suggest it. I hope that the Minister,
when he comes to reply, will give some
explanation of those three contemplated
appointments.
These are all the criticisms I wish to
bring to bear on the services of this House
and of the Ministry, but I do again plead
with the Minister to consider these secretarial offices, the number of them and
their value, the Serjeant-at-Arms and
the Assistant Serjeant-at-Arms, and also
Black Rod. I should say that we all
recognise gratefully the services of Black
Rod, and that the sum be put down
here-
The CHAIRMAN: I think I should
point out to the hon. Member that it is
somewhat delicate to discuss in connection
with the Ministry of Finance here the
sums paid for appointments over which
the Ministry has no control. It is somewhat delicate to discuss Black Rod who is
not a servant of this particular House,
the appointment being within the prerogative of the other Chamber.
Mr. COOTE: But, with great respect,
surely it is within the prerogative of the
House of Commons to discuss this matter.
The question of finance in regard to all
servants, whether they belong to the
Senate or the Commons, must come before
this House.
The CHAIRMAN: Yes, but once the
appointment has been approved, and the
salary fixed, it is difficult to see how you
can break a bargain by way of criticisin.
Mr. COOTE: I am very sorry. I
claim your indulgence. We did not enter
into any bargain with Black Rod, or with
these other officers.
The CHAIRMAN: For the good and
sufficient reason that he is not an officer
of this House. He has been appointed
by the other House. A bargain was
made as to the emoluments that he is to
receive for the services that he there
renders. Criticism directed to a breach
of that bargain is hardly in order.
Mr. COOTE: I must bow to your ruling,
but I demur to it. I must stand strongly
0.1 the point that the Senate cannot make
any bargain on any matter of finance that
cannot be amended in this House. If the

tribute to the very generous attitude of
the present holder of the office of Black
Rod. This salary is merely down here in
case he might resign and the office become
I mentioned the matter really
vacant.
