Question, "That this House do
adjourn," put, and agreed to.
now
Adjourned accordingly at a quarter
after Four of the clock.
Public Business.
Motion for Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
HOUSE OF COMMOMS,

Wednesday, 21st September, 1921.
The House met at a quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
MESSAGE TO THE SENATE.
Ordered, That a message be sent to the
Senate to request that the Senate will be
pleased to appoint an equal number of
members to join with the Commons on the
Kitchen and Refreshment Rooms (Joint
Committee), and to appoint the time and
place of meeting.
PUBLIC BUSINESS.
BILLS PRESENTED.
Bills were presented to deal with-
Exchequer and Audit-Bill to amend
the Law applicable to the Exchequer and
Consolidated Fund of Northern Ireland,
and to make provision with respect to the
Audit of the Accounts of that Fund, the
Receipt, Custody and Issue of Public
Moneys and the Powers, Duties, Tenure of
Office and Salary of the Comptroller and
Auditor-General of Northern Ireland.-
[The Minister of Finance (Mr. Pollock.)]
Read the first time; to be read a second
time to-morrow.
Exchequer (Temporary Borrowing.)-
Bill to authorise the borrowing and repayment of certain sums, which have been
placed to the credit of the Exchequer of
Northern Ireland. [The Minister of
Finance.]
Read the first time; to be read a second
time to-morrow.
Superannuation-Bill for the application of the Superannuation Acts, 1834 to
1914, to Officers of the Government and
Parliament of Northern Ireland and for
purposes connected therewith. [The
Minister of Finance.]
Read the first time; to be read a second
time to-morrow.
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
SUPPLY (COMMITTEE).
WAYS AND MEANS (COMMITTEE).
Postponed until to-morrow.-[Captain
Dixon.]
MOTION FOR ADJOUrnment.
Captain DIXON: I beg to
I beg to move-
"That this House do now adjourn."
Mr. LYNN: I have, in the first place,
to thank the Prime Minister for giving us
a day for the general consideration of the
Irish question in so far as it relates to
Ulster. If the situation was not so grave
we could get a good deal of amusement out
of the fact that three gentlemen-Mr. De
Valera, a Spaniard, Mr. Griffiths, a
Welshman, and Mr. Erskine Childers, an
Englishman, are all shrieking to high
heaven for the independence of Ireland.
But the question is much too serious to
deal with those aspects. We in Ulster,
as the Prime Minister pointed out in that
letter to Mr. Lloyd George, never wanted
a Parliament here. We were quite
satisfied to remain in the Imperial Parliament which we believed, and still believe,
was best suited for the needs of Ulster,
and for the whole of the United Kingdom.
I must say I sympathise with the Prime
Minister, because I think he has not been
fairly treated by Mr. Lloyd George. We
agreed to accept our Parliament in the
interests of peace, not because we believed
it was the best thing, but then the moment
we agreed to accept that Parliament we
find Mr. Lloyd George-who, let me say
at once, in my opinion, is very mercurial
-entering into a conference that is
calculated to upset the whole arrangement
that is embodied in the Government of
Ireland Act. It places the Ulster Government, and it places this Parliament, in a
very anomalous position. We have got
Dublin Castle, which can work and will
not work, we have got the Ulster Government, which would work but cannot work,
and we have got what must have struck
people as being something that ought not
to be in the body politic. We have got
Commandant O'Duffy, who does work and
seems to work as it seems to please him.
Now, whatever doubts may have arisen
with regard to the wisdom of Mr. Lloyd
78 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 74
21 SEPTEMBER 1921.
George going into conference with the
murderer of Mrs. Lindsay and many others,
I think there is no doubt that the Prime
Minister of Northern Ireland did the right
thing in agreeing to go into a conference
with Mr. Lloyd George and the Sinn Fein
representatives in London. I am sure it
was as disagreeable for him to go into that
conference as it would be to anyone
in
Northern Ireland. But he knows, as anyone
interested in practical politics knows, sometimes it is much easier to stop a dangerous
thing from happening than by having it
altered once it comes round.
I can say,
from practical experience, that the Prime
Minister has rendered very fine service to
Ulster in the past in that very direction.
There are many things I might refer to,
of inestimable value to Ulster, which he has
rendered when the Government of Ireland
Bill was going through the House. Some
people, of course, say that Ulster has no
interest in anything that happens between
Mr. Lloyd George and the Sinn Fein representatives. I deny that at once.
I say
that we are deeply interested in everything
that affects the United Kingdom and that
affects the British Empire. There is not a
stone in that Empire of ours-and we are
all as proud of it as the people in other
parts of the Empire-there is not a stone
in that edifice that Ulstermen have not
taken a part in laying, and laying soundly
and well. The Empire has been built by
the united co-operation and work of men
from all parts of the Empire, and we in
Northern Ireland can at least congratulate
ourselves that we have done our share in
building up that Empire. Therefore, I say
we have no intention of sitting with our
arms folded whilst others are trying to pull
the Empire to pieces. Our business is to
see that the Empire is kept strong, and
kept whole, especially in the heart.
We
know that the Sinn Feiners are out, as they
have admitted-quite honestly admitted
-to destroy the Empire. Therefore, I say
that the Prime Minister was quite right in
the attitude he has taken up.
Well, I am not going to weary this House
with regard to the correspondence. I think
Mr. De Valera has sent seven messages, and
seven, I believe, is a mystical number. The
only advantage I have derived from reading
these is the satisfaction of knowing that for
the transmission of two or three of them he
had to pay the Postmaster a little for the
telegrams. But I doubt if in other ways
they take us very far. We are told, of
course, by Mr. De Valera, when he is in
a cooing mood, that Ulster can have far
more from a Sinn Fein Parliament in Dublin
than it has ever got from the Imperial
Parliament. There are two comments I
wish to make on that. The first is that we
do not recognise this Sinn Fein rebel organi--
sation in Dublin. We want nothing from
it, and we are certainly not going to place
our liberties and our rights under the control of the men who have brought disgrace
on Ireland during the last few years. The
other point I make is this-That if we admitted the right of the Sinn Fein party to
give us this or that liberty, we would admit
the corresponding right of them to take it
away any time they choose. The Imperial
Parliament is good enough for us. We
mean to cling to the Imperial Parliament,
and we are not going to become subservient
to a Dublin Parliament.
I am glad the Prime Minister and his
Government have made that point clear.
The Prime Minister said yesterday, and
has said before in his letters, we have
nothing to give away, and we do not mean
to give anything away. There is nothing
to be gained by this humbug, by throwing
dust in the air and trying to make the
people believe you are really going to give
something when you are not. We have
reached the limits so far as we are côncerned, and we have no intention to
give anything away. I think this
Parliament should back up the Government and endorse what the Prime
Minister has said on that point-that we
have nothing to give away. It is said
also in some quarters that there is no
difference really between Mr. Lloyd George
and Mr De Valera. I think there is all
the difference in the world between loyalty
and disloyalty there is the difference
between Union and Separation. What is
it that Mr. Lloyd George is fighting for
at the moment? Why, he is fighting for
the very same principle that Lincoln
fought for many years ago in America
when the American nation carried on war
for four years, lost a million of men, and
had untold loss in life and property
rather than allow separation to take place.
Mr. Lloyd George in one of his recent
messages said if he agreed to what the
Sinn Fein leader asks he would be disloyal to his King, and disloyal to the
Empire. That put the whole case in a
nutshell. The two parties to this controversy approach it
it from absolutely
different standpoints. One wants to
maintain the British Empire, which, I
think, in the judgment of this House, is
one of the best instruments for civilisation
the world has ever seen. The other
wants to destroy that and put in its place,
75 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 76
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Lynn.]
I suppose, something like a Soviet in
Russia. It is rather strange that the
Russian Soviet is being run as Sinn Fein
in Ireland is being run-by men who are
not natives of the country at all. Mr.
SPEAKER, I hold-I may be wrong-I hold
the opinion very strongly that Mr. De
Valera is not sincere in his protestations
with regard to peace. If he wants peace
in Ireland between Ireland and Great
Britain he would never put forward the
absurd claim that he has put forward of
Ireland a Nation; Ireland an independent
State, Ireland a Republic. It is absolutely
absurd to think of Ireland as an independent State. It is absurd, as any student
of history knows, to contend that at the
time the English landed in Ireland, Ireland
was an independent State with independent institutions; that these institutions were destroyed, and that in
their place we had alien institutions.
Every political institution that Ireland
ever possessed has been of English origin.
We hear a good deal with regard to racial
limits. Mr. SPEAKER, the English are not
all Teutons nor are the Irish all Celts. In
my humble judgment there is really no
racial frontier in the United Kingdom. I
think, from the race point of view, Mr.
de Valera has no claim whatever to put
forward for the establishment of an independent race. As I say, there was no real
independent State. When the Norman
English arrived in this country-from the
time of St. Patrick until the coming of
the Normans-large parts of Ireland were
inhabited by pre-Milesians, who had been
subdued by the Milesians. Sometimes one
is inclined to ask who are the real Irish,
and if we went into that minute question
probably we could put forward a better
claim in Northern Ireland than in any
part of the country, because in the North-
East of Ireland the people were never subdued by the Milesians. One might almost
say that in Elizabethan times the North-
East of Ireland was the part that struck
out strongly against the rule of Eliza
bethan Ministers, and I think, Mr.
SPEAKER, there were some clans who bore
your name that put up a very sturdy fight
and absolutely refused to surrender. So
that, so far as North-East Ireland is concerned, it never was under the rest of
Ireland. Certainly, so far as we are concerned, we are determined it will not go,
at least so far as we can prevent it. As I
understand history, I think Mr. de
Valera cannot make out a case for the
independence of Ireland.
Well, with regard to never having
acknowledged the English Kings, one has
only got to look down the records. I do
not want to weary the House with the
matter, but there are one or two points
I would just like to mention. The first
is that Henry the Second, who came here
on the invitation of the infallible Pope,
was received by the Irish people, who
agreed to pay tribute to him. As far
back as 1541 the Irish Parliament conferred the title of King of Ireland upon
In 1641 a conference of
Henry VIII.
Roman Catholic clergymen at Kilkenny
declared Charles as their just and lawful
Sovereign. The first act of the Roman
Catholic Parliament, in 1669, was pre
recognising James II. as King. Another
more modern point is that really this
question of Irish independence was first
introduced in the middle of last century
by the Fenians. These Fenians were excommunicated by the Vatican the
ground that they were conspiring against
lawful government. Sir, on the grounds
of history Mr. de Valera fails in the case
he is trying to make out.
on
But there are other reasons why I think
Mr. de Valera is not sincere in his offers
of peace.
One of these is that he has
not kept the truce. From the very beginning of the truce up to the present
moment the Sinn Feiners have been
utilising the opportunity for the purpose
of increasing their army and increasing
the efficiency of their murder gang, and
the Prime Minister of England would
have been justified if he had refused after
the first week to go on any longer. That
truce ought to have been kept; it has not
been kept. Mr. Lloyd George has been
very patient, very long suffering, and
very indulgent in dealing with the Sinn
Feiners. My only fear is that for his
patience, his indulgence, his long suffering, a terrible price will have to be paid
by lawful and law-abiding citizens in
Ireland, who see men who have carried
on a campaign of murder during the last
two years, strengthening their arms,
and getting ready for another campaign. Surely if there had been
desire for peace
on the part of
a
the
Sinn Feiners, they would have
accepted at once Mr. Lloyd George's
offer. In my humble judgment the terms
that Mr. Lloyd George offered were not
only generous, but they were generous
to the point of folly. I think he has gone
far, too far, in the offer he has made.
We have been told about the great freedom America has, but we have a number
Debate on Adjournment.

21 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment.
of things granted to us that no American | first occasion that I have an opportunity
State has. No American State is given
control of the postal service, no American
State has active control of the railway
services, no American State is given control of the public health, and I think-my
honourable Friend, the member for Fermanagh and Tyrone, will know better-
I think none or very few of the American
States have the right to control the
liquor traffic in the same way as it will be
controlled by a Parliament in Northern
Ireland or a Parliament in Southern Ireland. No American State has any control over the coinage or the currency, or
the foreign trade.
re-
Those of us who are members of the
Imperial House of Commons will
member in November of last year, on
the third reading debate on the Government of Ireland Bill, in the House of
Commons, Mr. Lloyd George made a very
effective speech, and two of the most important points he made in that speech
were with regard to fiscal autonomy
and the creation of
army in
Ireland. He pointed out very fittingly
and in a manner that would, I think,
have appealed to my friend the Chancellor of the Exchequer, how absurd it
was to have two fiscal systems for Great
Britain and Ireland.
an
I believe every business man will see
it is an absurdity to set up two fiscal
systems for a small unit like Great Britain
and Ireland. Another point he made,
and that he stressed even more than the
fiscal point, was with regard to establishing an Irish army. He pointed out to the
Labour people especially, that if the Irish
Parliament-the Southern Parliament--
had the right to create an army it would
mean conscription for Great Britain. He
pointed out, with very good effect on that
occasion, that Ireland could easily raise
an army of five hundred thousand, and
that with that army in Southern Ireland
it would be absolutely necessary for Great
Britain to have conscription.
SPEAKER, nothing has happened since
November to alter the danger. The only
thing that has altered has been Mr. Lloyd
George's opinions. We who live in Ireland, and can look at it from two points
of view from the Imperial and local point
of view-believe it would be absolutely
dangerous, and a constant menace to
peace in Ireland and a constant menace to
the peace of the United Kingdom, to have
an army in Ireland except under Imperial
control; and, therefore, I want on the very
Mr.
of speaking on the subject to protest as
strongly as I can against the idea of
Ireland being allowed to raise and maintain an army,
If Southern Ireland is
allowed to raise and maintain an army
then Northern Ireland will also have to
raise and maintain an army, and it will
lead to the unpleasant situation. when
France and Germany were watching one
another for about 40 years, standing to
arms, standing looking at each other,
waiting possibly to create an opportunity
for using those arms. Why has the Prime
Minister gone so far? Well, he is a bighearted man; he is a man of great fascination, and those who have come in contact
with him like his personality, but if I had
to decide between Mr. Lloyd George and
Pitt, I would have Pitt. Pitt saw the
situation in Ireland in critical times in
Great Britain, and he saw how impossible
and dangerous it would be to have two
armies, and, therefore, he brought about
the Union, and certainly in any powers
that are delegated to a subordinate
Parliament the question of the army is
one that should never be delegated. The
terrible situation in Ireland really might
have been over long ago if it had not been
for the weakness-even the cowardice-of
successive Governments.
It
I
It is not necessary in this House to deal
with the different causes that have led up
to the present deplorable state of affairs.
We know it has been due to weakness.
was due to weakness in 1906, and weakness has been characterising the Irish
Government all through. We all thought
when Mr. Birrell left it would be
impossible to find a greater misfit.
think Mr. Birrell, though he has gone,
has left a great many of the same kind in
his place. The weakness that has been
characterising the present Government in
its dealing with Ireland is simply deplorable, and has been more deplorable of
late than it was even six months ago.
One cannot wonder that the ignorant Sinn
Feiner-who really does not know very
much outside his own small districtcan one wonder that he thinks the
British Government has been beaten to
its knees That is really the cause of the
trouble in Ireland at the present moment.
The average Sinn Feiner imagines he has
beaten the British Government to its
knees. The British Government really
never started to operate. The Irish
people should realise that patient and
tolerant as Great Britain always is, there
is a limit to her tolerance, and the time
Debate on Adjournment. HoUSE OF COMMONS
Debate on Adjournment.
[Mr. Lynn.]
may come when the British people will
take in hand the restoration of order in
Ireland, and will carry it out as
effectively as they have always carried out
any serious undertaking in the past.
But Sinn Feiners are playing on the
tolerance of the British Government, and
they are playing on the tolerance of the
people of Ulster as well. The moment
the truce was announced there was an
intensified campaign in Ulster. Gunmen
have been imported during July and
August, large quantities of
arms and
ammunition have been imported, and at
the present moment over the six counties
there are large Sinn Fein camps which are
indulging in all forms of military exercises,
including bomb-throwing. Is there any
other government save the Government in
Dublin Castle that would tolerate that
sort of thing? .I am sure if my right
hon. Friend opposite (the Prime
Minister) were in control this sort of
thing would be stamped out quickly.
They would make it clear to these people
that the law cannot be broken in this
fashion, but so long as we are hanging,
like Mahomet's coffin, between heaven and
earth, it is impossible to get things done.
I think it is unfair to our Ministry
because they are being blamed by outsiders who do not know the facts, and do
not understand the difficulties that are
in the way of the Ulster Government
functioning, especially in regard to the
preservation of law and order. I think
the campaign in Ulster in July and
August was started for two purposes-one
the foolish purpose of terrorising the
Ulster people into accepting some scheme
for a Sinn
Sinn Fein republic. Whatever
other faults the Ulsterman has got, one
is not that he will submit to terrorism no
matter what form it takes. Ulstermen
have been face to face with even greater
terror than the Spanish-Welsh-English
combination can bring forward, and they
are not going to be terrorised. One
reason why this campaign was carried on
in Ulster was for the purpose of getting
up a propaganda across the Channel
against Ulster.
Speaking as a journalist, I am sorry to
say there are some newspapers-I am
glad to think they are few-that are
really in lineal descent from Ananias, and
who publish falsehoods with regard to
Ulster. Any falsehood with regard to
Ulster is good enough for newspapers of
the stamp of the "Daily News," and they
have utilised this campaign that has been
carried on in Ulster for the purpose of
vilifying Ulster, and trying to show that
the Prime Minister and his Cabinet are
the real villains of the piece in Ireland.
But, unfortunately for them, that part
of the campaign has been destroyed by
the bluntness of Commandant O'Duffy.
Commandant O'Duffy probably is suffer
ing from what is vulgarily known as
"swollen head," but Commandant O'Duffy
was good enough to boast that he had put
his men on the street and that they had
been very effective. I believe the first
day they were on the streets the casualties
were sixty; and they shot down people
about the centre of Belfast and in other
streets in Belfast regardless, I think, of
whether they were Protestant or Roman
Catholic. Their idea was to create terrorism, to get the blame put for the
trouble on the so-called Orange mob.
The Orangemen of Belfast had nothing to
do with the trouble. They have been very
tolerant under great provocation, and
I hope they will remain tolerant,
because they have a strong case,
and it is not their business to play into
the hands of the enemy. But it is worthy
of note, and of serious note, that the
moment Commandant O'Duffy, the confidant of my Friend's friend, the Assistant
Under-Secretary in Dublin Castle, to
whom he referred yesterday, that as soon
as he gives the order to cease firing the
order to cease firing is obeyed, and then
Belfast lapses into peace, and the moment
Commandant O'Duffy gives his order to
start firing the trouble breaks out, and
hundreds of innocent people are shot
down.
I say that is an intolerable state
of affairs, intolerable that, for the sake
of propaganda, a gentleman of this type
should be allowed to hold a great city
like Belfast at his mercy.
And I want.
in the most serious fashion, to warn the
Government that the patience of the
people of Belfast and of Ulster generally
has nearly reached breaking point. Now
for months they do not know when someone is going to fall to the assassin's bullet.
That is a state of affairs that should not
be allowed in any well-regulated or wellorganised community. We ought to have
peace, and nothing else is worth having
in this country if we cannot have
peace and order maintained; and the
very first thing the Ulster Government
has got to do is to re-establish peace in
this part of Ireland at any rate. If Mr.
de Valera wants peace why does he allow
his followers in Belfast to behave as they
81 Debate on Adjournment.

21 SEPTEMBER 1921. Debate on Adjournment.
have been behaving for months? At his
bidding murders ceased in other parts of
Ireland, so we may safely assume from
this that he controls those who carry out
the murders, otherwise he could not stop
them inside a few hours. If he wants
peace in Ulster why does not he call off
his murder men, and let us live in peace
as we want to? Human nature being
what it is, I say there is a limit to
patience, and that limit may be overreached before we are much older. This
House has no desire to see the mob taking
control. We say that crime must be
suppressed, that law must be maintained,
but these are things that must be done
by the recognised authorities, and the
recognised authorities must see to it that
law and order are maintained.
I to the Prime Minister and to his
say
colleagues-I do not ask them to say what
steps they propose to take in order to
suppress crime, and to restore law and
order but I say this, that in any steps
taken towards that end they may count
on the whole-hearted support of this
House and of the loyal people throughout
the Six Counties. We are determined to
suppress that disorder, we are determined
that in this part which has been allo-
Icated to us, every man, no matter
what his religion, and no matter what his
political views, will live under the flag of
freedom, and that all law-abiding citizens
will be treated in the same way under
the law, and that all men will be free to
go about their business and do what is
best without fear of being shot.
I say, in conclusion, that I am sure the
earnest prayer of every Member of this
House is that we should have peace, not
only here, but throughout the whole of
Ireland. Nothing in the world is easier
than to preach a vendetta against political
opponents, nothing would be easier with
all the material they have placed at our
disposal than for us to preach a vendetta
against Sinn Fein. In the whole history
of Ireland there never has been such a
record of crime. Their steps are spattered
with blood. They have disgraced the
country, but because they have done
wrong is no reason why we should do
wrong. Our business is to approach this
whole question in the words of Lincoln:
With malice towards none, with charity
to all.
We have no feeling of ill-will against
our brethren in other parts of Ireland.
All we ask of them is that they let us live
F
in peace, and we can assure them if they
do we will allow them to live in peace. We
want to co-operate so far as co-operation is
possible in those things which make for
the advancement of the welfare of the
country. But, Mr. SPEAKER, we want at
this first Session of this Parliament to
make it clear and plain that so far as we
are concerned we are not going to submit
to the terrorism that has disgraced and
outraged Ireland, and made it a disgrace
among the nations of the world.
Mr.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON:
SPEAKER, may I crave the indulgence of
the House on this occasion. I am not an
old Parliamentary hand, and I hesitate
to-day to say a few words on the very important matters that were referred to
yesterday by the Prime Minister, who
gave us a very full, a very concise, and a
very illuminating statement on the whole
position of affairs in connection with the
Northern Parliament as he understands
it at the present moment. I think the
first part of his statement, if I may say so,
was devoted to the history of matters since
the opening of our Parliament by His
Gracious Majesty the King, and events in
detail that have taken place since then.
I beg leave to say that the course of action
that he followed in consultation with his
Cabinet in regard to the invitation extended to him by the British Prime
Minister-the course he followed was
received with the fullest approbation, not
only by the Members of this House, but by
the people as a whole.
It is hardly necessary for me to go into
the reasons why the course he followed was
the wise course to follow. I think he himself yesterday told us in the most effective
way the reasons that induced him to accept
the invitation extended to him, and to my
mind these reasons were absolutely conclusive. I think the letter which the Prime
Minister read, setting forth his reasons in
full, must remain an historic document. It
stated fully and clearly that Ulster had
reached the limit of her concessions, and that
she had nothing more to give away.
Now
I think this House and the people of Ulster
perceived in the course of action followed
by the Prime Minister and the Members of
his Cabinet they were acting in harmony
with the wishes of the people, and I think
the wisdom of the course followed has been
amply justified by events since. I can quite
appreciate the difficult position in which
the Prime Minister and his colleagues
found themselves. And let me say here
that Ulster has passed through many
83 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 84
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Sir Robert Anderson.]
critical periods in her history and has had
many difficulties to contend with, and her
leaders have had many anxious and worrying moments, but, to my mind, the events
of the past two or three months must have
been the most worrying and anxious time
that any leader could possibly pass through.
I think also that it is only right for me
to say, not only what I believe to be my
own views, but the views of the people as
a whole, that we have been wisely guided.
We have had absolute confidence in the
course pursued by our leaders, and we have
had, above all, what I hope we will always
have, absolute unity and harmony-not only
in our Parliamentary ranks, but absolute
harmony and support throughout the province. That has been Ulster's strength and
mainstay during the past thirty years, and
as long as Ulster is absolutely united and
firm in her conviction as to the course she
should follow, having a clean-cut policy.
and determined to pursue that policy with
leaders who will carry out what is the expressed mind of the people of Ulster, then
I am absolutely convinced we need not have
the slightest fear as to what the future may
have in store for us. Up to that point I
think we may say the course has been pretty
clear. One point that has been emphasized
in this debate by every speaker has been
the maintenance of law and order. There
is another matter equally important that
is, how quickly we can get to work and carry
out the functions that this Parliament was
created to carry out. I quite recognise the
difficulties that we have had to contend
with. Mr. SPEAKER, I beg leave to say,
although I possibly may disagree with some
of those who have spoken in this debate,
that at this particular stage, when the
Prime Minister is negotiating with the
spokesmen representing Southern Ireland,
there would be a great danger if we
unnecessarily precipitated matters.
I am not at all sure of the wisdom of
making a demand to take over immediately the police, force. The British.
Government are responsible at present for
law and order in this country. They are
responsible for it in the Northern Parliamentary area, and I believe I must
accept the statement, and I accept it
without the slighest hesitation-the statement made yesterday by the Prime
Minister that he and his colleagues have
done their very utmost to impress upon
the Imperial authorities the necessity of
maintaining law and order and putting
down crime in every form in this Northern
am
area. I hope they will use the utmost
pressure they possibly can to see that life
and property are protected until such
time as we have the full control of the
police force of the province. I
absolutely certain they are convinced it
is their duty to impress upon
authorities that they should maintain law
and order, give confidence to the people
and give security. Without security no
government can possibly exist; society
itself would be dissolved. The foundation
of all good government, the foundation of
civilised society is the maintenance of
law and order, and I am perfectly certain
that until such times when Ulster is able
to take over the police force it is the duty
and the imperative duty of the Imperial
authorities to send sufficient forces here
to prevent people from being molested, to
prevent their property being destroyed,
and to secure the liberty of the subject.
Now, that is the first aspect of the question
raised by the Prime Minister yesterday
in his most admirable and comprehensive
statement.
I wish to refer to another matter which
is bound up, most inevitably bound up, in
the present negotiations at present taking
place between representatives of Southern
Ireland and Mr. Lloyd George. Ulster
cannot look on at these negotiations without feeling that her interests are bound
to be affected by such negotiations. We
have passed through, as I have stated,
critical periods-very critical periodsand successfully passed through them up
to the present moment. Now we mark
another stage, and, to my mind, a very
serious stage in connection with the
Government of Northern Ireland. When
the 1920 Bill was passed Northern Ireland and Southern Ireland were placed
upon the basis of equality. The laws
governing the Parliament of Southern
Ireland were the same as the laws governing Northern Ireland. From the financial
standpoint the question of police,
the control of education-everything was
exactly on a similar basis. Crown Colony
Government was to be established in the
South of Ireland in case they would not
fulfil certain conditions. The same thing
exactly applied to Northern Ireland if
Northern Ireland would not fulfil the
same conditions. Accordingly all Ireland
was to be treated equally fair; financial
relations to the Imperial Parliament are
exactly the same, but anyone who
reads now the proposals made by Mr.
85 Debate on Adjournment. 21 SEPTEMBER 1921

Debate on Adjournment.
Lloyd George to Southern Ireland cannot
but see that Ulster is going to be vitally
affected. Just think for a moment of one
or two of the special things that will
affect us.
Customs and Excise. These are to be
handed over to the Southern Parliament.
Those of us who sat in the Irish Convention fought that question with all our
might for many months. Then the question of the contribution towards Imperial
services-that is to be a matter for the
two Parliaments to decide just as they
like themselves. Well, you can have a
fair idea of what the contribution will be
from one of the Parliaments if it comes
into existence. Then the other question
it is a very serious one-the question
of the National Debt, and the suggestion
made by Mr. Lloyd George is this-that
failing an agreement an arbitrator is to
be appointed, in order to determine what
the contribution of Ireland shall be for
that
particular purpose. If these
things are offered now to Southern Ireland
I am not at all sure that the British
Government have even yet reached the
limit of their concessions. I hold that we
will reach a stage here when we will be up
against these proposals made to Southern
Ireland, which, if carried into effect,
will have a very serious bearing upon
the
prosperity and the progress of the territory over which we shall have control.
I hold if there is to be an arrangement
of this character, that the basis underlying the 1920 Act was the basis of absolute equality. I am sure that our
Government-the Prime Minister and
our Cabinet-will see to it that Ulster,
under the re-arrangement that may take
place if a conference is held by Mr. Lloyd
George with Mr. de Valera and the representatives of Dail Eireann-will not be
prejudiced by those proposals. I take
leave to say that it is not the duty of any
British Government to reward rebellion
at the expense of loyalty, and that we are
not to be placed in a false position—in a
position in which we cannot carry on our
industries and advance the progress and
prosperity of Ulster as compared with
those in the Southern area. I am sure it
is hardly necessary for me to say, or even
suggest, to the Prime Minister that when
these suggestions are made, if we reach
that stage in the negotiations, Ulster
will be in a position of extreme gravity.
I am sure he will consult Parliament here
in regard to what course will be followed.
I could pursue this line of argument in
regard to what we ought to do and what
we should demand, but I hesitate to prolong my speech knowing there are others
here who would desire to take part in
this debate.
Now these are the two special things I
wish to refer to. There are other matters
calling for immediate attention I desire to
refer to in a sentence or two-
The PRIME MINISTER: Perhaps my
hon. Friend will allow me, in the middle
of his excellent speech, if I may say so,
to just requote the terms of our letter to
the Prime Minister on the very point to
which my hon. Friend the Member for
Derry City (Sir Robert Anderson) has referred. It is-
In adopting this course we rely on the
British people.
We go beyond the Prime Minister of England, beyond the Cabinet, and beyond the
Parliament, and we go to the British people.
We rely upon the British people who
charged us with the responsibility of undertaking our own Parliamentary institution to
safeguard the ties that bind us to Great
Britain and the Empire, and to ensure that
we are not prejudiced by any terms entered
into between them and Mr. De Valera, and
to maintain the just equality exhibited
throughout the Government of Ireland Act.
I think that covers the point.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: That fully
covers the point I desire to make, but
there has been a good deal of loose thinking
abroad in connection with this important
matter. I am not one of those who will put
myself in a position of great disadvantage,
but I will try as far as I can not to put,
the province in a position of disadvantage
in connection with its development and progress if those other schemes mature.
glad, indeed, that the Prime Minister has
quoted that very important paragraph,
which I think fully covers the point I
desired to comment upon.
I am
The other matter has to do with education.
I think the question of secondary education
is a matter of the most urgent and pressing
importance just now. I think every
Member of the House fully understands and
appreciates the position that secondary
teachers and secondary schools find themselves in at this present moment.
There is nothing in the way of a grant,
as I understand it, to be expected from
the British Treasury; and the secondary
teachers and the schools are looking to this
Parliament for assistance and support to
tide them over a difficult period. Now,
I know that a Commission or a Committee
87 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 88
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Sir Robert Anderson.]
is going to be appointed to take up the
whole question of education in its various
branches-a most admirable thing.
These Commissions and these Committees
are appointed for a purpose that I need
not indicate; Members of the House will
know exactly why they are appointed,-
but I do not believe such a thing applies
in this case.
I believe that those who are
in charge of education are anxious and
desirous at the earliest possible moment
to effect an improvement all round, and
place education in Ulster in a position at
least equal to what we find it in England
and in Scotland. I know that my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Finance, will
tell us that he has got no money. That
is the answer that all Chancellors give.
He will tell you that the Joint Exchequer
Board has not come into existence yet;
that he does not know when it is to come
into existence, and that, consequently, he
cannot tell what the financial position is
going to be. Now, I understand that my
right hon. Friend, the Finance Minister,
will to-morrow give us some indication
of how he looks upon matters financial in
connection with this Parliament. I have
my own ideas on the subject. I have
never hesitated to declare them.
I may
have an opportunity to-morrow of dealing
with the subject in detail; but I see upon
the Order Paper that there is going to be
money to pay all the Cabinet Ministers,
and I do not object to that at all, provided
the figures are reasonable.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Do you include the
Members?
""
Well,
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON:
now, I must answer that question with a
famous quotation from another Statesman-my hon, Friend will have to wait
and see. "" I think we will have to settle
down to a bit more business before we
receive remuneration. In any event, on a
Vote on Account, I presume we can have
money for those other objects that have
been named, and salaries for the different
Departments, and rightly so.
The same
principle there is no use in telling the
House that the principle is differentstands for voting a reasonable amount to
strengthen the education carried on in
secondary schools. We all know that they
haye reached the point of collapse.
Teachers are leaving the country because
they are not sufficiently remunerated, and
the schools themselves are not able to carry
on. Now, if there is one thing that we
in this Parliament should do it is that
we should sacrifice a great deal in order to
have education in all its branches efficiently
maintained, and I hope the Parliamentary Secretary for Education will be able
to give some assurance to the House that
this matter is going to be dealt with
fully and promptly, and not merely give
us a promise of a Committee or something of that kind.
I hope something more will be forthcoming, or if it is not forthcoming it may
be necessary to test the House upon this
whole particular question of a grant to
assist those who are labouring in connection with secondary education, and who
ought to receive all the help and support
this Parliament can give. I do not
intend to occupy the time of the House
any longer. I thank the House for their
indulgence on the first occasion on which
I have spoken in it. I think we have
met, let me say, on the whole in very happy
and encouraging circumstances. I am no
pessimist in regard to this matter. We
have had unprecedented difficulties to
contend with since we received this Parliament-difficulties which could not have
been foreseen. They have been met in
the most admirable manner so far, and I
believe with the hearty co-operation of all
the Members of this House, working
steadily together with one set purpose,
with unity and harmony throughout
Ulster, all working for the common good.
-I believe this Parliament of ours wil!
be a splendid instrument to advance the
prosperity, happiness, and contentment of
the masses of the people over whom we
have jurisdiction.
Dr. MORRISON: I would just like to
say a word or two regarding this question
of education introduced by the hon.
Member for Co. Londonderry (Sir Robert
Anderson). I speak in regard to this
matter as a representative of the
secondary teachers. These secondary
teachers represent 80 per cent. of the
secondary teachers of Ireland, and I am
giving you the views they hold on this
matter. I am glad to see the hon.
Member for County Londonderry
interested in this matter, and I am very
glad also to know that my friend, one of
the hon. Members for Tyrone and
Fermanagh, is also interested in the
matter, but we who represent the university consider ourselves specially the
representatives of the secondary teachers,
and indeed we think on those matters connected with education we have a special
right to be consulted and to speak for
educational matters generally. After

21 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment.
are
consulting with the secondary teachers I
arranged yesterday for a conference with
the Prime Minister this afternoon on this
very important matter, and the University
Members and those other Members representing rural constituencies who
interested in this question, and whose
help and assistance we very gladly welcome, are to come with us and see the
Minister of Education, and also the Prime
Minister to see if the very serious difficulties that arise in the financial position
cannot be overcome. I think it would be
an unwise thing for the representatives of
education in this House to give a definite
answer until we have an opportunity of
trying to persuade them this money must
be forthcoming. If members take up a
position and commit themselves to-day
there might be difficulty in getting them
to change their minds. I would be glad
if they would not commit themselves until
they have an opportunity of hearing the
arguments we are going to put before
them, but without any disrespect intended
I want to impress upon them if this
money is not by some means forthcoming
-I am not going now into the financial
position at all, but if the money is not
forthcoming by
by pleasant, agreeable,
courteous methods, we are determined to
adopt methods which I hope will not be
necessary.
Now I think that is a very
wise suggestion to make. I make it in the
interests of peace, and I must say this
that from the Prime Minister down, my
colleagues in the University recognise the
courtesy with which they have been
treated, and I can assure the teachers and
the House if there is any action by which
this very serious and difficult position
can be remedied with the Prime Minister
and Minister of Education they are perfectly willing to do their utmost in the
matter.
Mr. McGUFFIN: I only intend to
occupy the attention of the House for a
short time, and perhaps I might be permitted in the opening portion of my speech
to congratulate the Member for Derry
City (Sir Robert Anderson) on the very
excellent speech he has delivered here today. It has been my privilege to hear
many speeches in the Imperial House of
Commons, but I question very much if
in my entire experience I ever heard a
more lucid speech than that just made
by the Member for Derry. I congratulate
him with all my heart, and hope it is only
an augury of even greater performances
Another
in this new House of Commons.
thing I would like to say is that I would
like to refer to the very candid and
courageous statement made in the course
of the Prime Minister's speech yesterday.
We all have the utmost sympathy with
the Prime Minister in the tremendous
ordeal he is passing through at the
moment in the inauguration of the new
Parliament. I am sure I am only voicing
the feelings of all present when I wish him
the greatest possible success.
And I may
add that if he displays his usual courage
in the pursuit of his duties in this matter
we can look forward to a very signal
success. One thing about the Prime
Minister that always impresses me is his
optimism. If he will permit me to say
so, I was reminded during his speech.
yesterday of a quotation from Shakepeare's "Julius Caesar.” I have an impression that the great Caesar, according
t Shakespeare, had some such personage
as the Prime Minister when he said :.
"Let me have men about me who are
fat."
་
And then again he said:-
"Yond Cassius has a lean and hungry
look."
If it is true, as this great observer has
said, that thin men are dangerous, then
the reverse is true that fat men are
trustworthy. However, Mr. SPEAKER,
while we all have the greatest possible
respect for
the authority of the
Prime Minister, we cannot, perhaps,
on all occasions agree with him.
And my Friend, the hon. Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote), took
occasion to criticise the Prime Minister's
speech. There is no person in the House
believes more in the sincerity of the hon.
Gentleman than I do. I believe that
under all circumstances he speaks according to his convictions, but I am afraid
that we will have to take time to analyse
it to see that he does not exert undue
influence, or disproportionate influence as
compared with the matters involved. It
appears to me that the hon. Gentleman
had made up his mind to a certain course;
but I am quite convinced of this-that
the Prime Minister's speech, as it appeared
to me, was a very guarded utterance, the
utterance, in all probability, of a person
who was conscious of the difficulties of the
situation, but who was not at liberty to
confess them, though he was called on at
the moment to recognise them. If the
hon. Gentleman, just like myself, knew
Debate on Adjournment. 92
Debate on Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. McGuffin.]
all that worried Cabinet Ministers at the
moment it might modify his opinion.
But I am not concerned at the moment
about our Cabinet. Our Cabinet, in my
opinion, is all right. There is a criticism
of the Cabinet abroad, but what Cabinet
was ever constructed about which there
was no criticism? I have heard it said,
as representing the opinion of the man in
the street, that the Prime Minister is very
much like a celebrated football player
who was so conscious of his own ability
that he always thought his team could win
on whatever ground they played. He
felt so absolutely conscious himself of his
ability. Some seem to think that the
Prime Minister perhaps presumes on his
great experience as a Parliamentarian, or
his experience in Government, that he
will be quite capable of conducting the
affairs of the Northern Parliament even
with a mediocre Cabinet. But I am not
concerned about our Cabinet-I am concerned about the Cabinet of England at
the moment. I am not one of those of
the opinion that we can afford to be indifferent to the situation as we find it.
In fact I am sure we cannot. What is
the situation? I am bound to think at
the moment that the position is only representative of an alternative policy by
the British Parliament to the one which
it formerly pursued, and which, alas,
failed. It tried a policy of repression,
and those who were permitted to be in
the House of Commons and to hear the
Chief Secretary, can remember how he
boasted on several occasions that he had
the gunmen on the run-that he had Sinn
Fein by the throat. You could almost
have fancied you saw the monster with
its fangs extracted lying a helpless corpse
at his feet. But is that the case? It is
not the case, and I would ask this House
to consider for one moment if there was
the slightest probability that we would
have had conferences and negotiations if
the Government had succeeded in its renot
pressive policy in Ireland. I do
but
believe for one moment we would,
when the Government failed in that
they had to devise a fresh policy, and
what I have got to say is just this,
that the two words that are most in
the air at the moment are "conference" and
"negotiation." Now, I have heard misgivings amongst our own supporters with
regard to how this conference came about.
We cannot forget that the first conference
that took place was an irresponsible one
between representatives of the North and
the South of Ireland.
We had De Valera on the one side and
our own Prime Minister on the other, engaged in what was, perhaps, an informal
conference, but after all a conference, and
then later when the difficulty became accentuated, and when the British Prime Minister
saw there was no way out of it, Mr. Lloyd
George said to himself, "Now that there
has been a conference between representatives of Northern and Southern
Ireland may I not come in too, and
see what I can do in the way of a conference, and invite both representatives
to come together with myself as umpire,
and with the glory of determining the problem." In working out that policy the King's
visit takes place at the opening of our
Parliament, and what we have to recollect
is this, that the Speech delivered by His
Majesty, though it was, I believe, endorsed
with all his heart, was not, after all, his
Speech. It was a Speech written by a
Member of the Cabinet, endorsed by the
Cabinet as a whole, and as such it was
delivered here. The British Prime Minister
waits to ascertain the effect of the King's
Speech, and when he finds it acclaimed
throughout the country he comes forward
and says,
"This is the psychological
moment to have a conference," and accordingly he proposes a conference with Sinn
Fein. Now, I need not go into the history
of this conference. The negotiations up to
this moment are a bit confusing. I think
they are even a subject of confusion to
those concerned, but I will not say anything more about that. I heard the
Prime Minister of England say,
in response to Sir Edward Carson,
when he put his question directly
to him at the introduction of the Bill
which is now an Act, If we accept this
will you put it into operation," and Mr.
Lloyd George replying, turned, his head and
said, "I will." Has he fulfilled that pledge,
and if not why not? Is there a condition
in the Bill that he will furnish an alternative to that course? There is.
There is. Why
does he not furnish it? The only reason
that can be assigned is that his adminis
tration in the country has been a failurefailure to restore law and order in the
country. His attitude is simply one of
capitulation to Sinn Fein. He says in
effect, "We cannot repress you, and we
will try and negotiate with you." That is
the position. If we are not going to get the
Act as it is framed, and as we understood
it would be put into operation, what are
we going to get? Are there going to be
two different forms of administration in the
.
