Dublin, from the existing departments.
But in the meantime it has been necessary
to appoint a considerable number of the
clerical staff and typists, and these have
all been appointed by a Selection Board
which I have had specially appointed in
order that not only the question of
remuneration, but also the question of
stability and status and fitness should be
This Selection Board
fully considered.
has, as I say, already made a number of
appointments in connection with the
clerical staff which may be useful in indicating the permanent scheme of finance
-the permanent scheme of finance as
Perhaps it
contemplated by the Act.
may be appropriate here if I remove some
misconceptions which have been current,
In the first
I know, in the matter.
instance there are the resources of the
Northern Parliament, which consist of the
whole of the revenue collected, subject to,
first of all, our contribution to the
Imperial Parliament and Government.
That revenue must meet the expenditure,
and it is immaterial whether that revenue
consists of the reserve services collected
by the Imperial authority or transferred
services, or transferred revenue collected
by us. The reserve revenue, of course,
might be administered by the Imperial
funds, but we do not suffer in any respect
because of that. The Imperial authority
can presumably collect this taxation quite
as economically as we can do, and the
money that is collected is handed over to
us from the Imperial Treasury to meet our
liabilities. But what we want to urge is
this that Ulster is interested in every
penny of the revenue that is raised,
whether it is raised and collected through
the machinery of our own departments or
whether it is collected through the
machinery of the Imperial departments.
(Hon. Members: "Hear! hear!")
being interested in every penny, the
greater the revenue the greater the
balance we shall have left for the development of our own schemes of local reform.
On the other side of the account, if Ulster
is to successfully extend the activities of
And
her Government in schemes of permanent
advantage to the country, we are interested, I say, in expenditure economically
in every direction.
((
seems
It is a fallacious idea, a widely mistaken
notion, to think that the more we spend
the better our position when we come
before the Joint Exchequer Board and
the amount of our contribution is determined. That is entirely a fallacy. The
determination of our contribution from
Great Britain will be reached by other
methods than the expenditure of the
Province. (Hon. Members: 'Hear!
hear!") The taxable capacity of the
Province will be taken undoubtedly into
account. That is what we must bear in
mind, and it is because of that I consider
it my duty to emphasise that point. The
greater our economy in carrying on the
machinery of our Government the larger
will be the sum for the purpose of our
development. I would also impress this
upon the Committee that we have only
ourselves to depend upon. Indeed, .I
think that idea should almost be posted
up in every administrative department of
the Province. It is only out of our own
resources that expenditure can be met.
And while general approval may be given
to economy in the abstract, it
sometimes to be rather a different matter
when personal influences and personal desires enter into it. With the idea
which is in my mind at the present moment
I recall that last evening was it not?-my
hon. Friend, the Member for Tyrone and
Fermanagh (Mr. Coote), with a look of
unusual fierceness for one of such an
amiable disposition, said, "You must hold
the purse strings tight," and then, “Sir,
appeal to you on behalf of the secondary
teachers, whose condition is deplorable.
You have on the one side, almost in the
same sentence, that the purse strings have
got to be held tight, and on the other hand
that you are to make a grant to a body of
men who, I agree, are in very deplorable
circumstances. But I would remind my
hon. Friend and others who have touched
upon that subject of secondary education,
that I myself have been brought into very
close touch with, and have served my
apprenticeship, so to speak, in educational
matters during the past few years. In my
former official capacity I was responsible,
or mainly responsible, for the preparation
of a Bill to relieve educational conditions
in Belfast-a Bill which my hon. Friend,
the Member for North Belfast (Mr.
M'Guffin), presented in the Imperial
Parliament, and argued with a cogency
I
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
which gave us general satisfaction. We
all know how that Bill was talked out. I
only mention that to let the Committee
know that I have not any lack of sympathy
whatever for the condition of secondary
education. But, Sir, I wish to say that we
have no money for special grants in view
of the liabilities that are facing us. It is
better that I should be outspoken in this
matter. There is no use pretending we
have an inexhaustible well out of which we
can draw money for any matter which, in
the opinion either of a body or of an individual may be required during the period
with which we are now faced. We cannot
have it both ways. With the funds at our
disposal we must exercise that strict
economy which is necessary, and which
my hon. Friend is determined we shall
exact, I am very glad, I may say, and I
hope my hon. Friend will keep a watchful
eye upon the doings of the Treasury. It
will all contribute to that rigidity of rule
which is so necessary in departments dealing
with finance. But the summary of the
matter is, we must conserve our resources
until we can see a way out of the present
difficulties; and when we can see the way,
and when we reach the point when we have
a surplus, then I think such questions and
such claims as those of secondary teachers
are among the very first that should call
for our sympathy and support. I should
also like to remind my hon. Friend and
colleague in South Belfast in passing that
he seemed to be under the impression
yesterday, when speaking on the housing
question, that a department relating to
housing had been transferred, and that
we as a Govermnent were in some position
to deal with that very serious and important matter.
I should remind my hon. Friend that
there has been no transfer of any department connected with housing or any other
subject which will enable us to finance
either it or take any part in dealing with
the conditions as they exist to-day and
which I know are present in the mind of
my hon. Friend who has taken such a
deep interest in the question of the housing of the people. I think it right I
should mention that, because the report
of the speech may convey the impression
that we are in a position to deal with that
very difficult subject. I have thus far
dealt in a general way with the general
scope of our finance. I hope I have made
the position clear.
I think it will be expected I should
consider for a little what the Ulster position would be, assuming the transfer of
the services had taken place. (Hon. Member: "Hear! hear!'') In order to meet
this question I think it will be necessary
to go back in retrospect, and to touch
upon the earlier arrangements connected
with the financial proposals of the Home
Rule Bill as it was, the Home Rule Act
as it is. It is idle to suppose that we
were satisfied with the financial proposals of that Act. We entered, if I
might mention, one very striking itemwhen I say we, I mean the Finance Committee that was appointed by the
necessary authority to investigate the
whole incidence of the proposals-we
entered a very vigorous protest against
the claim for half of the revenue of our
Province as a contribution to the
Imperial services, when at the same time
the Government was satisfied with a
contribution from Southern Ireland of less
than 30 per cent.-50 per cent. in our
case, and 30 per cent. in the case of the
South of Ireland. We urged upon the
Minister that not only was the revenue of
the Province over-estimated, but that the
expenditure of the Province was underestimated. Let me say now that every
argument we put forward-
An HON. MEMBER: 56 per cent.,
and 44 per cent.
The MINISTER of FINANCE: I was
speaking of contributions in regard to
revenue,
half of our revenue, almost
£8,000,000 was to be the contribution to
the Imperial expenditure in the one case,
while the South of Ireland only pays
30 per cent., or £10,000,000. I say every
argument that we used and every point we
made has been fully verified-absolutely
and literally verified.. Indications of the
serious slump in trade were just beginning
to manifest themselves. We warned the
Minister that it was not proper to rely
on the continuance of the artificial condition of things that obtained during the
boom-then in the early part of 1920.
It was quite obvious at the time of our
discussion that we had reached the peak,
and we were then going down the decline,
while on the other hand we pointed out
that the schemes of social reform which
were continually being pressed on by the
Imperial authority would inevitably increase the cost of expenditure in our
Northern Province. Looking back upon
the estimates made by our Finance
Committee, we have the satisfaction of
knowing that all these estimates were
I
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Minister of Finance.]
absolutely right and those of the Government were absolutely wrong. However,
we had this safeguard, a safeguard which
was provided by our Ulster Members that
the contribution should be reviewed, and
I have no doubt whatever that in the review of the contribution by the Joint
Exchequer Board, regard will be had to
all the circumstances which I have
mentioned, the changed conditions,
the increase in expenditure caused by
legislation over which we had no control,
and also by the decrease of revenue
caused by circumstances over which
equally we had no control.
The White Paper Estimate on which the
financial Proposals of the Bill were based
showed a surplus revenue, after all expenditure was provided for, of £1,920,000,
whilst some provision was made for some
later expenditure which raised the surplus
to £2,250,000. I think when I touch on
this matter I should read the declaration
of the Minister who had charge of the
Bill, because it gives an indication of the
intentions of the Government, and will
show that it was the intention of the
Government that Ulster should be
generously treated in this matter. Sir
L. Worthington Evans used these
very
significant words which I hope will sink
into the minds of all present:-
The Government recognise that it is most
desirable that the Irish Governments should'
not be hampered, but should be allowed a
substantial margin with which to meet any
additional expense which may be incurred
by the establishment of the new Parliaments,
or may be called for by various services
which have hitherto not been developed
perhaps in accordance with the requirements
of the Irish people, and the Imperial contribution has, therefore, been fixed for the two
years at £18,000,000, which, upon the Estimates will leave a surplus of £4,250.000
revenue a year. Nor is this all. As the
Prime Minister announced earlier in the year
the Government have decided to make a free
gift of the existing land annuities, amounting
at present to £3,250,000, which will be available as additional revenue for Ireland. The
surplus and the land purchase annuities
together amount to £7,500,000. There is
included in the expenditure a margin for
future services, which have not yet been set
up, and will not mature fully for some years
to come, of £1,750,000. If this is added there
is a total surplus of £9,250,000 available
for Ireland over and above all existing
expenses after providing for the Imperial
contribution. This gives Southern Ireland a
total surplus of about £7,000,000, and
Northern Ireland a total surplus of £2,250,000.
I do not think, therefore, that it can be said
that an Imperial contribution has been fixed
too high upon the information at present
available. On the contrary, it has been fixed
at an amount which leaves for expenditure
in Ireland a sum very much in excess of the
highest rate of expenditure that there has
ever been in Ireland in times past.
Now, while that statement of the
Minister without Portfolio, the Minister
who had charge of the financial proposals
of the Bill, is not in the nature of a
statutory obligation, we claim that there
is something even more than a moral
obligation on Great Britain to see that at
least in the first year of the functioning
of our Ulster Parliament there shall be,
after all our expenses are paid, a surplus
of £2,250,000. Otherwise the intentions
of the Government will not have been
carried out, and the generous treatment
on which they prided themselves so much
will not be in evidence. I have not the
slightest fear, however, but that the Joint
Exchequer Board, presided over by a
gentleman of the well-known fairness and
strict impartiality of Lord Colwyn will deal
fairly with us, when we come to present
our accounts in this matter.
The Committee will remember that the
Prime Minister announced that Lord
Colwyn had agreed to accept the position
of chairman of that very important body.
From the information which we have compiled it would seem that the expenditure
of the services of Northern Ireland this
year has been greatly increased as compared with last year.
Of that there can
be no doubt, thus again verifying the argu
ment that we used in our conference with
the Minister without Portfolio. Education
alone is expected to cost this year in which
we now stand £337,000 more than last
year, and that is a cumulative increase,
because the full effect of the arbitrator's
award in relation to the salaries of the
National School teachers has not yet had its
full effect. Health and Labour alone, the
Department over which any right hon.
Friend on my right (Mr. Andrews) presides, is estimated to cost £186,000 more
than last year.
The Post Office services
are increased by £210,000, arising, of
course, from the additional bonuses and
larger salaries paid to the officials as com
pared with previous years, and, indeed, it
can be said that in every Department of
the Province there has been an estimated
increased expenditure for the present year.
In addition to what I have already men.
tioned, there has been a very large increase
in Police expenditure--
Mr. M'GUFFIN: The Government
should pay that.
Mr. POLLOCK: For which, indeed, 1
hesitate to mention the amount, because it
is something which would introduce a very
grave subject if we thought that we were
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
to be involved in any liability in regard to
it. But in the opinion of our Government
this is an alternative to military expenditure, and therefore is such that we should
not be called upon to pay.
That is to say
it should be an Imperial charge. We
have every reason to believe, and we are
confident that the Imperial Government
will assume, that we will not be under any
liability for the increased cost of the police
in consequence of the extraordinary conditions which have obtained in Ireland
generally. But under circumstances that
are altogether so surrounded by doubt and
that are so indefinite, no Finance Minister
could be expected to arrive at a definite
conclusion in regard to the financial results
of revenue and expenditure for the coming
year. I am confident that this Province
will be fairly treated in regard to the
financial arrangements that must neces
sarily ensue between our Province and the
Imperial authorities, but with the nebulous
character of the outlook it would not he
prudent for the Government to enter into
fresh obligations involving expenditure
until its resources and the necessary current charges upon it are more definitely
and accurately determined. Now, I think
I have travelled over most of the ground in
relation to the financial situation of our
Province. I should like, as I close, to
touch cursorily on a question of some importance, referred to by my hon. Friend
the Member for Londonderry (Sir Robert
Anderson) in his very interesting speech
yesterday. My hon. Friend spoke on the
subject of fiscal autonomy with an authority
derived from experience wider than that of
the average man, and his opinion on subjects of that kind is entitled to respect.
He said rightly that we cannot interfere
in any arrangements made between the
British Government and Southern Ireland.
That is obvious. My right hon. Friend,
the Prime Minister, has made that abundantly clear in all his communications. I
agree with him that we have a very direct
interest in the results of that arrangement,
and I can give this assurance that the
Government will watch it with the closest
interest.
It is a matter on which we must speak
with the utmost reserve, and no good object
can be served by instituting a discussion
on matters that are purely speculative and
without any knowledge whatever of the
circumstances which we may be called upon
to face. Our Parliament, our Government,
and our province may be called upon to face
serious issues, but we have met serious
issues in the past, and I am perfectly confident, if we only get rid of our doubts,
our fears, and our suspicions, and stand
together shoulder to shoulder as brothers
should stand, that as we have come through
many trials, many sufferings, and many
sacrifices during our forty years of travelling in the wilderness, though perhaps we
are not yet in sight of promised land, yet
I am convinced that with courage and determination-and I speak it reverently
-that if we exhibit courage and determination, the Providence that guided our
fathers so long will carry us safely through.
I
Had
Mr. LYNN: I beg to move that the sum
of £130,000 be reduced by a sum amounting
to £20,000. In the Imperial Parliament it
is usual to put down a small sum of £5
or £10 simply in order to ventilate any
different matters that may arise in the Vote,
but in this instance I think it is more
important that we should get to grips and
realities at once. If the Chancellor of the
Exchequer will allow me to say so, it would
be impossible for him to make a prosaic
speech on any subject. I have listened to
him often and I can say with all sincerity
that anything he touches he adorns.
have only one regret with regard to his
speech, and that is that it was not made
before the Estimates were drafted.
it been I probably would be spared the
necessity of saying anything, because the
pruning knife would have been used a great
deal more vigorously than it has been. I
sympathise with the Chancellor of the
Exchequer just as I sympathise with the
Prime Minister and other members of the
Government in the anomalous position in
which they find themselves. It is not their
fault-do not let the public imagine, let
no one imagine we are blaming them for
the anomalous position of affairs. We
know they are as anxious as we are, and
even more so, if it were possible to get
out of that anomalous position. Hitherto
we have been dealing with questions in
academic fashion, because we are dealing
with questions we had really no control
over. To-day we are brought face to face
with reality. We are asked to deal with
questions which the Government of this
House have control over-I mean the finance
of Northern Ireland.
I would like to impress, especially on the
Members who have come into Parliament
this session, how very important it is that
the House of Commons should keep a very
firm control over the national purse. A
good deal of the trouble in the Imperial
Parliament for centuries past has been in
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Lynn.]
regard to the control of the purse by the
House of Commons. I hope this House
will show its independence and make it
clear that they are going to control the
finances and the national purse, and see
that there is no extravagance in this part
of the United Kingdom. I am sorry to
say in the Imperial Parliament things.
have got so big that it is very difficult to
control finance. Anyone who has seen
them passing one hundred million pounds.
in an hour or so, or in a few minutes, will
realise how difficult it is to keep in touch
with finance in the Imperial Parliament
But we have no excuse of that kind here.
The Chancellor, in his excellent speech,
pointed out that our revenue will not be
so big that it will not be possible for us to
look over every detail-even the smallest
details. This is a matter over which we can
exercise control, and over which I ask
every Member of the House to exercise
control. I agree
with the excellent and very eloquent speech that
the Minister of Labour made yesterday.
In any criticism we offer here
there is not the semblance of disunion.
We stand solidly behind the Ministry, but
I want to say for myself and I am sure I
speak for other Members-that we are not
going to close our eyes and open our
mouths and take anything the Ministry
like to give us. We are here as independent Members, and are going to vote for
the Ministry on every vital question affecting Ulster, but we are not going to accept
everything, especially in the way of public
expenditure, without seeing into it.
want, on my own behalf, to make that
point clear. I shall be an independent
critic of the Government, as I have been
of the Government on the other side on
matters of finance. I shall vote against
them if I think they are wrong and sup
port them if I think they are right, but
I shall do what I think best. Do not let
it be said that because we criticise the
Ministry that we think the Ministry is
incapable of doing its work or that we hold
different views.
I
The PRIME MINISTER: Perhaps my
hon. Friend will allow me, on behalf of
the Government, to thank the hon. Member and accept every word he says. We
know perfectly well, with regard to the
larger questions affecting the interests of
Ulster, that we have the whole party with
us.
It would be very ungracious of us to
suggest for a moment that the fullest
criticism of these funds, or of any matter
of that kind, should not be
offered by anybody in any part
of the House. It is criticism we want and
desire, and therefore no Member need feel
in the slightest degree hampered by the
idea that we will take it in any sense as a
want of loyalty towards the party. We
want frank criticism of our estimates or
anything that is before us. I think it is
just as well that Members in all parts of
the House should recognise that.
Mr. LYNN: It is extremely characteris
tic of the straightforwardness which has
always marked the Prime Minister that
he should get up at this stage and make a
statement like that. My criticism is not
put forward for the purpose of weakening
confidence in Ministers. We do not want
to do that, and any criticism that I do
offer and I promise to offer criticism on
the finances of the Government-is with
the object of strengthening the position
of the Government rather than weakening
it. The Ulsterman always wants to get 20s.
worth of value for every £1 he spends,
and if he had his way he would like to get
21s. worth. It only shows his good sense,
and, therefore, we have to look over every
one of the items raised by Mr. Chancellor
to see that there is not a single £1 spent
that should not be spent. Now, Mr.
Chancellor expressed a pious hope-I hope
it will be realised that it would be possible
to carry on the Government of Northern
Ireland as cheaply as before this Act was
passed. Quite true, we had a great many
unnecessary Departments in Dublin, but
that is no reason why we should have one
single unnecessary Department or official in
Northern Ireland'. (Hon. Members:
"Hear! hear!") I am glad to hear that
"Hear!" and I hope when the division is
taken the Members of the Committee will
go into the Lobby and vote against some
of the things shown in the estimate which
I hope to prove to the Committee there is
not the slightest necessity for. It is out of
its own revenue that Northern Ireland must
pay for the expense incurred. Now, when
we were linked up with Great Britain, the
people did not realise how the revenue
came. The Imperial purse was a huge one,
but let me point out a fallacy which I aan
afraid exists, namely, that the more money
that is spent the more you get out of Great
Britain when you come to revise the finan
cial estimate. I would like to make two
observations with regard to that. The first
is that even if it were possible, I do not
think it would be honest. The second is
that I think it would be exceedingly ad
economy to start on an extravagant systeni
Supply.
Supply .
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
because you were going to get a few extra
millions out of Great Britain. Let me tell
you this, that you have less chance to-day
of getting extra millions out of Great
Britain than you had when this Act was
passed. Only in the last Session two very
important schemes have been scrapped by
the British Ministry owing to the exigencies of the finance market, namely-Housing
which the Government could not finance,
and which we cannot finance either, and the
scheme with regard to agriculture. I know
the Imperial Government incurred a good
deal of displeasure because of scrapping
them, but do you think they are going to
give us extra millions to be spent here?
Do not let us for a moment vote these large
sums, because they are large sums for us;
do not let us vote these large sums, imagining that we are going to get anything more
out of Great Britain than the statutory
liability. We have got, as Mr. Chancellor
has said, to depend upon ourselves, and we
have got to exercise the most ruthless
economy in every Department of the State.
He pointed out that while a great many
people paid lip service to economy they
came along afterwards and asked for a huge
expenditure. Mr. Chancellor, I can promise
you, so far as I am concerned, I am going
to practice what I preach. I am not going
to come here and try to get popular applause, which is a very cheap thing to get,
and then come along and press you to make
large payments of money. That is one of
the things we have got to guard against.
We have got to see we only ask for money
that is absolutely necessary in the interests
of the public.
I say there must be the most absolute
economy, and I want to point out some
lines where, in my judgment, there is not
economy. The Prime Minister, on Tuesday
-when I interjected a few remarks on this
question of economy, to the effect that an
initial mistake had been made in creating
too costly a Government, I pointed out
not only in this House, but through another
medium of reaching the public, that
the Northern Parliament was the only
Provincial Parliament in the Empire
that had got Parliamentary Under-Secretaries-the Prime Minister, I am afraid,
seemed to doubt that the other day, and
quite unintentionally, I am sure, he left the
impression in the minds of the House that
in Colonial Parliaments there were deputy Ministers. May I point out to the
Prime Minister that as a matter of fact
deputy Ministers in a Colonial Parliament
are only the permanent heads of Departments, and are not members of Parliament at all. So that this Northern Parliament is the first Parliament in the Empire,
is the only Parliament in the Empire, except the Imperial Parliament, that has got
Parliamentary Under-Secretaries.
I mention that in passing because we have been
starting our career as a Parliament and a
Government on far too large a scale. We
are like a young couple who want to start
where their parents left off. That is not the
right road to go. We ought to begin in a
small way; we ought to economise our resources, we ought, if necessary, to add to
the number of Ministers. I know one
Member of the House rather objected to
what I said about Under-Secretaries. He
suggested, as a matter of fact, that you
ought to have a Deputy-Secretary and Secretaries to the Deputy-Secretarv.
It is very
easy for the Member of a millionaire constituency to make a suggestion of that kind,
but it is not the sort of thing which appeals
to a constituency like the one I represent,
or the bulk of the constituencies in Ulster.
They want to see things done decently and
in order, but not extravagantly. We are beginning our career by apeing the Imperial
Government, which has huge wealth behind
it. And I want to draw the attention of
the Committee very seriously to a
number of items that they will find if
they turn to Page 5 of the Estimates, for
example; I say nothing with regard to
the Chairman of Ways and Means, or
the Chairman of Committees in the
Senate. One point I would like to ask
the Under Secretary for Finance is how
this money is to be allocated? Is the
£1,500 going to the Chairman of Ways
and Means to be divided between the
Chairman of Ways and Means and the
Chairman of Committees in the Senate ?
If there is one case in which I would sink
public feeling for private interest I think
it would be, at any rate, in the case of
the Chairman of Ways and Means. I want
to be fair to the Government. There is
at least one thing, as far as I can gather
from these Estimates, which is not going
to cost much, and that is our prayers.
Probably the Government have come to
the conclusion that we are either too good
to need praying for, or that we are past
praying for. At any rate, I see we are
getting three chaplains for the modest
sum of £120. Evidently those chaplains
have not got a trade union, or they would
insist on a larger scale than that. It is a
very easy step from chaplains to the law.
I
see we are appointing, or have
appointed, I do not know which, a Counsel
to the Speakers of the House of Commons
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Lynn.]
and the Senate at a salary starting at
£1,000, and rising to £1,200.
An HON. MEMBER: Is there any
bonus with that?
A
Mr. LYNN: Of that I am not sure.
You will find it if you look at your
Estimates on page 5. I think there is.
At any rate, this gentleman is to be
Examiner of Petitions for Private Bills,
and Taxing Officer for both Houses.
similiar officer exists in the Imperial
Parliament, but there they have got one
for the Commons and one for the Lords.
This House may have the temerity to cross
swords with the Senate, and legal points
may arise which will have to be settled,
and we can hardly expect a Counsel to give
advice to us and at the same time give
advice to the Senate. I warn the Committee that this is only the beginning, and
we will find probably when we get the
next Estimate that there will be a Deputy
for this Counsel to the Speakers.
a
Now I come to another matter, and one
which I personally do not like to deal
with. We are going to spend a very large
sum in reporting and publishing our
speeches. Well, if I may say so, I think
it is a tribute to our vanity rather than
a tribute to our business common-sense.
I have been a Member of the Imperial
Parliament for three years, and, except
looking for quotation to hurl at a
political opponent, I do not believe I have
ever opened a copy of Hansard; and, as
far as Hansard is concerned, it is not of
the slightest value to me, and, as far as
it is concerned here, I think the Government could have made a very much
cheaper arrangement. They would have
got quite ample reports from the newspapers, far too ample some of them, that
might very easily have done as records of
this House. I do not know really how
much it is going to cost, because you will
find in some other part large sums for
printing; at any rate it must run into a
very large sum, and the House should ask
itself whether it is prepared, in order to
satisfy its own vanity-because it is
nothing else, there is no other necessity
for it to spend so much money in reporting and publishing these debates.
There is another point on the same page.
One really hardly likes to deal with these
things, but it is necessary that someone
should have the courage to deal with them,
and they must be dealt with. If they are
not dealt with here, they will be dealt with
by the public outside. There is a House
allowance of £500 for Mr. SPEAKER. Ι
would like my hon. Friend the Under-Secretary for Finance, when he is replying, to
give us some information with regard to
that. Mind you, I want, in passing, to say
we do not expect this House is going to sit
Ι
more than three months in the year.
have noticed during the last two or three
days the trouble is we are all so modest.
The difficulty will be to keep debates going
for three months in the year. I should say
at the outset three months in the year
ought to do the work of this Parliament
without any trouble. You will see on Page
6, "Department of the Clerks of the
Houses" it, of course, includes both
Houses-is going to cost us £3,340.
may be worth it. I, at anv rate, have my
doubts. I think it could have been done
much cheaper, and if the Prime Minister
will be good enough to refer to the case of
Ontario, to which he referred at the outset,
he will see that they do these things much
more economically than we propose to do
them here, although I may say Ontario
has a population something over 2,000,000,
whereas we have only 1,300,000.
It
I
I would like the Committee to look at
Pages 9 of the Estimates, under
the head: "A-Salaries, Wages, and
Allowances, etc." I want to draw
attention to two important appointments there. One 18 a permanent
Secretary to the Cabinet and the other is
an Assistant-Secretary to the Cabinet.
do not know whether Members of this House
take or have taken a keen interest in constitutional matters. May I point out that
in the long history of Great Britain there
never was a permanent Secretary or, indeed, a Secretary of any sort to a British
Cabinet until the late war. It was only
during the war when there were so many
duties thrown on the Members of the
Cabinet that this institution of a permanent Secretary to the Cabinet was
lished. What I ask the Government is, and
I ask the House in all seriousness-what
necessity is there for these two offices? It
may be a reasonable thing to have. It may
be-when my hon. Friend, the Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) gets up
an agitation against the Minister of Ariculture because he will not appoint Roman
Catholics, or when the Member for Londonderry (Sir Robert Anderson) has a row with
the Home Secretary because the strength
of whisky is too weak-it may be that these
things must be brought before the Cabinet
and must be dealt with, but is it really
necessary when these Cabinet Ministers
estab
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
meet together that we should have two officials, one starting at £1,200, and the other
at £1,000, plus bonus? I would like some
explanation with regard to these two.
The PRIME MINISTER: Would it be
convenient to my hon. Friend in
case J
would be called out of the House, to deal
with that very point. Some of my colleagues are not so familiar as I am myself
with it. I must thank my hon. Friend for
permitting me to break into his speech on
the subject. In starting this whole new
fabric, 1 found that there were a great
many points in connection with the Govern
ment of the country with which I myself
was not altogether familiar. I had to begin
at the very beginning. I could not step into
an office-I and my colleagues--to find
myself and them surrounded with experts to
keep us right by precedents and so forth.
The work in connection with the negotiations across the water cecupied a great deal
of my time, and I sent Colonel Spender
specially over to the secretariat of the
Cabinet in London for a period of a couple
of months in order to pick up from
Colonel Hankey there, who is the Chief
Secretary of the Cabinet, the reins of
office, as it were, as regards the connection
of the Cabinet with the various Ministers.
But with that extraordinary activity
which most Members are aware of, he
picked up his duties in a very short time,
and I readily confess to admitting that
had it not been for his assistance and the
assistance of my own private secretary, I
could not have carried out the work in the
time between when I was asked to do so
and when the Government of Ulster was
established. I could not have done it
single-handed. I would have found it
utterly impossible. It may be that up
till the year 1914 there was no such thing
as a Secretary to the Cabinet-that is
perfectly correct-but from 1914 onwards
the work of the Cabinet and the work of
the Prime Minister has undoubtedly been
very much accelerated. A Prime
Minister's life is really a life of extraordinary variety and a busy one. He does
not get very much time to himself. His
interests from morning till night are devoted to the public service, and most
willingly devoted in that direction-no
complaint whatever in that regard-but
I doubt very much whether it would be
possible for any one to take on the responsibility without having a secretary'to
the Cabinet. May I say that on no less
than seven separate occasions I was
telegraphed or telephoned for to go over
to London to enter into delicate negotiations with the Prime Minister and with
the Chief Secretary, and unless I had
a secretary behind me fully au fait with
the intricacies of the work to keep in touch
with me, I fear that a great deal of difficulty might possibly arise. It may be
said why don't you
introduce
secretary from one of the other departments, but the position is one requiring
very special knowledge; it is very secret,
a great deal of the work is very secret, and
requires a man of special stamp to be able
to work alongside the Prime Minister, to
be fully in his confidence, and in the
confidence of his colleagues, and to be able
to reply to all questions. I hope that
during my tenure of office there shall be
no unnecessary delay in answering those
many inquiries that come to hand. But
over and above all that I hope Members
on all sides will bear in mind that the
erection of this great fabric is not a simple
matter. Some of my colleagues have not
been in ministerial positions before, but
my own experience is always at their
service, and at the present moment the
Cabinet is a centre of discussion as to the
best way to set on foot the various
schemes which are originated in an
Act of Parliament. I do think it would be
very false economy to cut down the salary
of the secretary or the smaller sum which
is made here as a contribution towards
the assistant secretary's salary. My own
private secretary helps and assists Colonel
Spender in the work which is being done.
Take a case in point, if one took
ill and had to go away, and I had
to get somebody to help me. Let
me divide this work into two partsfirst the setting up of the machinery, and
secondly the business of getting into working order. I can assure the House and
my hon. Friend opposite that when we
get started, when I have not to spend
my time arranging about buildings, about
employment, and other matters, it might
be quite possible to do away with the
assistance, but I am particularly anxious
there should be no slip made in
the initial stage of getting our
machinery into thorough working order.
That is my defence of the Secretary for
the Cabinet. I ask the Committee to be
good enough to give me that assistance
during the very specially trying times that
I am at present confronting.
Mr. COOTE: May I ask why do you
not take a simple Vote on Account?
Supply.

Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
The PRIME MINISTER: This is a
Vote on Account.
Mr. LYNN: I thank the Prime
Minister for replying now, but so far as
his defence is concerned it certainly does
not convince me however it may convince
other Members of the House.
He says
the reason why he must have this permanent secretary is because of his skilled
knowledge in helping to build up the new
Constitution, and yet he admits in the
course of his speech that he had to send
him over to the British Cabinet to get him
trained. That means that the permanent
secretary, who is absolutely essential to
the Cabinet in building up the Constitution had to be sent after his appointment
The PRIME MINISTER: Prior to his
appointment.
Mr. LYNN: I do not know how long
prior. No Member of this House, not
even the Prime Minister, has a higher
regard for the gentleman he has named
than I have, but I hold very strongly that
the post is absolutely unnecessary, and
that this House if it is going to do its
duty to the public who have returned
them here will vote against it when it
comes to the question of voting.
With regard to the Ministry of Finance, probably
the Under-Secretary could enlighten me
in this matter. I notice that where some
of the officials are loaned by the British
Departments you seem to be paying them,
whereas in other cases, such as say the
Ministry of Agriculture, where they are
loaned from Dublin, they are not paid.
I would like some explanation as to why
the English civil servant is paid while the
Irish civil servant is not. Another point
I would like to ask the Under-Secretary of
Finance is in regard to the whole question
of Parliamentary secretaries to the
Ministry. On page 10 I see, for example,
that the Minister has an allowance of
£100, and the secretaries £50. If
Members will turn to the Labour
Ministry they will discover there is at
private secretary to the Labour Ministry
at £500 inclusive. Evidently there is a
trade union in regard to the private
secretaries who are allowed to go to the
Labour Ministry. Can it be that there
is some mistake in the printing on page
22? I would like the Under-Secretary to
answer that when he comes to deal with
this whole question. Now if the Committee will turn to page 14. It deals
with the question of sites and buildings,
and there I know I will be met with the
footnote that is here that this money is
going to be returned by the Imperial
Parliament, but I would ask those who
use that argument to read carefully the
remarks of the Minister of Finance a
short time ago with regard to the fallacy
of spending more money on any object
than you ought to spend. So far as "A"
is concerned (Purchase of Sites and Build
ings) I think everyone will congratulate
the Cabinet on having made an excellent
bargain. When we come to the question
of "B" (Rents), I can speak freely on
this point as a Presbyterian, and I want
to say I think you have given far too
large a sum for this building. £8,000
a
year is in
in my judgment absolutely
absurd, and in that I am confirmed by
people who know the value of buildings
all over the city of Belfast.
The sum is much too large. Or take the
temporary public offices for which
we are to pay £5,000 a year. There
again you are paying far more
than the market value. Some people
think, of course, that Government
departments can pay anything and everything. I for one want to utter a protest
against the money paid for these two
things. On page 17, under the heading
B, there is an amount of £27,500, which
I would ask the Under-Secretary to explain, and what it covers. In regard to
the Civil Contingencies Fund, this House
will readily agree that it is absolutely
necessary to have a fund such as this, and
I am sure we can depend upon the
Minister of Finance to see that it is not
squandered and that the purse strings are
kept tight.
I come to the Ministry of Home Affairs,
which was never more interesting to this
House than it is to-day. I notice that the
Minister for Home Affairs has two assistant secretaries in this department, and I
notice there is an item of £200 put
down by the Minister of Home Affairs for
books. I would like some information
with regard to these books if they have
been bought, and if they have not will Mr.
Chancellor, in appointing a committee, see
that there 19 representation
the lady members on that committee.
of
want some information with
regard to the spending of this
£200. The only other point, and it is a
very important one, to which I wish to
draw attention is the Ministry of Commerce. In a short time a pretty large
sum of £2,625 is allocated to the Ministry
of Commerce. I contend, and I think I
have the bulk of the business community
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
with me, that there is no necessity for a
Ministry of Commerce. We boast of the
industry of this city, how great it has
become; and we also boast, and rightly
boast, that it has become great without a
single penny of Government money. What
advantage will the commerce of Ulster get
from the Ministry of Commerce? My own
impression is that the Ministry does not
take itself seriously, because the gentleman
in charge of the Ministry is a lawyer.
Lawyers have many amiable qualities, and
one of them is that they are good at drawing up bills of cost, and probably they will
assist the Minister of Finance in drawing
up bills of cost to present to this House,
but I fail to see any advantage that commerce is going to get from this Ministry.
Would my hon. Friend, the Under-Secretary, who has been at the head of a
great Department of Commerce, think, if
he wanted to make a deal in linen or any
of the stuff in which he is interested, of
going to a Government office to get advice?
I remember at one time I was foolish
enough to publish a Summary of Reports
issued by the Board of Trade, and I was
told by the business men of Belfast that
they were always 12 months too late.
Business is a highly specialised thing, and
business can only be worked by people
who are in charge of the business, and if
the commerce of Belfast is going to go on
progressing, for Heaven's sake keep
Government Departments out of it;
because I have never known of a Government Department in industrialism ever
doing anything but incalculable harm.
At the present time we are suffering from
the effects of Government Departments in
our industries. I fail to see there is any
reason whatever for a Ministry of Commerce. We have got on well in the past
without it, and I do not think we will get
two thousand pence worth of value out of
it. I beg to move the Amendment.
Mr. COOTE: I wish to second the
Amendment proposed by the hon. Member
for West Belfast, and as he has gone into
all the detail included in the Estimate I
do not need to go into it. I would refer
to some points raised by the Chancellor,
but before doing so I wish to congratulate
you, Mr. Moles, on the position you
occupy here to-day. As an old colleague
of yours, and a fellow-Member of the old
British House of Commons, we recognise
your worth and kindness, and your great
industry; and while we realise that it is
an honourable office you hold, we believe
you should have a more exalted position
in the Government of the country, and we
hope the day is not far distant when you
will go higher. I wish also to congratulate my friend, the Finance Minister.
Of course, we all have the most abundant
confidence in the Finance Minister. He
is one of the ornaments and pillars of the
commercial and industrial life of this great
city, and his name is a household word
throughout the province. We have
listened to him to-day with the greatest
attention, and, personally, I am delighted
to see he does not detract one iota from
the words he spoke before he came into the
House, and the attitude he has taken up
all along the line on matters of finance.
I assure you we are here to help him, and
we shall be exceedingly sorry if, in the
discharge of our duties, we find ourselves against him on this question.
I hope we will not be driven to a procedure
of this kind. If I am right he has tried
to visualise the situation something like
this. On the debit side the Government
Owes £3,229, but, of course, the
Chancellor has committed himself to other
to be paid between this and
February or March. The Government
have made certain agreements to carry
on the business of the Government.
sums
as
But
But
I do appeal to the Government, and to
the Finance Minister, to take a simple
Vote on Account, and refrain from
attempting a procedure which we au not
look upon at least an honourable
transaction, if I may say so. Is it honourable to deal with the House in a covert
way bringing in these offices, and having
them filled by people at certain salaries
which will be fixed upon us in February
next? Suppose we pass this Vote of
Credit and these Estimates, and supposing
we wish to criticise the appointment of
any of the secretaries, next year we will
be met with the suggestion from the
Treasury Bench-" You passed a Vote on
Account on which we adopted these Estimates last year. You gave tacit consent
to these offices and these allowances, and
accordingly we employed men to fill them
and carry on the work Is it honourable
on your part now to object?"
Now that is our difficulty. We want
you to put yourselves in our position, and
it is a serious difficulty, because the major
portion of these offices are not yet filled.
There is no necessity for these estimates
at all. The Chancellor himself has correctly stated and we accept every word
he said in his speech-that he had at the
present hour civil servants in this House
K
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
supplied from two sources. There are those
loaned by the British Government free,
but even if they are loaned, it would not
be honourable of us not to pay them for
doing very estimable work; and there is
another class of servants that may come
in here as soon as an agreement is come
to with Southern Ireland-namely, civil
servants from Dublin. Those who will
be sent here we are bound to accept at the
salaries current, or we have another alternative-we can let them go. But what
you wish an estimate passed for is that
you may pay the salaries fixed for secretaries, assistant secretaries, and private
secretaries to assistant secretaries, and we
believe that is simply in the nature of a
bribe that they may vote with the Government whenever necessary—
The PRIME MINISTER: It would be
very unfitting of me to allow that remark. It is a very unfair and spiteful
thing to say. God forbid that any Prime
Minister or any responsible person would
dream for a moment, at this time of peril
for Ulster, that I or anybody else should
appoint any officer in order that he should
be tied to a certain policy. It is a charge
of bribery and corruption, and I am
ashamed and astonished that an old
Parliamentary hand like Mr. Coote should
make such a base insinuation, which
reflects not alone on me and my colleagues
but on the honour of the under-secretaries
who have come along to help us in this
difficult time. It is a disgrace to the
House that such a statement should be
made. (Hon. Members: "Hear! hear!")
Mr. COOTE: I will not withdraw, but
if you withdraw the salary here I will be
very glad to withdraw. But while it is
here I must put my own interpretation
upon it. In private as well as in public
life it behoves the individual as well as
the State to try and economise that they
may do some useful work.
Now, I say to my Friends, I say to the
right hon. Member, I say to this Committee, will you not grant these teachers
any increment to assist them over their
difficulties during this winter, and you
say you will not-teachers whose salaries
are only £150 or £200 a year? These
men are men who have gone through their
university course, and have got their
university degrees second to none
of those got by any secretary or undersecretary. Will you create offices in these
Estimates for the officials-
The CHAIRMAN: I think the hon.
Member (Mr. Coote) will recognise that
while I am giving a very wide latitude, as
is proper in a new House like this, to the
various speakers, none the less we must keep
somewhere within the compass of the Vote
on Account we are now discussing. The
question of policy, with respect to education, is now the subject of investigation
by a Committee. If the hon. Member
will bring his observations to bear on the
particular subject under discussion it will
be more desirable.
Mr. COOTE: But, Mr. Chairman, you
did not call my hon. Friend to order.
The CHAIRMAN: I have said I desire
to give a wide latitude, but a mere passing
phrase must not be construed into a sort
of second reading debate.
Mr. COOTE: Well, these men who are
living in this city will see the vote of a
vast amount of money while they are
starving on £150 a year. If you are
going to have even Ulster pure there must
be a sense of propriety all round. Here
are men qualified for any position-for
that of secretary or under-secretary-but
you are going to keep them on a small
pittance. Here you are to pay secretaries
£1,200, and they are, if I might say so,
inferior men in every way. I congratulate the Finance Minister in clearing the
air of the fallacy which has obtained
among men, and among leading politicians
in Ulster, in regard to the question of
economy. Many men had the idea that
we might be as profligate as we wished to
be, and inflate our salaries to officials as
much as we like, and it would all stand
to the good for the first two years, until
we should have a union between Southern
and Northern Ireland; or the bigger we
could put up our debit balance the more
we could claim from the Joint Exchequer
Board. I am glad you have shown that
is a fallacy, and I hope everyone in this
House will realise it is a fallacy. The
more we economise the more we will
have for the public services in Ulster.
I want also to point out to this Committee, in all seriousness, that at the
last election we made promise after
promise. We promised to look after the
public purse, and promised the people to
see that Ulster was worked on the most
economical lines compatible with efficiency.
But what have we here? A plethora of
under-secretaries and assistant undersecretaries coming here in force equal
to the British House of Commons.
We must realise we are only a
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
small people and only a small province.
Hon. Members. the right hon. Members of
the Cabinet must not get into their minds
that we are thinking in millions the same
as they are at Westminster.
We have
only something like 17 millions. We must
have a due sense of proportion, and not
go about with the idea we should have
secretaries and under-secretries running
about at our beck and call, and that
Ulster is going to pay for it.
once.
Let us get down to facts at
The taxpayers will never stand it in
face of the rate of taxation which they
will have to pay in the coming days. I
say that the taxpayers of this country are
watching us to-day. I do not try to work
on the feelings of Members here. I do
not try to work up a case in view of the
taxpayers of Ulster; but I do warn the
Government that they are playing with
fire. I say you should withdraw this
Estimate of services. You do not want
half the offices. You cannot fill the offices
at the moment, and many of them
will not be filled by Government
officials, many of them will not be
filled from the Civil Service; many
of them will be filled from private life,
through the influence people can bring to
bear upon Members of the Cabinet and
this House. Many of them have not been
brought up to the job and know nothing
about it. Many of them are not required. Many of them are not capable
of doing the services. I say it is not fair
to trade upon the people who trusted us.
It is not fair; and it is simply the most
damaging thing we can do here to-day to
pass this Vote as it is. I plead with the
Minister of Finance to withdraw these
services, and keep the matter over until
we can go into it item by item, till we
can decide as to whether we shall have
Hansard. We cannot express our opinion
on these matters at present.
Mr. POLLOCK: May I enlighten my
hon. Friend that it is a Vote on Account
we are discussing, and that ample opportunity for criticising the details will be
allowed on a Vote in Supply.
Mr. COOTE: Criticism is one thing,
but the power to expunge them out of the
Vote is another thing. This is not the
liberty of the House of Commons-of
the old House of Commons-and it is a
very unfortunate way to start to give us
an expounding of the liberties of this
House. I say you are gagging us in this
Vote to-day. We cannot eliminate a
single item on the list; we must discard
the whole, and therefore we have the
painful necessity of putting down this
amendment and moving that the whole
thi
ng must be cut down to a certain
amount. I want to say to the Ministry
that we will press this matter to a vote
whatever the consequences may be. We
I will not have this House of Commons
gagged. We want the ordinary methods
of the British House of Commons here.
The CHAIRMAN: I pointed out to the
hon. Member that I had ruled when the
point of order was taken earlier, that
this is merely a Vote on Account, that
the procedure in discussing a Vote on
Account is one thing and discussing the
business of Supply is another and quite
distinct thing. The business of Supply
will come before the House in due course,
and it then will be open to the hon.
Member to indulge in any criticism and
amendment he desires within the compass
of the rules.
Mr. COOTE: If this is only a Vote on
Account, have we not been attacking and
defending the items all afternoon?
The CHAIRMAN: I have pointed out
to the hon. Member that the procedure in
discussing a Vote on Account is strictly
limited, and I must adhere to the ruling
though I would desire to give him the
utmost freedom. I would remind him
again that he will have the most ample
opportunity when the Estimates in due and
proper form come forward for discussion,
and it is not therefore just for him to say
there will be no opportunity, when, in
fact, it will arise.
Mr. COOTE: There is no opportunity as
there is in the British House of Commons.
We can attack Estimates when we like.
Mr. POLLOCK: On Supply. This is
not Supply.
Mr. COOTE: There is a memorandum of
certain offices, and if these offices are put
in our hands we are morally bound to those
offices. There is no getting out of it.
Surely we are not children.
Surely they do not expect these offices
will be filled if the occasion does not arise.
Therefore, we should have the right, if
these offices are brought up in this form,
to say which offices we shall retain, and
which we shall do away with? Or there
is the alternative course. Take a lump
sum on account, discharge your obligations
Supply..
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
to your people as best you can, and meet
us next February. That is the favourable course for the Ministry to adopt, the
only course that would be taken in the
British House of Commons.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: I do not
intend to occupy the time of this Committee this afternoon, for more than a very
brief period. First of all I would like to
compliment, if I may,
the Finance
Minister on the very clear, very exhaustive, and very lucid statement he has made
to the public. He has placed before us a
great deal of information, and he has
given us a great many figures and facts
that have a very serious bearing upon
the whole Government of Northern Ireland. I do not intend to go into the
details as to the whole financial position
that we find ourselves in at this moment,
but I think there are some things that
ought to be emphasised at this stage. The
Finance Minister has given us an idea, a
rough idea, as to how things are likely to
work out. He has told us that the contributions under the 1920 Act for Ireland
for Imperial purposes is fixed at
£18,000,000. 44 per cent. was to come
from Six Counties, and 56 from 26
counties- a very large and generous
contribution from the North-East corner
of Ireland, within 12 per cent. of the
entire contribution from the rest of Ireland. He omitted to tell us if he had
the facts. I would like to hear them from
him; perhaps the Prime Minister or some
of those who were in the Imperial House
of Commons when the Bill passed will tell
us on what principle was this discrimination arrived at. Why was the Northern
Parliament assessed in 44 per cent. and
the Southern Parliament in 56
per cent?
Mr. M'GUFFIN: It was never explained.
It was
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON:
never explained; and here we stand today, as representatives of the Northern
Parliament, expected to administer our
affairs with economy, and to do the very
best we can in the interests of this Imperial Province, and we really are in the
position that we do not know the basis on
which this assessment was made upon us.
Roughly speaking, we are to contribute
£8,000,000 to the Imperial Exchequer,
and the rest of Ireland roughly
£10,000,000. I do hope that before this
debate comes to an end that this Committee will know, and those we represent
will know, on what fixed principle or basis
the British Minister arrived at such an
assessment. I have never yet been able
to understand it. I have never been satisfied with the financial situation as it
exists; I think the Chancellor clearly intimated that the result of the financial
arrangements under the Bill are not going
to work out in a very satisfactory way.
Of course he told us, I think, that after the
lapse of two years these arrangements are
subject to revision. I think that is right,
and then the Exchequer Board will meet,
and if the contributions can be proved to
be too large, bearing in mind the taxable
capacity of the people and other matters,
there will be a refund given.
Now that is a very unsatisfactory
arrangement. We meet here in this
Committee to-day, and it is our duty to
ascertain exactly what we have got to
distribute, what money we have got to
spend. In my judgment we have not got
a penny piece of surplus to look forward
(Hear, hear). What is the fact.
to.
The Finance Minister has told us that
owing to the slump in trade and to other
facts which the representatives of Ulster
pressed, and pressed very strongly upon
the British Ministers the balance is
likely to be on the other side. He gave us
three sets of figures-One of £337,000 in
connection with education, though I took
from him that that amount would
certainly reach if not a million, at least
£500,000. Then there were two other
sets of figures, roughly amounting to a
million, and the doubtful question of
whether we are going to pay for the
police force or not. Taking the serious
drop in revenue, the additional expenses
that have accrued and others that are
likely to arise, the surplus which we hoped
to have of two million pounds will have
disappeared. It will be entirely obsolete,
and there will be a debit balance against
us. That is the position we find our.
selves in to-day, but as the Chancellor of
the Exchequer has told us it will be up
to the British Government to make good
their promises and treat us generously.
With regard to the other side of the
question which I dealt with yesterday, I
will not touch upon it to-day. Peace
negotiations are taking place, and if they
reach fruition, and if there is a settlement
arrived at, we all hope that peace and good
government will come to Ireland. In
that event, the position may be somewhat altered, and may be entirely
different from what we find it to-day.
What is the lesson that this Committee
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
has to gain by thus admitting facts? It
is this that until our financial position
on
is accurately ascertained, or until we have
some rough idea of the funds we are going
to administer, we must proceed very
cautiously, and must not make many commitments. That is the kernel of the
whole matter. The question of economy
is a necessity. To-day we are going to
Vote on Account certain moneys that are
absolutely essential for the carrying on of
the services of Government. I am not
in a position to say whether all those items
are absolutely correct or not. I am
waiting for the Financial Secretary to the
Treasury to give some detailed information in regard to the various points made
by the hon. Member for West Belfast and
the hon. Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh.
I
I think we have no alternative if we are
going to carry on as a Government at all
but to vote money.
In the meantime,
however, there is certainly no room for
experiments or for unnecessary expenditure at all. I do not know with regard to
some of these items here to-day. You,
Mr. Chairman, have ruled that all items
on the Vote in Supply will be open to
criticism, and, I presume, for deletion.
I think all these items will have to be gone
into in the most careful manner possible,
to see if economies can be effected, but I
have sufficient confidence in the heads of
the Ministry that they realise fully the true
position of affairs, and that they are going
to put before us no unnecessary expenditure or anything that cannot be fully
justified. There is one observation I would
like to make on one of the items criticised
by the hon. Member for West Belfast.
agree with him about unnecessary expenditure in Government departments in regard
to trade, but I would take leave to say
that a Ministry of Commerce can be one
of the most useful things if properly
worked (Hon. Members: "Hear! hear!")
worked-(Hon.
-in connection with this Northern Parliament. Why, if we take the overseas
Dominions, they have Ministers of this
character to push their wares in the different markets in the world; and if certain
changes do arise that are already indicated
in the Government of Ireland-if Customs
and Excise are handed over to the two
Irish Parliaments-then the question of
commerce and the development of our resources will be of greater importance still.
I certainly would not like to see the
Ministry of Commerce wiped out. It can
do
an immense amount of
Germany made a success
of
good.
it
because
through
no
she conducted her trade
Chambers of Commerce and
secured a strong hold which has been held
for many years in connection with the
different markets of the world. I do hope
we will realise fully that from the financial
standpoint we have no money,
resources to fall back on. We have got
to vote these services to carry on as best
we can until a financial settlement is
arrived at. I hope it will be possible for
our people of Northern Ireland in security
and peace to develop our resources, to live
in peace and harmony with our neighbours
in the South of Ireland, to extend our influence in every direction, to help our
people to make their lives happier,
brighter, and better.
The FINANCIAL SECRETARY to the
MINISTRY of FINANCE (Mr. Barbour):
I think a fairly wide field has been
given to the speakers discussing
the Estimate. I daresay in Parliament
itself it is more usual that those
occupying public positions are charged
with the paucity of the information which
they may put before Members, and while
any experience will be new to me it is
refreshing to think that the care which has
been taken to afford hon. Members the
fullest information as to probable expenditure involved in the services of the
Government is actually interpreted by
some as a dishonourable method, and
actually an attempt to slide in in an unusual way, list of salaries to which the
House is committed.
Mr. COOTE: Bringing the Vote in in
this way when we cannot vote on each
item.
Mr. BARBOUR: I accept the explanation which has been made, but I was only
giving expression to the impression
formed in my mind by what the Member
for Tyrone and Fermanagh stated. These
documents which are given to Members
do not, I think, sometimes receive the
careful study their importance demands.
I think a great deal of the present
discussion might have been eliminated if
hon. Members had read a little more carefully the preface which I put to the
Estimates of Services, so that they might
thoroughly grasp the documents which
had been put into their hands. You will
observe this by reading from the preface
On the present occasion the Government
confines itself to asking the House of
Commons to vote a sum on Account of the
services entailed. By adopting this method
of proceedure the House will reserve to itself
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
the opportunity for criticism which will arise
when the financial situation is more fully
developed.
It was not intended to restrict the
liberties of the hon. Members
in
criticising any of the items mentioned in
that document. I am very glad, and I
am sure my right hon. Friend, the
Minister for Finance, will be greatly
gratified to know he has a great deal of
support from hon. Members, who have
criticised items in these Estimates, of his
policy in whatever effort he might make
to safeguard the public purse. That has
been emphasised by every hon. Member
who has spoken, and I am sure such a
policy is one that will be endorsed by everyone of us, especially those of us who may
have connections on the other side of the
channel and recognise that they should
enjoy every bit of remedial and beneficent
legislation which people in their situation
in England and Scotland enjoy; yet at
the same time our industries should not
be hampered or embarrassed by any higher
system of taxation than that which prevails in England and Scotland. In this
connection, while smaller items of expenditure may receive very acute criticism
on occasions of this kind, recommendations
for sympathetic treatment of grievances
or hardships or suffering brought forward
by hon. Members involve very much
larger sums than possibly the whole
amount involved in the present Estimates.
I think it has been sufficiently emphasised
by my right hon. Friend, the Minister for
Finance, that while the Vote on Account
is £130,000, which is a formidable sum,
and one which appears impressive when
it appears at the head of a newspaper
column, as a matter of fact, out of sums
for which you are asked to-day there is upwards of £64,000, I think, which is recoverable from the British Government in
respect of permanent buildings, and the
sum of £50,000 which is placed to the
credit of the Civil Contingencies Fund,
is only to he drawn upon in case
the necessity should rise before the next
meeting of Parliament.
To refer in detail to some of the points
raised by the hon. Member for West Belfast.
I would express the same comment again,
for if he had read a little further down
on page 5 when he asked the question as
to the Chairman of Committees, and of
how the sum of £1,500 is devoted, he
would have seen that the sum is applicable
to the salary of the Chairman of Ways end
Means in the House of Commons. The
duties of the Chairman of
Committees
in the Senate will be discharged by the
Speaker of the Senate.
Mr. LYNN: Does that mean the salary
of the Chairman of Committees of the
Senate is not stated here, and will it have
to be added afterwards!
Mr. BARBOUR: There is only one salary
to be paid to the Speaker of the Senate,
and my understanding is that in this salary
is included such duties as he performs, not
only in his capacity as Speaker of the Senate
but also as Chairman of Committees in
the Senate.
Mr. LYNN: I am sorry to interrupt you
again. Here we have a sum of £1,500 put
down after the Chairman of Ways and
Means. Is the Chairman of Ways and
Means of the House of Commons to be paid
this salary of £1,500 a year?
Mr. BARBOUR: Yes, he is.
Mr. LYNN: That is all I want to know.
Mr. BARBOUR: In the matter of Counsel
to the Speakers we are admittedly a small
body, but the importance of the legislation
coming forward before this Parliament and
the necessity of having absolutely the best
advice that we can get is just as imperative
to us as it is to the Imperial Parliament,
and, therefore, I think it would be a poor
policy to adopt to attempt to economise by
going in for a cheap staff. I think we
should get as efficient officials as we can tʊ
fill the different offices, and then try to get
the best value we can out of them by imposing such other duties on them as they
are able to discharge, and we will get value
for our money.
There is one Counsel to
assist both the Speaker of the House of
Commons and the Speaker of the Senate.
and he would be doing the duty that is
being carried out, by six officers in the
United Kingdom. Again, in the Department of Clerks to the House, you have here
a staff of six, as compared with forty-five
required for the two British Houses, and
the subordinate staff will compare even
more favourably. Naturally in a small
House such as this we cannot expect to
work with the same efficiency as a large
House such as the House of Commons at
Westminster. They are able to take much
better value out of their officers over there.
As regards the reporting and publishing,
this is also a matter which may be capable
of revision, but surely in starting a new
Parliament it would have been an action that
would have been open to criticism had we
made any radical departure from the method
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
of carrying on our proceedings, and possibly
in reporting our debates. As we grow
older and Acquire experience it is
hoped we will take into review every
procedure that involves the expenditure of
money, and if we find any things we can
cut out, I hope such will be cut out
soon as we are satisfied they are no longer
worth the cost of keeping.
as
Then there were the house allowances
made to the Speaker and, I think, two
other officers of the House of Commons.
That was following the usual procedure,
but as the Committee that has just been
appointed has not yet taken the matter
into consideration as to what the remuneration of the Speakers of the House of
Commons and the Senate should be, I think
it would hardly be right to criticise the
items at present.
As regards the different charges made
for members of the staff, and why those
who are loaned from Dublin do not receive
additional compensation, while those who
are borrowed from London do receive
additional compensation--
Mr. M'GUFFIN: May I interrupt the
hon. Member? Does he mean to imply
that the remuneration of the Speakers is
to be the subject of consideration by a
Committee? We do not understand that.
Mr. BARBOUR: It is to be the subject matter of a Bill which is to come
before the House.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: That is a different
matter.
Mr. BARBOUR: I am speaking about
the transferred officials. When final
severance is made in the Departments in
Dublin, and certain officials come North,
and others remain in Dublin, it hardly
remains within the option of the officials
so transferred as to what their future is
to be. They have no option. They have
to come North if they are required North,
whereas it is a different matter in the case
of the officers connected with any of the
Departments in London. In establishing
all the machinery of a new Government it really requires more thorough
knowledge than would be required
by a Department already existing,
and so, while I am sure it is the wish of
every Irishman that all the necessary
positions should be placed in the hands of
our own countrymen—
Mr. LYNN: I did not make any suggestion with regard to taking Englishmen.
I have no objection to them. The only
point I raised was that you were paying an
Englishman while you were not giving the
same pay for the same work to an Irishman.
Mr. BARBOUR: That is just the point
I was explaining. To establish a new
Parliament requires a more thorough
knowledge than it does to carry on an
efficiently-running Department, and in
order that we might have the best possible
advice on the present occasion it was considered that there were certain offices
which could be filled with better advantage
by officials from London, and officials from
London not being under the obligation that officials in Ireland are, namely,
to go where they are sent under this
scheme of distribution, a certain inducement had to be offered to them to come
over here and undertake the work. That
is the reason for that course. I have
already alluded to the matter of the
printing of the Parliamentary Debates. A
very careful estimate has been made to
try to establish some sort of proportion
between the cost of printing at Westminster and here, having regard to the
number of our Members, and the time
during the year that we are likely to sit.
I have already alluded to the criticism by
the hon. Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh as to the form in which the
Estimates are brought forward, and I
would mention that official provision in
the Estimate does not necessarily imply
that the staff has received the approval
of the Ministry of Finance either as to
number or scales of salaries.
In connection with the scales of salaries,
these are already to a great extent fixed
in the terms of the Civil Service. Officials
who are transferred here will be transferred under the same conditions as to
salary and bonus that they enjoy in their
present capacity.
Mr. DONALD: I beg your pardon, Mr.
Chairman, but I want to ask my hon.
Friend a question on that point. Does he
mean to say that a civil servant
transferred from London, with a wife and
family, will come to Belfast and take up
a similar position at the same salary?
Mr. BARBOUR: I am trying to explain that anyone coming from London
has an inducement to come over.
Mr. DONALD: From Dublin?
Mr. BARBOUR: In Dublin they are
under the Civil Service and under instructions, and have to go where they are required. As I was just saying, the provision in the Estimate does not necessarily
imply that the staff there shown has received the approval of the Ministry of
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
Finance. The Departments make their
proposals separately, and it frequently
happens that sanctions have not matured
in time for the Estimate, and if a proposal
appears reasonable it is naturally a wise
precaution to make provision for it in the
Estimate, even though the particulars may
be varied to some extent after discussion.
Mr. CRAWFORD: I feel I would be
lacking in my duty to those who sent me
to this House if Idid not enter as strongly
as I possibly can my protest against the
proposals of the Government this afternoon. I regret exceedingly my first word
here should be a word of protest, and I
protest as far as I am able. A great many
of the positions the Government propose
to fill are, so far as I can see, unnecessary.
They are far too numerous, and all of them
are overpaid. I desire, in the strongest
possible manner, to protest against it, and
I do so. Anyone who does not enter his
protest now will feel his hands tied later.
I am exceedingly pleased my first word,
Mr. Chairman, is under your chairmanship. I have known you for many years,
and I hope you will continue to prosper.
Capt. MULHOLLAND: The general
policy of the Government is, I think,
thoroughly approved by this Committee in
every respect, and we stand solid behind
the Prime Minister and his Cabinet in the
measures he has taken in the past and the
measures he intends to take in the future.
But there is a body of opinion in this
Committee, and I admit I am one of that
body, who is afraid of the horde of officials
entering into
into these departments of
Northern Ireland, and becoming so strong
that in the future we shall not be able
to displace them. (Hon. Members: "Hear!
hear!") In England, as you know, great
efforts have been made to economise in
public departments, and the mistake they
have made is a mistake that I hope the
Cabinet will not make here. That is
that they have got rid of the highly paid
experts and retained hordes of low-paid
servants who are entirely unsuited for the
administration of their departments, and
the result is chaos. In my opinion it is
the best economy to engage the best man
at a proper remuneration to look after a
department, but for heaven's sake let us
not get involved in a complex system of
hordes of officials who are going to act as
parasites, and who in the future we shall
not be able to disturb. There are those
present who know as well as I do that the
overhead charges of a small business are
comparatively very much greater in proportion than the overhead charges of a
large business, and we must, therefore, act
thus in order to establish this Northern
Parliament here. The proportion of public officials necessary to the sound running
of this administration is very much greater
than the proportion required for the
British Isles.
As you know in London, supposing we
have a very clever Air Minister in the Imperial Parliament, and he has an engaging
personality and voluble tongue, he is
probably transferred to an office of
greater merit and becomes, perhaps, Home
