Secretary. Now he knows something
about the air, perhaps, probably not, but
when he
goes into the Home Office he
knows absolutely nothing at all about his
job, and is surrounded by experts who
keep him straight, put words into his
mouth, and he has no trouble
whatsoever if he is a man of some
brains. We must make allowances for the
Government. They must have an
absolutely efficient staff to start this
business, and the Minister of Finance is,
as we know, the very finest man in his
Department that we could have. Let me
say at once that I think it is the general
opinion that every man in the Cabinet is
an expert in his particular line. I do not
take exception to the personnel of the
Cabinet or the way they carry out their
duties, but I would utter one word of
warning—and it cannot be too often repeated-that we have not got here in the
North of Ireland an inexhaustible pool
of money to draw from, and we cannot
afford hordes of officials. I myself, if it
came to a division, would not vote against
the Government, because they are placed
in a very difficult position, but I do feel
that the body of opinion is for Heaven's
sake practice economy and keep your staff
of officials as low as possible, consistent
with efficiency.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I do not intend to
occupy the time of the House very much
this evening, but I feel I would be
neglecting my duty if I did not say that
in a large degree I coincide with the very
trenchant criticism which has been made
in the first instance by the hon. Member
for West Belfast (Mr. Lynn), and in a
more emphatic way by my Friend, the hon.
Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr.
Coote). I do not want to convey to the
House the impression that I endorse, perhaps, the extravaganza of language that
your attention has been drawn to; but
apart from that there is a great deal to
Supply.
Supply.
22 SEPTEMBER 1920.
urge in respect of the manner in which
this Vote is presented, and I am bound to
say it has great justification. I want to say
that the reason I am opposed to the Vote
being presented to-day is that I regard
this Parliament at the moment to a very
large extent as simply nebulous. I think
it is an actuary that we require at the
moment to advise us with respect to the
financial risks we are running rather than
auditors to determine as to the accuracy
of the accounts later on. I feel that if we
consider these questions on a business basis,
and think, for instance, that instead of a
Parliament you are dealing with a business, then you have got to consider that
at the moment we have not actually got
the house in which to house our
machinery. I have not seen yet the architecture of the House we are to occupy.
But, forsooth, there is a Ministry in
operation at the moment, and business of
the House is to be undertaken. I wonder
what is the type of the machinery.
to be adopted? We presume it is on the
basis of the Act on which we as a Parliament have been established. I beg leave
to differ from those who think so, for I am
not disposed to believe that with negotiations going on with Sinn Fein at the
moment the position upon which we exist
as a Parliament may not be absolutely
upset. My own opinion is it will be.
How do we know at the moment what the
Prime Minister may determine with regard to the Southern Parliament-how
seriously it may jeopardise our position and
basis, and the reciprocity existing
between one Parliament and the other
under this Act. There are certain obligations devolving upon the one or other
already that may disappear. It may be
that the Prime Minister will see his way to
forego the claim so far as Ireland is concerned for the payment of any contribution
or proportion of the British Sinking
Fund. It may be they will not be asked
to contribute anything at all, that they
may not be called upon to pay anything in
respect to the liquidation of the war
debt. That would make a wonderful
difference. Things are in a nebulous and
problematical state, yet we have got our
machinery as if the whole Parliament were
acting and all the departments were
functioning. We have got officials
arranged here for the control of departments, whether they are doing anything
at the moment I cannot say. I hope they
are. If they are sketching plans as to
how best to develop their different departments they would be doing a good service
to the nation. I hope during the extended period of their noviates they will
qualify themselves for entering into
the debates of this House where the doings
of their departments will be frequently
subjected to criticism. I am not disposed
to believe at the moment that we as a
House should be considering our finances
in an aspect of finality. I do not think at
the moment we have even the slighest
suggestion as to what may eventuate from
the conference going on, and we cannot
well study all these problematical matters
here. We cannot do so. The Prime
Minister, the other day, in speaking of
his confidence in this matter, said that he
had enjoyed the friendship of the Cabinet
Committee called in to deal with the Irish
question. I noticed that while he enumerated them all he omitted to name the
Prime Minister. He is the one man I
suspect with regard to the whole affair,
and I think I am not a wrong prophet.
While at the same time he may be desirous to settle the question and not in
the slightest way interfere with the interests of the Northern Parliament, I am not
at all sure that if that were the only obstacle that stood in his way he would not
scruple with regard to it. Sinn Fein and
the representatives of Sinn Fein are taking
very good care that they will present all
the difficulties that concern them. Deputations waited upon Mr. de Valera from
Derry, Tyrone, and Fermanagh, and they
put the case to him that so far as they are
concerned they are not prepared to submit
to taxation.
Mr.
The CHAIRMAN: I think I am bound
to remind the hon. Member that we are
really getting somewhat away from the
subject matter before the House; which
is that a given sum be voted on account.
I repeat again, I desire to give very wide
latitude, but I think my hon. Friend must
keep the observations hé is making within
the scope
of the subject under discussion.
M'GUFFIN: I will try to
obey
your behest in the matter
while I also recognise its justice.
I think perhaps you have given me a
license which I have drawn upon to a
degree. I was presenting the case
suggested by my hon. Friend, the Member for Derry, but which he did not
undertake to elaborate. I have got to
understand now that our particularly
acute Chairman is not likely to let us go
beyond the range of Privilege. Well,
sir, I have no other observations to add
L
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. M'Guffin.]
except this. I believe we are practising
economy in a very peculiar way. We
have a very limited reserve. I wonder if
the Chancellor of the Exchequer in the
course of his splendid speech could have
told us what he estimates will be the income of Ireland, and in particular the
Northern portion of Ireland in the
coming year. I do not believe that notwithstanding all his astuteness in this
matter he could give us the faintest idea
what that revenue is going to be, and yet
we are discussing those things as if we
were sure of them. It is at the moment
wholly nebulous what the income of Ireland will be. We cannot even be sure of
the understanding in respect of those
matters that are to come before the Joint
Exchequer Board. It
It should be our
object to curtail our expenses and reduce
them to the very minimum. And I do
not think we are doing that. We are
providing secretaries and under-secretaries. We are duplicating offices which
perhaps at the moment are not necessary
at all. These are questions that require
to be gone
into, and though I am in
absolute sympathy with the Government in
its tremendous ordeal to carry on, at the
same time I must not neglect my right or
neglect my duty in bringing before the
House any criticism I think necessary.
ļ
Mr. POLLOCK: Since my hon. Friend,
the Financial Secretary to the
Ministry of Finance, sat down, I have
heard some surprising criticism on the
employment of officials which I thought
was fully answered by my friend,
who dealt with the details of the subject.
The hon. Member for Down especially
described in very graphic language the
incongruity of hordes of officials in departmental offices attached to those
offices like so many parasites. It seems
to me such an exaggeration of language
that I was inclined to ask what he
really meant by the word "horde." If
he referred to the Departmental Offices
here the word is totally inapplicable.
do not suppose we have in all our departments more than a couple of dozen
employes after eliminating the typists and
stenographers and such who are
necessarily required for the work of
dictation. When my hon. Friend, therefore speaks of hordes of officials, and
hordes of unnecessary officials and makes
such
I
an extraordinary assertion, I
join issue with him at once.
As far as my knowledge goes, and my
knowledge extends to all the departments,
there is not a scintilla of evidence to support that statement.
Mr. LYNN: What about the Ministry
of Commerce?
Mr. POLLOCK: The Ministry of
Commerce is necessary for the establishment of a new and, I think, a very important department of the Government.
I do not think that in an industrial and
commercial centre like Belfast, and even in
the agricultural districts such as the outlying districts are, that any criticism can
be made of the appointment of a Minister
who will devote his time to all the necessities of the development of the industrial
and commercial activities of the Province.
We have lacked in the past an intimate
association between finance and commerce,
and I have no hesitation in saying that
the establishment of a Ministry of Commerce is one of the important matters that
should confront this Parliament.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: It is only a very
recent creation in the British Parliament.
Mr. POLLOCK: Because they have
discovered that they have suffered in the
competition they have had to meet from
the outside world. That is the basis of
the groundwork of the appointment, and
we, guided by their experience, need not
be ashamed of following their example.
I think that is a very sufficient and strong
reason for defending the Ministry of
Commerce.
from
Passing
the hon.
Member for Down-
My
Capt. MULHOLLAND: Might I, at
this juncture, offer an explanation.
remarks were really in the nature of a
warning for the future. I am very pleased
to see the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Finance, has taken to heart
such remarks as have fallen from my lips.
My views are that we have reached the
furthest possible limits in appointments
in this Northern Parliament. At the same
time I pointed out that owing to the exceptional difficulties with which the
Government was confronted I was not prepared to vote against them in this matter.
My few words were in the nature of a
warning rather than criticism applying to
the present.
Mr. POLLOCK: I am very glad indeed
to hear from the hon. Member that his
suggestions were intended rather in the
nature of a warning than a statement of
actual facts, because I am bound to admit

un-

those

the hon. Member has in mind the officials

I may regard as the Central Department.
from which flows out, as it were, the
ap.
pointments of the officials in connection

he would come to the conclusion that the

I remind hon. Members that
who are employed in these Departments.
are frequently working up till 8 o'clock,
9 o'clock, or 10 o'clock at night in order
to overcome the work which has to be
undertaken in the establishment of these
departments. I think it is probably from
want of intimate knowledge of all that the
establishment of departments implies,
that our friends are disposed to be hypercritical. I hope that as we advance in
our arrangements we will reach a stage
at which there will be general satisfaction not only at the efficiency of the
work, but the economical manner in
which it is performed. My hon. Friend
for the North Division, Mr. M'Guffin, was
critical of other matters. He was afraid
that probably the present negotiations
going on may affect our future, and
though it is not strictly relative to the
Vote on Account-
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I was of opinion it
was.
Mr. POLLOCK: I do not criticise it
at all. What I desire to say in relation
to that is that my hon. Friend is perfectly
right. No human being can forecast
what the revenue of this province will be
next year or the
ever changing.
year
after. Conditions are
We have the rise and fall
of trade conditions. We have many
elements entering into it which even
the most accomplished statistician, and
experienced Chancellor of the Exchequer,
could not possibly overcome and foresee.
Therefore, so far as we can judge, it is
our duty to-day to go ahead preparing our
Government work, our Departmental work,
and everything of that kind, on the
assumption that we shall carry through,
and on the assumption that we shall
succeed, no matter what may happen, in
establishing our position as a unit in the
governmental system of the country. I
have no fear in regard to the negotiations
to which my hon. Friend has referred.
It has been clearly stated by the Prime
Minister that Ulster must be ruled out of
these negotiations.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: That is the first time
we have heard that.
Mr. POLLOCK: If my hon. Friend
consults the offer made by the Prime
Minister to De Valera he will see that
there were two conditions—one an agreement to remain within the Empire; the
second that any discussion in regard to the
position of Ulster should be ruled out.
We have, therefore, the security that so
far as our present position is concerned, so
far as the Constitution which has been
created is concerned, we regard our
position as impregnable. Therefore, I
need not enter any further into that, for it
is hardly relevant to the fact that we have
to face certain liabilities. We only ask
that a Vote on Account should be passed
by the Committee to enable the Government to meet its liabilities, which we know
have been incurred, and the liabilities
which might possibly arise in any changed
conditions between now and the meeting
of Parliament in November, and not in
February, as my hon. Friend assumed in
his remarks.
ment
same
I do not think there is any further

principles that govern my hon. Friends/who

emphatically that I place economy in the

like to see that word imprinted above the

Department in the province, and I hope it
will be. I hope the Government will
keep in mind that one fact, that just in

23 SEPTEMBER 1921.
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Friday, 23rd September, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
(SUPPLY).
"That
Resolved,
the Business of
Supply have precedence this day of the
other Orders of the Day."-[Captain
Dixon.]
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
SUPPLY.
Report (22nd September).
Resolution reported:
""
VOTE ON ACCOUNT.
1- That a sum not exceeding
£130,000 be granted to His Majesty on
Account, for or towards defraying the
charges of the several services for which
a Vote on Account is required for the
period ending 31st March, 1922."
Question put: "That this House doth
the Committee in the said
agree with
resolution."
The PRIME MINISTER: I would like
to make a few remarks with regard to
the questions which appear to me to
outstand as the result of yesterday's exhaustive debate. I am quite satisfied, I
may say at the outset, with the results
of our deliberations, and have but little
fault to find with the criticisms which
fell from hon. Members, who had the
same desire that we in the Government
have to practice the utmost economy,
coupled with efficiency, in the administration of the affairs of Northern Ireland. There are two principal points
about which I wish to reassure the House
and the public outside. The first point
is that when we began at the very bottom
of the foundation to establish our
Parliament and administrative machinery,
I discussed with our Minister for
Finance and other Members of the
Cabinet the charges that
that would fall
upon us in the early stages of our work,
and I want to impress upon all, that in
framing the financial part of the Act of
1920 the Imperial Parliament, through
its Treasury officials, divided up the whole
cost of the Civil Service as at present
existing, and allocated to the Northern
area what was considered the fair proportion of the present cost of administration to be expended in Northern Ireland,
or to be allocated to Northern Ireland,
for carrying on the Government when it
started. I asked the Chancellor-I like
to call him that-one specific question:
made in
"Has any provision been
any portion of the White Paper to
cover the extra cost necessitated by
In
Ministerial appointments?"
other
words, "Does the White Paper in its first
compilation include within its ambit any
elasticity in the prognostications which
were formed to allow for inclusion in the
sum mentioned, the necessary Ministerial
and secretarial salaries?" Now, I am not
referring to the Civil Service, that is to
say, to any extension of the Civil Service
in the North, but merely to the question
of whether, in their White Paper, they
had made any allowance whatever for the
formation of a Ministry and Ministerial
salaries. Now, the answer to that
question is in the negative; in other
words, the White Paper was prepared,
and the figures extracted, allowing
for a fair share of the transfer of the
Civil Service personnel to the North.
I then, with the consent of
said to
colleagues,
the
Chancellor,
Where was that proposed to come
from?" and he knows, and I know, that
it was proposed to come from the surplus
which, I believe, works out at something
like two and a quarter millions on paper,
which is practically promised to us by the
Imperial authorities as our surplus after
a complete financial year. My Ministers
and myself placed an obligation upon the
Chancellor to immediately attempt, in
transferring some twenty-nine departments, and, contracting them into five in.
the North here, to include within the limits
of the figure mentioned in the White
Paper the whole Ministerial salaries,
and under-secretaries' salaries in order
that Northern Ireland, when it comes to
function, should not feel that any of the
surplus, which was promised by the Imperial Parliament, had been entrenched
upon so as to add to the weight and
size of the administration in the North.
It is along those lines that we are proceeding. In other words, we are, first of

my
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister.]
three when
the
all, attempting to economise by bringing
the whole of the network covered by the
present twenty-nine departments-I say
twenty-nine but it may be thirtyyou include some of
the Ministerial services, but twentynine at all events we are attempting
to first concentrate that network
of departments which is covered by
the twenty-nine in Dublin into five simple
Ministries here with their various headssix
as against twenty-nine with their
various heads. I am really not considering the question of commerce for
moment, but five principal Ministries with
five Ministers at this Table, in order to
answer any question which may crop up
in the future in reference to the administration of the affairs in this Northern area.
We are endeavouring strictly to carry
out that line of policy, first in the
compression into five Ministries,
and I really do not think it will
be possible to go further after the exhaustive examination which I and my colleagues
have given to the matter, and from the
views of the experts whom we have consulted. They feel that the financial arrangements of our Northern area should be in
the hands of one Minister, because one
Minister in charge of finance has the
super-imposed task put upon him of
criticising from an adverse point of view
any financial proposals that are put forward by the other four or five Ministers,
and therefore it would be a grave error
of judgment to couple up with the
Ministry of Finance any department compelled from its functions to spend money
on public services. That is the reason for
keeping the Ministry of Finance entirely in
a water-tight compartment, and free from
any charges that might be made against
On the one hand it was conserving
it.
public money and on the other it was
associated indirectly with one of the spending departments. Then take the Home
Office, which is one of the most intricate
administrations in Ireland. We felt that
when we picked out of the various
Government departments in Dublin the
duties which were rightly attributable to
the Home Office, and when we examined
them and I do not intend to go into
details to-day--we decided they were quite
sufficient for any Minister to attempt to
grapple with, especially in the initial
stages of the Ministry.
Then I pass on to the question of Labour.
Now, however labour interests may have
influenced legislation and administration
in the past, I have no hesitation in saying
that in this great centre of labour and
industry it is absolutely essential to have
a Ministry dealing with the problem of
Labour in all its aspects and that alone.
(Hon. Members: "Hear! hear!") And I
look upon the Ministry of Labour as one
of the most important Ministries in my
Government. I believe that in future
years the decision that we have come to
in this matter will be generally approved
by the great mass of the working classes
of our community. They will see that
whilst we have Finance, Home Office,
Education, and Agriculture, we have also
marked out for special attention all those
particular problems which go to the very
root of the life of the great mass of the
working classes in our midst. I do not
think anybody will differ from me with
regard to that question. I then pass on
to the question of Agriculture. Agriculture is, I believe, destined to play a very
large part in the future of our Province,
have had the privilege in
Imperial House of Commons of being the
intimate friend of many Agricultural
Ministers and under-secretaries for many
years past, and I have been struck with
the complexity and multitude of the questions that arise in connection with that
great industry.
the
It would appear to me that if I were
to ask our Minister of Agriculture here to
take on any other heavy duties he would
do so to the detriment of the greatest,
for it is the greatest, industry in our
midst. It is quite true that we have
created a Ministry of Commerce, which
my right hon. Friend yesterday emphasized as a very important Department, but
we have asked the Minister of Agriculture
to take that in his charge and we have
asked the Under Secretary for Education
to answer those questions which may arise
in connection with it, and to which I am
sure my friend behind me is prepared to
devote his great energy and professional
ability in dealing with any matters of
commerce.
I then come to education. I cannot
help thinking, and it is admitted on all
hands, that our educational system in the
North is not one that can be even
amended, but one that requires to be
absolutely rooted out of the soil in order
that we may begin to plant a new fabric,
meeting all those difficulties which have
confronted us in the past. It is not,
therefore, too much to have a Ministry
which will devote its whole time to a
Supply.

Supply.

a
problem which so nearly touches the lives. represented in the Mother of Parliaments,
of our people. That is the compass of
therefore all our establishment should
my Government. I do not intend, at
be one-fortieth of what it is in that

But I am not prepared to take
the Ministry, whoever may be the Prime

attached to those most complicated and

as Parliamentary or Under-Secretaries. House or for any member of the public to
There are many other arguments in its decry the importance of our Parliamentary
favour, but it appears to me to be the
Institutions. It is rather a dangerous
great asset in an assembly of this sort, thing to do. I can quite picture our
especially when we are starting the work, opponents who are very alert at every
that we may be assured when, in the event
of a Minister being laid up or away, his
place may be immediately taken by his
Under-Secretary, who comes here, not as
one sent by the Head of his Department,
but with a live knowledge of the Department, not acquired as a civil servant,
but as one with a wide and practical
experience, able to answer to the House--
what the Honse invariably appreciatesthe questions addressed to his Department
from personal knowledge and not merely
as the mouthpiece of someone else. The
same remark applies all round the line:
I do not think there is a single Minister
who could do without an understudy of
some sort. I take the labour problem.
We do desire most earnestly that the
great labour community in this portion
of the world should have a large say in
the adminstration of the affairs in which
they have a preponderating voice. I do
not believe there is, at the moment, a
desire, even amongst the labour element
themselves, that a man should step into
the Ministry and conduct a great department without having at first some preliminary training. Therefore, I look
upon those preliminary steps, and to a
more enlarged and weighty responsibility,
as absolutely necessary if we are to
get the Government machine into anything like the same scheme, the
same system, as operates so successfully in the mother of Parliaments.
That is what I wish to say with regard
to the wide aspect of setting up our machinery. And let me say at this
point that I am not prepared to
accept the view that because we are some
fortieth of the part of the world that is
moment to seize hold of some of these
things, saying, "Why the very Members
are saying this is a small affair. Why, if
it is So, how easy to wipe it away."
There must be a dignity about our
Parliament, and the roots of that Parliament must be very deeply seated in Ulster
soil so that no opponents at any time dare
come forward and say of that great
structure I do not refer to the architectural structure, but the real moral
structure, which is combined in the
Parliamentary Institution-"that is
only a small affair, and we can
easily sweep it to one side."
I desire further, whilst encouraging and
insisting upon economy at every turn, not
to go along lines which would appear to
the outside world as though we here in
Ulster itself placed a minor importance
on our Institution, and felt that a small,
trifling, and niggardly treatment of the
subject was sufficient for the Ulster people.
I do not believe it. Let me put my
finger on a very important point.
hon. Friend for the West Division (Mr.
Lynn) yesterday complained that our estimates included a figure of £8,000 for the
rent of this building, Well, I was not
here, unfortunately, when my hon. Friend,
the Under-Secretary for Finance, was
speaking, but neither my right hon.
Friend nor I nor any Member of our
Government had anything whatever to do
with the contract that was entered into
between the First Commissioner of Works
in London and the Commissioners or
Trustees of the Assembly's College.
My
Mr. LYNN: I am very glad to hear it.
The PRIME MINISTER: The position
of affairs was this: under the Act the
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister.]
Imperial Parliament undertook to build
us Parliament Houses, to furnish them,
to provide Ministerial buildings, to
furnish them, and to equip them in all
respects, and the necessary finance for all
that comes out of the British Exchequer.
This £8,000 appears on our Estimates,
because under the Act everything—and
quite properly so--should come forward
for review in this House; but it was immediately recouped out of Imperial funds,
and therefore it does not cost this Parliament or this country one shilling for the
initial setting-up of our Parliament House
or for the occupation of this building as
temporary premises pending the erection
of the others. I wish to make that perfectly clear. The Commissioner of Works
arranged with me personally that so far
as the taking of buildings in Belfast was
concerned, his Department would be responsible for entering into negotiations.
and coming to a satisfactory conclusion
where we should sit. And, if it were not
an inappropriate moment to say so, I think
that our thanks are due to the Office of
Works for the extraordinary skill and
rapidity and generosity with which they
have met us in this first attempt to give
us a temporary dwelling-place. The first
Commissioner came over himself, and
brought with him his officials.
He gave
official directions, and they were carried
out with a speed and with a courtesy and
a generosity, as I have already said, which
we here are well prepared to acknowledge.
I have no hesitation in saying that I hope
their provision for the future accommodation of our Parliament will be planned in
the same generous and friendly spirit. I
say this in order to clear the air, and also
in order to point out that although the
figures appear on our Estimates here it
does not mean one penny of taxation or
one shilling of any person's money, so far
as Ulster is concerned, in setting up our
Parliament and getting it into working
order.
•
Then, I come to another point—that is
with regard to the permanent buildings
which are to be erected. In one of the
newspapers the other day I saw an analogy
drawn between our position and that of
the Imperial Parliament as a mother
giving a wedding present to her daughter.
and that it behoved the daughter to be
very careful. Well, that is all very well,
but I do not know that the writer of that
particular letter in the paper-I think it
was signed "Ratepayer"-I am not quite
sure that he was a married man, because
as a rule when a parent is giving a present
to his child on marriage-as a rule, I do
not say always-the parent says,
"Here
is something on your marriage, and I hope
that you will make good use of it." It is
on a very, very rare occasion that the gift
is flung back, with the remark, "It is too
big, too generous, too large." What I
want to say is that we have now an opportunity which may never occur again, and
if we
were put in small and confined
quarters for the first seven or eight years
of our history, it would be in vain to hope
the British Treasury would give us something better at the end of that time because we had outgrown our accommodation. But do not for a moment let it be
taken that we are exaggerating the matter.
We are a very business-like community,
and there is no desire whatever on our
part to say, Give us a palace like that at
Westminster, or give us some gorgeous
house to which we can point as the home
of our Ulster Parliament." Not in the
slightest degree, Mr. SPEAKER. What we
desire, what we will get, and what we are
entitled to get, is a building and Ministerial
business premises which will be on the one
hand convenient to us, and on the other
hand, I hope, will be sufficiently stately
to maintain the dignity which should
attach to any House of Parliament.
""
But I think that I detected in
that letter a very wise sugestion,
which was that we should not
allow the fabric which has been erected
at the cost of Imperial money-should not
allow ourselves to be rushed into some
great expenditure in order to keep up
something which, after all, is too big for
our present needs. So far as that part of
the letter is concerned I heartily agree with
the writer. In other words, none of us
must think that this is a honeymoon, and
that funds can be thrown here and there
on luxuries, while possibly on calmer reflection on returning home it might be
said that you could have done it a little
better. I have, I think, put the case as
well as I can. We are not on a honeymoon. We are seriously settling down to,
on the one hand, finding everything fitting
and proper as it should be, and on the
other seeing that there is no gorgeous waste
of money, or any pouring out of funds in
order to try to live up to a dwelling-house
too large really for our needs. Let there
be no mistake upon that point. When I
was asked to take on the heavy responsibilities of the task which has been placed
upon my shoulders I saw clearly all these
Supply.
23 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Supply.
difficulties, and it was due to the kindness
of some persons in saying that my slight
knowledge of affairs across the water might
be able to guide my colleagues and my
friends in the House in choosing a fair
course between all the conflicting interests
that I consented. I do not wish to say
more on the matter except this that
everyone will realise that these problems
have been very cautiously considered. They
have exercised the whole of my time, but
I am determined quite determined that
in no circumstances will I be swayed from
exercising a fair and just decision on the
conflicting interests which arise practically
every day, in order that all of us here may
be perfectly satisfied in our Own minds
that we have queathed to our successors
something which is not too costly to carry,
and at the same time is of sufficient
character to distinguish our Northern
Parliament. Now may I leave that subject
alone in order to deal with the second.
am only prepared to deal with two of
what I call the larger aspects of this
question on this occasion. The second
I
point is to allay any feelings in the minds.
of those present and of the outside public
that there is the slightest extravagances
in the appointments that have already been
made, either as regards number or as
regard's salary. We might have refused
to appoint anybody to any of the Departments the heads of the Departments, five
of which we have get up, .or
Clerk at the Table, or anyone. We might
have refused and said-"We cannot and
will not move in this matter until
such times as the appointed day is made
for transfer of the Civil Servants." That
Our
We
is an attitude we might have taken up..
might have said" The Government of
Ireland Act prescribes that on a certain
day the Committee to deal with the transfer
of Civil Servants must function, and when
it functions the Northern area will be
given out of the pool a certain number of
officials who, under the Act, it is obliged
to absorb into the various Government
Departments."
Now, delay in Ministers getting familiar
with the intimate details of their departmental work would have been inevitable
if that course had been pursued, but we
wanted our Ministers in the taking up of
their departmental duties to get into
touch with the civil head of each department in order to grasp the intricacies of
the machine. We
would have had
to take whoever was allocated to us, and
this I treat as an all-important point. I
really hope hon. Members will remember
that if we had waited until the Appointed
Day we would have had handed to us by
the Civil Service Committee a certain
number of gentlemen from A to Z, with
their qualifications marked opposite to
their names.
We would have been told.
"You have a vacancy here, you have a
vacancy there, there is a head wanted for
labour, one wanted for education, one
wanted for another thing. Here are
these men whom we as a Committee are
allocating for these positions." There
would have been grave danger of that
procedure, and I therefore exercised the
perogative-there are very few, but one
of the perogatives of a Prime Minister is
that he shall hold in his own hands
nominations to the higher appointments
in the Civil Service-and, therefore, I
immediately took in hands, with the
assistance of my Ministers the selection of
the top dog-the head of each of these
administrative departments, because on
the one hand I wanted my Ministers and
the heads of the departments to get into
harmonious relationships as soon as
possible, to understand one another and
get the air clear as to the line of policy
they proposed to pursue, and on the other
hand I have always felt that the smooth
working the proper working-of a department depends to an enormous extent
I may say principally on the chief Civil
Servant of the Department. (Hon. Members, "Hear! hear!") When one enters a
section of a manufacturing concern you
can tell almost from room to room who
the head of the department is, and you
will often find that the trouble and
difficulty in certain departments of one of
these great centres of industry are because of the failure of the head of the
department. We have not had much
experience yet of our
our choice, but with
what we have had, I believe we have laid
the foundation of a proper Civil Service
in Northern Ireland. Esprit de Corps
almost has already been established,
which will go a long way to remove
suspicions from the Civil Servicesuspicions which have existed so long
throughout all parts of Ireland. That is
no small accomplishment, but in order to
do that we had to produce in detail the
sketch Estimates-I will call them thatwhich were in your hands yesterday. If
we had not moved in the matter we would
have been sitting here without any
necessity for Estimates at all, but we
thought it would be prudent to get our
hands on these appointments and have
these made preceding the Appointed Day
M
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister.]
for the transfer of the services, because
any servant who is engaged in the North
of Ireland on the Appointed Day is transferred practically without question.
Another point is, these men
who have been appointed have not
been given extravagant salaries.
They are being remunerated at the ordinary
rate ruling in the Civil Service according
to their grade, and although some people
may think that the salaries Civil servants
receive are more than they ought to be,
I want to point out two considerations.
One is, if we depart from the Civil Service
rate of remuneration and conditions of
service, it is just possible we may prevent
some of the best grades from coming in
to our service. Now it would be a disaster
if we run the slightest risk of not getting
the best men available for the various
positions. (Hear! hear!) I say that most
emphatically. Let us have as few as
possible, but let us get the very best. I
believe myself our choice up to the moment
has been universally approved, and that
all those who have been appointed secured
the confidence at all events of
the Ministers under whom they
served. Let me point out another very
important thing. Many of these appointments are of a highly technical nature.
(Hon. Members: Hear! hear!) It
is no use Members saying Civil
Servants." There are Civil Servants
whose whole lifetime has been spent
in acquiring technical knowledge,
and if you go to the Treasury
in London and sit down there
with some of the finest Civil Servants to be
found in the world, and have a talk with
them, you will find men expert in Customs,
Excise, the Home Office, thoroughly familiar
with the Exchequer and Audit Department
or with the Excise all of them being developed into experts in their own departments. It is naturally easy to say that A
or B or C is a Civil Servant who will do
this or that or the other thing, but it requires a considerable amount of care in
selecting the best of men.

Now that is one course which created
a little uneasiness in the North here--that
we were choosing these persons in addition
to what would be allotted to us subsequently. That is not so. These men will
take the place of a certain number who
it might not be possible to allot to us, and,
therefore, we are not saddled with anything
additional, because, of course, having the
right and privilege of filling such appointments, I filled them in advance in order
to secure the best men. This is another
point that causes a little mischief.
But if you will only reflect for a moment
what a vista is open for the Ulster of the
future. Because we have to take
experienced men-men who will carry us
over that stage, over the difficult stage,
it does not mean that these positions will
not be open to the youth of Ulster to
compete for in the future. Where we can
possibly do it we propose to put in an
understudy. I can picture this chair
being filled by an Ulsterman— a young
Ulsterman coming from one of our
institutions; it may be a college, or it may
be one of the lesser scholastic institutions;
sitting under our present experienced
clerk at the Table, and picking up the
office so that in the fullness of time he
may be able to step into the position of
Chief Clerk at our table. And throughout all the service I am hoping in time the
same thing will occur. But it is
impossible for us to run any risk of the
machinery breaking down. May I take
our Clerk at the Table to-day. I venture
to say there are not six men in the whole
world who could fill that position. We
are extremely fortunate in having such
ripe experience at our command in this
House. And in every department the
same thing occurs, for, remember, legislation based upon any mistakes made either
by Ministers or Chiefs of departmentslegislation based upon mistakes will be
anxiously awaited by our opponents from
the very first day we put an Act on the
Statute Book. We must be perfect in every action, and the
accumulation of scientific knowledge
embodied in 2 Bill is much
more than the majority of Members
appreciate. But they will later
on.
I can only say, charged with the responsibility as I am, of seeing this whole fabric
built up on safe grounds, I could not
take that responsibility unless I had those
who were qualified to make positively
certain that no flaw will occur and no mistakes will be made.
I have occupied the time of the House
especially to clear the air on these two
points, and I do not propose to touch upon
anything else. Hon. Members may wish
to raise something fresh to-day which we
did not touch yesterday. If there is any
ex, lanation wanted my right hon. Friend
the Minister of Finance, and my
hon.
Friend behind me (Mr. Barbour) will be
only too ready to give any explanation
necessary. My concluding remarks to the
House are these-that some Members have
Supply.
Supply.
23 SEPTEMBER 1921.
not quite yet realised the responsibility of
caution, not only in this House, but outside. The public must not be too much
shaken. They must not be rattled.
Let us have criticism. I hope criticism
will be guided in what I call the same
friendly way between us, but there is no
necessity, I assure you, to use harsh words.
Let our language be couched in such a
way as not to give a handle to our
opponents outside. (Hon. Members:
"Hear, hear.") The transition stage
through which we are going is one of the
most anxious that has been confronted for
years by the Ulster people. A false word,
a false tone will convey to certain of those
outside the idea that we are not all one
way of thinking. I dealt yesterday with
an interruption of my hon. Friend, to
assure the outside public, and especially
our friends that I will as far as I can
give the fullest scope for criticism in this
House at all times, and not take the
slightest degree of risk. It would be
perfectly ridiculous for me to attempt anything of the kind in the direction of risk
in the first instance, and in the second,
I have no desire to do So.
But do let us in our deliberations
put our heads together with regard
to these matters rather than attempt
to knock our heads together in the spirit
of any opposition. (Hon. Members: "Hear!
Hear!")
Mr. LYNN: I agree with the concluding
words of the Prime Minister that it is of
the
the most absolute importance that
public should not be rattled.
I also agree
with him that there is no necessity that we
cannot carry on our debates, hold different
views if necessary without any lack of unity,
because, as I said yesterday, there is no
lack of unity in this House (Hon. Members: Hear; Hear!")-and there is no
lack of unity in Ulster. (Hon.. Members:

Hear! Hear!"') And I want to emphasise that because I do not want to be
always met when I get up to put my views,
which may differ from those of Ministers,
with the accusation that I am trying to destroy the Ministry or sow the seed of discord
in Ulster. I have only one object in view,
and that is to strengthen the Ministry and
to strengthen them not only here but in the
country. It is important, as the Prime
Minister has said, that we should strengthen
the Ministry and this Parliament in the
country. The country is looking to us to
set them a good example, not only in unity
but an example that can be followed in
many other respects. I think in the concluding part of the Prime Minister's speech
he raised and knocked down with great
agility a number of objections which I have
never heard seriously raised anywhere yet.
I for one- and I suppose I have been as
hard a critic as anyone-I have never said
a single word in regard to Civil Service
appointments. I go further and I that
say
it is of the utmost importance that Ulster
should have the best Civil Service that it
is possible to get. I know something of
the Civil Service throughout the United
Kingdom. The Civil Service is often abused,
but, believe me, the country owes more to
the Civil Service than it can ever repay. 1
have met Civil Servants who are not getting
one-fourth of what they should be getting
in return for their brains and ability; and
I have known a great many who are getting
four times too much. But that is not the
point. I think no one here objects to the
Ministry getting the best Civil Servants
that can be had, and I, too, like the Prime
Minister, look forward to the time when
Ulstermen--who, by the way, have never in
the past gone in much for the Civil Service
-when young Ulstermen will be attracted
to Our Civil Service, and will devote
their brains to solving the problems that
Ulster will have to meet from time to time.
I think this House, and, indeed, all that is
worth having in Ulster, will agree with the
Prime Minister when he says he wants firstclass, and, indeed, the best, Civil Servants
to be got, not only in Ireland, but in the
Empire. I raised no objection, and I do
not know that any member who criticised
the Government raised any objection. But
in that connection, Sir, I would like to ask
the Prime Minister whether, in making these
appointments it is not necessary to take
over our quota from Dublin. As I read the
Act, I thought when the Civil Service
Committee was set up that Committee
would allocate a certain number of Civil
Servants to the North and a certain number
to the South, and that we had the right to
say whether we would take them or not.
Now, it is very interesting; it raises a very
important point, and one I would like Mr.
Chancellor in replying to enlighten us on.
If it is not necessary for us to take over the
Civil Servants in Dublin, then let us go on
at once and establish our whole Civil
Service, and not wait for this Committee.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): It would bankrupt you.
Mr. LYNN: If I interpret the Prime
Minister rightly, it is not necessary for us
to take over any of these people from
Dublin if we have already filled the positions
they would fill.
The PRIME MINISTER: Perhaps I may
put my hon, Friend right about this at
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister.]
if Canada or any of the provinces of Canada
neglected the dignity of their Parliamentary institutions?
I say we
Whatonce. The Prime Minister has only the
right to nominate the heads of Departments,
which in this case amount to five or seven
or whatever the number may be.
ever is considered the head of a Department only is filled by the Prime Minister.
All other Departments have to be filled
from Departments in Dublin, but the point
I wish to impress upon the Committee is
that if we had not appointed the top men
now we might have only had Dublin officials
to select from when we arrived here, and
whether we have to superannuate five or
six officers is to me a matter of indifference,
because I would sooner have the head of
a Department nominated by my Ministers
and myself than to take someone we do
not agree with from Dublin or elsewhere.
Mr. LYNN: I am glad I have given the
Prime Minister an opportunity of clearing
up this point, because it was the impression
left in my mind. I quite agree that he
has done the right thing in appointing the
Heads of Departments, and I go a step
further and say that in respect of every one
of those heads he could not have made a
better appointment. Now I pass from the
Civil Service. I pass to the question that
I raised in this debate on Tuesday, that
I raised yesterday, and it is that we
are starting our Parliament and Parlimentary institutions on far too large a
scale. I am quite as keen as the Prime
Minister is that the dignity of Parliament
should be maintained, that our Parliament
should be worthy of the province, but you
cannot super-impose institutions of the
Imperial Parliament on a small province.
After all, there is no man more rroud of
Ulster than I am, but I realise that Ulster
has only got a population of 1,300,000, and
it is not possible for us to carry on our
backs huge institutions that can be carried
by a larger people. Mr. Chancellor knows
the conditions in the Colonies, and
not going to weary the House by going into
details with regard to the Parliamentary
side but I ask the Chancellor and I
ask the Prime Minister to compare the
arrangements they are making for this
Parliament with, say, the Dominion Parliament of Canada, which represents a
population of eight millions, or the
Parliament of Ontario, to which the
Prime Minister referred on Tuesday
last. I ask them to compare the
arrangements they are. måking here
with the arrangements made there; I ask
him to compare the salaries that are being
voted here with the salaries that have been
paid there for a long time, and I ask him
I am
are beginning on a far too
extravagant scale. I am very sorry to
disagree with the Prime Minister. There
is no Member of this House has a higher
respect for the Prime Minister than I have,
but when I disagree with him I say so,
and when I cannot agree with him I will
go into the Lobby and vote against him,
and I would think less of the Prime Minister
if he disagreed with me and went into the
same Lobby. But I do say emphatically
and with all respect to the Prime Minister
that I think our Ministry is too large and
might have been compressed still further.
I am not going to pass any criticism on
the Ministry of Labour. If there is one
Ministry I would not criticise it is the Ministry of Labour, because I have such a regard
for it, and I, for one, am prepared to judge
it by results. There was no Ministry
of Labour in the United Kingdom until
1916, and the Ministry of Labour was
brought in with the object not of benefitting Labour in Great Britain, because it
is never done, but with the object of providing political appointments for the friends
of Mr. Asquith and Mr. Lloyd George.
am sure my right hon. Friend the Minister
of Labour will see to it that in his Department they do not follow the example of
the Ministry across the Channel, where
it costs £20 to get a man employment,
whereas the same work is done by a trade
union for 6d.
I
That is not the sort of thing we want.
We want efficiency. We are a young institution, and I still contend we could have
done without a Ministry of Commerce.
Those who know anything of the history of
Belfast-and no one knows it better than
the Chancellor of the Exchequer-knows
that Belfast has grown from a very small
village to one of the greatest cities in the
Empire. Its commerce is second to none,
and that commerce has been built up not
by Government Departments. I am really
sorry that the Prime Minister seems to
think that the future success of Ulster depends upon the interference of Government
Departments. Personally, I think the
future success of Ulster depends on the
inactivity of Government Departinents. It
depends upon the Government Departments leaving us alone to do our own work
in our own way as far as that is possible.
There is not, in my opinion, the slightest
necessity for a Ministry of Commerce.
There are very many points that one
might raise. I am not going to weary the
Supply.
Supply.
23 SEPTEMBER 1921.
I went
House by going into them now.
into them yesterday as far as it was legiti
mate to raise them on the Estimates, but I
do say, and I say it in all sincerity to the
Prime Minister, that unless he and his
Cabinet, and, indeed, unless this Parliament
insist on the most rigid economy, there
will be a sorry day of reckoning in Ulster.
Dr. MORRISON: I desire to avail
myself of the wide field opened up
by the Prime Minister in this discussion,
and I would like to say at the outset that
until the question of expense and salaries
arose, the conduct of the Prime Minister
and the Cabinet had my entire sympathy
and approval, and, so far as I know, the
unanimous support of every Unionist in the
Six Counties area. He raised the point
about the allocation of the amount that
should come to Ulster when the service between Southern and Northern Ireland is
separated, and so far as I could gather, he
seemed to imply that we are perfectly at
liberty to spend the money proposed to be
expended. I join issue with him on that
point at once, and for this very good and
substantial reason. Everybody who knows
anything about administration in Ireland
knows that in this country the common
cause of grievance has been Dublin Castle,
and the administration of Dublin Castle ;
that it has been out of touch with the
people, and that when we got our local
Councils and Parliament we would be able
to save a great deal of expense. The Prime
Minister has assured us also that with the
surplus of two millions we have a right to
take a frolic in initial legislation, and dip
our hands as readily and as often as we
chose. I occupy a very unusual position.
All my life I have been fighting for
salaries, but it was always for salaries of badly-paid men. That is a
very different thing to supporting high
salaries for men whose duties are
light. I abstained from voting yesterday,
and I did it with great reluctance, because
I thought those salaries were too high, and
also as a protest against voting high
salaries, unnecessarily high salaries in my
opinion, when professional men, clergymen,
and secondary teachers are living practically on the verge of starvation. I did it,
I repeat, with great reluctance.
And I say
now deliberately-it may be roughly, but
it is my considered opinion that the
salaries of officials should be reduced by
one-third.
The Prime Minister pointed out as one
of his arguments that the placing of high
salaried officials lent a dignity-(hon.
Members: "No, no. Efficiency ")—well,
efficiency-to the House. He also, I think,
for I took a note of it,, referred to the
dignity, and there is truth in it. There
is a certain amount of dignity in a man
who draws £1,500 a year. (Laughter.)
I should be delighted to have that experience. But if that is so, I should like
to ask how is it that your chaplains get
£50 a year? Is the Church, the high
office these men hold, the fact that they
are trained university graduates-does
not that entitle them to some, at least, of
this dignity, and to raise them from the
position of charwomen? What I think
we are going to have in Ulster as the
result of this question of salaries—and I
speak as a man who has been in politics
since 1886-we will not have an antiwaste candidate, but we will have anti-
Ulster candidates who will attempt to
lead our people to a united Parliament in
Dublin. That is my opinion, and I regret
to state it. But it is the opinion I have
formed. There was one sentence in the
Prime Minister's speech that I regret.
He said and he said it with emphasisthat he is not to be forced from certain
principles. Now, Sir, that is a very high.
attitude to take. I consider we
here, not to consider ourselves individually, but we are representatives of the
electorate. The Prime Minister is not
only the representative of the electors,
but he is the representative of this House.
And I think it would be wise of him to
take up the position that whatever the
Members desire he is willing to conform to,
and that we should all take up this position which in the end we will be forced
to take up--that the people of Ulster are
the people who are going to control these
services and control this expenditure.
are
am ex-
Mr. COOTE: I will not detain the
House very long. I wish to say at the
outset and it is only due to the House to
say it that I am very sorry that any
words of mine spoken yesterday were
taken up
by the Prime Minister as a
personal attack upon him.
ceedingly sorry that he should think so.
I used the criticism that I did absolutely
honestly, and I should we very sorry if
any individual within the House should
feel himself wounded in any way. I could
have retorted to the Prime Minister. I
have any quantity of language which I
might have used. I am not here to try
to create bad feeling. I am here to try
to represent my people in the best way I
can. I am not here to insult anybody.
I have no right to insult anybody. I do
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
not apologise. I had not the Prime
Minister in my mind at the time.
The PRIME MINISTER: I most
gladly accept the very full explanation
of my hon. Friend, the Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh. I must say
that my experience of Parliamentary
life has led me to believe that the very
fact of standing up in one's place is an
attitude towards the House that is always
deeply appreciated, even apart from any
words the Member uses. I am sure I speak
on behalf of my colleagues, to whose
defence I will always spring if 1 think
anything is said that should not be
said. But my hon. Friend has met me
and the House in a generous spirit, which
reminds me of our old and cordial days
when we worked together in the Imperial
House of Commons. I accept his statement most gladly.
Of
Mr. COOTE: I wish to say on the
general question that I have feelings
of the greatest apprehension, and I do
try to guard this principle with the
greatest jealousy, that there will be nothing done that will have a tendency to
create a bureaucracy in this House.
I object to anything like a bureaucracy
in this House, and I say that this multiplying of offices-Parliamentary assistantsecretaries and other secretaries-took the
breath from me when I heard of it
yesterday. I object very strongly to
some of the items in the schedule that
was brought before us yesterday.
course, Mr. SPEAKER, I do not propose to
make any attempt to divide the House on
this question to-day. We had the temerity
to divide the House yesterday. We then
made our protest; and we are not here
to waste the time of the House, or to
create any friction unnecessarily. We
have made our protest, and our protest
is before the country; and I think that
the Chancellor of the Exchequer will be
well advised, especially as he peruses even
the friendly Press of Ulster this morning
-he will be well advised to have regard
to the handwriting on the wall.
We do
not object to the Civil Service, but outside
the Civil Service you might cut down
these Estimates for officials by at least
fifty per cent., and even then you will be
giving them higher salaries than ever they
earned in their lives before. That is our
point, shortly and simply, and we also
raise a protest in view of the situation
that is around us.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE:
Will you mention in detail the particular
officers whose salaries you propose to
reduce by fifty per cent., as I am not
aware that any salaries are fixed for the
officials in question?
There
Mr. COOTE: We made our objection
yesterday; we gave our views and mentioned all the offices. We were very
careful not to go outside the bounds that
were assigned to us, and we exhausted all
the limits that we could possibly exhaust,
in order to bring before the House those
offices. I believe that we covered the
whole ground. In the times in which
we live we must not close our eyes to the
fact that in business circles all over the
three kingdoms there is hardly a business
firm with from a quarter to half a million
of money at its back which has earned a
thousand pounds of profit for the last year,
That is a very big statement.
are many employers who, instead of
having a salary of £1,000 or £1,500,
probably lost £10,000 last year. And
yet you propose to bring in here men of
an ordinary type from the public outside
-relations and friends, probably, of
people in this House and outside this
House and you endow them with salaries
of from £1,000 to £1,500 a year.
that the public will not stand it. I know
many ex-Service men and officers are
walking the streets without employment.
You are doing what you can to do harm
to Ulster. These are the points which I
wish to put before the House. I want to
make
my protest in the most public
manner, and I want to say that we shall
follow this up next year. Owing to the
peculiar way in which you have brought
the matter forward-I do not say you
have withheld the Estimates from
have withheld them
you
from amendments being put down, and
from passing a vote on each particular
item. I have made my protest, and you
must not feel that we are doing wrong

We warn you that if you fill these offices
