criticism-but
I say
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I rise to say, in the
presence of the assembled House, that I
am absolutely unrepentant with respect to
the action I took on the voting yesterday.
If I voted against the Government, I did
so conscientiously, and I do not think that
the Government are entitled to feel
Supply.
Supply.
23 SEPTEMBER 1921.
offended in view of the fact that the
Prime Minister invited every Member of
the House to furnish him with as much
criticism as he thought fit. I took
advantage of that invitation; and expressed my opinion. My great objection
to the matter was the form in which it
was brought before the House. It could
as easily have been done, as the hon.
Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh
suggests, by coming forward and asking
for a Vote on Account. But instead of
that there is included in it by arrangement, a method by which fixed charges
of a permanent nature are laid upon the
House. One thing that struck me as very
peculiar was that although you have
appointed a Committee for the purpose of
considering what remuneration should be
given to Ministers, you do not think it
right and consistent to include within that
category the two highest offices in the
Senate and the House of Commons.
You take upon yourselves to fix the
salaries of these offices, to which, let me
say, I do not in the slightest degree demur.
I believe that these officers are not being
remunerated above what their dignity and
importance deserve; but what I do object.
to is this: you single them out and deal
with them yourselves, whereas you relegate
the claims of your own officers to a committee.
Mr. POLLOCK : May I intervene? The
hon. Member will have an opportunity of
discussing the salaries of the officials in
question through the machinery of a Bill
which will be presented to this House in
the ordinary way. When that opportunity
arises he can criticise and move reductions
if he so pleases.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: The interpolation of
the Chancellor is hardly to the point.
What I am contending is this. You fix
the salaries in respect--
Mr. POLLOCK: Propose. We cannot
fix the salaries. The House fixes them.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: You have stipulated
them, whereas, in the case of Ministers, you
have not, that is the point. It would
be for us then to move a reduction in order
to criticise them, a course that is abhorrent
to some of us, and we would not like to
do SO.
But if the matter had been
submitted to a committee, we would
have felt freer to approach it in
respect of criticism. I think the course
that should have been taken was for the
Chancellor to ask for a Vote on Account,
That
which, I am persuaded, the House would
have accorded him, instead of adopting
the course which he did. A great deal
has been said by the Prime Minister that
was unnecessary-a great deal that was
put up and knocked down very simply.
I am
absolutely in accord with every
Member of this House who says that in
the future debates of this House, and
especially in regard to finance, it will be
the duty of those who call themselves
genuine representatives of the people, to
consider in the veriest detail every item
of expenditure, and to see that nothing of
an unnecessary expense is incurred.
is a privilege that every Member has a
right to enjoy; it is a duty that every
Member has a right to discharge. I am
very glad, indeed, to think that the criticism of the Vote yesterday is not going to
convey to the Prime Minister or to any
Member of the Cabinet the impression that
offence or insult was implied. If that was
the motive of any observation I dissociate
myself from it immediately. I
hold every Member in this House
in the highest respect, and
challenge any Member of it, or any
Member of the Imperial House, to say
that ever in the course of my public career
I used one offensive expression with regard
to any man. I treat a man and his office
differently. A man may be perfectly loyal SO far as the cause
we represent is concerned, he may
have its interests at heart in the
highest degree, but we cannot write him a
diploma of infallibility. That I will not
subscribe to. Every Member, from the
Prime Minister down, is capable of making
a mistake, but according to some of the
replies from the Ministerial Bench
yesterday, you would have thought we
were the only persons who could be in
error. I want to say my actions in this
House are not to be regarded in any sense
as detracting from the power and prestige
of the Government.
I am as loyal a

supporter of the Government as any man
in the House, but what is done must be
done democratically. That is my position.
Mr. M'MULLAN: I wish to thank the
Prime Minister for the very fine and reassuring statement he made to-day. I
have listened during the last three or four
days to statements, to criticisms, and to
explanations, but to me the statement
made to-day has given more satisfaction
than any other statement I have heard.
I rise without premeditation because I
think that our Cabinet has been suffering
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. M'Mullan.]
rather severely from adverse criticism, and
I would like to express my faith in our
ministry. I must admit that yesterday I
listened to the adverse criticism of the
financial proposals, and I felt a little shaken
and a little uncomfortable, and yet unlike
the member for Queen's University, I
went into the Division Lobby with the
Government because in spite . of
the information I had gathered from the
speeches from the critical speechesmade yesterday, and from the short glance
I had had occasion to make through
the papers with which we were supplied,
I felt that the Members of the Government and the Prime Minister knew the
subject and had thought it out themselves, and that they were as keen on
economy as I was, and that it was fair
and right that I should trust their judgment. I would like to say that I am very
much in favour with the Prime Minister's
remarks about the payment of the higher
officials. I do not think that for such a
House as this and for a Parliament
which is intended to rule over a million
and a quarter of people we should have
anything but the greatest efficiency in our
permanent officials. I say pay the top
man well and get the best that is
available, though I would like-I do not
intend to press the matter-but I would
like to have heard that the Assistant-
Secretaries to Under-Secretaries, might
have been appointed temporarily during
the press of business, and later on when
business became normal and more easily
dealt with it would be possible to do away
with the Under-officials. I do not think
that question has been raised, and it may
be dealt with perhaps by our Minister of
Finance. I understand it is a very
general axiom that an honest politician is
something of a rara avis. There was once
an honest politician in Westminster, and
apparently at the time he was there honest
politicians were scarce. He was known as
"Honest John," and he said no man was
worth more than £500 a year, but
"Honest John" was offered £2,000 a year
Hon. MEMBERS: £5,000.
Mr. M'MULLAN: It was £2,000 at
first, and he had no hesitation in accepting it. I feel that we in this Northern
Parliament that the people of Ulsterare very fortunate in having a Cabinet
composed of honest politicians. I feel we
have in this Cabinet men of great ability,
men of sterling honesty, who are absolutely
trustworthy, and have the interests of
Ulster at heart. I hope I shall be able
to support them freely during the time I
have the privilege of sitting in this House.
I do not propose to support them in all
measures. I want to use my own judgment, but my tendency will be to support
the Government in everything they bring
forward when I feel I can rightly and
honourably do so.
I think our Prime Minister is to be
congratulated that in the selection of
Ministers and Parliamentary Secretaries
he has secured some of the most capable,
most highly respected, most experienced,
and most trustworthy men in our
munity, and I trust- indeed I am surethat the work they have undertaken they
will carry out faithfully and well.
com-
Mr. CRAWFORD: I trust I have had
a wrong impression from some things I
have heard this afternoon, because those
who had the temerity yesterday to say
anything against the Government were
looked upon as wreckers. (Hon. Members:
"No! no!") I resent any idea of that
description. I am as truly in touch with
the cause of Ulster as any Member of this
House, and yet I am unrepentant of what
I did yesterday notwithstanding what I
heard even to-day. I was talking to a
countryman this morning, and he said he
greatly resented the fact that while the
Imperial Parliament chastises
us with
whips in taxation, this Parliament of
Ulster is going to chastise us with scorpions. But I still hope, notwithstanding
the offices created and the large salaries
paid, that the Chancellor of the Exchequer and the Prime Minister will considerably reduce some of the salaries we
have seen in this Paper. I have risen
because I felt it was necessary to give some
explanation of my action yesterday, and
I hope the opposition I have given will
bear fruit by and by.
Mr. POLLOCK: Following the general
indication of the Government's policy that
the Prime Minister so ably gave to-day,
I should like to enter into some further
detail, not only in regard to the general
question of salaries, but also to the number of positions which have been filled, and
the nature of the appointments themselves. But before I do that I may be
permitted to deal with some of the
criticisms which hon. Members submitted, I have no doubt in the very best
spirit, and so far as criticism is concerned,
Supply.
Supply.
23 SEPTEMBER 1921.
I
I not only welcome it, but I would like to
have it here. It brings out into the light any
mistakes which might have been made,
and enables them to be removed. We need
have no fear of being whipped with
scorpions in the matter of taxation.
assure the House I will not be Minister
of Finance when it will be necessary to
impose any additional taxation to that
imposed by the Imperial Parliament.
(Hear, hear.) It is not necessary to
follow that question now, because matters
at this moment are in a nebulous condition. But I am afraid that these apprehensions and fears held by people receive
some encouragement by the expressions
occasionally used in this House. (Hear,
hear.)
""
I
My Friend, the hon. Member for Down,
spoke of the Assistant Secretaries.
presume he meant the Assistant Secretaries of the Administrative Departments.
There are so few of these who have
already been appointed that I do not think
in the meantime they are open to
criticism, and any provisions made in the
Provisional Estimates are for those who
may be appointed between now and when
Parliament next meets, if such becomes
necessary. But I will deal with them in
detail as I proceed. The gentleman, who
was described as "Honest John,'
accepted a salary I suppose far beyond his
expectations. I believe he vindicated his
action from the fact that he would not
receive less than the standard wage
because he was a trade unionist, and to
have done SO he would have been a
blackleg."
But I wish to take some of
my hon. Friends in order, and to remove,
as I say, some misconceptions. The hon.
Member for the North Division objected
most strongly to the non-inclusion of the
consideration of the Speakers' salary-the
Speaker of the Senate and the Speaker of
this House-in the work of the Committee
which has just been started to settle
salaries. Let me remind the House again
that the traditional practice in regard to
the salaries of Speakers of the House is
that they are drawn from the Consolidated Fund. The object of that is to
remove these officers from criticism, and
I think it is wise. Therefore, the course
that was pursued in moving the Address
to the King was that he would grant a
sufficient provision for these dignataries,
That was simply following tradition, and
could not, I think, be departed from.
Therefore, the criticism of my hon. Friend
in that respect does not seem to have been,
at any rate, very effective.
He himself,
I mean my hon. Friend for the North
Division, made it perfectly clear he intended to criticise every item in detail.
Again, I say, we welcome that, because
any item in these Estimates presented
here is open to criticism, and even open to
attack and defeat. Therefore, the suggestion that runs through-
Mr. COOTE: Not now.
Mr. POLLOCK Nót at this moment,
but when in Supply and the Estimates
are submitted in detail.
Mr. COOTE: Next year.
Mr. POLLOCK: I must join issue
with that. There will be an opportunity
long before next year, as my hon. Friend
suggests. The hon. Member for the
North Division seemed also to suggest that
it was possible to appoint all our own officials
here, and refuse to take those who were
allocated from Dublin. I wonder if he
would justify that? I wonder if he remembers that whether these gentlemen
were pensioned, or whether their pensions
were compounded, it would possibly require such a sum as would possibly stagger
my hon. Friend, the member for Tyrone.
and Fermanagh, and possibly would have
given ground for a larger and fiercer attack
than anything we have been subjected to
yet in regard to these matters.
Mr. COOTE: I do not propose you
should pension officials sent to you from
Dublin. I said you had that alternative
if you do not wish to retain their services.
Mr. POLLOCK: I fully agree. Well,
I may pass over individual criticism,
because that can be dealt with when I
touch the details on which I propose to
speak to this House. Now, in regard to
appointments; it may interest the House
to learn the sum total of all the appointments that have been made in the administrative and Executive Offices. The
number is 12. My hon. Friend, the
Member for Down, who spoke in such
picturesque language of hordes of parasites, will, perhaps, take that to heartthat already twelve Administrative and
Executive officers have been appointed.
I am not speaking of typists and such like.
By contrast with that series of appointments we have 27 trained and valuable
officials who have been loaned.
Now, those are the appointments which
have already been made, and which, I
suppose, have given ground for the
N
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Pollock.]
expressions we see in the Press of orgies
of extravagance. May I be permitted to
take the items in detail which my hon.
Friend, the Member for Tyrone and
Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) has proposed to
criticise, and deal with them categorically,
at any rate in regard to those to which
attention has been drawn in the Estimates. Speaking of the Clerks of
the House who were dealt with by the
right hon. the Prime Minister, is the
hon. Member aware that the panel, from
which these gentlemen can be drawn. is
so limited that it is Hobson's choice
almost whether you will get those who are
competent to perform their extremely
technical duties or otherwise. Clerkships of the House of Commons are
probably not a score in number, and in
order that you may start your new Parliament in conformity with the necessary procedure that must be adopted you must
draw your officers from that panel. And
do hon. Members suppose that gentlemen
in that position in the House of Commons
will come here to live in the North of
Ireland and bring their families with
them at a lower emolument than they
are receiving in the House of Commons?
But, as a matter of fact, we have gentlemen who came here, with one exception,
who were content to come at a slight
difference because of the different cost
which naturally obtained here. When
Members criticise those particular salaries
I hope they will bear that in mind,
because, as I say, it was not of our own
volition that we made the appointments
from this particular panel. It was
because we could not do otherwise and
could not get the men to come at any
lower salaries.
We are not accustomed to such figures in regard to
what may be regarded as clerkships of an
undoubtedly highly technical nature, men
who are highly trained, and also men of
legal minds.
Now, the question also arises. of
Counsel to the Speakers of the House of
Commons and the Senate at a salary of
£1,000, rising to £1,200 a year. That
appointment has not been made, but it is
a most important office. It must be filled
by a man of highly trained legal
experience who can investigate and
scrutinise and criticise Bills that are submitted to this House. Whether that
work can be accomplished by a gentleman
who will be paid fees, or whether a
permanent official will be appointed has
not yet been determined. Therefore, that
appointment at least does not yet come
within the orgy. I am rather concerned to remove a misapprehension with
regard to Black Rod. There seems to be
a common impression that our temporary
official Black Rod is in receipt of a salary
from this House. As a matter of fact, Sir
Frederick Moneypenny kindly undertook
to fill the position provisionally during the
first Session of this Parliament, and
refused to take a single penny by way of
fee or reward-(Hon. Members: "Hear,
hear") and that notwithstanding the
fact that it has been circulated through
the community that he has been employed
by this House at a considerable salary. I
again appeal to hon. Members, as far as
possible, by their speeches in this House,
and by their actions in the community,
to endeavour to restrain these false--I do
not want to use any more vigorous
adjective to restrain these false statements that are circulated through the
community in the way I have indicated.
Mr. COOTE: Why put Black Rod in
your Estimates then!
Mr. POLLOCK: Because it may be
necessary before the end of this financial
year to appoint a permanent official to
that office.
Mr. COOTE: How are the public to
know when you have not published it in
black and white in the Press. Is it fair
to Black Rod? We are exceedingly sorry
if we were misinformed or misinformed
ourselves, but it ought to have been made
plain.
Mr. POLLOCK: I must protest against
a charge of misleading. I wonder if the
hon. Member appreciates that it is necessary
to make provision on a Vote for possible
expenditure ahead?
Mr. COOTE: You ought to say so.
Mr. POLLOCK: The time has not been
reached to scrutinise each individual vote,
and it has not been asked for. Hon.
Members of the Imperial Parliament must
know that what I am saying is correct.
Such is never done there on a
Vote on
Account. I am dealing with these facts to
remove misapprehensions which, I am sorry
to say, are the basis of much of the criticism
of Members of this House. Now I come
to the question of the Assistant Secretaries
and Secretaries of permanent Departments.
They will be included in the heads, and
there are only twelve appointed. I wonder
if hon. Members realise the exceedingly
technical work that devolves upon the
Supply.
Supply.
23 SEPTEMBER 1921.
The
heads of these great Departments.
idea of calling a man a Secretary, I think,
is rather unfortunate for the man who
occupies that position. In the minds of
our people here, I have no doubt, that to
speak of a secretary of a Department implies a man who is simply doing what is
called secretarial work. As a matter of
fact the word that could be more properly
applied to that position is manager or
managing director. If, as an example, I
indicate the work of one of these gentlemen,
and take my own Department as that
example, I think the House will realise the
head of the Department of Finance is, to
say the least, no sinecure, and cannot be
undertaken by any except a
man of
immense technical and professional experience. He has not only to be responsible
for all Treasury work-matters dealing
with general finance that come before
this House, and which consist of a great
amount of detail-but also Civil Service
Commission, Inland Revenue, Charitable
Donations and Bequests Office, Registrar-
General's Office, Public Works Department,
General Valuation and Boundary Survey,
Stationery Office, Public Trustee, Irish Land
Commission, and Teachers' Pension Fund.
All these come under the purview of the
permanent head of the Finance Department, who is euphemistically called a
Secretary. I appeal to the House; where
are you going to get a man with the
highly technical experience necessary to
deal with all those varied subjects at less
than £1,000 a year.
Mr. LYNN: Who is objecting?
Mr. POLLOCK: It is part of the general
objection of this House, and the Assistant
Secretaries of these Departments have also
been criticised. In such a Department as
Home
the Ministry of
Affairs, can
any man act as managing director
of an institution embracing Pensions
Board, Registrar of Petty Sessions
Clerks, Home Office, Reformatory and
Industrial Schools, Office of Inspectors of
Lunatics, Local Government Board-which
includes Hospitals, Lunacy, Poor Law
Relief, School Children, Tuberculosis,
Grants to Local Boards, and Law Offices.
And yet in the paper this morning a list
is prepared of the salaries, and I believe
that in the same Press it was spoken of
as an orgy of extravagance. I submit to
the House that so far as the Department
is concerned and so far as I had anything
to do with these appointments, I cannot
say that there is any position in this list
that is extravagantly paid. I compare it
as a business man who has had a long
experience of business matters in large
concerns. Is there a business in Belfast
the managing director of which would
except £1,000 or £1,200 a year.
You could not get them. Is there a
public Board in Belfast the head of which
would accept £1,200 a year? Therefore,
I am afraid that Members have got into
their minds a totally wrong standard, if
I may say so, in speaking of those highly
difficult, onerous and responsible offices
which those gentlemen fill. My hon.
Friend, the Member for Tyrone and
Fermanagh, also stated in regard to the
salaries of secondary school teachers,
which are disgracefully low, I must admit,
that any one of these men could take any
one of the positions I have indicated.
Such a man, sir, would be a perfect baby
at such work.
Mr. COOTE: You took a Colonel from
the Army and paid him from £1,200 to
£1,500 a year.
Mr. POLLOCK : In regard to the Prime
Minister's Department, my hon. Friend
speaks in relation to the Secretary of the
Cabinet. Let me remind the House of the
responsibility of that office. The Prime
Minister has to be in constant touch with
the Imperial Government. The Secretary
is the avenue through which all communications from the Imperial Government passes,
and I do not think I am speaking extravagantly, when I say it is, if not the most
important, one of the most important.
offices in the whole Ministry.
It is a
position of a highly confidential nature,
it is held by one in whom thorough trust
is reposed, and whose duties are highly
important to the interests of the whole
community.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: That is exactly the
reason we object. The position is of such
a confidential nature that we think any
man who is not a member of the Cabinet
should not be introduced to it.
Mr. SPEAKER: I would remind the
hon. Member for North Belfast that we
cannot conduct debates if these interruptions
go on.
Mr. POLLOCK: I will now refer to
another apparently highly salaried office—
that of draughtsman, to which is attached
a salary of £1,200 a year. Here we have
a gentleman of legal training-he is a
barrister and of wide technical knowledge, and I am sure the legal gentlemen
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Pollock.]
in this House will appreciate what I say
when I suggest that the work devolving
upon that official is of such a character
that £1,200 a year seems to me an unusually low rate of remuneration for the
class of work undertaken.
Mr. ROBB: "Hear, hear."
Mr. POLLOCK: I am sure I have the
sympathy of the Members of the legal profession in that remark. The duties of this
gentleman include the examination of and
reporting on Public Bills introduced by
Private Members-in England this work
is done by a separate department of the
Treasury-the settlement of Provisional
Orders, etc. May I also point out that
the Parliamentary Draughtsman is also
intended to be solicitor to the Treasury.
We have brought together a number of
Departments under five heads, and I am
in hopes that by the extinction of some
departments and concentrating them
under other heads we will effect on the
whole such economy as will enable us to
pay the costs of
Parliamentary
machine without increasing the estimates
originally made or contemplated by the
Imperial Parliament.
Our
Speaking generally upon the subject
now before us, I can only repeat what
I said yesterday that in any appointments
that have been made, or in any appointments that have been outlined, I have not
been able to discover anything in the
nature of extravagance, because it is so
necessary in the establishment of these
departments to have men of highly
specialised training, men who can only be
brought here by giving them something
to look forward to, and some inducement.
We have kept before us that word
economy from A to Z, and from first to
last.
It is very unfortunate in my judgment that at the initial stages of our
Parliament and of our Government an
impression should get abroad such as has
been repeated by the hon. Member for
Antrim (Mr. Crawford) in regard to
extravagance and additional taxation, and
the idea suggested by one of my hon.
Friends that we would be throwing the
surplus away in chimerical schemes. All
that is conveying a very wrong impression
to our people in the Province, and I hope
that my words will reach a large
constituency when I say that so long as
I occupy this position here there is not a
post in the Ministry, for which I am responsible at least, where efficiency will not
be considered in the first place, and then
economy. I wish to say that efficiency
and economy are entirely the watchwords
of my Department.
Mr. DONALD: I merely rise to make
one or two observations with regard to
the speeches not only from the Government benches, but also from this side of
the House. As I listened to the speeches
yesterday I thought that really the
Government was as much responsible for
the criticism as the hon. Members of the
House. In my opinion it behoves the
Minister of any Department to outline
clearly what the position really is. The
more time he occupies in putting matters
plainly before the Members of this House
the less misunderstanding will there be.
I put a question yesterday to my hon.
Friend, the Parliamentary Secretary to
the Ministry of Finance, in regard to the
Civil Servants. You cannot expect
highly-paid officials in the Civil Service to
come from London or Dublin to reside in
Belfast without giving them an increase
in their salary. In fact, I have a friend
in a high position in the Civil Service, and
he said he would not come to Belfast for
£5,000 a year.
I clearly understand that
we will not get these officials to come here
where they cannot obtain a house, and
when their families are probably in
positions in other cities, without offering them an inducement. There is no
doubt about that. The point I wish to
make is this: had we been told as much
yesterday by the Ministers as we have
been told to-day, there would have been
no need for criticism. May I say that the
Ministers need not take too much to heart
the criticisms that are offered. Personally
I am going to hold myself free and unfettered to criticise any matter that comes
before this House, but I want the Minister
to understand that my criticism is not
directed to him personally. It is the duty
of every Member to watch carefully on
behalf of the ratepayers the expenditure
of every penny. We were returned to this
House for that purpose. I think that our
Ministers have taken yesterday's criticisms
to heart, but of course that is due to want
of experience. The criticism will not do
a bit of harm in the province, and I
know that as time goes on they will turn
a deaf ear to a good lot of it. That is
my experience of the other House.
In a
word, I think the whole matter rests with
the Ministers themselves. Let them give
the House a clear outline of what they
have been doing and of what their
Supply.
Adjournment.
23 SEPTEMBER 1921.
intentions are, and I am quite sure that
we are sensible enough to know if they
are doing the right thing. We have every
confidence in our Ministers. A good deal
of time has been wasted in criticism, but
as I have said, the responsibility rests as
much with the Ministers as with hon.
Members.
Question: "That this House doth agree
with the Committee in the said resolution,"
put and agreed to.
The remaining Orders were read and

MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
Captain DIXON: I beg to move
this House do now adjourn."
That
Mr. COOTE: Might I ask the Prime
Minister if he will outline the business for
next week.
The SPEAKER: I will put the question
for the adjournment first, and the hon.
Member can speak on any subject after that.
The question is: "That the House doth
now adjourn."
The PRIME MINISTER: I may say
that the Bills which are on the paper will
be taken, I hope, in the order in which
they appear on the paper. My anxiety at
the present moment is that we may keep
the House sitting for a certain time with
a view to being able to convey to Members
at the earliest possible time
developments that
arise, and
it might be convenient for me
to say to the House that I feel so confident
with regard to the necessity of this House
sitting in the event of any unforeseen
emergency, that when we have completed
our business, I propose only to move that
may
any
the House do adjourn until the end of
November, with a special provision that
should Mr. Speaker, on the advice of the
Cabinet, consider that any emergency such
as is contemplated in Orders of this kind
has arisen, he will immediately call
Parliament together on the notice of Mr.
Speaker. 1 think that will be for the
convenience not only of this House but of
the general public outside.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: Might I
suggest to the Leader of the House the
advisability of leaving Monday out and
letting the House meet on Tuesday. It
might suit the convenience of Members if
that could possibly be arranged, and I
throw out the suggestion accordingly.
Mr. SPEAKER: That is really a point
of order. I think it is impossible now.
If the House had wished to avoid sitting on
Monday a resolution to that effect should
have been moved to-day. I am afraid it
is too late now. In the Standing Orders
the House must meet next Monday..
The PRIME MINISTER: That could
be got round in this way.
If the general
feeling of the House is not to sit on
Monday, we could have a formal meeting
on that day and adjourn at once by securing a quorum. Country Members might
understand that the House will not raise
any question in their absence. If that be
the general feeling of the Committee we
will carry it out.
Hon. MEMBERS: Agreed, agreed.
Question: "That this House do now
adjourn," put and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty-five minutes after
Four of the clock, until
Monday, 26th September.
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Monday, 26th September, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
ORAL ANSWERS TO QUESTIONS
PRESERVATION OF PEACE.
1. Mr. LYNN asked the Minister of
Home Affairs whether he is aware that
during the last two months large quantities of arms and ammunition have been
brought into Northern Ireland by Sinn
Feiners; whether he is aware that the
Sinn Feiners are constantly engaged
in drilling; and whether he can state if
the Government will approach the Imperial Government, and bring these facts
to their notice, with the object of reestablishing law and order in Northern
Ireland?
The PRIME MINISTER: With
my hon. Friend's permission I will take
the answering of this question myself.
According to information received by this
Government from trustworthy sources,
the reply to the first two parts of the
question is in the affirmative. My
Government have again had under review
the existing state of affairs, and at a
conference of the military, the police, the
Lord Mayor of Belfast, and myself this
morning it was decided to re-mobilise the
Special Constabulary in certain parts of
Northern Ireland. It must be understood that in such areas as are under
military control the specials will be under
the military for all tactical purposes. I
appeal to law-abiding citizens to assist
the military and police authorities in the
arduous and dangerous duty of restoring
and maintaining law and order and in
protecting life and property. In the
city the Commissioner is arranging to
take the necessary immediate steps. If
further military are required they will be
available.
PUBLIC BOARDS IN NORTHERN
IRELAND.
2. Mr. LYNN asked the Minister of
Home Affairs whether his attention has
Deen drawn to resolutions passed by
several Public Boards in Northern Ireland
declaring their determination to resist the
decrees of the Northern Parliament, and
whether he proposes to take any action in
regard to them?
The MINISTER of HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): My attention has
been drawn to the resolutions referred to.
The local bodies in question are not under
the control of the Government of Northern
Ireland. If, however, they persist in this
attitude when we are responsible for
Local Government in Northern Ireland,
this Government will not hesitate to take
all steps necessary to enforce compliance
with the law, and to ask this Parliament
for any further powers that may be
required. I hope, however, that such a
course may not be found to be necessary.
STATE GRANTS FOR LOCAL
SERVICES.
3. Mr. LYNN (for Mr. CRAWFORD)
asked the Minister of Home Affairs
whether he is in a position to
state the total amount of State
Grants at present paid in aid of
Local Services in Northern Ireland, and
whether he can give an assurance that
these grants will be continued after the
transfer of services to the Northern
Government?
Sir DAWSON BATES: The total
amount of State Grants at present paid
in aid of Local Services, which will come
under the jurisdiction of the Ministry of
Home Affairs in Northern Ireland, is
estimated to be between £400,000 and
£450,000. The local authorities in
Northern Ireland may rest assured that
they will receive no less favourable
treatment than they would have
received if the Government of Ireland
Act had not been passed.
4. Sir
ROADS BOARDS.
TRANSFER OF DUTIES.
CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH
asked the Minister of Home Affairs
whether a Roads Board will be formed
under the Ministry of Home Affairs
(similar to that existing under the
British Board of Trade prior to the formation of the Ministry of Transport),
representative of Local Government
Bodies and Road Transport Interests as
an Advisory Board on Road Policy,
Motor Taxation, and the Disbursement of
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
26 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Funds received from Motor Licence
Puties to Local Government Authorities
for Road Maintenance, and, if so, when
does he propose to constitute it?
Sir DAWSON BATES: Section 22 of
the Ministry of Transport Act, 1919,
imposed a duty on the Minister of Transport to appoint a Roads Advisory Committee, comprising representatives of
Road Authorities and other interests concerned. The duty in question will in due
course be transferred to the Minister of
Home Affairs. Pending the making of
the adaptation order and the formal
transfer of powers and duties, I am
taking such steps as are possible in connection with the constitution of such a
Committee for Northern Ireland.
MOTOR LICENCE DUTIES (ROAD MAIN-
TENANCE).
5. Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH
asked the Minister of Home Affairs if he
is in a position to state what amount, if
any, has been disbursed by the Ministry
of Transport to Local Governing
Authorities in the Northern area for road
maintenance during the present year
from Motor Licence Duties collected
within the Six Counties, and the amounts,
if any, paid to each County Council?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I have communicated with the Ministry of Transport, and am informed that no moneys
from motor licence duties collected this
year within the Six Counties area have
yet been disbursed. Up to the present
the Ministry of Transport have received
proposals from Down County only, and
are awaiting proposals from other road
authorities. I trust that these bodies
will put forward their proposals without
delay, and they may rest assured that
nothing will be left undone to expedite
the distribution of the grants.
Sir CRAWFORD M'CULLAGH :
Arising out of that I would like to ask
is the right hon. Gentleman aware that
a sum of over £45,000 has been
collected in the city of Belfast alone and
lodged in the bank?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I cannot
answer that.
UNDERTAKING FROM MINISTRY OF
TRANSPORT.
6. Sir CRAWFORD M'CULLAGH
asked the Minister of Home Affairs if he
has received an undertaking from the
Ministry of Transport or the Imperial
Treasury that all the money paid in respect
of Motor Licence Duties as from 1st
January last within the Six Counties
will be handed over to the Northern
Government for road maintenance?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am happy to
be able to inform the hon. Member that
I had already arranged with the Minister
of Transport that the proceeds of the
Motor Licence Duties collected in
Northern Ireland would be specially earmarked for that area, and that, subject
to certain small deductions to cover expenses of collection and refunds of tax,
the amount collected will be available for
distribution to road authorities in this
area, as soon as their respective schemes
are formulated and approved.
I may
also say that, pending the transfer to my
department, the Ministry of Transport
have agreed to consult me in regard to
the distribution of the money, and I trust
that the authorities will expedite their
schemes and submit them to the Ministry
of Transport.
TYRONE ASYLUM COMMITTEE.
7. Mr. LYNN (for Mr. COOPER)
asked the Minister of Home Affairs if he
is aware that the Unionist ratepayers of
Fermanagh and Tyrone pay over 75 per
cent. of the rates; and if he is aware that
at the last meeting of Fermanagh and
Tyrone Asylum Committee all Unionist
tenders were ruled out and higher tenders
accepted, and whether the Government
will take steps to have the contracts
entered into declared illegal, and to have
Archdeacon Tierney and the others who
ruled out the tenders surcharged for the
loss to the ratepayers?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I understand
that Unionist ratepayers in the counties
referred to pay a substantial proportion
of the rates in these counties. I have
seen a report of the proceedings of the
Asylum Committee in question. However, as the hon. Member is aware, as the
question refers to a matter of administration, which has not yet been transferred
to the Government of Northern Ireland,
it is one that should properly be addressed
to the responsible Minister of the Imperial Parliament, and not to me.
MINISTRY OF LABOUR.
8. Mr. LYNN asked the Minister of
Labour whether he is in the position to
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
state the number of officials employed by
the Ministry of Labour in Northern
Ireland, and whether he can give an
estimate of the amount of salaries for the
coming year?
The MINISTER of LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): The number of officials at
present employed by the Ministry of
Labour is 7. The expenditure which it is
estimated will fall on the Ministry during
the current financial year is shown in the
Estimates circulated on Thursday.
Mr. DONALD: Arising out of that
question, may I ask my right hon. Friend
if he will state the number of officials who
are on loan to his department at the
present time?
Mr. ANDREWS: I think I can best
answer that question in this way. I
have got at the present time a permanent
secretary, three other civil servants (all of
whom are lent to the Ministry by the
Government of the United Kingdom) a
temporary officer, a shorthand typist, and
a messenger.
Mr. DONALD: Thank you.
OFFICIALS APPOINTED BY
GOVERNMENT.
9. Mr. LYNN asked the Prime
Minister whether he can give the names
of the officials who have been appointed
by the Government of Northern Ireland,
and also the salaries proposed to be paid
in each case?
The PRIME MINISTER: The
officials appointed by the Government of
Northern Ireland are:-
Mr. James Huggett, C.B.E., Comptroller and Auditor-General.
Lieut.-Colonel W. B. Spender, C.B.E.,
D.S.O., M.C., Secretary to the Cabinet.
Mr. A. I. Dasent, Clerk to the Houses
of Parliament.
Colonel-Commandant H. G. Young,
Serjeant-at-Arms.
Mr. C. Litchfield, Permanent Secretary
to the Ministry of Commerce.
Mr. C. A. C. J. Hendriks, Private
Secretary to the Ministry of Education.
Mr. G. O. Young, Private Secretary to
the Speaker.
Major G. Thomson, Librarian to the
Houses of Parliament.
Mr. Gerald Browne, Editor of Debates.
Mr. A. Clarke, Assistant Editor of
Debates.
Mr. S. J. Blackburn, Parliamentary
Reporter.
Mr. T. Wilson,
Reporter.
Parliamentary
and a small number of temporary clerks
(ex-Service men), typists, and messengers.
The salaries proposed to be paid were
shown in the Estimates presented on the
22nd instant.
PRIVATE BILLS.
10. Mr. DONALD (for Sir ROBERT
ANDERSON) asked the Prime
Minister whether in view of the desire of
some authorities to promote Private Bills
in this Parliament, early steps will be
to frame and adopt Standing
Orders governing Private Bill procedure?
The PRIME MINISTER: In order to
save the promoters of Private Bills time
and money, arrangements are being made
for a joint procedure both as regards the
Senate and Commons. As soon as these
arrangements are completed, the earliest
date on which the interested parties can
deposit their petitions for Private Bills
will be announced.
EXCHEQUER (TEMPORARY
BORROWING)-COMMITTEE THEREUPON.
Resolved: "That this House will tomorrow resolve itself into a Committee to
consider the charge upon the Consolidated
Fund in respect of certain sums borrowed
by the Minister of Finance, and placed to
the credit of the Exchequer (Lord Lieutenant's Recommendation signified).".
[Captain Dixon.]
EXCHEQUER AND AUDIT
(MONEY)-COMMITTEE THEREUPON.
Resolved: "That this House will tomorrow resolve itself into a Committee to
consider the charge upon the Consolidated
Fund in respect of the salary of the Comptroller and Auditor-General, and upon
moneys provided by Parliament in respect
of the salaries of his staff (Lord Lieutenant's Recommendation signified)."—
[Captain Dixon.]

Orders of the Day. 27 SEPTEMBER 1921.
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
Supply
Thursday.
Vays
Committee.
Deferred till
W and Means Committee. Deferred
till Thursday.
Exchequer and Audit Bill.

Second

Borrowing Bill.
Borrowing Bill.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Tuesday, 27th September, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Exchequer [Temporary
Borrowing].
Considered in Committee.
[Mr. MOLES in the Chair.]
Resolved: "That it is expedient that
there be charged upon the Consolidated
Fund, or the growing produce thereof,
the principal sum of One Hundred and
Sixty Thousand Pounds, and any interest
due thereon in respect of sums borrowed
from time to time by the Minister of
Finance, and placed to the credit of the
Exchequer under any Act of the present
Session."[The Minister of Finance
(Mr. Pollock)].
Ordered: "That the resolution be reported forthwith without amendment.”
Resolution reported.
Question: "That the House do agree
with the Committee in the said resolution," put, and agreed to.
Exchequer [Temporary
Borrowing] Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Motion made and Question proposed:
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): Mr. SPEAKER, the purport of
the Bill of which it is my duty to move
the Second Reading is intended really to
authorise and legalise the borrowing
which has already taken place in order
to provide for the various services of the
Province from 7th June until the end of
the financial year, and for a further
period. In order to understand the necessity for the borrowing, we must go back
to the circumstances which were contemplated by the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920. The basis of our scheme of
finance under the Act is that the whole
revenue of Northern Ireland, whether
transferred or reserved, should be sufficient to meet, in the first place, the whole
expenditure of Northern Ireland, whether
transferred or reserved; secondly, to
provide the contribution to the Imperial
services; and thirdly, to make provision
for whatever schemes of development our
Parliament may think it wise to undertake out of surplus revenue. On the expenditure side of the account it was
realised that some time would be necessary for legislative machinery to get into.
working order, as it were. Therefore,
under the Government of Ireland Act it
was provided that for a period of three
months from the first meeting of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland it should
be lawful for the Joint Exchequer Board
to authorise the Lord Lieutenant to make
such payments from the Exchequer as
might be necessary in order to provide for
bringing the Act into operation. It was
realised also that the transferred revenues
would not be sufficient to meet current
expenditure of the Province, amounting,
as it did, to a little over one million
pounds per annum, and therefore it would
be necessary for the Imperial Treasury to
supplement the revenues collected by the
Northern Province by advancing sums on
account of revenue collected by the Imperial authority itself in the North of
Ireland. In effect, however, the whole
machinery of the Bill had broken down,
because of political conditions which I
need not enter into, and which, as the
House is aware, prevented the transference of the various services. Therefore
we had to depend upon borrowing, which
was undertaken, and with which I dealt
with some fullness, in the financial statement which I made on the adjournment.
The purpose of Clause No. 1 of the Bill
is to obtain the authority of this Parliament for the borrowing on behalf of the
Northern Government, for which at the
time it was undertaken there was no legal
power, but the liabilities had, in fact,
been incurred before the date on which
the borrowing took place-that is to say,
on the King's visit. There were certain
sums expended in the reception of the
King and also the meeting of the House.
At any rate, it was not until the month
of July that it began to be apparent that
money would need to be borrowed.
total amount borrowed is £160,000.
first sum borrowed was an item of £20,000
on a provisional estimate of expenditure
which was then made up and which might
possibly have to be incurred during the
first two or three months of the existence
of our Parliament. It was in the middle
of August that we began to realise from
some ominous signs that we were not
going to see the transference of the
The
The
27 SEPTEMBER 1921.
Borrowing Bill.

Borrowing Bill.
claim was made by the Imperial Government for interest in respect of these
advances, but we had to undertake that
whatever bank interest would accrue upon
the deposits which we made in the
Exchequer Bank, would be credited to the
Imperial Treasury. Those are the facts
in connection with this Bill, and I do not
think it is necessary to amplify the matter
any further, because I think that the
House is fully aware of the circumstances
under which the borrowing took place. I
therefore beg to move that the Bill be
read a Second time.
various services that we had fully expected to have taken place even at an
earlier date, not later than the 12th July,
and in order to make provision for further
liabilities which might accrue, application
was made to the Civil Contingencies Fund
for a further sum of £10,000. It was at
the end of the third month that we had
a forecast made of the expenditure which
would likely be required to carry the
Parliament and the Government to the
end of the financial year. Assuming
that the transfers of the Services were
made at that period, and that our real
and full functions of Government began
at the end of this financial year or the
beginning of the next, it was obviously
necessary that we should provide for the
expenditure of at least a month in
addition, because it could hardly be contemplated that any material amount of
revenue could be collected before a month
had passed over our heads. We then
made application for the sum of £130,000,
bringing the total borrowing from the
Civil Contingencies Fund to a
sum of
£160,000. The Estimates that are now
presented amount to £152,000, the sums
being almost comparable. The estimate
and the borrowing, I have explained, include one month's additional expenditure, but we have also included in the
Vote on Account that we asked, the
provision of £50,000 for unforeseen
contingencies. I do not think that the
House will regard that as an unwise provision, in view of the somewhat difficult
and uncertain conditions under which we
find ourselves at the present time. The
purpose of Sub-Section 2 of Clause I. of
the Bill-and I will not detain the House
more than a minute on this matter-is to
authorise re-payment out of the Consolidated Fund to which receipts on
account of revenue will be accruing. That
Sub-Section reads:
:-
Such moneys shall be repaid, with any
interest due thereon, out of the growing
produce of the Consolidated Fund.
I may state to the House that before the
Treasury would advance from the Civil
Contingencies Fund the money required
to carry us on, I, as Minister of Finance,
had to give an undertaking that the
money would be repaid out of the first
revenue collected by the Northern Parliament, because the theory of the existence.
of the Civil Contingencies Fund is that it
must always be kept up to strength, and
not permanently interfered with. No
Mr. LYNN: The statement of the
Minister of Finance shows the absolute
necessity that I emphasised on previous
occasions that there is for the most rigid
economy and care. When listening to
the statement of the Chancellor we see the
difficulty of the position we are in. We
know by looking around that the
Imperial Government is in an equally
difficult position. The revenue is falling.
There are difficulties in every way, and
therefore it is essential that this House
should exercise discretion, and not only
discretion, but that the pruning knife
should be applied in every possible
direction. It is very easy for the House
to say that the spending of an extra £100
or £500 is a matter of small importance.
I suggest that the spending of every
pound in this House is a matter of
importance. There is an old saying in
Ulster that if you take care of the
pence the pounds will take care of themselves. Well, now, I think it is essential
that we should take care of the pence,
and that we should take care that the
establishment of our Government is on
such a basis that we can enlarge if
necessary. I am not here to say that we
can lay down a scheme that will be suitable for five, ten, or twenty years hence.
It may be-
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member is trespassing beyond the scope
of this Bill. All the Bill does if it is
passed is to give sanction to the House to
the borrowing of this money. I do not
think the hon. Member is entitled to go
into the method in which that money is
to be spent. We discussed that the other
day.
I
Mr. LYNN: I bow to your ruling. It
will be possible to go into the details later,
and into the details we mean to go.
merely want to emphasise how necessary
it is that we should exercise economy.
215 Exchequer and Audit Bill. HOUSE OF COMMONS Exchequer and Audit Bill. 216
[Mr. Lynn.]
Question put, and agreed to.
Bill read a Second time, and committed
to a Committee of the whole House for
to-morrow.
Exchequer and Audit [Money].
Considered in Committee.
[Mr. MOLES in the Chair.]
Resolved: "That it is expedient that
there be charged upon the Consolidated
Fund, or the growing produce thereof,
the sum of One Thousand Three Hundred
and Fifty Pounds per annum, in respect
of the Comptroller and Auditor-General,
and upon moneys provided by Parliament
under any Act of the present Session, such
sum as may be hereafter determined by
the Ministry of Finance, in respect of the
salaries of such officers and servants as
may be appointed by the Comptroller and
Auditor-General with the sanction of that
Ministry." [Mr. Pollock.]
Ordered: " That the Resolution be reported forthwith without amendment."
Resolution reported.
Question: "That the House do agree
with the Committee in the said resolution," put, and agreed to.
Exchequer and Audit Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Motion made and Question proposed:
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. POLLOCK: The Bill which I propose now to move the Second Reading of is
somewhat more formidable than the Borrowing Bill with which I dealt a few
minutes ago.
As stated on the front
page of the Bill, it is really a Measure.
To amend the Law applicable to the Exchequer and Consolidated Fund of Northern
Ireland, and to make provision with respect
to the Audit of the Accounts of that Fund,
the receipt, custody and issue of public
moneys, and the powers, duties, tenure of
office and salary of the Comptroller and
Auditor-General for Northern Ireland.
As I say, this Bill is a little more comprehensive than anything we have yet submitted. However, it does not sound
exactly very inspiring, but nevertheless it
provides the foundation upon which the
whole framework and superstructure of
our complete financial system rests. It
may seem to hon. Members very complex
and technical, and apparently somewhat
cumbrous in its machinery.
Notwithstanding that fact it can be confidently
stated that the methods of dealing with
revenue and expenditure provided in this
Bill furnish a safeguard which guarantee
the control of Parliament over the most
minute items in connection with our
whole administrative system.
In moving the Second Reading I do not
think it necessary to discuss the Bill
clause by clause. In fact, this would be
opposed to all the canons of Parliamentary Procedure on the Second Reading of
Measures. When the Measure reaches
Committee stage hon. Members will have
full opportunity to discuss and criticise, if
necessary, line by line, and full explanation can then be offered of every detail
of the Measure, and anything
that may seem obscure or require
inore explicit statement. As the House
will notice the Measure embodies the
financial proposals and the powers and
authority provided under section 20 of
the Government of Ireland Act with the
necessary modifications and additions to
make it applicable to our own present
necessities. I think perhaps it will be
informative if I recite the clause of the
Government of Ireland Act upon which
our
own Measure is based, as it will
enable the House to grasp fully the
nature of the foundation upon which this
Bill, which I am now presenting, is based.
Clause 20 provides that
(1) There shall be an Exchequer and Consolidated Fund of Southern Ireland, and an
Exchequer and Consolidated Fund of Northern
Ireland, separate from one another and from
those of the United Kingdom.
or
(2) All sums paid into the Exchequer of
Southern Ireland and the Exchequer of
Northern Ireland shall form the Consolidated
Fund of Southern Ireland and the Consolidated Fund of Northern Ireland respectively,
and subject to the provisions of any Act of
the Parliament of Southern Ireland
Northern Ireland or this Act or any other
Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom
charging any sums on any such Consolidated
Fund, all such sums shall be appropriated to
the public service of Southern Ireland or
Northern Ireland as the case may be
by the Act of Parliament of Southern Ireland
or Northern Ireland, and shall not be applied
for any purpose for which they are not eo
appropriated,"
(3) Save as may be otherwise provided by
Act of the Parliament of Southern Ireland
or Northern Ireland, the existing law relating
to the Exchequer and Consolidated Fund of
the United Kingdom shall apply with the
necessary modifications to the Exchequer and
Consolidated Fund of Southern Ireland and
Northern Ireland, and an officer shall be
appointed by the Lord Lieutenant to be Comp-
217 Exchequer and Audit Bill. 27 SEPTEMBER 1921. Exchequer and Audit Bill. 218
troller and Auditor-General for Southern
Ireland and Northern Ireland respectively.
(4) Save as may be otherwise provided by Act
of the Parliament of Southern Ireland or
Northern Ireland, the Accounts of the Consolidated Fund of Southern Ireland and
Northern Ireland respectively, shall be
audited as appropriation accounts, in manner
provided by the Exchequer and Audit Departments Act, 1866, and any Act amending the
same, by or under the direction of the appropriate Comptroller and Auditor-General.
Clauses 7 to 9 of the Bill provide that
special directions are to be given to the
Ministry of Finance for the keeping and
management of a banking account relating to the Government funds. The additions and improvements in the Bill, to
which I have referred, are based upon the
Exchequer and Audit Acts of 1866, and
also on the recent and perhaps more important Act passed so lately as the month
of August of this year. This Act was
passed since our own Government of
Ireland Act, and, therefore, it was not
made applicable to Ireland. In order that
we might take advantage of the necessary
improvements that were gained by the experience of recent years, we incorporated
those parts of the Exchequer and Audit
Act, 1921, in this Measure which is now
before the House. The object of the more
recent Act to which I have referred, as
distinguished from the Exchequer and
Audit Act of 1866, was to provide for a
closer scrutiny and a greater and closer
exercise of Parliamentary authority over
every item of expenditure.
Members: "Hear, Hear.")
(Hon.
Clauses 10 to 18 of the Act have as their
outstanding features the preparation by
each Department of an annual account of
the Parliamentary grants entrusted to it,
showing on one side of the account the
sums that are appropriated by this
Parliament for the various Services,
while on the other side will appear the
actual payments that have been made by
the Department, and also the examination
of the accounts by the Comptroller and
Auditor-General, for the purpose of
ascertaining that no payments have been
made without Parliamentary authority.
a
The third point is that it provides for
scrutiny by the Comptroller and
Auditor-General not only of the expenditure, but also of the balances that may
be left on hand after the expenditure is
met out of the transferred moneys.
The fourth point is the annual laying
of the Certified Accounts and the Report
of the Comptroller and Auditor-General
on the Table of the House of Commons.
So that the whole work of the administrative Departments is summarised by the
Comptroller and Auditor-General, and is
submitted to this House, either through
the machinery of a Public Accounts Committee, which may or may not be set up,
or directly to the House itself.
In some of these Clauses special directions are given to the Ministry of Finance
for the keeping and management of the
banking accounts relating to Government
funds. This is a most important feature
of the Bill, which will, I think, be of
advantage, though I enter into very little
detail as to the special arrangements which
have been made in regard to this particular
branch of the work. I would remind the
House that it is a common practice to use
one bank through which all payments
issue. This involves the establishment of
a special Department, known as the Paymaster-General's Department.
We have decided to dispense with that
highly-paid functionary and his staff.
(Hon. Members: "Hear, hear.") I am
glad that that economic feature appeals
to my hon. Friends opposite. I hope they
will realise that we are not altogether devoid of a sense of the necessity for
economy-
Mr. LYNN: Not quite.
Mr. POLLOCK : I repeat that we
have decided to dispense with the office
of Paymaster-General and his staff, and
to permit each Ministry to make the disbursements for its own services from a
banking account of its own, kept in the
name of the chief officer of the Department. To enable the Departments to
meet these payments transfers of money
will be made from the Treasury to the
different banks on the certificates of their
requirements, and those certificates will
be issued by the principal officers of the
Departments. From the best calculation
that I can make there should be a saving
effected, of from £1,500 to £2,000 a year,
without any loss of efficiency whatever, in
fact, I think the advantages far overweigh
any criticism that might be made by
of the change from traditional
practice. The Treasury and the Comptroller and Auditor-General will naturally
exercise effective scrutiny over the whole
of this system.
reason
Clauses 10 to 18 set up a system of
appropriation audit. I have mentioned
some of the outstanding features of those
clauses, and I need not, therefore, go over
them. but the House will notice the
219 Exchequer and Audit Bill. HOUSE OF COMMONS Exchequer and Audit Bill. 220
[Mr. Pollock.]
frequent reference which is made in the
Bill from first to last to the duties and the
powers and the responsibilities of the
Comptroller and Auditor-General—such
reference as, I think, must satisfy the
House of the importance of the functions
to be discharged by this official. He
derives his authority from Parliament
direct, he is responsible to Parliament
alone, he is independent of all Departmental authority, and he is bound to report to Parliament anything which in his
judgment conflicts with the appropriations that have been made by this House
for the requirements of the public
services.
There is no Department which is free
from his supervision. His auditorship is
a guarantee that the financial mandate of
Parliament is exercised and executed in
every detail, and in order to ensure his
independence his salary is made a charge
upon the Consolidated Fund; therefore,
it does not appear in the Estimates. The
House will notice that provision is made
in the Bill for the payment of salary to
the Comptroller and Auditor-General,
and this, I think, is the last important
matter in the Bill which requires any
elaboration from me to-day. I repeat
that full opportunity will be given in
Committee for a close criticism of all the
clauses of the Bill, and therefore I will
content myself with simply moving the
Second Reading.
Mr. ROBB: In moving the Second
Reading the Minister of Finance made
use of this expression: That the accounts
will eventually be laid before this House,
examined by the Auditor-General, and
possibly examined by a Public Accounts
Committee, which, in his words, may or
may not be set up. I would like to have
an explanation from the right hon.
Gentleman as to what he means by that,
because according to the Standing Orders
of this House-Standing Order No. 70it is laid down
Thero shall be a Standing Committee, to
be designated the Committee of Public
Accounts, for the examination of the
Accounts, showing the appropriation of the
sums granted by Parliament to meet the
public expenditure, to consist of five members,
who shall be nominated at the commencement of every Session, and of whom three
shall be a quorum.
Perhaps the Minister of Finance will
tell us what he meant by saying that
these accounts will be examined by a
Public Accounts Committee, which may
may not be set up, the Standing
or
Orders having decreed that it shall be set
up.
Mr. POLLOCK: There was no special
significance at all in my remarks. I was
speaking on that point extemporaneously,
and I had not in my mind at the moment
the Standing Order to which my hon.
Friend refers. It is quite obvious that
unless and until the Standing Order is
changed it will be necessary to set up a
Standing Committee for the examination
of accounts.
Mr. LYNN: I have criticised some
points of the Chancellor's policy, and you
must now allow me to say that I
thoroughly agree with this Bill.
It is a
Bill which does credit not only to the
Chancellor, but to every one who has
helped to draft it. There is only one
point that I would direct the Chancellor's
attention to and that is the salary that is
being paid to the Comptroller and
Auditor-General. As he has quite rightly
pointed out to the House, the Comptroller
and Auditor-General will exercise, next
to the Minister himself, the most important of the functions which have to be
discharged. His business will be to see
that all money voted by this House is
properly applied, and it rests very largely
on his judgment whether the money hat
is voted by this House is properly spent.
I cannot think of any other servant of
this State who will discharge more important functions, and I think I may cɔngratulate the Chancellor on the fact that
he has got one of the finest civil servants
to look after the Department that he
possibly could have got in the whole of
the United Kingdom. I was going to
refer him to one point, however; I think
it is in Clause 28, and it refers to the
salary that is to be paid to the Comptroller
and Auditor-General. It is a sum of
£1,350. I would like the Minister of
Finance to compare this sum with the
other payments that are being made; and
although I am an economist I say that if
you are paying a sufficient sum to the
Comptroller and Auditor-General, then
you are paying much too large a sum in
some of the other Departments.
Mr. COOTE: The matter to which I
would like to draw attention is really a
committee matter. It arises on Clause
which says:
28,
The Comptroller and Auditor-General may
appoint such officers and servants as he may,
with the sanction of the Ministry of Finance,
determine, and there shall be paid to those
officers and servants, out of money provided
by Parliament, such salaries as that Ministry
may determine.
221 Superannuation Bill. 27 SEPTEMBER 1921.

Superannuation Bill.
I should like to have the Chancellor's definition of part of this Clause, and that
is as to whether it will be a matter for the
Ministry, or the Cabinet known as the
Ministry, or for the whole House to determine what are to be the salaries of the
Department of the Comptroller and
Auditor-General. I understand the
Clause provides that we shall vote the
money, but I do not know whether we
shall apportion the money so voted, or
will it be apportioned by the Department itself and the House have nothing
to do with the apportionment? If so, I
should like to put down an amendment in
Committee.
Question put, and agreed to.
Bill read
a
Second time and committed to a Committee of the whole House
for to-morrow.
SUPERANNUATION BILL.
Order for Second Reading read.
Motion made and Question proposed:
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
The FINANCIAL SECRETARY
TO THE MINISTRY OF FINANCE
(Mr. Barbour): I rise to move the
Second
what
Reading of
will b
known as the Superannuation Bill.
need hardly remind hon. Members of this
House that under the Government of
Ireland Act our responsibilities to those
officers of the Civil Service of the United
Kingdom who either have been or will be
transferred to the service of the Northern
Parliament, are pretty well laid down,
and the interests of those officers of the
Civil Service have been, I think. adequately protected. But in creating our various
departments here, there are many positions for which there has been in the past
no exact counterpart of the office which
it is now necessary to set up.
take, in order to fill those offices, officers
either from other departments in Ireland,
which are not being transferred to us, or
we may get officers from the Civil Service
of the United Kingdom, and get them to
undertake duty under us. For those two
classes of officials no provision has been
made in the Government of Ireland' Act,
and while I sympathise most heartily with
the desire with which the House is imbued, that every economy should be exercised in every direction, I do not think
We may
we should refrain from the spending of
money which is really in the interests of
justice and in order to create a feeling of
satisfaction and fair treatment in the
minds of every one of our public servants,
irrespective of the manner in which they
come into our employment. Consequently
you will understand that the whole spirit
of the transfer of these civil servants to
the Northern Parliament is that the
service to which they have devoted
their lives in the past, that the conditions under which they entered that
service should in no way be prejudiced by
the fact that the reins of Government
have passed from Westminster to Northern
Ireland. When I say
conditions of
service," it is not only such salaries as
they may draw at the present time, but
it also refers to those other benefits which
are coupled up with the salaries they receive. These are, of course, superannuation, and in the event of an officer not.
reaching that time when his superannuation would come, whether it is through
sickness or any other chance occurrence,
such as abolition of office, that he should
be in some way compensated or treated
as considerately as he probably would be
were he employed in ordinary commerce.

There are one or two departures in this
Bill, and I think even now it might be
right to draw the attention of hon. Mem-
One is that it is proposed,
bers to them.
while not creating any right of such
officers to claim consideration under this
Bill, it is proposed to render it permissible
that whole-time officers of the Parliament
of Northern Ireland should be entitled to
be regarded as pensionable officers. When
I say
"whole-time officials of the Parliament," I do not allude in any way to
Members of the Government.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Does it include charwomen?
Mr. BARBOUR: I think I may
answer that and similar questions by saying such officials as may be regarded as
being employed under similar conditions
and rendering similar services as officials
of the Civil Service; so that is really
amply provided for under that description. It is Clause 55 of the Government
of Ireland Act which really lays down
what we have to take over. That Clause
provides pretty well for the superannuation allowance which will be given to those
officers who are transferred along with the
transferred services. But we have to
remember when starting out on what I
Superannuation Bill.
Northern Judiciary.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
hope is the very long career which may
be in store for this Parliament-(hon.
Members. "Hear, hear")-in fact I hope
no limit may be put to it--we have to
make provision for those officers who may
come into our service in the future, and
so it is proposed in this Bill to adopt the
Superannuation Acts which have been in
existence in the United Kingdom, and
which govern to a great extent conditions
of service and superannuation which will
be enjoyed by those officers who have
either now or will in the near future be
transferred to us. The Acts, which
govern these services, extend over the
long period of from 1834 to 1914. They
have been amended from time to time, of
course. Civil officers who have been a
long time in the service will be affected
in varying degrees according to the date
of any Act which may have been passed
to modify conditions of service. In this
way we hope to cover the whole ground
of superannuation, namely, to the transferred service officials and to the specially
appointed officers, and also to those who
may enter our service in the future; and
it will be a matter for consideration in
Committee whether the clauses, as now
drawn, are well designed to meet the end
we have in view. In the meantime, I
beg to move the Second Reading of this
Bill.
Mr. LYNN: As the hon. Member has
pointed out, we are really under an
obligation; we are not masters, so to
speak, in our own house. The Government of Ireland Act of 1920 laid down
the lines on which we must pension the
officers who were taken over by us, and
really so far as this Bill deals with anything we have control over it simply deals
with those who are outside the Civil
Service and may be taken into the
employment of this Government.
I agree
with the Financial Secretary so far as
officials and the whole staff of the Parliament are concerned, that it is absolutely
right and proper to make such provision
as will, first of all, attract the best men,
and, secondly, keep the men we have in
our employment contented, because if you
have not a contented and loyal staff they
can lose you a great deal more than you
will ever spend in pensions. Therefore,
I think it is the wisest economy to pay
your staff well, and to make ample provision for pensions afterwards. I think
there is nothing in the Bill to which any
Member of the House can take exception.
We want to have a model staff, and we
want to have an
Thereefficient staff.
fore, I think the House will have no
hesitation in agreeing to the motion that
has been so ably moved by the Under-
Secretary for Finance.
Question put, and agreed to.
Bill read a Second time, and committed to a Committee of the whole
House for to-morrow.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
Motion made and Question put.
That this House do now adjourn.'
-[Captain Dixon].
THE NORTHERN JUDICIARY.
The PRIME MINISTER: I think it
only right to say to the House that if I
have to make any important announcement, I prefer to do it in the House
rather than through any other channel,
and as I have just received a telegram to
say that the Order in Council will be issued
fixing Saturday, 1st October, as the
Appointed Day for the complete control
of the Judiciary being handed over to
Northern Ireland, I think it is right that
I should announce that important fact
in the House, because there seems to
have been some uncertainty, not only in
the minds of the general public, but also
in the minds of the legal profession.
Consequently it may be taken that as and
from Saturday next the old system of
judiciary throughout Ireland is done away
with, and we in the North have our own
Courts.
The second important announcement I
have to make is that a most amicable
arrangement has been come to by the
Lord Chief Justice and the Antrim County
Council, co-operating with which I understand is also the City Corporation, who
have always met us in the most sympathetic way in everything that has to do
with the setting up of our new legislature.
The County Council of Antrim and the
City Corporation have met the Lord Chief
Justice, and the other Judges who have
approached them on the matter, in such at
spirit that I understand they can take
up the work on Saturday as though they
had been long established. (Hon. Members: "Hear, hear.") The centre of
the High Courts of Justice from Saturday
onwards will now be the County Antrim
Courthouse, which has been slightly
adapted to meet the special requirements.
Northern Judiciary.
Northern Judiciary.
27 SEPTEMBER, 1921.
[Prime Minister.]
I am quite sure that that announcement,
bald as it is and as brief as I can make it,
will give satisfaction to all. showing, as
it does, that one more step has been taken
in carrying out the Government of Ireland
Act.
Mr. DONALD: There is a matter
which I should like to oring before the
House, which I think will meet with the
approval of every Member of this House.
It is with reference to the British Empire
Exhibition to be held in 1923. I would
suggest that, with the approval of this
House, our Prime Minister should be
elected a Vice-President of that exhibition,
in view of the fact that all the Prime
Ministers of our Colonies occupy such
positions. In fact, this is an Imperial
matter, and I think Ulster at least should
have its Prime Minister represented at
that Imperial Exhibition in London. We
can send forward the name to the Secretary.
There is quite a number, including those
of the Prime Minister, Lord Milner, and
others.
Question: "That this House do now
adjourn," put, and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at twelve
minutes after Three of the clock.

of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.

ROWING) BILL.

Exchequer and Audit Bill.
Considered in Committee.

into Exchequer and rendering of daily
returns), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Fund in Bank Applicable to Exchequer
Issues), ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Accounts of Receipts into and Issues out
of Consolidated Fund), ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Credits for Services Charged
on Consolidated Fund), ordered to stand

The CHAIRMAN: There are a number of these Clauses that appear to be
non-controversial. To save the time of the
Committee I propose to take four or five
of them together.
CLAUSES 5 (Order of Lord Lieutenant
for Issue of Sums Granted for Expenses
of Specified Public Services), and 6
(Credits for Ways and Means Granted
to make good Supplies, and Issues to

to
PUBLIC BANKING ACCOUNTS.

of Accounts be deemed Public
Accounts), and 8 (Consolidation of Public
