throughout that period there will be an
average of 1,250,000 insured persons unemployed. On the income side, I should hope
to get in the year contributions from :-
Workpeople
Employers
The State
...
Millions.

15 食

That is a total income of just under 37
millions.
On the expenditure side I get:—
Benefits to be paid
Interest on debt
...
Millions.

Grants for administrative expenses 3
That is a total expenditure of about 503
millions."
In a word, in my judgment, if we
offered to the employers of Great Britain
and Ireland to-day 50 million pounds,
and said to them "Supply employment
for something like 5 million people," it
would solve the unemployment problem
very much better than by paying people
for doing nothing. As a Trades Unionist,
I stand here, and I tell this House that
this policy of money for nothing has been
the curse of our country. (Hon. Members: "Hear, hear.") I feel keenly on
this matter, and I want to help my right
hon. Friend in every possible way so that
when we get the reins of office properly
into our hands, when we take over these
services, the Ulster people may give a lead
in this matter.
signature on them and passes them on.
He is signing these sheets for Sinn.
Feiners who are drawing the taxpayers'
money, and then they go to the hills to
drill. These are the observations I want
to put forward, and I hope they will bear
fruit in the future.
Mr. LYNN: The Minister of Labour
in his concluding remarks asked for the
sympathy and support of the Members of
this House. He is not making that
appeal in vain, because there is not a
Member of this House who does not
realise the difficulties with which he is
confronted, and there is not a Member of
this House who is not as anxious as he
can possibly be to make his office a success.
And I say more. I that the honesty
say
and the ability which I know my right
hon. Friend will bring to the work is in
itself a guarantee that we will get a great
deal more satisfaction out of our Labour
Department, if we must have one here,
than we get out of the Labour Department on the other side of the Channel.
The figures that the right hon. Gentleman gave are undoubtedly grave, and in
reading, not as he suggested, the "Daily
Mail," but the Nationalist, Sinn Fein,
and Radical newspapers, I sometimes
come to the conclusion that if the right
hon. Gentleman and his colleagues had
not been appointed, and if this Parliament had not been established, there
would have been no such thing as un-
You would
employment in Ulster.
It is in the interests not
only of the workers but of the employers
that the money should be kept in the
country for another purpose than giving
it to people for nothing.
I
The other day a friend of mine told me
that he had been down at Carlingford.
What did he find there? He found Sinn
Feiners by the hundred drawing the unemployment dole, going out and catching
fish, bringing them in and selling them,
and making a good livelihood. Yet there.
is no check on this state of affairs.
want this Committee set up because this
Referees' Court has no jurisdiction whatever. What right has a local magistrate,
who has no knowledge either of unemployment or of how to find employment
for them, to sign documents by the
hundred ? My information is that the
chairman of the Court of Referees has a
pile of these benefit sheets to
be
scrutinised, and he merely puts his
the
imagine to read what appears in some of
these papers that "Partition" is the one
word that has brought about unemployment in the North of Ireland. We all
know that this question of unemployment
is not confined to the North of Ireland.
There is world-wide unemployment, and
while our difficulties are great, they are
just as great in other parts of
United Kingdom, and indeed in
other parts of the world, as they
are with us at home. We are, as
the right hon. Gentleman said, badly hit
with regard to shipbuilding, but I may
say that we are not nearly as badly hit in
shipbuilding as they are on the other side.
of the Channel.
We are also hit with regard to our linen
industry, but I know myself, as the right
hon. Gentleman has said, that the linen
industry is showing signs of
brightening up, and that we may
look forward to better times than we
271 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 272
4 OCTOBER 1921.
have had for at least the past twelve months.
I am not going to enter into the question
that he has raised that tariffs in America
may hamper us in future. That is a
debatable question. One might argue that
tariffs hit the people who impose the tariffs,
and vice-versa, but I do not intend to
argue that point at the present time.
I agree with my right hon. Friend that
really good service was rendered to the
people under abnormal conditions by the
Insurance Acts. We have reached a stage
in the development of the community when
it is impossible to allow people to starve,
when it is necessary for every one of us,
not only on humanitarian but also on
business grounds, to see that the men and
the women, and the boys and the girls, are
kept from want. Insurance, I think, on
the
whole is probably the best
way we have of dealing with
that question. I am quite sure that
when my right hon. Friend gets into the
saddle, and gets rid of those influences
which hover- round Dublin Castle, and
which seem to sting him and some of his
colleagues occasionally, he will see that the
Insurance Acts are administered with
greater care than, as my hon. Friend, the
Member for East Belfast (Mr. Donald) has
pointed out, is exercised at the present
time. We are all men of the world.
We
know the abuses that are going on in connection with these things, and we want as
far as possible to see those abuses removed.
I want to give the right hon. Gentleman
credit for what he has done in the way of
making peace. You would hardly think
sometimes that he is a man of peace, but
he really is. He certainly rendered
practical service with regard to an old strike
that had been going on for six or seven
months, and he has done a great deal of
good. But, if he is out to break a lance
in favour of the Labour Ministry of Great
Britain, may I point out to him that the
Labour Ministry in Great Britain has done
nothing to prevent strikes, and
that when strikes have come the
settlement of them has always
been taken I cut of the hands of the
Labour Department, and has been effected
by other Departments altogether. I hope,
therefore, that so far as running the Labour
Department is concerned, the one thing my
right hon. Friend will do will be to pay
no attention to the Labour Department in
Great Britain, which from the beginning to
the end has been a huge and unmitigated
failure. It has not been able, as he suggested, to negotiate, to conciliate, or to
arbitrate. It has failed in all three of
these, and I hope my right hon. Friend will
not follow it.

I am not going to say anything with
regard to Trade Boards, as he has promised
to set up a Committee. He may have
noticed, even in other papers beside the
Daily Mail," that Trade Boards are not
particularly popular at the present time,
either with employers or employed. They
have been doing harm. They have been
doing a great deal of harm.
Now, I come down to what
really brought me to my feet.
He referred to the question of
Labour Exchanges. The right hon.
Gentleman said I must have got my information from the "Daily Mail.” If it is of
interest to him, I may tell him that I do
not read the "Daily Mail."
to
Mr. ANDREWS: I am glad to hear it.
rate.
Mr. LYNN: When I want to get information on special subjects I always go to
headquarters. I take the official figures,
and I work them out as best as I can for
myself. I commend my right hon. Friend
do that with regard to the
will
Labour Exchanges, and if he
I can assure him he will find
my figures are not in the slightest inaccu-
He will find, if he takes the trouble
of going through the figures, that sometimes
the Labour Exchange counts the one person
who does a job ten or twelve times over.
It is the most extraordinary puzzle to go
through the figures. Let us get
rid of all that sort of thing.
The question arises : are Labour
Exchanges doing any good in the
way of getting rid of unemployment?
Personally, the only people that I know
who have received employment from the
Labour Exchanges are Government
officials. I do not think they
of the slightest benefit to
the community as a whole. A
great many people whom they claim to
have placed in employment have not been
placed through the agency of the Exchange
at all. I do not want to prejudice the mind
of the right hon. Gentleman. I think he
really wants to come to the consideration
of these problems with an open mind, with
a desire to do what is best, and to help in
making this Northern Ireland of ours a
model community. I would just say this
to him. Unemployment is widespread,
and it is widespread because of many conditions that we all know of. One of the
conditions that is aggravating unemploy
are
273 Debate on Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS Debate on Adjournment. 274
[Mr. Lynn.]
ment at the present time is the excessive
taxation that we all have to bear. A great
deal of that taxation was necessary because
of the war, a great deal of it is necessary
because of what shall we call it?-the
squandermania, if you like, of Ministers,
and one of the ways to hasten employment
is to reduce taxation. I think that is one
of the things that Ministers want to do, to
see that where it is possible to save even
the smallest amount they will save it.
I was
If I might offer-it is with some
temerity I do it-any advice to my right
hon. Friend, I would say to him, if he
wants get rid of unemployment he
must help those forces that make for and
create employment. You can never get
rid of unemployment by doles.
glad to hear my right hon. Friend
emphasising that point so much. Doles
will only carry you over an abnormal
period. You cannot go on spending
your money on people who are not producing, and we want to get a producing
community. I say to the right hon.
Gentleman that the real duty of a department like his is not to put a huge body of
officials over us, to watch us at every
turn, but to keep the channels clear and
let trade flow through those channels in
the natural way.
Mr. COOTE: I wish also to thank the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Labour, for his very lucid and informative speech, and to say that he has the
sympathy of every part of the House.
We expect of him that he will bring wo
bear on a very difficult task the splendid
energy and the splendid business ability
which he possesses, which he has developed in his own life, and that he will
also develop it in the life of this new
Parliament in the sphere to which he has
been called. I should like to point out to
him a few things which trouble us who
are looking on. We are not very much
concerned with those who are opposed to
us in regard to labour conditions in
Ulster, but we are very much concerned
as to future labour conditions in Ulster.
We have a people in Ulster who probably
are different from the people of any other
part of the United Kingdom.
speaking of the labouring classes..
have a very practical and very proud
people. They do not believe in doles;
they do not like doles; they like to do
some
I
am
We
honest work for honest money.
(Hon. Members: "Hear, hear.") If the
Minister would bear this in mind, in all
the work of his Committees, in trying to
adumbrate some permanent scheme that
will probably be handed down in the days
coming after as being that which emanated
from his Ministry, he would do a great
service to Ulster. Along these lines may
I point out some difficulties and some unfortunate circumstances in connec-
I was
tion with his Ministry. I inquired
about eighteen months or two
years ago as to the cost of running the
Ministry of Labour in Ireland-that is,
the cost of the officials and of the office
staff, the upkeep of the office work.
informed that it takes something like
£240,000 a year to pay for the upkeep of
the Labour Ministry in Ireland, anu that
in Dublin alone there are eight departments with the principal officers earning
salaries of about £1,000 a year each.
am not now speaking of the Comptroller
above these eight salaried officials. Had
I
I known that this matter would have been
mentioned to-day I would have brought
the official figures with me-1 am speaking
now from memory—but I am perfectly
well aware the answer I got was that it
takes about £240,000 a year to run the
Labour Ministry in Ireland. Now, I
think that is an appaling set of figures,
and that it ought to be cut down tremendously. So far as Ulster is concerned,
we ought to work out our labour offices
on strict economical lines, and obtain the
most efficient men we can get for those
offices. When everything is turned to
practical account we ought to be able to
cut down generally the cost of the Labour
Ministry. I think we have far too many
Boards. Really when we go into the
office of the Ministry of Labour we do not
know where we are. We go to one place,
and we are told it is some other place,
some other branch of the Ministry, and it
is bewildering, as well as tending to create
too many offices, too many officials, and
too many salaries. The right hon.
Gentleman also mentioned that the shipbuilding industry of Belfast is more or
less hit. I think the shipbuilding industry in Great Britain is worse hit.
it be known to the world that Belfast
stands better, has more ships to build and
to be repaired, than probably any other
set of shipyards in the three Kingdoms.
Let
There is another point I would like to
draw the right hon. Gentleman's attention
to. The Admiralty are about to issue
orders for the building of six capital ships.
Why should we not get the construction
of one of these for Belfast?
Debate on Adjournment. 4 OCTOBER 1921.
Debate on Adjournment. 276
be able to make such changes as will be
helpful not only to the working people of
Ulster, but to the taxpayers of the
country as well.
The PRIME MINISTER: Perhaps it
would interest my hon. Friend to know
that I understand Belfast has been asked
to tender for one of these ships. I wrote
to the First Lord urging that our men
here should be given permission to work
on one of these capital ships, and I got
information to say that it was hoped one
of the firms would be enabled to tender.
Mr. COOTE: We are delighted to hear
this from the Prime Minister, and we congratulate him on looking so very closely
after the interests of a great industry like
shipbuilding in Belfast. If we can have
one of these ships, the equivalent of six
or seven millions to spend in the next low
years, it would be a great boon to our
people.
May I also point out, not in a critical
mood, but in a friendly critical mood, if
I may so describe it, the great benefit you
would confer upon rural Ulster if you
were to try and set up a Roads Board here
in Ulster, get the power to collect the
cash, and to centralise a Roads Board
here, with the consent of the various
public bodies throughout the province.
The roads are in a deplorable condition
over the Six Counties. That is one of
the causes of unemployment and why food.
is so very expensive. The farmer cannot
get his produce sent to the central markets
in the province without such tremendous
cost as makes the commodity more than
double the original price. There are
many private industries and enterprises.
that would send their commodities to
market by motor if the roads were in a
better condition. We could organise over
the whole province a system that would
bring the food of the province to the seaboards at a much less cost than is involved at the present time in railway
transit. But we are confronted by the
fact that our roads are in such
a
lamentable condition that the project for
the time being is unworkable. My right
hon. Friend would confer a very great
boon upon rural Ulster if he would try
to expedite the passing of the powers from
the Local Government Board in Dublin
to the Local Government Board here in
Belfast. This is all the friendly criticism
I wish to offer my right hon. Friend. I
do feel strongly on the question of the
Roads Board, and I feel strongly on the
tremendous cost of the official side of the
Labour Ministry. I think that if my
right hon. Friend will bring his very keen
mind to bear upon these problems he will
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I question if
I would have risen this evening
it it had not been for the
purpose of complimenting my right hon.
Friend (Mr. Andrews) upon the very explicit
statement he has made with regard to his
Department. I, for one, will never have
any suspicion with regard to how that
Department will be controlled or administered while the present Minister presides
over it. We feel that he is hampered at
the moment for want of those powers that
as Minister of Labour he is entitled to, and
during the interval we feel that he should
not be subjected to any censorious or un

I am quite convinced in my own mind it
is not necessary. It would not be justified,
and I feel that all that can be done is being
done by the right hon. Gentleman at the
moment. He is entitled to our thanks for
the efforts he has made since he came into
office and his Department was set up, to
prevent and
to settle disputes.
If he had done nothing else
but settle the
and
carpenters'
joiners' strike he would have justified his
existence. We feel we have the utmost
confidence in the right hon. Gentleman.
I have always thought, as a Trades
Unionist that we proceeded the wrong way
about matters. We struck for conditions
that we did not fully understand. We set
forth charges and made demands that we
had not examined to see whether they were
reasonable or not, and then, forsooth,
having got ourselves into difficulty we
appealed for an arbitrator. It would be
far better to deliberate beforehand, and I
have always been one of those who believed
that the strikes that take place from time
to time in this country could be in a large
proportion of cases
if
prevented,
only there
friendly consideration between the employer
the employed. Whenever there is an
outbreak in respect to a labour demand,
and when thousands of men are thrown
out of employment, the hardships do not
fall upon the men, but upon the wives and
the helpless children. This should be taken
into consideration, and I am glad that the
Bolshevic element in England is being
awakened at the moment with regard to the
condition at which affairs have arrived, for

and

HOUSE OF COMMONS Debate on Adjournment.
[Mr. M'Guffin.]
they are responsible for bringing it about.
Who, now, having the opportunities to reflect upon the miners' strike, would for one
moment sanction the procedure that was
adopted? Not even those who led them
in the dispute would say, to-day, that they
took a wise course in the matter. When
we find one of the leaders going to South
Wales, regretting that better results did.
not attend the strike, and expressing the
opinion that it was a great mistake to have
gone into it we begin to realise that those
men who recklessly led the working people
of this country in the past are being forced
by the circumstance of the case to reflect.
Now, I believe that the right
hon. Gentleman will be
in negotiations. I think he has
the confidence not alone of the employers but of the employed, and that is
a unique position for any, gentleman to be
in who undertakes the work that he has
in hands. I know how the working people
feel with respect to him-they feel that in
him they have a friend. We are not asking
him to act partially with respect to us, or
to adopt the position of a partisan in regard
to our claims. We simply ask him to continue in his official position the spirit that
has characterised him throughout his life.
If he does that he can be assured of our
continued confidence.
(6

a
success
With regard to Trade Boards, I am not
one of those that condemn them, because
I feel of course the right hon. Gentleman
admitted it—that when they first came
into operation they were almost imperative. There were sweated conditions in
this country. I would prefer not to use
the word sweated if I could avoid it.
The conditions were not just exactly what
they should be, and I believe that when
the Trade Boards were instituted they
remedied grievances to a large extent.
Even the right hon. Gentleman admits
that while they increased wages they did
not inflict injustice upon the employers.
Why, then, should these Boards that
effected such an improvement, not continue to operate? I know that they are
not condemned by those that are connected
with them. Certain employers I know
that meet at these Trade Boards, say that
things can be arranged very well there,
and moreover, in their own interest, for
they get certain ground on which they
can stand. Whereas if there is not a
uniform wage paid with respect to any
industry, then it is within the province
of the Board to act.
With regard to Labour Exchanges, I
am absolutely against them, despite what
the hon. Gentleman has quoted. Whatever they may have done across the water
they certainly have not accomplished the
same thing here. I know that across the
water they are suspect, and the Labour
Party would do away with them immediately if they could. The report is universally and unanimously against them. But
in this country what do we find? I had
to draw the attention of Dr. Macnamara
a year ago to what was going on in the
Labour Exchange in Frederick Street. I
could use much stronger language about it
if I cared to do so. As a matter of fact it
was manned by Sinn Feiners, some of
whom had pistols, and offensive and
abusive language was used by them in
respect to certain applicants. I had to
draw Dr. Macnamara's attention to it.
He sent across a deputy to inquire into
it. He wrote to me more fully upon the
matter, and said that if I was not satisfied
with the report he had given he would
order a Committee to be set up in Dublin
and sent down to inquire into the matter.
This statement was made upon the authority of ex-service men, who could have
defended themselves, but very wisely,
as I say, they preferred to live within the
limits of the law, rather than take up
arms in their own defence. They could
have cleared the place if they had cared
to do so, but they were law-abiding.
I am at one with the hon. Member for
East Belfast (Mr. Donald) with regard to
doles. Doles remedy nothing. They
create evil; they feed upon the evil that
they are supposed to cure. Doles never
remedy anything, but degrade the individual that has to use them. I will not
go so far as to say that it is the right of
every individual to have work at the hands
of the nation. It may be in time it will
come to that. I hope the right hon.
Gentleman will not set up before him the
precedent that he has seen across the
water in regard to a department that he
thinks has functioned successfully.
hope he will use his own initiative, which
will be more beneficial than anything he
could copy from across the water.
him deal with the position as he finds it
here. As has been pointed out, the cir
cumstances in connection with this country
are in many respects very widely different
from those that prevail across the water.
I
Let
Before resuming my seat, seeing that
we are on the question of the adjournment,
279 Debate on Adjournment.
Debate on Adjournment. 280
4 OCTOBER 1921.
I would like to say one or two words
which I am sure the Prime Minister will
interpret in the most friendly spirit. I
want to assure him that if he has a more
loyal supporter than I intend to be I
Iwould like to find where he is. I will
never desert him in the effort he is making
to accomplish for us what without his aid
we would have found it very difficult
indeed to do. But we are about to
adjourn until the middle of December,
and I have the impression that the Prime
Minister should make the present an
opportunity for saying something of an
explanatory or informative nature to the
people outside, who are at the moment
anxious to know how we stand. For
instance, they have the impression, despite
all that we can say to the contrary, that
our Parliament is not going to function.
They have an idea that during the
adjournment something will take place
across the water that will annul the
Parliament that we are all so proud to be
associated with. It is only by the Prime
Minister taking an opportunity like the
present that he can dispel a fear or
illusion of that description, and I think
if he adjourned at the moment without
any reference being made to this matter
it would have a serious effect throughout
the community. I would like, for
instance, to ask the Prime Minister will
he seek the consent of the House to any
further deliberations with the British
Cabinet, any arrangements with the representatives of Sinn Fein, or will he proceed on Cabinet authority? When he
first took action in the matter the House
had not met, and could not meet, and he
was entitled to take that course then;
but to-day we find things different. The
House is met at the moment, and can
be reassembled on the shortest notice.
The question I would like to put to him
18: Will he attend the next Conference
upon his own initiative or the decision vi
the Cabinet, without consultation with
this House, and further, if he attends,
will he feel free to make any concessions
in respect of demands which may be made
upon him without first consulting the
House? These are matters of a serious
nature. He might not even think it wise
to answer these questions, but whether
that is so or not all I can say is that
people at the moment say we are too
much in the dark with respect to them.
Sinn Fein knows more about the demands
they are making; the Southern party are
apparently more made up with respect to
what they will have than we seem to be
at the moment, and people have a sort of
misgiving about matters that should not
be allowed to continue if it can be
dissipated, lest it result disastrously for
us.
That is all I want to say, and I am
sure the Prime Minister will take 1 în
the best spirit. As a Member of Parliament I could not have been face to face
with certain feelings and convictions in
the matter without at least expressing
them in the House. if I have gone
beyond the limit of privilege in this
matter I hope the Prime Minister will
pardon me. All I can say is that the
people at the moment have a strange
feeling that an impasse has been reached,
and if that feeling is allowed to continue
it will have a very disastrous effect.
The PRIME MINISTER: I immediately respond to the appeal of my hon.
Friend opposite in putting in a few words.
a reply to the distinct questions which he
has asked. One of them was whether, if
again invited by the Prime Minister of
England I would enter into a conference
with him. I would only say that our
position still holds good for the reasons that
I gave to this House very fully a few days
ago. In other words, the reasons that
enabled us to take the decision to go into
a conference in Downing Street still hold
good. To refuse to go in, if I may repeat
a few of the reasons, would be open to the
gravest misrepresentation across the
water. Secondly, I have a feeling that
we might be let down behind our backs
If there was no one there to say a word
for us, Ulster might come out of this very
badly. Another important point is that
we really have got a good case; we are
not ashamed of it, and are prepared to put
that case before the Prime Minister and
before any meeting of the Cabinet across
the water, and also, if others are in
attendance, to take up the attitude we
laid down in the letter which we wrote
when we replied to Mr. Lloyd George's
invitation to go into the conference.
The second question is one that has been
asked on many occasions and answered
just as frequently. Are we, if we go into
the conference, going to give anything
away? Well, Mr. SPEAKER, I presume
these will be the last words at this Table
before we adjourn till December, and I
hope the public outside will be really
re-assured when I tell them that not only
have we nothing to give away, but my
colleagues and I have no desire to give
anything away, and if there was even a
suggestion to give anything away we would
281 Debate on Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS Debate on Adjournment. 282

""
[The Prime Minister.]
immediately, in the terms of the motion
which is now before the House, call this
House together, take it into our fullest
British
confidence, and say,
The
Government has placed the following proposals before us, and I as your leader
would recommend you to accept those
proposals or to say I leave myself in your
hands.' But under no circumstances
will anything be done behind the backs
of the Members of this House or the backs
of the people outside. We do not work in
secret. As I have said before, our daily
work is open to everyone, and we are prepared to carry on as openly as possible.
My hon. Friend may carry this encouraging message-that this House now established is the proper sphere for ventilating
all grievances, and for at the same time
hearing the decision of the Government.
Therefore the decision of the Government
is and will be maintained-that nothing
will be done without the concurrence of
this House and the Senate.
With regard to the third and last part
of the question that my hon, Friend
referred to, I would like just to make a
slight appeal to the people outside as a
whole not to get unnecessarily rattled
during the trying times that lie immediately ahead of us. Far be it from
me to suggest for a moment that all of us,
including myself, have not every reason
to be irritated, to be stirred up, in fact
from time to time to boil over with anger
at some of the things which take place
with which we really have no concern
and nothing to do. But let them show
extra patience. Let them show a complete
trust in us, and we will never betray them.
Let them show an example not only
to other classes and creeds in the city of
Belfast, but throughout the whole of our
Northern area; let them maintain
that behaviour which has earned for them
such a splendid reputation throughout the
whole world, let them steadily
go on earning that high reputation, and I can assure them that their
reward will be one to which there is no
bounds, for if we can get through these
difficult times without unfortunate incident, without disturbance in our midst,
without stirring up bad blood at a crítical
moment, then I say we will have achieved
something of which we may be very
proud. We have gone through time and
again crises very serious not only for ourselves but for posterity. In my opinion
we are going through now an even more
crucial time than ever before. We want
very level heads, we want caution. I do
not know as to our heads or as to our
know
I
caution, but
this--it wants
courage, and no one of us is wanting in
courage. (Hon. Members: "Hear,
hear.") We will see this matter through,
I hope, finally, so that there will be no
But you may
more question about it.
take it from me that it will help me very
much in this delicate time if hon. Members who are leaders of their own parts,
and other leaders outside the House,
assume real leadership of those over whom
they have control, and take upon themselves part of the responsibility that rests
upon us, to calm the people, to see that
they are not stirred up by agencies which,
I often think, come from outside sources.
Remember that our opponents are
very shrewd, they
very underground in some of their methods,
and what appears on the face of it to a
Loyalist association, or to a Loyalist
assembly as advice helpful, perhaps, to
the leaders at the moment may be the
most injudicious advice that could possibly
be given. Therefore, let those leaders
are
make sure of the source from which all
this comes, and do their utmost to keep
calm and collected, and do what they can
to help us over this time, and I for my
part can say to them all that nothing
shall be left undone to save them from
disaster.
Mr. ANDREWS: I only want to say
a few words-
Mr. SPEAKER: The right hon.
Gentleman can only speak a second time
on this motion with the leave of the
House.
The House indicated assent.
to me.
Mr. ANDREWS: I wish to acknowledge very fully the very kind and
generous way in which all those hon.
Members who have spoken have referred
I did not for a moment suggest
that the shipbuilding trade in Belfast was
worse hit than in other places; quite the
contrary. But the point I wanted to
make was this-that Belfast was SO
largely dependent upon this great industry that any depression in that trade
naturally hits us harder than it does
places across the water, where the country
has so many other important industries
as well to depend upon.
283 Debate on Adjournment.
Message from the Senate. 284
4 OCTOBER 1921.
The question of unemployment benefit
has been referred to to-day. I know
quite well that what our people want is
work. I agree entirely with what has
been said in that connection; but at the
same time I want to point out that there
are times-and the present is one - when
it is impossible to find work to keep all
our people fully employed. Therefore, I
think that not a dole, for it is not a doleit is a wrong word to use- but an insurance system on a contributory basis, is
necessary in order that our people may be
saved from starvation, and from being
called on to endure hardships which it is
not to be expected that any human being
can bear. Those are all the words that I
need say to-day.
Question put and agreed to.
MESSAGE FROM THE SENATE.
That the Senate have agreed to the
Exchequer (Temporary Borrowing) Bill,
Exchequer and Audit Bill,
Superannuation Bill without amendment.
ROYAL ASSENT.
The PRIME MINISTER reported that
His Excellency the Lord Lieutenant, in
the name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his assent
to the following Acts agreed upon by both
Houses:-
Exchequer (Temporary Borrowing) Act,
1921;
Exchequer and Audit Act, 1921;
Superannuation Act, 1921, and
Interpretation Act [S.], 1921.
Resolved: "That this House do now
adjourn."-[Captain Dixon.]
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
Minutes after Four of the Clock
till Tuesday, 13th December
next.
285-6-7 Oral Answers

HOUSE OF COMMONS
Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Tuesday, 29th November, 1921.
The House met at a quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Oral Answers to Questions.
MAINTENANCE OF LAW AND
ORDER.
Prime
1. Mr. DONALD asked the
Minister whether he will state the policy
of the Government with regard to the reestablishing and maintaining of law and
order in Northern Ireland in order to
give some degree of safety and protection
to lives and property?
The PRIME MINISTER: Owing to the
outrages which have been committed by
Sinn Fein during the past week, arrangements
were made with the Imperial
Authorities for the drafting into Ulster of
additional Troops. The Cabinet of
Northern Ireland gave instructions on
Saturday for the immediate enrolling of
700 "A" and 5,000 "B" Specials to complete these two forces up to establishment.
It is hoped that these steps will be found
sufficient, but no matter what the financial
cost, the Government of Northern Ireland
is determined to perform its primary duty
of maintaining protection of life and property. I propose to deal further with
this subject in my statement on the motion
for adjournment this afternoon.
Offices of the Speakers.
LORD LIEUTENANT'S REPLY TO
ADDRESS.
The PRIME MINISTER reported to the
House the Lord Lieutenant's answer to
the Addresses presented to him by order
of the House of Commons on the 20th
September, praying him, on behalf of His
Majesty, to make provision for the support
and dignity of the Offices of Speaker of
the Senate and Speaker of the House of
Commons, as followeth :-
I have received your Address praying
that I will make provision for the support and dignity of the offices of Speaker
of the Senate of Northern Ireland, and
Speaker of the House of Commons of
Northern Ireland. I will gladly give
directions for carrying your proposal
into effect, and I trust that you will
make good the expense attending the
same.
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
Supply Committee.-Deferred till tomorrow.
Ways and Means Committee.-Deferred
till to-morrow.-[Captain Dixon.]
((
Motion for Adjournment.
THE PRIME MINISTER'S
STATEMENT.
Motion made and question proposed:
That this House do now adjourn."-
[Captain Dixon.]
House
The PRIME MINISTER: I would like
at the outset to explain that since we last
met we have found it necessary to ask you,
Sir, under the reservation contained in our
last resolution, as to the adjournment of
the House, to call us together earlier than
we had anticipated. The reason for doing
so is two-fold; first of all, because of the
necessity for making provision in connection with the services that have been
transferred to us since we last met, and,
secondly, because of the grave crisis
through which we have been passing,
and which my colleagues and
myself consider justification for
placing this
and the
public outside in complete possession of
all the facts as far as I am at liberty to
give them. I venture to make an earnest
appeal to the House to treat this debate
on the very highest lines. I would ask
them not to allow it to develop into on the
one hand a mutual admiration society
because of any action that any of
us have taken, or to develop
on the other hand into one which
would lead to recrimination against any
parties or any persons. Needless to say it is
unnecessary for any hon. Member to urge
upon my colleagues or myself either to
stand firm at this particular moment-we
shall always stand firm-(hon. Members:
Hear, hear)-or to point out the dangers
either that lie behind us or that lie in
front of us. We are fully alive, and, perhaps, in better possession of the knowledge
of these dangers than anyone else.
I propose to divide my remarks into
three parts for convenience. First of all to
address them, if I may do so, to the
British Cabinet and to the British people;
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
29 NOVEMBER 1921.
I
secondly, to the representatives of Southern Ireland, who are at present engaged in
negotiation with the British Government;
and, thirdly, to deal, as I have just promised my hon. Friend opposite (Mr.
Donald), somewhat further with the ques-
Lion of the maintenance of law and order
in our midst. But before I address my
remarks to the British people and the
British Cabinet, I would like to go into
some detail with regard to the steps that
have had to be taken since last we met
here at the beginning of last month.
went over to London on Saturday,
the 5th of this month-I arrived
in London, I should say, on Saturday, the 5th of this month-for the inno
cuous purpose in the first place of meeting
my family at the ordinary half term, an
secondly to endeavour to secure a day or
two's rest, which I felt at that time
I was somewhat in need of. I
emphasise that in order to clear away
at once that I went there with any knowledge whatsoever that the situation had
changed beyond what was represented to
the Members of this House at our last
meeting. On
On that black Saturday, I
call it black Saturday, for it will
always stand out in my memory as one
of the darkest days that I have had to
deal with since I have been associated
with the Ulster question, I received on
arrival an urgent message from Mr.
Lloyd George to meet him to talk
matters over at twelve o'clock. Prior
to that I had a friendly hint as to the
matters around which the conversation
would turn. I had to make up my mind
once more instantly as to the proper
action to pursue, and I saw clearly that
it was my duty to this House and to
Ulster before entering into any conversations of a fresh nature opening up new
vistas, to press for the transfer of all
the services to which we were entitled,
and that immediately. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) If to you
anxiously watching events in London,
there appeared to be any hesitation on
my part or a prolonged interval, I would
ask you to bear in mind that that hesitatation and that long interval were
necessitated simply and solely because of
my endeavours to get all the machinery
into our own hands before we began to
open up any fresh conversations of any
kind whatever. I do not for a moment
desire to create the impression that
either the Prime Minister or any Member of His Majesty's Government was
here
using the holding back of the transfer
of the services as an engine to manoeuvre us into a different position. Far
from that being the case I can only say
that the moment word was given that we
were to have these Services transferred,
each one of His Majesty's Ministers
wholeheartedly and energetically set to
work to see that we were met in our just
demands, and the result is, of course, as
you all know that to-day we have the
most important of all the Services transferred, that regarding Finance, and
also that in regard to the maintenance of law and order. On 1st
December all the other Services will be
automatically transferred with the
exception that on the 1st of January
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Agriculture will get his Services transferred, and on the 1st February my
noble Friend, the Marquess of Londondery, Minister of Education, is prepared
to take up all those Services applicable
to his high office in the Government.
Therefore that portion of my work stood
out clearly in the early hours of the
Saturday morning. But when that was
done with, and when I was asked to meet
the Prime Minister at half-past four on
that afternoon and to enter into the main
object for which he had summoned me
to Downing Street, I felt that I was once
more face to face with a matter of the
greatest gravity and importance.
proposal in rough outline having been.
placed before me by Mr. Lloyd George, not
a moment was necessary to enable me to
make up my mind. We were joined in
our deliberations by Mr. Austin
Chamberlain, Sir Robert Horne, and Sir
Laming Worthington Evans. Part of
the time, the earlier part, was occupied
in arranging for the transfer of the
Services, towards which they gave every
possible assistance. I then took the
opportunity of giving them a hurried,
but I hope an accurate outline of the
situation in which we here found ourselves; how we had upheld, we hoped,
the best traditions of the Ulster people,
how against great odds we had carried
on and done our best to fulfil those
obligations, which after all were placed
upon us by His Majesty the King and
by the Cabinet of Great Britain.
The
Afterwards Mr. Lloyd George ran
through roughly the scheme which he
proposed should be the basis of dis
russion between the Ulster representatives and his Cabinet. I told Mr.
Lloyd George that the thing was utterly

Adjournment.

was a
[The Prime Minister.]
impossible, and 1 went further. I said
that I felt so instinctively that this was
not a matter affecting Ulster; that this
was not a matter merely between the Six
Counties in the North and the twentysix counties elsewhere, but it
matter which went right to the very root
of not only Great Britain but of the
whole Empire-(hon. Members: Hear,
hear)—and I said to him that if it was
even entertained by the members of my
Cabinet, or entertained by this House of
Commons or by the people of Ulster,
they would have to get someone else to
lead them, for I would not touch it.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). There
was great pressure brought to bear, and
we parted determined on my part not on
that Saturday nor on the Monday when
I met him again, finally to break off
negotiations, because I felt it would be
unfair to ask me to put proposals before
iny colleagues unless those proposals
were definitely put in writing.
him it was impossible for me after an
hour and a half's long conversation to
convey the meaning and the spirit of
these proposals which he now put forward on behalf of His Majesty's
Government, and I must have them in
writing. That carried us over Saturday
and Monday, and I then took the step
which I know meets with all your
approval, of summoning to London the
other members of our Cabinet. I asked,
in addition that our Chairman of Committees should also join us to give us the
benefit of his wide experience which he
gained when a member of the Convention, and also the hon. Member for South
Antrim in the Imperial Parliament, who
as Whip of the Ulster members at
Westminster, is well able to judge of the
feeling not only amongst his own
colleagues from Ulster, but also of the
great Unionist Party to which they have
always been proud to belong.
1 told
The
Cabinet and those two with me settled
down there to consider these proposals all
through the Wednesday. At the request of Mr. Lloyd George we were
asked to postpone consideration of them,
as he required somewhat longer to put
them in writing, and on the Thursday,
when they reached us we were in a position immediately to begin to compose an
answer. That answer was handed to
the Government on Friday, and I may
say that that answer was one which
although it is impossible for me at the
moment to indicate its nature-it is one
which I know will meet with the universal
approval of all Members of this House,
of the Senate, and of our people in
Ulster. That I feel confident about.
Since that black Saturday I may recall memories to the fact that there
your
has been a Press campaign, without
parallel in the history of Great Britain,
directed against the people of Ulster.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). That
campaign was skilful, malignant, and
unrelenting. While Sinn Fein was held up
to the whole world as a good boy, we
here in Ulster were pictured as a bad
boy.
What the Press asked for were
concessions, what they really meant was
Surrender. My colleagues and I, needless to say, got very busy, and I need
hardly attempt to picture the anxiety
possessing all of us as we visited first one
and then another of those prominent
Statesmen in London whom we thought
it well to take counsel of and to whom
we were most anxious to present the real
cause of Ulster as against the malicious
falsehoods that were appearing in the
Press. I think it would be unfitting if
I were to pass by at this stage the great
encouragement we all received by the
open, frank, courageous, and splendid
attitude of Mr. Bonar Law.
I have no
means of knowing what that great
Statesman's intentions are for the future,
but I venture to predict that if ever
Ulster finds itself in a difficulty similar
to that from which she has just emerged,
we will not call in vain upon Mr. Bonar
Law to take up our cause.
We saw many. We did a great deal of
private work, the fruits of which have
gradually, but slowly, become apparent to
the people. I can only say in this connection that there are two outstanding
features impressed upon my imagination
in connection with this feeling of the
Unionist Party in England and of its
leaders. (1.) Many of them had been
innocently led astray. (2) Had my
suggestion made at the passage of the 1920
Act been taken advantage of by the
Government this could not have occurred.
My suggestion was that
that until these
questions of the settling of the Northern
and the Southern Parliaments had been
completed and I visualised it taking not
months but possibly years to get these
properly settled down as going concerns
--the Prime Minister of Southern Ireland
and the Prime Minister of Northern Ireland should be members of the British
Cabinet until the whole thing was settled
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
29 NOVEMBER 1921.
and done with. And I hope the House
will remember that then I had no more
idea of being Leader of the Ulster
Party than anyone here in this House.
It was made quite on its merits when the
Bill was just coming towards its conclusion, and had that advice been carried
out I venture to say that the impasse in
which the Government finds itself to-day
never could have been created, because
there would have been some one there representing Ulster interests who would never
have let them drift into the position in
which they find themselves to-day. (Hon.
Members; Hear, hear.) However, this
crisis has arisen. It has to be got over.
Other correspondence has taken place, of
course, as you are all aware, between Mr.
Lloyd George, my colleagues, and myself, but of a comparatively unimportant
character compared with the
principle that was put before us.
We pressed in every communication for
full publication of all these letters that
had passed between us.
We felt strongly
anyone
main
that the malicious represent. tion in the
Press would very soon give place to a
reasoned examination of the case which
we had put up on your behalf. To this
day I fail to see why we should be
scolded by
for demanding
publication, under the circumstances, for
hold in my hand here, the first publicaion by His Majesty's Government of the
correspondence relating to the proposals of
His Majesty's Government for an Irish
settlement and to put our correspondence
on the same footing as that of Sinn Fein,
would at all events have conveyed some
sense of fairness to the British people.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) But even
in this publication our first two letters to
His Majesty's Government are studiously
omitted. And I consider,
colleagues consider, that we should press
for this correspondence being all thrown
open to public examination and criticism.
However, we are not going here to play
the small part, and I, for my part, am
quite satisfied to forego having the
correspondence published; and I earnestly
recommend all my hon. Friends here not
to press for it, and I will tell you why.
and my
It is really a matter not of politics,
but of statesmanship, and I am extremely
anxious that no word of ours, and
certainly no action of ours, should in any
way be thrown up against us as being the
cause that has broken down the negotiations between the British Authorities and
Sinn Fein; and, therefore, if it is to help
anyone towards that end, the end of peace
in Ireland, I say that we should acquiesce
in the request of His Majesty's Government, and, therefore, not press to have the
correspondence published at the moment.
But let me say this, that if I observe
the necessity for doing so I will not ask
their permission, no matter what the consequences are. After all, we are most
long suffering and tolerant, but a time
comes when for the safety of our people
it is necessary to do what in ordinary
circumstances we are prepared to pass
over. But I say on this occasion we will
not hold out for the publication of this
correspondence which might possibly embarrass some of those who are at present
taking part in the negotiations. And I
ask you all to bear in mind to-day, that
your enemies to-day may be your firm
friends to-morrow.
It is very difficult in the flux of political
feeling in England to know who is your
friend and who is not; and we have examples in the past few days of some men
coming forward to me and my colleagues
offering help, offering every assistance in
the future struggle that may be before us,
from whom it was to me really astonishing
that we should get such offers.
therefore, I am extremely anxious-I am
sure you are all extremely anxious that
nothing should be done to make it more
difficult for those who desire to help us
in the times that lie ahead.
And,
Let me say what I have to say to the
British Cabinet and to the British Government. I have portrayed very accurately
the situation as it occurred from day to
day from I left here until I left London
a few days ago. It would be improper and
undesirable that I should mention what the
proposals are but I will go so far as to
indicate that the Press are fairly accurate
in what they described as the scheme put
before us by Mr. Lloyd George and the
British Government. That scheme was a
scheme for an All-Ireland Parliament.
There is no use disguising the fact.
has been given by the Government to the
Press. Therefore it no longer need be
shrouded in mystery, or treated as a secret.
It is fairly accurate all that has appeared
in explanation of that preposterous proposal. I do not propose here to go into
our objections to an All-Ireland Parliament. I believe we would be wasting our
time. I do not believe that there is a
single Member of this House or a single
T
It
295 Adjournment.

HOUSE OF COMMONS
Adjournment.
[The Prime Minister.]
man in Ulster who is not ready with
hundreds and thousands of arguments
against anything of the kind. I recollect
very well a Liberal-I think he was a
Liberal---Pressman asking one of our sturdy
Islandmen what were his reasons for not
having Home Rule. And the Islandman
said: We won't have it." The Pressman said: " Yes, I understand that is
your determination, and the determination of your fellow-workers in the Island.
But I want to know your reason. I want
to know your reason for it."
And the
Islandman said: "We won't have it."
That is sufficient for us here to-day. I
can only say that all the arguments that
were produced in the Press were bad
arguments, and the only one that made me
pause for a moment was that which
appeared in the Evening News of the 25th
November last, when I observed a short
article headed "Ulster must help
England." That made me think, and I
feel confident that if by any fair and
reasonable sacrifice a proposal was put
before this House for some means of
settlement, that would help England, it
would engage all our attention and our
undefatigable energy in order to attempt
in every way to master the difficulty, and
in order to help England and the Empire.
But how could it help England? How
could it help the Empire to place the
loyal population in the North under the
heel of Sinn Fein in the South and West?
Therefore the last of all the arguments
that were used, so far as I was concerned,
immediately went by the board. No, just
as it is impossible for us to discuss such
a question with the British Government,
sɔ it is unnecessary for us to discuss it here
among ourselves. Our minds are made
up. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) What
we feel, as I tried to explain on a former
occasion to an outdoor meeting—what we
feel is that it would be dishonest of us,
literally dishonest, to sit down at a conference table while such a proposal was
before us, because if you once sit down to
such a thing it is tantamount to an
admission that along that line there is
some hope.
There is no hope, and,
therefore, it is far better to make our
conditions perfectly plain and understandable by the British Government. And
after all it cannot be said that because
we refused to discuss one method of
coming to some settlement with the South
and West we have closed every avenue.
Far from it.
We ourselves put up an alternative to
the suggestion made to us by His Majesty's
Government, and there are other proposals which are well worthy of careful
consideration. They, at all events, do
not interfere with our powers and
privileges here. They, at all events, do
not encroach
in any
way upon the
determination that we arrived at before;
that under no conditions whatever would
we attempt to determine or interfere with
the terms of the settlement that might be
reached between Great Britain and Sinn
Fein. There are other avenues to be explored, and we are prepared to talk them
over. We are prepared to meet in conference, and to do all we can to reach the
peace which we desire perhaps more than
any other people in Great Britain or Ireland. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
For peace to us means practically living
once more in security of life and property,
and the business community being able to
get on with their contribution to our great
Empire trade. But I feel instinctively
that Sinn Fein has misunderstood us,
and I shall come to that point in a
few moments when I have done with
my message to the British Cabinet.
I want them, therefore, to understand,
and to understand finally, that it is not
the determination of your Cabinet and of
this House alone, but it is the determination of the whole people, as was so very
well shown by the great mass demonstrations held here and throughout all parts
of Ulster not so very long ago, that they
will under no circumstances whatever contemplate going under a Sinn Fein government under the present conditions.
this after all seems to take the country
and to take the Government by surprise.
I really cannot understand why, because,
although the British Cabinet refused to
publish our correspondence when they
were issuing the White Paper we, to
protect ourselves, published the letter to
which I referred on a former occasion in
this House. May I quote once more a
passage of it that has a very close bearing
upon this particular point? I said in my
letter on behalf of my colleagues and
myself:-
But
We rely upon the British people, who charged
us with the responsibility of undertaking our
own Parliamentary institutions, to safeguard
the ties that bind us to Great Britain and
the Empire, to ensure that we are not prejudiced by any terms entered into between
them and Mr. De Valera, and to maintain the
just equality exhibited throughout the Government of Ireland Act.
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
29 NOVEMBER 1921.
Now, that is part of the letter that we
sent to the British Government. Another
part is:-
:-
Our acceptance of your original invitation to
meet in conference in London still holds
good, and if at any time our assistance is
again desired we are available, but I feel
bound to acquaint you that no meeting is
possible between Mr. De Valera and myself
until he recognises that Northern Ireland
will not submit to any authority other than
His Majesty the King and the Parliament
of the United Kingdom.
Therefore. in the action which my colleagues and I took we were merely reiterating what had become public property
through the publication of that letter subsequent to the issue by the Government of
their White Paper. There we stand.
There we propose to stand, and to hang
on to the very end with all the power that
we possess, and it is great power. We
propose to stand by that, and by our subsequent ultimatum to the Government not
even to consider those proposals.
We
Now may I turn for a few moments to
the question of Sinn Fein? They cannot
get Ulster. What they can do is they can
take the advice offered them by General
Smuts. Those in power amongst them at
the moment still appear to hold that Ulster
can be intimidated or can be coerced into
the acceptance of a position of subordination. That is not so.
are neither
to be intimidated nor coerced. The only
way that they can ever hope to secure
Ulster is by winning her, by starting out
upon a path such as we have begun to
tread, the end of which is happiness,
prosperity and peace throughout the
length and breadth of the land, showing
to all classes and all creeds firm, jost, and
upright government, throwing to one side
all this abominable machinery of crime and
outrage, asking forgiveness for the dastardly deeds that have been done in the
past, and which so recently have stained
the fair name even of our own city. So
how can we be asked by anyone, however
simple, however foolish, to clasp hands
with people who, at the moment they are
pretending at all events to come to a settlement on these matters in London, are
flinging their bombs and shooting from
behind the chimney tops here in the city.
of Belfast. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
It is preposterous, and if the "I's" require dotting and the "T's" crossing, they
have been dotted and crossed over and
over again since General Smuts wrote these
words in his letter of the 4th August to
Mr. de Valera :-
My conviction is that for the present no
solution based on Ulster coming into the
Irish State will succeed. Ulster will not
agree. She cannot be forced, and any solution on those lines is at present foredoomed
to failure. At present an Irish settlement is only possible if the hard facts are
calmly faced and Ulster is left alone.
My strong advice to you is to leave Ulster
alone for the present as the only line along
which a solution is practicable, to concentrate on a free constitution for the remaining
twenty-six counties, and through a successful
running of the Irish State, and a pull of
economic and other peaceful forces, eventually to bring Ulster into that State.
Those are not my words. Those are
not the words of one of our muchmaligned Orange brethren outside this.
House. Those are the words of a man
who was at one time himself a leading
Republican, and who knows what it is,
and what it has been to live under a Republic, and who now comes forward and
recommends Sinn Fein to set their own
house in order, and to begin at the right
end, which is to show good feeling not
only to Ulster but to Great Britain and
the Empire, and having done so then to
ask Ulster whether she will throw in her
lot with them or not. That is statesmanship, and it is only along some line of
that nature that there can ever hope to
be a settlement.
is
We
renew
May I just in conclusion say this, and
1 say it emphatically. We are not
blocking the way to a settlement.
want to have a settlement, and if there
any prospect of Sinn Fein using commonsense, even at this late hour, we
the offer we made to them before, that
through the machinery of the Council of
Ireland we are prepared to meet them,
to talk round a table, to discuss these
matters which are of vital interest to the
whole prosperity of our common land.
We are prepared to enter into friendly
rivalry with them in all matters of trade
and business, and we are prepared also
to enter into competition with them as
to whether the North or the South can
produce the best governed part of the
country. Our laws, we hope, will be
just laws. Let them produce laws
which we may copy in the interests of
our people. We will be only too happy
to do what we can to come to some
common settlement with regard to maintaining the good name of our common
land.
But I cannot tell, and no one can tell,
what view the Sinn Fein party may take.
Do not let anyone here imagine that they
have it all their own way. Do not let
anyone here imagine that they have not
got great difficulty and trouble in their
own ranks. But it was they who started
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister.]
this deluge, and therefore it is for them
to stop it before it is too late. We have
been no party to the destruction of our
country. We are not wreckers; we are
builders. Let them cast aside this old
feud of thousands of years that they
have had with the so-called cruel,
tyrannous British. Let them take off
the table the Act which is lying there
waiting for them. Let them take the
initial steps that we have taken. They
will not find it so very hard. They must
make a beginning somewhere.
Let
them start with their small Parliament.
Let them begin to get round them the
best men in the South and West. Let
them set their shoulders to something of
a constructive character, and then they
will at least have made a beginning, and
all we will say to them is "God bless you
in the attempt that you are making.'
""
The message, or rather the statement,
which the Prime Minister of England
authorised me to make to-day was one
which possibly may cause some disappointment on account of its brevity. It
will cause no disappointment to those
to-day
who
matter
here
are
I
in the
it contains. He asked me to
see him just before I left London.
had a very short conversation with him.
But I felt that our time would have
been wasted if I were not able to state
in this House some message crystallising
the situation as he saw it at the moment.
The message which he agreed that both h›
and I might release at three o'clock is
this:-
By Tuesday next either negotiations will
have broken down or the Prime Minister will
send me new proposals for consideration by
the Cabinet.
In the meantime the rights of Ulster will
be in no way sacrificed or compromised
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.") The
significance of this statement is that one
more week only is given to say either yes
or no.
Now that means that Sinn Fein, fully
alive now to our unflinching determination
not to go in to an all-Ireland Parliament,
has either got to say by Tuesday next that
she will still work for a settlement or else
all negotiations are broken off. I do not
think that anyone can say that there has
been undue haste in asking them to make
up their minds. For five whole months
they have had an opportunity to decide
the initial question with regard to taking
up the Government of the South and West
of Ireland. At all events we here stand
perfectly free from any blame in connection with the breakdown of the negotiations, if they do break down. We have
proposed to the Government to enter into
conversations on any plan, other than
going under a Dublin Parliament.
They have had an opportunity of exploring
all the avenues, and ever. at this eleventh
hour I would ask those, who are not carried
away by the glamour of republican idea,
and the peculiar sentimental nonsense
that has been talked in the past, to band
themselves together in the South and
West and insist on attempting to get a
settlement along other lines. There is a
great mass of opinion in the South and
West that should be heard in this matter,
and these people, owing to their
numbers, should make their influence
felt in the ranks of Sinn Fein, for it is
only a settlement can bring peace and
prosperity to our land. Therefore I hope
some strides may be made by them towards
coming to a settlement now, and if so we
here, as I have already said, will most
gladly enter into communication with
them along the lines I have already indicated.
I think I have covered the whole of
the ground. It is impossible for me to
sit down without making some reference
to the great Unionist party across the
Channel, and to give you my barometrical
reading of the conference that took place
in Liverpool. It is my deliberate opinion
that we have practically the whole people
who are worth having with us. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) Before I left this
House I was satisfied on that point. Now
I am certain. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) If you can picture anyone in England going to the country and standing on
a platform in the course of an election
with a proposal we will not say to
coerce Ulster but to bring forward
a Bili which means that Ulster will
have to take something she does not
want. you immediately tar that man
all over as a Sinn Feiner, and I venture to say that there is not a single
Unionist, Conservative,. or Liberal
Unionist in England, who would stand on
any platform in any part of the country
with such a proposal, and be elected,
once it became known that he was adopting Sinn Fein principles while his opponent
was fighting for the Empire, and for the
Crown, and for Ulster. Therefore I do
not think our party has been at all shaken
by the Liverpool meeting, but rather that
it has been further knit together by that
trial through which we have just passed,
and that, although statesmen may be
carried away for the moment in their
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
29 NOVEMBER 1921.
anxiety to reach a settlement of some sort,
still at the same time undoubtedly there
is, right down in the hearts of the great
British public, throbbing there all the
time, and never ceasing, the conviction
that old England has an honour, a prestige
that cannot be shaken, and that must not
be given away.
(Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) That is what will carry us
through, because with those sentiments
we have always been associated. I would
like to pay a special tribute to our old
friend, and my colleague for many years
in the British House of Commons, Mr.
Ronald McNeill-(hon. Members: Hear,
hear)-who, when it was considered inadvisable for the Ulster Members to voice
their case at the Liverpool Conference,
took up our cudgels and made a speech
which, I am told by all who heard it, had
a great influence upon that vast meeting.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) Mr. Ronald
M'Neill is like, perhaps I might say, my
Friend the Chairman of Committees, who
really does not shine in the sun, he does
not show at his best when the wind is
blowing fair, but when the storms threaten
and when Ulster seems to be in danger,
then he is a new man, he comes forward,
and no one that I know is better suited to
put our case before a British audience.
We owe him a great deal for the firm
stand he took and for the very great influence he exercised upon that conference.
Now, might I, before I close, meet my
hon. Friend (Mr. Donald) with regard to
the question of law and order?
I was
pained to a degree, and filled with anxiety
when in London, to have the news conveyed of the dastardly crimes which had
been perpetrated by Sinn Fein in our
midst. I have said before,and I repeat again
here to-day, that if Ulster has to go
bankrupt we will pay for special constabulary to preserve law and order.
Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) As far as I can
control we are going to be very careful
with regard to finance in every region and
in every department of the Government,
but I feel that all of you, all of my colleagues in the House here and our Ulster
people, would be showing a want of gratitude, to start with, and a want of foresight, if we were to hold back a single
thing that is considered necessary by those
gallant men who, while we are at home,
are out guarding our lives and our property. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) 1
have very deep feelings in connection with
this matter, and I can assure one and all
that, no matter where it leads us, if it
All
requires every man in Ulster to be either
an "A" or a "B" or a "C," those men will
be picked, as my advisers tell me it is
necessary in order to protect the
community, whether here in our own
streets of the city of Belfast, or
whether it be on the outskirts of
Fermanagh, Tyrone, Derry, or anywhere else. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) And there will be no choosing,
there will be no pets amongst them.
must be protected and all must be treated
the same. Every inch of land inside the
Six Counties is sacred soil. Every inch of
soil inside the Six Counties must be a
harbour of refuge for those who may be
driven across our border for safety of life
and limb. And if we cannot get sufficient
protection by the methods I have already
indicated, I say, Sir, I will lay down the
reins of office the moment this Parliament
refuses to back me up, or to give us sufficient funds to equip and maintain at the
highest possible standard the Special
Constabulary necessary to preserve law
and order. Let that be taken as no threat
to our opponents. None whatever is intended, but I feel so strongly that setting
out, as we are, as a new State we must
begin at the very foundation of law and
order. A State cannot live, cannot succeed, and cannot prosper if it is based on
Tammany Hall methods. It must be
governed according to righteousness and
justice, and I can promise that, so far as
that is concerned, you will never find me
wanting.

There is another point on which I
would like to touch. It may be wrong
to say these words here, but, after all,
the importance of them must give way
to the occasion. We have been asked by
many influential persons in the region
over which we hold sway to put our
whole energies into propaganda, which is
one of the most expensive ways of spending money. I say if the time comes
for such a thing we will not flinch at all
-of course it is quite clear of the
Government and my colleagues-but
what I want to say to those who are urging us to pour out our money in this way
is: "Pour out your money in aid of those
poor people who were shot down in our
streets. Bring forth these hundreds,
these thousands of pounds that you wish
spent in Canada, Australia and elsewhere, bring them forth, do not wait, because of all those who deserve sympathy
and support from every man in our
midst it is those, who have to bear
the shock, living as they do day by day,
Adjournment.
Adjournment..
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister.]
have
or a
and sleeping as they are night by night
right up against these foul murderers. We
cannot do too much for those who, for
their loyalty to their country,
suffered the loss of a father,
brother, or, it may be, a wife, or a
daughter. I earnestly urge that the

in such great distress. Remember that
many of them could have purchased their

half-way the flag of surrender.
the credit of the people of this Province
that they cannot be cowed, they cannot
be over-awed, they cannot be coerced by
