(Cheers.)
even
It is to
Mr. LYNN: The Prime Minister in
his opening remarks said he hoped this
Parliament would not turn this occasion
into a mutual admiration society. Whatever else the Northern Parliament may
become the last thing it ever will become,
I think, is a mutual admiration society.
I am sure the Prime Minister will allow
me to voice my own personal feeling in
saying how delighted I have been in
listening to his splendid speech. I have
listened to a great many speeches he has
made since he came into public life, and
I can say that I have never listened to
any with greater pleasure than the one
I have just listened to now. From beginning to end of these negotiations he
has played the part we expected him to
play, the part of a loyal Ulsterman. I
think in the whole history of Ulster it
is true that there was only one occasion
on which a traitor was found, and
fortunately he was not an Irishman, he
was an Englishman. There are no
traitors in the Ulster party to-day from
the highest to the lowest, and the Prime
Minister in every step he has taken has
the cordial approval not only, I am sure,
of the Members of this House but of the
rank and file throughout the Northern
Province. I am sorry, and I am sure
the House is sorry that the Prime
Minister was not able to give us fuller
details regarding these negotiations.
is not to blame for that. As he has told
us, and as we all know, he has done his
very best to get the details of the proceedings published. Why they have not
been published it is easy for us to guess,
and I think our Prime Minister is quite
correct when he says that the details
He
that have been published in the Press
are fairly accurate. They are accurate
to the extent that they give one side of
the case, and I think it would be much
better for Ulster if we had both sides of
the case, and I go a step further and say
that Mr. Lloyd George has not treated
our Prime Minister and this Parliament,
or, indeed, the English people fairly in
withholding information which he has
given to his friends in the Press. Our
Prime Minister referred to Black Saturday.
If I may say SO it was not a Black
Saturday for us because it showed
Ulster in her true colours, it showed that
Ulster, as the Prime Minister has said,
is neither to be coerced nor cajoled into
surrender.
We in Ulster have some grievance
against the Government. Last year we
were told that if we only agreed to the
setting up of two Parliaments for Ireland
it would bring peace to this distracted
country. Most of us still hold that the
Union is the best form of government for
Ireland; most of us were anxious to cling
to the Union, but we listened to those
arguments, and in the interests of peace
we agreed to set up this Parliament in
Northern Ireland, and to work it to the
best advantage. A cynical friend of mine.
remarked to me this morning that the
irrevocable decrees of the Lloyd Georgian
Government are always revoked, and the
things that are inevitable never happen.
Mr. Lloyd George knew very well that we
did not want this Parliament. He knew
that we made a great concession when we
agreed to set up and work a Parliament
here, and when people to-day tell us that
we have made no concession I think wo
are entitled to refer to that fact. What
concession have the Sinn Feiners made?
We have not been told. I should say that
if the Sinn Feiners had accepted
allegiance to the Crown the Prime
Minister would have been more overjoyed
than the father was when the prodigal returned; but we have not had any assurance
that they have accepted allegiance to the
Crown, or that they have altered in any
way from the position in which they were
when they entered the parleys. I for one,
speaking for myself, have always doubted
the wisdom of the British Government in
going into this convention. I have felt
that it is a mistake to confer with men
whose hands are covered with the blood
of loyal officials of the Crown and loyal
civilians. I remember a few years ago
when a distinguished politician, Mr. F.
E. Smith, was horrified at the very re-
305 Adjournment
29 NOVEMBER 1921.

Adjournment.
now
order that the people would forget his own
weakness. Some people have referred to
the action of the British Government as
treachery. I like to use moderate language.
Treachery is an ugly word, and, perhaps,
it is too strong to use in this connection,
and it might not be accurate, because
treachery means that someone is going to
give way. Well, we are not going to give
way. Mr. Lloyd George has not treated
us fairly. He was afraid of giving offence
to Mr. de Valera, and then he thought he
would make a deal, and the price of that
deal was to be Ulster. Very like many
other foolish men he made a mistake,
because Ulster is not for sale, and if
Ulster is ever to be sold she will not be
sold by an outsider. Ulster can only be
sold by an Ulsterman, and the Ulster men
have no intention of selling Ulster. If I
may quote such a high dignitary as Lord
Birkenhead I would use his words when
.he said:
collection of Gladstone's associations with
Parnell. Parnell was an angel of
innocence compared with the gentlemen
with whom Lord Birkenhead and his
colleagues are now associated, and I for
one do not envy those men the position
they occupy. I would beseech them to do
one thing, and that is to stop making
these sickening references to the characters
of the men with whom they are
associated. What has brought these
men into notoriety? The fact that they
have organised and carried on one of the
most diabolical murder societies the world
has ever known. That, and nothing else,
has brought them to the forefront. The
cause of all the trouble in Ireland has
really been cowardice. If it had not been
for cowardice there would not have been
any trouble to start with, and if it had
not been for continued cowardice on the
part of the British Government, over
which Mr. Lloyd George presides, the
trouble would have been brought to a complete end long ago. It was in cowardice
that this conference was conceived, and
cowardice is written in large letters across
the face of it. We do not forget the
language Mr. Lloyd George used to de
Valera. He told him in those letters, to
which our Prime Minister has referred,
that there could be no conference unless
they gave up the idea of a republic and
declared their allegiance to the King.
Mr. de Valera has neither repudiated the
republic nor stated that he is in favour
of swearing allegiance to the King. Yet,
in spite of his refusal to do these things,
Mr. Lloyd George has done what he said
was impossible. He said himself he
would be disloyal to his King if he
entered into a conference with those men
unless they admitted allegiance. Well,
they have not admitted allegiance, and
they have gone into the conference with
Mr. Lloyd George. Mr. Lloyd George
can square with his own conscience-I
should
rather elastic consciencesay
whether in his opinion he has been loyal
to his King.
Then in the middle of the conference
there was that notorious telegram to the
Pope which caused trouble to Mr. Lloyd
George. He came down to the British
House of Commons, and indulged in some
heroics. He used brave words about
taking up the challenge, and how did he
take up the challenge? Like the clever
politician that he is, instead of meeting
it fairly and squarely, he dragged Ulster
into the controversy, and he did so in
Ulster is a province that can neither be
conquered by cowards nor cheated by knaves,
It is true that three years ago Mr.
Lloyd George entered into another
solemn undertaking, and I would like
Members of this House who have not
done SO to refresh their memories by
looking back at that manifesto.
It was
issued by Mr. Lloyd George and Mr.
Bonar Law before the last General
Election, and in it Mr. Lloyd George
agreed that the Six Counties--mind you
not part of the Six Counties, the whole
of the Six Counties-were to be left out
of any settlement of the Irish question.
I do not see how any honest man can
wriggle out of that promise. I am not
surprised at the statement that our own
Prime Minister has made with regard to
Mr. Bonar Law. Mr. Bonar Law is the
soul of honour, and one who knows him,
knows him well, as some of us do, cannot
think of Mr. Bonar Law stooping to
some of the things that have been done
during the past few weeks.
We are
asked why will not Ulster
go
Dublin Parliament, why will not we keep
the powers we have now and allow the
all-Ireland Parliament to
manage the
Customs and Excise, the Post Office, and
all the other reserved services.
Again I
would answer in the words of Lord
Birkenhead :
into a
We are a Protestant community and we
will not be governed by a Roman Catholic
Government.
I hope that answer which Mr. F. E.
Smith, now Lord Birkenhead, gave some
years ago will convince him that we are
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Lynn.]
justified in the steps we have taken.
And if we are asked why we are not prepared to go under a Roman Catholic
a
government we say it is because we know
the antecedents of these men who would
control an all-Ireland Parliament. if
our objections were strong in 1912 they
are a thousand-fold stronger to-day.
From 1916 onwards we have seen Sinn
Fein in all its ugliness, and much as we
disliked the old Nationalist party and
the old Nationalist methods we dislike
Sinn Fein more, and we have no more
intention of placing our liberties under
a Dublin Parliament than we have of
placing them under Ali Baba and his
forty thieves. It is time to speak
plainly to the British Government, and
I am glad the Prime Minister has done
so. It is well that he should tell our
false friends and open enemies that, so
far as we are concerned, we have no intention whatever of going under
Dublin Parliament. I think it was Mr.
Austen Chamberlain who remarked
quite recently that Ulster was mistress of
her fate. We are well aware of that
fact, and we say to Mr. Austen
Chamberlain, and everyone else to whom
it is of any importance, that Ulster
means to be mistress of her fate, and has
no intention of allowing any man or any
party to rob her of her liberties.
Politicians may babble and talk as much
as they like, but Ulster's mind is firmly
made up.
Ulster is determined, that
come what may, she has no intention of
going under a Dublin Parliament.
Some people are very hostile to us because
we refuse to commit suicide, while there
are others who ask us what sauce we would
like to be cooked with. We have no intention of committing suicide, nor have
we any intention of selecting which sauce
we will be cooked with, because we are
not going to be cooked in any shape or
form. All we ask is to be left alone.
are told we are unreasonable people. Is
it unreasonable to ask to be left alone?
Have Sinn Feiners such love for us that
they want to take us under their control tɔ
do us good? Really, their anxiety to get
Ulster under their control 1S very
touching, especially after the object
lessons we have had within the last twelve
months, and even during the last twelve
days. Mr. Austin Chamberlain in most
pleading tones says" Look at South
Africa."
There is no comparison between
South Africa and Ireland. The people
We
•
over whom General Smuts presides with
such great tact and great ability have not
resorted to murder as a political weapon.
They are not subservient to the Vatican.
This is a question the average Englishman
is afraid to face. He is afraid of being
called a religious bigot, and yet we know it
is a religious question. A Member of the
Cabinet, a personal friend of my own, says
Ireland must not be partitioned; but Ireland has never been one political unit, and
the divisions were never more marked or
wider than at the present time. Lloyd
George admitted the fact in 1920 that
Ireland had never been one political unit.
It is well-known that Ireland is not only
divided into two parts, but that for over
2,000 years there have been two Irelands.
Yet people say Ireland must not be
partitioned. The men who are strongest
in their protest about partitioning Ireland are men who have no scruples whatever in partitioning the United Kingdom.
There are two peoples in Ireland, one industrious, law-abiding, and God-fearing,
and the other slothful, murderous, and
disloyal. These two Irelands are not
going to be brought into union by the
passing of an Act of Parliament. If
there is ever to be one Ireland the process
of building it must be slow. Ulster will
never be forced into a Dublin Parliament.
She has never refused to pay her fair
share, either in blood or in treasure, if
it was in the interest of the United Kingdom or the Empire that it should be so.
Many threats have been made with the
object of coercing Ulster into a Dublin
Parliament. One of these is the absurd
one about a differential income-tax. It
would be a strange reversal of things if a
man's income tax is to go down in
ratio to the number of fellow-citizens
he has murdered. Is a Sinn Feiner
to go and say to the income-tax
collector "I have murdered twenty
or thirty policemen, and, therefore, I
should be let off." The more policemen,
the more soldiers, and the more loyal
citizens that are killed the greater reason
there will be for the reduction of incometax. That is a form of threat that no sane
man would think of putting forward in
the British House of Commons, and we
may leave it to the silly newspapers wo
are using that argument at present.
people of Ulster would rather pay 6/-
income-tax and be free, than pay 1/-
income-tax and be under a Sinn Fein
Parliament. I agree with the Prime
Minister that we are anxious for peace.
The
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
29 NOVEMBER 1921.
We want peace, but we believe peace cannot be purchased by cowardly surrender,
and for that reason we refuse to barter
our liberties, and what is more important,
the liberties of those who will come after
us. I am convinced that her severest
critics will yet live to see that in the step
she is taking Ulster is not only rendering
the best service to her own people, but
to the United Kingdom. People will
realise that we are doing what intelligent
men ought to do. Signs are not lacking
that public opinion is trying to assert itself
in spite of the Lloyd Georgian Press. It
is a very significant fact that a few days
us
a huge circulation
ago a paper with
published an article from the pen of Lord
Rothermere a manly, courageous article,
which showed that powerful forces are on
the side of Ulster. I for one am optimistic
so far as Ulster is concerned. I have
always been optimistic, because I believe
that Ulster's cause is just, and that Ulster
is bound to succeed. Why has Ulster
been able to weather the storms in the
past? Because her faith
was wellfounded, and because Ulster men and
Ulster women have been always loyal to
one another. Ten days ago some of you
were engaged in dedicating a memorial to
our glorious dead at Thiepval. These
men gained immortality by courage and
self-sacrifice, and by the exercise of the
same qualities we are going to succeed.
To those weak-kneed politicians who
ask us to go into an All-Ireland Parliaand
ment
surrender
sedition and murder we say "Never."
had our way we should never have
had this Act in either North or South.
Nothing could be conceived that will
bring more blessings to Ireland than the
Act of Union, and a thorough working
of that Act. When we were appealed
to by the British people after the war to
make some great sacrifices for peace, and
to satisfy the sentiments of Irish
nationality so called, we consented to
accept a Parliament for the area known
as Northern Ireland. We therefore
started housekeeping on our own
account, and we were agreeable that the
people of Southern Ireland should do the
same. And to bridge the chasm between
there was
to be the Council of .
Ireland to discuss matters that
mutually concerned both of us.
But when we were asked to make
this sacrifice there were two promises
held out to us by the British people and
the British Government. Number one
was an expressed condition that Ulster
was not to relinquish her representation
in the British House of Commons and
her direct citizenship in the Empire.
The second promise given to us, written
in every line of the Act, was that under
no circumstances would any concession
or privilege be given to one Parliament
in one part of Ireland without being
given to the other. In other words we
in the North were to be on equal terms
with the Southern Parliament. We
wish to re-state these facts because in the
doped Press of Great Britain they are
in the attempt to
largely lost sight of
to the forces of
Mr. COOTE: I wish to contribute my
quota of congratulation to the Prime
Minister on the able and lucid statement
made to us here to-day. He has done
much to clear the air in re-stating the
position of the Ulster people before the
world in such unmistakable language
that I hope and believe it cannot
possibly be misunderstood. We congratulate him and the other members of
the Cabinet on the stand they have taken
in this matter. We congratulate them
in the name of the Ulster people who
are deeply opposed to any collusion cr
collaboration with Sinn Fein, with a resentment stronger than ever was shown
against any Home Rule Acts. There
are two points I should like to emphasise
as being necessary to keep before the
British public when we are dealing with
the Ulster question, because we are
as unrepenting Unionists as ever. If we
We
capture British opinion. With all that
has happened in the conference between
the Prime Minister of Northern Ireland
and the Prime Minister of Great Britain
we have nothing so far as our own Prime
Minister is concerned to do but to express our thanks and gratitude.
think that viewing the whole situationat least personally I have always held
the view and hold it more strongly now
than ever, that it was a mistake for us to
have anything to do with this conference,
and I think it is a mistake to continue
the negotiations with a lot of men who
are unrepentant murderers, and who
have not publicly avowed allegiance to
the King. I think our Prime Minister
has truly and properly described the
situation when he said it is an insult to
the Ulster people to be asked to go into
a conference on such terms. Reference
has been made to the mentality of Sinn
Fein and what might be their attitude
in the crisis in which they find them-
U
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
selves. I agree with what the Prime
Minister has said that the Sinn Feiners
have their own troubles. I agree probably
for another reason, because I believe
that the Sinn Feiners have not the determination of their own line of action.
They are driven by malefactors, and all
the time, whether Sinn Feiners, or any of
the other revolutionary parties are
in power, the great dominating factor is
sacerdotalism. When sacerdotal authority
overrides the civil law in any country,
there must be trouble. That is the source
of the trouble in Ireland. It is the ecclesiastical authority, especially in Southern.
Ireland, trying to dictate to the Government of the day what they should do and
what they should not do. This will always
be the case so long as the ecclesiastical
authority has a grip of the minds of the
people of Ireland. Therefore let us face
the facts. When we talk about Sinn Fein
remember there is a great boycotting campaign going on to destroy the liberties not
only of Ulster but of the British Empire.
It is a bigger machine than appears on
the surface, for although Sinn Feiners are
nominally at the head they cannot take
whatever direction or course their minds
might suggest. Therefore let us realise
this fact that whatever troubles there are
in Ireland, and whatever troubles may
still be in the womb of the future, the
responsibility for these troubles does not
rest with Sinn Fein, but with the forces
behind Sinn Fein.
Passing from this, I should like to come
to the question of law and order, and I
wish it to be recognised at once that in
any criticism I wish to make I am not
blaming the Cabinet of Northern Ireland.
They had merely the shadow without the
substance. They were in a most humiliating position, and had the sympathy of
every right-minded person in Ireland.
Now law and order have passed into their
hands, and I do hope that the strong
language and sentiments expressed by the
Prime Minister will be registered in
action to the very letter. We assure him
the people of Ulster are behind him in
his determination to restore law and order
at all costs, even to the cost of denying
ourselves some of the necessaries of
life. We have a huge system of boycotting over all Ireland, and very particularly
over the six counties. It has developed into
a religious boycott. In every village an attempt is being made to boycott the loyalist
and Protestant traders and shopkeepers,
and also Roman Catholics who refuse to
obey the dictates of Sinn Fein. This
boycott has occurred in every village.
Sinn Feiners hold their courts. Mere
corner-boys control the whole thing, but
they have got the power to terrorise the
people-the power of the revolver. And
so they dictate the policy of Roman
Catholic people coming into a village to
do their ordinary business. They
dictate to them as to the people
with whom they shall trade and
shall not trade; they terrorise them
and put a stoppage to the free exercise of their rights. Many Roman Catholics
would dearly love to trade with the concerns with whom they have been doing
business for twenty years, but under the
dictates of these people, who are of no
account in the district, they are terrorised,
and their free action is forbidden. I can
say if this thing is not gripped very soon
by the authorities there will be trouble.
The Protestant people of these districts
will give these boycotters a little of their
own sauce. Why should they not? I can
see every butcher and blacksmith put out
of trade by this cruel, reckless boycott
which is now put in force by Sinn Fein.
I do hope the Minister of Home Affairs will
soon get his Department into full working
order, and see that the constabulary are
strengthened so that they may pacify the
country. What are the facts in connection with the Royal Irish Constabulary?
How can law and order be maintained
when members of that force have gone to
these Sinn Fein Courts and found them
at work? They have dispersed them,
but dare not arrest those who hold them.
There is the truce. You cannot bring
them before any British tribunal. A
more humiliating state of things one could
hardly conceive.
Since the truce, so-called, if a County
Inspector of Constabulary has to remove
some of his Special Constabulary from one
police barrack to another, he must first
give notice to the Sinn Fein liason
officer. He cannot change his men from
one barrack to another without acquainting that Sinn Fein officer. How can you
expect law and order to be maintained
under those circumstances? You will
only grip that situation by adopting the
spirit of the Prime Minister, and counting
no cost too much if it achieves the desired
result, so that it will be possible for every
decent person, Protestant and Roman
Catholic, to sleep comfortably in his bed
at night, and go about his business during
Adjournment.
Adjournment.

during the past thirty or forty years is
this and they have struggled under
great difficulties and made many sacrifices,
and their views and opinions are to-day
stronger than they have ever been during
the past thirty or forty years-it is this,
consideration
will they
the day. We are tired of this agitation.
We are tired of this play-acting with

British Government : "Here you have
given us a Parliament, and you have given
that under no
the promise of the security of our
rights, which we are determined to enjoy

us
you
We are
going to take care of our business, and
develop our resources under the Union
Jack, and in loyalty to the King and
Constitution of Great Britain. We are
not going to interfere whether you treat
with Sinn Fein or not.
If you wish to
treat with Sinn Fein then treat with them
in their own area. We offer no opposition, but we ask for some protection for
the Loyalists who are being driven out of
the South of Ireland, and who at present
seem of no consideration to the British
Government. After have given some
measure of protection and compensation to
these people, we say do as you please with
the South of Ireland, but we have a right
to the same self-determination, and we
shall maintain that right. In
any
arrangement that is come to, we shall
maintain our connection with the British
Parliament, having our membership in it
as of yore. No part of our citizenship
must be taken from us, and we shall hand
on our liberties to our children. Ulster
will always be an outpost of the British
Empire, and one of the most loyal parts
in His Majesty's Dominions.'
""
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: I find
some difficulty in rising to address this
House, because of the fact that so much
ground has already been covered that it
is almost impossible for me to say anything absolutely new upon the various
subjects now before the House. There is
one thing, however, that it is absolutely
necessary to emphasise, and it is this.
The Prime Minister has given us a very
illuminating, a very full statement of the
negotiations which he and his colleagues
have been engaged in during the past two
weeks, and I think I am only expressing
the unanimous opinion of the House,
and of the constituencies that
we represent in this House, when I say
that the Prime Minister in his negotiations with the British Cabinet has most
accurately expressed the mind, the views,
and the fixed determination of the people
in the Northern area. It is necessary to
bear in mind to-day one or two things of
supreme importance in considering this
whole question. One of the things that
the Ulster people have contended for
consent to place their destinies, civil and
religious, commercial and industrial, in
the hands of the men who would control a
Parliament established under existing
conditions in Dublin. That is the cardinal
principle of the whole thing. We have
always loved the Union, and we prefer it
still. It is hardly necessary for me to
remind the House that, after the long
struggle of 30 years, in 1920 we were faced
with a
The
very difficult problem.
problem was whether we would accept a
Parliament for ourselves, much as we
preferred the Union, or be driven into an
All-Ireland Parliament. With much misgiving and doubtfulness we accepted a
Parliament for ourselves, and we were
then assured by British Statesmen that
that was the last sacrifice we would have
to make; that when we accepted that
the laws
Parliament, and all
that
appertained to that Parliament, we had
the word of British Statesmen that so far
as we are concerned no further interference would be made with our powers
and liberties. What did we do? We
accepted that Parliament in the best of
His
faith, and we proceeded to work it.
Majesty the King opened it, and it is
hardly necessary for me to recall his
memorable words on that occasion. What
happened then? A similar, identical
Parliament, with the same powers, the
same rights and liberties, was granted to
the South of Ireland, and we are not going
to accept any less liberties, rights, and
privileges, than those now proposed by
the British Government to be given to the
people of the South.
What course did they follow ?
Instead of working their Parliament,
as we have done ours Sinn Fein
declined to function it. They started
a campaign of terrorism, of outrage, of cruel oppression, of assassina-
Lion in order to defeat the aims and
objects of the British Government, and
what do we find happened? In July,
almost immediately after the Northern
Parliament had functioned, the Prime
Minister issued his famous letter of July
20 to Mr. De Valera, making certain
suggestions and certain proposals to him
as representing Southern Ireland. Now,
let us bear this point in mind, that up to
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Sir Robert Anderson.]
this moment
the cardinal principle
underlying the Act of 1920 in all its
clauses was this: the absolute equality
between the two Parliaments and the
absolute equality not only in the
different functions pertaining to those
Parliaments, but the territory was just
as equally sacred in connection with the
arrangements then made as any other
part of the Act. We are told to-day that
there is to be no interference with the
privileges the special privileges-that
were granted to Ulster in that measure;
Ulster got no special privileges that the
rest of Ireland did not get, absolutely
none-but when these privileges were
granted they were granted equally to
both, and the foundation principle of
both Parliaments is their absolute equality
both in regard to territory and to powers.
And it would be a violation of the
Act if something else was introduced
in the British House of Commons and
passed into law that would nullify or
cancel or interfere with the powers that
were then given to Ulster under the 1920
Act.
In
Now we come to this famous letter.
Let me say here--and I am only repeating what I said in my first speech in this
House certain services were reserved to
the Imperial Parliament. It is well for
us to bear this in mind. Customs and
Excise, Income Tax, Post Offices, and
Army and Navy, all these things were
reserved to the Imperial Parliament.
his letter of July Mr. Lloyd George, representing the British Cabinet, proposes
to hand these things over to Southern
Ireland at least the offer is made to
Southern Ireland. It is well to remember that while British Statesmen today are telling us that if all these great
privileges and advantages are given to
Southern Ireland they may be withheld
from Northern Ireland. That strikes
right across the principles laid down by
Mr. Lloyd George himself. What does
he state? After stating the different
items that are now to be handed over instead of being controlled by the British
Government, he tells us:
"The British Government will therefore
leave Irishmen themselves to determine by
negotiations between them "-
contemplating evidently the Council of
Ireland taking these functions and doing
its workwhether the new powers which the Parliamentary Act defines shall be taken over by
Ireland as a whole and administered by a
single Irish body, or taken "
-and these are the words I want to
impress upon the House-

or taken over separately by Southern Ireland and by Northern Ireland, with or without joint authority to harmonise their
common interests."
There it is clearly laid down as one of
the cardinal principles in the start of
the negotiations that these additional
powers, vital powers, as far as we are
concerned, were to be handled by
Northern Ireland or by Southern Ireland, and there is no difficulty whatever
to my mind in all these additional powers
that are now in dispute being handed
over to the Northern Parliament to deal
with SO far as they affect their own
territory. That is one of the chief
things we have to bear in mind.
At the same time the invitation was
given to the Premier of Northern
Ireland, which he accepted. But
what do we find? Although the
invitation was accepted, the Prime
Minister of Northern Ireland was never
called into consultation at any stage until
the British Government had made up its
mind in regard to certain proposals that
vitally affected the Province. I hold that
that was a very unfair position to place
the Northern Government in. The Prime
Minister has told us to-day-and we respect his views that we ought not to do
anything in any way that would endanger
negotiations, but what is the position as
we view it, as some of us view it in any
case? We are told by the Lord Chancellor, and by the Leader of the Unionist
party, Mr. Chamberlain, that to disclose
the correspondence that has passed between the Ulster Cabinet and the British
Cabinet is a thing that could not be
thought of, because it might endanger
everything. I wonder what they are
ashamed of. Is there anything in that
correspondence that cannot see the light
of day? The Prime Minister has told us
that so far as he is concerned he is for
open diplomacy in regard to this whole
matter. He is prepared to put all the cards
on the table and give to the whole world
the position that Ulster occupies. I cannot understand why the British Cabinet
is not equally willing to put before the
British public all the correspondence too,
so that all the people, not only of Great
Britain and Ireland, but the world, will
understand the true nature of the present
position.
I should like to see the correspondence
that passed between the Sinn Fein dele-
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
29 NOVEMBER 1921.
gates and the British Cabinet. It would
be interesting to know whether they have
conceded the right of allegiance. It would
be interesting to know what proposals
they have made. There cannot be only
one side to this question. There is
another. My complaint is that the dice
has been loaded from the outset against
the Northern Parliament. I wonder, if
there has been such secrecy, where the
information came from.
It could only
come from the Conference room or somebody that got the information from
the Conference room. Suddenly we
will wake up one day to find that
the whole press of the United Kingdom
are out against Ulster, to prejudice Ulster,
and to interfere with Ülster's position
before the public. It reminds me of the
shock troops that the Germans used in
connection with the war to try and force
a decision at once, and to try and secure
victory and take the other party by surprise.
To-day we have received from the Prime
Minister just as much information in regard to the negotiations as we had yesterday. We have had two sentences practically giving the position of the British
Cabinet. Is that reasonable or fair? One
of them says that Ulster is not to be
sacrificed or compromised. If Ulster is
not to be sacrificed or compromised why
are the negotiations going on on the lines
that have been indicated? Evidently we
must take these phrases for what they
are worth. But I would like to say this
much, that there is a strong feeling abroad
that Ulster has not been fairly treated by
the British Cabinet in regard to the Press
campaign that has been engaged in by the
Government Press on the other side with
some few honourable exceptions.
may I be permitted to say that all Ulster
feels grateful to the "Morning Post" for
the magnificent way it has stood by Ulster
and by Unionist principles.
And
I think we have to consider how we
stand in regard to these reserved services.
A great many people, I am afraid,
throughout the Province, when you tell
them about the reserved services don't
know exactly what you mean. Twothirds or three-fourths of the entire
tax revenue of Ireland comes from this
source. A very large proportion is collected in the Province of Ulster.
And we
are asked to hand over all these moneys
to the control and direction of a Parliament in Dublin. That is the proposal,
and we are to have control without finance
except as it is doled out by the Parliament
established under the control of Sinn Fein.
I take leave to say that this Parliament
would be committing suicide, and the
people of Ulster would be committing
suicide, if they consented for one moment
to proposals of such a far-reaching character. We have a right to control
all our own moneys collected in our own
area, and not to hand them to Sinn Fein
authorities in an All-Ireland Parliament
with full taxing power. I am afraid no
matter what securities we get I have
not a scrap of faith in any of these paper
guarantees; and Sinn Fein will give us a
basketful of guarantees and all that sort
of thing-where there is no goodwill
paper guarantees are not worth very
much. I think the only guarantee that
we can possibly accept, that we claim, is
that if these services are going to be transferred to Ireland, Ulster must get the
same right, the same treatment as the
South of Ireland, so that we shall have the
handling of our own money collected by
our own authorities.
We
It is sometimes said that we want to
interfere with Southern Ireland, that we
want to coerce her Nobody wants to
coerce Southern Ireland. They have received their Parliament with the same
powers and rights that we possess. We
wish them well. We have nothing but
the kindliest feelings towards them.
desire to see all Ireland prosperous, happy
and contented. And in the Council of Ireland, as the Prime Minister has pointed
out, we can meet on equal terms, and all
those matters that are now in dispute can
be discussed with our friends in the South
and West of Ireland. We have powers to
pass laws that we are agreed upon, and
let me say that it is along those linesand the Prime Minister in reading the
letter of General Smuts put the position
in a nutshell-it is only upon those lines
that there can ever be unity or harmony
or joint co-operation in this island home
of ours. Statesmanship does not lie in
the direction in which the British Cabinet
wants us to move to-day. To force or
coerce Ulster into an All-Ireland Parliament will only mean disaster to Ulster,
disaster to Ireland, and disaster to the
Empire itself. Better to move along the
other lines of getting into the Council of
Ireland, where we may get a better understanding of each other. seek to study
together the different problems that affect
Ireland as a whole, and in that way slowly
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Sir Robert Anderson.]
but surely we may move in the direction
of unity, peace and harmony. I am
perfectly certain that this Parliament representing the Northern area will do its
part loyally and faithfully to work the
Parliament that is given to us, and that
any additional powers that may come to
us will be worked by us fairly in the
interests of all classes of the community.
I am certain that the Prime Minister and
those associated with him will, in trying
to lay the true foundation of a wellordered society, basing it upon law and
order, show no discrimination
of any
kind whatsoever, but that they will
exercise the functions in such a way that
they will see right, and fair play and
justice done to all classes in the
community.
I can say this much, that the Prime
Minister's statement to-day has
strengthened his hold upon the affections
and loyalty of the people of Ulster.
Had he or his Cabinet acted in any
different manner from the way they
have acted, this House would have
hurled them from office in twenty-four
hours, and they would have behind them
the masses of the people throughout the
Province of Ulster. But I believe after
the Prime Minister's statement to-day
they will feel and realise that in the
future negotiations that may take place,
and in all future developments that
may arise he will preserve the rights and
liberties of this Parliament that is now,
I believe, firmly established, and that he
will also secure the rights, liberties, and
privileges of the Province of Ulster and
all its interests, civil, religious, industrial and in all other matters that
pertain to the welfare of the great community that has always proved its loyalty
and devotion to high principles and
loyalty and devotion to that great
Empire that we are proud
proud to
associated with.
be
Mr. CRAWFORD: It is within the
recollection of the House that my constituents called upon me on a previous
occasion to enter my protest and record
my vote against certain proposals made
by this Cabinet. Since I performed
that action I have received undoubted
proof that I interpreted the feeling of
my constituents thoroughly. I feel that
I would be interpreting their minds
quite as thoroughly when I say that I
agree fully with the action of the Prime
Minister and the Cabinet in the
negotiations they have carried through.
It may be some people thought that
action of mine and two or three others
on that occasion indicated a rift in the
lutė. If SO
mistaken.
they are
We
stand this afternoon and with one voice
say we admire the courage, the patience,
and the ability of the Prime Minister
and those associated with him in the
carrying out of these negotiations. I
feel certain I am speaking for the people
throughout County Antrim when I say
there has been and will be a chorus of
approval of the action they have taken,
and that behind Sir James Craig and his
Cabinet there will be a united House and
six counties. I have been greatly
pleased to hear of the Prime Minister's
determination to secure law and order.
I say again that the law should be made
a terror to evil doers. I was delighted
to hear him say that. Although I have
been instructed by those whom I represent to criticise any financial statement
put before this House, I believe I am
speaking the mind of this House when I
say they are prepared to vote the money
necessary to carry out law and order; so
that behind him in this matter the
Prime Minister has those who a few
weeks ago he might have considered
were against him.
Mr. McGUFFIN: I only intend, to
occupy a few minutes and use remarks
strictly relevant to the very important
statement submitted to the House by the
Prime Minister. We came here to-day to
hear something with regard to the disclosures made to our own Prime Minister
by the Prime Minister of the British
Parliament, in respect to the negotiations
going on between the Cabinet and Sinn
Finn representatives, and if we confine
our remarks to all that is disclosed this
afternoon there is not much room for
great discussion. I rise, however, to say
this much-that though at the outset of
the proposal to enter into negotiations I
Idared to differ with the Prime Minister
with regard to the wisdom of entering into
a conference--I still entertain that view
-I am not weakened in the slightest
degree in my loyalty and devotion to the
Prime Minister. I am afraid that there
has been a misinterpretation put upon
my attitude. I would like to assure the
Prime Minister, as I did on a public
occasion, that whether his policy, as he
determined it, was right or wrong, I
would follow loyally his lead.
doing so to-day. I had misgivings about
the conference from beginning to end.

And the reason I had these misgivings was
this that if the Bill that was offered to
us, and that has now become an Act, had
been faithfully interpreted by the British
Prime Minister and the Cabinet there
would have been no need whatever for a
conference.
The Bill as it was drafted
really had in anticipation that a crux like
the present would arise, and, accordingly,
a clause was put into it as an alternative
to the refusal of the representatives of
Southern Ireland to operate the Act.
No British Government is justified in
saying that anything that has developed
up to the moment has justified them in
ignoring the clause that was put in as a
special provision to meet this contingency.
And then in order to escape from the consequences of their own act they invite a
conference to consider what concessions
we, as the Northern Parliament, would
make to the demands of Sinn Fein. The
demands have been all on the one side.
The concessions up to the present have
been all on ours. We never wanted the
Act that is now on the Statute Book.
What we wanted to do was to maintain
our alliance and our association with the
Parliament of Great Britain. That has
been denied us, and on the advice of our
late leader, Sir Edward Carson, now
Lord Carson, we came to the conclusion
that it was necessary in our own interests
that we should accept our Parliament, in
order that we might no longer continue to
be a shuttlecock between the two parties in
England. We accepted it as an alternative, and Lord Carson said if the Government were prepared to put the Act on
the Statute Book we were prepared to
loyally operate it. We are fulfilling that
promise now, but we are asking the
Government to assist us to carry out our
terms by the due transfer of those powers
that have been provided for in the Act.
Up to the moment we have not had
those powers transferred as we were entitled to expect, although we have the
assurance of the Prime Minister that
they are on the way of being transferred,
and that in the early part of next year
they will be fully transferred.
My objection to enter the Conference
was this, that there could be no Conference of a genuine and reasonable
nature where we were not prepared to
make concessions on both sides. You
cannot have a one-sided Conference, a
Conference with the demands on the one
side and the
concessions on the other.
You cannot have a Conference of that
sort, and to agree to come into a Conference at all implied we were prepared
to make concessions. I do not see under
the circumstances how we can make concessions or be justified in making them,
and that is the reason why I had the
temerity to differ with the Prime
Minister in regard to the wisdom of going
into the Conference; but in regard to
the action he has taken in connection
with the Conference I am absolutely unequivocal in regard to my admiration of
it. No man could play a straighter
part; no man could be more loyal in the
action he has taken. We admire him,
and we are going to trust him, seeing he
is there. My hon. Friend, the Member
for Derry, has said all the disclosures
that have been made do not amount to
much. The Prime Minister himself in
the course of his speech hinted that we
nearly got all in the Press that he was
prepared to disclose here. We understood an agreed statement was to be
read. It is of a very brief character, but
the most encouraging part of the whole
of it was that the Prime Minister has
definitely put down his foot, and has delivered what to my mind is an
ultimatum in regard to time.
It is nearly time it was brought to an end.
it has been protracted for many months,
and the only thing is it could have been
got over in a much shorter time. If they
had introduced these questions at the
outset of the conference the conference
would have been upset long ago. If the
question of allegiance had been introduced
and decided forthwith, it must have ended
the conference immediately. We have no
assurance up to the moment that the Sinn
Fein representatives have altered their
attitude in the slightest degree in regard
to their demands. They have said.
nothing with regard to allegiance. They
are insisting on the full demand for a
republic so far as we know, and yet representatives of the Government are
meeting them still in conference: to decide
what? To my mind it has been to try
and induce them to take a lower position
than that which they are bound to take if
they represent the demands and views of
Sinn Fein, which they say they represent.
I do not know what is going to come out
of the Conference. You hear a good many
anticipations and a good many forebodings.
There is one thing sure that we all trust
our own Prime Minister in this matter.
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. McGuffin.]
We have good reason to trust him. There
is no man who understands Ulster's cause
and is prepared to stand by Ulster's interests more than the Prime Minister. He
has been associated with her in all her
great troubles and in all her conflicts for
the past twenty years, and we have absolute
conndence in him in this matter. The
question has got to be solved. I can assure
the Prime Minister we have made up our
minds that we will have the Act as framed,
as we have agreed to accept it, and as the
British House of Commons has legislated.
We will have nothing less than that.
If we stand firm by our own decision in
this matter and in our support and loyalty
to the Prime Minister, I have every reason
to believe that the thing will work out to
our satisfaction. I am speaking this evening as representing what are called the industrial classes in this city, for whom the
Prime Minister has the greatest possible
respect. It is a great thing to know that
no class interests are represented in the
support that is being given to the Prime
Minister at the moment. He has universal support. The merchant traders, the
business men of all sorts, the democracy
in all its aspects, are behind his back, and
he has decided, following the transfer of
the powers for law and order, to organise
a force that, without threatening the Sinn
Fein party, will be sufficient to protect the
interests and security of the people here in
the Northern area. The members of the
force that is to be organised will be drawn
from the democracy of the city.
The
democracy of the city are absolutely with
the Prime Minister, and if he makes a call
upon their loyalty, their allegiance to his
leadership, and their courage to carry out
his views, he will not make that call in
vain. We have suffered much, and we
have been very patient under the injunction of the Prime Minister. If it had not
been for the respect we have for his authority and advice in this matter, we would
have had a very serious fracas in the city
long ago. We have too much respect for
him to betray him when he makes a request of that sort, but we think the juncture has been reached when it will be possible for the Prime Minister, by his own
effort and wisdom, to give us all the security and protection that we are entitled to
expect, and having that we will be quite
satisfied.
Dr. MORRISON: I think that we are
entirely in agreement with everything that
our Prime Minister has said, and I am
certain that his speech will be read with
great satisfaction all over the Six County
area. We all knew that in matters of
high politics our Prime Minister knew his
way about, and his handling of this serious crisis has justified us in that view.
Some of the speakers have said that we are
not much wiser than before this House
met. I think we are. The Prime Minister
has expressed his satisfaction with and his
confidence in the position that Uister occupies, and it will be conveyed by his speech
to the whole of the people in the Six
County area. Without any disclosure, that
is quite sufficient for us all at this time. I
do not think there is any great need for
speeches after what the Prime Minister has
said, and I would not have got on my feet
but for one thing. He emphasised, and I
think very wisely, that we should be careful in what we say with regard to public
men in discussing this serious question. I
think the Member for Londonderry (Sir
Robert Anderson) was rather severe in his
references to some members of the British
Cabinet. He said the dice had been loaded
against Ulster. In my opinion, as far as
I can judge, that is not so. I have been
in this struggle since 1886, and I can never
forget that the first strong position that
we occupy has been wholly due to the present Coalition in England. There is absolutely no question that this Cabinet gave
us our Parliament, and having got our
Parliament, we occupy a stronger position
than we ever occupied in this great controversy since it first originated. I think that
the special reference that the Prime
Minister made to opinion in England and
Ireland was a warning to us that we should
be careful, because the men who seem to
be against us to-day are the men who may
be on our side to-morrow. We have seen
that in international politics; we have also
seen it in local politics, and it is specially
worthy of being remembered by us at this
particular crisis. Our position now is so
strong that I think we can face the future
with an absolutely easy mind. I can never
forget that before the passing of this Act
of 1920 the Coalition Government resisted
pressure-pressure so strong and so determined that many a time I was unhappy
and uneasy lest the Government would
give way. They had to resist pressure not
only from Radicals, but also from Lord
Middleton and his followers in Ireland.
That pressure was successfully resisted,
and this Bill was placed upon the Statute
Book, leaving us now in an unassailable
position. As far as I can judge, the Ulster
people may be quite happy and contented
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
29 NOVEMBER 1921.
with the prospect that lies before them.
With firm administration of the law, and
full use of the powers that are now conferred on us, we will be able to put down
crime and establish law and order, and
make our Ulster area what we all hope it
will be prosperous, free, and ultimately
content and free from crime.
CAPTAIN
DIXON: I have to anthat the House will meet tomorrow for purely formal business.
nounce
Mr. DONALD: I merely want to ask
what progress has been made with regard
to the amendments of Standing Orders for
Private Bill legislation, and when local
legislation will be introduced.
CAPTAIN DIXON: A Committee has
been set up, and that will be announced
on Thursday next.
Question: "That this House do now
adjourn." Put, and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at five
minutes before Five of the
clock.
W
Notice of Motion.
Orders of the Day.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Wednesday 30th November, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
NOTICE OF MOTION.
LOCAL BILLS.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
That in the case of Local Bills to be
presented in the Session of 1922. it be
an instruction to the Examiner of Standing Orders for Local Bills not to report
non-compliance by promoters in respect
of any date fixed by such Standing Orders,
if, in his judgment, the non-compliance
was due solely to the date at which such
Standing Orders were adopted by Parliament."
THE CHAIRMAN OF WAYS AND
MEANS (Mr. Moles): It is unnecessary
I think to say more than a few words in
explanation of this motion. It is obvious
that where local bodies desire to promote
Bills they must not, either through
default or otherwise on the part of this
Parliament, be prejudiced in the rights.
which they would possess if promoting
such Bills in the Imperial Parliament.
All that this motion proposes is that
where the Standing Orders would be
affected by these dates non-compliance
shall not penalise the promoters of such
Bills. I beg to move.
Question put, and agreed to.
BILL PRESENTED.
Ministries of Northern Ireland.—Bill
to provide for the administration of
certain public services in Northern Ireland, by the Departments established by
the Lord Lieutenant, and for purposes
connected therewith.-[Captain Dixon.]
Read a First Time. Ordered to be
read a Second Time to-morrow, and to
be printed.
SALARIES OF THE SPEAKERS.
Resolved: "That this House will tomorrow resolve itself into a Committee to
consider the charge on the Consolidated
Fund in respect of the salaries of the
Speaker of the Senate and the Speaker
of the House of Commons."—[Captain
Dixon.]
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
Supply Committee.--Deferred till tomorrow.
Ways and Means Committee.--Deferred
till to-morrow.

BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
,,
Motion made, and Question proposed:
That this House do now adjourn.
[Captain Dixon.]
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: I would
like if the Government would give us some
indication to-day as to the course of
future business during the next three
or four days. I think it is very advisable
for the House to know exactly what
business will be undertaken during the
next three or four days, or the next week,
if they are in a position to inform the
House exactly what course they will take.
Captain DIXON: To-morrow the
House in Supply will discuss the report
on the salaries, which was laid on the
Table yesterday, and is now in the hands
of hon. Members. That discussion may
last over Friday. We are putting no
Government business on for Friday on
that account. On Monday the House
will go into Supply on Money Bills, and
we think it will probably last the whole
week.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: Do I
understand my hon. and gallant Friend
to indicate that he will be in a position
to-morrow to move the adjournment of
the House until the Monday or Tuesday
following?
Captain DIXON: It all depends upon
the condition of affairs to-morrow evening. If we have nothing to do on
Friday, and if the House has had full
time for the discussion of the Report,
which is in its hands now, we will then
adjourn the House until Monday.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON : Not
Tuesday?
Captain DIXON: Or Tuesday. In all
probability, on the other hand, the
business may be such that we will have to
Business of the House.
Adjournment.
30 NOVEMBER 1921.
carry it over Friday, but I will try to let
my hon. Friend the Member for Derry
(Sir Robert Anderson) know in plenty
of time to get away if possible.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: That is
not exactly what is in my mind.
Mr. SPEAKER: A question of order
arises on this point. Perhaps I should
remind the hon. and gallant Gentleman
that if the House is not to sit on Friday
notice to that effect must appear at the
commencement of public business tomorrow.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I would like if it
were possible for the hon. and gallant
Gentleman to be a little more explicit on
that point. Assuming that the discussion
terminates to-morrow evening, can you
not give a definite assurance then that the
House will not meet on Friday. It is
very inconvenient for certain Members
who come from the North, and I think
their convenience and interests should be
consulted in the matter. If the hon. and
gallant Gentleman could say definitely
whether the House will sit on Friday it
would be very acceptable.
I
Captain DIXON: I think I have made
it perfectly clear, as far as I have been
able, that we cannot say definitely that
this will not continue over Friday.
think there will be very considerable
discussion on this Report, in regard to
the remuneration of Ministers. Then
we have the Speaker's Salaries Bill, and
it is impossible,
as Mr. SPEAKER has
pointed out, for me to adjourn the House
unless I give notice now, and I am sorry
to say I cannot give you notice now,
because I have no idea how long the
discussion will last.
Mr. COOTE: Would not it be in order
to adjourn till Monday, and then continue
the discussion on Monday?
Mr. SPEAKER: That would be perfectly in order if the Government put
down to-morrow notice that the House
will not sit on Friday, and will adjourn
from Thursday evening till Monday.
Mr. MILLER: I understood some time
ago that it was agreed that the House
would not sit on Mondays, but would sit
on Tuesdays. Some time ago that was
agreed to on the suggestion of the hon.
Member for Derry (Sir Robert Anderson),
and I understood that in future we
should not, except in case of emergency,
meet on Mondays. I understood that
the House would sit as a general rule from
Tuesday till Friday. I am sure that
would be more agreeable not only to the
country Members particularly, but to all
the Members.
Mr. DONALD: I would like to ask the
Minister of Home Affairs can he give us
any information about the latest shooting
outrage that has taken place in this city,
and what steps he has taken to prevent a
recurrence of this state of affairs?
THE MINISTER OF HOME
AFFAIR (Sir Dawson Bates): I have
already had a report in regard to the
matter referred to by the hon. Member'
for East Belfast (Mr. Donald), and I
shall have a conference with Colonel
Wickham this afternoon in regard to the
matter, every step will be taken to
protect lives and property.
Question: "That this House do now
adjourn," put, and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Half after Two of the Clock.
MOTOR TAXATION GRANTS.
Mr. CRAWFORD asked the Minister
for Home Affairs on 1st December if
arrangements had been completed for the
transfer to the Northern Parliament on
1st December of moneys collected for
motor taxation since 1st January last, and
if the classification of the roads and other
with the Ministry of Transport, whereby,
pending the final allocation of the Road
Fund, a substantial sum on account is
about to be placed at the disposal of the
Ministry of Home Affairs for distribution
to road authorities in Northern Ireland.
The roads classification schemes have been
completed, and the road authorities
concerned have been asked to send in to
the Ministry their estimates of expenditure under their respective schemes. I
have not yet received these estimates, but
when I do so I will be in a position to pay
the
the appropriate instalments
repliednecessary work has been advanced
sufficiently to ensure that an instalment
will be paid out of the Motor Taxation
Fund to the various local authorities
immediately after the 1st December.
Sir DAWSON BATES
Arrangements have been made by me
authorities.
to
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Thursday, 1st December, 1921.
The House met at a quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
ORAL ANSWERS TO QUESTIONS
PILOT STREET FERRY SERVICE.
Mr. DONALD (for Mr. Hanna) asked
the Minister of Home Affairs what steps
have been taken or will be taken to restore
the ferry service from Pilot Street, so as
to ensure that those employees of Messrs.
Harland and Wolff who arrive from the
country will expeditiously reach the
Queen's Island, and further what steps
have been taken to protect the lives of such
and others using that ferry.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): Owing to the treacherous and dastardly sniping by Sinn Fein of
the workmen using this ferry it has been
considered advisable, in the interests of the
workmen themselves, temporarily to suspend this particular ferry service. In
.order, however, to minimise the inconvenience caused to the workmen, the number
of boats on the lower ferry has been increased. The Pilot Street ferry will be reopened as soon as the measures of protection which are now under consideration
have been completed. It would not be in
the public interest to disclose these measures, but I may say that this question has
been engaging my personal attention, and
I am sparing no effort to see that every
possible protection is afforded those workers
who are carrying on their ordinary avocation. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
ROADS ADVISORY COMMITTEE.
Mr. COOTE asked the Minister of Home
Affairs if he will state the steps he has
taken in the establishment of a Road
Board, and when we may expect it to be
set up and at work.
Sir DAWSON BATES: As I explained
to the hon. Member for South Belfast (Sir
Crawford M'Cullagh) in this House on the
26th September last, I propose to set up a
Roads Advisory Committee for Northern
Ireland as soon as the necessary Adaptation
Order adapting the Ministry of Transport
Act, 1919, has been made, which I hope
will be very shortly.
TEMPERANCE LEGISLATION.
Mr. COOPER asked the Minister of
Home Affairs is he aware that two publichouse licenses were granted in Enniskillen
at the last Quarter Sessions for houses
where the licenses had lapsed for 15 and
3 years respectively, and whether, seeing
that the temperance party and the licensed
trade are agreed that further licenses are
unnecessary, the Government will forthwith introduce legislation to prevent the
creation of new licenses pending the introduction of its temperance proposals; if the
Minister for Home Affairs is aware of the
very extensive and increasing practice of
drinking methylated spirits, and whether
the Government is prepared to introduce
immediate legislation to have sellers of
such spirits licensed, and to have all spirits
exposed for sale adulterated with other ingredients to render same unfit for drinking
purposes, or what steps he proposes to take
in the matter.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The police authorities inform me that two public-house
licenses were granted by the last Quarter
Sessions in Enniskillen. In one case the
license had expired in October, 1910, and
in the other, October, 1920. These applications were opposed, both by the Licensing Trade and by the Temperance Association. As the law now stands, new licenses
can only be granted in respect of premises
which were licensed before the passing of
the Licensing Act, 1902, and I assume that
the premises in question satisfied this condition. I am making inquiries with a view
to ascertain whether the number of old
licenses which could be revived under the
Act of 1902 is sufficiently large to warrant
the introduction of special interim legislation. As regards the second part of the
question, I am informed by the police that,
so far as they are aware, there has been
no recent increase in the drinking of
methylated spirits, and an Inland Revenue
license is required for the sale by retail of
methylated spirits. The suggestion in the
concluding part of the hon. Member's question might be attended with considerable
danger, and would need the most careful
consideration.
TRADE FACILITIES ACT.
Mr. M'MULLAN asked the Minister of
Oral Answers.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
Commerce whether he can give any information to the House regarding the application to the North of Ireland of the
£25,000,000 scheme under the Trade
Facilities Act.
The MINISTER OF COMMERCE,
(Mr. Archdale): I am glad to say
that, as a result of negotiations between the Ministry of Commerce and
the Imperial Government, an assurance has been received that the interests
of the Northern Counties in reference
to this scheme can be regarded as
sufficiently safeguarded. Complete details
regarding the scheme can be obtained from
the Ministry of Commerce. I would like,
however, to take the opportunity of announcing to the House that under the Act
the Treasury are not empowered to make
loans, but only to guarantee the principal
and/or interests of loans. Before recommending the approval of an application the
Committee will require to be satisfied :-
:-
(1) That the undertaking or scheme
cannot reasonably be financed without State assistance.
(2) That it is of such a nature as to
reduce unemployment in the United
Kingdom, and, by increasing the means
of production or reducing their cost or
otherwise, to secure an adequate benefit
to the public in return for the public assistance given.
It is the intention that where possible
loans for which the Government guarantee
has been approved shall be offered for
public subscription. In such cases the
issue will be made by the Bank of England.
Preliminary applications under this Act
should be made as soon as possible to the
Secretary, Trade Facilities Act, Advisory
Committee, 7 Whitehall Gardens, London.
I need hardly say that the Ministry of
Commerce will give all assistance possible
in the preparation and submission of
schemes that will ensure the results contemplated by the Act, and it may be of
general assistance if applicants consult the
Ministry before submitting their propositions, so that the Committee in reviewing
an application may have before them as
complete a picture as is possible.
NOTICES OF MOTION.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE-
SUPPLY.
Resolved: That at this day's sitting, notwithstanding anything contained in Standing Order No. 15, other business have
precedence of the business of Supply.
(Captain Dixon.)
REMUNERATION OF
MINISTERS, ETC.
REPORT OF SELECT COMMITTEE.
Motion made and question proposed:
That this House doth adopt the Report of
the Select Committee on Remuneration of
Ministers, etc. (Laid upon the table November 29th.)
his
The PARLIAMENTARY AND
FINANCIAL SECRETARY TO THE
MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Barbour): I would like to preface my remarks this afternoon by apologising on behalf of the Prime Minister that he is still
detained in his house by the doctor's
orders, and so, very much to his own regret, is unable to be present here this afternoon to recommend to you the resolution
which has been confided to my care.
I am
glad to say that he is making good progress
-(Hon. Members: Hear, hear)—but we all
must realise the very critical times through
which we are passing, and the possibility
that may arise in the near future of even
more serious demands being made upon
strength and resources than possibly those
with which he has had to contend up till
the present. I feel it would be in accordance with the wishes of the House that the
Prime Minister should husband his resources in every way he can to be equal to
any emergency that may arise. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) My object in rising now is to explain the position of the
Government in respect to this Report from
the Select Committee on the Remuneration
of Ministers. We must all remember that
at the outset of our Government-here there
was a great deal of reconstruction work to
be done. We had to devise new departments and group under that reduced number of Ministries a great many of the old
Boards, which amounted in Dublin to upwards of twenty-three, I think, and which
had hitherto conducted the great work of
Government in this country. Incidental to
that was naturally the desire of economișing and yet at the same time to fix some
suitable remuneration for the Ministries
which are being set up On the one hand
we have to provide for the Government of
a very small area, and on the other hand
we have to consider the greater concentration of administration which has now been
thrown on the shoulders of those right hon.
Members who have accepted Ministries
under the Northern Parliament.
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
The great work of carrying on these
Ministries under normal conditions would,
I think, be sufficiently serious, but at the
present time wher the whole work of reconstructing and devising our new form of
Government is taking place it has added
very materially to the labours which have
been undertaken by these right hon.
Gentlemen. Added to that again
we have to take into consideration the exceedingly trying and
anxious times through which we are
passing. The Government naturally have
had to take to themselves the responsibility
of fixing the salaries of the permanent staff
and minor officials in the different departments which have been set up, and when
it came to determining the amount of
salary which should be attached to themselves they considered they would rather
leave the matter to the absolute discretion
of the House, and so in September a Committee was appointed by this House. A
number of hon. Members were selected
and invited to act on the Committee in
order that every phase of opinion might
be represented on that Committee. And to
those hon. Members who came forward in
response to that invitation I think the
thanks of the Government, and indeed of
this House, may be extended for the care,
the time, and the conscientious way in
which they carried out the duties entrusted
to them. They have made their report and
their recommendations, and these came as
fresh to the members of the Government,
and just as much without their knowledge,
as to the general body of hon. Members of
this House. The Government, having consented to the appointment of this Committee, feel that there is hardly any other
course open to them, were they anxious to
follow any other course, than to adopt the
report which has been made. And so I am
entrusted on behalf of the Government to
state that the Government are willing to
fall in with the recommendations contained
in this report, and to move that this report
be adopted.
We had hoped, when this report would
first be brought before the House, that it
might be brought before it in such a way
as would limit to the present occasion the
discussion not only of the principal but also
of the various items in the report, so that
when we come to deal with our estimates,
which will take up most of our time next
week, our attention might have been
focussed upon those much more serious
items which will be presented to you for the
Services under the Estimates. But I believe that to conform with Parliamentary
procedure if you adopt this report which I
am now proposing it will not in any way
restrict your liberty or preclude your criticising in any way you wish individual items
when they come before you as items in the
estimates. I would like to pay my tribute
once again to those hon. Members of the
Committee for the manner in which they
have discharged their duties. They have
gone into the matter very completely and
most conscientiously, and they have really
left no means unexplored whereby they
might compare conditions as they exist in
this Parliament with those of other Parliaments throughout the Empire in so far as
they are compatible with the conditions as
they exist over here.
I sincerely hope that in Ulster the sense
of patriotism and devotion to the public.
interest will always be a far more powerful
incentive to those members of the community who have perhaps the taste, and
possibly the talent, for assisting in the
conduct of public affairs, to come forward
and give their services to the community
than any pecuniary consideration which
this House may be prepared to vote. On
the other hand, I do not think it would
be a right thing to take advantage of that
public spirit in the minds of those in whom
it may exist and that they should thus be
placed at any disadvantage by reason of
coming forward and offering their services
on behalf of the general public. It is a
difficult matter to draw a real and just line
between a desire for public economy and
a just compensation for those who
may come forward'. We really do
want, especially in these first few
years of our history, to command
in the administration of our public
affairs the very best talent and ability we
can get. We want to recognise services
that may be given, and on the other hand
we do desire to study the interests of the
taxpayer. The burdens that are laid on
our shoulders now, whether in the form of
taxation or rates, have, in the minds of
most of us, almost reached breaking point.
But however patriotic any of us may be,
patriotism is not a very sustaining diet.
We have to consider the individual services
of those who may come forward to take up
Ministerial posts. There may be some to
whom the salary offered may be of small
moment, but on the other hand we may
have in the community men of privileged
talents but yet moderate means to whom
earned income, whether it is given or not,
may be the deciding factor whether they
place their services at the disposal of the
community or not. And so it is to strike
this balance I am sure the members of this
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
Committee have striven to accomplish, and
on behalf of the Government, possibly the
first to become recipients of the salaries
they have fixed, I am glad to say that they
fall in with the Committee's views and are
willing to accept their recommendations.
Therefore, on behalf of the Government I
move the adoption of the report.
Moved: That this House doth adopt the
Report of the Select Committee on Remuneration of Ministers, etc.
Dr. CAMPBELL: I rise to support the
resolution which is at present before the
House. The Committee were entrusted
with the task of drawing up a report for
presentation to the House. They all felt
the responsibility which was placed upon
them and they acknowledge the honour
which the House has done them in taking
them into.its confidence to that degree. The
Committee felt that they were responsible
to the taxpayer and that it was necessary
for them to consider the question of
economy as closely as they possibly could.
They also, however, recognised that they
had to consider the matter of efficiency,
and furthermore that the question was one
of appointing salaries to offices, not to men.
They had to keep clearly in their minds the
fact that they were dealing with Offices of
State and with offices of great importance
and dignity, and that however much they
might individually or collectively be imbued with ideas of economy they must have
due regard to the position of the offices for
which they were recommending salaries.
The Committee have endeavoured to go
into the matter as fully as they possibly
could and to get all the information which
was likely to bear on the result of their deliberations, and they have done their best
to base the claims of economy on the high
position of the offices for which they are.
recommending salaries. They hope the
majority of this House will say that they
have done what was fair, just, and reasonable towards both the taxpayers and the
offices for which they have recommended
salaries. I beg to support the resolution.

juncture the following amendment:-
To leave out all the words after "House"
and insert these words: "do recommit the
report to the Committee with instructions
to reduce the amount contained in the report by the sum of £4,300, made up as
follows:-
The 5 Cabinet Ministers £500

statemanwe be-
I wish to say at the outset on my own
behalf, and I hope on behalf of any Member of this House who may take exception
to the report of the Committee, that we
approach the matter in no hasty, or crying, or irritating spirit. We wish to approach the whole question on its merits.
Nor are we considering the men, but the
offices that are being set up. We are really
legislating for the future of this Parliament, and therefore I think we ought to
leave our minds free from all
cheese-paring, if I may use the
word, and might make
like provision, as far as
lieve the resources of our income will warrant us, for the permanent offices of State
in connection with this House. I wish to
congratulate the Committee, and I am very
sorry that I have to differ with them, but
we recognise the painstaking work of the
Committee and the great amount of research which they have brought to bear on
their labours. We quite understand the
great difficulties they had in keeping out
of their minds as far as possible the idea
that they were not legislating for the payment of Members of the British Cabinet,
but for offices in a minor Parliament and
in a small Government within the British
Isles which has, however, very great responsibilities to bear.
I am afraid the Committee were drawn
into the idea that we are bigger people
than we really are.
Of course we Ulster
people have big ideas of ourselves and of
our powers, but we must realise that the
whole income of this House is only something like £12,000,000, while the
British House of Commons budget for
anything from £1,000,000,000 to
£1,200,000,000. We wish to preserve a due
sense of proportion as we approach this
subject, not to be carried away on the one
Remuneration.
Remuneration.

HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
hand by inflated ideas
of our own
importance or to be induced on the other
hand to pander to the cheese-paring ideas
of anybody who may be outside and whom
we wish to placate at the coming elections.
We should banish from our minds all these
considerations-they are mean, unmanly,
and improper. We should not approach
the matter in one spirit or the other. We
should try to do the right thing.
When we take up this report there are
some things we agree with. For instance,
we agree with the figure stated—probably
if we had been asked, we would have stated
a smaller figure-for the Prime Minister.
We feel that the Prime Minister of this
Parliament has a position to maintain, a
dignity to keep up, a dignified and
onerous office to discharge, and has a
greater demand upon his purse than any
other Cabinet Minister. We agree. We
would not wish to see him put in a
secondary position financially, or a position
that would be looked upon as unworthy
of the dignity of a Prime Minister. There
is another office with which we do not find
any fault, the office of Attorney-General.
We think it is properly endowed, and we
should be very sorry if there was any cavilling about it. While the figure attached
to this office may strike ordinary people
outside as very high, we must remember
that it is one of the most responsible
positions in connection with carrying on
the Government of this country, and it
will be doubly responsible in the days that
are before us. Every legal action taken
for the maintenance of law and order will
have to be submitted to and pass through
the hands of the Attorney-General. His
is an office of great responsibility, and he
has our entire sympathy. We feel he
should have such remuneration as will
enable him to realise that he is a valued
servant of the Crown.
With these two exceptions we should
like to take up the report. In the first
place we take exception to the conclusions
of the Committee on page 5, clause 13—
"Your Committee wish to make a distinction between the salary of a Cabinet
