Minister and that of a political head of a
Department."
For the first time we meet with the idea
of a new office-the political head of a
Department. In this we do not recognise
any such person. In the "Belfast
Gazette," the Government asked that a
Cabinet of five should be set up; that five
Departments should be established, and I
think the Committee realised their position
when they used these words:
-:
"But although it may be going somewhat
beyond their terms of reference, your Committee wish to leave it on record that it is
their opinion that the headship of a Department should not, of necessity, entail Cabinet
rank."
There is an old saying that it is time
enough to meet trouble when it comes, and
when this House realises the necessity of
creating new Departments with a Minister
who may be without Portfolio, or who may
not have a seat in the Cabinet, then I say
the House will certainly accede to any
reasonable request that may be made, if
they find that it is necessary for the conduct of the business. In the meantime,
to forestall us they mention that matter
in advance. I say that in doing so they
went entirely beyond their jurisdiction.
We have to deal with five Cabinet
Ministers. There is to be no ambiguity
about the thing or about the arrangements
in connection with the salaries of these
five Ministers. It is suggested that we
should create a standing bonus of £2,500,
to be divided over five Cabinet Ministers.
In case there should be the head of a Department who is not a Cabinet Minister,
he is not to participate in this sum. This
is really a lot of padding thrown in by
some very clever permanent official who
has been manoeuvring the Committee, if
I
may say so— -I hope the Committee will
not think I am trying to minimise their
action-and by his astute clever manipulative mind brought in a very fine theory
that they are only getting £1,500, while
in fact they will get £2,000. To my mind
that suggestion is too involved and too
intricate. If the Committee wish to have
the Cabinet Ministers endowed to the tune
of £2,000 a year each, why do they not say
30? Why is there this juggling with figures
in the Report, that the man in the street
knows nothing about? That is what we
object to, and we believe the House will
not tolerate it. We are plain people, and
we look at this in a matter-of-fact way.
To set up this machine reminds me very
much of the Cat and Mouse Act that used
to be applied to the Suffragettes. We are
not going to have a Mouse and Cat Act for
finance, especially in connection with
Cabinet Ministers. I think it is degrading to the rank of Cabinet Minister to
have this sort of playing with figures in
order to give them a salary. If we want
to give them a salary let us do it straight
and honestly, and not go out of the way
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
to give an extra £500 a year that does not
appear on the surface to lie there at all.
In dealing with the subject of Cabinet
Ministers, the language that I am using
applies also to some extent to the Parliamentary Secretaries. I wish to endorse
the sentiment of the Parliamentary and
Financial Secretary to the Ministry of
Finance (Mr. Barbour), whom I congratulate heartily on his able conduct of the
business in the regrettable absence of his
chief (Mr. Pollock). I think he sounds
the true note, but should there not be
something of patriotism, especially at the
inception of this Parliament, and in the
appalling state of finance in the country.
and indeed in the world. Patriotism
must stand for something, and while I
agree that a man cannot live on patriotism, while it does not support an empty
stomach, it helps us through many a tight
corner and enables us to see the light of
day. It is patriotism that carries us
through, and Ulster has not lost its
patriotism. Will it be said that a Prime
Minister or a Financial Secretary drops
his patriotism once he comes into this
House and accepts office? I do not
believe it.
If we
For our part we wish to endow every
office reasonably and properly. I know I
shall be met with the inevitable answer
when I say that Cabinet Ministers are not
precluded from associating themselves.
from the business callings in which they
live and have been brought up.
asked, for instance, the Financial
Secretary to the Ministry of Finance to
turn his back on his business and other
interests once he came into Parliament,
and to devote his whole time to the duties
of his office at £1,000 a year, what would
he say? He would say, "A fig for your
£1,000. I will give it as a present to some
of your hospitals," And he would be
right. We do not give him this salary as
a fee for all-time services. We do not
need the whole-time services of any
Minister, save the Prime Minister. That
is the reason we do not cavil at his emoluments. He must maintain a sort of
independent control of the functions of
State, and he cannot dabble in the
ordinary affairs of life.
What would you think if the
Financial Secretary to the Ministry of
Finance was told that he could not attend
a Board of Directors, or take part in the
counsels of that Board, or direct the
great industries which we are glad to say
he does direct with such distinction and
success. To say that he must not do these
things would be utter nonsense, and if any
Member of this House has got this idea,
we want him here and now to get rid of it.
We are not so high and mighty in this
Northern Parliament. We are just
ordinary people, and are meeting ordinary
people every day. Whether he is a
Parliamentary Secretary or a Government
Whip, it does not give him a sort of ultra
dignity. It does not take away from the
fact that we are just human Ulster people
attending to our business and attending
to the business of the State.
That is my
conception of a Cabinet Minister, to
attend to the State first, and to attend to
his own business afterwards. Somebody
may say—“Yes, but then everybody is not
a member of a Board of Directors.'
We have no objection to any Parliamentary Secretary devoting his time to his
business and the affairs of the State, nor
have we any objection to a Cabinet
Minister-say the Minister of Agriculture
-going into his field to look after his men
and having a talk with them.
""
Mr. ARCHDALE: Supposing the men
are 90 miles away?
excellent
Mr. COOTE: He has an
motor car, and can very easily get over to
see them. He can direct the great operations and concerns controlled by him; he
will look at his annual account and attend
to the direction of his business, whether
agricultural or anything else, and will not
be the man I take him for if he does not.
Let us not get an exaggerated view of
the importance of this Northern Parliament and its Ministers. We are not to
shut ourselves away from the affairs of
If you do you are sillier people than
gave you credit for.
men.
[
Mr. ANDREWS: It has not been suggested that we should.
Mr. COOTE: I am delighted to hear
that statement from the Minister of
Labour. It is the practical common sense
which we all gave him credit for.
Indeed,
that being so, I think we are fully endowing these offices when we propose to
give them a steady £1,500 a year. We
are, I fear, exceeding the limits that we
ever expected we should go to. There
are many people who would like to cavil
at the amount, and we shall have some
X
Remuneration
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
trouble in getting our people, who take a
more extreme view, to come into line so
that we may strike a happy mean. I do
hope the Government will see their way
to accept this amendment, and recommit
the matter on these lines. We should
like to keep them popular with the people
they represent, and that they will be able
to return here and long enjoy the patrimony we are giving when these sums are
voted. We hope that they will agree
handsomely to it, and that there will be
no strife in our Northern Parliament.
When you come to the Parliamentary
Secretaries, these are not men who have
got the same responsibility. They are
men whose time will be largely occupied
during the time the House is sitting.
That is three or four months in the year.
But they have other intermittent duties,
and to say that they are going to give up
their other callings in life is utter
nonsence. They can engage in callings
which are in conformity with their
dignity. They can be secretaries; they
can be accountants-anything they like
in this city-and be Parliamentary Secretaries to Ministries in this House. Therefore, I say that most of us really believe
these amounts of £750 are really too high.
We do not cavil. Let there be unanimity.
We want to bring all our people with us
in this matter, and we want to do what
we conscientiously believe to be reasonable
for all the offices. I congratulate the
Committee that they eliminated the idea
of paid Assistant Secretary to a Parliamentary Secretary. This House took
strong exception to that before. It is,
perhaps, a painful subject, and I will not
labour the matter. We congratulate the
Committee on such common sense.
The
We come now to the most terrorising
part of the whole thing. We come to the
Government Whips. We have great
courage in trying to suggest any restrictions in the office of the Government
Whip. But we have a duty to perform.
It may be painful to some of us.
after-effects when the Whip comes down
upon us may also be painful. We have
the greatest confidence in our Whips, and
we are not doing anything personal in any
strictures we bring upon the office.
could not look into the face of the Parliamentary Secretary the present Parliamentary Secretary, and be angry.
keeps smiling all the time. He is a good
ellow. If exception is taken remember
there is nothing personal. We are considering the office in this country. Now,
I
He
I do say, in all seriousness, that £2,200 a
year is too much to pay for the Whipping
of Members of this House and of the other
House-far too much money for this
House and the Senate, consisting of under
100 Members in both Houses. Indeed,
many people consider that one Whip for
the House of Commons is enough. We can
conceive a time when the Whip is not
present, and when he ought to be represented. We want to do everything
reasonable and right as far as we can.
If we do not come up to the ideas of
certain representatives in this House let
them not think it is because we wish to
hurt them, or to offer them any indignity.
Nothing of the kind. It is because we
want to try and have some level and
principle through all the offices of the
House.
I submit that even £1,400 a year is too
large a sum for the Whipping of the two
Houses. Take, for instance, the Senate.
You are suggesting £600 for the Parliamentary Secretary to the Prime Minister.
No one has a greater admiration for the
noble Lord who fills that office than I
have. But personalities do not come in.
I say that for that Senate, which is sitting
only intermittently, and which has at
present adjourned sine die, the office is
almost a sinecure. Of course, it cannot
be Whipped from this House, because it
would interfere with its dignity. But I
maintain that £600 is too much for that
office, and we have had the temerity,
realising the justice of the claim, to
reduce that sum by at least £200.
Indeed, most Members who are questioning the acts of the Committee feel that it
should be cut down by half. But we
have put the figure at a reduction of £200.
And then the Parliamentary Secretary to
the Ministry of Finance-that is the Chief
Whip, we thought that £600 ought to be
quite enough to endow that office.
also consider that £400 is quite in keeping
with the endowment of the office of
Assistant Whip. I know that it will be
felt outside--I know that some will
suggest that the hon. Member for Tyrone
and Fermanagh has lost his head, and that
he has gone over to the spendthrifts, as
our people bluntly say. I do not mind
that. I will take the risk, even if I am
not returned again to represent them. I
want to do the right thing for the offices
of this House we are setting up. We are
legislating for those that come after. But
we must take our courage in our hands,
and we must, as I said before, have a
sense of proportion in all that we do.
We
Remuneration.
Remuneration
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
May I make another argument here? I
tried to bring out the idea which I hope
the House will agree with, namely, that
we do not wish our people or our Ministers
to work for patriotism.
Neither do we
wish to set up these extra well-paid offices
to be an attraction for the mere
carpet bag politician, or some fellow with
a glib tongue who will come and get entrenched in these offices. We are not
going to have that. We have them in the
British House of Commons. That is what
brings most misfortune on the country today--that there are men who are simply
there for the living they can make. We
want men to make a decent living for
the time that they are occupied. We do
not restrict them from any reasonable and
respectable occupation. We put no restriction upon them. But we do not mean
to allow any avenue to the more
political mountebank for coming into this
House, if he can bamboozle the people outside, in order to exploit the taxpayers and
the Ulster Parliament. No, we want
between all these divergent views to hold
an even balance, and to do the right and
proper thing in connection with the various
offices. I think that the Government are
agreeably surprised at the sweet reasonableness of the friendly critics in this
House when they see the reasonableness of
our amendment. I do appeal to the
Government not to permit this to go to a
division, because our amendment is put in
the most reasonable spirit in order to conciliate their views with the views of the
people who sent us here. There may be
more
extremists outside than in this
House, I know there will be, and, therefore, I do appeal to the Government to
seize the psychological moment; to do
the handsome thing, and put themselves
in a proper position with the people outside. I appeal to them to consider the
state of the country. I did not like, I
must confess, the sort of sneering laugh
that went up from some Members when I
said that we should not open any avenue
to permit a man to come in here to make
money in his office-to have that merely
as the object for which he came into this
House. I do not like that feeling, because
we ought to have more sympathy and
consideration for the state of things that
not only exists in Ulster, but exists over
the three Kingdoms, and over the world
to-day. I venture to suggest to the
Government, and I venture to throw this
down as a challenge-I venture to say that
you will have very few limited companies
in the three Kingdoms, as well as in
Ulster, which for the past year will
have a balance on their audited accountsa credit balance of £500 on the year's
trading before paying for officials at all.
We have been living on capital for the
last year; we have not been living on income. We have been living on the banks
last year; living on credit; living on our
good name. A good many of us do not
know what is going to happen in this
country. I ask the Government to accept
this amendment. In doing so they will
contribute practically to a distracted exchequer, to the state of things which prevails not only in Ireland-less in Ireland
than in England and Scotland. It will show
that this House wishes to do the reasonable
thing, not to rush to extremes in any
Department, and not to set up any source
of expenditure that moderate people
might consider unnecessary. I beg to
move.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: At the outset I would
like to offer my congratulations to the
hon. Gentleman, the Parliamentary Secretary to the Ministry of Finance (Mr. Barbour), on the admirable, and I might say,
the magnificent statement that he made
in moving the adoption of the report.
Certainly most of us who demur to the
proposals of that report have no fault to
find with his manner of presentation. It
has been done in a gentlemanly spirit and
in a most conciliatory one too. With regard to the hon. Gentleman who has just
sat down, and who has moved the amendment, I might say that he has obviated
the necessity for my re-traversing what
he has so admirably set forth in the form
of criticism. It will not be necessary,
and I am glad to think it will not be
necessary because I think there is quite
a number of Members in this House who
are very anxious to take part in the discussion. But I hope the time has not
arrived in respect to this House when it
will be impossible for Members to exercise
an independent opinion with regard to
any proposal that may be submitted to
the House. When we came into this
House as Members of Parliament, I say,
and I say it advisedly, it was not for the
sole purpose of supporting the Government. We have a duty to discharge to
the public at large. There are in this
area very considerable taxpayers indeed,
and, therefore, they have a claim on our
sympathy and our protection.
If we allocated to the Government the
them
right to introduce measures and pass
without criticism, I do not see why Par-
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. M'Guffin.]
liament might not be liberated and the
thing be confined entirely to the Members
of the Cabinet. We do not recognise that
such a course as that would be right.
Though critics of the Government in this
instance, we subscribe to the fundamental
policy of the Government the policy that
we were sent to maintain. We do that
ungrudgingly and right loyally. But I
think there is reason why those who differ
with proposals that are not only of a sweeping nature but of a permanent character
should be heard as to what they think of
the matter. When we subscribed to the
policy of the Government we are not to be
committed to the Government in detail.
That is the thing we want to resist. Take
the late war. There was scarcely a Member of the Imperial House of Commons
who did not accord his support to the
Government in going into the war. But
they did not waive their right to criticise
the expenditure in connection with the
war, and it was regarded by the House as
an
incumbent duty to scrutinise, and
scrutinise very carefully too, all expenditure in connection with the war. They
supported the policy, but claimed the
right to criticise expenditure in connection
with it.
There must be an Opposition in this
House if the House is to successfully
function. Nothing could be worse for
the community than to give them the impression that things are introduced into
this House and passed without scrutiny
or examination, and if the proposals contained in this report were regarded as
non-contentious, and passed through this
House without criticism, the public at
large would be alarmed at the ease with
which we take things and the reluctance
on our part to discharge what is a very
important duty. I thought in coming
into the House that whether there was to
be an established or recognised Opposition, there should be one group in the House
to which I should be said to belong.
refer to the Labour Group. Six gentlemen have been returned to the House to
represent that party. I do not know
whether our attitude represents the ideal
of those who returned us here, but whether
it does or not, I fulfil mv pledge and
maintain my independence in regard to
how I treat finance, and any other proposal
subordinate to the main policy of the
Government.
I
There surely can be no fault found with
us in trying to criticise the proposals in
order to secure economy. It was only the
other day that the Prime Minister, in an
admirable speech that he made in the
House here, spoke of what may be the
probable cost of maintaining law and order
in this country, and he even went so far as
to say that should it necessitate the bankruptcy of the Government they were determined to maintain law and order. We
commended the courage and the resolution of the Prime Minister in that matter.
We feel that it is one of those sources of
expenditure that the House would feel
justified in acceding to. But surely the
note that was sounded was a note of
economy, the necessity, in order that that
demand should be met, of seeing that all
the expenditure in connection with the
House was critically and carefully examined. Some of us had the temerity a
short time ago to criticise some of the
proposals that were introduced here of a
financial character, and to go into the
Lobby in opposition to the Government.
Now I have heard it said in the House
that Member is entitled to the right
every
of private opinion-(hon. Members: Hear,
hear) and to the liberty to express it
in the House, and the liberty, moreover,
of entering, if needs be, the opposition
Lobby to the Government. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) It is all very good to
give us the assurance that we are entitled
to that privilege, but I hope it is not
given ungrudgingly. I hope that when
we do it our actions will not be subjected-
I was almost going to say to scurrilous
criticism. If it has not been tried directly
it has been indirectly. There is no use
in encouraging Members of the House,
and encouraging Members who come forward and state plainly and boldly what
their opinions are, if their action in the
matter is to be discussed outside and
offensive epithets used in regard to their
conduct.
I do not propose going through the
various items that have been so trenchantly criticised by the hon. Member who
has preceded me. There is no necessity
for doing that because the case could
hardly be better stated. But I do, like
the hon. Member, admire very much the
suggestion of the Parliamentry Secretary
when he said that patriotism has its place
in this matter. If patriotism has no place
in it then there are at least certain Members of the Ministry who must have
entered upon their duties here conscious
of a very serious loss. I for one, and I
say it advisedly and after some delibera
tion, quite recognise in the case of some
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
Members of the Cabinet that they have
laid aside very serious considerations in
respect to their business in order to fill
the positions they occupy.
Take, for instance, the Minister of
Labour. The right hon. Gentleman has,
in my opinion, a very unique position
with respect to the government of his department. He enjoys the confidence of
the business classes and of the industrial
classes alike; and in coming into this
position I am quite convinced of this, that
he has been well equipped through experience to perform the very important
duties of it in a praisworthy and satisfactory manner. In his case I would not
hesitate for a moment to consider that the
acceptance of office has entailed great loss.
The same is true in regard to those who
have taken up minor positions; say the
case of the Financial Secretary to the
Minister of Finance. Now that is
another instance where I am quite sure
great sacrifice, and perhaps even
greater sacrifice than in the other instance, has been entailed in the acceptance
of office; and we have to recognise the fact
that the hon. Gentleman is conspicuously
qualified for the position that he has
undertaken. He is a recognised authority
on finance. He has very little to learn
in that direction from any person.
He
has controlled schemes and considered proposals of finance far in excess of what he
will be called upon to consider even if
Chancellor of the Exchequer. But we
recognise in that case there is undoubtedly
a sacrifice implied in the undertaking of
office.
Now with regard to the proposals as
a whole, I am not entering into a detailed
criticism of them. I would like to say
that they were the proposals of a Committee which on the whole did not commend itself to me by reason of its constitution. A very strange thing in connection with it was that it did not include
anyone of Parliamentary experience
though it was available here. Why were
representatives of the Imperial Parliament excluded from that Committee?
Was it assumed they might be too wise
or too experienced? Why were they excluded? I, for my part, fail to see why
at least some of them were not invited to
come in. I had no particular ambition.
I would not have consented even I had
been invited. It is rather strange. It
gives me the impression that after all the
Committee were hardly as wise or so well
informed as necessary to carry out their
task.
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE:
Might I interrupt the hon. Member for a
minute. I fancy two hon. Members with
Parliamentary experience were invited
but declined to act. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.)
have
mem-
But
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Very well. I was
not aware of the fact. Of course these
things can be carried on privately and the
House may not be aware of them. But if
they failed to elicit the co-operation of one
or two of those eligible there is no reason
why the invitation should not have been
passed round. However, as I have
already stated, I would not
been inclined to become a
ber of the Committee myself.
then this Committee I suggest by reason
of its incompetence and its inexperience—
appoints a sub-committee to go to London
to consult authorities upon the matter.
wonder whom they approached there.
We are told in the Report the names of
some of those that they approached, and
they certainly include great financial
authorities, but I would not suggest that
they were such as should be consulted in
respect to matters of economy.
I wonder
I
did they approach any anti-waste authorities in connection with the House of
Commons to ascertain whether their proposals were too magnanimous or too
small. I am disposed to think, perhaps,
they evaded those, and they consulted and
conferred with those that the anti-waste
Members of the House are out to penalise
and expose the heads of Departments.
I do not want to say anything with regard
to the way the items have been set out,
but there is one thing that does occur to
me as rather strange, and that has already
been commented on by my hon. Friend
(Mr. Coote) who moved the amendment
--that is, the form in which the Cabinet
Ministers salaries have been allocated. I
do want to emphasise this, that it would
have been much more explicit and much
more manly to have said they wanted the
Cabinet Ministers to be paid £2,000.
We could have understood that. But to
pay the Cabinet a sum of £2,500 to be
divided equally among the Members is to
my mind a ridiculous and a ludicrous proposal in connection with offices of such
dignity and importance. I wonder if
there is any precedent-and I speak under
the correction of some that have Parliamentary experience and perhaps legal
knowledge in this matter-for a Member
Cabinet being paid a salary.
he
I know it,
does not
of
As
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. M'Guffin.]
derive his salary by
by reason of his
being a Member of the Cabinet at all,
but by reason of being associated with
some specific office. Even when Mr.
Gladstone was Prime Minister of England
he was in some instances Chancellor of the
Exchequer, too. He drew nothing by
reason of being Prime Minister, or by
reason of being a Member of the Cabinet,
but he drew his salary with respect to the
office of Chancellor of the Exchequer.
And if it was not Chancellor of the Exchequer in the case of some of his predecessors then it was Lord Privy Seal, or
some other office. But in every instance,
even should it have been a sinecure, he was
associated with office, and was not paid by
reason of being a member of the Cabinet.
I do not think that that should have been
the form in which the recommendations
should have been set forth. I do not
demur to the £1,500 that has been suggested in connection with the office, but
1 say that the remuneration should be in
connection with the office, and not for any
position in connection with the Cabinet.
Another thing that occurred to me in
considering this question was what might
be underlying the proposal. I thought,
perhaps, it might have meant that there
was a prospect of introducing Ministers
without portfolio into it, and if that was
the intention then we as a House-I can
speak for myself in this matter--are
determined to resist it. There will be no
sinecures of that description in connection
with this Parliament if I know anything
about it. I have only got to say that so
far as the Parliamentary Secretaries are
concerned, I do not differ from the
criticism that has been offered by the hon.
Gentleman who has just sat down. Their
emoluments have been fixed at £1,000,
and it is proposed to reduce that figure to
£750. I think that under all the circumstances they would be well remunerated by that sum.
I do not want to
differentiate in respect of them, and say
that one is entitled to more than another.
I want to put them all, as it were, on a
perfect parallel, and to say that whether
their offices are more or less responsible
they are entitled to just the same remuneration. I think it has been very
fairly and justly fixed at £750.
On the whole I do not regard the
report as satisfactory. It has hardly met
the purpose we have in view in respect
of economy. I expected that in some
respects it would have been much more
moderate, but I am with my hon. Friend
(Mr. Coote), and perhaps in unison with
every Member of this House, when I say
in regard to the salary of the Prime
Minister that we are absolutely unanimous. He has earned more in respect
of his position and the duties he has discharged than the House is likely to repay.
It would be impossible to estimate the
advantage that his services have been to
us in the House, and I question if ever
there was a Prime Minister in the history
of the country who was called upon to
undertake such an arduous work, and
who has discharged it with such dignity,
courage, and fidelity. Therefore we do
not hesitate or equivocate in the slightest
way with regard to the emoluments to be
paid to the Prime Minister. On the
whole I think the Report is rather unsatisfactory. It might have been much
more moderate, and in view of the insistence by the House upon its economical
proposals from time to time I think I am
justified in the position I adopt here this
evening in seconding the amendment proposed by the hon. Member for Tyrone
and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote).
Mrs. CHICHESTER: May I ask the
indulgence of the House for a few brief
moments in order that I may explain on
general lines my views on the Report of
the Committee of which I had the honour
to be a Member. In doing so I fully
realise that the subject is not one which
makes a very great appeal to the sentiment of hon. Members, nor is it one which
lends itself very readily to oratory, had
I the gift. Any branch of finance-and
I speak with all deference for the feelings
of the hon. Member for Antrim (Mr.
Barbour)—cannot be looked upon as, perhaps, a very noble or inspiring subject.
But it is certainly a very vital, necessary,
and useful one, and a moment like the
present, with the taxpayers and ratepayers of the country groaning under
what one might almost call intolerable
burdens, is not, perhaps, the most
auspicious one for presenting a Report
such as we have had to present for your
consideration to-day. But it must be
borne in mind that the Report does not
represent additional taxation, although
it is, as I have said, a very formidable
document, embodying, as it does, our
suggestions regarding the salaries for
fourteen posts in the Government. But,
whether the moment is an auspicious one
or not, this Report had to be presented,
and whether hon. Members accept it in its
present form or amend it, the Report
Remuneration
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
must be looked upon as a bill or an
account presented for payment, which has
to be honoured either in its present form
or in some modified form. We fully
realised that the value of services to the
State could not be expressed in pounds,
shillings, and pence, and it was our hope
that to the service of our State and the
Government of Northern Ireland might be
given the best brains in man or woman
that Ulster can produce, whether that
man or that woman was sufficiently endowed with this world's goods or not, and
we felt consequently that it was absolutely
essential that we should set apart a sum
for the office and not for the individual-
(hon. Members: Hear hear.)-let there
be no mistake about that-which would
enable any man or any woman in the
future to hold office even if his or her
private means were a negligible quantity,
and to be able to uphold the dignity of
that office, to maintain his or her position
as a Minister of the State which we hold,
although small, to be inferior to none.
The Committee recognised the difficulties
from the very outset with which they
would be confronted, and they realised
that they were endeavouring to suggest
salaries for posts which were filled by men
who were not merely carrying on the
Government of the country, but who were
making and building a new State. It is
hard for the public to realise the amount
of work which must be involved in these
offices, and I feel certain that it must be
hard even now for some of the right hon.
Members of the Government to realise the
full extent of their duties. The public
is very apt to judge by results, but hon.
Members will perhaps remember that
owing to no fault of our own, owing to
no fault of Ulster, we had no results by
which we were able to judge or on
which we could base our suggestions.
It would only be necessary to mention
some of those departments as given in the
"Belfast Gazette" of June 7th, in order
to realise the vast responsibility that must
rest with each of them. I feel that it is
unnecessary for me to weary hon. Members by reciting all those offices, as they
know them so well themselves. The real
reason--there are many other reasonsbut particularly for the reason that conditions are so very different in different
countries we came to our conclusion. For
instance, there is the difference in taxation
in different countries. The difference in
the cost of living and general conditions.
all over made it impossible for us to draw
As a
comparisons with the Colonies.
woman I know that there is a general feeling that we are popularly supposed not to
be very well up in questions of rigid
economy-(hon. Members: No)—as far,
perhaps we may say, as matters connected
with our wardrobe are concerned; but I
also think that it is popularly supposed,
and I think rightly, that my sex have
always been greatly, strongly in favour of
rigid economy, and against extravagance
in administration. Perhaps I may plead
guilty to sharing the failings I have mentioned, but also, I hope, it will be believed
that I share the good qualities.
I would like to say that I consider that
we have not erred in any way, despite the
strictures passed by hon. Members, on the
side of undue extravagance. Remember,
and it is a point that must not be forgotten, that all the salaries set out in that
schedule, or practically all of them, will
be subject to income tax, and possibly to
super-tax, which will reduce them probably by very nearly half. Again, from a
woman's point of view-from a housekeeper's point of view-and I wish to
mention this to bring out my point regarding different conditions in different
countries--I would like to mention that,
as far as the cost of living is concerned,
it is more expensive at the present
moment to live in Belfast than it is in
London. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
That is a point which I think perhaps the
general public do not realise. I think we
are all agreed that economy is absolutely
essential. By all means let us have
economy, but I will be merely uttering a
platitude when I say that there are two
kinds of economy. There is false
economy and there is real economy, and
are
to economise in brains would be the falsest
of false economy. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) We have a
duty to our taxpayers, we
taxpayers ourselves, we have a duty to the
State, and we have a duty to those who
represent the State. I am perfectly sure
we all welcome the criticism that has been
levelled at us, but I am not sure that we
all welcome the fact that we have been
accused of incompetence when we thought
we were doing our best in the interests
of all concerned. Nor do we welcome the
suggestion, which was made, I think, by
the hon. Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh, that influences had been brought
to bear to induce us to make the suggestions we have made in regard to the
salaries of Cabinet Ministers. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) We feel that it
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mrs. Chichester.]
would be a very easy way of gaining popularity to criticise, but we cannot forget, as
we have been told to-day, that some hon.
Members might, instead of being in a
position to criticise, have been in the position that they were asked to serve on that
Committee, to lend us their experience,
to work with us and to help us if they
had been as anxious to serve the State as
they are to criticise our actions. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. COOTE: On a point of order. I
am sorry if the hon. Member suggests that
I accused the Committee of incompetence.
Mrs. CHICHESTER: I am sorry that
the hon. Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) should have thought
that I meant him. I meant the hon.
Member for North Belfast (Mr. M'Guffin).
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Would the hon.
Member permit me to say that she has
rather misrepresented the form in which I
made that statement. I said "Was it due
to their incompetence that it was necessary
to arrange for a sub-committee to travel
to London." I did not allege that it was
incompetence, if I remember rightly.
Mrs. CHICHESTER: I am very glad
that the hon. Member has said that he did
not accuse us, but simply inferred that we
were incompetent. I think we ought to
look upon this subject from a business
point of view. It may not be the prettiest
point of view, but we are a business community, and I think it stands to common
sense to say that if a tradesman wishes
to have an assistant, or a merchant a
clerk, or a farmer to engage a labourer,
he knows that he will not get the best
unless he gives good wages. Can we expect to get the best without giving value
for what we receive? As I said before,
that is not the pretty point of view, and
it is not the point of view I wish really to
put before the House. I think we ought
to look at it from a very much nobler
point of view, and I feel sure hon. Members will agree with me. The posts in
the Government for which we have been
suggesting salaries are filled-and again
I wish to borrow a word from the hon.
Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr.
Coote) are filled by plain men. I hope
hon. Members will not misunderstand me,
and will realise that I do not use the
words in the ordinary sense.
Mr. COOTE: The hon. Member is a
good judge.
Mrs. CHICHESTER: We are all plain
men and women-(hon. Members: No,
no.)—at least we are plain in our ideas.
We have always been, and have always
shown ourselves perfectly plain in our
loyalty and patriotism-(hon. Members:
Hear, hear)-and we have shown and
proved ourselves to be absolutely plain
and straightforward in our dealings with
our friends and with our enemies. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) These plain,
straightforward men have become something more. They have become pioneers,
and if they will allow me to say so, they
have become sculptors, and from the pure,
white block of marble with their chisels
they are creating a figure, the figure of a
new State, a figure which will embody
all our ideals of the past, and which will
represent all that we look forward to in
and
the future-a figure of the new
greater Ulster. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) To these pioneers, these Statebuilders, these men, we should give our
confidence, our help, and our gratitude,
and it seems to me that the best way in
which we can show that we recognise, not
their work, because we want to get away
from the individuals, but the work
attached to the posts which they fill, the
important, the wonderful work devolving
upon those posts, the best way in
which we can show our gratitude and our
help is and I make this suggestion to
hon. Members who have spoken in the
hope that possibly we might see our way
to agree to the amendment, or that there
should be no division-that they might
see their way to avoid discussion, and
compromise on this subject, and thus show
to our friends in all quarters, as well as
to our enemies, that there are really no
differences in our midst, and that we all
recognise the work that is being done for
us. (Cheers.)
Mr. DONALD: At the outset may I
congratulate my hon. Friend on her
splendid utterance. I am sure I am voicing the opinion of every Member present
when I say that she has not only done
credit to herself but to her sex and to hon.
Members of this House. With regard to
this report I would have been better
pleased if the Members of the Committee
had attached their names to it, because
it is the usual procedure. Then we could
have called it "the Campbell Committee's
Report," as it is usually described in other
places. I want to approach this matter
from a different point of view entirely
from that of any of the speeches which
have been made here this afternoon. We
were told in a speech by the Prime
Minister that in the money that is being
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
allocated to set up this Parliament, not
only the Civil Servants but the Parliamentary Secretaries are included, and he
stated in his opening speech that some
two and a half millions will be allocated.
That means that that money is coming
from the Imperial Exchequer. I dare
say some of our hon. Members take out
of that that the money is not coming out
of the pockets of the taxpayers in Ulster,
but nevertheless it is coming out of the
pockets of the taxpayers. I rise to support the amendment, and I want to put
my views as forcibly as I can, and I want
to offer one or two suggestions in order
that we may compromise, as suggested by
my hon. Friend who has spoken last (Mrs.
Chichester).
As I said before, the Prime Minister
stated that something like two and a
quarter millions would be forthcoming, for
this purpose, and he also said at that time
that the utmost economy must be exercised
in all Departments not only in the allocation of salaries but in everything else.
The Committee which was appointed had
power to send for persons, papers, and records. Well, I only wish that they had
sent for me. If I had given evidence
before them it would not have been
necessary for me to have spoken here this
afternoon. I do not agree entirely with
the proposals contained in the amendment. I want a further reduction, and I
will tell you why. I want to reduce the
salaries proposed to be paid to Ministers
and their Parliamentary Secretaries and
the Whips by £400 each, and as this is a
democratic Parliament, I want that £400
to be forthcoming for the payment of hon.
Members of this Parliament, because
whether hon. Members agree with me or
not, Members of this House must get a
salary. That must be done. A great
deal was said during the elections about
setting up a democratic Parliament, and
as one of the democracy I say that that is
the only way we can live. There are five
other Members in this Parliament who
are in the same position as myself, and I
would have liked an opportunity to put
this matter before the Committee in order
that they could have fixed a salary. Seven
months have elapsed now since we took
the Oath, and there is no mention yet of
any salary for private Members of Parliament. That is one proposition I desire
to bring forward in support of this amendment. Those Members who are in a more
fortunate position financially than myself
will no doubt agree with me that it is very
should have labour
necessary that we
representation in this House, and if we
must get labour representatives we must
have-
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member is
quite entitled to refer to the possible payment to Ministers, but he cannot go into
a discussion on matters outside the report.
Mr. DONALD: I quite agree with your
ruling, and I will endeavour to confine my
remarks to the report. On page 3, clause
7 of the report, the Committee set out as
the opinions of some of those internamed
by the sub-Committee, that
"the
honour conferred upon a man who
is selected to fill a post of high
importance in the Government of
his country must be taken as representing some part of his
remuneration.” Some honour, I
think, is also attached to the
private Member. We are a new body,
and not one of our Ministers or Parliamentary Secretaries has had any experience, I think, in Government
Departments. We are setting out on an
entirely new scheme. Perhaps in the
course of 12 months or two years we may
have a change in the Ministries or Parliamentary Secretaries. They have not
proved their worth up to now, and therefore we should not pay these high salaries
at the presnt juncture. With regard to
the payment of travelling expenses, it is
very necessary that Members of this House
who come from counties should have their
travelling expenses paid. I heard an hon.
Member say yesterday that it cost him £2
to come here for ten minutes yesterday.
I now come to page 6. The Committee
draws the attention of the House to the
fact that several of those whose salaries
have been dealt with are Members of the
British House of Commons, and, as such,
receive a salary of £400 per annum.
do not see why the Committee should go
into that matter, because under the provisions of the Government of Ireland Act
it will adjust itself in due course. I quite
agree that the Prime Minister's salary is
little enough for that position. There is
not a more liberal gentleman than our
I
Prime Minister. I know that from
personal experience, and it was stated in
the Press the other day that the salary
proposed, of £3,200, will be a mere fleabite in comparison to what the Prime
Minister will have to disburse in personal
expenses. But I think a salary of £1,000
is just a shade too high for our Parlia-
Y
Remuneration
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Donald.]
inentary Secretaries. It may be said that
this is not a democratic view.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Not a Trade Union
view.
An
Mr. DONALD: A man in receipt of
£1,000 a year would hold a very high
position in any firm in this city. In view
of the state of the country, I say that this
is not the time to fix these high salaries.
I myself held a very high position which
only realised something like £500 a year.
The whole cry now is to keep everything
down to the pre-war level, but the
tendency here is to fix very high figures.
We should start low, and then if the
officials concerned show the ability which
we expect and hope they will show, this
House, I am quite sure, will be quite
willing to increase their salaries.
hon. Member said we required the best
talent and ability to protect the taxpayers. We are all out for the same
thing. Every hon. Member of this House
who represents his constituency faithfully
has a duty to perform equal to that of a
Minister or Parliamentary Secretary, and
so far as I can see the proposal to give a
Parliamentary Secretary £1,000 a year
for probably three months' work in the
year, or a little more, is outrageous.
Before I got up to speak at all I recognised
that any opposition that might be offered
was bound to be defeated, because in the
House are 14 Members mentioned in the
report, in addition to the Committee of 7,
and therefore it is useless to offer opposition.
At the same time we have a duty
to perform in the interests of the taxpayers, and I think we are quite justified
in offering the opposition we do, and I
hope the Government will take this
matter back for re-consideration.
But
Mr. TWADDELL: I happen to be one
of the Members of the Select Committee,
and I had hoped we were going to get
wholesale praise from hon. Members of
this House. In place of that, however, I
find that members of the Committee have
been receiving wholesale condemnation
since the beginning of this debate.
after all, where up to the present moment
has there been a single solid logical argument used in favour of the amendment
by the hon. Member for Tyrone and
Fermanagh? Not one single logical
argument has been put forward to support
the passing of that amendment, which, I
am confident, will not be adopted by this
House. Members of the Select Committee were most careful and most
cautious in the recommendations they
made. If hon. Members of this House
had condemned the Select Committee for
the salary they recommend for the Prime
Minister on the ground that it was
not sufficiently large, I would have
been the very last to get up to
defend it, We arrived at the recommendations after we had carefully
considered the views of men of high standing whose honesty of purpose is undoubted. We considered the opinion of
men who have had Parliamentary and
Ministerial experience, some of whom were
actually ex-Ministers of New Zealand. I
mention that fact in particular, because
prior to the Select Committee giving any
consideration to this matter it was
suggested from time to time by members,
both outside and inside this House, and
also by members of the general public,
that we should take into consideration the
salaries paid to Ministers in New Zealand,
which has a population about the same as
our own. The functions of the various
Departments, the salaries of Dominion
Ministers, as well as the salaries of
ordinary corporation officials, were considered, but it was utterly impossible for
us to make any comparison whatever with
business concerns, because of the fact that
we knew that in a good many instances
the tax alone on excess profits paid
during the last six or seven years would
have been much more than the salaries
we recommend. We also considered the
responsibilities of the various offices and
the dignity attached thereto, after having
made due allowance for the privilege and
honour of serving in the Parliament of
this loyal province. Let us consider the
actual facts in connection with the recommendations. I have given a brief outline of the principles which guided us in
making our recommendations. We gave
consideration to the salaries that were
paid to Ministers in the Dominions, and
we took into consideration--or at least we
had before us-the population of those
various Dominions. Take, for example,
British Columbia. We here have five
Cabinet Ministers and about seven Parliamentary Secretaries, and we have an
Attorney-General who discharges the
duties of both Attorney-General and
Solicitor-General. British Columbia has
a population of 392,000, with seven
Ministers and seven Deputy-Ministers, and
the actual cost is £14,748. It has less than
one-third of the population of our Northern
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
area, so that if you multiply that £14,000
by three you get £42,000, as against a recommendation of £20,000 for the Northern
Province. Let us now take Alberta. It
has a population of 496,000, and the actual
cost is £17,100. These figures may not be
absolutely accurate, but they are substantially correct. They were obtained in a
hurry, and it is difficult to give an exact
statement. In South Australia they have
a population of 468,194, and the actual
cost is £6,000. Multiply that latter figure
by three, Southern Australia being only
one-third of the population of the Northern area, and you have £18,000, as against
£20,000. In Western Australia they have
a population of 331,000, practically a
fourth of the population of our Northern
area, and the cost of that particular State
is £8,400. Therefore if you take the population and the actual cost as compared
with Northern Ireland you will find that
on the whole we have kept well within the
limits.
In New Zealand we find they have actually twelve Ministers. There is no statement as to the number of Parliamentary
Secretaries.
Mr. LYNN: There is none.
Mr. TWADDELL: Admitting there is
none, the cost of New Zealand is roughly
£16,000, or about £4,200 less than we recommend for the salaries in this Northern
Province. Now what do we find in this
morning's newspapers, which I am sure
have been read by the hon. Member for
West Belfast (Mr. Lynn)? The Governor
of Tasmania has resigned because he is unable to maintain his position on the salary
he receives. The same remark applies to
New Zealand. The information at our disposal is to the effect that the salaries paid
in New Zealand-£4,000 less than we propose here are much too small, and that it
is absolutely essential to have them increased. What are the arguments in favour
of the amendment? I venture to say that
the Committee is entitled to the loyal support of every hon. Member of this House,
because I do feel that we should treat this
matter on businesslike lines, fairly and
squarely, without fear, favour, or prejudice. It will be admitted that the Committee have brought in a very fair report.
I feel, and feel very strongly, that our
Ministers must be held responsible for the
Departments over which they preside. They
must submit their conduct for approval or
disapproval from time to time to those who
elect them to represent them in this House.
That is the responsibility which they must
undertake rather than that the management and government of this Province
should be in the hands of permanent officials.
Mr. GRANT: I occupy a somewhat
peculiar position as being a Labour Member of this House, but, like my colleague
in North Belfast (Mr. M'Guffin), I claim to
be independent and to vote in whatever
way I consider right. I, therefore, intend
to support the proposals before the House.
The hon. Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh, in moving his amendment, stated
on several occasions: "We could not agree
to certain things in this report." I was
aware of the fact that a Committee was set
up in this House to make recommendations
for the salaries of Ministers and others, but
I was not aware that a Committee had been
set up to criticise that report outside this
House. The suggestion made by the hon.
Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh when
he said "We do not agree" led me to think
that these matters had been discussed outside the House, and even if we had
brought in in our report the figures which
they have mentioned we could not have
anything else but opposition; that no matter what report was tabled they were prepared to oppose it. It is, therefore, a pleasure for the members of the Committeee to
know that the salaries of the Prime
Minister and of the Attorney-General are
accepted by the unanimous approval of the
House. That is something to be thankful
for.
It has been pointed out-through a mistake, I take it, perhaps, on my part-that
the Committee was incompetent to deal
with the question before them. If the
Committee were incompetent that is altogether the fault of the House which appointed them, and not the fault of the
Committee. Reference has been made to a
certain clause in our report, and that it
was put there at the suggestion of a permanent official. The Committee was composed of new members of this House, members who had no Parliamentary experience,
and, therefore, they were not in a position
to know the tactics employed by members
who had far more Parliamentary experience than they had. But we have since
discovered that there are members of the
Imperial Parliament who were asked to
serve on the Committee, but who, in their
wisdom, refused to act.
It has been suggested that Ministers should put their
patriotism before their remuneration. I
think the same thing should apply to members of the Imperial Parliament who were
asked to act on the Committee and to give
us the benefit of their wide experience. But
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Grant.]
they refused to do that. They preferred
to stand outside and to criticise the report
when brought into this House.
Mr. LYNN: No, no.
Mr. GRANT: I said at the beginning
that I was taking up a position of independence, and that I claimed the right to
hold my own opinion. I still maintain that
right. A question has been raised by the
hon. Member for East Belfast (Mr. Donald)
that the Members of the Cabinet and those
whose salaries are mentioned in this report
have not proved their worth yet. I say
again that is not the fault of the Committee which recommends these salaries.
It is the fault of the House which put these
people into these particular offices. Might
I also remind hon. Members that we are
just as zealous in economy in connection
with our Province as other Members of the
House. We fully discussed the question,
and realised the difficulties before us, but
I would remind the hon. Member for East
Belfast that we were not fixing the salaries
for three months or six months. The terms
of our reference were to fix the salaries of
Ministers, and we were not told whether
there would be reductions if the cost of
living went down and increases if the cost
of living went up.
I think it is hardly fair to say that the
hon. Members who have been appointed to
these positions have not justified their
worth. I would like to say in conclusion,
as a Labour Member, that I hold it is the
position we ought to have regard to and not
the individual. Therefore if people are put
into these positions who have not proved
themselves worthy, I repeat it is not the
fault of this Committee but of the House.
But people who occupy high offices ought
to be paid in accordance with the positions
which they hold.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: I rise for
one reason, because I do not intend to give
a silent vote when this question comes to be
decided this afternoon. I want to say that
if there is one question that has troubled
me in connection with this new Parliament
and one which has given me considerable
thought and anxiety it is the question of
finance. This House must be very cautious
in regard to the road along which it is
going to travel. I am sure hon. Members
are perfectly well aware that outside this
House there is a very strong feeling. We
are here as the representatives of our constituencies, and at the present time anything that may be done in connection with
the Vote and Supply, and in connection
with salaries that may be attached to any
office, has got to be scrutinised in the most
careful manner, because the ratepayers and
the taxpayers outside are extremely jealous
in regard to any action or decision the
House may arrive at.
That is the only aspect of the work of
the Government that I am a little nervous
and tossed about, and I think up to the
present moment the Government has shown
rather an inclination in the direction of,
perhaps I will not say extravagance, but
I think that the principles of economy have
not been as rigidly observed as they might
have been. I, therefore, sincerely hope
that in all proposals of a financial character every item will be closely scrutinised.
As some hon. Members in their speeches
have said, the desire should be not to aim
at some things fairly large in order to
maintain what is termed the dignity of the
office, but rather that we should aim at
trying under existing conditions at the outset of this Parliament to attach to every
office connected with it, whether in the
Civil Service or whether in the offices occupied by the different Ministries, what,
people would regard as moderate.
It is expected by our constituents that we
must go cautiously, and if we err at all we
must err on the side of economy rather
than on the side of generosity. What is
the position we are in to-day? As the
Government of Northern Ireland, from the
financial point of view, we really do not
know exactly where we stand. No Member
of this House, no Member of the Government up to the present moment, has been
able to give us the slightest indication of
the position we will be in at the end of the
financial year. I venture to say that
business man or any person associated with
business, large or small, before entering
into large commitments, and before proceeding to attach salaries to different positions would like to have a good
idea as to what his financial position is going to be in order to meet those commitments.
We
any
do not know where we
stand, whether there is going to
be a surplus or whether there
is going to be a deficit. Therefore
I
say that this House must be very careful
that at the outset it will not commit itself
to any financial proposals or
to any
salaries except such as are reasonable and
right, until we properly understand the
financial position we are in. Anything we
ought to do at the outset ought to leave
room and scope for advance ag our
Government is stabilised and as our
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
Then
financial position is thoroughly understood. That is the governing condition
and the frame of mind that I bring to
the study of this problem. The next
thing-and it has been referred to by
several speakers here to-day, but it cannot
be too much emphasised in all our dis
cussions on matters of this kind-what is
the position to-day of the people from
whom we are going to derive the revenue
for the Government? We have incometax at 6/- in the pound. It does not
really matter at the moment whether it
is 6/- or 10/- to a great many people.
A great many people will not have any
income-tax to pay. A great many business
men and other folk are going to live upon
their capital probably for the next twelve
months-6/- in the pound income-tax to
pay to the Imperial Parliament.
what is the position in regard to local
taxation? I would not be at all surprised
if some of our municipalities will be imposing a rate of 20/- in the pound in the
next financial statements they present to
the public. (Hon. Members: More.) I
am not at all sure that the City of Belfast
will not come near that figure. I hope
they will be considerably lower than that.
But whether it is 16/- or 20/- there is the
burden that the average taxpayer has got
to meet for local taxation. I speak largely
in regard to municipalities such as this,
that have corporations and such like.
But when we come to deal with rural
districts what is the position? What is
the position of the farming classes to-day
with everything tumbling down in price?
Their taxation to-day-and I have gone
into a great many cases the taxes that
these men must pay will probably exceed
the rent that they will have to pay during
the year.
Mr. ARCHDALE: It very often happens.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: That is a
huge burden. And with the farming
classes, the labouring classes are closely
allied, because the prosperity, of the one
depends upon the prosperity of the other.
And what about the vast masses of unemploved, and the figures that the Minister
of Labour submitted to us in this House
some weeks ago? In Ulster we have the
largest number of unemployed that we
find anywhere-21 per cent. of the entire
community unemployed. Now what will
all these interests think when we propose,
as I shall presently show--when we proceed in this House to vote away, as we
are
recommended to do-£21,000 for
Ministers, for Under-Secretaries, and for
Whips? That is the position that I find
myself in to-day as representing not only
an industrial community, but representing
an agricultural community. And I believe
that I am only in this House-that is my
conception of it-to help, as far as I
possibly can, the ratepayers and the voters
who sent me here. I do think that this
is a most inopportune time for this Parliament to proceed on the lines that are
indicated in this report. I think that the
amendment which has been submitted-a
good deal of extraneous matter has been
introduced in some of the speeches that I
do not agree with-but still the substance
of that amendment ought not to be passed
lightly over by this House. We ought
not to tie ourselves to these salaries that
are suggested without very serious thought
and reflection. But there is one thing
that we have got to do. We are all loyal
and devoted to the Government, and to
their policy, and we will support them in
everything I speak for myself-I will
support them in all these matters, and I
am sure they will not misunderstand ine
when I take the liberty of criticising the
financial proposals and making certain
suggestions. I have no desire to be
hypercritical, but I desire to emphasise
again that if this House is going to secure
the confidence of the vast masses of the
people we represent in Northern Ireland,
they must proceed a little more cautiously
and not be so desirous of trying to reach
these maximum salaries until we find our
feet, and until we definitely get our
Government thoroughly established. Then
we will know our financial position. We
will know the moneys that are available,
and I for one, as far as I am concerned,
will be prepared to support in the most
generous manner the position of this
Cabinet and the various offices of responsibility and trust in the Northern Parliament.
The next thing I wish to come down to
is the actual report. These are the general
governing principles I am acting. upon
to-day, and that I intend to act upon
until we have reached a further stage in
the history and development of this
Northern Government of ours. The
question we have got to consider is what
we can afford to pay at the present
moment. It is not what these offices
ought to receive; it is what we can afford
to pay.
The Government would be well
advised to allow this report to go down
for further consideration, and see if certain
changes could be effected. That would
not be unreasonable. Speaking for my-
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
-
[Sir Robert Anderson]
self, I do not wish to suggest anything in
the way of sweeping reductions. But I
do think that some salaries that are here
stated may be easily reduced without any
serious loss of prestige to the office, or
loss of dignity to the man, or loss of
efficiency to the work that may be carried
on from time to time. As to the report
itself one part of it is somewhat ingenius and extremely interesting. That
is the part where the Committee frankly
acknowledge that they went outside their
terms of reference. Clearly there is more
behind that than appears on the surface.
It evidently indicates a certain amou; of
inspiration, it may be inward inspiration.
Probably the learned Member for Queen's
University (Dr. Campbell) received inspiration of that kind But in any event
what seems behind this is clear to mealthough my brains may not be just as
clear as those of the hon Member for West
Belfast (Mr. Twaddell), who has just left
the building. I am sorry he is not here.
But he seems to think that there is no
intelligence on the part of any of those
who criticise the report, and that all the
brains, all the facts, and all the information are confined to himself and those who
are associated with him. It suggests that
there may be additional offices. I wonder
is it a dream of the hon. Member for the
University that there might be a Ministry
of Health. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Does he think there may be another lot
of officials necessary for something of that
character? There is then the other proposal with regard to £500 a year for
Cabinet Ministers. That clearly indicates
I think if I may use terms that are used
by medical men-that Cabinet Ministers
may be considered ordinary practitioners
and it is thought it would be a good idea
to call in occasionally a specialist or expert to consult with the Cabinet Ministers.
If it was necessary to call in persons of
that type, each Cabinet Minister, I sunpose, will be assessed at so much, in
order to
the requisite fees.
The only thing I was surprised at was that
the Committee, constituted as it was, did
not suggest, instead of £500, five hundred
guineas.
.
pav
Now, let us come down to the different
items. No question whatever has been
raised in regard to what has been allotted
to the Prime Minister, nor to the
Attorney-General, although, let me say,
that, in my judgment, if there is any man
underpaid in connection with the whole
suggestions that have been made here by
the Committee, it is the Attorney-General.
I am not, however, going to quarrel with
that.
Then we come to what I might group
together as the Parliamentary Secretary
to the Upper House, the Parliamentary
Secretary, and the Assistant Parliamentary Secretary to the House of Commons. Hon. Members will understand
me somewhat better if I call them the
Whips; I think that is the Parliamentary
language in which we have to describe
them. But just think of it for a moment.
The Senate consists of twenty-eight Members, and for charity, in common phraseology, for whipping twenty-eight Members
and making the necessary arrangements
for certain duties it is suggested that a
salary of £600 should be paid. Is that
a serious proposal by any Ulsterman, whom
we always boast of as being hard-headed?
A Senate which will only sit for a month
or two during the year!
It
Then I am somewhat hesitant to
criticise the other two offices: the Parliamentary Secretary and the Assistant
Parliamentary Secretary, the two Whips
that will have to do with the hon. Members in connection with this House.
has been suggested it is necessary to have
a second, as if one were not sufficient.
And for these duties, bearing in mind all
the facts stated in the early part of my
speech, it is seriously suggested we should
grant £1,600. There are forty Members
of this House. That works out at about
the rate of £40 per Member to look after
the shepherding and care of the Members of this House and the other functions
that generally pertain to the work of the
Whips' office.
These proposals ought not really to be.
seriously entertained under existing conditions. Now, it has been suggested that
£1,000 ought to cover it, and I do really
seriously press upon the Government that
to go to the public to-day and say that the
two Whips of this House are to be paid
£1,600 under existing conditions in this
country, bearing in mind the terrible state
of trade, is a thing I cannot possibly
support, and I am confident that my constituents will not support it either.
Now, for the Cabinet Ministers-the
proposal has been £2,000. I have in the
back of my mind an idea that the Members of the Committee must originally
seriously have considered that probably
£1,500 must have been in or about what
should have been paid to them. But they
hit upon these other most ingenious pro-
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
posals to make the amount £2,000. I am
not casting any reflection on the right
hon. Gentlemen who fill these offices, not
the slightest. But, taking into consideration the smallness of our territory, the
compactness of our territory, and taking
into consideration the work that may have
to be accomplished in the next two or
three years, I do think that the £1,500 is
on the whole reasonable. You can quote,
as the hon. Member for West Belfast has
quoted, statistics. Statistics are very
dangerous things, and John Morley had a
peculiar idea of what statistics could do.
But I think the hon. Member was most
unfortunate. I only refer to one of the
most unfortunate things-the quotation
about New Zealand. The Prime Minister
of New Zealand is actually paid £2,000,
and the other Ministers are paid £1,300.
Mr. TWADDELL: I did not quote the
Prime Minister at all. I quoted the
aggregate of the entire Ministers.
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: I am
filling in the details my hon. Friend has
most judiciously omitted. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear) That is the danger of
handling facts and telling the House facts
when the whole facts are not given.
There are ten others paid £1,300.
But
he omitted at the same time to tell us that
the revenue of New Zealand is from
£20,000,000 to £25,000,000, and to state
the territory that has got to be covered
there compared to the Six Counties of
Northern Ireland. The thing is not to be
thought of.. If you go accurately into
Colonial precedents and study revenue
and everything else you will discover that,
taken as a whole since these Commonwealths have been created, the figures that
are suggested by this Committee far outstep anything that these States ever paid
or contemplated paying. I will not go
into all the different States. I am sure
one of my hon. Friends will be quite equal
to that.
Then in regard to the Parliamentary
Secretaries, there is the suggested reduction of £250, making the salaries £750.
Hon. Members should not think that
these proposals are merely brought forward for the sake of opposition. Nothing
of the kind. I should never be associated
with anything of a factious nature, anything of a character to embarass the
Government, anything of a character that
I did not thoroughly believe in, and I
believe I express the minds of those whom
1 represent.
I do sincerely hope, and I make the
suggestion that the Government even now,
without necessarily accepting the actual
figures of reduction, should re-consider the
proposals that have been submitted, and
try and see if they cannot effect some
further economies under existing conditions. I believe in doing so they will
strengthen their position in this House;
they will strengthen their position
in the country; they will do no wrong to
any of the offices that it is proposed to pay
these salaries to, and I believe they will
do a splendid thing for the overburdened
taxpayer at this particular crisis in the
history of our country. The idea is
prevalent, and I find it occasionally expresed both inside and outside the House,
that there is too much of a desire to copy
the great Imperial Parliament in all these
matters. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
It is nice to have all the dignity associated
with Imperial Parliamentary life, but if
I may again use the phrase that has been
already used, there is a certain sober
plainness about the Ulster people. They
really do want to see this Government
efficient and well-conducted, and I for one
believe it can be conducted efficiently and
well by a little more cutting down of expenditure in all these departments. I
support the amendment that has been
moved, and, if necessary, will go into the
Division Lobby in favour of it.
Mr. CRAWFORD: I wish in a very
few brief words to support the amendment
before the House this afternoon. In the
early days of the election I foresaw something like what the hon. Member for
Derry described to the House this afternoon of a number of people interested in
the welfare of the country hammering out
a new State and establishing it upon the
foundations of truth and righteousness,
and the thought entered my mind that it
would be a grand thing if we could get
people with time and leisure and liberality
to devote their powers to this noble work.
But instead of seeing a vision I must have
dreamed a dream. Instead of anyone
being willing to give his services gratis,
everyone has to get paid for what he does.
I must say I am exceedingly sorry that
that state of things exists. If such people
came forward and did the work gratis for
a year or two, there would be one grand,
united throb of gratitude to these men.
Then at the end of that time, if things
had been properly managed, sufficient
salaries could be paid to those deserving of
them.
Remuneration
Remuneration.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Crawford,]
So far as 1 can see there are just three
ways in which we can get money to meet
our needs. It is a wise thing if you are
going to build a house to first sit down and
count the cost. Now these three things I
refer to are Excise, Customs, and Income
Tax. Excise, as you know, would include
liquors, and various sorts of stamps and
death duties. I hope before very long
this House will take steps to see that the
excise duties will be less than in times
past. I hope temperance legislation will
be passed by this House so that there will
not be so much duty from liquor as
formerly. We all know how difficult it is
to get money, and the different stamp
duties will not be so great in times to come
as in the past. There will be then the
death duties. I sincerely hope that there
will not be a great many people in Ulster
dying and that, therefore, there will be a
small revenue from death duties. Instead
of our revenue increasing it will be a
diminishing one. We know how bad
times are compared with what they were
formerly. We know that there is not the
same purchasing power, and there will be
less money in time to come. I feel
certain that income tax will be much
lower than in previous years. I have fairly
extensive business experience, and I know
a great many people connected with
business, and many will be satisfied to
come out square at the end of this year
without paying income tax at all. There
is therefore, great need for economy.
I
have the populations of the various Crown
Colonies and the incomes paid to all
officials. Notwithstanding all I have
heard this afternoon, I am still of the
opinion the pruning knife should be
applied much more vigorously. I will
support with all my heart and soul the
amendment. Look at the state of the
country. The farming industry, which is
the backbone of the country, is in a bad
state. The farmers are hardly able to
dispose of their produce. Transit is so
difficult that goods cannot be got away,
and it is impossible to compete with people
in England. The farmers are in a bad
state. It will be impossible to get much
from them. They are burdened to the
neck. They will scarcely be able to get
sufficient money to pay their debts.
great many people are leaving the
country. It is not fair to compare this
country with New Zealand. People are
emigrating to New Zealand, and while the
population and paying capacity of New
A
Zealand increases they are decreasing in
this country. The business people here
are very hard hit, and all are not able to
make the money they used to make. You
know the state of this city, which depends
so much on shipping and shipbuilding.
Fleets are standing as idle "as painted
ships upon a painted ocean."
""
If they succeed, as we hope they will, in
cutting down armaments at this great
Washington Conference, the sound of the
hammer will not be heard so much in this
city of Belfast as it has been in the past,
and the great shipbuilding industry will
suffer. I hope they will be successful in
reducing armaments, and stop building
warships to blow one another to pieces. I
sincerely hope they will arrange things so
that less money will be spent in battleships
in time to come, but that may bring hardship and suffering to many of our people.
Taking everything into consideration I
sincerely hope that the Government—I am
sorry more Members of the Cabinet are
not here will take this back, and bring in
a Report that we can all heartily agree
with.
Mr. LYNN: The hon. Member for
Londonderry (Mrs. Chichester) has delivered a very interesting speech; if I may
say so, one of the most able, finished, and
accomplished maiden speeches I have ever
listened to, and I pay a tribute to her
without any flattery whatever, because I
am going to apply the corrective later on.
I am only sorry that she is not on this
side of the House, because she would see
that Ministers are not plain men and she
would also be on the right side. The hon.
Member said that two Members of the
Imperial Parliament who had been asked
to serve on this Committee had not done
so, and she insinuated they had so acted
because they wanted to keep up a factious
opposition and that they did not want to
help the Government. The hon. Member
knows that I for one-and I am one of the
two who refused-am the chairman of a
very large Education Committee, and that
the work of the committee occupies at least
five or six hours a day every working day
of the week. I would like to know then
how it would have been possible
for me to have served on this
Committee, and I doubt whether
if I had acted I would have done
anything except add to the work of the
printer, because if I had been a Member
of the Committee--one does not know
whether the Committee is really unanimous or not; it is a most extraordinary
Remuneration.
Remuneration.
1 DECEMBER, 1921.
Report from that point of view-I would
certainly have put in a Minority Report.
I am sure the Members of this Committee
tried to do their duty conscientiously and
