Emergency Powers Bill. 400
Oral Answers.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Tuesday, 6th December, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
ORAL ANSWERS TO QUESTIONS
RECORDS REMOVED FROM
ENNISKILLEN.
Mr. COOPER asked the Minister for
Home Affairs whether he is aware that the
Registrar General, Charlemont House,
Dublin, on the 22nd October, 1921, removed from Enniskillen Workhouse the
Register of Births, Marriages and Deaths
in use for the electoral divisions of Gardenhill and Holywell, Co. Fermanagh, and con
taining all records from 1864 to 1921, and
whether this Register has been deposited
in Southern Ireland, and if he will take
steps to have it restored to the Superintendent Registrar at Enniskillen, or a
certified copy thereof lodged with him for
the convenience of the public.
The PARLIAMENTARY and FINAN-
CIAL SECRETARY to the MINISTRY of
FINANCE (Mr. Barbour): I am answering
this question directed to my right honourable Friend (Sir Dawson Bates) as the
functions of the Registrar General in
Northern Ireland are being transferred to
the Ministry of Finance. I have no
information yet on the point raised by the
honourable Member, but am in correspondence with the Registrar General, Dublin,
on the matter. The functions of the
Registrar General, so far as Northern Ireland is concerned, will not be transferred
until 1st proximo.
THE 9 P.M. CURFEW RESTRICTIONS.
Mr. DONALD (by private notice) asked
the Minister of Home Affairs whether he
would take steps on or before Saturday,
the 10th inst., to remove the 9 p.m. Curfew
restrictions in view of the fact that such
restrictions are involving great financial loss
to shopkeepers and business premises in
that area.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): The matter is under
consideration, and I am in communication
with the General Officer commanding the
troops in Belfast.
Mr. DONALD: Thank you, very much.
NOTICE OF MOTION.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
Resolved: "That, notwithstanding any.
thing contained in the Standing Orders of
this House, the Local Government [Emergency Powers] Bill be passed through all
its remaining stages at this day's Sitting."
-(Captain Dixon.)
ORDERS OF THE DAY.
LOCAL GOVERNMENT [EMERGENCY
POWERS] BILL.
Order for Second Reading read.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That the Bill be now read a Second time."
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Bill which
I beg to move requires very little explanation. It proposes to confer certain
additional powers on the Ministry of Home
Affairs in whom is now vested the Local
Government Board. Under the Bill the
Ministry, in the event of any of the Local
Authorities refusing to function or refusing
to carry out the duties imposed on them
under the Local Government Acts, can
dissolve such Authority and in its place
appoint a Commission to carry on the
duties of such Authority. I regret exceedingly that it is necessary for me
to have to come to this House
and ask for these powers.
Prior to the
Service of Local Government being taken
over by this Ministry a circular letter was
sent by the Ministry to each of the Authorities in the Six Counties. In that circular
the Ministry promised co-operation on its
part, and in return asked for the same cooperation. I regret that in the case of
County Tyrone County Council a reply was
received refusing to recognise this
Government and intimating the Council's
intention of going under Dail Eireann.
I cannot but think that the attitude of
the Council towards this Government is
somewhat inconsistent with that adopted by
them towards the Imperial Government.
Until the transfer, the Council recognised
the Local Government Board in Dublin,
whose President is an English Minister.
Now the Council refuse to recognise this
Ministry, controlled by Irishmen, even
though Ulstermen.
Emergency Powers Bill
6 DECEMBER 1921. Emergency Powers Bill.
amendment for that purpose at a
stage. I beg to move.
Honourable Members are aware that for
some time past most of the Local Authorities in the South of Ireland have refused
to recognise the Local Government Board
in Dublin. The attitude of the Imperial
Government in reply was, if you do not
recognise us you do not get our money,
withheld the
and, therefore,
in aid
Imperial grants
taxation. The course adopted has
failed to induce the local authorities to recognise the Board, the result
being chaos so far as Local Government was
concerned. The condition of affairs in the
various counties became deplorable.
of
various
local
Rates
were left uncollected except from Loyalists,
and then at the point of the revolver, and
where the rates were collected, the proceeds were used for anything but for the
The result to-day
purposes authorised.
is that roads are abandoned, the care of
the sick and poor neglected, and bankruptcy stares every County Council in the
South in the face.
I do not desire to criticise the Imperial
Government, though, in my opinion, it is
open to question whether the course was a
wise one. If this Government is a Government it has got to govern. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) It is very easy to
abandon the ratepayers to their fate, but
it is not statesmanship, and, therefore, I
hold that if a local authority will not carry
out the administration of the law entrusted
to it, and administer the affairs of the
county, it is the duty of this Government
to manage it for them and protect the ratepayers. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) 1
have
to believe that the vast
majority of the ratepayers of County
Tyrone are dead against the suicidal policy
adopted by the Tyrone County Council,
and will welcome this Act, under which all
classes of the community will be able to
benefit by the continuance of local administration in a regular and impartial manner.
reason
In asking this House to give me these
powers I should like it to be understood
that I do so from no vindictive motives,
but solely from a desire to save the ratepayers from the disastrous financial results
that have been produced in the South of
Ireland by the misguided action of South
of Ireland Local Authorities. I venture
to hope, and I say this with all sincerity,
that this emergency arrangement will be
of the shortest possible duration, and I
look forward to early resumption of their
responsibilities by the local authorities.
It is proposed to limit the duration of the
Bill to one year, and I shall move an
later
Mr. COOTE: I should not intervene in
this debate but for the fact that for the
last twenty-three years I happened to be
a member of the Tyrone County Council,
and I wish to explain our position to the
House. Up till the last election, when
the majority went over practically to Sinn
Fein, we had a very excellent Council, or
series of Councils. I question if there was
any county in Ireland better or more economically managed than the County of
Tyrone. But there came a new set of
people into power, and they wished to
change much of what had been carried on
as the ordinary business of the country,
with the result, and co-terminus with the
campaign of the Sinn Fein Party in
County Tyrone of outrage, maiming, and
killing, that the County Council finds itself very seriously on the wrong side of
the account to-day. In fact, they are—
I do not want to use the word bankrupt,
because I should be ashamed of my
county being bankrupt in two yearsvery seriously handicapped, as I will show
you before I finish. From the old
Council there are transmitted two or three
very sensible Nationalist men who would
like to do right, and work economically,
and, I believe, loyally, with the law as it
may exist from time to time, but these men
are driven into the lobby along with Sinn
Fein, driven at the threat, and, I suppose,
the unwritten law of the revolver, or the
fear of it at least, and they are simply
part of the whole voting power of that
County Council. I want to pay a tribute
to two or three of these Councillors. They
are not here to defend themselves; but
they are men of excellent business capacity, men, who, I believed, would like,
under all circumstances, to do right if
they had the power to do so, but they are
victimised and terrorised by the unwritten law of Sinn Fein.
Now, there is due by the County
Council of Tyrone an overdraft in the
bank of £25,495. The County Council
by permission of the Local Government
Board can draw up to £30,000, so that
they have to-day a credit, so far as drawing powers are concerned, of £4,505.
But I quite expect when the salaries of
the officials and the labourers on the roads
and quarries are paid there will be none
of this money available. It should, in
fact, all be expended by this time.
But
in addition the County Council owes the
Asylum the second transfer of £8,053,
403 Emergency Powers Bill.
HOUSE OF COMMONS Emergency Powers Bill. 404
[Mr. Coote.]
They
We, the Tyrone County Council; were to
meet to-morrow. I shall be glad if the
right hon. Gentleman, the Home Secretary, can inform me will the Tyrone
County Council be permitted to meet tomorrow? I hardly think so, but I would
like him to tell me whether we can or
not. The Finance Committee of the
County Council, of which I am a member,
was to meet to-morrow, and a sub-committee was to meet privately to have a
heart to heart talk, to see what we might
do in face of this terrible burden of
£30,000 for malicious injury claims
against the rates of the county.
which was due on the 1st July,
also owe a third transfer to this bodythe Tyrone and Fermanagh County
Asylum. Furthermore, they owe to
other subsidiary bodies, such as Boards
of Guardians and District Councils, a
total sum of £37,077. Then for
malicious injuries there is lodged in the
County Council offices something like
£15,000 worth of decrees, and there were
a number of further decrees granted at
the last sessions in the county. They
all amount to something like, roughly,
£30,000.
So that to-day the County Council
stands indebted to the amount of
£92, 572. Against this we have an unpaid rate of £79,000, which comes in
generally in the months of February and
March. The Government Grants amount
to from £22,000 to £25,000, but they cannot expect these unless they comply with
the law. And we should also receive
about. the month of March £9,000 from
the Ministry of Transport for the upkeep
of the roads.
Now, that is the position of the county.
It is a deplorable position for a great
county like my county of Tyrone, which
has always kept its head up very proudly
and realised it had probably one of the
lowest general rates in any county
in Ireland.
I venture to say
that any impartial man who
looks at and considers the operations of
Sinn Fein during its two years' rule
in Tyrone will not like Sinn Fein extending its rule to any county or counties
in Ireland. The record of Sinn Fein
certainly makes deplorable reading. One
of the very first things they did after they
got the power was to attempt to burn
their own building. They very nearly
succeeded. A patrol of constabulary,
however, saw the smoke, the military were
brought out, and attacked the fire with
the result that they were able to save the
building, and only a room in a part of the
structure was destroyed. A series of
malicious injuries, now known to everybody, took place, with the result that the
rates will have to be trebled if we are
obliged to pay these malicious injury
claims.
But the average Sinn Feiner has got an
idea that somehow somebody other than
himself is going to pay this money, and
that the ratepayers of Tyrone will never
have to pay.
The Sinn Feiners think
the British Government will
pay these
claims, and so the campaign goes on.
aus-
I think it is time to wind up this unfortunate Council, and I am sure the Sinn
Fein members of the Council will
be very glad. It is an
picious event for them. They
will be very much pleased, I am sure, on
the sly, at least privately, to get rid of
the whole thing, for they did not know
where they were being driven to, and
they were afraid that the public, that
Nemesis, was on their track. I want to
assure the right hon. Gentleman that
there are many people in the County of
Tyrone, non-unionists, Hibernians, and
the old type of Nationalists, who will be
delighted if some firm hand comes in that
will deal sensibly with the finances of the
county. They have been looking with
horror at the way things have been going,
but they dare not move a hand. That
is the state of things to-day.
I want to know also if the right hon.
Gentleman can inform us what will be the
position of the labourers. We have some
direct labour, and some road contracts by
farmers and other men who live by taking
out road contracts. Will they be assured
of their cash, or will they be obliged to
go without their pay because Sinn Fein
has gone mad? Will something not be
done to see that these people get their
money in these dark winter days? Many
of them are Sinn Feiners themselves, but
they are poor, and I am speaking for all
the labourers. Will some power not be
given whereby the work o the county
may be continued, and these labourers
no matter to what class they belong,
might have some employment given to
them, so that the acts and folly of their
leaders will not be visited upon the heads
alike of Unionist, Nationalist, and Sinn
Fein labourers who are trying to earn an
honest living in the county? As a member of the County Council, I will be able
to go back to the County Council, and say
I congratulate the Home Secretary on
405 Emergency Powers Bill. 6 DECEMBER 1921.
Emergency Powers Bill. 406
putting his foot down and doing what
should have been done in this county long
ago, and saying that the people must obey
the law. He will get greater respect by
thus doing the right thing, and there are
many ratepayers who will thank goodness
they have a Home Secretary who has got
some backbone.
Mr. ROBB: I only want to ask one
one or two questions. In common with
all other Members of the House I say
we welcome this Bill as a firm step which
has been taken by this Government to
deal with those who are recalcitrant
against their authority. In Paragraph
C," Section I, subsection 1, it says
the Ministry may

"Make such provision as may be required
for the resumption by the local authority of
its powers and duties, or (where the local
authority has been dissolved) for its
constitution."
re-
Perhaps the right hon. Gentleman will
tell us whether by re-constitution he contemplates a re-election, or whether it is
contemplated that the action of the
Ministry shall be by appointment of some
nominee or the calling together of the
former members of the Council? There
is one other thing I would like to ask.
This is a most extraordinary power to be
given into the hands of a Government
Department.
In a democratic country
it would be, to a considerable extent, resented from time to time, and would probably be the subject of criticism. I would,
therefore, like to ask the right hon.
Gentleman what measures will be taken
to acquaint, at the earliest possible
moment, the Members of this House of
any action which is to be taken by the
Ministry under this Bill.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I have not any
observations to offer with respect to either
this Bill, or the way it has been introduced; but there are one or two things
I would like to mention. One is that the
Home Secretary, in presenting this Bill
to the House, hardly made manifest the
necessity for it. Of course that remark
may be sneered at, but we are not the
only persons who have to be convinced,
and that is exactly where the point comes
in. In introducing an emergency measure
in the Imperial House, good reason is
shown why it is necessary. Up to the
moment we have not heard anything with
respect to that. The Home Secretary
might have entered into detail, and shown
to the world at large the reason why at
the very inception of this Parliament it
was necessary to use emergency powers.
If emergency powers are necessary I will
not equivocate for one moment with
respect to the use of them. Not being a
lawyer I did not know whether the
ordinary powers were not sufficient to deal
with the situation at the moment. If
they are they should be relied upon. The
hon. and learned Gentleman (Mr. Robb)
raised a point as to what is meant by
section B, Clause I.

Appoint such person as the Ministry
thinks fit to discharge such of the duties of
the local authority as may be specified in the
Order."
I am at one with the hon. and learned
Gentleman (Mr. Robb) in his objection.
Does it mean you can appoint a person to
carry on the functions of the authority?
It is a most undemocratic procedure. You
might allocate the power to a number of
men, but to place the authority in one
individual is to my mind a rather undemocratic procedure. Of course, it is
intended in view of Section C to make
provision as may be required for the resumption by the local authority of its
powers. I presume Section B is for the
interreguum, and that provision will be.
made as soon as possible for resumption
of authority by the recognised coporate
body.
From what I have heard from the hon.
Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh, I
have not the slightest doubt that a very
strange condition of affairs obtains. Ve
have all read in the papers about it.
After all, my difficulty in considering the
question is whether the purpose you have
in view in the introduction of this Bill
will ultimately operate, because it is not
the constitution of the body but the
getting of the body politic to recognise
the laws and put them in force. It is the
getting of the people to recognise their
obligation. There will be great difficulty in enforcing the emergency measure.
We have had emergency measures for
instance, introduced in the House of
Commons to strengthen the Chief Secretary in the administration of the laws of
this country. Disaster has been the
result. We were convinced in conceding
the measures. We gave all that was considered necessary to bring about order in
Ireland. This had not materialised.
hope there will be better results in this
case, but one great difficulty that will confront the Home Secretary in carrying out
this important duty will be insistence
upon the people observing law and order,
and contributing their part to the rates.
necessary to support these local bodies.
BB
I
407 Emergency Powers Bill. HOUSE OF COMMONS
Emergency Powers Bill. 408
[Mr. M'Guffin.]
I do not think any mention is made in
this Act in respect to that matter, which,
to my mind, is a great difficulty. I hope
the right hon. Gentleman will succeed, and
this House will certainly do everything to
secure the recognition of law and order.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I thought I had
explained the reason I had to introduce
this measure. It is to prevent the condition of affairs that exists in the South of
Ireland spreading into County Tyrone. I
propose to appoint a commissioner who
will take the place of the County Council,
and I am sure hon. Members will agree
when I say that the Commissioner should
consult the members of the County Council,
even though they are not sitting in a corporate capacity. As regards the question
put to me by the hon. and learned Member
for Queen's University (Mr. Robb), the
measure leaves it entirely open as to
whether the County Council will be reconstructed as now or whether an election will
be held for the county. With regard to the
question of the road men it is to ensure that
the ordinary work of the County Council
will be carried on, and that the wages will
be paid to these workmen and the other
men referred to, that this measure has been
introduced.
Mr. COOTE: Will the County Council
meet?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think it would
be undesirable for the County Council to
meet at the present time. The police are
in possession of the buildings and other
equipment.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Are we to understand
that the interests of the area under consideration will be absolutely in the hands
of one individual.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Certainly.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Until such times as the
reconstituted authority comes into being.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Yes. I am sure
the hon. Member will understand that the
commissioner will go down there, and his
object will be to carry on the ordinary work
of the County Council. And I can imagine
no better way of carrying on than by consulting the members of the Council.
Mr. THOMPSON DONALD : May I ask
if the present members regret their action,
and come back will they be reinstated?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I cannot give
the hon. Member any undertaking until I
see how things turn out.
the
Mr. MILLER: As one who has been the
Unionist Whip for Tyrone County Council
for a period of almost twenty
years until the election which
was lost by fraud and intimidation, I should like to say that
stand that has been taken by the Minister
of Home Affairs has been received in Co.
Tyrone with the greatest joy. Everywhere
since I left this House last week and went
down to the county there has been no
more popular move for many a long time
than the move that has been made by the
Minister of Home Affairs. Everyone was
waiting for the Minister to take a strong
stand in this matter, and I welcome the
Bill. At the same time there is one thing
that strikes me that I do not like in the
Bill. I do not like what I may call the
new-fangled fashion of legislation that
leaves a loophole for an order to upset
everything that is in the Bill. We have
seen that sort of thing of late years in the
Imperial Parliament. Legislation is introduced by a Bill, and then a clause comes
in at the end giving practically unlimited
powers to contradict everything that is in
the Bill if necessary. I would have been
much more pleased to have seen set out
in the Bill all the powers and orders that
are given under the Bill. It would have
been more satisfactory. I would much
rather that you would allow those members
of the County Council who are willing to
work under this Ministry to carry on the
work. (Hon. Members: "Hear, hear.")
And anyone who will not work then allow
them to stay out, just in the same way as
we are here in this House at the present
time. A certain number of Members come
here to work and a certain number stay
away. I would let those who will not work
stay away. I think that would be perfectly justified in the present circumstances.
We are here setting up a new Government
a different Government from what has
been here before. Supposing this had happened under the war and that we were
here as a German province, and not under
the control of England, would not the first
thing be to ask all members of that
province who were taking part in the Government to take an oath of allegiance
to the new authority? (Hon. Members:
"Hear, hear.") Here we are setting up a
new authority, and the first thing we
should ask the public bodies to do is to take
an oath of allegiance not only to His
Majesty the King but to the Government
409 Emergency Powers Bill. 6 DECEMBER 1921. Emergency Powers Bill. 410
of this country as legally established. That
is the sort of thing that I expected to
find in this Bill, when I received it yesterday afternoon. I must say that I was
rather disappointed. Those are the lines I
thought the Bill would have taken. That
would have allowed any person who has
been elected by the people of the counties
of Tyrone and Fermanagh-and I dare say
Derry City will be in the same way-it
would have allowed the representatives of
the people to come in and take the oath of
allegiance and conduct the affairs that they
were elected to conduct.
very
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: I just rise
to give expression to some thoughts on this
subject that is now before the House.
May I be permitted to congratulate the
Minister of Home Affairs on the lucid
way in which he has introduced this Bill
to-day and upon what is evidently the considered policy of the Government in matters of this kind. I take leave to say that
the Minister of Home Affairs has not proposed anything of a revolutionary character whatever in regard to the control of
local affairs. It has been done over and
over again under the old system. Boards
of Guardians have been superseded who
have failed to carry out their functions in
This law now applies to
a proper manner.
County Councils, so that any suggestion
that legislation is being made of a novel or
peculiar character, or anything that is undemocratic, does not really carry any great
weight. My friend the hon. Member for
North Belfast (Mr. M'Guffin) tells us that
this thing is not democratic. Well, when
you have to deal with forces of this character what is the use of talking about
democracy? You really have to deal with
them in a firm and determined way. Sending down a commissioner or two commissioners to deal with the functions and
duties of Boards of Guardians, County
Councils, District Councils, is not an unusual procedure at all. Men of ability
going down to carry out these functions and
duties will probably discharge them with
just as much efficiency, and possibly with
as great consideration for the interests of
the ratepayers as probably the popularly
elected Board itself. It is quite a common
thing in Canada, where municipalities are
conducted by two or three commissioners
who are paid for the purpose. Sometimes
I think that the work is probably as efficiently and as wisely conducted in this way
as by the large boards such as we have
constituted in this country to-day. I think
the Home Secretary has taken a good step
and one which I believe can do no injury
to any interests whatever in County
Tyrone. I think he is setting an example
of firm and just Government at the outset,
and it is bound to have an influence for
good in many directions. I have seen suggestions made from time to time to cut off
the grants to the local bodies. Very often
the cutting off of these grants affects very
seriously the majority of the ratepayers
who are loyal people. I do not approve of
that procedure. I think the lines that the
Home Secretary and Government are taking on this question and their policy will
receive the hearty and cordial support of
this House. I trust that this Bill now introduced by the Home Secretary will be
carried heartily and unanimously and that
its effect will be very beneficial in the interests of local Government right throughout the entire Northern area.
Dr. MORRISON: I have had experience
in my own union of the local authority refusing to carry out legislation when passed
with regard to the Labourers' Act, and the
result was that an inspector was sent down
from Dublin to administer the Act. The
first labourers' cottages were built by him,
and the objections on the part of the Guardians and the people were largely overcome. After the first demonstration of
wise and prudent legislation, with regard to
a long-suffering class, and after seeing the
beneficial effect of it, the boards have since
in a wise and prudent spirit carried out all
the legislation that was passed with regard
to Labourers' Acts, greatly to the satisfaction of the ratepayers. I am quite in
agreement that it was necessary that something should be done to remedy the deplorable condition of affairs that exists in Co.
Tyrone. I think, if anything, I am inclined
to complain that the commissioner as representing this House has hardly enough
power. There are restrictions with regard
to the carrying out of the Local Government Act requiring notice and requiring
certain times to expire before certain things
are done. It is quite possible that the commissioner who carries out this Act will be
opposed and harassed not only by disobedience on the part of officials but disobedience on the part of the ratepayers to contribute their rates. I would feel disposed to
give him liberty to appeal to the Cabinet
for any further powers that he might think
necessary to discharge his duties efficiently,
and that such powers, having been granted
to him by the Cabinet, would then be submitted for the approval of this House. I
411 Emergency Powers Bill. HOUSE OF COMMONS
Emergency Powers Bill. 412
[Dr. Morrison.]
am certain that the administration would
be wise and helpful to the ratepayers and
give satisfaction to many Nationalists in
Co. Tyrone and be an example of good
Government which would have the effect,
as was the case in Coleraine Union, of
showing public boards the necessity
of carrying out their duties wisely and in
accordance with the laws of the land.
Mr. COOPER: I wish merely to say
that I thoroughly approve of the Bill as
it stands, but I think the amendment which
the Minister of Home Affairs proposes to
the Bill is a bad one. He proposes that
the Bill should only be put into operation
for one year. The County Council of
Tyrone should remain in office for eighteen
months, and I think it would be very much
better if the amendment provided that the
Bill should remain in operation until the
next election. As far as the effect of the
Bill itself is concerned, my hon. Friend,
the Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh
(Mr. Coote) should know all about it, because he was very much up against the
Local Government Board on one occasion,
and they had to take over the Clogher
Board of Guardians, of which he was a
member.
Mr. COOTE: I was not then a member.
Mr. COOPER: I can assure you the
action of the Local Government Board was
highly effective.
Question: "That the Bill be read a
second time," put and agreed to.
Considered in Committee.
Mr. MOLES in the Chair.
CLAUSE 1.-(Default of Local Authorities).
(1) If the Ministry of Home Affairs is satisfied that any Local Authority in Northern
Ireland has refused or failed or is unable to
perform all or any of the duties imposed on
that local authority by or in pursuance of
any statute, or so imposed by any order or
direction issued or given by the Ministry in
pursuance of any statute, then notwithstanding anything contained in any
Ministry may by order do all or any of the
following things, namely:-
(c)
Make such provision as may be required for the resumption by the local
authority of its powers and duties, or
(where the local authority has been
dissolved) for its re-constitution.
(2) From and after the making of any such
Order, and so long as it remains in force, the
powers and duties of the local authority, or
where the Order relates to the performance
of any specified duty, the powers and duties
of the local authority in relation to that
duty, shall, as the case may require, be either
vested in the person appointed by the Order,
or exercised and performed by him, to the
exclusion of the local authority, and the remuneration and expenses of such person, as
fixed by the Ministry of Home Affairs, shall
be a debt due by the local authority to that
Ministry, and shall be paid out of such rate
or fund as that Ministry may determine.

(3) In this section "local authority' means
the council of any county, county borough,
or county district, and the commissioners of
any town, and includes any committee or joint
committee wholly or partly appointed by any
such council or commissioners.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I beg to move,
on page 1, line 10, after the words, "Home
Affairs" ["If the Ministry of Home Affairs
is satisfied"], to insert the words, "for
Northern Ireland."
""
Amendment agreed to.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I beg to move,
on page 1, line 14, leave out the words,
so imposed" ["or so imposed by any
order ", and insert the words, "imposed
on that Local Authority."

Amendment agreed to.

Amendment agreed to.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I beg to move,
on page 1, at the end of line 29 [" for its

(d) Revoke, amend, or vary any Order
made for any purpose mentioned in the foregoing paragraphs of thie Sub-Section."
There is some doubt as to whether the

it is proposed that if

413 Emergency Powers Bill
Emergency Powers Bill. 414
6 DECEMBER 1921.
Mr. ROBB: The right hon. Gentleman
did not give any answer to the question I
asked before. I asked what measures
would be taken to acquaint this House of
any orders that were made under this Act?.
I think it is very necessary that the earliest
possible information should be given to
Members of this House on any action that
may be taken by the Ministry under the
extraordinary powers given in this Bill,
and I move as an amendment, after line 13
on page 2 of the Bill ["as that Ministry
may determine"], to insert the following
words:-
Any Order made by the Ministry under the
powers of this Act shall be laid before both
Houses of Parliament on the first day
after the date of the Order upon which Parliament shall meet.
""
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: I hope the
hon. and learned Member for Queen's
University is not serious in pressing an
amendment of this character upon the
House. In small matters of this kind
surely we can trust the Government and
the powers concerned. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.") Every little matter
of the nature indicated in this Bill
surely is not going to be laid on the Table
of the House before being put into action.
I do hope the amendment will not be
pressed, and I think the Minister of Home
Affairs and those associated with him in
the administration of the law should be
trusted to carry out an Act of Parliament
of this character.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: The amendment refers
to an authority which is being set up, and
over which this House should have some
direct control, as it should also have some
say in the matter of how the authority
should regulate its affairs. If it applied to
an authority which existed in normal times
there would be some force in what the hon.
Member for Londonderry City and County
(Sir Robert Anderson) suggests, but this
is an Emergency Act, and we want to see
that the House which concedes these
emergency powers should have some say
as to how they are to be applied.
The PRIME MINISTER: Perhaps I
might ask my hon. and learned Friend
not to press this amendment at the
moment, and my right hon. Friend, my
colleague, and I will put our heads together
and see if something cannot be devised to
meet his desire and the desire of the hon.
Member for North Belfast. My hon. and
learned Friend will know that in the haste
of handing in manuscript amendments at
the Table some effect other than that
which he desires may be given, and may
cramp us in the action we propose to take,
and thus defeat the object we have in view.
But the House will understand there is no
desire on the part of the Government to
hide in any way the various steps we propose to take in regard to this or any other
defaulting authority in our area. And
the Press and the ordinary channels
will perhaps make known to the members
of the public what is being done by my
right hon. Friend in this matter.
If we
can still further meet the wishes of the
House by putting upon the Table the steps
that have been taken when the House is
sitting we will endeavour to do so.
Mr. ROBB: I am perfectly satisfied.
My sole object was that in case any
Members of this House wished to discuss
any order of the Ministry of Home Affairs,
they should have authoritative information
in their hands so that the criticism might
be well informed, and they should not be
groping in the dark. I have no hesitation
in withdrawing the amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2.-(Short Title and Construction).
(2) This Act may be cited as the Local
Government (Emergency Powers) Act, 1921,
and shall be construed as one with the Local
Government (Ireland) Acts, 1898 to 1919, to
the extent that those Acts are in force in
Northern Ireland.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I beg to move,
on page 2, at end of line 22 [" Northern
Ireland"], to insert the following subsection:-
"This Act shall continue in force for one
year and no longer, unless Parliament otherwise determines."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause 2 as amended ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Title ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Bill as amended reported.
Resolved: "That the Bill be now read
the third time, and passed."
-(Sir Dawson Bates).
Ordered: That the Clerk do carry the
Bill to the Senate and desire their concurrence.

HOUSE OF COMMONS
Salaries.
MINISTRIES OF NORTHERN
IRELAND BILL.
Considered in Committee.
MR. MOLES IN THE CHAIR.
CLAUSE 1 (Administration of Public
Services), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Seal, Style, and Acts of
Ministry.
The following provisions shall have
effect with respect to each Ministry named
as aforesaid, that is to say:—
(1) The Ministry shall be a body corporate, with a capacity to acquire and
hold land for the purpose of the
powers or duties of the Ministry, and
shall be styled by the name set forth
in the first column of the schedule to
this Act, and may sue or be sued in
that name.
(2) The Ministry shall have an official
seal which shall be officially and
judicially noticed, and that seal shall
be authenticated by the signature of
the Minister or of a secretary or
assistant secretary of the Ministry.
(3) In the execution or performance of
its powers or duties the Ministry shall
adopt and use the style and seal of
the Ministry:
Provided that the Ministry may
authorise the use of a special
designation by any officer, officers
or committee to whom any specified
powers or duties have been assigned
by him under this Act.
(4) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, any power or duty of the
Ministry may be exercised or performed by the Minister or by a
secretary or assistant secretary of the
Ministry.
(5) The provisions of this Act with.
respect to a secretary or assistant
secretary of the Ministry shall apply
to-
(a) any officer of the Ministry who
is appointed to be secretary or
assistant secretary' thereof; and
(b) any parliamentary secretary or
assistant parliamentary secretary of the Ministry.
THE PARLIAMENTARY SECRE-
TARY TO THE MINISTRY OF HOME
AFFAIRS (Mr. Megaw): I beg to move
in Clause 2, page 2, line 21, to leave out
"in" (and may sue or be sued in that
name), and insert "by."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Evidence of Acts of
Ministry): Ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Commencement and Effect
of Act): Ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Short Title): Ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
TITLE: Ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Bill, as amended, reported.
Report stage to-morrow.
Speaker of the Senate and Speaker
of the House of Commons.
[Salaries].
SPEAKER OF THE SENATE.
Resolution reported:
"That it is expedient that there be
charged upon the Consolidated Fund or
the growing produce thereof the sum of
One Thousand Pounds per annum in respect of the salary of the Speaker of the
Senate."
Mr. COOTE: On a point of Order, are
the two salaries in the one Motion?
Mr. SPEAKER: There is only one
Motion before the House.
Mr. COOTE: That is the Speaker of
the Senate?
Mr. SPEAKER: That is all.
Mr. COOTE: I wish, Sir, to move an
Amendment-
"That the words in the Motion, One
Thousand Pounds,' be deleted, and that
the words' Seven Hundred and Fifty' be
inserted in lieu thereof."
I wish to move this, because I feel that
the duties of the Senate are very smalla mere sinecure, in comparison with the
duties of the Speaker of this House in
practically all departments of legislation,
and many of us in this House feel very
strongly that the Senate is usurping
monies which, to some extent, are not
earned. We were very reluctant, at the
passing of the Act, in having a Senate at
all for obvious reasons, which are in the
minds of many. We did agree, but we
Salaries. 418
Salaries.
6 DECEMBER 1921.
did not attach and do not attach the
same importance to nor does the country
expect the same work from the Senate and
all its constituent parts as we do expect
from this Legislature, and I feel that the
amount of £1,000 for the Speaker of the
Senate is very much in excess of the duties
devolving upon him. I submit his duties
will not probably in the aggregate two
months engage him more than say two
months, probably less. The work will be
more or less intermittent, but take it in
the aggregate, probably six weeks will
cover all the duties devolving upon the
shoulders of the Speaker of the Senate.
I do not wish to labour the case unduly,
but I do feel strongly in this matter, that
this amount is much too high. If I consulted my own feeling it should be cut
down by half, to £500, and that would
compensate the Speaker of the Senate of
this Northern Area. Look at the amounts
subscribed to the Speakers of the Senates
in the Colonies. Many of them arrange
it in addition to the ordinary salary of an
ordinary Member. In Ontario it is £500
a year, and in New Zealand £600 a year.
I submit in face of this that it is going
too far into the pockets of the taxpayer
when you endow the Speaker's chair-the
Speaker's office in the Senate with
£1,000 a year. I beg leave to move.
Mr. SPEAKER: Does any hon. Member second the Amendment?
Mr. CRAWFORD: I rise with pleasure
to second the Amendment. I am of
opinion that the salary of £1,000 a year
is excessive, and it seems to be out of
reason as far as I can see for the duties he
has to discharge. The taxpayer is already
taxed up to the very hilt, and cannot
stand more, and I sincerely hope the
Government will see its way to accept this
Amendment.
Mr. SPEAKER: The Amendment proposed is that in line two to leave out "One
Thousand Pounds," and to insert the
words "Seven Hundred and Fifty
Pounds.'
The PRIME MINISTER: I would
ask my two hon. Friends not to press this
Amendment. I go back a little in the
history of the matter as respects the transfer to our Parliament of the duties of
Government. My hon. Friend the Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr.
Coote) says he and others were against
having a Senate at all. But the fact remains that the Senate has been handed to
us as part of our Parliamentary machinery,
and we have to deal with the matter as we
find it and not as we might have desired
at one time in the past. My hon. Friend
behind me (Mr. Crawford) says people are
taxed up to the hilt, and cannot stand any
more. I do not think he intentionally
intends to mislead anybody in this matter,
but I may explain here, as I did on one
occasion before, that the fixing up of our
Parliamentary machinery, either of the
Senate or of this House, does not add one
penny to the taxation of the people. My
hon. Friend repeats the charge that this
is added taxation of the people. That is
not so. I explained very fully that we
were allocated by the British Government
a sufficient sum to establish ourselves as
a Parliament, and that the extra cost of
doing that, of paying the Members of the
Government and paying the Speaker of
this House and the Speaker of the Senate
was all allowed for in the Finance White
Paper upon which the whole of our system
is based. That is the explanation; first of
all that there will be no extra charge
upon the people for this. Secondly, I
wish to develop the argument that if we
are to have a Senate, we who have charge
of the purse must treat that Senate in a
somewhat decent manner. After all,
with the exception of my Noble Friend,
who represents education, there is only one
Under-Secretary there for the purpose of
taking charge of Government business.
If anything takes away the noble Lord,
the only other person is the Speaker.
Now, the Speaker in the Senate is to perform the double duties of Speaker
and also Chairman of all Committees, besides which he has placed himself in the
hands of the Government for doing other
work, and I understand from my right
hon. Friend the Minister of Labour, that
he is at present engaged as Chairman of a
very important Committee dealing with
Labour matters. In other words, do not
allow this House to run away with the
idea that those who are taking on this
particular duty at the moment and trying
to erect and establish our Parliamentary
institution, are doing so for personal gain,
or because they desire something which
they cannot get by living in their own
private capacities, and it will be ill-fitting
this House to start on its career by denying to one of the only three who are paid
in the Upper House what we consider is
a fair and reasonable salary. For, after
Bill Presented.
Bill Presented.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
Salaries.
[Mr. Coote.]
all, it is very technical work, and I would
urge upon both my hon. Friends-my
hon. Friend the Member for Tyrone and
Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) knows it well,
that it is not merely taking the chair, as
the generally accepted chairman, so much
as it is making oneself acquainted with all
past rules and regulations, all the minor
duties of Government, and all the fabric
and the building of those measures which
pass into law. It is an onerous office
and a high position to be Speaker of the
Senate or Speaker of this House. If we
are ever driven to find that by taxation
you are unable to carry on, I guarantee to
say it will not be because the Government
has been extravagant in tempting the very
best people to come forward to help Ulster
in its present difficult situation. I do
hope my hon. Friends will not press this
to a division, but allow it to be said the
Lower House treated the Speaker of the
Upper House with great consideration, in
allowing what we consider to be a fair
salary to be passed without any
acrimonious debate in this House.
Mr. COOTE: I am not prepared to
divide the House in a matter of this kind.
Personally, I have made my protest and
given my reason for it. I do not wish to
proceed further.
Mr. SPEAKER: Does the hon. Member wish to withdraw the Amendment?
Mr. COOTE: I do.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question: "That the House doth agree
with the Committee in the said resolution," put, and agreed to.
Ordered: "That a Bill be brought in
upon the said Resolution, and that the
Prime Minister (supported by Mr. Milne
Barbour, Captain Dixon, and Mr. Burn)
do prepare and bring it in."
BILL PRESENTED.
Speaker of the Senate [Salary] Bill to
provide for the payment of a Salary to the
Speaker of the Senate.-(The Prime
Minister).
Read a First time. Ordered to be read
a Second time to-morrow, and to be
printed.
Salaries.
SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF
COMMONS.
Resolution Reported:

That it is expedient that there be
charged upon the Consolidated Fund, or
the growing produce thereof, the sum of
Two Thousand Pounds per annum in respect of the salary of the Speaker of the
House of Commons.'
Mr. COOTE rose-
Mr. SPEAKER: I know the hon.
Member has no intention of referring personally to me, but I think, perhaps, he
and others would feel it more appropriate
if I left the Chair.
Whereupon the Chairman of Ways and
Means (Mr. Moles) took the Chair as
Deputy Speaker, pursuant to the Standing Order.
Mr. COOTE: I wish to move the
following amendment:

"That the words Two Thousand
Pounds appearing in the Resolution be
deleted, and that the words 'Fifteen
Hundred Pounds" be inserted in lieu
thereof."
I wish to say absolutely freely that of
all the amendments which it has been my
duty to participate in there was none I
felt greater regret in moving than the
amendment I now move. We are dealing, of course, with the office, and, therefore, we must deal with the matter from
our heads, and not altogether from our
hearts. If we gauged matters by our
hearts there is nothing we would consider
too good for the hon. and learned Gentleman who presides over the affairs of this
House. Since his election to the position
of Speaker, he has added dignity to his
career, and he has discharged his duties
here with very great ability and efficiency
and assistance to the Members. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) I am sure I am
speaking for the united House when I
say he is just the Gentleman we expected
him to be, always did expect him to be,
and look forward to him being
when he presides over the destinies
of this House. But we
dealing with the other side of the case
as well. We must observe a due sense of
proportion. We must not allow our
hearts to carry us away beyond that which
is right and proper in the circumstances.
I should not press this amendment so
much were it not for the fact that the
amount with which we endow the
are
Speakers' Salaries. 422
Speakers' Salaries.
6 DECEMBER 1921.
Speaker's Chair to-day will be a tax upon
the Consolidated Fund of this Northern
Area, and, therefore, will not be reducible in the days to come. We cannot
attack the Speaker's salary twelve months
hence, or two years hence, as we can
attack the Prime Minister's salary or the
salary of the other Cabinet Ministers or
Parliamentary Secretaries. The Speaker's salary is almost outside the purview
of this House as soon as we agree to it,
and for this reason I ask you to pause
and consider before you endow this office
with any larger sum than in your hearts
you realise is just the amount there ought
to be for the position of Speaker of this
House of Commons.
I listened to the Prime Minister. I
hope he is right when he stated that the
Government is to be set up free of cost to
the taxpayers of the Northern Area. I
cannot understand that statement. I
hope the Prime Minister will correct me if
I am wrong, but my ideas of the situation
were and are that the Imperial Government have given us a sum with which to
start housekeeping. But does the Prime.
Minister tell us that for all the days that
are to come the salaries of the officers of
this House, including the Speakers, are
to be paid by the British Treasury, entirely apart from the cost to the taxpayers
of this country? As I said at the outset,
I doubt it. In fact, I am afraid there is
some mistake. It is the Consolidated
Fund that is the subject matter of
discussion now. Was not this Consolidated Fund created by ourselves here?
Did we not in the previous acts of this
House create a small Consolidated Fund,
and are these sums and the sums that will
be voted from time to time not chargeable
to it, and the British Government has
nothing whatever to do with it? Viewing
the matter in this light I ask myself can I
remain silent and allow this salary of the
Speaker to be made a charge for all time,
so to speak, on the Consolidated Fund,
salary of £2,000 a year and £500 for a
house, when we have taken up a whole
evening debating the question of the
salaries of the Members of the Cabinet
whom we objected to receiving a higher
sum than £1,500? I submit the Speaker
should have as great a salary as any of
the ordinary Ministers, but no greater.
I submit that the Chancellorship of the
Exchequer is as arduous a position as that
of the Speaker. I submit that practically
all the other offices stand in the same
category, and the action that we take in
a
onę instance we should take all round.
Therefore some of us felt it our duty to
put down this amendment. In this case
the Ministry comes in here and asks Parliament to endow the position of Speaker
with the sum of £500 in excess of not what
we offered to them, but what they asked
for themselves. I think that is an untenable position for the Government to
take up here to-day. They are attempting to endow the Speaker with £500 in
excess of any of their salaries, with the
exception of the Prime Minister, and, if
I may say so, he is apart altogether from
the others, and is in a different category
entirely. The Speaker need not necessarily devote all his time to this House.
He can be a Member of the Imperial
Parliament in addition to being the
Speaker of this House. Our present
Speaker is at the present time a Member
of the Imperial Parliament, and if he
wishes he can continue to occupy that
position. There is nothing to hinder him
taking part in the affairs of the Imperial
Parliament, or he can take up other
callings, other business in life, congenial
to his tastes and ability without in any
way interfering with the position of
Speaker. For this reason I suggest that
£1,500, plus £500 for a house, is a very
adequate salary for our Speaker.
Let us see how it works out in other
countries. In Canada, with a population
of 8,000,000, the Speaker enjoys a salary
of £1,200 a year, I suppose in addition to
the salary of an ordinary Member, but I
am not sure on that point, and I wish to
give the question all reasonable credit.
In Ontario, with
a population of
2,636,000, the Speaker enjoys a salary of
£600 a year, in addition, of course, to
the salary of an ordinary Member of
Parliament. In Quebec, with a population of 2,600,000, he enjoys a salary of
£800 a year.
In New Zealand, which is
very often quoted in this House in connection with these matters, he enjoys a
salary of £800 a year. I do hope the
Government will reconsider their proposal. It will be very unfortunate-it
is much against our own feelings-to
divide the House on the Speaker's salary.
I do not think it would be dignified; 1
do not think it would be gentlemanly;
I think it is the last thing we should do.
I appeal to the Government to do the
reasonable thing, and not to allow any of
us to rush into extreme action of this
kind. The office is about to be specially
endowed beyond that of the ordinary
cc
Salaries.
Salaries.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
Minister. I submit that is unfair-unfair to the Ministers, unfair to this House,
unfair to the country. I do not want to
harp on the old string-it is there all the
same although we never refer to it-that
the country is looking on, and that in
these days of stress and strain they are
watching all these salaries and all these
questions, and remember there will be a
day of accounting for all this. I do
appeal strongly to the Ministry not to
rush this matter to the extremity, but
to find some way by which they will reduce
this salary to normal size, and allow us to
pass it unanimously. I beg to move:
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I must confess at
the outset that I have no great cordiality
in seconding the amendment proposed by
the hon. Gentleman (Mr. Coote), but I
feel after all that he has been an advocate
of economy all along, and it would be
hardly fair to his good intentions that
the amendment on this occasion should be
allowed to fall without a seconder. The
only objection that I have to what is
recommended by the Government in this
matter is that the Speaker is being paid in
excess of what is paid to any Cabinet
Minister. I do not like the idea of the
Speaker being paid more than is paid to
any Minister in the Government, with the
exception of the Prime Minister, who, as
the hon. Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) has said, is outside
the category we are discussing at the
moment. Even in the Imperial House
of Commons the Speaker is not paid more
than a Minister. I do not regard myself
as second to any person or any Member
of the House in admiration of the
talents and ability of the Speaker.
It just happens as a strange coincidence
that the mover and seconder of the amendment read this afternoon moved and
seconded the Speaker to the Chair, and it
would ill become either of us to say one
word in detraction of the Speaker. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) I would be the
last to be associated with anything derogatory regarding him, and I assure you it is
from no sinister motive, or any unworthy
opinion of the Speaker, that we present
this proposal. The Cabinet Minister has
got £1,500, and I have entered my protest
against the scheme of adding a further
£500 by reason of his being included in
the Cabinet. Now had the recommendation put forward to the House been that
the Ministers be paid £2,000 I would not
have felt this afternoon that I would be
entitled to associate myself with this resolution, upon the ground that I thought
the Speaker was receiving just equivalent
to the Minister. But it is rather odd that
you have £1,500 associated with a specific
office and £500 paid in respect of
his being a Member of the Cabinet. I share
the feeling of the majority of the House
to-day that this should not be pushed to a
division, and if pushed to a division,
I would not feel justified in
voting for it. The right hon.
Gentleman who has just sat down is entitled to his views and to say every word
he has spoken in the matter.
We are
going in for economy. The public at large
will scrutinise very carefully everything
we do in such matters. I do not expect the
hon. Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh
will press the matter to a division. I hope
not. I would not like to do anything that
would be derogatory to the Chair. I have
much pleasure in seconding.
I
Sir ROBERT ANDERSON: I find
myself on this occasion in disagreement
with the proposer, the hon. Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh, and the seconder,
the hon. Member for North Belfast.
regret extremely that this whole question
of allowances to be made to Mr. Speaker
has been the subject of any controversy
in this House. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) The Speaker of Parliament stands.
above party; he stands above the Cabinet;
he stands in a peculiar relationship to the
House. That, I think, has been lost sight
of in connection with this whole matter.
I do not wish to enter into any lengthened
reply to any of the speeches that have been
made, but I sincerely hope that the hon.
Members who have raised the matter will
not for a single moment think of dividing
the House. It has been suggested the
Speaker should be paid £2,000 with
£500 for house allowance. Comparison
has been made with Cabinet Ministers.
There is no comparison in my judgment
whatsoever. The Speaker cannot be compared to a Cabinet Minister.. His
functions are entirely different, and he
stands in a peculiar relationship to this
House that should not be lost sight of.
It is not necessary for me to go into the
various duties he has to perform, or into
comparisons in regard to other places.
We should bear in mind the vote of this
House last Thursday in regard to other
Members, and the decision arrived at then
by the majority of the House. Having
regard to the dignity of the office of
Speaker and its importance, I
do not think the proposal is unreason-
425 Speakers' Salaries.

6 DECEMBER 1921.
Salaries.
able, and I think it ought to commend
itself to the House. I think we should
have no controversy in regard to the
Speaker. We should also bear in mind
the fact that the Speaker has fulfilled the
duties pertaining to his office with the
greatest dignity, efficiency, and, I think,
to the satisfaction of every Member of
this House. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
The PRIME MINISTER: I would like
to commend what the hon. Member for
Londonderry has said, and to ask the
House to arrive at a unanimous conclusion in this instance where our Speaker
is concerned. May I say that we as a
Government treated the House, I think,
with very great consideration in the steps
that we took in regard to these matters.
In the first instance we did not settle
upon the salary that we thought appropriate for Mr. Speaker until a Committee
had been set up to deal with the salaries
of Ministers and Parliamentary Secretaries, and the reason that we took that
step was out of consideration to all
quarters of this House, for we felt that,
as in the British House of Commons the
salary of the Speaker is exactly the same
as the salary of first-class Ministers, if we
did come down to this House and ask this
House to support a motion to pay a salary,
whatever it might be, to Mr. Speaker it
might be taken by the Committee which
the House itself set up as an indication of
the class of salary which should be paid to
Ministers on this Bench.
to
Therefore, we took the proper step,
and, I think, the step that will commend
itself to all quarters of the House, because
far from giving any indication
the Committee set up with regard to the
salaries of Ministers, we established that
Committee first of all, and made no provision for Mr. Speaker until the Report
was made to the House. And with regard
to that, I would like to point out to hon.
Members who may have forgotten that
that Committee was established by the
House itself, that there was no Member
of the Government upon it, and not a
Member of the Government was consulted
in any way
whilst the Committee was
undertaking its labours. Therefore, we
here on this Bench had no more knowledge whatever of what was suggested by
the Committee than any hon. Member
until the Report was placed on the Table
of the House.
But that Committee having reported,
and having fixed the salaries of first-class
Ministers in your Government, we felt
that it would be inappropriate and
against the wishes of this House that Mr.
Speaker should draw a less salary than
those of Ministers which have already
been arranged for by the Committee.
My hon. Friend, the Member
Tyrone and Fermanagh, and his seconder
have fallen again into error in talking
about £500 in addition to the
£2,000. The custom has always been
that the Speaker of the House of
Commons is supplied with a residence.
for
Mr. M'GUFFIN: Permit me to say I
made no reference to the amount allocated
with respect to residence.
The PRIME MINISTER: No; it was
my hon. Friend, the Member for Tyrone
and Fermanagh.
Mr. COOTE: It is in the Estimates.
The PRIME MINISTER: I wish to
clear this up at once. The hon. Member
for Tyrone and Fermanagh mentioned
that in addition to £2,000 this House
votes an additional £500 for the Speaker.
That £500 does not fall upon the votes of
this House at all. It is in lieu of a residence which the British Government have
promised to supply, as is always done in
every part of the world where there is a
Parliament. A residence will be attached
to and incorporated in the new Houses of
Parliament as a residence for the Speaker.
But, pending the provision of that, under
the Act of 1920, the British Government
are simply paying Mr. Speaker £500 a
year, because they cannot get a house
built for perhaps three or four years.
soon as the house is built, and Mr.
Speaker goes into it, that £500 is wiped
out; and although that sum appears on
the Votes here, it appears on the Votes to
be immediately wiped out by a credit from
the British Government to meet the
obligations under the Act of 1920. So
that all this House and all that the
Northern Parliament and the people of
Ulster are responsible for is the salary
which is embodied in the motion now
before the House.
As
There is another point. My hon.
Friend makes a great deal of the fact that
this goes on the Consolidated Fund. It
goes on the Consolidated Fund, because
the moment Mr. Speaker takes the Chair
he becomes non-party. Politics are left
Salaries.
Salaries.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Prime Minister.]
on one side, and he then becomes more in
the position of a judge of the High Court
than any other high position with which
one can draw an analogy. It is in order
to place him on an equally high plane
with the judges that he is placed, so far
as his salary is concerned, upon the Consolidated Fund. In other words, instead
of it coming up year after year like a
minister's salary, and being subject as a
minister's salary is, and quite properly,
subject to attack, it is placed on one side,
and the Speaker is placed on a plane
above the political element and above the
attacks which may be made from one
portion of the House or another.
I trust, under the circumstances, if my
hon. Friend, is satisfied with my explanation with regard to the £500, and
satisfied that at any time by bringing forward a motion he can raise the question of
the Speaker's salary (though I hope this
will not be considered necessary, but that
the House will bear in mind the
Speaker's high office, the highest indeed
that could be occupied), he will allow the
House to be unanimous in coming to a
decision.
Mr. COOTE: I am delighted, indeed,
to hear the explanation of the Prime
Minister. It clears the air considerably.
In face of the explanation that this £500
does not go as a direct charge upon this
House, but that it is coming in the way
which he has explained from the British
Treasury, and for the reasons which he
has explained, I do not think any of us
wish to press the matter. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear). We agree that anything that can contribute to the dignity
of the office and to
the
the comfort and
But, my hon. Friend is wrong in assuming that there is no method of attacking
that salary at any time. Exactly in the
same way as one can attack the salaries of
the judges of the High Court so one can
attack the salary of the Speaker by
putting a motion on the Table and asking
the Government of the day to give a day
for it to be discussed, and in that
way
House keeps complete control over the
salaries of the Speakers of both Houses in
exactly the same way as they do with regard to the salaries of Ministers.
In conclusion, I would like to add a
word to what has been so admirably said
by the hon. Member for Londonderry that
we here should congratulate ourselves on
having secured the services of our present
Speaker. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
It was well known in the British House of
Commons that he had a brilliant career
before him if he had remained in London.
His house was there, and he was settled
down. He gave it all up. He made no
bargain with anybody. He gave it all up
to come here to help us to establish our
Northern Parliament. I feel, and others
feel, that the success of our Parliamentary
institutions in future depends to an
enormous extent upon the way in which
we begin. If we begin with the dignity,
with the fairness, and with the regularity
which characterise the Mother of Parliaments, I feel that our institution will be
a great success and a credit to the Ulster
people. But if we had not got the right
man for the position at the present time,
a man who is the very essence of courtesy,
honour and of straightforward dealing
with all people, who knows he has to pay
great attention to the minority as well as
to the majority—if we had not got a man
of that type we would not set forth in the
fair way in which we are now doing.
encouragement of Mr. Speaker, who holds
the office, should gladly come from this
House.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: As seconder of the
motion, permit me to say I cordially endorse the course taken by the mover in
this matter.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question: "That this House doth agree
with the Committee in the said Resolution," put, and agreed to.
Ordered: "That a Bill be brought in
upon the said Resolution, and that the
Prime Minister (supported by Mr. Milne
Barbour, Captain Dixon, and Mr. Burn)
do prepare and bring it in.”
BILL PRESENTED.
Speaker of the House of Commons
[Salary] Bill, "to provide for the payment of a salary to the Speaker of the
House of Commons."-The Prime
Minister.]
Read a First time. Ordered to be read
a Second time to-morrow, and to be
printed.
The remaining Orders were read and
postponed.

THE IRISH NEGOTIATIONS.
MOTION FOR ADJOURnment.
Captain DIXON: I beg to move :
this House do now adjourn."
"That
The PRIME MINISTER: In accordance
with the principle I have frequently laid
down, and which I intend to continue, I
think it is only right to inform the House
that I received intimation early this morning that a special messenger was proceeding from London with new proposals to be
placed before my colleagues and myself. I
summoned a meeting of our Cabinet, and
we waited these new proposals practically
all morning till the House met. They did
not reach me. Since the House has met I
have received a message to say that one of
His Majesty's vessels has sailed the
up
Lough and has brought these proposals. I
have not yet even broken the seal of the
envelope owing to the necessity I felt to be
here to-day. I can only say that the
Cabinet will now proceed to peruse these
new proposals, and we will take what action
we think is in the best interests of the
people whom we represent, and needless to
say this House will be informed at the
earliest possible moment consistent with
what we consider is the best procedure in
the matter of the proposals and of our reply
to them. I cannot say any more to-day.
This has only just reached me, but I propose
to keep the House fully informed of all
that takes place.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I am quite sure we
all feel satisfied with the announcement
made by the Prime Minister. It is as
explicit as possible under the circumstances. But I should like to ask him
whether we are to understand that he is
free to publish the correspondence that
has taken place between the Cabinet and
the Sinn Fein representatives and our
own Parliament in connection with the
negotiations?
Mr. COOTE: May I further ask the
Prime Minister in view of the fact that
the Parliament is in Session and these
proposals have now arrived, will he now
bring the proposals with a recommendation from the Cabinet before this House
for their decision before a reply is sent to
these proposals to the Prime Minister of
the British House of Commons.
The PRIME MINISTER: In reply to
the first question, I have already asked
that our correspondence with the British
Government should be published. I do
not know what the reply will be to that.
With regard to the second question, of
course my hon. Friend knows that it is
quite impossible for me to indicate
what steps the Government would think
proper until we have considered the proposals. He may rest assured that the same
frankness and the same openness with
which we have dealt with the House hitherto will be carried out in this connection as
well. I should also say that the notice in
the Press this morning was to the effect
that the whole proposals would be published in the Press to-morrow morning, and
therefore each hon. Member will be in possession of them to-morrow morning, although we, the Cabinet, will have the opportunity of studying them this evening.
Question: "That this House do now
adjourn." Put and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at four
minutes after Four of the
Clock.

HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Wednesday, 7th December, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Orders of the Day.
MINISTRIES OF NORTHERN IRE-
LAND BILL
Bill reported; as amended, considered;
read the Third time, and passed.

the Bill to the Senate, and desire their
concurrence."

Resolved: "That the Bill be now read
the Second time.'

House for to-morrow.

COMMONS (SALARY) BILL.
Order for Second Reading read.
Resolved: "That the Bill be now read
the Second time.'
Committed to a Committee of the whole
House for to-morrow."
