And that it is expedient that there be
charged as aforesaid in respect of the
salaries of the persons (other than the
Prime Minister) being members of the
Executive Committee of Northern Ireland (commonly called the Cabinet) a
further annual sum not exceeding £2,500
to be divided equally amongst the Members of that Executive Committee, provided that no member thereof shall
receive, in his capacity as such Member,
a greater annual salary than £500."-
(The Parliamentary and Financial Secretary to the Minister of Finance (Mr.
Barbour.)
Mr. COOTE: I wish simply to protest
in this matter. I do not wish, in face
of what happened before, to divide the
House on the question of these salaries,
and I propose to take up as little time
as possible. I want, however, to draw
Ministers' attention to the fact, and to
make my protest, that there are some
salaries and some charges in this list which
are too high, and which make those of us
Ministerial Salaries.
Salaries.
7 DECEMBER 1921.
1S
who had the courage to go into the Lobby
feel that we were justified in doing so.
There are many others in the House who
feel that the charges are excessive, but who
did not wish to take such strong measures.
There are some £2,200 chargeable for the
cost of the Whipping of both Houses, and
if you take that as a sum per head of the
Members it works out at something like
£40. If you contrast that with
any other assembly, especially with the
British House of Commons, with all its
high salaries, you will find it is done there
at a much cheaper rate than that at which
it is proposed to be done in this schedule.
We say that the sum we had put down in
our amendment of £1,400, namely £600
for the Parliamentary Secretary, £400
for the assistant, and £400 for the
Parliamentary Secretary to the Prime
Minister, was a reasonable sum, and we
think that these sums in the list are
excessive for the cost of whipping both
Houses. In regard to the Parliamentary
Secretaries we think the sum
much in excess. There is one feature
which we did not present to this
House, one argument in favour of
a lower figure in connection with the
Parliamentary Secretaries. It is that the
position of Parliamentary Secretary is one
that is sought after by the type of public
man and politician which the Prime
Minister outlined in one of his earlier
speeches in this House, that is a man of
energy and of youth who wishes to get
higher. I do not believe there is a
Parliamentary Secretary to-day who does
not expect one day to be a full-blown
Cabinet Minister, and I should be sorry
if he had any other ambition before
him when he sets out on his career. Ι
submit that the very fact of becoming a
Parliamentary Secretary to a Ministry of
this House is in itself a very great
dignity and a very great honour, and
should not be altogether measured by the
money, and that the sum itself is much
too high a figure to be paid for the office
in this House.
take
I grant you at once that, if
you
some of the men who are Parliamentary
Secretaries and segregate their time from
their business, the sum here would not
pay them.
But it is not for money they
are in this House. It is to serve Northern
Ireland. It is to do something for their
common country, and to bring their ripe
and weighty experience to the work of
this House. But to many of us the sum
is much more than that with which the
office should be endowed, compatible with
the fact that it is a stepping stone to
higher honours that may be within their
reach if they are capable men. I hold
the view that if and when this House
may resolve to give some remuneration to
ordinary Members that a few hundred
pounds extra should be enough with
which to endow these minor offices, that
the office itself and the honour that
surrounds it ought to be a sufficiently
laudable prize for the ambition of any
man who wishes to get on in this House,
and that everything should not be valued
by mere money.
I also submit that the Parliamentary
Secretaries, and, indeed, the Ministers
generally if they have time, but the
Parliamentary Secretaries especially, are
not debarred, but will have time for
attending to other branches of business.
It is not to be expected that they will
devote their whole time through the year
to the Ministries which they represent.
Their time will be simply occupied; or for
the most part occupied, during the time
this House is sitting, and, therefore, they
will have a good deal of time on their
hands, and will be able to pursue some
other calling in life which is congenial
to their tastes, or probably by which they
are living at the present time. We object
very strongly to the distribution by way
of bonus of £2,500 to be given to the
Members of the Cabinet who are not
We
merely heads of departments.
believe this is another way of trying to
keep the salary at £1,500 a year when
really it means £2,000. Why should this
be placed here as it is? Why should not
the Government say plainly "We propose
to give Cabinet Ministers £2,000 a year?"
It is not a plain business statement on the
face of it, or such a one as we should
expect. For these reasons I have
troubled the House with my protest today. I do not wish to move an amendment, but simply to make my protest
against these various items which I have
mentioned in this list of salaries.
Mr. DONALD: I would not be consistent with my principles if I did not rise
to-day, having regard to the fact that I
supported a few days ago the amendment
for the reduction of these salaries. I do
not agree with my hon. Friend who spoke
last that we should not divide the House
on this question. I think we should divide
the House. Since our last debate I took
the trouble to look up some statistics with
reference to financial matters. I find that
the Select Committee appointed a deputa-
Salaries.
Salaries.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Donald.]
tion, which went to London to interview
certain representatives there--
The CHAIRMAN: I think I must ask
the hon. Member to bear in mind that we
are not discussing the Second Reading.
All this would have been pertinent to the
earlier discussion upon this matter.
But
it is not desirable that the debate should
rove again over the identical ground that
has been discussed at length.
Mr. DONALD: I do not wish to do so
at this juncture. But I will refer to a
gentleman who was interviewed on identical business with this question of salaries
of the permanent heads of departments.
The CHAIRMAN: I think the hon.
Member will see that I wish to give him
the utmost latitude. But that is not the
question we are discussing. We are discussing the exact terms that are set down
in this resolution, and no other matters.
Mr. DONALD: Very good. I would
like rather to look into the future, and to
what is going to happen within the next
twelve months, with regard to all these
salaries. Further, I think it is necessary
to look into the past with regard to
financial matters in this country of ours.
In 1910-11 we had a deficiency of some
thing like one million pounds--
The CHAIRMAN: This is getting entirely away from the resolution before us.
I would be delighted to hear the hon.
Member if it were in order, and so would
the Committee, but my business is to keep
to the matter that immediately engages
our attention.
Mr. DONALD: I am sorry if I have to
come up against the Chair so frequently,
but of course I will gain experience in
time. My right hon. Friend (the Prime
Minister) stated in the House something
about where these salaries were to be derived from. At the moment I am not quite
clear. Apparently the money is not coming
off the taxpayers of Ulster. But the
money is forthcoming from a grant or surplus to be given to tide over these expenses.
I am not satisfied with regard to these
high salaries and setting up new machinery
by new people. The only one really who
has any Parliamentary experience is my
right hon. Friend the Prime Minister. I
think we should have started a little lower
with regard to the salaries of both
Departments-the heads of Departments
and Parliamentary secretaries. Really
I do not know what is going to
happen in the near future with
the
regard to all those-not only in
Ministry but the staffs in each of the
departments-the whole horde of officials
who are going to be set up at enormous
expense.
The CHAIRMAN-May I ask my hon.
Friend to bear with me a moment. We
are discussing here specific salaries of the
specific officials indicated on this paper,
and not a horde of officials whose salaries
are not before us.
Mr. DONALD: May I ask how you
arrive at the £1,500, and how the other
£500 is made up with regard to the heads
of departments; also what amount is likely
to be charged for the expenses-if expenses
are going to be paid-of travelling to and
fro, here and there.
The CHAIRMAN: There will come a
time, as the hon. Member knows, to discuss
these matters. I cannot permit the discussion to travel outside the items on the
paper, and I must ask the hon. Member to
confine his attention to these matters.
The PRIME MINISTER: Perhaps I
shall be able to satisfy my hon. Friend
opposite. I know exactly what information he wants. As you have quite rightly
ruled, when we come to the Estimates
which will set forth each department, each
Minister, each under-secretary, each Parliamentary secretary, and the staff necessary
to carry on the work-I think that will
be the proper time to raise any question.
He need not think for a moment it is
the desire of the Government or anyone
else to prevent having the frankest possible
discussion on these very important
financial considerations. May I, with regard to the statement of the hon. Men.ber
for Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote),
point out, first of all, that with reference
to the sums set out here for Parliamentary
secretaries, to which he seemed to take the
greatest exception, my idea for the future
is that Young Ulster will be trained up to
take her part in the Government and the
Parliamentary institutions of Northern
Ireland, and that it would be "ossible for
some to make a career for themselves by
mastering all the intricate details of public
life, in order to be able to serve Ulster in
the future. Now if we take exception to
the figure of a thousand pounds a year, for
a Parliamentary secretary that becomes
utterly impossible, and it means that we
in difficult circumstances might only be
able to turn to rich men to fill these important positions. It would be the greatest
Salaries.
7 DECEMBER 1921.
Salaries.

for all time. Far from it-as the
Estimates come up each year they are
headed by the Ministers' salaries. That is
a very old established principle in
Ministerial procedure, and we are adopting it here.
Mr. DONALD: It is fixing a precedent.
mistake for a Government to differentiate
in any way either in its Ministers or in its
Parliamentary secretaries between those
who are blessed with great wealth and
those who live upon the income allocated
to the positions which they hold. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) That would
happen if you say I cannot
get the man I want to accept
this particular position, because he already
holds a position which assures to him and
to his family a decent livelihood. After
all it is not the individual; he may have a
wife, he may have a family, he may be
settled down in a different part of the
country, and he may be the very man to
cccupy the position and serve Ulster
best. He says, "I am exceedingly sorry I
am not a rich man, and I have a position
that at present brings me and my family
in so much a year. I cannot sacrifice that,
not because my whole heart and soul are
not with Ulster, but because it means
throwing up a certain amount of my
livelihood for my wife and my family."
Then you have to search about among
the richer men, who, although they are
blessed with riches in the material sense
may not be blessed with the very riches
that we require. I refer to the riches of
experience and knowledge-education it
may be or some other particularly delicate
question. You will not be able to get what
you want in riches. And, therefore, I
hold that in adopting the report of your
own Committee, a Committee of this
House with which we have nothing whatever to do, and not in fixing these salaries
ourselves, we are taking a wise step in
the interests of the future of Ulster. May
I
say also that there is a very great difference between the occupation of a Parliamentary secretary or Minister in the
Government and any other business.
Political life is sometimes very short, and
you ask a man to accept a salary of this
sort, it may be for nine months, and that
nine months is enough to break up his
business so that he cannot take it up again
at the end of that period. Therefore, I
say, with all these considerations, the report of the Committee on which this
resolution is based is a moderate living,
and I hope that the House will not hesitate
to support the Government in putting it
forward. My hon. Friend, the Member
for the East Division of Belfast (Mr.
Donald), seems to think that this
stereotypes the salaries for everybody
The PRIME MINISTER: Each year
the hon. Members may raise this question
as to whether a Minister deserves his salary
or not, and my colleagues and I, who are
here present to-day, will be quite willing
at the end of the year to stand criticism
as to whether we deserve what is placed
upon this motion here to-day. We are
finding it very difficult indeed to establish
our new departments, and to get on with
the very onerous work attached to the
Government. It is new to many of us.
Unfortunately the health of my right
honourable Friend the Minister of Finance
has broken down. I will not say for a
moment his health has broken down in
consequence of the heavy weight of responsibility he had to bear, but the very fact
that we have embodied these various
Parliamentary Secretaries in the motion.
to-day has been justified to the full. My
honourable Friend behind me (Mr. Barbour), as Parliamentary Secretary, has
had to bear the very heavy and onerous
responsibility of the Minister of Finance
since the Services have been taken over,
and even before that date. I, myself, am
extremely anxious to meet the House where
it is possible for economy's sake, but on
the whole I feel satisfied, after considering
the grave responsibility and enormous
amount of work which is attached to these
offices, that we were wisely advised in
adopting the list as it stands upon the
paper. Future years may make it necessary to have something lower; they may
require to have something higher, but that
will be for future years to decide. So far
as we are concerned we are simply adopting the recommendation of a Committee
which went into the whole matter with
great thoroughness.
Mr. COOTE:: Pardon me. are you
satisfied with the £2,200 for the Whips?
The PRIME MINISTER: I am perfectly satisfied with all the salaries that
have been put down upon the Paper. My
hon. Friend (Mr. Coote) does not quite
recognise the responsibility of the Whips.
In time he may. It is a very anxious
DD
Salaries.
Salaries.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Prime Minister.]
period. We all know in the present conditions the Whips' duty is not confined
simply to marshalling together the Members in support of the Government in this
House. The duty of the Whip is very
onerous outside the House as well as inside, and this House would be the very
first to experience the want either of a
Whip or of ability in the Whip. Therefore, we must consider the
matter
not from the point of view of the
great wealth of my hon. Friend, who is at
present occupying that position, but you
have to consider it from the point of view
of the office.
Let it be considered from the office, so
that you will not have to turn, as I say,
to those very wealthy men. We want to
be able to say to any person in the Six
Counties, a position is open to
because
you
there is a salary attached to it, at all
events, which will enable you to keep up
the dignity of the office. That is my
strong recommendation to the Committee
here that they adopt these figures. We
considered all the points raised by the
critics, and which have been raised today.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I would only regard
it as an incumbent duty to intervene in
the debate at the moment to justify the
position I have already taken up with
regard to the procedure of this House in
respect of finance. The only salary set
forth that I unequivocally support is the
salary of the Prime Minister. We do not
believe that he is in any sense overpaid,
and perhaps some of us would go the
length of suggesting he was not sufficiently
highly paid, for we know that there will be
a great drain upon his private means in
respect to the discharge of his high and
distinguished office. But having said
that, I am afraid I cannot accede
further. I admit that the salaries set
forth here are such as the House should
adopt. Fifteen hundred pounds in
respect of the several Ministers who have
Cabinet rank at the moment hardly represents to the House the sum that is bound
to be paid in respect of their offices. There
is £500 to be paid in respect of being
Members of the Cabinet. That is not set
forth here at all.
The PRIME MINISTER: Yes, at the
bottom.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I admit it is, but it
is rather misleading to have it that £1,500
is to be paid in respect of being head of a
particular Department, while £500 accrues
by reason of being a Member of the
Cabinet. The two offices, apparently, are
not associated. That is to say, a man—a
particular man, may be at the head of a
Department but not a Member of the
Cabinet, and is, therefore, confined to the
salary set forth here. On the other hand
he may be a Member of the Cabinet and
a Minister at the same time, and be entitled to £2,000. If that is the case with
respect to particular Ministers, I submit
it is only right it should be set forth as
£2,000 rather than £1,500. With regard
to the salaries payable in respect of Parliamentary Secretaries, I have only to say
that in almost every case, if it was the
measure of commercial value outside, it
would be admitted freely all round they
are overpaid. Is there a Parliamentary
Secretary in this list here, if he were an
applicant for a similar position in a mercantile department, who could command
the salary we are proposing to pay him in
respect to duties of which he must
necessarily be in the novitiate stage? I
do not think there is any Member, in all
probability, who at present supports the
salaries that are going to be paid in respect of the Under-Secretaries, would himself, if he were in the position requiring
such an individual, be prepared to pay
£1,000. Take the case of a very capable
and efficient director, serving continuously
from morning till night on every day of
the week and every month in the year in
a very important business-he could not
command £1,000 at the moment, and yet
we freely vote in respect of this office
£1,000, an office which in all probability
will only engage the occupant two or three
months in the year. Is it quite fair?
The PRIME MINISTER: Perhaps my
hon. Friend will allow me to point out that
work is carried on when the House is not
sitting.
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I would be sorry to
say it unjustly.
The PRIME MINISTER: I am sure of
it. No man has a keener sense of justice
than my hon. Friend. When this House
is not sitting the Members of the Government do not go over to Riviera or elsewhere to kick up their heels until Parliament meets again. The Government of a
country must go on, and Ministers and
Parliamentary and Under-Secretaries
engaged practically all the time in their
are
Salaries.
Salaries.
7 DECEMBER 1921.
particular work. It is
not
a matter
merely just of attending the House to
It is the management
answer questions.
of a Department.
un-
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I accept
reservedly the modification as presented
by the Prime Minister. Perhaps I have
gone too far, but I do insist in saying they
are not as much engaged while this House
is out of Session as when it is in Session.
I am bound to take that opinion, Sir.
The point has been raised by the Prime
Minister that this is a sort of contingent
Department; that a man may be here only
a short time, and that perhaps he has to
face a change of Government of some sort
by which he will be dispossessed of his
office. But the same thing is true of any
position in the mercantile world—no man
is assured. I never occupied a position
without contingencies in my life. I did
not hold it upon any assurance. A fortnight's notice was always between me and
the door at any moment. And I do not
think that that is a suggestion this House
will entertain, that because of the mere
contingencies of employment we should
pay a man more than in his office he is
entitled to. Then we have to consider
these Ministers are inexperienced-the
Under-Secretaries, the Parliamentary
Secretaries, and indeed that to a large
extent applies also to Ministers.
I know
that some of our most efficient Ministers
must have foregone many business
advantages in order to take up the
positions they occupy. I have always
recognised that, but after all the
sacrifices a man has entailed in taking
over a position should be no reason why
we should overpay a position or a post
lest, peradventure, a little later on some
person may come in to the position who
has not made similar sacrifice, and he will
be paid at a high remuneration.
With regard to the salaries contemplated to be paid to the Whips of the
House, I cannot offer any criticism on that
point. £2,200 for Whipping a House of
48 Members seems to be a large expenditure indeed, and probably, out of proportion to what is paid in the Imperial
House of Commons. I do not know if it
is justified. I should have thought, in all
probability, the sums mentioned by the
hon. Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh
(Mr. Coote) would have about met the
case that is to say, £600 in respect of the
Parliamentary Secretary to the Minister
of Finance, and £400 for the Assistant-
Parliamentary Secretary to the Minister
of Finance. All I have got to say is this.
I would be sorry to have the House
divided upon the matter, and give the
impression abroad that we were seriously
disagreed with respect to these matters,
but on the other hand I would not like the
community to have the idea that these
items were passed without consideration
and examination. That, to my mind,
would be a disaster. We have been sent
here not only to support the Government
in its fundamental policy, and in a way
which we are all obliged to do and are
prepared to do, but we are sent here also
to protect the taxpayer, who will feel very
much all the expenses that have arisen by
reason of this and similar proposals.
Mr. CRAWFORD: I will not trespass
on your time more than a minute or two.
I have listened with great admiration to
the remarkably frank words which have
fallen from the Prime Minister, to hold all
these offices open to the poor people of
Ulster. In view of what I know of the
state of the finance of the country, I enter
my strong protest against the salaries
proposed. I entirely agree with the hon.
Member for Tyrone and Fermanagh, and
I desire to enter my strong protest against
these salaries from first to last.
Question put, and agreed to.

ported to the House.
Resolution reported.

with the Committee in the said

The Irish Negotiations.
Motion for Adjournment.
Captain DIXON: I beg to
"That this House do now adjourn."
move
The PRIME MINISTER: I desire to
say that I think it only proper, after the
House and the country have been put in
full possession of the fresh proposals that
have reached us from His Majesty's
Government, that I should explain that I
think it inexpedient to-day to make any
statement whatever with regard to the
details of these proposals. First of all,
Irish Negotiations.
Irish Negotiations.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Prime Minister.]
there is ambiguity in the document.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) Secondly,
there is a considerable amount of further
information that we must be placed in
possession of before we can come to a right
conclusion. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) And, thirdly, I do not believe
that a settlement and peace will be
furthered in any way by rushing at it, or
by tumbling over one another at the.
moment. (Hon. Members. Hear, hear.)
A lasting peace requires sound foundations, and sound foundations can only be
laid by patience, by taking infinite pains
at the initial stages..
Therefore, I say until we have certain
matters further elucidated and further
information in our hands I think it would
be inadvisable for this House to begin
discussion. To-morrow we will have, I
hope, an opportunity under other conditions of placing our heads together in
free and frank consultation, and thereafter, if it is the desire of my colleagues,
I propose to cross over to London myself
in order on the spot to bring to a more
definite point some of those questions in
the document which seem to me to require
explanation. On my return it may be
necessary to have further consultations, or
it may be possible to report direct to this
House the result of my endeavours. But
I think we should hold our hand in the
meantime, and show that moderation
which has always characterised us when
any crisis has to be faced. Therefore
I leave that alone.
But one
matter in connection with
yesterday's proceedings I feel it quite impossible to allow to pass without making
strong protest. It may be without
parallel that the Prime Minister of one
of the Parliaments of the Empire should
have to criticise a speech of the highest
legal authority in England, the Lord
Chancellor. (Hon. Members. Hear,
hear.) But whatever the consequences
may be I really cannot allow it to go out
to the world that we did wrong, or anything, indeed, but right and proper, in
passing our Emergency Measure yesterday dealing with certain public bodies.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
I can
only hope that there has been some misrepresentation through ordinary errors in
reporting the speech;but taking to-day's
Press I quote the following sentence
which stands out by itself, and does not
require me to go over other ground dealing with other matters:
"We propose that a Boundary Commission," says the Lord Chancellor, in the
speech he delivered, "should examine into
the boundary lines with a view to rendering impossible such an unhappy incident
as that of a few days ago, in which the
popularly-elected bodies of one or two of
these districts were excluded from their
habitations by representatives of the
Northern Parliament on the ground that
they were not discharging their duties
properly."
First of all that short, and I hope,
effective measure was following precedents
created not only in this country but across
the Channel. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). In England if a defaulting
authority can not be brought to its senses
in any way, power is vested in the Crown
to send down a Commissioner to function
for that authority. In this country the
Imperial Government, of which the noble
Lord Chancellor is a Member, have put
into operation measures very much
similar to that we propose in the case of
County Tyrone. It looks, if the Lord
Chancellor is correctly reported, that we
excluded from their habitations certain
persons because we were vindictive towards them. We want to save them from
themselves (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear) and we want to save seventyfive per cent. of Loyalist ratepayers in
that county from destruction and ruin.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). If we had
held our hands and allowed the doors to
be shut and chaos to reign, the roads and
sanitation-
Mr. COOTE: And the hospitals and
asylums.
The PRIME MINISTER: And the
hospitals and asylums and everything that
goes to make up the life of the people-all
these would have gone to wreck and ruin,
and this, forsooth, is described by the
Lord Chancellor as "excluding them from
their habitations." I do not desire to
embarrass any man who is busily engaged
in attempting to bring peace to this
country, but it appears to me to be
an infringement of the rights and privileges of this House-(Hon. Members:
"Hear, hear!")-for the Lord Chancellor
or anyone else to make such a speech without apparently the full knowledge of the
facts before him; but to make a speech
at all was an incorrect attitude on his
part, especially at this time. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear!'') Now that only encourages the evil-doer. I hope and believe
that the warning that we gave to others
Irish Negotiations.
Adjournment.
7 DECEMBER 1921
who may be well-meaning in their attempts to bring about a satisfactory conclusion of these negotiations will make all
realise that they must not irritate the
Loyalists of Ulster. We have our own
difficulties. We have not by any means
come to a conclusion regarding the proposals put before us, and it seems to me
a very, very serious matter to throw out
the suggestion that the boundaries of
Ulster have to be altered because a County
Council, or indeed because any authority,
repudiated the Government under which
it was placed, not by any desire of our
own, but because of the charge that was
placed upon us by His Majesty the King
and the Parliament of Great Britain.
We were only doing our duty in what we
undertook yesterday, and I guarantee to
say that if we had not done our duty, but
copied the methods adopted by the British
Government through Dublin Castle, we
would have allowed things to slip quietly
into the same chaos and confusion that
have characterised the South. Therefore
it behoves us at the very foundation of our
new State to be firm and to be just, and
I guarantee to say in the County of Tyrone
we will be blessed by eighty per cent. of
the people for having stepped in and
rescued that county from going to destruction. (Hon. Members: "Hear, hear!"')
I merely enter this strong and earnest protest against interference with the governmental matters that have been handed
over to ourselves. If they did not think
we were fit to manage a recalcitrant county
council, then the Parliament of Great
Britain should never have placed us in the
position of attempting to rule the Six
Counties, but having done so, they must
allow us to manage these affairs in our own
way, and I guarantee to say that way will
be an effective way. We have done the
right thing, and if it had to be done again
in spite of that speech, I would do it
to-morrow. (Hon. Members: "Hear,
hear!'') I bring this out because it seems
to me it would be improper to pass it over.
The Lord Chancellor has been a life-long
political friend of mine, and I say it in
no vindictive manner, but in the interests
of the attempt to bring peace to this country and because under any circumstances
this House will repudiate any interference
by any man after the functions have been
transferred to us. (Hon. Members:
"Hear, hear!")
Question: "That this House do now
adjourn." Put, and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes after Three of the
Clock.

HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Thursday, 8th December, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.

Erysipelas ?

As regards the numbers shown under

INCREASE OF RENT (RESTRICTIONS)
ACT.
Mr. DONALD asked the Prime Minister
whether it is the intention of the Government to introduce an Amending Bill to
amend the Increase of Rent and Mortgage
Interest (Restrictions) Act, 1920, in view
of the reductions in wages and the great
unemployment existing.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Prime
Minister has asked me to reply to this
question. This matter is under the consideration of the Government, and it is
hoped that. a decision will be arrived at
before long. We have received representations both for and against any amendment of the Act. I think it right, therefore, to mention that the problem involved
is by no means a simple one, and in dealing
with the matter care will have to be taken
to avoid enacting legislation which may
increase the shortage of house accommodation by interfering unduly with private
enterprise and restricting opportunities for
employment.
Mr. DONALD: Arising out of that
answer, is my right hon. Friend aware
that I put this question on behalf of a
large number of poor people and ex-service
men who are receiving eviction notices for
non-payment of rent, and that is why I
want this House to alleviate that state of
affairs. I thank the right hon. Gentleman
for his answer.
Sir DAWSON BATES: As I have indicated, the matter is under the consideration of the Government at the present
time.
Mr. DONALD: Thank you.
BILL PRESENTED.
Salaries of Ministerial Offices.
Bill to
regulate the salaries to be paid to
Ministers of Northern Ireland, Parliamentary Secretaries, and Assistant Parliamentary Secretaries of the Government
of Northern Ireland, and the Attorney-
General for Northern Ireland.—[Mr.
Barbour, for the Prime Minister.]

8 DECEMBER 1921.

Second time to-morrow, and to be
