Orders of the Day.
SUPPLY.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That Mr. Speaker do now leave the
Chair."
THE PARLIAMENTARY AND
FINANCIAL SECRETARY TO THE
MINISTRY OF FINANCE (Mr.
Barbour): In moving that the House do
now go into Committee of Supply I think
I should like to make a reference to the
regret, which I am sure every Member of
this House feels, that my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, is not
with us to give us the benefit of his intimate knowledge of our accounts, and his
guidance in all the financial affairs over
which he has watched so very much to
our advantage ever since the Government
of Ireland Act was first under contemplation.
I am sure I am expressing the wish
of every one of us that he may soon be
restored to perfect health and strength,
and be able to resume his place at this
table. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
I do not think it necessary at the present
time to attempt to make any sort of complete Budget statement, as we are really
continuing under the old system of revenue and taxation that was imposed by
the old Imperial House, and we have only
therefore, to more particularly concern
ourselves with the four months of the
financial year that will elapse after the
transfer to us of the different services.
The House will understand that we, that
is the Treasury, have to come to the
House for every penny we spend, and it
is only by the approval of the House
that we are able to expend any of the
funds which we receive. In submitting
the different items of the Estimates
you I do not think it will be found
necessary to legislate as to any of them,
as
το
no new procedure is involved,
and, as I say, we are simply carrying
on the services on the same lines
as those on which they have been carried
on up till now.
I am sure that hon. Members on having
this rather formidable document put into
their hands, and learning possibly for the
first time of the very large sums that will
be passing through the Exchequer of
Northern Ireland, would like to have
some assurance beforehand as to where
the money, which we are now asking them
to vote for these different services, is to
come from. The Estimates have been
prepared, as I am sure hon. Members
will be ready to admit, under conditions
of very great pressure. It was only on
the 22nd November that the services of
the Ministry of Finance were really transferred to them, and so we have endeavoured
to press into this short period of eight,
nine, or ten days a work which at Westminster would occupy between three and four
months; but, as the supplies voted to us
at the last session of Parliament were only
on the original Estimates at a time when
the services had not been transferred, the
sums were not adequate to carry us on for
any length of time once the services began
to operate, and so it was necessary to come
to Parliament with the least possible
delay in order to get authority to deal
with finance. Some of the services are
transferred and some, of course, are reserved, but one thing we must always
bear in mind is that, whether the services
are transferred or reserved, every penny
of the expenditure comes out of the revenue
which is raised in Northern Ireland, and
the services--transferred or non-transferred-can in no way be set against the
transferred revenue or non-transferred
revenue. I will make that quite clear
a little later on when I give the figures
and details. Another very great difficulty with which we have been confronted
has been the delay on the part of the
Imperial Government in making up its
mind as to what course it should follow in
the case of Southern Ireland. You will
be familiar with Clause 72 of the Act,
which provided that, in the case of Southern Ireland refusing to start the operation
of this Act, the Imperial Government was
then to set up Crown Colony Government,
and appoint someone who would be able to
speak for Southern Ireland. The starting of our financial clause of the Act
hinged first of all on the appointment of
a Joint Exchequer Board, which consists
of two members appointed by the United
Kingdom Treasury, one by the Government of Northern Ireland, one by the
Government of Southern Ireland, and
one by His Majesty. The duty of this
Joint Exchequer Board is to properly
apportion between Northern Ireland and
Southern Ireland the revenue arising
from taxation in Ireland. Then there
was the appointment of the Civil Service
Committee. This consisted of one
nominee by the United Kingdom
Treasury, one by the Secretary of State,
one by the Government of Northern Ire-
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
land, one by the Government of Southern
Ireland, two by the existing Irish officers,
and the Lord Chief Justice of England
acts as Chairman of that Committee.
The duty of the Civil Service Committee
is to apportion between Northern Ireland
and Southern Ireland all those Civil
Service officers who have hitherto been
carrying on the duties which we have now
taken over, and, of course, which have
been discharged in Southern Ireland.
So you will see, on the one hand, that the
ascertainment of our correct share of the
revenue arising in Ireland could not be
determined until this Joint Exchequer
Board was set up, and, on the other
hand, we were equally unable to fix with
any degree of accuracy our liability in
respect of the salaries which would be
payable to those officers who would be
transferred to the service of Northern
Ireland.
I do not know yet what is the exact
position of Southern ireland in this matter, but it is gratifying to know that it
records a certain amount of progress, as
the Joint Exchequer Board has had one
meeting, at which, I am glad to say, satisfactory progress was made in that provisions were made for transferring to us
our share of the funds coming to
us arising out of taxation in Ireland. The
first meeting of the Civil Service Commission is taking place, I think, to-day, in
Dublin, where doubtless procedure will be
arranged, and progress after that should
be fairly rapid in getting the staff transferred to Northern Ireland. To arrive at
some statement of our income it is necessary to go back to the White Paper known
as the Further Memorandum of Financial
Provisions, or Command Paper No. 707.
which was issued in May, 1920, as upon
the Estimates for the year 1920-21, as set
forth in that White Paper the whole financial clauses of the 1920 Act depend. It
was an estimate then, and, in attempting
to judge the revenue and expenditure of
our Province, we have to depend on
estimates still. It is the most reliable
authority available yet, so in all figures I
give you, and in any comments I may
make, I hope you will always bear in mind.
I am speaking with reference to estimates
as then made, and I only deal with such
items of expenditure or revenue as were
contained or provided for in this White
Paper.
In that White Paper-I will speak in
round figures all the time for the convenience of Members-the revenue of the
Northern Government, consisting of the
various services which I will enumerate,
was estimated as follows:-
.--
Transferred services, which we now collect (they consist of Death Duties, Stamps,
and Entertainment Tax), £1,150,000, and
I may say in passing that this comparatively small sum is really the only revenue
which we ourselves are now collecting with
the exception of Land Purchase Annuities,
to which I will allude later on.
The great
bulk of the revenue of Northern Ireland
consists of reserved taxes, which, as you
are aware, are still collected by the
Imperial Government. These consist of
Customs, Excise on commodities, Income
Tax, Super Tax, Corporation Tax, and
Post Office revenue, and amounted in the
estimate to £14,000,000. Then at the
time the estimate was drawn up certain
legislation had been recently introduced,
the full effect of which was not at that
time apparent, and so that provided an
additional sum in a full year at that time
of £750,000 to meet these taxes as they
gradually came into force. Then the Land
Purchase Annuities, which were a free
gift from
from the Imperial Government,
amounting to £600,000, make up a total
estimated revenue on the basis named of
£16,500,000.
As against this revenue there is the cost
of transferred services of which we will
now gradually assume administration,
namely, Labour, Agriculture, Education,
Old Age Pensions, Health, &c., amounting to £3,800,000. Then the reserved
services, which continue to be administered
by the Imperial Parliament, but of course
remain as a charge against revenue raised
in Northern Ireland, and which consist of
the Supreme Court, Post Office, Revenue
Departments, that is to say, Customs, Excise, Inland Revenue, R.I.C., and Land
Purchase, amount to £2,150,000. Next
we come to our contribution to Imperial
Services, which was fixed at the figure of
£7,900,000. On this side again we have
to take in as a cross-entry, which hon.
Members will understand, figures which
are not yet fully developed, namely
£750,000, as this would come to be
expended under the different heads as soon
as the full working of the Act was reached.
That makes a total estimated expenditure
of £14,600,000. The revenue of which I
have given a total was £16,500,000, so
there remains a surplus of £1,900,000.
Then we might also take in as a surplus
during the development of these Acts to
which I have alluded, anything that we
might save up to £750,000. I am sure,
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
as a matter of interest, some hon. Members would like to know how it was that
this figure of surplus was arrived at, and
in order to do this you must remember
that the legislation dealing with the
Government of Ireland Act was before the
Imperial Parliament for a very considerable time, and so much so that during its
discussion several estimates were made as
to the probable revenue and expenditure
in Ireland. The first of these was made
during the year for the financial year
1919-20, and the way they approached
consideration of the matter at that time
was that they took the estimated expenditure on local Irish Services; that is to say,
the portion of the Imperial revenue which
was actually expended in Ireland, and as
you will all be aware there has been a great
deal of what I might call social or remedial
legislation passed in the last few years,
and there were at that time services, the
full effects of which had not yet been felt;
and so in these earlier estimates they provided £1,100,000 to meet these Acts as
they gradually came into operation. The
difference between these figures, that is the
cost of local services under this provision
for the further working of these different
Acts, and the estimated revenue was considered to represent the capability of the
Northern Government to contribute to
Imperial expenditure. In other words,
they took the total revenue that was raised
in Ireland and they deducted from that
amount all money that was actually spent
in Ireland.
I
development that it wishes to on assuming
the administration of this Province.
must just say in passing you will understand, as your interest is fixed on that, in
all these figures I am giving the figures as
they apply to Ulster and not as they apply
to Ireland. This £600,000 applies to
Northern Ireland. That was the first proposal from the financial clauses in Ireland.
But then time went by, and of course during the war taxation was increased very
much, and in the further estimates they
still took as the contribution towards
Imperial Services the sum which arose on
the 1919-20 Estimates, namely the balance
which remained after taking the cost of
local services which at that time was
£7,927,000. During later estimates made
for 1920-21, owing to increasing taxation
the revenue derived from the Province was
naturally larger, but in balancing their
account the British Government decided
only to ask for the same contribution
towards the Imperial Services as it had
appeared from the previous estimate that
Ireland was able to pay. The amount by
which the revenue exceeded expenditure
taken in the earlier estimate was
£1,300,000. So as they did not ask us
for an increased contribution towards
Imperial Services it meant that there was
this surplus of £1,300,000 which had become a free surplus to this Parliament.
This added to the £600,000 on Land Purchase Annuities represented a surplus of
£1,900,000 which it was intended to give
us to bring this Parliament into operation.
was clearly in the minds of the
British Parliament at the time of the
passing of this Act. It was not chance.
It did not slip through. They took particular care to draw our attention to this
surplus which was being given to us, and,
although this paragraph was read by my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Finance,
when we met in September in introducing
the original estimates, I would like to read
it again, as I think it is very important
you should bear this figure clearly in mind
as showing the intention of the Imperial
Parliament.
This
They then made this further
provision for these services which would be
administered in Ireland and the Imperial
Government proposes to take all of the
balance. Their first idea was that they
would hand over to Ireland a revenue and
expenditure that would exactly balance,
the Imperial Government receiving the
same contribution as heretofore. But I
suppose they were sufficiently aware of the
Irish character at that time, that they
knew no Irishman would ever be satisfied
to leave a bargain under those terms, but
that they realised there was a craving in
Ireland for luck penny, and that giving
back something out of the purchase price
would have to be taken into consideration.
I do not suppose they doubted the security
or the ability to collect the money, but in
any case the suggestion arose, and was
adopted, that they should make a present
to Ireland of the Land Purchase annuities,
and that amounted to £600.000. That
will be free income that will be available for this Parliament to make any new
Sir L. Worthington Evans, who had
charge of the Bill, used these words:
The Government recognise that it is most
desirable that the Irish Governments should
not be hampered, but should be allowed a
substantial margin with which to meet any
additional expense which may be incurred by
the establishment of the new Parliaments, or
may be called for by various services which
have hitherto not been developed perhaps in
accordance with the requirements of the Irish
people.
EE
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
Then he goes on to say that the Imperial
contribution has been fixed in order to
leave this margin. Then he says:
Nor is this all. As the Prime Minister
announced earlier in the year, the Government have decided to make a free gift of the
existing land annuities, amounting at present
to £3,350,000, which will be available as
additional revenue for Ireland. The surplus
and the land purchase annuities together
amount to £7,500,000. There is included in
the expenditure a margin for future services,
which have not yet been set up, and will not
mature fully for some years to come of
£1,750,000.
Then he goes on to say :--
This gives
Northern Ireland a total
surplus of £2,250,000. I do not think, therefore, that it can be said that an Imperial contribution has been fixed too high upon the information at present available. On the contrary it has been fixed at an amount which
leaves for expenditure in Ireland a sum very
much in excess of the highest rate of expenditure that there has ever been in Ireland in
times past.
That was the intention of the Imperial
Government. It was also the expectation
of those of our representatives at Westminster, and it was in the minds of such
meetings as we held over here to consider
the Government of Ireland Act proposals
from time to time, that we should start
off with this surplus available for the
development of any further Services that
this Parliament might consider necessary.
And so I feel that we have a very strong
case indeed to go to the Imperial Parliament now, and ask them to carry out their
intention and our expectation, and see
that we secure this surplus which was held
forth to us, and was, I am sure, a very
material factor in reconciling us to undertake the serious responsibility of working
this Act. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
Now we come to the year 1921-1922.
This is the nearest estimate that we can
get as to how our accounts will work out
for the present financial year. Here
again it is a matter of estimate. Hon.
Members will understand that the revenue does not flow into the Exchequer
in an even stream all through the year.
There has never been in the past much
reason to figure out and demonstrate the
fluctuation in the rate of flow either of expenditure or revenue during any part of
the year, and so in attempting to arrive at
conclusions of this kind we are bound to
take the whole year's operations into
account. Consequently these Estimates
are furnished for the present financial
year to the 31st March next, and I would
remind hon. Members once again that
they only take into computation such
items as were either included or provided
for in the Estimates that were originally
made.
The yield of Transferred Revenue is
estimated at £1,150,000, which coincides
with the amount taken in the White
Paper.
The Reserved Revenue—that is,
Customs, Excise on Commodities, Incometax, &c.—all show decreases, the estimated
yield being £13,050,000 against the White
Paper Estimate of £14,000,000. The
estimated yield of new taxes for this year
is £200,000; the figure provided in the
White Paper was £750,000. Then as to
Land Purchase Annuities, the figure is
£600,000, the same as in the White Paper.
This brings out on the Revenue side a
total of £15,000,000 as against £16,500,000
estimated in the White Paper, or a decrease on the Revenue side of £1,500,000.
On the expenditure side, on the same
basis as the White Paper figures, we have
an estimated expenditure on Transferred
Services, such as Education, Health,
Labour, &c. of £4,400,000, against the
White Paper figure of £3,800,000; that
is to say, an increase of £600,000. On
Reserved Services-that is, Post Office,
Revenue Departments, &c.-the estimated
expenditure is £2,600,000 against the
White Paper figure of £2,150,000, being
again an increase of £450,000. The Imperial contribution, of course, remains
the same, £7,900,000. We get a total
expenditure of £14,900,000, against the
original estimate of £13,850,000, or increased expenditure to the extent of
£1,050,000. Adding these together we
get a total difference in net surplus of
£2,550,000.
Now how are we going to meet this is
very naturally the question that will arise.
in the mind of everybody. Our financial
basis, so far as we can make an estimate
at the present time, will turn out to be
£2,550,000 worse than was expected under
the calculations made in the White Paper
to which I have referred. This means
that we have to set against that the
original surplus, which was estimated at
£1,900,000, and then we have such sums
as we may be able to save out of that
£750,000 which was provided for future
contingencies, or assuming that we can
save the whole of it, a total of £2,650,000
against the estimated shortage of
£2,550,000. This would leave us with a
surplus on the whole year of just
£100,000, or taking it for one-third of the
year, the four months still to come of the
present financial year, it would mean if
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
our estimate works out correctly, we would
come out with a surplus of £35,000.
I have been very careful to warn you
that I have contrasted revenue with expenditure on the basis only of including
such items or services as were included or
provided for in the White Paper Estimates, and, of course, you are only too
well aware of the very great change that
has taken place comparatively recently in
the cost of public administration. More
particularly I allude to the cost of providing the Special Constables. This is
an entirely new service, and, of course,
was not thought of at all at the time the
Estimates were framed. You have
already been told by my right hon.
Friend, the Prime Minister, and it was an
expression of opinion with which you
were all in most hearty concurrence, that
the prime essential in any civilised community is the maintenance of law and
order, and that cost what it may that is
a service which has to be provided. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). The contention
that I would like to put forward, and the
contention that has all along been held
by the Cabinet of Northern Ireland, is
that this is a charge which should not be
laid against the Northern Parliament.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). To what
have all these disturbances been due ?
Can we really say that they have been
caused by our own citizens who have been
regularly living in our midst? Have they
been desirous of promoting any political
aims or of achieving ends, commercial,
economical, or anything like that? It is
perfectly obvious to everybody that the
outrages which have taken place in our
city, to guard against which has involved
our province in such enormous expenditure have really been in the form of hostile
operations carried on by a part of our
country, which until even a few days ago
had as its ultimate aim the establishment
of an absolutely independent Republic in
that part of Ireland. Those outrages and
the cost of keeping them in check have
been entirely due to the political aims of
that part of Ireland. We on our part are
entitled to military and naval protection,
and the fact that we have come forward
and enlisted from our own people here
Constabulary, in order to lighten the
burden which otherwise would have fallen
on the military, I think, instead of constituting that a charge to which we are
liable, should entitle us to receive favourable consideration from His Majesty's
Government. In fact I consider that the
nature of the services which has involved
us in this expenditure decides the issue so
absolutely clearly that this is a charge.
which must be borne or provided for in
some way. There is no provision under
the Act, unfortunately, by which it can
be provided. But still it is morally a
charge to be borne by the Imperial
Government, and we have in the meantime excluded it from our Estimates. But
I do not want to disguise the fact at all;
I want to bring it clearly before the House.
There is a point in the financial clauses
of our Bill to which I desire to call
attention, and that is that the Imperial
contribution of £7,900,000 should come
up for revision, a revision, I think, which
can only take place in a manner favourable
to ourselves, at the end of two years.
Any effect of such revision will, of course,
be made retrospective, but if it should
come that money should be refunded to
us, if the contribution is larger than
should be found ultimately to be equitable, it would mean that we would not
get any refund of that sum for the period
of two years. Then, again, I explained
to you a little while ago the manner in
which our estimated surplus was determined. You will see that it was on no
scientific basis. It was simply a
fortuitous increase in the matter of
revenue over the revenue of the
standard year.
The basis upon
which our Imperial contribution will
come to be revised is a a rather vague
term, "on the basis of taxable capacity,'
and so I think, not understanding the
phrase, it will be absolutely impossible for
us to say what the effect of that revision
might be. But these are matters which
I hope will be brought before the
Imperial Parliament at the earliest
possible date, so that we may get a settlement of them and really know where we
are. The estimated cost of the Special
Constabulary, which will play such an
important part in the balancing of our
accounts, for the full year is £900,000,
and for the broken part of the year for
which we become responsible, now that
we are recruiting the Force to its full
authorised strength, £490,000.
""
Then another very serious and disturbing factor in our finances is that which
will be dealt with, I am sure, to a greater
extent by my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Labour, and that is the effect
on this community of the very great
paralysis in business which has taken
place during the last eighteen months, in-
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
an
volving unemployment on such
enormous scale, and particularly affecting
the Belfast area of our province.
The working of this unemployment has
really in the past been carried on by the
institution of a Fund to which contrbutions were made by the workpeople, the
employers, and by the State, and the
rate of contribution by each of the three
contributors was so calculated that the
Fund should have been self-supporting.
In fact, during the period of good employment, while the scheme was
in
operation, a surplus had been accumulated of £22,000,000. But then unemployment came, on such an unprecedented
scale that the Government had to draw
on this amount, and to make advances
to the Fund in order to enable relief to
be granted to the unemployed. To that
Fund already about £6,000,000 has been
advanced. The scale of contribution has
now been recalculated, and it is hoped.
that the Fund will again become solvent,
and accumulate a balance. But, in the
meantime, finance will have to be met by
this Fund in Northern Ireland to the
extent of nearly £100,000 a month.
Then there is one other service we have
got to provide for, and it is a matter
which, I hope, hon. Members will bear
in mind, that is local loans. We are
cut off from the service of the Local
Government Board. In the past the
Imperial Government enabled local
authorities to finance schemes of development. We have not had occasion really
or the time to consider how to make such
advances in the future, but in whatever
criticism may be directed against the
Estimate, or, indeed, against any item of
expenditure upon which the Northern
Parliament may embark, I hope hon.
Members will always consider the effect
of any inconsiderate statement upon the
credit of our Northern Treasury.
Therefore, criticism, or anything which
will tend to diminish the burden of our
administration, I can assure hon. Members, will only be too gladly welcomed,
and will be acted on wherever it is possible. Let us be guarded in our statements. We must remember, when
possibly we are feeling the pressure of
the slackness in trade, that is not the
time to go about in a depressed condition.
Mr. DONALD: Or for paying exorbitant salaries.
Mr. MILNE BARBOUR: If you want
to go for an overdraft to the bank that is
not the time to put on your worst clothes.
Mr. DONALD: What about the workpeople?
Mr. MILNE BARBOUR: We want
the credit of the province to stand high,
so that if we have to raise money we shall
raise it to the very best possible advantage. In the meantime I am glad to
say that we have made arrangements to
receive payments on account from time
to time from the Imperial Treasury, and
we do not anticipate our expenditure will
exceed the payments received in that
way. I hope there will be no necessity
to finance in the year in order to carry
us over the period for which the accounts
are now presented. We have been
carrying on all the services, as nearly as
possible, on the same lines as hitherto.
There are really very few new items
of expense introduced into the Estimates.
Of course, the Department of Commerce
is a new one altogether. It is a service
which had no counterpart in the Government of Ireland as hitherto carried on.
Then there is one modest difference which
has been made, it is the sum for bonuses
for Intermediate Teachers. That is a
matter which was brought prominently
before the electors in our recent campaign, and in my connection with the
Ministry of Finance I think hon. Members will pardon me if I did allow the
modest sum of £2,000 to cover bonuses
during the financial year. I beg to
move.
Dr. MORRISON: I would like, in the
first instance, to associate myself with
what the Deputy Finance Minister has
said about our Minister for Finance.
We all regret his continued absence from
this House through illness, but if I may
say so, I think the House has not suffered
by reason of his absence, owing to the
splendid speech we have had from his
deputy. I rise so early in the Debate
in order to speak generally on the
question of finance. I confess frequently
I am unable to follow all the multifarious
details the Parliamentary and Financial
Secretary to the Minister of Finance has
given us, but I cannot get over the fact
that £2,113,000, are being budgeted for.
That looks, in the eyes of the public, a
very large sum, but the explanation he
has given as to this very large expenditure should satisfy most people. He has
made it quite clear that these financial
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
conditions imposed upon us by the Imperial Parliament are open to revision,
and that the inadequacy of the amount
can be so well regulated, possibly at an
early date. I speak here to-day as an
apostle of economy, and I would imagine
that the statement we have heard justifies us in approaching all these questions
with the object of procuring as much
economy as possible. I am, however,
rather inclined to think that I, and
possibly some other Members, have made
too big a voice about economy-not so
much a voice about economy as a voice
about extravagance. From the knowledge
I have since acquired in connection with
the administration of the affairs of this
House I am inclined to modify the views
I expressed on a previous occasion. We
raised the question of the wisdom of
giving very big salaries to Parliamentary
Secretaries, and men who occupy the
positions of Whip. There is room for a
difference of opinion with regard to that.
After all, we should take a wider view,
and the wider view with which I have
approached it justifies me in taking an
optimistic opinion of the finances of this
House, and the way in which public
affairs will be administered. Roughly our
revenue is £14,00000D. There is an
Imperial charge of £8,000,000, leaving
us for administrative purposes something
like £6,000,000.
There is further, as was clearly put
before us by the Prime Minister, a compression of 25 Departments into five
Departments. That, I suomit, is bound
to produce a huge economy. In addition to that I have been behind the
scenes a little. We see in the Estimates
big salaries for men with whom we had
not been brought in contact, and about
whose work we knew very little. And I
found behind the scenes, in the Departments, men who are dealing with a sum
of probably three millions, and if I may
say so, they are all in my opinion Al men.
If you take the old wasteful, extravagant
administration which, through my lifetime and up to the present, has
characterised public administration in
this country, I am hopeful and am
practically certain that a big saving will
be made by the administrative capacites
of these men of whom we knew nothing.
Take the matter of education. We
had a responsible body in Dublin, but
here we have a Minister of Education
who is approachable, and a Deputy
Minister who is even more approachable,
and if you care to go behind and into the
Education Office you find a most capable
man there who welcomes you, if you care
to climb the almost inaccessible stairs
leading to the office. I went to him the
other day. I think I know as much
about education and its administration
under Dublin Castle as anyone, up to the
advent of this Parliament. You had a
Compulsory Attendance Committee in
connection with every County Council.
You had a Board of Works for keeping
school-houses in repair, and to look after
the arrangements for sanitation. Then
you had the unnecessary school carts conveying the children from one school to another an expensive system. But I went
into the Education Office, and I said
are these things going to go on,' and
I was amazed to find that the Education
Secretary knew all about these things
better than I did, and more, and I found
also that he is devising plans by which
these things are going to be checked. I
think that is going on in all our Departments, and by means of that very much
saving will be effected.

""
With regard to the question of Secondary Teachers, although I am an apostle
of economy, I am one of those who have
been urging upon the Education Minister
the necessity for increasing their pay.
He has consented to it, and in the present
Estimate a sum of £2,000 is set down
as extra payment. I submit, however,
that to give a large body of men like
these the sum of £2,000 to tide over some
emergency is wholly inadequate, and I
suggest that the sum he intends to give
will go in over the two months for the
next year.
I hope he will give them
some substantial relief, and I would say
in the allocation of this Fund, and in
dealing with the teachers, and, perhaps,
with other professional men, it is not
enough for them to draw up admirable
schemes of administration, but it will be
wise to consider the teachers, because any
arrangement that is made should be made
after consultation with both parties. Looking at all these things I cannot say that
we were unnaturally criticising the big
salaries. I myself never had more than
£300 or £400 of a salary in my life.
The misfortune is that I have always
found that I never could get the public to
estimate me at my own figure. It is a
difficulty from which most men suffer.
However, I would say that, having this
further knowledge, and knowing what is
going on, and knowing the comparison and
wise schemes that have been devised for
amalgamating big departments, and that
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Dr. Morrison.]
we have men behind the scenes dealing
with such huge sums as three million
pounds, I think it would be an unwise
policy on our part to cavil at a salary of a
thousand or even twelve hundred pounds
to them. I am not frightened at the
administration of this House. I think we
will always, and I claim to belong to the
economical section, we can always criticise,
and criticise severely unnecessary
expenditure, but I hope that as we get
experience we will not lose sight of proportion. While the country is passing
through a very serious financial crisis at
the present time, depressing both to
business and agricultural interests,
some Members are inclined to be pessimistic and to be alarmed by these large
salaries. In my opinion they need have
no fear. These times will pass away, and
by and by, and I think very soon, the sun
will be shining on a new and a model
Ulster. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
Mr. DONALD: I will not detain the
House more than a few moments. I just
want to make one or two observations in
reply, in a general way to my hon.
Friend, the Parliamentary and Financial
Secretary to the Ministry of Finance. The
more one goes into these financial matters
the more, to my mind, it becomes difficult
to understand how one can arrive at a
proper basis or estimate. The situation
as outlined by my hon. Friend to-day is
a very hopeful one. He referred to the
financial aspects of the question when the
Home Rule Bill was before the House of
Commons. May I just give this House
some idea of the arguments put forward
there with regard to finance in this
country? That was the argument I was
about to put yesterday, but it was not in
order. In 1910 and 1911 we were on the
wrong side of the balance sheet by one
million pounds in Ireland. In 1911 and
1913 we were £845,000 on the wrong side;
in 1914 £1,222,000 on the wrong side; in
1915 there was £266,000 of a deficit.
Then, of course, the war came, and the
whole tendency of finance rose, but that
was an artificial rise, and never up till

has the revenue risen above
£15,113,000; but my hon. Friend is hopeful now, according to his own statement,
that we are going to get £16,000,000.
That is where one feels a little bit puzzled
in view of the state of the finances of the
country. Then, of course, he goes on to
say that our expenditure will be something like fourteen millions, having a little
in reserve.
I rise to offer, if possible, so
far as my judgment goes, constructive
criticism, because I am most anxious, no
Member in this House is more anxious,
that we in this country should carry out
everything for the best interests of our
people. I will not object to a solitary
farthing provided it is expended in the
right way. But we are very much in
arrears of legislation for many years in
this country, and I am afraid that we will
not have sufficient surplus to develop such
questions as education. It has been
stated in the other House time and again
that we will require a million and a half
to put our education system right in this
country. Then there is another very
great question that has not been tackled,
nor yet has provision been made for it in
the establishment of the Ministries in
Ulster. It is the question of a Ministry
of Health. I think the Government have
overlooked that subject. Perhaps I am
not in order in referring to it.
Mr. SPEAKER: Yes, the hon. Member is out of order. It is not permissible
to refer to any matter which would require legislation in this debate. The hon.
Member may only discuss in general the
financial estimates which are put forward,
or, of course, he can make a general review of the financial situation such as
the Parliamentary and Financial Secretary to the Ministry of Finance gave, but
no matter involving legislation can be discussed.
Mr. DONALD: I thought perhaps my
argument was relevant to the question in
view of the statements made by my hon.
Friend who touched upon all the questions
upon which I have touched. I just want
to deal very briefly with one more matter.
I hope to say a few words when we come
to discuss Class 2, 1-8. I was very much
surprised to hear the statement of my hon
Friend with regard to the Special Constabulary. This country will not have to
pay a farthing or else we are very big fools.
Very big fools we are if we pay a solitary
farthing for keeping law and order when
the Imperial Government failed to do it
in this country. What do we find to-day?
Millions of money have been thrown out
to the South and West of Ireland, and
they were not even then able to keep law
and order, and the Government of that
part of the country will not be taxed one
farthing. With these observations I will
not detain the House any longer now. I
hope my hon. Friend will give us a
clear outline of the various items, from 1
Supply .
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
to 8, so that the House may get some
grasp of the situation.
Mr. COOTE: I wish in the first place
to compliment the Parliamentary and
Financial Secretary to the Ministry of
Finance on the able manner in which he
has presented his case. It is just what
we would expect from our hon. Friend in
ordinary circumstances, but in the
peculiar situation in which he finds himself, we are very grateful to him for the
lucid manner in which he has done his
work. We congratulate
him most
heartily. I do not wish to make many
strictures upon the Estimates as presented.
I am not capable of doing so on the short
notice we have had. We only had this
Paper yesterday, and everything is in a
hurry. We all know the hurry the
Government is in to get the money.
We
quite understand the situation, and I submit we cannot go very closely into
strictures on finance on this occasion,
but if we are spared for another and
year,
we see these offices going on, and whether
they are being manned properly, or overmanned, then it will be for us to make
very searching and minute inquiry in
every ramification and come here in the
most relentless fashion to look into the
finances of the House. In the meantime
we can quite see that the Government has
no option but to model their services on
the lines on which they were transferred
to them. Of course they must take these
men and women at the figures at which
they were employed under the Imperial
Government, and they must take them,
I expect, with all contingent liabilities.
That is only a matter of common honesty.
But I throw out the hint to them that it
will be for them to show, with their
Northern practical brain, some constructive manipulation of these offices so
that much of the waste and expense, which
have been going on, and which we all deplore, in His Majesty's Government, may
be eliminated from the offices in this
House. I congratulate the Government
on the leading officials they have got up to
the present. We have an excellent staff
of men as far as I can see, and I think it
would be well for us to get in touch more
with these permanent officials. It would
make some of us very small if we did, and
we would find that they are very capable
men.
But I submit, if you take the
Ministry of Labour and come to the Exchanges, that it is an intolerable circumstance that there are £25,500 spent on
staff.
the temporary clerical and messenger
The temporary staff and messengers will
cost this Northern area twenty-five
thousand pounds, and I should like to see
where it does half that amount of good to
the whole North of Ireland. It is a
huge fraud. It is there simply to give
jobs to certain people who come along. I
simply rise now on the general question
to suggest to the Ministries concerned that
they do put their practical brain into
their work and try and lop off the
excrescences that are unnecessary and
make it a decent, efficient service in connection with this House. Although these
items will be passed-and I quite expect
they will-I do not see how we can avoid
it, though they may be in more modified
form-I hope the Ministry will not take
it for granted that the House in passing
these estimates is agreeing with every
item in them. Some of these offices are
not yet filled, some of the money is only
provisional. I do appeal to the Ministries
to see that they economise if they can-I
sure they can-on many of the
services, and show to all concerned that
we can improve on the services already
given to us, and with greater economy and
efficiency we can have services in
Northern area that will be a credit to us.
am
this
SIR CRAWFORD M'CULLAGH: I
just want to make a few remarks. Let
me say that I join with my hon. Friend
in congratulating the Parliamentary
Secretary to the Ministry of Finance on
the very lucid manner in which he presented these estimates. In my mind, if
there was any disappointment at all, it
was caused by the rather small surplus
he had to show on his balance sheet. In
listening to him I could not but think
that the income was, perhaps rather overestimated, while the expenditure might,
perhaps, be under-estimated. I do hope
that what he said will be correct, and that
at the end of the financial year he will
have a surplus, although small, something
like £35,000. The point I wanted to
draw attention to is that in dealing with
the local funds my hon. Friend pointed
out that he had provided a sum of
£50,000. I wish to point out that
that sum is entirely inadequate
to meet the demands. Where he is to
get the money, or where it is to come
from, I am not in a position to say, but I
will give him one item alone that of
housing-on which the commitments of
the Government are far and away ahead
of £50,000. If this money is not forth-
Supply.

Supply .
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Sir Crawford McCullagh.]
coming, it will cause a great deal of hardship and unemployment. Take the Corporation of Belfast alone; they cannot
proceed with their work unless the
Imperial Government through this Parliament keep to their commitments as far as
housing is concerned. I see again there is
a provision made of £15,000 for grants to
local authorities under the Housing Act
of 1919. I am not in a position to say
what the commitments will be outside the
city of Belfast, but certainly they are
maturing every day in Belfast. Increasing
numbers of houses are being completed
every week, and a very substantial move
will have to be made in that direction.
There is another matter, which, perhaps,
is rather small, but is still important. I
do not see any reference whatever to the
collection of motor taxes. The motor tax
is rather a large amount.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member, perhaps, is under a misapprehension as to what he is allowed to
discuss in this debate. He can deal with
all these detailed questions when we come
to the different classes in the Estimates,
but this particular debate is always understood to be only an opportunity for the
House of Commons to air its general
grievances before it grants any supply to
the Government.
Sir CRAWFORD M'CULLAGH: I
bow to your ruling, and I will direct
attention to these matters at the proper
time. I think these estimates are
certainly put forward in a very good form,
and I am perfectly certain the Minister
of Finance could not have done anything
else. They seem to me to be moderate
and well thought out, and I only hope his
hope will be realised, and that we will
have a surplus.
Question: "That Mr. Speaker do now
leave the Chair," put, and agreed to.
In Committee.
MR. MOLES in the Chair.
The CHAIRMAN: It may be convenient to new Members in particular if
at this stage I indicate what is the procedure in the discussion of Estimates. If
they will turn to the Estimates on page
21 they will find there some items set out
under the heading "Class 2, Ministry of
Home Affairs," and a specific sum
indicated in respect of that. A motion
will be proposed that such sum be granted.
Looking further down the page you will
find heads "A", "B", "C", "D", down
to "H", inclusive. At subsequent stages
a distinct motion in respect of each sum
under each head will be submitted to the
Committee, and if hon. Members desire to
criticise any of the details set out in the
subsequent pages, the proper way to do
that is to interrogate the Minister if they
desire simply to put a question, but if they
desire to embark on a more general discussion, the procedure is to move that
Sub-head A, or any other Sub-head, be
reduced by £100 or any other sum. That
is the formal mode of procedure.
CLASS II. NO. 1.
MINISTRY OF HOME AFFAIRS.
Motion made and Question proposed:
"That a sum not exceeding Twenty-three
thousand, two hundred and ninety pounds,
including a Supplementary sum of Twentythree thousand and eighty pounds, be
granted to His Majesty for the Service of
the period ending 31st March, 1922, to
defray the Salaries and Expenses of the
Ministry of Home Affairs." (Voted on
Account, £2,500.)
Mr. COOTE: On a point of order, if we
permit this vote to pass unchallenged can
we then under sub-divisions A and B, and
later on, move amendments to the particular items?
The CHAIRMAN: Yes. Each distinctive vote permits of a particular field of
finance being discussed detail by detail if it
be so desired.
Sir DAWSON BATES: At this stage it
might be convenient to the Committee if
I were to state briefly some of the duties
which devolve upon the Ministry of Home
Affairs. I think it might clear up one or
two matters and so enable hon. Members
to concentrate on other items which they
desire to criticise. May I point out that
in this Ministry there are two main
branches, one being law and order, what
is known as home affairs, and the other
Local Government administration. The
duty of maintaining law and order
which has now been transferred to
this Ministry is, in my opinion,
and I am sure it is the opinion of Members
of this Committee, the most important that
has been transferred to the Northern Government. All the services really must become subordinate to the maintenance of
law and order, because unless you protect
the lives and property of the citizens every-
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
thing else falls to the ground. Unfortunately I find myself in the position not only
of having to maintain law and order, but
of having to restore law and order. The
laws which have been passed by the Imperial Government have been practically
abrogated within the last five or six years.
Crime has been made easy to those who
wish to use it as a political weapon. It
will take time to restore law and order,
and I hope, and I am sure, I can rely on
the sympathy and help of Members of this
Committee in the task that lies ahead of
my Department.
Under this particular branch, that is the
Home Office side, there will be found a
series of offices formerly administered
by the Chief Secretary's Office in
Dublin, and by the Home Office in
London, so far as it concerned itself with
Irish affairs. The Royal Irish Constabulary, the Special Constabulary, the General
Prisons Board, of which we have heard a
good deal lately in connection with the
Derry incident, the Inspectors of Lunatic
Asylums, Reformatories and Industrial
Schools, the Registrars of Petty Sessions,
the Chief Crown Solicitor and the
Crown Solicitors for the various counties,
and the County Court Judges and the officials under them-these are all grouped
together in one branch, which I may refer
to as the Home Office Branch. In the
other branch, to which we might refer
as the Local Government side, will be
found a various number of what we might
call sub-departments. First of all, we
come to the administration of Poor Law
Relief Acts. When I say there are
upwards of a hundred Acts of Parliament dealing with this particular matter,
and that they date back to the eighteenth
century, I am sure hon. Members will appreciate the necessity for reform in this
particular line. Poor Law administration
has been the subject matter of various
Commissions, but I think most of the reports of those Commissions shared the fate
of those of other Commissions, and little
or nothing was done. I hope when this
Ministry gets its feet under it we will be
able to see to this very necessary reform
of the Poor Law system. Then we come
to the administration of the various Acts
dealing with the prevention of disease.
Under that heading you will find such
acts as the Tuberculosis Acts, which
deal with the awful scourge of
consumption, Maternity and Child Welfare
Acts, and the Act of 1919 which enables
local authorities to provide for the medical
inspection and treatment of children
attending schools.
Then we come to the curative side of
the health administration. I think it is
about the most complicated of all.
Dealing with the question of public hospitals we have the county infirmaries, which
are controlled by joint committees
founded under Acts which go back to
1765. In the same county you will find
numerous union infirmaries and fever hospitals. They are all controlled by another organisation, namely the Board of
Guardians. Then again, in the same
county, you will find district hospitals
controlled by rural district councils, another organisation. In addition there is
the lunatic asylum controlled by still another committee, and then you come to the
tuberculosis sanatorium controlled by
yet another. There is no connecting link
between any one of these bodies, with the
result that there is waste in every direction and inefficiency. I propose, as I said
before, in regard to Poor Law Relief, that
this matter should also receive the earliest
consideration of the Department with a
view to seeing how best to consolidate these
institutions. I also hope to establish a Public Health Council, on which I will have
the benefit of the advice of members of the
Then we have the
medical profession.
various Housing Acts, which have been referred to by the hon. Member for South
Belfast (Sir Crawford M'Cullagh), both in
the rural and urban areas. There are
something like forty of those Acts, and no
one seems to have thought of consolidating
them and giving them some practical
form. Leaving what we might call these
specialised services, we come down to the
minor administration in regard to Local
Government. We have to look after appeals under the Old Age Pensions Acts.
Mr. DONALD: On a point of order, I
do not wish to interrupt my right hon.
Friend. I simply want to ask a question.
We have had a general statement. I take
it that your ruling was that we took up
Vote A.
The CHAIRMAN: The general discussion that has taken place was on the
motion: "That Mr. Speaker do now leave
the Chair." The discussion which now
takes place is upon the Minister introducing Estimates for his Department. It is
the rule and the usage that upon such occasions the Minister, in so doing, is permitted to travel somewhat far afield in
FF
Supply.

Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[The Chairman.]
order to obviate the necessity for meticulous
and detailed discussion by and by.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think hon.
Members wish to hear something of the
department so that it might shorten the
questions put to me afterwards. I do not
say, of course, I do not welcome the questions they may put to me. I was referring
to the question of road administration that
has been transferred to this department. 1
hope to set up at an early date a Road Advisory Committee, so that every county
will see that the money is well and properly spent. I found in taking over the
various departments from Dublin that up
to the present time they were all controlled
by a series of boards, and these boards
actually acted independently of each other.
which I think very largely accounts for
the fact that when legislation was introduced the officials in connection with these
boards were more anxious to see that their
vested interests were secure rather than
that the legislation that was being enacted
was suitable for the needs of the community. Therefore, in selecting the heads
of my department I set to work to try
and get men not wedded to the old
system, but men with elastic minds,
who could look ahead and at the same time
understand the needs of Ulster. I have
been very fortunate so far as the head of my
own department goes. In that case I found
a great deal of difficulty in getting a man
who not alone understood Local Government administration but who also understood the Home Office side in connection
with criminal law administration. I was
fortunate in getting a gentleman who spent
a very large part of his service in the
Local Government Board in Dublin, and
subsequently served under two Chief Secretaries in Dublin, and who thus learned
everything he could have learned and a
great deal more regarding maladministration. When I say he is an Ulsterman I am
sure you will not undervalue his merits.
As head of the Home Office side I have
got Mr. Magill, who has been under four
or five Chief Secretaries, and I think he
even knows more of the maladministration
of the affairs of Ireland. As head of the
Local Government side I have a gentleman
who had a most distinguished career in
the Local Government Board.
In regard to the other departments I
would like to refer to the medical side. I
decided there should be one Chief Medical
Officer-one who would control not alone
the public health side, but the prisons,
lunatic asylums, reformatories, and industrial schools. I may tell this House
that in Dublin these departments were
managed by four chief medical officers.
Under this department all these matters
will be controlled by one Chief Medical
Officer With regard to the total staff, it
numbers 91. I find that in the whole
of Ireland for those services which
have taken over the total number of
officials was something round about 460.
we
I would like to say one word about the
police. It is absolutely essential at the
present crisis through which we are passing
that the police force should be kept up to
its full strength. I hope conditions will
alter, but until these conditions alter I
cannot take the responsibility of reducing
the police force. I would like to take this
opportunity of thanking the General
Officer commanding the troops, the regular police, the specials, and more especially the B men, for the great help they
have rendered my department since we
took over control. We took over the
restoration and maintenance of law and
order on the 22nd November, and other
services on the 1st December, and, of
course, we have not during that short
period been able to do all that we should
like to do.
Mr. COOTE: I wish to congratulate the
Home Secretary on his presentation of his
case, and to say I hope that his part may
be more felicitous than probably he expects himself, and that when we meet
again for these Estimates in another year,
all this trouble will have blown over, and
that he will be able to present his case for
a great reduction in the cost of the services. We know, as I have pointed out
before, he has just come into office, and he
cannot effect any great reorganisation or
economy. When he comes to the question
of Local Government, I submit very
much can be done to help the
country. The administration of the Poor
Law needs to be put on a better basis. Old
Age Pensions administration to my mind
needs to be readjusted entirely. There is
a great deal of dishonesty in the working
of this Act. There are people in receipt
of pensions who have nice accounts in the
bank, accounts which would alarm
us if we knew the amount.
They are lying at very little interest, and,
therefore, these people draw their pensions. There are many other people,
of course who have very little subsistence,
and it would be a pity if we should have
to curtail these people's pensions. A
great deal, however, can be done in
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
regard to reconstruction of old age
pensions without taking away anything
from the deserving poor, or reducing the
amounts paid to them.
In regard to hospitals, I agree with
what my right hon. Friend has said as to
the condition of affairs. In my own
county we have a head county hospital,
and we have to contribute £1,200 to
£2,000 a year to it from the rates. Then
we have 3 or 4 or 5 local hospitals in the
county.
The CHAIRMAN: Perhaps you will
allow me to point out that the Minister
in submitting his Estimates in order to
avoid some discussion in detail travelled
some distance afield, but if we are going
as we are now into each item, I hope the
hon. Member will keep his criticism to
the particular Vote being dealt with. I
understand he desires to move an amendment to Section A-salaries, wages, and
allowances. If he desires to refer to
other matters that can be done later.
Does the hon. Member move the Amendment of which he has given me notice?
Mr. COOTE: Yes; if I am in order.
Taking up my strictures from where I
left off. This great waste of expenditure that we have in some of
counties--
our
The CHAIRMAN: Please formally
indicate your Amendment.
Mr. COOTE: I beg to move
"That Vote in Clause A-Salaries,
Wages, and Allowances, be reduced by
the sum of £100."
We have in these counties some small
hospitals, and some of them have up to
only about 15 or 16 patients. A fourth of
these would be old age pensioners. They
are really hostels. They are not
hospitals in the true sense of the word.
We have to keep night and day nursing
staffs, and
and we have to keep a fever
nursing staff. We have to keep these
nurses ready without fail. My contention is these hospitals in the county
should be grouped. We have an excellent motor service now, and patients
can be moved with comfort and ease to a
central hospital where there are better
appliances. In these local hospitals we
have very little appliances, and people
will not go to them. They go to Belfast
or the County Infirmary. They will not
go to the county hospitals because we
have not the appliances necessary for
their proper treatment.
I merely throw
out the suggestion to the Minister.
In regard to the upkeep of sanatoria,
I submit this whole work has been very
largely--
The CHAIRMAN: Perhaps the hon.
Member is aware he is now entering into
an actual detailed discussion, and as he
has given me a specific amendment under
a specific head criticism for the moment
must be restricted to that subject.
When he reaches other matters he can,
if he so desires, move an amendment, and
have them discussed.
Mr COOTE: The Minister referred to
the appointment of a medical superintendent, and talked of these things.
The CHAIRMAN: He was indicating
the various sub-heads, but we must
discuss them sub-head by sub-head, one
at a time. At a later stage the hon.
Member can, if he wishes, discuss the
specific matter of these hospitals.
Mr. COOTE: Under sub-head A,
taking it in its own compartment, the
point which I wish to raise is at the
bottom-war bonus- and I wish the
Minister to give a general statement on
that question. There is much misapprehension in the country and in this House
as to who, and how many, are receiving
these bonuses in the various departments.
We quite realise that in taking a permanent civil servant, transferring him
from Dublin to here, and taking him at
the salary which he enjoyed in Dublin,
we have a right also to give him the
emoluments of his office prescribed by
Parliament; that is his necessary bonus.
There is no opposition to that line of
action. But we do object to any official
who may be brought in as secretary, or
in any capacity other than a civil servant transferred to any of these departments, participating in a bonus. We
want to know how far many offices have
transgressed in this regard. We object
to their doing so. Probably they have
not transgressed at all. I raise this
point in order that the Minister may give
a general statement on this matter, and
save the time of the House in further
discussions that may come up on these
Estimates. I want to know whether
where the word "inclusive " appears as a
footnote, is that the only salary payable,
and it does not follow that there is a
bonus. We want to get a general
assurance which would satisfy the House.
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
We do not want to take up the time of
the House by raising this question in
every department. Then there are some
secretaries, and very important secretaries, who are appointed, and who are
not civil servants. Are they receiving
bonuses?
I could mention them, but I
do not wish to do so.
Mr. BARBOUR: As this is a general
matter perhaps it would be proper for
me to reply. I would like to assure the
hon. Member that the bonuses are only
payable to established civil servants.
They do not apply to temporary clerks
recently appointed.
Mr. COOTE: Or to any of the
secretaries?
Mr. BARBOUR: Where a salary is set
opposite a secretary and it is stated "inclusive the bonus does not apply.
If the word inclusive is not opposite
then the bonus applies.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put, and agreed to.
LAW CHARGES AND CRIMINAL
PROSECUTIONS.
Resolved: "That a sum not exceeding
Six thousand, eight hundred and twelve
pounds be granted to His Majesty for the
Service of the period ending 31st March,
1922, to defray the Expenses of Criminal
Prosecutions and other Law Charges."
COUNTY COURT OFFICERS.
Resolved: "That a sum not exceeding
Five thousand and forty pounds be granted
to His Majesty for the Service of the period
ending 31st March, 1922, to defray the
Salaries, Allowances and Expenses of various County Court Officers.'
""
Mr. COOTE: I had an amendment down
at B. Page 27. Should I have raised it on
your putting the motion?
The CHAIRMAN: The time to raise it
was when I put the Question.

PRISONS.
Motion made and Question proposed:
That a sum not exceeding Twenty-two
thousand, five hundred pounds be granted
to His Majesty for the Service of the period
ending 31st March, 1922, to defray the
Expenses of Prisons."
Mr. COOTE: I beg to move that “Sub-
Head A" (Pay and Allowances of Officers)
be reduced by £1,000."
I raise the question simply for the purpose of asking a question and to get some
idea as to what steps the Minister of Home
Affairs has taken in order to prevent a repetition of the deplorable events which
happened in Londonderry Prison the
other morning. It is one of the scandals
of Irish Prison Service all over that
prisoners of a certain type can escape, and
escape in such numbers, and so frequently,
and the only explanation apparently is that
there must be great sympathy inside and
that there is very lax administration inside. Whoever is Governor of these prisons
ought to be held responsible for such incidents. I hope that the Minister will
change the whole face of things and give
not only the impression but apply the
reality to the whole situation and see that
all the servants of the Crown will be loyal
to the work they have on hand, and carry
it out with the greatest desire to preserve
the lives and bodies of those inside, and
not under any circumstances aid and abet
those who will try to destroy law and order
in this Province.
Mr. DONALD: I rise to support the
amendment. I think it is very necessary
at this juncture we should have a discussion on this very important matter. I take
it the sum of £12,530 covers a period of
four months for pay and allowances of officers. I think there will have to be some
economy effected with regard
to our
prisons. We have prisons in Belfast, Londonderry, and Armagh. I am quite sure
my right hon. Friend (Sir Dawson Bates)
will go round these places himself and investigate what the procedure has been in
them. In reading the report of Mr. Justice
Dodd in connection with the Commission
that was set up, one could not help thinking the condition of affairs was a disgrace
to any country. An inquiry was held in
Belfast-
The CHAIRMAN: I must ask the hon.
Member to remember that having regard
to the fact that we only took over control
from an appointed day obviously the present Minister of Home Affairs could not
be responsible for proceedings long anterior
to his entering upon office. If the hon.
Member wishes, without specifically referring to matters which are clearly excluded
by the date, he can discuss the general incident.
Supply.
Supply.
8 DECEMBER 1921.
Mr. DONALD: I bow to your ruling. I
think these figures seem rather high.
£12,530 for four months means that it will
take a sum of £37,590 for the year. Surely
it is very necessary that discussion should
take place with a view to some economy
being reached with regard to these prisons.
Furthermore, the conduct of officials has
certainly not been creditable. I am quite
satisfied from the reports that I have received that they have not been looking
after their duties when people have been
allowed to escape time and again. Yet
the country is asked to pay high salaries
to these officials. I merely rise to ask my
right hon. Friend will he be good enough
to look into this whole question of our
prisons? I think the expenditure which it
takes to run these prisons should be very
much lower than that indicated in the
Vote.
Before sitting down may I say that I
am entirely dissatisfied with the number of
Members in this House just now, and I
want to enter my protest. We are discussing here an expenditure of over
£2,000,000, and there are only something
like a dozen Members present. If that is
the interest Members take in the affairs of
the House then we should close down. I
want to enter my protest against such a
disgraceful state of affairs.
Sir DAWSON BATES: With regard to
the point raised by the hon. Member for
Tyrone and Fermanagh (Mr. Coote) and
the hon. Member for East Belfast (Mr.
Donald) the prison service, you will
realise, was only taken over on the 1st
December, and we had to take it as it
stood. It was formerly administered by a
Board situated in Dublin. Personally I
am not satisfied with the way prisons are
being conducted in Northern Ireland, and
we are going very closely into the matter
to see, first of all, whether or not we can
get greater efficiency. I realise only too
well there is no Service that requires
loyalty more than the Prisons Service. The
points that the hon. Members have raised
will receive very careful consideration.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put and agreed to.
REFORMATORY AND INDUSTRIAL
SCHOOLS.
Resolved, "That a sum not exceeding
Four thousand eight hundred pounds be
granted to His Majesty for the Service of
the period ending 31st March, 1922, to
defray the Expenses of Reformatory and
Industrial Schools.'
CENTRAL CRIMINAL LUNATIC
ASYLUM.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That a sum not exceeding One
thousand six hundred and fifty pounds
be granted to His Majesty for the Service
of the period ending 31st March, 1922,
to defray the Expenses of the Maintenance of Criminal Lunatics."
Mr. CRAWFORD: I beg to ask a
question. Some time ago a promise was
made in regard to the capitation grant
to be given to the various authorities in
charge of asylums. That promise has
not been kept. The grant was smaller
than expected, and the balance has fallen
on local rates. I think some light should
be thrown on that subject before any
money is granted for this purpose.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think
there is some misunderstanding on the
part of the hon. Member. This resolution only deals with the Central Criminal
Lunatic Asylum.
Mr. DONALD: I am sorry for taking
up so much of the time of the House, but
this is a matter of very great importance,
and I rise to move a reduction of £100 in
the sum named in the resolution. I
want to raise an important point with
regard to these asylums. The grants in
1917 and 1918-
The CHAIRMAN: Perhaps there is
a little confusion in this matter. As the
Minister of Home Affairs has just explained, this relates merely
the
Central Criminal Lunatic Asylum, and
the subject raised by the hon. Member is
