9 DECEMBER 1921.
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Friday, 9th December, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Business of the House.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That, notwithstanding anything contained in Standing Order No. 15, Paragraph 5, business other than the business
of Supply may be taken before half past
six o'clock this day."
Mr. BURN: Under the Standing
Order referred to it is provided that on
the allotted days for Supply, of which
this is one, no other business can be taken
before half past six.
This necessarily
means that if the discussion on the
Estimates is ended to-day before that
hour we would be unable to take another
Order on the Paper, namely the Second
Reading of the Salaries of Ministerial
Offices Bill, and it is to enable us to do
so if necessary that I make this Motion
now. I have to make the Motion owing
to the fact that it does not appear on the
Order Paper, and I can only move it with
the general concurrence of the House.
Question put, and agreed to.
Orders of the Day.
SUPPLY-(2ND ALLOTTED DAY).
In Committee.
Mr. MOLES in the Chair.
CLASS I.
HOUSES OF PARLIAMENT.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
“That a sum not exceeding £2,695 be
granted to His Majesty to complete the
sum necessary for the service of the period
ending 31st March, 1922, to defray the
salaries and expenses of the Senate and
of the House of Commons (Voted on
Account £14,000).
Mr. COOTE: I desire to raise certain
matters upon this motion. I do not wish
-I do not think it would be proper considering that the Prime Minister, who
has something to do with some of these
negotiations, cannot be in the Houseto divide the House. Therefore, I do
not wish to move a concrete Amendment,
but I wish to call attention to certain
anomalies, and to make a formal protest.
In the salaries and the offices of the
House I see, in the first place, there is a
Counsel to the Speakers of the House of
Commons and of the Senate, who is Examiner of Petitions for Private Bills and
Taxing Officer for both Houses, at a fixed
salary of £1,000 rising to £1,200. We
also see in another place that there is
another Counsel being appointed, I think
to the Treasury. I would like to ask the
Minister in charge to-day if the Government contemplate keeping these salaried
officials, and what they will have to do
as whole-time officers? It needs some explanation. When the Minister replies I
will be very glad if he will take
matters.
against
a note of these two
I wish also to protest
the cost of administering the Clerkships
of both Houses. I do not know on what
condition-and that is the reason I do
not wish to put down any amendmentthe two principal Clerks, that is the Clerk
of the House of Commons and the Clerk
of the Senate, who also has a joint office
as Clerk Assistant to the House of Commons at a salary of £1,500 a year, I am
at a loss to know how these appointments
were made, and if the Government had
regard to all the circumstances and the
whole situation, because on the face of
them they look very excessive salaries.
I do not put the matter further because
I quite expect an explanation. We also
see that following those two very responsible and very proper and very estimable
officials there is a second Clerk Assistant
to the House of Commons. He is
available for both Houses. Well, I can
conceive under certain circumstances that
these gentlemen's services will be required
alternately as the case may be in either
House, but, apart from these officials,
where is the necessity for two assistant
clerks? Here we have five clerks between
the two Houses. Where is the necessity
for these two assistant clerks who follow
subsequently on the rota here at a salary
of £200 rising to £250 a year each?
Those are points I should like cleared up
by the Parliamentary and Financial
Secretary to the Ministry of Finance.
when he replies. On the same page, Sub-
Head D,' "Salaries, Wages, and
Allowances," we do with very great respect feel that the Serjeant-at-Arms and
Black Rod and all these offices are costing too much money. We feel they
should be cut down. What is the necessity for an Assistant Sergeant-at-Arms?
Is not it possible to have some gentleman
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Coote.]
in some of the departments, some of the
secretaries, if you like some clerk, to fill
the position? We can quite understand
that, under circumstances when the
Serjeant-at-Arms might be obliged to
leave the House, we might have some
person to sit in his chair and perform
his duties, but not to have to pay for an
Assistant Serjeant-at-Arms in addition
to what we consider is already an excessive amount. This is an office that
could be filled with great propriety by
most military men. I hope nothing that
I am saying will be taken personally-I
am sure it will not-because we do esteem
the gentleman who at the moment
fills the office; but surely £600 plus £200
for the rent of a house is too much money
for the position of Serjeant-at-Arms for
the time this House is sitting, which we
hope, when we get into our stride, will
only be about three months in the year.
These are matters I wish to put before
the Treasury, and I do it in no censorious
spirit, but in the interests of all concerned
they ought to be considered.
There is another point I wish to
mention, and that is in regard to the
Permanent Secretary to the Cabinet. I
have tried to get, and I have got, certain
information on this question. The present holder of this office, who was not a
civil servant, will now have conferred
upon him the dignity of a civil servant,
and, therefore, in addition to his salary
he will come in for bonus, and, if he survives in his office ten years, for pension.
Most of us take exception to the appointment of a Secretary and Assistant Secretrary to the Cabinet. We feel strongly
on the matter when we remember that
up till the war the Prime Minister of
Great Britain and Ireland had not an
official secretary. It seems out of place
that this small House, with its very
meagre income, should have attached to
the Cabinet and Prime Minister these
two very important officials.
I have dealt with the question of
Parliamentary Counsel. There are three
offices of this kind-Parliamentary
Counsel, who enjoys a salary of £1,000
rising to £1,200, Counsel to Mr. Speaker,
and Counsel to the Treasury. Surely we
do not want three counsel at salaries of
such importance. Surely those offices
could in some way be amalgamated. I
do not know if that could be done, but
I suggest it. I hope that the Minister,
when he comes to reply, will give some
explanation of those three contemplated
appointments.
These are all the criticisms I wish to
bring to bear on the services of this House
and of the Ministry, but I do again plead
with the Minister to consider these secretarial offices, the number of them and
their value, the Serjeant-at-Arms and
the Assistant Serjeant-at-Arms, and also
Black Rod. I should say that we all
recognise gratefully the services of Black
Rod, and that the sum be put down
here-
The CHAIRMAN: I think I should
point out to the hon. Member that it is
somewhat delicate to discuss in connection
with the Ministry of Finance here the
sums paid for appointments over which
the Ministry has no control. It is somewhat delicate to discuss Black Rod who is
not a servant of this particular House,
the appointment being within the prerogative of the other Chamber.
Mr. COOTE: But, with great respect,
surely it is within the prerogative of the
House of Commons to discuss this matter.
The question of finance in regard to all
servants, whether they belong to the
Senate or the Commons, must come before
this House.
The CHAIRMAN: Yes, but once the
appointment has been approved, and the
salary fixed, it is difficult to see how you
can break a bargain by way of criticisın.
I
Mr. COOTE: I am very sorry.
claim your indulgence. We did not enter
into any bargain with Black Rod, or with
these other officers.
The CHAIRMAN: For the good and
sufficient reason that he is not an officer
of this House. He has been appointed
by the other House. A bargain was
made as to the emoluments that he is to
receive for the services that he there
renders. Criticism directed to a breach
of that bargain is hardly in order.
Mr. COOTE: I must bow to your ruling,
but I demur to it. I must stand strongly
on the point that the Senate cannot make
any bargain on any matter of finance that
cannot be amended in this House. If the
Chairman had allowed me to go on another sentence, I was going to pay a
tribute to the very generous attitude of
the present holder of the office of Black
Rod. This salary is merely down here in
case he might resign and the office become
vacant. I mentioned the matter really
Supply.
Supply.
9 DECEMBER 1921.
in order to pay a tribute to the very
magnanimous way in which we have
been treated by the present Black Rod.
I must bow to your ruling, but with great
respect I do not accept it. This House
must always keep in its hands the control
of the Finance of both Houses.
AND
THE PARLIAMENTARY
FINANCIAL SECRETARY TO THE
MINISTRY OF FINANCE (Mr.
Barbour): I will endeavour to deal with
the points that have been raised by my
hon. Friend, the Member for Tyrone and
Fermanagh (Mr. Coote). I am glad to
be able to assure him that some of the
points to which he has very properly
drawn attention have had very serious
consideration by the different departments concerned. We were very desirous, if it was in any way possible, to
amalgamate these different services of
say, counsel for the different departments. In fact we carried that idea a
good deal further in regard to all the
services of the different Ministries.
Wherever there were officials carrying
out somewhat similiar duties it was our
desire that the officials for those duties
should be pooled as far as possible, so
that we might at least be able to employ
one really very expert officer, and have the
benefit of his knowledge and experience
extended to the other offices in which he
would be able to give assistance, and at
the same time enable a more moderately
paid official to carry out the work of the
different departments. Where that
policy has been possible it has been
adopted.
In the case of the officials to whom
attention has been drawn, I am glad to
say that there has just been completed
a change which will meet to a very great
extent the criticism which has been raised
by my hon. Friend. I refer to page 5 of
the Estimates, Counsel to the Speakers.
I am glad to say that instead of the salary
set down there, namely a salary rising
from £1,000 to £1,200, we have been
able to make arrangements to get this
work efficiently carried on for a sum of
£400 a year. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) So far as the Counsel to the
Finance Minister is concerned, the work
attached to this post is very onerous,
indeed. He is our Parliamentary
Draughtsman, and has to consider all
Bills of both a public and private character. I need hardly say that although
these are his specific duties he will be
glad at all times to give to the other
Departments the benefit of his legal
knowledge and experience.
As regards the Clerkships of the House
of Commons, we are able, I think, to
form very little idea of the extent of the
work, or its really onerous and very detailed character, by merely observing
the attitude and duties of the Clerks of
the House of Commons as we see them
here. It is the duty of the Clerk of the
House of Commons to examine all Bills
submitted to the House, and to arrange
the order of business during its sessions,
but after Parliament rises it is his duty
to go over all the records and business
of the House, to analyse these, and make
a detailed record and index, so that they
should be properly available for future
reference. And so his duties are not
merely confined to the time that the
House is sitting here. I will give you an
illustration. In thinking of all these
salaries we should always bear in mind
the case in which a Bill was once furnished for what was considered not only
a very short job, but also a very simple
The items for material expenditure
were absolutely negligible, but the sum
total of the Bill was a large one, and was
made up of one item of so much for
knowing how." So it is really not so
much the labour of carrying out the
duties that have been entrusted to these
gentlemen as the knowledge and experience which they acquired through
many years of public service. Take the
case of the present Clerk of the House of
Commons: he spent over forty years in
the Imperial House of Commons, acquiring the knowledge which enables him,
apparently with little effort, to ensure
that our whole proceedings can go forward constitutionally and smoothly.
After all, I think it is evidence of the
one.
(6
efficiency of any managing director
in any public company that the proceedings of the directors should be really
restricted to formal matters, and should
go through with the least possible delay
and criticism. In other words, if he
has done his work well and efficiently it
reduces the number of items which require discussion and ratification at the
more formal meetings. The Clerkships
of the House of Commons are really a
very important Department. I think
there are upwards of 35 Clerks in the Imperial House of Commons, and I think
that the Vote for that Department requires no less than a sum of £60,000 per
annum. The present occupant of our
Clerk's Chair has done a great deal in
HH
Supply.
Supply .
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
relieving our burden by occupying such
time as he may have to spare in doing
work of a comparatively junior character. In other words, he has not confined those duties to those major matters
which require his great knowledge and
skill, but has occupied his time-and
occupied it very fully--with carrying out
staff work which he could do, in order to
facilitate the progress of the busir.ess of
the House. We have to provide for the
Clerks not only of the House of Commons, but also of the Senate, and we
have to provide that these posts shall be
occupied even though the sittings of both
Houses, possibly concurrently, may not be
of extended duration. We must have someone to come in and give relief, so that
both Houses may be properly staffed, if
need be, at the same time. As regards
the other dignatories of the House, I
would hope that although it may cost
money sentiment and tradition will
always carry some weight in the
minds of hon. Members. After all,
these posts are not the growth
of the moment, or due to any
sudden inspiration or any aspiration to
magnify the importance of this House.
They were also survivals of old-time
customs of far greater institutions than
ours. Each little detail, if we care to
investigate it, will, I am sure, point back
to some cause for its origin which we may
respect, and we may be unwilling to see
these old customs and traditions dispensed with. If we were to go further
afield into the Houses of other countries
which may, perhaps, pride themselves on
their newness and freedom from traditions, I think we will find that many of
these old customs have been introduced and still obtain, and in our early
stages here let us try to follow the noble
example that has been set us. After
all, the Mother of Parliaments has been
copied by nearly every deliberative
assembly in the world, and let us give it
a trial until we have acquired greater
experience and are in a position to see
whether any of these old institutions can
be dispensed with. These items only
commit us to the one year, and in any
future year when these Estimates are
introduced, the House can re-consider
these items, and, if necessary, amend or
abolish them, but in the meantime we
have always the gratification of knowing
that these positions, ornamental though
you may term them, are at least occupied
by those who have given the best years
of their life to real and meritorious
service for their country. As regards
the Secretary of the Cabinet, I believe
that in the Imperial Cabinet it was not
the custom to keep records of the proceedings at Cabinet meetings, but since the
war all such duties as would ordinarily
attach themselves to the Secretariet of
the Cabinet were discharged by the
Secretary of the Prime Minister, and he
has always had a Secretariet who could
discharge these duties. As you know,
the only experience I have had of
Cabinet work has been rather of æ
privileged character, but I must say that
the present Secretary of our Cabinet has
had to carry on his work under conditions
of very great stress indeed.
I was over in London when the proposals were submitted to us which led up
to what may or may not turn out to be
a settlement of the Irish question. We
were meeting in London, and there is
one thing to which the public are entitled,
and that is to know what is going on
during such critical times as exist at
present. If you had seen our Secretary
endeavouring to keep track of what was
proceeding, jumping up every two or
three minutes to attend to some inquiry
at the telephone, and after we got
through in the evening, to give such an
account of our proceedings as it was
proper for the public to receive, compatible with the secrecy which it was
necessary should attend such negotiations,
I think you would have admitted that it
requires a man of suitable temperament,
and a man of good judgment and
diplomacy to discharge the duties of that
position. It is hard for an outsider, as
I was until a comparatively short time
ago, to judge the nature of the duties
which are attached to these positions, but
from the experience I have had I consider
the duties are very onerous, and I hope
the House will not cavil at the salaries
which have been recommended for these
offices.
Mr. CRAWFORD: I desire to call
attention to the miserable salaries proposed to be paid to the Chaplains in
comparison to the salaries paid to other
officials. I think the salaries of the
Chaplains are disgraceful and ought to
be increased. I hope the Minister of
Finance will see his way to make these
salaries commensurate with the reverence
and respect he ought to have for those
who lead our devotions.
I associate myself with the remarks that have fallen
from the hon. Member for Tyrone and
Supply.
Supply.
9 DECEMBER 1921.
Fermanagh. When you have a sum of
£2,500,000 less than the Estimate put
before us in the White Paper it behoves
all of us to do all we can to reduce expenditure in this House, and if there are
any positions which are purely ornamental and sentimental, we ought, in
filling them, to remember that the ratepayers have got to foot the bill. While
we would all be disposed to agree to whatever proposals are put before us by the
Cabinet, we have to remember that
money is scarce, and that these positions,
filled as they are at present by very
eminent men, may in time to come be
filled by less able men who will claim
the same salaries. We cannot always
allow considerations for the services
officials have already rendered, to guide
us when filling these posts. It would be
a very unwise policy. We must try
to cut down salaries to the level
of those that are paid outside. I consider
the salaries attached to the Clerks of the
Houses are exceedingly high, and I trust
we may be able to reduce the total amount
much more. Under Section D, I think the
sum of £2,700 for the department of the
Serjeant-at-Arms is too much. I know we
are at a critical stage, and that it is very
difficult for Members of Parliament to put
estimates before us because they have not
had experience in the past, but there are
certain items about which we ought to be
very careful and to which the pruning
knife could be applied.
Question put and agreed to.
CABINET OFFICES.
Resolved: "That a sum not exceeding
£6,372, including a supplementary sum of
£5,887, be granted to His Majesty to complete the sum necessary for the services of
the period ending 31st March, 1922, to
defray the salaries of the Cabinet and the
salaries and expenses of the Department
of the Prime Minister. (Voted on account
£3,000.) "
MINISTRY OF FINANCE.
Resolved: "That a sum not exceeding
£43,325, including a supplementary sum
of £42,485, be granted to His Majesty, to
complete the sum necessary for the service
of the period ending 31st March, 1922, to
defray the salaries and expenses of the
Ministry of Finance, including Revenue
services, Valuation, Registration, Loan
services, Public Works. (Voted on account
£4,300.)"
CONTROLLER AND AUDITOR-
GENERAL.
Resolved: That a sum not exceeding
£725, including a supplementary sum of
£705, be granted to His Majesty, to complete the sum necessary for the service of
the period ending 31st March, 1922, to
defray the expenses of the Department of
the Controller and Auditor-General.
(Voted on account £80.)"
PUBLIC WORKS AND BUILDINGS.
Motion made and Question proposed:
"That a sum not exceeding £66,265,
including a supplementary sum of
£55,485, be granted to His Majesty, to
'complete the sum necessary for the service
of the period ending 31st March, 1922, in
respect of Public Works and Buildings
(Voted on account £50,000.)"
Mr. CRAWFORD: I understand that
these buildings are to be provided and paid
for by the Imperial Parliament, but as a
representative of a country district I would
like to point out to the Cabinet that even
if we do get a present of these buildings it
will be incumbent on them to see that
extravagant buildings are not erected, because the expense of keeping them up will
fall on the country. If someone came to a
farmer and offered to erect a castle on his
land it would be a white elephant to him.
I hope that only the amount of money
necessary to erect a suitable Parliament
House will be spent, and that we will not
be left with an immense building to keep
up in future years.
Mr. BARBOUR: My hon. Friend, the
Member for Antrim (Mr. Crawford), has
quite realized the nature of these estimates
as they appear here, and is quite familiar
with the fact that these buildings are a
gift from the Imperial Parliament at
Westminster. The procedure is that the
Board of Works in London come over here.
and it is within their province to provide
a site for the new Parliament House and
Ministerial Offices, which will be given to
us, and while the amounts expended
thereon will pass through our accounts
they will be refunded by the Imperial
Treasury later on, and so will constitute
no burden on this Province. The decision
in these matters really rests with the
Board of Works, and we have a certain
hesitation about interfering in any way
with their decision, although they have
up to now treated us with every consideration in this matter. At the same time we
must realize that while they are paying
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Barbour.]
the bill they must be satisfied with the
expenditure, as they have to account for it
to the Imperial Parliament. I know that
certain items and certain prices they have
given in respect of properties they have
purchased in Belfast have been criticised,
but I can assure hon. Members that the
Board of Works have inquired into these
matters in as thorough and searching a
manner as they possibly could have done.
We would not wish to be in a position of
actually commandeering property which
may be required for our public offices, and
we have felt that if we interfered in any
way with the exercise of the discretion of
the Board of Works they might in some
way or other throw back some responsi
bility on us when these amounts come to
be justified before the Imperial Parliament.
As for attempting to restrict them in their
expenditure, and inducing them to go on
more moderate lines than they are disposed
to, I can assure hon. Members that we
shall take every step we can to ensure that
the buildings as a whole will be most economical to maintain.
Question put, and agreed to.
STATIONERY AND PRINTING.
Motion made and Question proposed:
That a sum not exceeding £245, including
a supplementary sum of £50, be granted to
His Majesty to complete the sum necessary
for the services of the period ending 31st
March, 1922, to defray the expenses of
providing stationery, printing paper, binding
books, and office supplies, for the public
service, and for the Houses of Parliament,
and for the production of the "Belfast
Gazette." (Voted on Account, £530.)
Mr. DONALD: I merely rise to ask a
question. I noticed in the Press this morning in the financial statement of my right
hon. Friend yesterday, the amount for
printing is £50. I think that is misleading, because we are really dealing with that
matter to-day. I should like to ask has
a contract been given out for printing for
the House, and what is the sum per annum
for that contract-the printing of the debates and so forth?
The CHAIRMAN: The question that is
asked of the Minister is outside the scope
of this particular matter. The total sum
he is called upon to account for is a sum
not exceeding £245, including £50, and my
right honourable Friend must restrict his
questions to that.
Mr. DONALD: I think I am in order in
asking the question: What is the amount
of expenditure for printing given out by
this House?
The CHAIRMAN: No; he is not in
order. He is restricted to the specific matters in the total of £245. There will be an
opportunity of raising the question on another Vote.
Question put and agreed to.
VISIT OF THEIR MAJESTIES THE
KING AND QUEEN.
Resolved: That a sum not exceeding £60
be granted to His Majesty to complete the
sum necessary for the service of the period
eending 31st March, 1922, to pay such of
the expenses in connection with the visit of
their Majesties the King and Queen to open
Parliament as fall to be defrayed by the
Government of Northern Ireland. (Voted
on Account, £1,140.)
CIVIL CONTINGENCIES FUND.
Resolved: That a sum not exceeding
£5,000 be granted to His Majesty to complete the sum necessary for the service of
the period ending 31st March, 1922, to provide for the establishment of the Civil Contingencies Fund of the Government of
Northern Ireland. (Voted on Account,
£45,000.)
OLD AGE PENSIONS.
Resolved: That a sum not exceeding
£410,200 be granted to His Majesty for the
service of the period ending 31st March,
1922, for the payment of Old Age Pensions
for certain administrative expenses in connection therewith and for Pensions under
the Blind Pensions Act.
SUPERANNUATION AND RETIRED
ALLOWANCES.
Resolved: That a sum not exceeding
£3,000 be granted to His Majesty for the
service of the period ending 31st March,
1922, to pay Superannuation and additional allowances and gratuities under
sundry statutes and supplementary pensions, and allowances granted by the
Ministry of Finance.
LOCAL LOANS.
Resolved: That a sum not exceeding
£50,000 be granted to His Majesty for the
Supply
Supply .
9 DECEMBER 1921.
service of the period ending 31st March,
1922, to provide for Loans to Local Authorities for Public Works in connection with
Health, Housing, etc., Services.
CLASS IV.
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION.
Motion made and Question proposed:
That a sum not exceeding £19,313, including a supplementary sum of £18,538, be
granted to His Majesty to complete the sum
necessary for the service of the period ending 31st March, 1922, to defray the salaries
and expenses of the Ministry of Education. (Voted on Account, £4,500.)
Mr. M'KEOWN: The Minister has asked
me to bring these Estimates before the
House, and I shall endeavour to do so in as
simple, and, at the same time, as lucid a
manner as I possibly can. These Estimates
cover the period from 1st February, 1922,
the date at which the services are to be
transferred to Northern Ireland, until 31st
March, 1922. Hon. Members will recollect
that the Estimates, which received the approval of the House in September last, did
not provide for the full administrative expenditure which would have to be met by
the Northern Government. Nor did they
make provision for Educational Grants or
direct expenditure in connection with the
various services. In the absence of any
date for the Transfer of the Educational
Services, it was impracticable to make any
provision of the nature indicated. Now
that the date of transfer has been fixed,
namely, the 1st February, it becomes necessary to provide for the expenditure which
the Ministry will have to meet for the last
two months of the financial year.
The Estimate is submitted under two
heads, viz. :-(1) For the Salaries and Expenses of the Ministry, and (2) for Grants
in aid of Education. In regard to the
Estimate for Salaries and Expenses of the
Ministry, it should be explained that until
the Civil Service Committee has sat and
determined the allocation of the existing
staffs of the various Educational Boards as
between the North and the South, the
organisation of the staff of the Ministry on
anything except a provisional basis must
Lecessarily remain in abeyance.
For the purpose of the Estimates it has
been necessary to anticipate, to some extent
on a tentative basis the decision of the
Civil Service Committee. The staff from
assistant secretaries downwards, and including Inspectors, has been taken as the total
staff that the Ministry has reason to suppose will be transferred from the three
Dublin Educational Offices. In some cases
the Ministry has the names of the officials
who have been provisionally allocated to
the North; in the case of others the names
are not available, and the Ministry has information only as to the numbers and
grades proposed to be transferred.
All the inspectors and organisers included in the Estimate are at present on the
staffs of one or other of the Dublin Boards.
The item of £250 for Occasional Inspectors
has been inserted simply to meet emergencies.
It may be interesting to point out that
the financial proposals now submitted for
the two months of February and March
compare favourably with the total cost of
administration and inspection under existing arrangements in the South, and represent about 28 per cent. of the all-Ireland
expenditure.
The amounts taken under the other subheads of Vote number I. are necessarily
somewhat speculative. Under Sub-head B
-travelling subsistence and removal expenses- the travelling expenses are based
on a proportion of the total Estimated expenditure for all Ireland-in 1921 and
1922. Provision has also had to be made
to meet the cost of removal of office staff
from Dublin in February and March, and
both removal expenses and subsistence allowances payable to the transferred officers
are to some extent an unknown quantity.
Many of the salaries will not mature before the end of the financial year, but any
portion of the Vote not expended before
the end of the financial year will, of course.
be surrendered.
Sub-Head C. Rent: As honourable
Members know, that is recoverable from
the Imperial Government.
Sub-Heads D., E., F., G., H. are more
or less speculative.
Sub-Head I. Special Inquiries: This
Estimate is mainly for the expenses of the
Committee of Inquiry which is now at
work. Provision was made for this service
in the Estimates of September last, and
credit is taken for the amount which it is
estimated will at 31st January next remain
unexpended out of the sum then provided.
I would now refer honourable Members
to the grants in aid of Education under
Heading No. 2. Before dealing with the
various sub-heads of this note it might be
well to refer to the Ministries of Northern
Ireland Bill now before Parliament-as a
matter of fact I think it is almost the law
of the House. If that Bill is passed it
will not be necessary, as was originally con-
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
:-
[Mr. McKeown.]
templated, to set up the following separate
bodies :- -Commissioners of National Education for Northern Ireland, Intermediate
Education Board for Northern Ireland.
Commissioners for Endowed Schools for
Northern Ireland. Instead, the whole adm.inistration will be vested directly in the
Ministry. But as honourable Members will
recollect, it will be open to the Minister to
distribute the business of his Ministry
amongst the various officers, and, if he
thinks fit, by Minute to be laid before Parliament, to appoint Boards or Committees
to assist him. But whatever arrangement
is made, it is expressly provided in the Bill
that the Minister cannot delegate his control or responsibility in respect of any of
the services. In regard to the administration of the statutory educational grants it
is not contemplated that any important
change should be made at once. Indeed,
looking to the various educational statutes
now in force, and which must remain in
force until amended or repealed by Parliament, it would not be practicable to do so.
Moreover, as hon. Members are aware, a
Committee, presided over by the hon. Member for West Belfast is inquiring into the
educational services in Northern Ireland.
And I would just like to say here how
much this House and how much the country is indebted to the honourable Member
for West Belfast and to the ladies and
gentlemen who are endeavouring to solve
this very important question. That Committee will probably report at no distant
date, and their recommendation will no
doubt be the foundation for legislation by
Parliament. The measure to be submitted
to Parliament as soon as circumstances permit will be a comprehensive one and will
make suitable provision for the proper coordination of all the various services. In
the meantime, while no important changes
can be introduced pending such legislation,
it is hoped that the Ministry will be in a
position to proceed with such improvements
as can be effected administratively, that is,
in regard to the inspection of schools.
I would like to point out that in framing these Estimates for the two months
of February and March, 1922, the
general principle has been observed that
provision should be made only for such
sums as the Ministry will actually have
to disburse within the period in question.
Where the Dublin Departments have
been able or are likely to be able to expend before the 31st January next the
money provided in the Estimates for
1921-22, it has not been considered
necessary to make any provision in the
Northern Estimates for the months of
February and March, 1922, it being
understood that financial adjustments as
between the Imperial Government and
Northern Ireland and Southern Ireland
respectively in respect of the period
following the 22nd November, will be
made by the Joint Exchequer Board.
Where, however, the existing Departments will not be able to expend the
money provided for in the Imperial Estimates for 1921-22, before the Appointed
Day for the transfer of the Educational
Services, viz., 1st February, 1922, provision has been made in the Northern
Ireland Estimates for February and
March, 1922, for a sum corresponding to
what is likely to remain unspent by the
Departments in question at the 1st
February, it being assumed that after the
transfer of the services no further payments in respect of Northern Ireland will
be made by the existing Departments,
and that the claims will have to be met
by the Northern Government. In so far
as any such payments between 1st
February and 31st March, may relate to
Educational Service in respect of a
period before the 22nd November, 1921,
it is assumed the necessary adjustment
will be effected by the Joint Exchequer
Board.
Coming now to the details of the Estimate, Sub-Head A. deals with Elementary Education. The first item
(Sub-Head A1) relates to training
Colleges. The expenditure for all Ireland for 1921-22 has been estimated at
rather over £104,000. About thirtytwo per cent. for the entire teaching staff
is absorbed by the schools in Northern
Ireland, and sixty-eight per cent, by the
schools in the South. It is assumed that
so long as the existing colleges discharge
their present functions, the grants made
respectively to the North and South will
be in the same proportion. I am quite
sure that this question of training
colleges will receive every consideration
by the Committee which is now sitting,
and that it will afterwards be dealt with
by the Ministry. The sum of £6,897,
taken on the Estimate under Sub-Head
Al represents thirty-two per cent. of the
estimated expenditure in February and
March upon all the training colleges in
Ireland. The disbursement of this sum
will be made under arrangements with
the authorities in the South.
9 DECEMBER 1921.
Supply.

Supply.
The
paid to them on certain conditions as to
the employment of duly qualified
teachers. The grant does not go directly
to teachers.
Under Sub-Head A2 (Model Schools)
the sum of £350 appears.
note appearing in the Estimate explains
this item. The corresponding figure for
all Ireland for the years 1921-22 is
£4,231.
Under Sub-Head A3 (National
Schools) salaries of teachers, the sum is
£180,000. The corresponding figures for
all Ireland for 1921-22 are £4,059,450.
This is the main charge in the Estimate,
and includes the "placing" charges incidental to the new scales, of salaries
recently introduced for the National
School Teachers. The sum of £180,000
represents the amounts to be disbursed in
Northern Ireland in February and
March next.
We come now to a point which I think
requires a special explanation from me.
It is the question of Intermediate or
Secondary Education. Hon. Members
will recall the fact that prior to the
Northern Parliament being set up, and
after the Northern Parliament was set
up, strong representations were made to
the Chief Secretary, and through him to
the Imperial Government in aid of Intermediate Teachers and of Intermediate
Education in Ireland. The Burnham
Committee recommendations had been
introduced into England and Wales, and
similar scales are in operation in Scotland. The consequence is that Intermediate Teachers in Ireland are labouring under what must be considered a
great disadvantage.
Before stating what the Ministry proposes to do, I would like to draw the
attention of hon. Members to the State
Grants which have hitherto been in
existence.
(1) The Parliamentary Grant voted
under the Act of 1914-£40,000 for all
Ireland. This is known as the
"Teachers' Salaries Grant," and is sometimes described as the "Birrell" Grant.
I can not help remarking at this stage
that that word "Birrell" seems to be a
rather ominous one. I am sure we
would all like very much to forget that
period of Irish Administration with
which Mr. Birrell. was particularly
identified. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). This Grant was given for the
purpose of promoting the employment of
an adequate number of duly qualified
lay teachers in Intermediate Schools.
The money is distributed to managers
rateably in proportion to the results fees
(2) The Grant for Intermediate
Education, £500,000 for all Ireland. This
was first voted in 1918, and is sometimes
referred to as the "Duke" Grant. It
has hitherto been distributed in accordance with rules made by the Lord
Lieutenant. The Grant may be applied
(1) as regards a sum not exceeding
£2,000 for the establishment of courses
of instruction for teachers; (2) as regards
a sum of £5,000 for advances to managers
for new schools, extension and alteration
of existing schools, equipment, &c.
residue is distributed as a Capitation
Grant to managers of schools under conditions specified in the rules.
The
(3) The Interim Grant-£50,000 for
all Ireland. This Grant was distributed
for the first time in 1920, under Rule
dated 3rd August, 1920, made by the
Lord Lieutenant. These Rules provided
that the Grant was to be allocated between the schools on a basis similar to
that actually adopted in the distribution
of the Capitation Grant, and each school
had to distribute its share of the Grants
amongst the teachers employed therein
during the school year 1919-1920 in proportion to the salary paid to such
teacher for that period. Certain anomalies and defects having been discovered
in the Rules, a new set was made by the
Lord Lieutenant in June, 1921. These
Rules defined the term "Employed in an
Intermediate School," made special provision for the share of the Grant to be
allotted to each owner and headmaster,
and allowed the Intermediate Board to deduct a fixed annual sum of £200 to meet
the cost of administering the Grant. The
Grant provided in the Estimates for the
current financial year (1st April, 1921, to
31st March, 1922) was in respect of a
school year ending 31st July, 1921, and
was distributed to the schools in August
last. To prevent confusion, it would be
well to note that for the two years during
which the Grant has been in operation,
the School Session has been well under
weigh before the Grant in respect of that
Session was actually provided. The first
term of the current school session is now
approaching completion. In ordinary
circumstances the whole provision for the
current session would be made in the
Estimates for the financial year beginn
Supply.
Supply .
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. McKeown.]
ing 1st April next, and the money so
provided would be distributed in August
next after the close of the session.
As
The three Grants referred to, namely.
the "Birrell," the "Duke," and the
"Interim" Grants, being met for moneys
voted by the Imperial Parliament will
now have to be met by the Northern
Government. The present condition of
Intermediate Education in Northern
Ireland is such that, however necessary
it may
be in the present circumstances
to observe the strictest economy in
educational administration, there can be
no question of any reduction in the
amount of these Grants. On the contrary, after careful consideration, the
Ministry have come to the conclusion
that some additional assistance towards
an increase in the salaries of Intermediate Teachers is needed at once.
hon. Members are aware, the administration of the educational services will not
pass into the hands of the Northern
Government till 1st February next, and
the Government propose to provide an
additional Grant of £2,000 to cover the
period from that date to the end of the
financial year (31st March). The share
of the Annual All-Ireland Grant of
£50,000 applicable to Northern Ireland
is about £12,000. That is £1,000 per
month. The total Grants in respect of
the current school session will, therefore,
be at the rate of £1,000 per month for
the six months from 31st July, 1921, to
31st January, 1922. Thereafter-as
from the date on which the administration
passes into the hands of the
Northern Government-it will be in.
creased to £2,000 per month for the six
remaining months of the school session,
1921-22, and appropriate provision will
be made in the Estimates for 1922-23 for
the balance required to meet the expenditure at the enhanced rate for what
remains of the school session, and also for
a continuance of the Grant at the higher
rate for the school session, 1922-23. It
is hoped that this provisional measure
will be accepted as an earnest of the
Government's intention to deal sympathetically with the question of Intermediate Education, and that it will help
to meet the difficulties that at present
surround the question.
While this provision of £2,000 in respect of the months of February and
March, 1922, is made in the Estimates
in order to permit of an immediate payment on account to the teachers as soon
as practicable, after the money becomes.
available, it is not to be taken that the
actual distribution of the £2,000 for
February and March, or indeed the distribution of the Grant for the previous
part of the current school year, will
follow the lines adopted in
distribution of the Grant in
spect of the last school session.
the
re-
The actual distribution will be in accordance with a scheme to be submitted to
Parliament by the Ministry after full consideration of the returns which the Minister
has recently called for from all Intermediate schools in Northern Ireland. The
examination of those returns is now well
advanced, and the Minister hopes to be
able to formulate his proposals at an early
date.
I would like now to draw the attention
of hon. Members to sub-head C-Technical
Instruction. I have noticed that the
Technical Instruction Committees throughout Northern Ireland held a meeting very
recently, and as far as I can gather they
believe they have a grievance owing to the
fact that they were not more fully represented on the Committee which is now
sitting. I would like to take this oppor
tunity of pointing out that the Ministry is
fully alive to the importance of Technical
Education. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
At the same time it was quite impossible
to continue adding names to the Committee
owing to the fact that if a committee of
that kind is too large it becomes unwieldy.
I was pleased to note that at this meeting
they appointed a Committee prepared to
give evidence before the Commission, and
I am quite sure that the effect of their
evidence will just be as good and as effective
as if they were actually serving on the
Commission itself. As explained in the
note in the Estimates, this amount for
Technical Instruction was formerly payable
from the Endowment Fund of the Department of Agriculture and Technical Instruction. The figure for all Ireland for the
year 1921-1922 is £62,500, consisting,
inter alia, of moneys derivable from (1)
Local Taxation (Ireland) Account; (2) Irish
Church Temporalities Fund, and (3) certain
Parliamentary Grants. Northern Ireland's
share of the £62,500 would be about
£23,270 (£21,187 annual allocation and
£2,085 distributed as special grants.) The
whole of the £2,085 will have been distributed to the Committees before the 31st
January, 1922. The figure £3,500 therefore represents the proportion of the
Supply

Supply.
9 DECEMBER 1921.
£21,187 payable in respect of endowment
for the period covered by these Estimates.
Sub-head C 1 (2).-Annual Grants to
schools and classes of Science and Art and
Technical Instruction, etc. The administration of these grants was transferred to
the Department of Agriculture and Technical Instruction by the Act of 1899. These
grants were originally payable under the
conditions of the Science and Art Directory
Board of Education. The figure for all
Ireland for the year 1921-22 is £83,800.
and Northern Ireland's share thereof is
£30,000. The grants are in respect of work
done during the school session ended 31st
July, 1921. The figure of £7,500 inserted
in the Estimates represents the amount
which it is estimated will be required to
meet the claims which will still have to
be dealt with by the Department of
Agriculture and Technical Instruction
before the 31st January, 1922.
Sub-head C 1 (3).—Bonus of whole-time
staff paid from the funds of the local
Technical Instruction Committees. Since
1918 a Parliamentary grant has been made
to enable the local Committees to pay to
their whole-time staff a bonus at Civil
Service rates. The figure for all Ireland
for the year 1921-22 is £105,000, of which
£42,000 is payable to Committees in
Northern Ireland. £7,000, therefore, is
the amount due in respect of the months of
February and March.
Sub-head C 1 (5).-Grants for Day
Secondary Schools (Science, Drawing,
Manual Instruction and Domestic Economy). These were originally payable as
part of the Grants for Science and Art
under the conditions of the Science and
Art Directory. In 1901 a separate programme was adopted under which grants.
were payable for instruction in Experimental Science, Drawing, Manual Instruction and Domestic Economy. The figure
for all Ireland for the year 1921-22 is
£36,000, of which £11,000 is earned by
schools in Northern Ireland. These grants
are in respect of work done during the
school session ended 31st July, 1921. No
provision is made in the Estimates, as all
these claims have been paid by the Department of Agriculture and Technical Instruction.
You will notice in the Estimates a sum
of £4,210 in respect of the Queen's University, Belfast." This grant is under
Section 64 of the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, at the rate of £26,000 per
annum. I think there is no institution of
its kind in the world that is more satisfactory in its results than the Queen's
University of Belfast, and I am sure hon.
Members who represent that institution
are very proud of it, and they have every
right to be so. There was a further sum
paid on the 31st July of £18,000, of which
£16,000 was a very special grant and
£2,000 a-non-recurring grant. No doubt
representation regarding these grants
will be made to the Ministry by the University. I would like to say that in
considering these Estimates, and in going
through the work of administration, 1
found that under the existing organisations
much of the work is done in a very complicated and complex way. I feel that as
a result of the work of the Committee,
which is at present considering the question, and after they have made representations to the Ministry, it will be possible
for the Ministry to introduce a co-ordinated
system of education, based on a much more
practical, simple and effective method, and
that through those methods it will be possible for the Ministry to bring about a
considerable diminution in the cost of the
administration. I beg to submit the
Estimates.

Dr. MORRISON: I would just like to
make a few remarks. I wish, in the first
instance, to compliment the Parliamentary
Secretary to the Ministry of Education on
the lucidity and clearness with which he
put the facts before us. The first thing I
would like to ask about is, in the first page
of the Estimates, under the heading
Special Inquiries," there is an item of
£1,500, and, secondly, with regard to
primary education. I would like if the
Minister of Education could devise some
means by which an aggrieved teacher could
appeal to some responsible authority, or
by which an easy court of appeal would be
provided to revise any case of any act of
injustice or supposed injustice done to the
teacher. It was found necessary to provide a court of appeal for managers, and I
think it would be a very wise thing if such
a court were established for teachers.
Although I speak as a manager, I think if
it holds good for managers it should hold
good for teachers and other officials. Under
the old vicious system that was administered from Dublin we used to have the
salaries sent to the managers and not to
the men who earned them. Fortunately
that has been wiped away. It certainly
would not have continued long in a democratic community such as the North of
Ireland.
II
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
The CHAIRMAN: Perhaps I might call
the attention of the hon. Member to the
fact that there is great difficulty in discus
sing a question of policy on Estimates. I
have allowed the hon. Member to develop
his point, and perhaps he will now pass
from policy to the Estimates.
Dr. MORRISON: I have not very much
to say.
As a representative of the University I wish to say that while you give
£26,000 to the University I consider it
much too small a grant. I might venture
to say that when money becomes more
plentiful as I think it will in Northern
Ireland-you will probably become more
generous to the University. With regard
to secondary teachers this House is practically unanimous in recognising that something should be done for them, and the
Parliamentary Secretary to the Ministry
of Education has stated that an earnest has
been given of what the Department will do
for them. I have been in communication
with secondary teachers since this grant
was made, or since there was a possibility
of it being made. I understand
that there is a sum of £2,000
to be given for two months of
I
this year but a much larger sum
is to be given for the following year.
think the Education Office should see their
way to not confine themselves to the
£2,000, which is simply a dole, and if
spread over all the secondary teachers will
not give them more than a mere trifle, but
if they would take what they intend giving
for the coming year and add that to the
£2,000 it would go more closely to meeting
the case.
The CHAIRMAN: Perhaps I might
point out that it is not possible for the
Committee to increase an Estimate. They
can confirm or reduce, but it is for the
Ministry to make an increase, and even
then they cannot do it on these Estimates,
but must present Supplementary
Estimate.
a
Dr. MORRISON: I thought we were
allowed to make suggestions. I am merely
making a suggestion, and if we are so
bound up that we cannot make suggestions
there is not much use in discussing the
Estimates.

The CHAIRMAN: There is a good deal
of use in discussing these Estimates.
understand the suggestion made by the
hon. Member is that forthwith the

by the rules.
Dr. MORRISON: I bow to your ruling.
I admit freely that the Education Office
have met the teachers as far as they were
able in a very critical financial position,
I think the teachers recognise that the
Ministry from the Prime Minister down
through the Minister of Education and
the
the
Parliamentary Secretary to
Ministry of Education, are thoroughly in

the teachers I thank them for what F
hope is an earnest of the good times that
are coming.
I
Mr. GRANT: I would just like
to take this opportunity of congratulating my colleague the hon.
Member for North Belfast (Mr.
M'Keown) on the very lucid manner in
which he presented the accounts for the
Education Department. I do not wish to
criticise the Estimates, but I do wish to
take this opportunity of asking a couple of
questions. One is under the heading of
"Salaries, Wages, and Allowances."
want to know if the Minister of Education
considers it advisable to make provision for
an Organiser of Irish Language Instruction.
under this House, because the people who
are most in favour of that particular language do not take their seats in this House,
and I think during their absence we ought
not to make any provision for that particular matter. The second question is
under the heading "Educational Services.'
I want to know if it is the intention of the
Ministry under the sub-head A 3, "National
Schools," to ensure that before any money
can be paid at all the schools which are
to benefit by that money are agreeable to
throw open their schools before they receive a grant from this particular Department.
I
Mr. DONALD: Like the previous
speaker, I congratulate my hon. Friend
on the very clear and detailed statement
which he has delivered here to-day. Like
my hon. Friend who has just sat down
(Mr. Grant), I simply rise to ask a few
questions. Might I say at the outset
that the whole future of our province depends upon its educational system.
trust, however, that the future will be
very much better with regard to the
administration and payment of teachers
than it has been in the past. Under the
heading, "Salaries, Wages and Allowances'—according to your ruling, we
cannot discuss policy, but this House will
not have an opportunity of discussing
this matter immediately after the
services are transferred to Northern Ireland-I would take the opportunity of
Supply.
Supply.
9 DECEMBER 1921.
criticising the salaries and the different
offices set out under that heading. In
the first place, I should like to ask what
is meant by two Assistant Secretaries,
on page 53, also one Examiner and one
Assistant Principal. Further down we
have one Senior Chief Inspector. I do
not object to that, but you have two
Chief Inspectors, six Inspectors, two Inspectors, two Inspectors, fourteen Inspectors, one Woman Inspector. Then
I agree with my hon. Friend (Mr. Grant)
there is no need for one Organiser of
Irish Language Instruction. What do
we want with the Irish language here?
There is no need for it at all. I was
going to ask a question about the item
Occasional Inspectors (Temporary"),
but it has been explained by my hon.
Friend (Mr. M'Keown) that that arcse
out of an emergency.
I think repre-
We
sentation has already been made to the
Ministry of Education that you have
here something like twenty-five Inspectors, and really in the area covered
by this Parliament one-half of that
number would be quite sufficient.
have got a population just a little over
the population of Glasgow, where there
are only two inspectors. My information is that representation has been made
by the teachers that really three Inspectors-One Chief and two other Inspectors would be quite sufficient for
our Ministry of Education. I do not
wish to criticise at all the grants as outlined by my hon. Friend, but I would
appeal to him, and to the Minister, that
they will make an effort in the very near
future to get some money for the Intermediate Teachers. The best brains of
our country have been going across the
water for some time past, owing to the
fact that the Imperial Government have
failed to give these people a living wage.
Thank God we have got to some extent
the National Teachers, the elementary
teachers, fixed up. I am satisfied from
the speech made here to-day that they
are really going to grapple with this
question in earnest.
Reference was made to technical instruction. I will give my wholehearted
support to any money that is granted for
this purpose.
I trust the system will be
laid down that a boy when he leaves the
elementary school will go to a technical
school, and will eventually land in that
splendid institution that my hon. Friend
(Mr. M'Keown) has mentioned 'to-day,
namely, the Queen's University. That
is my hope. A great deal of the unrest
in this country is due to the fact that
the teachers were trying and striving day
after day to do their best on starvation
wages, and it was only when they realised
that there was nothing for them but to
rebel, that they rose and asserted their
demands. Much of the distress in our
country is due to the fact that these men
have gone away to other circles because
they were not able to make a living at
the teaching profession, and many of
them had University degrees. I do not
wish to take up the time of the House
further. I am satisfied with the progress made.
Time will tell whether it
will be continued. A million and a half
of money will be required, but I am
satisfied from what my hon. Friend, the
Parliamentary and Financial Secretary
to the Ministry of Finance said that he
is going to get in fourteen millions, and
I hope he will be able to give two
millions of that money to education.
References was made to the building of
schools. I think my hon. Friend (Mr.
M'Keown) said £5,000 was to be allocated
for the purpose, but it would take something like £50,000 to be expended in the
near future for the building of healthy
and well-ventilated schools. Some of
our schools will have to be scrapped.
They are not fit for the teaching of
children, they are not really in a sanitary
condition. But I know that this and the
other questions which have been raised
will receive the very careful consideration
not only of my hon. Friend, but also of
my noble Friend, the Minister of Education. I must congratulate the Minister
of Education on the energy he has displayed in the interests of education. I
know that day after day he is visiting
not only our elementary schools, but our
Intermediate Schools, and he is getting
a real grasp of the situation. That is
how it ought to be done. If he has got
to spend this amount of money in the
proper way, he must know where to
spend it, and I say here we are indebted
to our noble Friend, and also to the hon.
Member for West Belfast (Mr. Lynn),
who is giving a great deal of time, for
which this House will not pay him,
sitting day after day as Chairman of the
Education Committee. I know he is
giving it with free will, for he is very
much interested in this question of
education, as, indeed, we all are. This
House will support my hon. Friend in
any money he wants with regard to education. I simply offer the suggestion to him
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Donald.]
that I do not see why twenty-five Inspectors are required. That leads me
to say that I think that a great
deal of the dissatisfaction which exists
among the teachers is due to the fact that
in the Appeal Court the Inspectors form
the Court. I think you should set up an
independent tribunal to deal with
appeals between the Inspectors and
teachers.

urge upon the Minister that there should

EDUCATIONAL SERVICES.
Motion made and Question proposed:
That a sum not exceeding £224,842
be granted to Ifis Majesty for
the service of the period ending 31st
March, 1922, to provide for Educational
Services.
Question put and agreed to.
Dr. MORRISON: Have we done with
education?

Question.
Dr. MORRISON: 1 thought the
ordinary course would be followed, and
that the Minister representing the Office
