CLASS V.
MINISTRY OF AGRICULTURE.
Motion made and Question proposed:
That a sum not exceeding £20,127, including a supplementary sum of £19,887,
be granted to His Majesty to complete the
sum necessary for the service of the period
ending 31st March, 1922, to defray the
salaries and expenses of the Ministry of
Agriculture.' (Voted on Account,
£1,100.)
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Mr. Archdale): I and my officials have
been so careful in regard to economy that
our only fear is the Committee will regret
that they cannot raise our Estimates. My
hon. Friend and colleague in the Imperial
Parliament (Mr. Donald) said just now
that Education was the principal thing. I
do not know what this country would do
without Agriculture. Every man in the
country depends upon Agriculture; there is
not the slightest doubt about that, and the
principal trades depend upon Agriculture.
You will see that we have made a start
with 75 officials.
is to take effect
January only, and course after
that date we
will be responsible for
their salaries. Up to the present the only
officials appointed by myself have been five.
The Department of Agriculture in Dublin
has between 250 and 300 officials, so I
think the proportion that is going to be
allocated to us is not excessive.
The transfer
from the 1st
of
The next item is travelling, subsistence,
and removal expenses, £3,800. Out of
that sum £800 is for the travelling and
subsistence expenses of the officials of the
Department on Departmental work, when
they visit various schools, and things of
that sort. The remaining part is for moving officials down from Dublin and from
various other places, when they come to
take over their duties. It seems a large
sum, but officials of long experience in the
Department believe that it will come to
pretty nearly that amount, there are so
many men with families among them. Of
course, this is the only year that that
amount will be on our Estimates.
I need not delay to make any remarks
on the next item, rent, rates, furniture,
and fittings, and the sum for lighting and
heating is only moderate. Printing, £350;
that is a large sum, but it is only for the
first year. In years to come we hope that
will be greatly reduced. Stationery and
office supplies are a usual item.
We come now to the collection of agricultural statistics. We have arranged for
Supply.
Supply.
9 DECEMBER 1921.
only £103 this year. The collection of
statistics at fairs and markets and at ports
is a most important part of the Agricultural Ministry's work, and I would like to
warn the Committee that in the next few
years we expect that item will be largely
increased. It is the only one, I think,
which will be increased.
I would like to make a few remarks
about the Ordnance Survey. The Director-
General of Ordnance at Southampton estimates that in Northern Ireland the expenses up to the present have been about
£20,000 a year.
Sir Charles Close, the
head of the Ordnance Survey, came over
here and had a long interview with myself
and the permanent head of the Department, Mr. Gordon, and we made an arrangement by which we think the charge
will be only about £12,000, instead of
£20,000. It will certainly not be more
than £12,000. A considerable amount of
that, of course, will come off in maps which
are sold to the public, and there will be
a very great rebate coming into our funds.
Forestry is the next item. This expense
has been borne by the Forestry Commissioners up to the present, and of course we
must carry on the work already started.
There are several forestry stations in Northern Ireland now-one in County Derry,
one in Castlecaldwell in County Fermanagh, and one in Augher, County
Tyrone. The Forestry Commissioners in
London have been able to take on lease
over 2,000 acres of the Duke of Abercorn's
estate at Baronscourt, which will be a very
fine start for forestry schools, for planting,
and for seed stations for trees to replant
our Northern Province, which has seen so
devastated during the war by the cutting
and selling of timber.
Next I would like to speak about the
Research Department, which is one of the
most important sections of our work. It
is divided into four main heads: Seed Testing and Plant Diseases, Plant Breeding,
Poultry and Chemical. It will astonish the
Committee to hear that during the period
of the war 14,000 samples, nearly all seeds,
were tested from the Six Counties. As you
all know, our Northern Counties are the
principal seed centres in the world for ryegrass. Therefore when we get proper seedtesting stations established, as we are
going to do at Queen's University-the
University authorities have been kind
enough to turn over to us some of their
buildings; one part as a free gift, and another part at a very moderate rent-we
hope there will be many more samples sent
there from all parts of the country to be
tested. The amount of grass seed exported
from the North of Ireland in the year 1920
came to £450,000, so I think that anything
that can foster that trade will do some good
to our farmers.
The Poultry Division would please our
lady Members. They will be glad to know
that the amount of poultry and poultry
produce exported from Ireland comes next
to cattle. In the year 1919 the value of
the poultry industry was over £18,000,000,
and we have estimated that of that amount
about £6,800,000 came from our Six
Counties. It is a curious fact that up ull
now all the poultry work of the Department was carried on in the County Cork, at
the Agricultural School there, and that 40
per cent. of the people who sent fowl there
for egg-laying competitions and things of
that sort came from the Six Counties.
When we have a proper egg-laying establishment at one of our Agricultural Schools
in Antrim, Strabane, or Cookstown, we
hope to extend that industry very greatly.
I now come to our Agricultural Institutions, which are down for £1,909. We
have a Dairy School at Cookstown with a
farm attached, and we teach there every
sort of work that is good for young women
to learn-housewifery, buttermaking, and
everything of that sort. There is great
competition for the girls going there, and
we hope to encourage it as much as we possibly can. We have also a similar school
at Strabane, where teaching is carried on
on the same lines, and there is another
school at Greenmount, in the County of
Antrim, only a few acres. We hope to encourage and foster those schools in every
possible way.
A sum of £4,860 is down for the improvement of live stock. That is rather a
large amount, but the Committee should
remember that that is principally for loans
to people who buy stallions or bulls. It is
an extraordinary thing, and it speaks well
for the honesty of the Irish farmer, that
during all the years that the Department
has been in being in Dublin there has
never been a single halfpenny lost in money
loaned for the purchase of stallions and
bulls, although on many occasions there has
been £15,000 out on loan. We wish to
encourage farmers who are not able themselves to buy good bulls by lending money
to them at a fair rate of interest which will
keep us safe, and thus help on the work of
improving the live stock in their neighbourhood.
The grants to the County Committees
have been going on for many years, and we
Supply .
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
[Mr. Archdale.]
are only carrying on the commitments of
the Departments in Dublin.
The flax growing industry is a very important one, and we have been doing our
best by trying a number of varieties to find
a flax which it will pay the Irish farmer
to grow.
During the war there was such
a boom in nax, and the wages for flax pulling went up to such a height, that many
men started flax who knew nothing at all
about growing it. They grew it, badly, and
the result
it was not
fitable to
was
them.
In the
been a
prolast
year or SO there has
good
deal of complaint in the Northern Counties
-everyone has seen it in the papers-because flax did not pay. The farmers did
not get as much money for it as they did
in the boom during the war. We hope to
produce flax-the Linen Trade in the city
of Belfast is laying out a great deal of
money in the same way—that will enable
the farmer to have a paying crop, and thus
help on this great stand-by of the Northern
Province.
The next item is the Potato industry,
which is a very big thing in the Northern
Counties. It may interest the Committee
to know that potatoes to the value of
£2,260,000 were exported from the Northern ports in 1919. We are determined
to do everything we can to encourage the
free sale of potatoes, and to keep out dis-
I am very sorry to that a very
ease.
say
bad disease-black scab-exists in several
parts of our Northern Province. There is
little in Southern Ireland. In County
very
Down and County Derry there have been
a great many instances of black scab, and
we hope by scheduling the affected areas
and seeing that people only plant immune
varieties, to prevent it increasing, and
cause it to die out. We will have great
trouble in doing so, and we hope for the
assistance of all the farmers in the Province
in seeing that our orders are carried out.
We will do as little damage to them as
possible in carrying out our preventive
measures.
Some of the hon. Members might like to
know something about the Stormont Castle
item. When Stormont Castle was bought
we found that several of the fields were
lying derelict. It would not be at all satisfactory to the Northern Department to see
a lot of land at the Parliament House left
in a state of nature, growing nothing but
weeds, and it would not be encouraging to
the farmers to see the place in that condition, so we put a small sum down to proone
vide for tilling and planting and cleaning
those lands. When they are cleaned and
laid out properly, as they ought to be, I
think there will be no more expenditure
for us in connection with Stormont Castle.
The Dairying industry is only a very
small item here, but it is
of the
greatest industries in the North of Ireland,
and we hope with the assistance of the
farmers to improve the dairying produce
and the milking powers of the cows in our
Six Counties. We hardly hope to get ne
cows up to the 3,000 gallons of the American Friesian. The average Irish cow produces about 400 gallons in the year, and if
we make the average cow in Northern Ireland produce 800 gallons in the year on the
same food as the 400 gallon cow, I think
we will have an answer for the spending of
this money. I will be very glad to have
criticism from any hon. Member, and
thing they have to say will be most carefully attended to by myself or the officials
of my Department.
any-
I would also like to say that I think I
am most fortunate in getting the officials
I have earmarked from the Department of
Agriculture in Dublin for our Northern
Department. I have been so long connected with agriculture in Ireland that T
have a considerable knowledge of many of
the officials of the Department, and owing
to their kind help I have-I would not like
to say anything derogatory to the officers
obtained the cream of the whole Department for our Northern Province. I am
certain they will do all they can to help
agriculture to attain the position it should
occupy, that of the primary business in
Northern Ireland.
Mr. DONALD: I only want to say a
word or two of congratulation to my
right hon. Friend and colleague who has
put his case so clearly before the House.
I think in the Imperial House some provision was made in the Emergency Powers
Bill for finding employment in connection with Forestry. I hope my right
hon. Friend will do his best to find employment with regard to that matter, for
it is very necessary at this juncture.
With regard to the black scab in potatoes,
my attention was drawn not very long
ago to a very remarkable thing in the
County Down. One side of the ditch
was a prohibited area, and the other was
not. That state of affairs was very
detrimental to the poor people who were
not allowed to put their potatoes in, and
yet on the other side they were allowed
to do so. The people in the Strangford
district of County Down cannot under-
Supply.
Supply.
9 DECEMBER 1921.
stand it. This disease has
very
farreaching effects. I saw some of the effects
myself, and I know that the right hon.
Gentleman is aware of them, but I think
he should investigate the circumstances
if it hurts poor people who are dependent
to some extent on the
crop for their
livelihood, and see that they are not prevented until a full investigation has been
made. I am satisfied that this Department will go on and develop to a very
large extent a great many things in connection with the poultry trade. I think
you could transfer some of that great industrial exhibition to the North, so that
our people will not have to go to Cork,
and you should try to induce our people,
and especially the young women, to make
butter and so forth as you describe.
That in itself would give employment.
I rose to make these few remarks, and I
am quite satisfied to leave the matter in
the hands of the right hon. Gentleman.
w
Dr. MORRISON: I happen to know
something about agriculture, having lived
all my life in the country, and being president of one of the biggest creameries in
County Derry, or perhaps in Ulster.
That is my justification for intervening
in the discussion. I am glad to know
that the Minister of Agriculture is a
practical man. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) He is a man who knows his work;
he is accessible; he is open to suggestion,
and there is hope when have
you
Department run by a man of that type.
I have been in his office; I have seen the
officials, and I agree with everything he
has said with regard to their grip of the
questions that are affecting our interests
so closely. I am sure this change of the
Department from Dublin to the Northern
Area will be of great advantage to our
people. There are one or two things I
would like to say in regard to the statement which he has submitted. In connection with the collection of statistics,
I would like to ask the Minister if he has
ever thought of getting a return as to
the loss that the agricultural community
suffers through milk fever? Has he ever
thought of getting statistics to demonstrate to the public the injury and the
material loss sustained by the farmers
and breeders through the loss of horses by
tetanus, and is he thinking of designing
some remedy to prevent both these
things. There is another point to which
I would draw the attention of the
Minister specifically, and that is in connection with the efforts he is making with
the object of increasing the milk capacity
of our cows. That is a very important
subject. We are deplorably behind most
countries where they pay attention to
these matters. The right hon. Gentleman is familiar with the controversy that
has arisen as to the best type of cow, and
I find that breeders and others interested
in this question complain that in Dublin
--as I know perfectly well from long experience they would not listen to any
suggestions. I know now that the Minister here is prepared to consider this
question and decide it on its merits, but I
think the question of whether it is more
profitable to develop the Friesian milking
strain or the Shorthorn is a very serious
problem for the farmers, and should have
the consideration of our Department.
I look forward to increased efficiency on
the part of this Department in connection
with agriculture, and on behalf of the
farmers I welcome the right hon. Gentleman and his staff to our Northern Area.
Mr. CRAWFORD: I rejoice greatly
at the statement made by the
Minister of Agriculture this afternoon, and I
forward
am looking
great things from this Department. It is a good thing that our Northern Cabinet recognises farming as one of
our most important industries, and I am
sure none of us will hesitate to vote whatever sum is necessary to carry on the
work. I feel certain that in the hands
of the right hon. Gentleman the interests of the agricultural community
will be well looked after.
to
We
Mr. GRANT: Being a city man I cannot very well criticise the Minister of
Agriculture, but there are one or two
things I would like to mention. I am
particularly interested in the creamery
business, and I am sorry the Minister of
Agriculture has not made more provision for dealing with that matter.
Irish people used to be proud of our Irish
butter. To-day we cannot say anything
in its favour. The majority of people in
Belfast to-day have abolished Irish butter
from their tea table, and I think that is
a deplorable state of affairs. I hope this
Department will inquire into this matter,
and that Irish butter will be restored to
its own proper place. Regarding the
question of milk, my hon. Friend, the
Member for the University (Dr.
Morrison), has mentioned Friesians and
Shorthorns. I am not an expert on that
subject, but I do hope that our Department will soon obtain a two thousand
gallon Friesian.
Supply.
$32
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
Mr. ARCHDALE: I will only take a
few moments to answer the various
questions. The hon. Member for East
Belfast (Mr. Donald) spoke about the
grant given for forestry to assist employment. I went to London to see the
Forestry Commission on that matter, and
I also saw Lord Lovat, the Chairman of
the Forestry Commission, and the Minister for Agriculture, Sir Arthur Boscawen.
I have arranged that if anyone here goes
in for planting he will get money for employment, but the difficulty is that the
unemployment exists in the towns, and
that there is no unemployment where
there is planting. You must have a man
near his work to get the benefit of the unemployment grant. The hon. Member
also referred to the black scab, and to a
man not being allowed to grow potatoes
on one side of a hedge while they were
growing on the other side. That has to
be done because black scab has been
found on one side and not on the other,
We intend to circle that small area round
so as to let as many people as possible
grow what they like. The man on the
left hand side of the hedge has ground
free from black scab. You must have a
division somewhere, and the hedge marks
the area of that man's farm. By circling
round small affected areas, more freedom
is allowed to the poor persons to grow
what potatoes they want. I have to
thank the hon. Member for the University (Dr. Morrison) for his kind remarks,
and I hope we will get statistics about
milk fever, to which he referred.
knows as a practical man that the dairy
herds of this country were nearly ruined
by abortion some years ago, and it is one
of the greatest curses that ever came to
any dairy or farming country. We will
try to get statistics on these various
subjects. He also mentioned Friesian
COWS. We are considering the matter at
present. I suppose every breed has its
strong adherents, but there is a pretty
strong controversy going on in the papers
at present in regard to the rival merits
of the Friesian and the Shorthorn. 1
believe that has gone on for the last
twenty years, but how it will end I cannot say.
I have also to thank the hon.
Member for Antrim (Mr. Crawford) for
his kind remarks, and I can assure him
we will try our best to earn his enconiums
in future years when we come to this Committee for an increased grant. As regards the statement of the hon. Member
for North Belfast (Mr. Grant), we have
He
inspectors for the creameries, and we are
doing our best to see that Irish butter
regains its good qualities. Irish butter
is not to be beaten if the producers uu
not play the fool with it and mix it with
other trash which they call butter. I
believe that was done in privately-owned
factories, not co-operative factories.
They mixed up a lot of rubbish with the
butter and sent it over to London as
creamery butter. We are determined to
prevent that, and to see that only Irish
butter goes to the market as Irish butter,
and I hope it will beat the Danish in a
very short time.
Question put, and agreed to.
AGRICULTURAL SERVICES.
Resolved, That a sum not exceeding
£19,049 be granted to His Majesty for
the service of the period ending 31st
March, 1922, to defray the expenses of
the Ministry of Agriculture in respect of
Agricultural Institutions, Improvement
of Live Stock, Agricultural Research
Development, including sundry grants in
aid."
CLASS VI.
MINISTRY OF COMMERCE.
Motion made and Question put: "That
sum not exceeding £5,535, including a
supplementary sum of £5,010, be granted
to His Majesty to complete the sum necessary for the service of the period ending
31st March, 1922, to defray the salaries
and expenses of the Ministry of Commerce.
(Voted on Account, £2,100.)"
new
In,
Mr. M'KEOWN: My right hon. Friend.
the Minister of Commerce (Mr. Archdale),
has asked me to place these Estimates before the House. The acting Minister for
Finance (Mr. Barbour) yesterday referred
to the Ministry of Commerce as a
Ministry, inasmuch as we had had no corresponding Department in Ireland.
however, practically every European
country, in the Colonies, and in America,
there is a Ministry of Trade, or a Ministry
under some name, which is interested in
the commerce and industry of the country.
In pre-war days we knew how valuable
such a Ministry was to the commercial development of Germany. In Britain we
have the Board of Trade, and there is a
natural tendency in considering the setting.
up of this Ministry to look at that organization. There is, however, a vast differ-
The Board of Trade, in its general
ence.
Supply.
Supply.
9 DECEMBER 1921.
economic work, is, on account of the vastness of the problems to be dealt with, compelled to generalize in its advice and assistance, and in its view of all matters coming
before it. The Ministry of Commerce, it
is true, cannot ignore any of these world
problems. It must, in fact, study them.
closely, but the Ministry will not so much
be responsible for them as interested in
them. Its duty is to be able to give active
and close detailed assistance to the Commerce of the Province; to be, in fact, more
domestic in operation but influential and
equally knowledgable in the bigger issues.
While, therefore, it would be unwise to
take the Board of Trade as a close precedent, it will be wisdom to study its organization, and the effects of its activities having in mind the formation of a small but
compact, and active but non-interfering,
Department, and also the fact that the
Board of Trade still exists, and that a
strong link with it will ensure close touch
with, and it is hoped, influence upon the
big problems as they affect the manufacturers and traders of this Province, and
the general interests of its people. Broadly
speaking, therefore, the primary function
of the Ministry of Commerce is to foster
without interfering, to help without sultifying enterprise and initiative, and by re
search, acting as Intelligence Department, and by other means to develop
and to help to develop at the proper time,
all roads to increased productivity and increased wealth in the Province,
It is true that the Ministry has certain
statutory and administrative duties. The
performance of these duties satisfactorily
is important. They are most of them linked
up with trade, and, in fact, if wisely administered, can be made useful to traders,
instead of creating an impression of State
Control, and, moreover, in the administration of same lies the germ of useful development. It follows, therefore, that the
Ministry is divided into two main departments. Policy, to be known as Commerce
and Economic Department, and Administration. Much organizing on economic
subjects has already been done, and many
traders have sought the advice and assistance of the Department. Therefore, it
will not be difficult for hon. Members to
see that the attention of those who have
to administer this work is going to be very
fully engaged.
The administrative work will comprise
registration of Joint Stock Companies and
allied matters, such as registration of
business names, newspaper proprietors,
moneylenders, friendly societies, all matters
which will require expert knowledge and
great accuracy. The Ministry will also
have the administration of the Electricity
Acts, Gas Acts, Water Acts, Mining Acts,
Weights and Measures Acts, Inland Navigation, Canals, Harbours, Docks and Piers,
and certain powers in regard to railway construction and transport development. This,
as far as general language can convey it, is
the task of the Ministry. In presenting
this Supplementary Estimate there is not
much to comment upon. It covers the
period from the 1st January, 1922, to 31st
March, 1922. In September last this
House approved a Vote of £2,625, and it
is estimated that on 31st December next
there will be unexpended out of this Vote
the sum of £590. This saving has been
taken into account in preparing the
Supplementary Estimate now presented of
£5,010, so that the gross estimated expenditure of the Ministry of Commerce for the
quarter ending 31st March next is estimated at £5,010 plus £590, making a total
of £5,600.
One feature of the Estimates calling for
explanation is the Sub-head "H" (Fees to
Consultants). The Ministry will, as far
as possible, utilise the services of a General
Engineer attached to the Government for
all its works of inspection and technical
advice, in connection with gas, water,
electricity and mining. It would, however, seem too much to hope that such an
officer could combine all the qualifications
necessary to afford; the Ministry expert
technical advice on its very varied functions,
and accordingly, the services of a consultant or the expert advice of some body, such
as the Electricity Commissioners, must necessarily be estimated for. There is one
other feature which I am sure hon. Members will forgive me for drawing attention
to. I do it in consequence of the debates
that have recently taken place in this
House. The matter to which I refer is
that the Minister of Commerce and the
Parliamentary Secretary do not draw any
salaries. I beg to move the Estimates.
Mr. CRAWFORD: There is one question
I would like to ask. In Clause B there is
an item of £1,500-Removal and Subsistence Expenses of Officers appointed to the
Ministry at Belfast from other centres.
The Ministry has altogether twenty-nine
employees, and making allowance for charwomen, messengers, etc., they will probably have fifteen officials, and it is to cost
£1,500 to bring them here! May I ask
if they are being brought from the planet
Mars? I think such a sum is absurd.
KK
Supply.
Supply.
HOUSE OF COMMONS
Mr. DONALD: Will my hon. Friend,
the Parliamentary Secretary to the
Ministry of Commerce, tell us what are the
views of his Department with regard to
the development of roads, canals, railways,
etc., in order to find employment? This
question was discussed for hours in the
Imperial Parliament, and they are most
anxious that all Ministries should develop
in every possible way canals, bridges, railways, or anything that will help to find
employment. I hope my hon. Friend will
be able to give us some enlightenment.
Mr. GRANT: I would like to again
congratulate my hon. Friend, the Parliamentary Secretary to the Ministry of
Commerce for the manner in which he
has presented the Estimates. He
mentioned some of the duties of the
Ministry, and referred to Harbours and
Piers. I would like to know whether we
require any new Piers or Harbour extensions, and if the Ministry will be responsible for finding the money? If
they will there is a very wide field before
the Ministry.
Mr. McKEOWN: In answer to my
hon. Friend, the Member for Antrim,
the sum of £1,500 for removals and subsistence is of course problematical to a
considerable extent, but we have got to
estimate for it. You must bear in mind
that we were obliged to take a number of
officials from Dublin. Very often it is
not possible to get these people houses
immediately they arrive here, and so a
certain amount of expense devolves on
this Department, as on other Departments, in the matter of subsistence. This
is an estimate, but in all probability the
amount will not be spent, and whatever
is not spent will of course be accounted
for. With regard to the question asked
by the Member for East Belfast (Mr.
Donald), there are certain things which
it is hoped the Minister of Commerce
will be able to do. As he stated, the
Imperial Parliament has been making
efforts to find employment, and has provided certain grants. Through the
Ministry of Commerce we have got in
touch with Committees in charge of these
grants in London, and we are doing all
we possibly can to get a due proportion
of this money
for Northern Ireland.
With reference to the matter raised by
the hon. Member for North Belfast (Mr.
Grant), there are duties devolving on the
Ministry of Commerce regarding Harbours, etc., which are spread over the
whole of the Six Counties, but it would
be quite impossible for me to give any
estimate as to the work that will be
undertaken. I thank hon. Members for
the way they have received the estimates.
Question put, and agreed to.
Ordered: That the resolutions be reported forthwith.
Resolutions reported.
on Monday next.
Report stage
SUPPLY (8th DECEMBER) REPORT.
Resolutions reported as follows.

"That a sum not exceeding Twentythree thousand, two hundred and eighty
pounds, including a Supplementary sum
of Twenty-three thousand and eighty
pounds, be granted to His Majesty to
defray the charge which will come in
course of payment during the year ending 31st March, 1922, for the Expenses
of the Ministry of Home Affairs."
2. CRIMINAL PROSECUTIONS AND
OTHER LAW CHARGES.
"That a sum not exceeding Six
thousand, eight hundred and twelve
pounds be granted to His Majesty to
defray the charge which will come in
course of payment during the year ending 31st March, 1922, for the Expenses
of Criminal Prosecutions and other Law
Charges."
3. COUNTY COURT OFFICERS.
"That a sum not exceeding Five
thousand and forty pounds be granted
to His Majesty to defray the charge
which will come in course of payment
during the year ending 31st March,
1922, for the Salaries, Allowances and
Expenses of various County Court
Officers.

"That a sum not exceeding Twentytwo thousand, five hundred pounds be
granted to His Majesty to defray the
charge which will come in course of payment during the year ending 31st March,
1922, for the Expenses of Prisons."

thousand eight hundred pounds be

This

Bill, as hon. Members are aware, has
It was

drawn up on the lines of the .Report of

Salaries of Ministerial Offices Bill; on

Mr. DONALD: Would it require a

Mr. SPEAKER: It would require a

which the hon. Member (Mr. Burn)
has just made I think all the
business that remains is practically
formal, except possibly the Appropriation
Bill, on which certain questions of
general policy can be raised. Unless
there is such a question, the business is
purely formal.

Motion for Adjournment.
PRIME MINISTER'S STATEMENT.
Motion made and Question proposed:
"That this House do now Adjourn"-
Mr. Burn.]
The PRIME MINISTER: I desire to
address the House, and through the House
the country, who are so anxiously awaiting
some report of our latest experiences when
in London, and also some indication of the
course we intend to pursue. I may say
that in this matter not only is my own
personal advice being accepted but that of
all the Members of our Cabinet, and I
believe I may say of all the Members of
our party both in the Senate and the House
of Commons-(hon. Members: Hear, hear)
-because the recent developments necessitated my taking into my entire confidence
the whole party which I have the honour
543 Prime Minister's Statement. 12 DECEMBER 1921. Prime Minister's Statement.544
to lead. We have put our heads together.
We have most anxiously considered the
difficult and trying position that lies immediately ahead. Having come to a conclusion I think it will best satisfy public
opinion outside if I make a short statement, to a certain extent a reassuring statement, certainly one which I earnestly trust
and believe will be acted upon. Το
characterise the position as anything short
of grave, serious, would be a great mistake.
I have faced many a difficult problem. I
have faced, we have all faced, times of
grave crisis. Sometimes it has been possible to make up our minds in a moment
as to the right attitude to observe, and on
other occasions it has taken us a considerable time to thresh out the proper course
to pursue; but never before has the situation been so complicated as that which has
been created by the signatures attached to
what is called a Treaty between the British
representatives on the one hand and the
Sinn Fein representatives on the other.
We are not included in the Treaty. We
were not invited to sign the Treaty. In
pursuance of our attitude throughout we
refused to either interfere with or attempt
to determine the terms of any settlement
which might be reached between those two
parties, but we reserved to ourselves the
right to go into conference with British
Ministers when Ulster's rights and privileges became affected. On that clear and
distinct understanding the Prime Minister
of England assured me--assured this House
by the statement he permitted me to read
at this Table-that by last Tuesday either
the negotiations would have broken
down or that fresh proposals would be
submitted, and that, in the meantime, the
rights of Ulster would neither be sacrificed
nor prejudiced. I think those were the
exact words. Without going into details
in the matter I must confess that the
Treaty has not carried out that solemr
pledge to this House and to the Ulster
people that their rights would not be prejudiced or sacrificed.
However, feeling
strongly as I do, and as I know all my
colleagues do, in regard to this matter, I
would ask the people to bear in mind that
the steps we take one after the other must
be constitutional steps. We have pledged
ourselves, and I hope I will never depart.
from that pledge-whatever others may do
we will treat these matters in all seriousness-we will never depart from the pledge
that we are only anxious to remain part and
parcel of Great Britain and of the Empire.
That pledge holding good whatever steps
we are determined upon must be constitutional steps. Now what are the constitutional steps to be taken in a grave situation
of the kind? What can this House do?
What can this Parliament do except enter
its most earnest protest against the treatment that has been meted out to our
people, and to call upon our representatives
in the Imperial Parliament to now take
up the cause and to see it through? There
are two points at the moment facing our
people that cause grave dissatisfaction, and,
indeed, alarm. One is with regard to
finance, and the other is with regard to the
appointment of a Commission to inquire
into our boundaries. It would be easy for
me to deal with both of these subjects at
great length here in the House this afternoon, and I would have much to say about
them, and could speak very strongly, but
I have consulted those leaders whose
opinion I still value in London, and I have
consulted my colleagues and my whole
party, and we think that under all the
circumstances the constitutional steps to
be taken are to work in close harmony with
that gallant band of Ulster representative
in the House of Commons at Westminster,
and with those loyal Unionist peers who
are prepared to stand up for us in the
House of Lords. They will tackle both of
these subjects, and I may say that before
leaving London I made the fullest arrangements covering every detail of the action
that is to be taken. One reason why it is
perhaps better for the exercise of caution
on our part to-day is because I am extremely anxious not to put our enemies into
possession of the steps that we propose to
take, and also because I believe we here
in this Parliament are acting along much
more proper lines by allowing a matter
affecting the alteration of the Act of 1920,
which was passed by the Imperial Parliament, to be debated in the first instance
within the walls of the Imperial Parliament, and to stand up for those rights and
privileges to which we are entitled.
Another very important point is that it
would seem improper of me to anticipate
the debates which will take place there on
Wednesday. I have been in touch, as I
say, with many of those who are to take
part in the debates in both the House of
Commons and the House of Lords, and
having come to an amicable agreement with
them dealing with very serious matters, it
is better far that they should come fresh
from those two Houses than that I should
anticipate the arguments that they will
probably use. Then there is a third point,
and a very important one. We here, so
far back as I can go in political history,
545 Prime Minister's Statement.

HOUSE OF COMMONS
Adjournment.
[The Prime Minister.]
have never deviated from what our aim
has always been. Parties come and parties
go but the people of Ulster have kept before them the one straight path, and along
that they have always walked. At the present moment your Government, I myself,
and the whole party, are determined not
to swerve one inch from that path or to
alter the ideals we have always et before
us. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) At the
same time this is a matter of great gravity,
the steps that are to be taken to ensure the
best interests of Ulster, and I strongly recommend all my colleagues of this House
and in the Senate to maintain an attitude
of caution during the next few days, of
courage, of prudence, and above all of
hopeful optimism, that if a settlement
comes through the Treaty that has been
signed by the two parties-if a settlement
does come-it will bring peace and happiness to our land. I myself am not dissatisfied at the moment with the outlook.
There are dark clouds; there are grave
difficulties. I believe that the one will be
swept away and the others will be overcome. We have constantly faced similar
predicaments-not exactly the same, but
problems of equal weight and equal difficulty and so far, I think we have
triumphed over them all. The point I am
trying to make is that just as many of them
were overcome not by shouting, not by
resolutions, not by meetings here and
there, but by maintaining a calm and reassuring attitude, so do I recommend
to-day, at another crisis in our history, that
the people should adopt that course and
trust those who have been so recently returned as their representatives in all parts
of the Six Counties, to see that in no way
are their rights and privileges encroached
upon. I cannot say more to-day without
transgressing from the path I have laid out
for myself and my collegues, I simply
say to the people that if they will trust us
to the fullest extent, we on our part will
cautiously lead them once more to victory.
(Cheers.)
Mr. M'GUFFIN: I do not propose to
make any observations on the exceedingly
able statement just made by the Prime
Minister. On another occasion I interrogated him with regard to the publication of
the correspondence between the Northern
Cabinet and the British Cabinet, I
would like to ask the right hon.
Gentleman if that question was under
consideration on the occasion of his visit
to London, and if so has he arrived at a
specific conclusion in respect to the matter,
and when we are likely to see that correspondence? I feel that it would be very
prejudicial to the discussion which will
arise in another place if we have not this
information before us. The Press of the
country has consistently misrepresented
our case, which I feel has been adequately
stated in the negotiations with the Cabinet,
and if we had only an opportunity of reading that correspondence it would be good
for the country and good for us.
As
The PRIME MINISTER: I can only
say in answer to that question that over
and over again we have pressed His
Majesty's Government to publish the whole
correspondence. We feel, as my hon
Friend feels, that if it were published the
public would then be put in possession of
the actual facts as regards Ulster, and that
it would clear away an enormous amount
of misrepresentation to which we have been
subjected during the past few mon.hs.
a rule, when one goes into private neg tiations the publication of the correspondence
is dependent upon the acquiescence of both
sides. Up to the present, I regret to say,
I have been unable to persuade is
Majesty's Government to piace the whole
correspondence before the public. I would
be the last person to want to take a false
step at the moment. I said when I stood
at this Table before that if in the interests
of Ulster I thought it would be a good
thing to publish the correspondence I would
do so unhesitatingly. I do not think it
would ease the situation at the moment.
am sure the House will be guided by me
when I say that to publish that correspon-
Idence at the moment without the leave of
the British Cabinet would possibly do harm
instead of good to the Ulster cause.
I
Question: That this House do now
adjourn," put, and agreed to.
Adjourned
accordingly at
a Quarter before Three of the
Clock.

13 DECEMBER 1921.
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Tuesday, 13th December, 1921.
The House met at a Quarter after Two
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Oral Answers to Questions.
LOCAL GOVERNMENT
(EMERGENCY POWERS) ACT.
Mr. ROBB asked the Minister of
Home Affairs if he will lay upon the Table
of the House a copy of any Order made
by the Ministry of Home Affairs under
the Local Government [Emergency
Powers] Act, 1921, on the first opportunity after the making of any such
Order?
THE MINISTER OF HOME
AFFAIRS : The answer to the hon.
Member's question is in the affirmative.
DISTRICT-INSPECTOR JOSEPH
EDWARDS, R.I.C.
Mr. DONALD (for Mr. COOTE) asked
the Minister for Home Affairs whether
District-Inspector Joseph Edwards,
R.I.C., stationed at Omagh, Co. Tyrone,
was reported to the Divisional Commissioner for North-East Ulster, at Belfast,
within the three months last past, that at
Omagh he did associate with Sinn Feiners,
and what was the result of that Officer's
enquiry, regarding the District-Inspector's conduct?
The hon. Member (for Mr. Coote) also
asked the Minister for Home Affairs by
what Authority was District-Inspector
Joseph Edwards, stationed at Omagh,
without charge, placed in charge of the
R.I.C. County Tyrone, during the
absence of the County-Inspector, for
three months last past, notwithstanding
there are three Senior Officers in the
County, and that District-Inspector
Edwards is a Junior, 3rd Class, contrary
to the Regulations?
Sir DAWSON BATES: With the hon.
Member's permission I will answer both
these questions together. The Divisional
Commissioner informs me that District-
Inspector Edwards has not been in charge
of County Tyrone at any time since the
Northern Government became responsible
for law and order in Northern Ireland,
and I cannot answer for any events prior
to that date. He further informs me
that he received no official report of the
nature indicated, and no enquiry has,
therefore, been held into this officer's
conduct. If a definite charge is made
by any responsible person affecting the
conduct of a police officer, I shall have it
fully inquired into.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That, notwithstanding anything contained in Standing Order No. 1 (Sittings
of the House), this House do meet tomorrow at Twelve of the Clock."
Mr. BURN: I beg to move the Motion
standing in my name- that the House do
meet to-morrow at 12 o'clock. Perhaps
I had better tell the House why we are
asking that this should be done. It is
to enable us to have the Royal Assent
given to whatever measures we pass. The
House can suspend the sitting for a short
time, probably until 2-15 p.m., and after
that, when we have received the Royal
Assent to these Bills, the Prorogation
will take place.
、
Question put. and agreed to.
STANDING ORDERS RELATIVE TO

Report from the Joint Select Committee to lie upon the Table.

The Irish Negotiations.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That this House do now adjourn,"-
[Captain Dixon].
The PRIME MINISTER: In moving
the adjournment, I would like to inform
the House that since we met yesterday
I have received a communication from
Mr. Lloyd George to say that in view of
my remarks yesterday in this House he
proposes to publish the correspondence
up-to-date in connection with the Treaty
that has been entered into between the
representatives of His Majesty's Government and the representatives of the South
and West of Ireland. I can only say
that on receiving that intimation I immediately acquiesced, and, therefore, the
correspondence should be in the hands of
everybody through the Press to-morrow.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I might
say, with regret, that owing to the extraordinary pressure on the time of my
colleagues and myself we have been unable to frame a reply to the last letter
we received from Mr. Lloyd George, but
in order to make the correspondence upto-date, as soon as we have written that
letter dealing with the last matters submitted to us, we will publish it ourselves
in order that you here in the North may
be in possession of the whole correspondence from the beginning to the end.
Question: "That this House do now
adjourn," put, and agreed to.
Adjourned
accordingly at
Half after Two of the Clock
till 12 O'Clock to-morrow.
Order of the Day. 552
Notices of Motion.
14 DECEMBER 1921
HOUSE OF COMMONS.

Wednesday, 14th December, 1921.
The House met at Twelve Noon of the
Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Notices of Motion.
LOCAL BILLS, STANDING ORDERS.
Motion made, and Question proposed:
"That the Standing Orders relating to
Local Bills, as amended in the Joint Committee, be adopted."-[Mr. Burn.]
The CHAIRMAN OF WAYS AND
MEANS (Mr. Moles): It will not be
necessary for me to occupy the time of the
House more than a minute or two while I
explain to them very briefly what is involved in this somewhat cumbersome
document. What we have done in the
Committee which prepared these draft
Standing Orders is to follow as closely as
was necessary for our purpose the exact
procedure laid down in the Imperial
Parliament, and to take from the Standing Orders in respect of Private Bills of
the House of Lords as well as the House
of Commons such as suited our purpose,
and we have made what we regard, and
what we ask the House to accept as an
ideal set of Standing Orders. In so far
as those who desire to promote Private
Bills are concerned, I may be permitted
to call attention to one matter. The
scale of fees which we have laid down
differs enormously from those on the other
side, and it differs in favour of the promoter. We have cut this scale of fees
down in a most drastic way, so that small
bodies desiring to promote Private Bills.
may not feel that the cost of promoting
them is undue. We have aimed rather
at facilitating and cheapening the whole.
procedure, and we ask the House in those
circumstances to adopt these Standing
Orders.
Question put, and agreed to.
Ordered, That the said Standing
Orders as amended be printed.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That notwithstanding anything contained in the Standing Orders,
of the custom of this House, the Consolidated Fund (Appropriation) Bill be
passed through all its remaining stages
at this day's sitting."-[Mr. Burn.]
Order of the Day.
CONSOLIDATED FUND (APPRO-
PRIATION) BILL.
Order for Second Reading read.
Resolved, "That the Bill be now read
the Second Time."-[Mr. Andrews.]

Mr. MOLES in the Chair.

Mr. SPEAKER: That concludes the
business on the Order Paper, and, as we
have to await the Royal Assent to some
Bills, I propose to suspend the sitting.
The exact time at which I will take the
Chair, according to when the Royal
Assent is received, will be communicated
to hon. Members by the ringing of the
bell.
Sitting suspended at Ten minutes after
Twelve noon of the Clock.

Twenty-five Minutes after Four of the

HOUSE OF COMMONS
MESSAGE FROM THE SENATE.
The Senate agree to:
The Speaker of the Senate Bill,
The Speaker of the House of Commons
Bill,
Salaries of Ministerial Offices Bill, and
The Appropriation Bill,
Without any amendment.
ROYAL ASSENT.
The PRIME MINISTER at the Bar
reported to the House that his Excellency
the Lord Lieutenant, in the name and on
behalf of His Majesty the King, had been
pleased to give his assent to the following
Acts agreed upon by both Houses :
1. Local Government (Emergency
Powers) Act, 1921.
2. Ministries of Northern Ireland
Act, 1921.
3. Speaker of the Senate Act, 1921.
4. Speaker of the House of Commons
Act, 1921.
5. Salaries of Ministerial Offices Act,
1921.
6. Appropriation Act, 1921.
Prorogation.
MESSAGE FROM THE SENATE.
A message was delivered by Sir
Frederick Moneypenny, C.V.O., C.B.E.,
Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod, as
followeth :
I am commanded by the Senate to
inform this Honourable House that the
Speech of His Excellency the Lord
Lieutenant-General and General
Governor of Ireland at the Prorogation
of Parliament is to be communicated in
the Senate forthwith, and I am commanded to invite this Honourable
House to attend in the Senate in order
to hear the said Speech.
The House went, and having returned
Mr. SPEAKER (standing in the
Clerk's place at the Table): I have to inform the House that the House has been
to the Senate where the Speaker of the
Senate communicated His Excellency's
Speech to both Houses of Parliament, as
follows:
HIS EXCELLENCY'S SPEECH.
MEMBERS OF THE SENATE AND OF THE
HOUSE OF COMMONS.
The first Session of your Parliament
which closes to-day has necessarily been
of a routine character in consequence
of the delay in transferring the Services under the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920. I note with satisfaction
that progress in this direction is now
being made, and that it is confidently
anticipated that every Department of
Government will be established early in
the New Year.
The Joint Exchequer Board and
Civil Service Committee have been set
up by Order in Council, and the
necessary representatives on them have
been duly nominated. I feel certain
that they will function with smoothness and efficiency.
The Ministry of Home Affairs being
now in possession of full authority over
matters relating to Law and Order will
henceforth be in a position to take all
necessary steps to protect life and property, and to restore confidence among
the people.
As is general throughout the world,
unemployment, I regret to say, affects
the community in Northern Ireland in
an intensified form. You are to be
congratulated upon having secured
the benefits provided by legislation
recently passed in the Imperial Parliament. I am aware that the Ministry of
Labour has appointed two Committees
to enquire into local conditions affecting Trade Boards, Employment Exchanges, and Unemployment Insurance,
and is closely watching the situation
with a view to alleviating it so far as is
possible.
For the convenience of the Department concerned, the 1st of February
next has been fixed for the transfer of
the services connected with the
Ministry of Education. Meanwhile
the "Londonderry Committee" has
under review the whole question of
Education, and it is hoped that a Bill
will in due time be based upon its
report.
Agriculture has suffered a set back
in sympathy with falling trade, unem-
555 His Excellency's Speech.
His Excellency's Speech. 556
14 DECEMBER 1921
ployment and uncertainty as to the
future. I trust, however, that the
signs of revival in other industries,
however slight, may encourage those
engaged in the production of food for
the people, and that the New Year
may find us restored to more normal
conditions.
MEMBERS OF THE HOUSE OF COMMONS.
I thank you for the provisions for
the Speakers of both Houses and for
the public services.
Financial responsibility has been
transferred to the Government at a
time of unprecedented depression; it
now appears impossible to realize the
expectations of a large surplus, foreshadowed in the white paper on which
the financial Clauses of the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, were based. It,
therefore, becomes imperative that expenditure in many departments should
be restricted to the bare necessities of
the case. I confidently rely upon the
people, in their own best interests,
loyally to support the Ministers of the
Crown in their endeavours to promote
economy.
MEMBERS OF THE SENATE AND OF THE
HOUSE OF COMMONS.
I earnestly pray that the efforts of
the British Government towards securing peace throughout Ireland may meet
with success, and, that the result of
such peace may penetrate to every
hearth, so that, when next you are
summoned to meet, it may be in circumstances far different from those
which lie immediately behind us.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your labours.
After which the Proclamation for the
Proroguing of Parliament was read as
follows:
BY HIS EXCELLENCY THE LORD
LIEUTENANT-GENERAL AND GEN-
ERAL GOVERNOR OF IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
Proroguing the present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, that
the Lord
His
Lieutenant shall, in
Majesty's name, summon, prorogue, and
dissolve the Parliament of Northern
Ireland.
And Whereas it is desirable that this
present Parliament of Northern Ireland should be prorogued to the twentyfirst day of February One Thousand
Nine Hundred and Twenty Two:
Now I, the Right Honourable
Edmund Bernard, Viscount Fitzalan
of Derwent, Lieutenant-General and
General Governor of Ireland, by this
Proclamation do publish and declare
His Majesty's Pleasure that the said
Parliament be as from the reading of
this Proclamation in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland prorogued to Tuesday
the twenty-first day of February One
Thousand Nine Hundred and Twenty
Two:
And I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to
be that the said Parliament shall on
the said Twenty First day of February
One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Twenty Two assemble and be holden for
the despatch of divers urgent and important affairs, and the Senators and
Members of the House of Commons of
Northern Ireland are hereby required
to give their attendance accordingly.
Given at His Majesty's Castle of
Dublin, this twelfth day of
December, 1921.
(Signed)
FITZALAN OF DERWENT.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the First Session (opened

Tuesday, 7th June, 1921) of the First Parliament of Northern Ireland, in the twelfth
year of the Reign of His Majesty King
George the Fifth.

INDEX.
VOLUME OF FIRST SESSION. 1921.
:
7th JUNE to 14th DECEMBER.
